### [1] [SalesSim: Benchmarking and Aligning Multimodal Language Models as Retail User Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08334)
*Yada Pruksachatkun,Elaine Wan,Lyanna Chen,Kai-Wei Chang,Chien-Sheng Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SalesSim is a framework and benchmark to test how well multimodal LLMs can act as realistic, persona-driven online shoppers in tool-augmented retail chats, plus a new RL method (UserGRPO) that improves persona-consistent decision-making and conversation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing user simulators for dialogue systems often reduce users to shallow text generators, ignoring realistic shopping behavior, grounded decision-making, and strict adherence to diverse user personas. The authors want a principled way to evaluate whether MLLMs can faithfully follow user personas and behave like real shoppers in multi-turn, multi-modal retail scenarios, and to identify and fix the behavioral gaps compared to humans.

Method: 1) Build SalesSim, a framework where simulated shoppers have rich personas (background, preferences, dealbreakers) and interact with a sales agent in multi-turn, multi-modal, tool-augmented conversations to reach a purchase decision. 2) Define decision-alignment metrics that quantify how well the simulator’s actions and final decisions follow the specified persona, along with conversational quality metrics. 3) Benchmark 6 state-of-the-art open and closed-source MLLMs on these tasks. 4) Propose UserGRPO, a multi-turn, multi-objective RL training recipe that jointly optimizes for decision alignment with persona specs and conversational fluency, and evaluate its effect on the baseline model.

Result: Benchmarking shows that current MLLMs, while fluent, lack lexical diversity, over-expose decision criteria in ways not consistent across personas, and are easily swayed by the sales agent, drifting away from their persona constraints. Even the best model reaches under 79% average decision alignment with the underlying persona. Applying UserGRPO to a baseline model yields a 13.8% absolute improvement in decision alignment, along with better conversational quality metrics.

Conclusion: SalesSim reveals substantial gaps between current MLLM-based user simulators and realistic, persona-faithful shopper behavior in online retail dialogues. The decision-alignment metrics and empirical results show that adherence to persona constraints and robustness to persuasion are not yet solved. UserGRPO offers a promising RL-based approach to narrow these gaps, improving both persona-consistent decision-making and conversation quality, and SalesSim serves as a reusable testbed for further research on user simulators in goal-oriented settings.

Abstract: We present SalesSim, a framework and testbed for evaluating the ability of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to simulate realistic, persona-driven customer behavior in multi-turn, multi-modal, tool-augmented online retail conversations. Unlike prior work that treat user simulation as surface-level dialogue generation, SalesSim models retail interaction and decision-making as a grounded, agentic process, where shoppers with diverse backgrounds, preferences, and dealbreakers interact with a sales agent, seek clarifications, and make informed purchasing decisions. For evaluation, we design a suite of metrics centered on decision alignment, measuring the consistency between the simulator's actions and its persona specifications, as well as conversational quality. We find several behavioral gaps after benchmarking 6 open and closed-source state-of-the-art models. First, while models produce fluent conversations, they display significantly lower lexical diversity and overdisclosure of criteria across personas compared to human conversations. Second, models tend to be persuaded by sales agent suggestions and drift from persona specifications. Even the strongest model achieves less than 79% average alignment with its underlying persona specifications. To make progress on these limitations, we propose UserGRPO, a multi-turn, multi-objective reinforcement learning recipe to optimize both conversational fluency and decision alignment under persona specifications. Our experiments demonstrate that UserGRPO boosts decision alignment of the baseline model by 13.8% while improving conversational quality. By introducing SalesSim, we provide a new testbed for the community to investigate and improve the adherence of user simulators in goal-oriented settings.

</details>


### [2] [Sanity Checks for Long-Form Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08346)
*Geigh Zollicoffer,Minh Vu,Hongli Zhan,Raymond Li,Manish Bhattarai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether hallucination detectors for large language models truly evaluate chain-of-thought reasoning or just rely on artifacts of the final answer, and introduces a methodology and a lightweight method (TRACT) to disentangle and better exploit reasoning signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting becomes common, many hallucination detection methods claim to be reasoning-aware, but it is unclear if they actually analyze reasoning or simply pick up on superficial features of the final answer. This ambiguity makes it difficult to design reliable detectors and to understand what current methods are really doing.

Method: The authors propose a controlled-invariance methodology with two oracle tests applied to CoT traces: (1) FORCE, which replaces each response's final answer with the ground-truth answer while leaving the reasoning trace unchanged; and (2) REMOVE, which removes explicit answer-announcement steps but preserves the rest of the reasoning trajectory. By observing how detectors’ performance changes under these manipulations, they can diagnose whether detectors depend on final-answer artifacts. They then design TRACT, a lightweight hallucination scorer based purely on lexical trajectory features of the reasoning process, including hedging patterns over steps, how step lengths evolve, and the degree of vocabulary convergence across multiple responses.

Result: The oracle tests show that many existing hallucination detectors heavily rely on answer-level artifacts instead of the structure or validity of the reasoning trace. When these artifacts are controlled via FORCE and REMOVE, the performance of such detectors drops, revealing limited true reasoning awareness. TRACT, despite using simple lexical trajectory features rather than complex learned representations, remains robust under these controls and performs competitively with or better than existing baselines on standard (unperturbed) CoT traces.

Conclusion: The study concludes that chain-of-thought traces contain meaningful signals for hallucination detection, but current methods often fail to isolate these signals from superficial endpoint cues in the final answer. With controlled-invariance testing, one can disentangle these effects, and simple feature-based approaches like TRACT can effectively leverage reasoning trajectories. The main challenge ahead is not extracting signal from reasoning traces but carefully separating genuine reasoning evidence from confounding answer-level artifacts.

Abstract: Hallucination detection methods for large language models increasingly operate on chain-of-thought reasoning traces, yet it remains unclear whether they evaluate the reasoning itself or merely exploit surface correlates of the final answer. We introduce a controlled-invariance methodology that exposes this distinction through two oracle tests: \textsc{Force}, which replaces each response's final answer with the ground truth while preserving the reasoning trace, and \textsc{Remove}, which strips answer-announcement steps while leaving the trajectory intact. This reveals if their predictive power derives from answer-level artifacts rather than from the structure or validity of intermediate reasoning. We further show that once these artifacts are controlled for, effective detection does not necessarily require complex learned representations: TRACT, a lightweight scorer built on lexical trajectory features (hedging trends, step-length dynamics, and cross-response vocabulary convergence), achieves strong robustness while remaining competitive with or outperforming existing baselines on unperturbed traces. These findings suggest that the current central challenge in reasoning-aware hallucination detection is not the absence of signal in the trace, but the failure to isolate it from endpoint cues.

</details>


### [3] [How Much Do Circuits Tell Us? Measuring the Consistency and Specificity of Language Model Circuits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08348)
*Michael Li,Nishant Subramani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how much neural network circuits are reused within and across tasks, finding that important components are heavily shared and not task-specific, which challenges hopes for highly targeted circuit-level interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability often assumes we can find sparse, task-specific circuits whose components are causally responsible for particular behaviors, enabling precise understanding and control. However, it is unclear whether discovered circuits are actually consistent within tasks and specific to them, or whether models instead reuse the same components across many tasks. This paper aims to clarify how circuits are reused and whether they can truly support targeted interpretability and intervention.

Method: The authors define and measure circuit reuse, splitting it into consistency (how often components recur across examples within a task) and specificity (how unique they are to a task). They use edge attribution patching to extract per-example circuits across six tasks and seven models, then quantify shared components and perform causal evaluations via ablation experiments. They compare the performance impact of ablating within-task shared components, task-specific components, and circuits discovered for other tasks.

Result: They find high within-task reuse: many components appear across per-example circuits for the same task and are causally necessary, as ablating them can cause up to ~100% relative accuracy drops. However, these circuits are not task-specific: ablating the circuit for one task harms another task nearly as much as ablating that other task’s own circuit. Analysis shows large overlaps between circuits across tasks, and these overlapping components are themselves causally important. Some circuits contain smaller subsets of task-specific components, but these explain only a modest fraction of total circuit performance.

Conclusion: Circuit discovery at the granularity of attention heads and MLP layers does identify causally important components, and these components are reused consistently within tasks. However, because the same components are heavily reused across tasks and are not strongly task-specific, circuit-based interpretations may be less suited to fine-grained, task-targeted explanations or interventions than hoped. This calls into question the extent to which current circuit-level methods can cleanly isolate distinct task mechanisms and suggests a need for either finer-grained analysis or revised assumptions about how models implement tasks.

Abstract: The circuits framework in mechanistic interpretability aims to identify causally important sparse subgraphs of model components, typically evaluated by measuring necessity and sufficiency. We measure circuit reuse, the proportion of components shared across per-example circuits within a task, and investigate two less-studied properties of this: consistency, the recurrence of components within a task, and specificity, their uniqueness to a task. Using edge attribution patching across six tasks and seven models, we find that within-task reuse is high and that shared components are necessary for task performance, with ablations causing up to $\sim$100% relative accuracy drops. However, circuits turn out not to be task-specific: ablating one task's circuit damages another task's performance about as much as that task's own circuit does. We discover that this is due to substantial overlap between circuits across tasks, which are causally important for performance. Some circuits do contain a smaller set of task-specific components, but these account for only a modest portion of circuit performance. Overall, our findings suggest that while circuit discovery at the level of attention heads and MLP layers identifies important components, their lack of task-specificity raises questions about the degree to which circuits can support targeted understanding and intervention on model behavior.

</details>


### [4] [Change My View? The Dynamics of Persuasion and Polarization in Online Discourse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08383)
*David Freeborn,Malihe Alikani,Anthony Sicilia*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study uses large language models to predict belief change in Reddit debates and finds that empathetic, concessive rhetoric promotes persuasion while aggressive refutation and attacks hinder it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Philosophical theories often assume rational argument with shared evidence should make peers converge in their views, but real-life discussions frequently do not. The authors want to understand which rhetorical strategies in actual online debates are associated with successful belief revision and to test whether these strategies improve predictions beyond a baseline conversational model.

Method: They compile a corpus of debates from Reddit’s r/ChangeMyView, where users explicitly indicate when their views have changed. For each discussion, a large language model is given the first half of the conversation and asked to predict, probabilistically, whether belief change will be acknowledged, forming a baseline forecast. Then, each reply is coded, using a hybrid of machine assistance and human oversight, for ten specific rhetorical strategies (e.g., concession, empathy, logical challenge, credibility appeals). These strategy features are added to predictive models to assess their impact on forecasting belief change.

Result: Including the coded rhetorical strategies significantly boosts prediction accuracy compared to the large language model baseline alone. A clear pattern emerges: rhetorical moves that signal concession or empathetic alignment correlate with a higher likelihood of belief revision, while strategies such as direct refutation, attacking credibility, or changing the topic correlate with a lower likelihood of belief change.

Conclusion: Effective persuasion in public online reasoning hinges not only on evidence and logical argument but also on relational and interpersonal framing. Empathy and concession facilitate belief change, whereas confrontational tactics and credibility attacks impede it. These findings suggest that normative models of rational dialogue should incorporate relational rhetoric, not just evidential and argumentative quality, when describing how rational persuasion works in practice.

Abstract: Philosophical accounts of persuasion often assume that shared evidence and rational argumentation should lead to a convergence of views between peers, yet everyday discourse often suggests otherwise. In this study, we use large language models to analyze a corpus of debates on Reddit's r/ChangeMyView, where belief revision is publicly signaled. Large language models were asked, halfway through each discussion, to forecast whether such an acknowledgement would arise; their probabilistic estimates serve as a conversational baseline. Each reply was then coded, through a hybrid machine-assisted procedure, for ten familiar rhetorical strategies -- concession, empathy, logical challenge, credibility appeals, and so forth. Adding these strategic features markedly improves predictive power and yields a consistent pattern: moves that express concession or empathetic alignment substantially increase the prospect of belief change, whereas frontal refutation, credibility attacks, and topic deflection diminish it. The findings indicate that effective public reasoning depends as much on relational framing as on evidential content, and they invite a refinement of normative accounts of rational dialogue.

</details>


### [5] [jina-embeddings-v5-omni: Text-Geometry-Preserving Multimodal Embeddings via Frozen-Tower Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08384)
*Florian Hönicke,Michael Günther,Andreas Koukounas,Kalim Akram,Scott Martens,Saba Sturua,Han Xiao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new way to build multimodal embedding models by freezing existing text and non-text encoders and only training small connector layers, creating Jina Embeddings v5 Omni that jointly embed text, images, audio, and video efficiently with competitive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full-parameter training of large multimodal embedding models is expensive and often alters the behavior of strong existing text embedding models. The authors want a method that (1) cheaply extends powerful text embedding models to additional modalities, (2) preserves their original text-only performance and behavior, and (3) still achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art results across modalities.

Method: They start from two existing Jina Embeddings v5 text models as the backbone. For each new modality (images, audio, and hence video via audio/frames), they add a dedicated encoder that transforms the raw non-text input into a representation suitable for a language model (VLM-style). Crucially, both the backbone text model and the added modality encoders are kept frozen. Only small connecting components that map the non-text encoder outputs into the language model’s input space are trained. These connectors account for just 0.35% of the joint model’s parameters, making training highly parameter-efficient while keeping the language model behavior on text unchanged.

Result: The resulting jina-embeddings-v5-omni suite can encode text, image, audio, and video into a unified semantic embedding space. Because only a tiny fraction of parameters is trained, training is much cheaper than full-parameter multimodal training, and the text-only embeddings remain bit-identical to the original Jina Embeddings v5 Text models. In benchmarks, the models achieve performance that is competitive with state-of-the-art multimodal embedding systems, coming close to the performance of larger, fully trained multimodal models.

Conclusion: Frozen-encoder model composition is an effective and efficient strategy for building multimodal embedding models. By freezing both the backbone text model and the non-text encoders and training only small connector modules, one can preserve the original text embedding quality, significantly reduce training cost, and still reach near state-of-the-art performance on multimodal tasks. This demonstrates that expensive full-model retraining is not strictly necessary to obtain strong unified multimodal embeddings.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce frozen-encoder model composition, a novel approach to multimodal embedding models. We build on the VLM-style architecture, in which non-text encoders are adapted to produce input for a language model, which in turn generates embeddings for all varieties of input. We present the result: the jina-embeddings-v5-omni suite, a pair of models that encode text, image, audio, and video input into a single semantic embedding space. Our method is to extend the two Jina Embeddings v5 Text models to support additional media by adding encoders for images and audio. The backbone text embedding models and the added non-text media encoders remain frozen. We only trained the connecting components, representing 0.35% of the total weights of the joint model. Training is therefore much more efficient than full-parameter retraining. Additionally, the language model remains effectively unaltered, producing exactly the same embeddings for text inputs as the Jina Embeddings v5 Text models. Our evaluations show that this approach produces results that are competitive with the state-of-the-art, yielding nearly equal performance to larger multimodal embedding models.

</details>


### [6] [AIPO: : Learning to Reason from Active Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08401)
*Junnan Liu,Linhao Luo,Thuy-Trang Vu,Gholamreza Haffari*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes AIPO, a multi-agent-assisted RL framework that improves LLM reasoning by allowing the policy model to actively query specialized helper agents during training, then distills the benefits into a single standalone policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has boosted LLM reasoning, but exploration is limited by the current policy’s capabilities. Existing methods that add external expert demonstrations typically provide full trajectories that are sample-inefficient, give coarse guidance, and restrict exploration to a fixed, static guidance space. There is a need for a more dynamic, fine-grained, and efficient way to expand an LLM’s reasoning capability boundary during RL training.

Method: The authors design AIPO, a reinforcement learning framework where the main policy LLM can actively consult three collaborative agents during training: a Verify Agent (for checking correctness and providing feedback), a Knowledge Agent (for supplying necessary facts or domain knowledge), and a Reasoning Agent (for proposing intermediate reasoning steps or strategies). These agents are engaged only when the policy hits reasoning bottlenecks, providing targeted hints rather than full demonstrations. The agent feedback is incorporated via an RL algorithm augmented with a tailored importance sampling coefficient and a clipping strategy to correct for off-policy bias and prevent gradient vanishing when learning from agent-guided trajectories. After RL training, the policy is deployed alone, without needing the helper agents at inference time.

Result: On several challenging reasoning benchmarks—AIME, MATH500, GPQA-Diamond, and LiveCodeBench—AIPO-trained policies outperform baselines using standard RLVR and other enhancement techniques. The improvements are consistent across different base policy models and different underlying RLVR algorithms, indicating robust gains in reasoning performance and an expanded effective capability boundary for the trained LLMs.

Conclusion: Active multi-agent interaction during RL exploration can effectively extend an LLM’s reasoning capabilities beyond what static policy-only or demonstration-based training achieves. By using specialized helper agents for verification, knowledge, and reasoning, and carefully correcting for off-policy learning issues, AIPO yields a single, stronger policy that reasons independently at test time and generalizes across models and RLVR setups.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning capabilities, largely stimulated by Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). However, existing RL algorithms face a fundamental limitation: their exploration remains largely constrained by the inherent capability boundary of the policy model. Although recent methods introduce external expert demonstrations to extend this boundary, they typically rely on complete trajectory-level guidance, which is sample-inefficient, information-sparse, and may confine exploration to a static guidance space. Inspired by the potential of multi-agent systems, we propose $\textbf{AIPO}$, an enhanced reinforcement learning framework that improves LLM reasoning through active multi-agent interaction during exploration. Specifically, AIPO enables the policy model to proactively consult three functional collaborative agents, $\textit{Verify Agent}$, $\textit{Knowledge Agent}$, and $\textit{Reasoning Agent}$, when encountering reasoning bottlenecks, thereby receiving fine-grained and targeted guidance to actively expand its capability boundary during training. We further introduce a tailored importance sampling coefficient together with a clipping strategy to mitigate the off-policy bias and gradient vanishing issues that arise when learning from agent-provided feedback. After training, the policy model performs reasoning independently without relying on collaborative agents. Extensive experiments on diverse reasoning benchmarks, including AIME, MATH500, GPQA-Diamond, and LiveCodeBench, show that AIPO consistently improves reasoning performance, generalizes robustly across different policy models and RLVR algorithms, and effectively expands the reasoning capability boundary of the policy model.

</details>


### [7] [Built Environment Reasoning from Remote Sensing Imagery Using Large Vision--Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08404)
*Dongdong Wang,Deepak Balakrishnan,Ravi Srinivasan,Shenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They use multimodal large language models with remote sensing images to reason about and make recommendations for the built environment in smart cities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smart cities need better tools to understand and manage the built environment—such as land use, construction feasibility, and risks—at scale. Remote sensing imagery contains rich spatial information, but traditional analysis methods are limited in semantic reasoning and natural-language recommendations. The paper aims to see whether modern large language models, extended to handle images, can bridge this gap and support more intelligent decision-making for urban environments.

Method: They feed remote sensing images at multiple spatial scales (likely from neighborhood to city level) into multimodal LLMs. These models are asked to perform built-environment tasks: giving design suggestions, assessing constructability, characterizing land-use patterns, and identifying risks. They systematically vary the image scale to study its effect on reasoning performance. They also benchmark different state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs, such as InternVL and Qwen, comparing their accuracy and reliability in generating recommendations about the built environment.

Result: The experiments show that multimodal LLMs can meaningfully interpret remote sensing imagery and provide useful built-environment-related reasoning and recommendations. Different spatial scales of imagery impact reasoning quality, and there are measurable performance differences among models like InternVL and Qwen in accuracy and reliability on these tasks. Overall, the integration improves the ability to analyze urban environments for smart-city applications.

Conclusion: Integrating remote sensing imagery with multimodal large language models is a promising approach for supporting smart city planning and decision-making. Multiscale image inputs help the models reason about land use, constructability, and risks, and state-of-the-art LLMs already show sufficient capability to assist human experts. This suggests a path towards more automated, intelligent tools for understanding and managing the built environment in cities.

Abstract: This work investigates the use of large language models (LLMs) for tasks in smart cities. The core idea is to leverage remote sensing imagery to characterize the built environment, including design suggestions, constructability assessment, landuse patterns, and risk identification. We examine remote sensing imagery at multiple spatial scales as inputs for multimodal language modeling and evaluate their effects on built-environment-related reasoning. In addition, we compare state-of-the-art LLMs, including InternVL and Qwen, in terms of accuracy and reliability when generating built environment recommendations. The results demonstrate the potential of integrating remote sensing imagery with large language models to assist smart cities and decision-making.

</details>


### [8] [Effective Explanations Support Planning Under Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08406)
*Hanqi Zhou,Britt Besch,Charley M. Wu,Tobias Gerstenberg*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They model explanations as instructions that a planner can execute, using language models to turn text into navigation policies, and show that better-scoring explanations lead to better human navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Giving clear route directions is hard because speakers must anticipate how listeners will use the information to act in the world under uncertainty. The authors want a formal, testable account of how natural-language procedural explanations translate into actions, and how to evaluate the quality of such explanations.

Method: They build a computational pipeline where a large language model converts an explanation into a program-like representation consisting of a policy prior and a value map. A planning agent then uses these to navigate under partial observability, incurring penalties for needing to replan. Explanation quality is operationalized via the efficiency and reliability of the resulting navigation. They run four preregistered experiments over 24 maps, collecting 1,200 natural-language explanations, human helpfulness ratings, baseline navigation data, and navigation performance when participants receive explanations of different computed quality.

Result: The model’s score of an explanation—based on the simulated paths’ efficiency and reliability—correlates with human judgments of helpfulness. Participants who receive explanations navigate more successfully than those who do not, and explanations that the model scores highly yield better human navigation performance than low-scored ones.

Conclusion: Procedural explanations can be understood as utility-guided communication: people produce instructions that are useful insofar as they can be grounded in effective action plans under uncertainty. The proposed model, which turns language into policy and value structures for planning, captures important aspects of how explanations guide behavior and provides a quantitative way to assess explanation quality.

Abstract: Explaining how to get from A to B can be challenging. It requires mentally simulating what the listener will do based on what they are told. To capture this process, we propose a computational model that converts utterances into action plans: a large language model translates an explanation into program-like guidance (a policy prior and value map), and a planning agent executes it under partial observability. We score explanations by the efficiency and reliability of the resulting paths, penalizing replanning. Across four preregistered experiments, we collect a corpus of 1,200 explanations over 24 maps, elicit helpfulness judgments, measure baseline navigation, and test behavior with explanations of differing quality. Higher-scored explanations are judged more helpful and improve navigation: participants with explanations outperform those without, and high-scoring explanations help more than low-scoring ones. Together, these results show procedural explanation as utility-guided communication shaped by how language can be grounded into action under uncertainty.

</details>


### [9] [A Semantic-Sampling Framework for Evaluating Calibration in Open-Ended Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08432)
*Zhanliang Wang,Jiancong Xiao,Ruochen Jin,Shu Yang,Bojian Hou,Li Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Sem-ECE, a new framework to evaluate how well large language models’ confidence matches their accuracy in open-ended question answering by using sampled answer frequencies over semantic classes instead of logits or self-reported confidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing calibration evaluation methods for large language models work poorly in realistic open-ended QA settings. Logit-based metrics require access to internal probabilities and constrained outputs, verbalized confidence is unreliable and overconfident, and current sampling-based approaches depend on ad-hoc extraction rules and lack a well-defined finite-sample target. There is a need for a principled, model-agnostic way to measure calibration for open-ended answers when internal logits are unavailable.

Method: They propose Sem-ECE (Semantic-Sampling Expected Calibration Error). The method repeatedly samples answers from the model for each question, clusters or groups those answers into semantic equivalence classes, and uses the empirical frequencies of these semantic classes as the model’s confidence estimates. Within this framework, they define two estimators: Sem1-ECE, which uses the same samples both to choose the predicted answer and to estimate its confidence (same-sample self-consistency), and Sem2-ECE, which separates answer selection and confidence estimation by using held-out samples for the confidence calculation. They analyze these estimators theoretically within the Sem-ECE framework.

Result: They prove that both Sem1-ECE and Sem2-ECE are asymptotically unbiased estimators of calibration error under their framework. The analysis shows that on easy questions the two estimators coincide, while on hard questions they diverge, with Sem2-ECE yielding a strictly smaller (better) calibration error; the difference between them acts as a proxy for question difficulty. Empirically, on three open-ended QA benchmarks and five commercial LLMs, Sem-ECE correlates with theory and surpasses verbalized confidence and prior sampling-based methods, and complements logit-based calibration metrics when logits are not accessible.

Conclusion: Sem-ECE provides a principled, sampling-based way to evaluate calibration of LLMs in open-ended QA without needing logits or relying on self-reported confidence. Its two estimators, Sem1-ECE and Sem2-ECE, are theoretically sound, diagnostically informative about question difficulty, and empirically outperform existing approaches, making Sem-ECE a practical tool for assessing and comparing LLM calibration in real-world deployment scenarios.

Abstract: Calibration measures whether a model's predicted confidence aligns with its empirical accuracy, and is central to the reliable deployment of large language models (LLMs) in high-stakes domains such as medicine and law. While much recent work focuses on improving LLM calibration, the equally important question of how to evaluate it in realistic settings remains underdeveloped. Open-ended question answering (QA), the most common deployment setting for modern LLMs, is where existing evaluation methods fall short: logit-based metrics need restricted output formats and internal probabilities; verbalized confidence is self-reported and often overconfident; and sampling-based methods rely on task-specific extraction rules without a clear finite-sample target. We introduce Sem-ECE (Semantic-Sampling Expected Calibration Error), a calibration evaluation framework for open-ended QA that samples answers from the model, groups them into semantic classes, and uses the resulting frequencies as confidence. We study two estimators within this framework: Sem$_1$-ECE, the same-sample self-consistency score, and Sem$_2$-ECE, a held-out variant that separates answer selection from confidence evaluation. We prove both are asymptotically unbiased, and further show that they agree on easy questions but diverge on hard ones with Sem$_2$ achieving strictly smaller calibration error, so their gap also serves as a diagnostic for question difficulty. Experiments on three open-ended QA benchmarks across five leading commercial LLMs match our theoretical predictions and show that Sem-ECE outperforms verbalized confidence and existing sampling-based methods, while complementing logit-based evaluation when internal probabilities are unavailable.

</details>


### [10] [Magis-Bench: Evaluating LLMs on Magistrate-Level Legal Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08437)
*Ramon Pires,Thales Sales Almeida,Celio Larcher Junior,Giovana Bonás,Hugo Abonizio,Marcos Piau,Roseval Malaquias Junior,Thiago Laitz,Rodrigo Nogueira*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Magis-Bench, a new benchmark that tests how well legal AI systems can act as magistrates—judging and writing decisions—rather than just generating legal arguments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing legal AI benchmarks evaluate a model’s ability to argue or draft documents, but do not directly assess its ability to judge: weigh competing arguments, apply legal doctrine to facts, and write reasoned decisions. Since judging is a core function of legal systems, there is a need for a benchmark that captures magistrate-level reasoning and decision-writing, especially in real-world, high-stakes selection contexts like judicial exams.

Method: The authors construct Magis-Bench from 74 questions taken from eight Brazilian competitive exams for judicial positions (2023–2025). The tasks include discursive, multi-turn legal analysis questions and full practical exercises requiring complete civil and criminal judicial sentences. They then evaluate 23 state-of-the-art LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge approach, where four independent frontier models serve as evaluators of each model’s outputs. They measure inter-judge agreement using Kendall’s W and pairwise Kendall’s τ, and compute average scores out of 10 for each evaluated model.

Result: The LLM-as-a-judge evaluators show very high agreement (Kendall’s W = 0.984; pairwise Kendall’s τ ≥ 0.897), suggesting reliable comparative scoring. Among the 23 models, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview performs best with an average score of 6.97/10, followed by Gemini-3-Flash-Preview (6.67) and Claude-4.5-Opus (6.46). No model reaches 70% of the maximum possible score.

Conclusion: Magis-Bench provides a rigorous, magistrate-level benchmark for legal AI that goes beyond advocacy to evaluate judging and judicial writing. Current state-of-the-art LLMs still fall well short of expert-level performance on these tasks, indicating that high-quality judicial reasoning and decision drafting remain unsolved challenges. The released benchmark, outputs, and code are intended to facilitate further research on legal AI capabilities and improvements.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks for legal AI focus primarily on tasks where LLMs must produce legal arguments or documents, yet the capacity to \emph{judge} such arguments -- weighing competing claims, applying doctrine to facts, and rendering reasoned decisions -- is arguably as fundamental to a well-functioning legal system as advocacy itself. We introduce Magis-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on magistrate-level writing tasks derived from recent Brazilian competitive examinations for judicial positions. Magis-Bench comprises 74 questions from eight examinations conducted between 2023 and 2025, including discursive legal analysis questions with multi-turn structure and practical exercises requiring the composition of complete civil and criminal judicial sentences. We evaluate 23 state-of-the-art LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge methodology with four independent frontier models as evaluators. Our results show strong inter-judge agreement (Kendall's $W = 0.984$; pairwise Kendall's $τ\ge 0.897$), with Google's Gemini-3-Pro-Preview achieving the highest average score (6.97/10), followed by Gemini-3-Flash-Preview (6.67) and Claude-4.5-Opus (6.46). Even the best-performing models score below 70\% of the maximum, indicating that judicial-level legal reasoning and writing remain challenging for current LLMs. We release the complete benchmark, model outputs, and evaluation code to support further research on legal AI capabilities.

</details>


### [11] [Can Language Models Identify Side Effects of Breast Cancer Radiation Treatments?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08439)
*Natalie Seah,Danielle S. Bitterman,Daphna Spiegel,Thomas Hartvigsen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how reliably large language models can list radiation therapy side effects for breast cancer survivors, using a stress-testing framework and clinician-curated references, and identifies key limitations and design improvements for safer use in survivorship care.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cancer survivors need clear, accurate communication about treatment side effects, especially for informed consent and survivorship planning. Clinicians face difficulties due to fragmented adverse effect information in EHRs and incomplete clinical knowledge about toxicities. With growing interest in using large language models to support clinical communication, there is a critical need to understand how well these models perform specifically in oncology survivorship contexts, and whether they can be trusted to enumerate relevant radiation therapy side effects.

Method: The authors design a deployment-oriented stress-testing framework for large language models in a breast cancer survivorship setting. Using 21 breast cancer patient profiles, they create paired clinical scenarios that differ only in the radiotherapy regimen. Seven instruction-tuned LLMs are evaluated under multiple prompting strategies. For each scenario, model-generated radiation side effect lists are compared against a clinician-curated reference derived from informed consent documents from two major academic centers and validated by more than seven breast radiation oncologists. The reference dataset links detailed radiation parameters (dose-fractionation, fields, locations) to side effects annotated by frequency and temporal onset. The analysis examines sensitivity to documentation changes, precision–recall trade-offs, and error patterns such as under-recall of rare or late toxicities, and tests the effect of constraining output length and grounding in curated side effect lists.

Result: The study finds that all tested LLMs are sensitive to minor changes in clinical documentation, leading to variability in generated side effect lists. Models exhibit clear trade-offs between precision and recall: improving one often degrades the other. A consistent pattern of under-recall is observed for rare and long-term radiation-related toxicities, meaning that important survivorship risks are frequently omitted. Imposing hard limits on the number of side effects that models can output tends to reduce precision rather than focus the list effectively. In contrast, grounding model outputs in clinician-curated side effect lists markedly improves their reliability and robustness across scenarios and prompting regimes.

Conclusion: LLMs, when used naively to generate radiation therapy side effect information for breast cancer survivors, are unreliable, particularly for capturing rare and long-term toxicities that matter in survivorship care. Their outputs are unstable to minor documentation changes and subject to unfavorable precision–recall trade-offs. However, performance improves substantially when models are constrained and grounded with high-quality clinician-curated references. The paper concludes that current LLMs should not be used as standalone tools for informed consent or survivorship counseling in oncology, and instead should be embedded within carefully designed, clinically grounded systems that mitigate their limitations and prioritize patient safety and completeness of side effect information.

Abstract: Accurately communicating the side effects of cancer treatments to cancer survivors is critical, particularly in settings such as informed consent, where clinicians must clearly and comprehensively convey potential treatment toxicities. However, this task remains challenging due to clinical knowledge deficits about adverse treatment effects and fragmentation across electronic health record (EHR) systems. Large language models (LLMs) have the potential to assist in this task, though their reliability in oncology survivorship contexts remains poorly understood. We present a deployment-oriented stress-testing framework for evaluating LLM-generated radiation side effect lists in breast cancer treatment and survivorship care. Using 21 breast cancer patient profiles, we construct paired patient clinical scenarios that differ only in radiotherapy regimens to evaluate seven instruction-tuned LLMs under multiple prompting regimes. We then compare LLM outputs to a clinician-curated reference derived from informed consent documents at two major academic medical centers and developed by a team including more than seven breast radiation oncologists. The reference maps radiation dose-fractionation, fields, and locations to associated toxicities, broken down by frequency and temporal onset. Across models, we reveal sensitivity to minor documentation changes, trade-offs between precision and recall, and systematic under-recall of rare and long-term side effects. When used alone, constraints on the number of side effects generated reduce precision, and grounding outputs in clinician-curated side effect lists substantially improves reliability and robustness. These findings highlight important limitations of LLM use in oncology and suggest practical design choices for safer and more informative survivorship-focused applications.

</details>


### [12] [Revisiting the syntax of imperatives in Yemeni Arabic: An Agree across phases approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08447)
*Mohammed Q. Shormani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an Agree-across-phases (AAP) account of Yemeni Arabic imperatives, linking their syntactic structure tightly to discourse notions like topic and aboutness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better explain the syntax of imperatives in Yemeni Arabic, especially both simple and complex (A'-movement) cases, and to capture how their form is shaped by discourse and interpretive/performative functions, something not fully handled by prior accounts.

Method: The author adopts a formal generative-syntax approach, using the Agree-across-phases (AAP) mechanism. They reanalyze subjects and preverbal elements in imperatives within a cartographic C-domain (TopP, etc.), and derive patterns of local and non-local A'-chains through matching and coreference between a 2nd-person null pronoun (pro) and discourse topics.

Result: The analysis shows that (i) the thematic subject in Yemeni Arabic imperatives is a 2nd-person pro, (ii) preverbal DPs/pronouns are not subjects but aboutness topics in the C-domain, and (iii) the relationship between topic and pro can be modeled as Agree-across-phases, deriving both simple and complex imperative structures, including ones with A'-movement.

Conclusion: Yemeni Arabic imperatives are best captured by an AAP-based syntax–discourse interface: their logical subject is a C-domain aboutness topic coreferential with a 2nd-person pro subject, and even apparently subjectless “core” imperatives involve a null topic in Spec,TopP whose reference is resolved by discourse.

Abstract: This article revisits the syntax of imperatives in Yemeni Arabic proposing an Agree acros phases (AAP) approach. I argue that the AAP approach successfully accounts for both simple and complex imperative constructions, including A'-chain structures, by establishing a close interactions between syntax and discourse. The study demonstrates that this interface is motivated by the interpretive and performative functions associated with imperatives, linking informational structure with propositional structure. It is also proposed that the thematic subject of imperatives is a 2-person pro, whereas any overt pronominal or nominal element occurring preverbally is not a subject, but rather a C-domain element, precisely aboutness topic. These topics serve as the logical subjects of imperatives and enter into a coreferentiality relationship with pro. This relation is analyzed as APP involving Match, yielding both local and non-local A'-chains. For core imperatives, viz., lacking an overt topic, I propose a null topic to (re)merge in Spec,TopP, whose interpretation depends on the discourse.

</details>


### [13] [Do Benchmarks Underestimate LLM Performance? Evaluating Hallucination Detection With LLM-First Human-Adjudicated Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08462)
*I. F. Atasoy,B. Mutlu,E. A. Sezer,A. Wahdan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies hallucination detection in LLM-based summarization and shows that using LLMs with explicit reasoning to help re-evaluate benchmarks makes labels more consistent and improves measured model accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination is a key failure mode for LLMs in context-grounded applications like RAG and agents, yet existing hallucination benchmarks may contain noisy or ambiguous human labels. The authors want to understand how well LLMs can judge contextual hallucinations and whether model-assisted re-annotation can yield more reliable evaluation datasets.

Method: They examine two summarization hallucination datasets, QAGS-C and SummEval. For each sample, they compare the original human benchmark labels with span- and reason-based predictions from two LLMs: Gemini 2.5 Flash and GPT-5 Mini. Whenever there is disagreement between human labels and model judgments, they send those conflicted cases to a human adjudication procedure with two cross-cultural adjudicators, who review the context, summary, and LLM rationales to produce revised ground-truth labels. They then recompute agreement statistics and model accuracies using these adjudicated labels.

Result: After adjudication, triple agreement between human, GPT, and Gemini rose by 6.38 percentage points on QAGS-C and 7.62 on SummEval. Both models’ measured accuracies improved: GPT’s by 4.25 points on QAGS-C and 2.34 on SummEval; Gemini’s by 8.51 and 3.80, respectively. In a substantial fraction of conflicts, adjudicators agreed with the models over the original benchmark labels, especially when the models provided explicit reasoning. Overall inter-adjudicator agreement was high, between 83% and 87%.

Conclusion: Single-pass human annotations are often unreliable for ambiguity-prone hallucination detection tasks. Incorporating LLM judgments with explicit reasoning into a human-in-the-loop adjudication pipeline leads to cleaner benchmarks, higher agreement among humans and models, and more trustworthy evaluations of hallucination detection performance.

Abstract: Hallucination remains a persistent challenge in Large Language Models (LLMs), particularly in context-grounded settings such as RAG and agentic AI systems. This study focuses on contextual hallucination detection in summarization tasks. We analyze the QAGS-C and SummEval datasets by comparing original benchmark annotations with reason and span-based predictions from Gemini 2.5 Flash and GPT-5 Mini. To address systematic divergences between human labels and LLM judgments, we re-evaluated all conflicted samples through a human adjudication process involving 2 cross-cultural adjudicators. Following this re-evaluation, triple agreement (between human, GPT, and Gemini) increased by 6.38% for QAGS-C and 7.62% for SummEval. Similarly, model accuracy improved, with GPT increasing by 4.25% on QAGS-C and 2.34% on SummEval, while Gemini showed gains of 8.51% and 3.80%, respectively. Notably, adjudicators frequently sided with the models' judgments over original human annotations when LLMs provided explicit reasoning. Overall human adjudicator agreement ranged between 83% and 87%. These findings suggest that for ambiguity-prone tasks, single-pass annotations may be insufficient, and model-assisted re-evaluation yields more reliable benchmarks.

</details>


### [14] [PYTHALAB-MERA: Validation-Grounded Memory, Retrieval, and Acceptance Control for Frozen-LLM Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08468)
*Mehmet Iscan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents PYTHALAB-MERA, an external controller that augments a frozen local LLM with validation-based memory, retrieval, and reinforcement-style credit assignment to improve code generation in strict RL coding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing local LLM coding agents often succeed by producing a single fluent answer, but many realistic coding tasks require iterative interaction with execution feedback, persistent state, and bounded repair budgets. Current techniques—static retrieval, long-context prompting, self-refinement, execution-based repair, and RL over model weights—only partially address this setting and typically lack integrated support for validation-grounded episodic memory, adaptive retrieval, delayed credit assignment, and structured skill reuse using a frozen model. The authors aim to design a lightweight controller that can sit outside a local LLM and leverage validation signals to organize memory and learning without retraining the model.

Method: The authors introduce PYTHALAB-MERA, an external controller that orchestrates validation-conditioned code generation around a frozen language model. The LLM generates complete source files. The controller (1) selects which memory records and AST-derived skills to include in the next prompt, (2) runs each candidate solution through a fail-fast validation pipeline, (3) converts validation outcomes into bounded, shaped rewards, and (4) propagates delayed credit using TD(lambda)-style eligibility traces across the controller’s internal state and decisions. This yields an episodic, reinforcement-learning-like loop that updates the controller’s retrieval and action-selection policies while keeping the LLM weights fixed.

Result: In a local command-line implementation tested on reinforcement-learning-style coding tasks with strict validation gates, the system is evaluated on three tasks, each with three repetitions and a maximum of three attempts per repetition. Under this hard RL setting (a total of nine strict validation opportunities), PYTHALAB-MERA succeeds on 8 out of 9 validations. In contrast, both a self-refinement baseline and a GRACE-based extension each achieve 0 out of 9 passes under the same conditions.

Conclusion: The experiments support a narrow claim: in the specific recorded RL coding setting, the external memory-and-retrieval controller significantly improves validation success over baselines while using a frozen LLM. The work does not claim general-purpose code synthesis, state-of-the-art performance, or formal guarantees about program correctness or safety; rather, it demonstrates that validation-conditioned control with episodic memory and TD-style credit assignment can be an effective way to enhance local coding agents in constrained environments.

Abstract: Local LLM-based coding agents increasingly work in settings where correctness is earned through execution feedback, persistent state, and bounded repair, not through a single fluent answer. Static retrieval, long-context prompting, self-refinement, execution-feedback repair, and reinforcement learning over model weights each address part of this setting, but they do not jointly provide validation-grounded episodic memory, adaptive retrieval-action selection, delayed credit assignment, and structural skill reuse around a frozen local model. We introduce PYTHALAB-MERA, a lightweight external controller for local validation-conditioned code generation. The frozen language model proposes complete source files; the controller decides which memory records and AST-derived skills should enter the next prompt, validates each candidate through a fail-fast pipeline, converts validation outcomes into bounded shaped rewards, and propagates delayed credit through TD(lambda)-style eligibility traces. We evaluate the implementation as a local CLI artifact on reinforcement-learning coding tasks with strict validation gates. In the measured hard RL setting with three tasks, three repetitions, and a three-attempt budget, PYTHALAB-MERA passed 8/9 strict validations; the self-refinement baseline and the investigated GRACE extension each passed 0/9. These results support a deliberately bounded claim: in this recorded setting, the external memory-and-retrieval controller improved validation success. They do not establish general-purpose code synthesis, state-of-the-art performance, formal program correctness, or formal safety.

</details>


### [15] [A Computational Operationalisation of Competing Maturational Theories of Syntactic Development via Statistical Grammar Induction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08476)
*Mila Marcheva,Suchir Salhan,Weiwei Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares two maturational theories of how syntactic categories emerge in child language, by computationally simulating staged category acquisition with statistical grammar induction, and finds strong support for a bottom-up (GROWING) order over an inward, discourse-first (INWARD) order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is ongoing debate in language acquisition about which syntactic categories children acquire first and in what sequence, especially how much is driven by bottom-up lexical/inflectional structure versus early access to higher, discourse-related syntax. Existing maturational theories make different qualitative predictions, but they are often not made precise enough to compare directly or to test against the same input and learning conditions. The authors aim to formalize and directly compare these competing accounts in a controlled, computational setting.

Method: The authors create a computational framework that explicitly encodes hypothesized maturational orderings of syntactic category availability (GROWING vs INWARD). They then use statistical grammar induction as the learning algorithm, feeding it the same input but constraining which syntactic categories can be used at each stage according to each theory. This allows them to measure what structure is learnable under each proposed ordering. They evaluate the induced grammars quantitatively with three different metrics (e.g., fit to data, accuracy of learned categories, or similar).

Result: Under identical input and learning conditions, the maturational ordering corresponding to the bottom-up GROWING theory yields significantly better learning outcomes than the inward, discourse-first INWARD theory, across all three evaluation metrics. That is, grammars induced under GROWING constraints capture more of the target structure or perform better on the tested measures.

Conclusion: The computational operationalization of maturational theories shows that a bottom-up acquisition path—where lexical and inflectional categories emerge before discourse-related ones—leads to more successful syntactic learning than a discourse-first path. This supports GROWING-type maturational accounts over INWARD-type ones and demonstrates the value of making theoretical claims explicit in computational terms to test their learnability implications.

Abstract: This paper is concerned with what intermediate syntactic categories children acquire during first language development, and in what order. Maturational theories make different predictions. Bottom-up accounts (GROWING) propose that lexical and inflectional structure emerges first, while inward accounts (INWARD) predict early access to discourse-related categories. We computationally operationalise these hypotheses of staged syntactic emergence using statistical grammar induction, asking what each proposed ordering makes learnable when input and learning algorithm are held constant. Our framework makes category acquisition explicit and allows us to explore how different maturational orderings shape the structure that can be learned under identical conditions. Based on this operationalisation, the GROWING account significantly outperforms the INWARD account across three evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [16] [Do Agents Need to Plan Step-by-Step? Rethinking Planning Horizon in Data-Centric Tool Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08477)
*Naoki Otani,Nikita Bhutani,Hannah Kim,Dan Zhang,Estevam Hruschka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares full-horizon (plan-then-act) vs single-step (think-act-think) planning for LLM agents on data-centric tasks, finding that full-horizon planning with occasional replanning matches accuracy while using far fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents solving data-centric tasks need reliable planning over tool calls to external data sources. Most current systems favor step-by-step execution under the belief that continuous monitoring is required for adaptability, but this may be unnecessarily expensive. The authors want to isolate how much the planning horizon itself matters for accuracy, robustness, and efficiency, and whether cheaper full-horizon planning can be a better default for well-defined tasks.

Method: They define two paradigms: full-horizon (FH), where the agent generates an entire tool-use plan before execution and only replans when needed, and single-step horizon (SH), where the agent plans and executes one tool call at a time, updating its reasoning after each observation. In controlled experiments, they hold other factors fixed and vary graph/topological complexity (depth and breadth of tool chains) and tool robustness. They evaluate both paradigms on Knowledge Base QA and multi-hop QA tasks, comparing accuracy and token usage under different conditions.

Result: Across KBQA and multi-hop QA benchmarks, FH planning with lazy (on-demand) replanning attains similar accuracy to SH planning across a range of depths, breadths, and levels of tool robustness, while consistently using 2–3 times fewer tokens. This indicates that the expected adaptability advantage of SH does not substantially materialize in these settings, whereas its token cost is significant.

Conclusion: For well-defined data-centric tasks, continuously interleaving reasoning and tool execution (single-step horizon) is often unnecessary. A full-horizon, plan-then-execute strategy augmented with lazy replanning can achieve comparable performance with much greater token efficiency, and should be considered a more efficient default planning architecture for LLM-based tool-using agents in such domains.

Abstract: Explicit planning is a critical capability for LLM-based agents solving complex data-centric tasks, which require precise tool calling over external data sources. Existing strategies fall into two paradigms based on planning horizon: (1) full-horizon (FH), which generates a complete plan before execution, and (2) single-step horizon (SH), which interleaves each action (tool call) with incremental reasoning and observation. While step-by-step execution is a common default under the assumption that eager execution monitoring is necessary for adaptability, we revisit this assumption for well-defined data-centric tasks. Our controlled empirical study isolates planning horizon as the key architectural feature and systematically analyzes the effects of topological complexity and tool robustness on both paradigms. Our experiments across Knowledge Base Question Answering and Multi-hop QA show that FH planning with lazy replanning achieves accuracy parity with SH across varying depths, breadths, and robustness levels, while using 2-3x fewer tokens. These findings suggest that for well-defined data-centric tasks, eager step-wise monitoring is often unnecessary, and full-horizon planning with on-demand replanning can offer a more efficient default.

</details>


### [17] [NARRA-Gym for Evaluating Interactive Narrative Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08503)
*Yue Huang,Yuchen Ma,Jiayi Ye,Wenjie Wang,Zipeng Ling,Xingjian Hu,Yuexing Hao,Zichen Chen,Zhangchen Xu,Yunhong He,Zhengqing Yuan,Yujun Zhou,Kehan Guo,Chaoran Chen,Toby Jia-Jun Li,Stefan Feuerriegel,Xiangliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces NARRA-Gym, an executable benchmark environment for evaluating how well large language models handle multi-turn interactive storytelling with user adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing story benchmarks mainly use static prompts, single-shot generations, or post-hoc ratings, which fail to capture whether models can maintain long-term coherence, track story state, pace narratives, simulate characters, personalize empathetically to users, and generate story-grounded artifacts across multiple turns. The authors want a benchmark that reflects this richer, interactive narrative setting.

Method: They build NARRA-Gym, which takes a sparse emotional seed and turns it into a full interactive story episode. The environment logs the entire model-in-the-loop process, including story construction, memory updates, planning, pacing actions, and optional artifact creation. Using this gym, they evaluate nine state-of-the-art LLMs via an LLM-as-judge protocol over eight benchmark personas and complement this with a human study where participants rate customized model outputs.

Result: The evaluation reveals large differences across models, personas, and metrics. Some models that generate fluent, readable stories nevertheless perform poorly on robustness, user experience, or personalization that is sensitive to user preferences and constraints. This indicates that strong text generation alone does not guarantee good interactive narrative performance.

Conclusion: Interactive narrative, as captured by NARRA-Gym, serves as a valuable benchmark for assessing long-horizon, user-adaptive behavior in LLMs. It highlights limitations that are invisible when judging only isolated story quality and motivates more holistic evaluations of model performance in dynamic, user-centered tasks.

Abstract: Interactive narrative tasks require LLMs to sustain a coherent, evolving story while adapting to a user over multiple turns. However, suitable benchmarks for this setting are limited: existing evaluations often focus on static prompts, isolated story generations, or post-hoc ratings, and therefore miss whether models can jointly manage story generation, long-context state and pacing, character simulation, empathic personalization, and story-grounded artifacts. We introduce NARRA-Gym, an executable evaluation environment that turns a sparse emotional seed into a complete interactive story episode and logs the full model-in-the-loop trajectory, including story construction, memory updates, planning, pacing interventions, and optional artifact synthesis. We evaluate nine frontier LLMs using a controlled LLM-as-judge sweep over eight benchmark personas and a human evaluation in which participants rate customized model outputs. Our results show substantial variation across models, personas, and evaluation dimensions: models that produce fluent stories can still fail on robustness, user experience, or resistance-sensitive personalization. These findings suggest that interactive narrative offers a useful benchmark for evaluating long-horizon, user-adaptive LLM behavior beyond isolated story quality.

</details>


### [18] [A Single Layer to Explain Them All:Understanding Massive Activations in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08504)
*Zeru Shi,Zhenting Wang,Fan Yang,Qifan Wang,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies a specific layer in LLMs where massive activations first appear, shows how RMSNorm and FFN jointly cause them and make token representations rigid, and proposes a simple method that softens these activations, improving performance and mitigating attention sinks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Massive activations in LLMs can harm representation diversity and create attention sinks, but their layer-wise origin and concrete mechanisms are not well understood. Better understanding and controlling them could improve LLM performance and make mitigation of attention pathologies more principled.

Method: The authors empirically analyze multiple LLM families to locate the first layer where massive activations occur, naming it the Massive Emergence Layer (ME Layer). They dissect the contributions of RMSNorm and FFN parameters to these activations, track how the resulting token representations propagate and remain invariant across layers via residual connections, and then introduce a simple modification that reduces the rigidity of the massive activation token. They evaluate this method in both training-free and fine-tuning settings on instruction-following and math reasoning benchmarks, and study its effect on attention sinks.

Result: They find a consistent ME Layer across models where massive activations first arise, with both RMSNorm and FFN responsible. The resulting token representation becomes almost invariant as it propagates, limiting hidden-state diversity and affecting attention behavior. Their proposed mitigation method makes these representations less rigid, systematically improving LLM performance on multiple tasks and reducing the influence of attention sinks.

Conclusion: Massive activations in LLMs originate in a specific layer and are shaped jointly by RMSNorm and FFN, leading to rigid token states that impair downstream processing and foster attention sinks. A simple intervention at the hidden-state level can both improve task performance and weaken attention sinks, offering a principled direction for understanding and mitigating activation pathologies in LLMs.

Abstract: We investigate the origins of massive activations in large language models (LLMs) and identify a specific layer named the \textbf{Massive Emergence Layer (ME Layer)}, that is consistently observed across model families, where massive activations first emerge and subsequently propagate to deeper layers through residual connections. We show that, within the ME Layer both the RMSNorm and the FFN parameters jointly contribute to the emergence of massive activations. Once formed, the massive activation token representation remains largely invariant across layers, reducing the diversity of hidden representations passed to the attention module. Motivated by this limitation, we propose a simple and effective method to reduce the rigidity of the massive activation token. Our approach consistently improves LLM performance across multiple tasks, including instruction following and math reasoning, in both training free and fine tuning settings. Moreover, we show that our method mitigates attention sinks by selectively weakening their influence, elucidating their origin at the hidden state level and shedding new light on principled mitigation strategies.

</details>


### [19] [A Single Neuron Is Sufficient to Bypass Safety Alignment in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08513)
*Hamid Kazemi,Atoosa Chegini,Maria Safi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper finds that LLM safety alignment relies on a small set of specific neurons: refusal neurons that decide whether to answer harmful requests, and concept neurons that store harmful knowledge. By directly editing single neurons, the authors can both bypass safety and elicit harmful content without retraining or prompt tricks, across many model sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how safety alignment is mechanistically implemented inside LLMs and whether it is robustly distributed or vulnerable to small, targeted changes. Identify concrete internal components responsible for refusal behavior and harmful-knowledge representation to better assess and improve safety robustness.

Method: Use mechanistic interpretability techniques to locate two types of neurons in multiple LLMs: (1) refusal neurons correlated with safety refusals and (2) concept neurons encoding harmful knowledge. Perform causal interventions by suppressing or amplifying single neurons at inference time, then test effects on model responses to harmful and benign prompts across seven models (two families, 1.7B–70B parameters). Evaluate whether modifying these neurons systematically bypasses or induces harmful behavior.

Result: They identify individual neurons whose activity is causally sufficient to control refusal behavior: suppressing a single refusal neuron reliably removes safety refusals on harmful prompts, while amplifying a concept neuron can inject harmful content into otherwise benign generations. These effects generalize across diverse harmful requests and hold for multiple model sizes and architectures, without fine-tuning or prompt engineering.

Conclusion: Safety alignment in current LLMs is localized rather than fully distributed: individual neurons play a decisive gating role over harmful content. Because suppressing one refusal neuron can broadly disable safety, alignment is brittle and vulnerable to simple, targeted weight edits. Future safety methods should avoid over-reliance on such single-point-of-failure mechanisms and instead aim for more robust, distributed alignment strategies.

Abstract: Safety alignment in language models operates through two mechanistically distinct systems: refusal neurons that gate whether harmful knowledge is expressed, and concept neurons that encode the harmful knowledge itself. By targeting a single neuron in each system, we demonstrate both directions of failure -- bypassing safety on explicit harmful requests via suppression, and inducing harmful content from innocent prompts via amplification -- across seven models spanning two families and 1.7B to 70B parameters, without any training or prompt engineering. Our findings suggest that safety alignment is not robustly distributed across model weights but is mediated by individual neurons that are each causally sufficient to gate refusal behavior -- suppressing any one of the identified refusal neurons bypasses safety alignment across diverse harmful requests.

</details>


### [20] [Coordinates of Capability: A Unified MTMM-Geometric Framework for LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08522)
*Adib Sakhawat,Tahsin Islam,Takia Farhin,Syed Rifat Raiyan,Hasan Mahmud,Md Kamrul Hasan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified, geometry-based framework to evaluate large language models, treating various robustness and alignment metrics as measurements in a shared latent space to better separate true capabilities from artifacts like prompt sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluation suffers from poor construct validity: many benchmarks and metrics are fragmented, ad hoc, and conflate prompt- or method-specific variance with the underlying capabilities of the model. At the same time, recent work hints that LLM behavior can be understood geometrically, as lying on continuous manifolds. The authors aim to reconcile these strands to provide a more principled and stable way to evaluate LLMs.

Method: They introduce a generalized Multi-Trait Multi-Method (MTMM) framework adapted to LLMs. Within this framework, they formalize nine existing evaluation metrics (such as Paraphrase Instability, Drift Score, Overton Width, Pluralism Score) and reinterpret them as geometric quantities in a shared latent coordinate space. This re-interpretation allows them to factorize observed behavior into orthogonal latent dimensions and to distinguish task-irrelevant perturbations from genuine capability variation.

Result: The unified spatial interpretation shows that diverse metrics can be mapped into three orthogonal latent dimensions capturing instability/sensitivity, position/alignment, and coverage/expressiveness. Under this factorization, metrics that previously seemed unrelated are revealed as different projections of the same underlying geometric structure, clarifying what each metric is actually measuring and how they interact.

Conclusion: By casting LLM evaluation metrics as geometric measurements in a shared latent space and organizing them with an MTMM framework, the paper offers a domain-agnostic taxonomy of model behavior. This enables more robust, construct-valid, and empirically stable benchmark design, helping evaluators disentangle superficial method variance (like prompt changes) from true model capabilities.

Abstract: The evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) faces a critical challenge in construct validity, where fragmented benchmarks and ad hoc metrics frequently conflate method variance, such as prompt sensitivity, with true latent capabilities. Concurrently, emerging research suggests that LLM capabilities and outputs can be modeled as continuous geometric manifolds. In this Systematization of Knowledge (SoK), we bridge these paradigms by proposing a generalized Multi-Trait Multi-Method (MTMM) framework for LLM evaluation. We formalize and unify nine evaluation metrics, including Paraphrase Instability, Drift Score, Overton Width, and Pluralism Score, interpreting them not as isolated scalar values but as geometric measurements within a shared latent coordinate space. This spatial unification factorizes model behavior into three orthogonal latent dimensions: (1) Instability and Sensitivity, (2) Position and Alignment, and (3) Coverage and Expressiveness. By systematically separating task-irrelevant perturbations from true capability spans, the framework provides a theoretically grounded and domain-agnostic taxonomy for robust and empirically stable benchmark design.

</details>


### [21] [Source or It Didn't Happen: A Multi-Agent Framework for Citation Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08583)
*Mingzhe Li,Zhiqiang Lin,Shiqing Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CiteTracer, a system for fine-grained detection of hallucinated citations produced by large language models, using a taxonomy-aligned, multi-agent verification pipeline and achieving high accuracy on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are widely used in scientific writing but often generate fabricated citations that look plausible yet cannot be verified. Current detectors mostly provide coarse binary judgments (found/not-found), depend on brittle parsing, and use incomplete retrieval methods, offering limited, fine-grained feedback about which parts of a citation are incorrect. There is a need for a more nuanced, structured, and reliable approach to auditing LLM-generated references.

Method: The authors propose reframing citation hallucination detection as field-level adjudication using a 12-code taxonomy that categorizes citations into Real, Potential, and Hallucinated types. They develop CiteTracer, a cascading multi-agent system that (1) extracts structured citation data from PDFs and BibTeX, (2) retrieves evidence from multiple sources including caches, URLs, scholarly databases, and web search, (3) applies deterministic field-matching rules, and (4) routes ambiguous or complex cases to specialized judging agents trained for specific classes in the taxonomy. They also construct two datasets: a synthetic benchmark of 2,450 citations derived from real seeds with controlled LLM perturbations, and a real-world set of 957 fabricated citations from ICLR 2026 and an anonymous conference's desk rejections.

Result: On the synthetic benchmark, CiteTracer achieves 97.1% overall accuracy, with F1 scores of 97.0 for Real citations, 95.8 for Potential citations, and 98.5 for Hallucinated citations.
