On the real-world dataset of fabricated citations, CiteTracer successfully detects 97.1% of the hallucinations without abstentions, demonstrating strong robustness and practical utility in realistic conference submission settings.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that citation hallucination detection benefits significantly from a taxonomy-aligned, field-level, multi-agent approach instead of simplistic binary verification. CiteTracer proves effective at both recognizing valid and potential citations and catching hallucinations with high precision and recall. The released taxonomy, system, codebase, and benchmarks aim to support more reliable auditing of LLM-assisted scientific writing and to provide a foundation for future research on fine-grained bibliographic verification.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used in scientific writing, yet they can fabricate citation-shaped references that appear plausible but fail bibliographic verification. Existing detectors often reduce verification to binary found/not-found decisions and rely on brittle parsing or incomplete retrieval, offering little field-level signal to auditors. We reframe citation hallucination detection as taxonomy-aligned field-level adjudication and introduce a 12-code taxonomy spanning Real, Potential, and Hallucinated citations. Based on this taxonomy, we build CiteTracer, a cascading multi-agent detector that extracts structured citations from PDF and BibTeX, retrieves evidence through cache lookup, URL fetch, scholar connectors, and web search, applies deterministic field matching, and routes ambiguous cases to class-specialist judgers. We release a benchmark of 2,450 synthetic citations built from real seeds with controlled LLM mutations, paired with 957 real-world fabricated citations drawn from ICLR 2026 and an anonymous conference desk-rejected submissions. CiteTracer reaches 97.1% accuracy on the synthetic benchmark, with class-level F1 scores of 97.0, 95.8, and 98.5 for Real, Potential, and Hallucinated, respectively, and detects 97.1% of fabrications on the real-world set without abstaining. Code: https://github.com/aaFrostnova/CiteTracer.

</details>


### [22] [100,000+ Movie Reviews from Kazakhstan: Russian, Kazakh, and Code-Switched Texts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08600)
*Rustem Yeshpanov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A new multilingual Kazakhstani movie review corpus (100k+ reviews) with language and sentiment labels is introduced, and sentiment models are benchmarked on polarity and rating prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of large, publicly available, sentiment-annotated datasets for Kazakhstani content, especially multilingual and code-switched texts in Russian, Kazakh, and mixed language, limiting research and model development for this region and languages.

Method: The authors collect 100,502 movie reviews from kino.kz (2001–2025), manually annotate each review for language and sentiment polarity, and use existing user ratings for a subset. They define two sentiment analysis tasks (three-way polarity and five-level score prediction) and benchmark classical bag-of-words/TF-IDF models against multilingual transformer models like mBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, and RemBERT under careful, leakage-controlled evaluation.

Result: Transformers (mBERT, XLM-R, RemBERT) outperform classical BoW/TF-IDF approaches on three-way sentiment polarity classification. However, predicting fine-grained five-class ratings remains difficult, mainly because of strong class imbalance and nuanced differences between neighboring rating levels, even when controlling for data leakage.

Conclusion: The new kino.kz corpus provides a valuable multilingual resource for sentiment analysis in the Kazakhstani context. While modern multilingual transformers handle coarse-grained polarity well, fine-grained rating prediction is still challenging, highlighting the need for improved methods and possibly better-balanced data or task formulations.

Abstract: We present a new publicly available corpus of 100,502 movie reviews from Kazakhstan collected from kino.kz, spanning 2001-2025 and covering 4,943 unique titles. The dataset is multilingual, consisting mainly of Russian reviews alongside Kazakh and code-switched texts. Reviews are manually annotated for language and sentiment polarity, and 11,309 reviews additionally contain explicit user-provided ratings. We define two sentiment tasks -- three-way polarity classification and five-class score classification -- and benchmark classical BoW/TF-IDF baselines against multilingual transformer models (mBERT, XLM-RoBERTa, RemBERT). Experimental results show that transformer models consistently outperform classical baselines on polarity classification, while score classification remains challenging under leakage-controlled evaluation due to severe class imbalance and subtle distinctions between adjacent rating levels.

</details>


### [23] [PARD-2: Target-Aligned Parallel Draft Model for Dual-Mode Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08632)
*Zihao An,Taichi Liu,Ziqiong Liu,Dong Li,Ruofeng Liu,Emad Barsoum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PARD-2 is a speculative decoding framework that retrains the draft model with a new objective focused on maximizing accepted token runs, achieving significantly higher lossless speedups over prior methods on Llama3.1-8B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding uses a small draft model to propose tokens for a larger target model, but the draft is typically trained to maximize per-token prediction accuracy, which is misaligned with the real inference-time objective: maximizing how many consecutive proposed tokens the target model accepts. This mismatch limits achievable acceleration. The authors want an objective and framework that directly optimize for longer accepted token segments and can flexibly support different deployment settings (target-dependent and target-independent).

Method: The authors extend the previous PARD framework to PARD-2, introducing a dual-mode speculative decoding system and a new Confidence-Adaptive Token (CAT) optimization. Instead of treating all tokens equally in the draft model’s loss, CAT adaptively reweights tokens according to their confidence and contribution to downstream acceptance by the verifier (target model), effectively optimizing for acceptance length rather than raw accuracy. The framework is designed so a single trained draft model can operate in both target-dependent and target-independent modes, enabling wider reuse and deployment flexibility.

Result: On a range of LLMs and tasks, PARD-2 delivers up to 6.94× lossless inference acceleration. On Llama3.1-8B, it outperforms EAGLE-3 by 1.9× and the prior PARD method by 1.3× in speedup while maintaining output quality (lossless setting).

Conclusion: By aligning the draft model’s training objective with the true inference-time goal—maximizing consecutive accepted tokens—PARD-2 with CAT optimization substantially improves speculative decoding efficiency. Its dual-mode design allows a single draft model to support different deployment scenarios, providing a practical and more general solution for accelerating LLM inference without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Models (LLMs) inference by using a lightweight draft model to propose candidate tokens that are verified in parallel by the target model. However, existing draft model training objectives are not directly aligned with the inference-time goal of maximizing consecutive token acceptance. To address this issue, we reformulate the draft model optimization objective, shifting the focus from token prediction accuracy to the overall acceptance length. In this paper, we build upon PARD to propose PARD-2, a dual-mode speculative decoding framework with Confidence-Adaptive Token (CAT) optimization. This approach adaptively reweights each token to better align with the verification process. Notably, PARD-2 enables a single draft model to support both target-dependent and target-independent modes. Experiments across diverse models and tasks demonstrate that PARD-2 achieves up to 6.94$\times$ lossless acceleration, surpassing EAGLE-3 by 1.9$\times$ and PARD by 1.3$\times$ on Llama3.1-8B. Our code is available at https://github.com/AMD-AGI/PARD.

</details>


### [24] [EdgeFlowerTune: Evaluating Federated LLM Fine-Tuning Under Realistic Edge System Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08636)
*Jiaxiang Geng,Yiyi Lu,Lunyu Zhao,Yan Gao,Nicholas D. Lane,Bing Luo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EdgeFlowerTune is a deployment-oriented benchmark to evaluate federated fine-tuning of LLMs on real edge devices, jointly measuring model quality and system costs under realistic constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While federated fine-tuning of LLMs on edge devices is attractive for personalization and privacy, most existing work uses cross-silo or simulated settings and ignores real-world edge constraints like limited compute, memory, communication bandwidth, and energy. This makes it unclear whether proposed methods are practically deployable on actual smartphones and IoT devices, and accuracy-only evaluations can be misleading.

Method: The authors design EdgeFlowerTune, a benchmark that evaluates federated LLM fine-tuning on real edge hardware. It measures both model performance and system-level metrics such as communication overhead, wall-clock training latency, memory footprint, energy consumption, and robustness to dynamic device conditions. They define three evaluation protocols—Quality-under-Budget (performance under given resource limits), Cost-to-Target (resources needed to reach a target quality), and Robustness (behavior under varying and unstable edge conditions). They implement EdgeFlowerTune as a real-device platform built on top of the Flower federated learning framework and MobileFineTuner, deploying across commercial Android smartphones and NVIDIA edge development boards.

Result: Running different federated LLM fine-tuning methods on the EdgeFlowerTune platform reveals that techniques with similar final accuracy can have very different system costs and deployability profiles. Some methods that look competitive by accuracy alone become impractical when communication, latency, memory, or energy constraints are considered. The benchmark exposes these trade-offs empirically on heterogeneous, real edge hardware.

Conclusion: System-aware, deployment-oriented evaluation is essential for federated LLM fine-tuning on edge devices. EdgeFlowerTune provides a reproducible real-device benchmark and standardized protocols to compare methods in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness, demonstrating that accuracy-only evaluations can be misleading and encouraging the community to design and assess methods that are genuinely deployable on resource-constrained edge systems.

Abstract: Federated fine-tuning offers a promising paradigm for adapting large language models (LLMs) on edge devices by leveraging the rich, diverse, and continuously generated data from smartphones and IoT devices without compromising user data privacy. Such edge-side adaptation can improve model personalization, robustness, and responsiveness to local contexts. However, the practical feasibility of federated LLM fine-tuning on real edge devices remains unclear, as most existing work focuses on cross-silo or simulation-based settings, overlooking the resource and runtime constraints that determine whether a method is deployable on real edge systems. We present EdgeFlowerTune, a deployment-oriented benchmark for federated LLM fine-tuning under realistic edge-system constraints. EdgeFlowerTune jointly evaluates model quality and system costs, including communication, wall-clock latency, memory usage, energy consumption, and robustness to dynamic edge conditions. To compare methods in terms of effectiveness, efficiency, and robustness, EdgeFlowerTune introduces three complementary protocols: Quality-under-Budget, Cost-to-Target, and Robustness. We instantiate EdgeFlowerTune as a real-device platform built on Flower and MobileFineTuner, spanning commercial Android smartphones and NVIDIA edge development boards. Our benchmark results show that accuracy-only evaluation can lead to misleading conclusions: methods with similar final quality may differ substantially in deployability once realistic system constraints are considered. EdgeFlowerTune provides a reproducible benchmark for system-aware evaluation of federated LLM fine-tuning at the edge.

</details>


### [25] [AgentCollabBench: Diagnosing When Good Agents Make Bad Collaborators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08647)
*Aritra Mazumder,Shubhashis Roy Dipta,Nusrat Jahan Lia,Tanzila Khan,Kainat Raisa Hossain,Nehaa Shri,Shubhrangshu Debsarkar,Humayra Tasnim,Gour Gupal Talukder Shawon,Debjoty Mitra,Sumaiya Ahmed Rani,Al Jami Islam Anik,Al Nafeu Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces AgentCollabBench, a benchmark to diagnose hidden failure modes in multi-agent LLM systems, focusing on how constraints and information survive (or fail) as messages pass through multiple collaborating agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While multi-agent LLM systems achieve strong results via collaboration, they may suffer from silent reasoning-chain failures when intermediate agents drop constraints or propagate false beliefs. Standard evaluations that look only at final outcomes cannot detect these process-level vulnerabilities, making it hard to assess reliability before deployment. The authors aim to create a way to systematically measure and compare these hidden risks.

Method: The authors design AgentCollabBench, a benchmark of 900 human-validated tasks in software engineering, DevOps, and data engineering. Each task is constructed to isolate one of four risks in multi-hop agent communication: (1) instruction decay, (2) false-belief contagion, (3) context leakage, and (4) tracer durability. They then evaluate four contemporary LLMs (GPT 4.1 mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, Qwen-3.5-35B-A3B, and Llama 3.1 8B Instruct) under different communication topologies (e.g., linear chains vs converging DAGs) to quantify how information survives or is lost across agents.

Result: The benchmark reveals that different models have distinct vulnerability profiles that outcome-only evaluation would miss. For instance, Qwen-3.5-35B-A3B performs best on tracer durability and instruction stability, while GPT 4.1 mini performs best on limiting context leakage and resisting false-belief contagion. Across models, communication topology explains 7–40% of the variance in information survival; converging DAG nodes, where an agent must synthesize multiple parent inputs, are especially prone to dropping minority-branch constraints, whereas linear chains do not exhibit this structural bottleneck as strongly.

Conclusion: AgentCollabBench shows that multi-agent reliability is not just a matter of model capability but also of system architecture. Certain topologies, especially those with converging synthesis nodes, can erase safeguards even in strong models by structurally favoring majority inputs and discarding minority-carried constraints. Therefore, improving multi-agent systems requires designing robust communication structures in addition to scaling model intelligence, and diagnostic process-level benchmarks like AgentCollabBench are essential for uncovering such architectural risks before deployment.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems achieve state-of-the-art outcomes through peer collaboration. However, when an agent in the pipeline silently drops a constraint, the system's final output may look correct even though the reasoning chain was quietly corrupted, and existing outcome-based evaluations are blind to such multi-hop process failures. To make these vulnerabilities measurable before deployment, we introduce AgentCollabBench, a diagnostic benchmark of 900 human-validated tasks spanning software engineering, DevOps, and data engineering. Each task isolates one of four behavioral risks: instruction decay (does a constraint survive peer pressure?), false-belief contagion (does a falsehood spread through consensus?), context leakage (does information bleed between tasks?), and tracer durability (does marked data reach the final agent?). Evaluating four modern LLMs (GPT 4.1 mini, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite, Qwen-3.5-35B-A3B, and Llama 3.1 8B Instruct), we expose model-specific vulnerability profiles invisible to outcome-only evaluation; Qwen-3.5-35B-A3B, for example, leads on tracer durability and instruction stability, while GPT 4.1 mini leads on leakage containment and false-belief resistance. Beyond per-model differences, communication topology emerges as a primary risk factor that explains 7-40% of the variance in multi-hop information survival. The effect traces to a synthesis bottleneck specific to converging-DAG nodes: an agent weighing competing parent inputs discards constraints carried by a minority branch, a bottleneck structurally absent from linear chains. AgentCollabBench demonstrates that suboptimal topology can silently erase the safeguards of highly capable models, arguing that multi-agent reliability is fundamentally a structural problem and that scaling model intelligence alone is no substitute for architecture.

</details>


### [26] [Hint Tuning: Less Data Makes Better Reasoners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08665)
*Siqi Fan,Minghao Li,Xiaoqian Ma,Xiusheng Huang,Zhuo Chen,Bowen Qin,Liujie Zhang,Shuo Shang,Weihang Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Hint Tuning, a method to reduce unnecessary chain-of-thought length in large reasoning models while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models are accurate but overly verbose, producing 5–8 times more tokens than needed and using long reasoning chains even for easy problems. This verbosity increases cost and latency, and existing methods to control it often require large distillation datasets or expensive RL. There is a need for a data-efficient way to calibrate reasoning depth to problem difficulty.

Method: The authors propose Hint Tuning, which uses a paired instruct model as a difficulty probe for a reasoning model. By testing what the instruct model can solve under different levels of guidance, they automatically label tasks into three regimes: No-Hint (the problem can be answered directly), Sparse-Hint (it needs a short reasoning prefix), and Full-Hint (it requires full chain-of-thought). These self-annotated examples (about 1K samples) are then used to fine-tune the reasoning model so it learns to adapt its reasoning length according to inferred difficulty, aligning its behavior with the instruct model’s demonstrated capabilities.

Result: Across multiple mainstream reasoning models (e.g., Qwen3-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1-Distill) and parameter scales from 4B to 32B, Hint Tuning reduces token usage by 24–66% (31.5% on average) while retaining competitive performance on five reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Hint Tuning offers a simple, data-efficient alignment technique that calibrates reasoning depth without large-scale distillation or reinforcement learning. By leveraging an instruct model as a practical difficulty probe, it significantly cuts token overhead in reasoning models while keeping accuracy comparable to the untuned models.

Abstract: Large reasoning models achieve high accuracy through extended chain-of-thought but generate 5--8 more tokens than necessary, applying verbose reasoning uniformly regardless of problem difficulty. We propose Hint Tuning, a data-efficient approach that teaches models to calibrate reasoning depth. Our key insight: the corresponding instruct model serves as an ideal difficulty probe. By testing what the instruct model can solve with varying guidance, we automatically construct training data across three states: No-Hint (direct answer), Sparse-Hint (minimal prefix), and Full-Hint (complete reasoning). This converts the abstract challenge of difficulty labeling into a measurable consistency check between the instruct and reasoning models. With only 1K self-annotated samples, Hint Tuning achieves 24--66% token reduction (31.5% average) across mainstream reasoning models (Qwen3-Thinking, DeepSeek-R1-Distill) at multiple scales (4B--32B) while maintaining competitive accuracy on five benchmarks. Unlike methods requiring massive distillation datasets or expensive RL, we achieve superior efficiency through simple alignment with the instruct model's capabilities.

</details>


### [27] [Explanation Fairness in Large Language Models: An Empirical Analysis of Disparities in How LLMs Justify Decisions Across Demographic Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08671)
*Gautam Veldanda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces a taxonomy and metrics to measure and audit fairness of LLM-generated explanations across demographic groups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While much work studies whether AI decisions are fair, far less is known about whether the explanations of those decisions are themselves fair and equitable across different demographic groups. Unequal explanation quality or style could undermine trust, accountability, and downstream outcomes, even if decisions are statistically fair. The paper aims to systematically define and measure this neglected dimension of fairness.

Method: The authors propose the Explanation Fairness Taxonomy (EFT), consisting of five measurable dimensions: Verbosity Disparity, Sentiment Disparity, Epistemic Hedging Disparity, Decision-Linked Explanation Disparity, and Lexical Complexity Disparity. They operationalize these with eight metrics, including two new black-box measures—Hedging Density Score (HDS) and Explanation Faithfulness Proxy (EFP). Using up to 400 controlled prompt pairs that differ only in demographic attributes, they evaluate five LLMs across four high-stakes domains (hiring, medical triage, credit, legal judgment), then conduct statistical analysis of disparities and test prompt-based mitigation strategies.

Result: All eight EFT metrics reveal statistically significant disparities between demographic groups (Cohen’s d from small to large, all Benjamini–Hochberg adjusted p-values < 10^(-62)). Disparity magnitude varies substantially by model; for instance, Qwen3 32B shows verbosity disparities nearly six times larger than LLaMA 3.3 70B. Prompting-based mitigations substantially reduce EFP disparity (78–95%) but fail to meaningfully change stylistic disparities (verbosity, sentiment, hedging, lexical complexity).

Conclusion: Explanation fairness is a measurable and systematically violated property of current LLMs, even when only black-box access is available. Model architecture and training lead to sizable differences in disparity profiles, and simple deployment-time prompting cannot remove stylistic explanation inequalities that appear rooted in pre-training. The authors provide a reproducible framework and metrics for explanation-level fairness auditing, highlighting the need to incorporate explanation fairness into regulation, model selection, and deployment practices.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed not only to make decisions but to explain them. While AI decision fairness has been studied extensively, the fairness of AI explanations (whether LLMs justify decisions with equal quality, depth, tone, and linguistic sophistication across demographic groups) has received little attention. This paper introduces the Explanation Fairness Taxonomy (EFT), a framework comprising five formally defined, operationalizable dimensions: Verbosity Disparity, Sentiment Disparity, Epistemic Hedging Disparity, Decision-Linked Explanation Disparity, and Lexical Complexity Disparity. The taxonomy is instantiated in a controlled empirical study across 80 prompt templates, four consequential decision domains (hiring, medical triage, credit assessment, legal judgment), and five LLMs: GPT-4.1, Claude Sonnet, LLaMA 3.3 70B, GPT-OSS 120B, and Qwen3 32B. Two novel black-box metrics are introduced: the Hedging Density Score (HDS) and the Explanation Faithfulness Proxy (EFP), a heuristic indicator of decision-linked explanation variation. Across up to 400 prompt pairs, all eight EFT metrics show statistically significant disparities (Cohen's d ranging from small to large, all p_BH < 10^(-62)). Model choice is strongly associated with disparity magnitude: Qwen3 32B exhibits verbosity disparities 5.9x larger than LLaMA 3.3 70B. Two prompting-based mitigations show significant reductions in EFP disparity (78-95%) but no significant effect on stylistic dimensions, consistent with the hypothesis that stylistic explanation inequalities are encoded in pre-training distributions and are not resolvable through deployment-level instruction alone. A reproducible measurement framework is offered for explanation-level fairness auditing, with implications for AI regulation and deployment practice.

</details>


### [28] [AgentForesight: Online Auditing for Early Failure Prediction in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08715)
*Boxuan Zhang,Jianing Zhu,Zeru Shi,Dongfang Liu,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces AgentForesight, an online auditor that detects decisive errors in multi-agent LLM trajectories during execution, enabling early intervention rather than post-hoc diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems perform long-horizon tasks, where a single decisive error can propagate and cause full-trajectory failure. Prior work mainly does post-hoc failure attribution—finding which agent and step caused the failure after the trajectory ends—so it cannot prevent failures in real time. There is a need for a method that can monitor trajectories as they unfold and flag decisive errors at the earliest possible step, using only partial information (the prefix) instead of the full trajectory.

Method: They reframe failure attribution as an online auditing problem: at each step of a trajectory, an auditor sees only the current prefix and must decide to continue or raise an alarm at the earliest decisive error. They curate AFTraj-2K, a dataset of multi-agent trajectories in Coding, Math, and general Agentic tasks. Safe trajectories are curated via a strict process; unsafe ones are annotated with the decisive error step using consensus among multiple LLM judges. On this data they train AgentForesight-7B, a 7B-parameter online auditor using a coarse-to-fine RL training recipe. First, the model learns a risk-anticipation prior on safe/unsafe prefix pairs around the failure boundary; then this prior is refined with a three-axis reward that jointly trains it to identify what the error is, where (which step) it occurs, and who (which agent) is responsible.

Result: On both the in-domain AFTraj-2K dataset and an external Who&When benchmark, AgentForesight-7B outperforms strong proprietary baselines such as GPT-4.1 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro. It achieves up to 19.9% performance gains and threefold reduction in step localization error for decisive errors in trajectories.

Conclusion: AgentForesight demonstrates that online auditing of multi-agent LLM trajectories is feasible and effective. By moving from post-hoc failure attribution to real-time error detection with precise step and agent localization, it enables deployment-time intervention that can prevent trajectory-level failures in long-horizon tasks. The AFTraj-2K dataset and the AgentForesight-7B auditor provide a foundation for future work on reliable multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed on long-horizon tasks, but a single decisive error is often accepted by downstream agents and cascades into trajectory-level failure. Existing work frames this as \emph{post-hoc failure attribution}, diagnosing the responsible agent and step after the trajectory has ended. However, this paradigm forfeits any opportunity to intervene while trajectory is still unfolding. In this work, we introduce AgentForesight, a framework that reframes this problem as online auditing: at each step of an unfolding trajectory, an auditor observes only the current prefix and must either continue the run or alarm at the earliest decisive error, without access to future steps. To this end, we curate AFTraj-2K, a corpus of agentic trajectories across Coding, Math, and Agentic domains, in which safe trajectories are retained under a strict curation pipeline and unsafe trajectories are annotated at the step of their decisive error via consensus among multiple LLM judges. Built on that, we develop AgentForesight-7B, a compact online auditor trained with a coarse-to-fine reinforcement learning recipe that first equips it with a risk-anticipation prior at the failure boundary on adjacent safe/unsafe prefix pairs, then sharpens this prior into precise step-level localization under a three-axis reward jointly targeting the what, where, and who of an audit verdict. Across AFTraj-2K and an external Who\&When benchmark, AgentForesight-7B outperforms leading proprietary models, including GPT-4.1 and DeepSeek-V4-Pro, achieving up to +19.9% performance gain and 3$\times$ lower step localization error, opening the loop from post-hoc failures detection to enabling deployment-time intervention. Project page: https://zbox1005.github.io/agent-foresight/

</details>


### [29] [Breaking the Impasse: Dual-Scale Evolutionary Policy Training for Social Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08721)
*Minzheng Wang,Run Luo,Yanbo Wang,Zichen Liu,Yuqiao Tan,Tao Tan,Xu Nan,Yinhe Zheng,Wenji Mao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and addresses an 'evolution impasse' in self-play reinforcement learning for open-ended social language games, proposing DEPT, a dual-scale evolutionary training method that detects strategic collapse and reshapes learning dynamics to maintain exploration and prevent policy degeneration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) works well for closed-ended tasks but struggles in open-ended social language games, where agents trained via self-play tend to converge to homogeneous, deterministic strategies. This 'evolution impasse' eliminates useful learning signals because matches become predictable and gradients vanish, blocking further policy improvement. The authors are motivated to design a method that can detect and break out of this impasse so language agents can keep evolving strategically in rich, open-ended interaction settings.

Method: The authors propose Dual-scale Evolutionary Policy Training (DEPT). DEPT introduces a time-scaled evolutionary perception mechanism that monitors training dynamics by measuring dual-scale value baseline divergence (short-term vs. long-term or different temporal resolutions of value estimates) together with match outcome entropy. When these signals indicate strategic collapse—low entropy and converging baselines—DEPT triggers an asymmetric advantage reshaping process, which dynamically modifies the optimization landscape, e.g., by altering how advantages are computed or weighted across agents. This intervention restores variability in learning signals and encourages agents to explore alternative strategies, rather than remaining stuck in homogenized behavior.

Result: Across multiple social language game benchmarks, DEPT consistently outperforms strong self-play and RLVR-based baselines. The empirical results show that DEPT prevents policy degeneration, maintains higher entropy in match outcomes, and supports continued diversification of strategies and better performance over time, compared with methods that fall into the evolution impasse.

Conclusion: DEPT provides an effective solution to the evolution impasse in open-ended social language games by combining dual-scale monitoring of training dynamics with targeted reshaping of learning advantages. This approach maintains meaningful gradient signals and sustained exploration, enabling social language agents to keep improving rather than collapsing to deterministic, homogeneous strategies. The work suggests that adaptive, evolution-aware training mechanisms are crucial for scaling RL-based language agents to complex, open-ended interaction domains.

Abstract: While Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has proven effective for closed-ended tasks, extending it to open-ended social language games via self-play reveals a critical issue: evolution impasse. Due to the vast strategy space, language agents frequently converge to homogenized behaviors, leading to deterministic match outcomes that eliminate the gradient signals necessary for policy evolution. To tackle this issue, we propose Dual-scale Evolutionary Policy Training (DEPT) for social language games. DEPT introduces a time-scaled evolutionary perception mechanism that detects impasse by quantifying dual-scale value baseline divergence alongside match entropy. Upon perceiving the collapse, it then activates asymmetric advantage reshaping to dynamically modulate the optimization landscape for intervention. Thus, our method effectively restores gradient signals and enforces sustained strategic exploration. Extensive experiments on multiple social language games demonstrate that DEPT outperforms strong baselines, avoiding policy degeneration and driving the continuous evolution of social language agents.

</details>


### [30] [Training with Harnesses: On-Policy Harness Self-Distillation for Complex Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08741)
*Zhengyang Zhao,Lu Ma,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose OPHSD, a training method that uses inference-time harness workflows as teachers so their benefits become part of the base model instead of remaining external.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time harnesses (like draft–verify or plan–solve) can significantly boost reasoning performance of LLMs, but only as external scaffolds; the underlying model itself does not truly improve and still depends on these workflows at inference. The authors want a way to permanently internalize the advantages of such harnesses into the model so it can perform well even without them.

Method: They introduce On-Policy Harness Self-Distillation (OPHSD). During training, they run the current model together with a task-specific harness (e.g., draft–verify for classification, plan–solve for math) and treat this harness-augmented system as a teacher. The student model is then trained by self-distillation on these enhanced trajectories, incorporating additional supervision signals that come from the harness behavior rather than just the original dataset, with training done in an on-policy manner using the evolving model.

Result: Across multiple reasoning tasks, including text classification with a draft–verify harness and math problem solving with a plan–solve harness, OPHSD consistently outperforms strong baselines, such as achieving a 10.83% improvement over OPSD on HMMT25. The student models gain strong standalone performance and robust generalization without needing the harness at test time.

Conclusion: Complex inference-time harnesses can be treated as temporary training scaffolds rather than permanent deployment mechanisms. By using OPHSD to distill the harness-augmented model back into the base model, one can permanently transfer harness capabilities into the model itself; after this, reattaching the harness at inference offers no benefit and may even hurt performance.

Abstract: Inference-time harnesses substantially improve large language models on complex reasoning tasks. However, the intrinsic capabilities of the underlying model remain unchanged by the addition of these external workflows. To bridge this gap, we introduce \emph{On-Policy Harness Self-Distillation} (OPHSD), which employs the harness-augmented current model as a teacher for self-distillation, thereby introducing extra supervisory signals from the harness beyond training data. OPHSD internalizes task-specific harness capabilities into the student model, yielding robust generalizability and strong standalone performance across diverse reasoning tasks. Evaluated across draft--verify harness for text classification and plan--solve for mathematical reasoning tasks, OPHSD consistently outperforms strong baselines (e.g., +10.83\% over OPSD on HMMT25). Our analysis further indicates that reattaching the harness during inference yields no additional benefits and can even degrade performance, suggesting that complex harnesses need not always be permanent fixtures; instead, they can serve as temporary training scaffolds whose benefits are permanently fed back into the base model. Our code and training data are available at https://github.com/zzy1127/OPHSD-On-Policy-Harness-Self-Distillation.

</details>


### [31] [Narrative Landscape: Mapping Narrative Dispositions Across LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08742)
*Donghoon Jung,Jiwoo Choi,Songeun Chae,Seohyon Jung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a quantitative framework to measure and compare large language model (LLM) dispositions—how consistently and diversely they make choices under controlled prompts—and visualizes these behaviors in a shared space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often show characteristic patterns of responses that feel like “personality” or style, but there is no clear quantitative way to define and compare these stable behavioral tendencies across models and instructions. The paper aims to provide a principled, repeatable method to profile such dispositions beyond anecdotal observation, and to reveal when similar numerical scores may hide qualitatively different behaviors.

Method: The authors design a structured narrative constraint-selection task that is run repeatedly on six advanced LLMs under three different instruction types. Disposition is operationalized with two metrics: (1) consistency, quantified as the Jaccard similarity of selected options across replications, and (2) diversity, quantified using the inverse Simpson index to capture dispersion over possible choices. They also develop “Narrative Landscape,” a PCA-based visualization that embeds each model’s full selection profile into a shared low-dimensional space, enabling direct visual comparison of models and instructions.

Result: Across models, the authors identify a spectrum from rigid (highly consistent, less exploratory selection patterns) to exploratory (more varied, less rigid patterns). They find that different instruction types can significantly reshape the geometry of the selection space in the Narrative Landscape, even when scalar metrics like average consistency or diversity appear similar across conditions.

Conclusion: LLM dispositions can be systematically quantified as stable, model-specific regularities using consistency and diversity metrics derived from controlled tasks. However, simple scalar scores are not sufficient: models with similar aggregate metrics can still exhibit qualitatively different selection topologies. Visualization in a shared Narrative Landscape is thus crucial for uncovering deeper structural differences in how models explore and commit to narrative options.

Abstract: This study proposes a quantitative framework for profiling LLM dispositions as stable, model-specific regularities in output under repeated, controlled elicitation. Using a structured narrative constraint-selection task administered across six frontier models and three instruction types, we operationalize disposition through two dimensions: "consistency", measured as cross-replication selection overlap via Jaccard similarity, and "diversity", measured as dispersion across options via the inverse Simpson index. We further introduce Narrative Landscape, a PCA-based visualization that maps each model's selection profile into a shared space for direct comparison. Results reveal a clear rigidity-exploration spectrum across model families and show that instruction types shift the geometry of selection spaces even when scalar metrics appear similar, indicating that comparable scores can mask qualitatively distinct selection topologies.

</details>


### [32] [SimReg: Achieving Higher Performance in the Pretraining via Embedding Similarity Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08809)
*Yan Sun,Guoxia Wang,Jinle Zeng,JiaBin Yang,Shuai Li,Li Shen,Dacheng Tao,DianHai Yu,Haifeng Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SimReg, a similarity-based regularization loss for LLM pretraining that reduces intra-class variance and increases inter-class separation of token embeddings, accelerating convergence and improving zero-shot performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Next-token-prediction-pretrained LLMs produce context-dependent token embeddings that suffer from high intra-class variance and inter-class similarity, limiting representation efficiency. While similarity-based regularization helps in supervised and classification settings, its impact during large-scale LLM pretraining is not well understood or exploited.

Method: They propose SimReg, an embedding similarity regularization loss added during LLM pretraining. SimReg encourages tokens with the same ground-truth label within a sequence to have similar representations and pushes apart embeddings of tokens with different labels through a contrastive loss. They analyze its effect on multi-class decision margins and conduct experiments on both dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures, alongside ablations on hyperparameters and components of the loss.

Result: SimReg speeds up training convergence by more than 30% and improves average zero-shot downstream benchmark performance by more than 1% compared to standard next-token prediction pretraining without this regularization, across both dense and MoE models.

Conclusion: Similarity regularization at the embedding level during LLM pretraining can significantly enhance representation quality and training efficiency. SimReg improves classification margins, accelerates convergence, and yields better zero-shot performance, and the ablation studies provide guidance for effectively tuning its hyperparameters and integrating it into large-scale LLM training.

Abstract: Pretraining large language models (LLMs) with next-token prediction has led to remarkable advances, yet the context-dependent nature of token embeddings in such models results in high intra-class variance and inter-class similarity, thus hindering the efficiency of representation learning. While similarity-based regularization has demonstrated benefit in supervised fine-tuning and classification tasks, its application and efficacy in large-scale LLM pretraining remains underexplored. In this work, we propose the SimReg, an embedding similarity regularization loss that explicitly encourages token representations with the same ground-truth label within each sequence to be more similar, while enforcing separation from different-label tokens via a contrastive loss. Our analysis reveals that this mechanism introduces gains by enlarging multi-classification margins, thereby enabling more efficient classification. Extensive experiments across dense and Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures demonstrate that SimReg consistently accelerates training convergence by over 30% and improves average zero-shot downstream performance by over 1% across standard benchmarks. Further ablation studies and analyses offer practical insights into hyperparameter tuning and loss effectiveness.

</details>


### [33] [The Grounding Gap: How LLMs Anchor the Meaning of Abstract Concepts Differently from Humans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08837)
*Odysseas S. Chlapanis,Orfeas Menis Mastromichalakis,Christos H. Papadimitriou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) ground abstract concepts like humans do, and finds a substantial gap between LLM and human conceptual grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Abstract concepts in humans are grounded in experiences, emotions, and social contexts rather than single sensory referents. It is unclear whether LLMs, trained on text, develop similar grounded representations. Understanding this matters for evaluating whether LLMs truly understand abstract meaning or merely manipulate word associations.

Method: The authors replicate classic cognitive science property-generation experiments on 21 frontier and open-weight LLMs, comparing model-generated properties of abstract concepts to human data. They compute correlations between model and human property profiles. They also replicate a rating experiment where both humans and models rate how much concepts involve different grounding categories (e.g., sensorimotor, social, emotional), and assess alignment as a function of model size. Finally, they apply sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to model activations to identify internal features corresponding to grounding dimensions such as sensorimotor and social information.

Result: Across models and experiments, LLMs rely more on surface word associations and generate fewer properties linked to emotions and internal states than humans. Model–human correlations for property generation are low (maximum Pearson r≈0.37) versus a human–human ceiling above r=0.9, revealing a large grounding gap. However, in the rating task, LLMs’ judgments on grounding dimensions align more closely with humans, and this alignment improves with model size. SAE analysis uncovers internal features associated with grounding dimensions like sensorimotor and social properties inside the models.

Conclusion: Current LLMs encode information related to grounding dimensions and can access it when directly asked to rate concepts, but they fail to recruit these dimensions in a human-like way during free generation of properties for abstract concepts. This indicates a persistent, systematic grounding gap between LLMs and human conceptual representations, especially for emotional and internal-state aspects of meaning.

Abstract: Abstract concepts - justice, theory, availability - have no single perceivable referent; in the human brain, their meaning emerges from a web of experiences, affect, and social context. Do large language models (LLMs) ground abstract concepts in a similar way? We study this by replicating property-generation experiments from cognitive science on 21 frontier and open-weight LLMs. Across models and experiments, we find a consistent pattern: when compared to humans, models rely too heavily on word associations, and underproduce properties tied to emotion and internal states. This yields a large and consistent grounding gap: no model exceeds a Pearson correlation r=0.37 with human responses, compared to a human-to-human ceiling above r=0.9. To better interpret this gap, we also replicate a rating experiment on grounding categories and find that here LLMs align more closely with human judgment, and alignment improves as models get larger. We then use sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to inspect whether this information is also reflected in the models' internal features, and we do identify features connected to grounding dimensions such as "sensorimotor" and "social". These findings suggest that current LLMs can recover grounding dimensions when explicitly queried, but do not recruit them in a human-like way when words are generated freely.

</details>


### [34] [Generating Leakage-Free Benchmarks for Robust RAG Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08838)
*Jiayi Liu,Jiaxing Zhang,Bowen Jin,Jennifer Neville*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SeedRG, a pipeline to create new, leakage-resistant RAG benchmarks by transforming existing datasets into novel but structurally similar questions, and filtering out items answerable without retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG benchmarks are increasingly unreliable because many questions can already be answered from an LLM’s internal (parametric) knowledge, a problem termed knowledge leakage, which worsens as models are trained on benchmark content (benchmark aging). The authors want a way to evaluate retrieval itself, not just memorized knowledge, and thus need benchmarks whose answers truly require retrieval.

Method: SeedRG starts from an existing RAG benchmark and: (1) extracts a reasoning graph from each question-context pair to represent the abstract reasoning structure; (2) generates new examples via type-constrained entity replacement so that entities are swapped with novel but type-compatible ones, preserving reasoning patterns; (3) applies a reasoning-graph consistency check to ensure the new examples have similar difficulty and reasoning structure; and (4) applies a knowledge-leakage filter that removes any new items that an LLM can answer correctly without retrieving the associated context.

Result: SeedRG yields semi-synthetic benchmark instances that remain structurally similar to the originals but use novel entities and pass the two verification stages, making them unlikely to exist in model parametric memory and more reliable for testing RAG. (Concrete empirical results are not given in the abstract, but the claimed outcome is higher-quality, leakage-resistant RAG evaluation data.)

Conclusion: By systematically transforming existing RAG benchmarks into semi-synthetic, verified variants, SeedRG mitigates knowledge leakage and benchmark aging, yielding more faithful evaluations of retrieval-augmented generation systems where success depends on retrieval rather than memorized knowledge.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is widely used to augment large language models (LLMs) with external knowledge. However, many benchmark datasets, designed to test RAG performance, comprise many questions that can already be answered from an LLM's parametric memory. This leads to unreliable evaluation. We refer to this phenomenon as knowledge leakage: cases where RAG tasks are solvable without retrieval. This issue worsens over time due to benchmark aging. As benchmarks are reused for training, their contents are increasingly absorbed into model parameters, making them less effective for evaluating retrieval.
  We introduce SeedRG, a semi-synthetic benchmark generation pipeline that mitigates knowledge leakage and addresses the issue of benchmark aging. Starting from a seed benchmark dataset, SeedRG extracts a reasoning graph from question-context pairs to capture their underlying reasoning structure, and then generates new examples via type-constrained entity replacement. This process produces structurally similar but novel instances that are unlikely to exist in the model's parametric knowledge, while preserving the original reasoning patterns. To ensure quality, we incorporate two verification steps: (1) a reasoning-graph consistency check to maintain task difficulty, and (2) a knowledge-leakage filter to exclude instances answerable without retrieval.

</details>


### [35] [ReST-KV: Robust KV Cache Eviction with Layer-wise Output Reconstruction and Spatial-Temporal Smoothing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08840)
*Yongqi An,Chang Lu,Kuan Zhu,Tao Yu,Chaoyang Zhao,Hong Wu,Ming Tang,Jinqiao Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ReST-KV proposes a new KV-cache eviction strategy that minimizes output change via layer-wise reconstruction and spatial-temporal smoothing, enabling large speedups on long-context inference while preserving or improving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs process longer sequences, the memory and latency cost of storing full key-value (KV) caches during generation becomes prohibitive. Existing eviction methods mostly rank tokens by raw attention weights, but this ignores how removing tokens redistributes attention and how importance can vary across layers and over time. A more principled and robust way to decide which KV entries to drop is needed to support efficient long-context inference without sacrificing quality.

Method: ReST-KV formulates KV eviction as an optimization problem: select KV entries to remove such that the discrepancy between the original model output and the approximated output is minimized. It performs efficient layer-wise output reconstruction to estimate how removing each token’s KV will affect the layer’s output, directly modeling token influence rather than using attention weights alone. To stabilize this signal, it applies exponential moving average smoothing over time (temporal dimension) and an adaptive window-based mechanism over positions (spatial dimension), yielding a spatial-temporal importance score that guides which KVs to evict.

Result: On long-context benchmarks, ReST-KV outperforms previous KV eviction baselines by notable margins: +2.58% on LongBench and +15.2% on RULER. It also consistently beats existing methods on Needle-in-a-Haystack and InfiniteBench. Importantly, it achieves these quality gains while providing substantial efficiency benefits, including a reported 10.61× reduction in decoding latency at 128k context length.

Conclusion: ReST-KV shows that treating KV eviction as an output reconstruction optimization problem, enhanced with spatial-temporal smoothing, yields a more faithful measure of token importance than raw attention. This leads to both better accuracy and much lower decoding latency for long-context LLM inference, offering a practical and robust approach to KV-cache compression and suggesting a promising direction for future work on efficient long-sequence generation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) face growing challenges in efficient generative inference due to the increasing memory demands of Key-Value (KV) caches, especially for long sequences. Existing eviction methods typically retain KV pairs with high attention weights but overlook the impact of attention redistribution caused by token removal, as well as the spatial-temporal dynamics in KV selection. In this paper, we propose ReST-KV, a robust KV eviction method that combines layer-wise output Reconstruction and Spatial-Temporal smoothing to provide a more comprehensive perspective for the KV cache eviction task. Specifically, ReST-KV formulates KV cache eviction as an optimization problem that minimizes output discrepancies through efficient layer-wise reconstruction. By directly modeling how each token's removal affects the model output, our method naturally captures attention redistribution effects, going beyond simplistic reliance on raw attention weights. To further enhance robustness, we design exponential moving average smoothing to handle temporal variations and an adaptive window-based mechanism to capture spatial patterns. Our method, ReST-KV, significantly advances performance on long-context benchmarks. It surpasses state-of-the-art baselines by 2.58% on LongBench and 15.2% on RULER. Additionally, ReST-KV consistently outperforms existing methods on Needle-in-a-Haystack and InfiniteBench, all while achieving a remarkable 10.61$\times$ reduction in decoding latency at 128k context length. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/an-yongqi/rest-kv to facilitate reproducibility and further research.

</details>


### [36] [XPERT: Expert Knowledge Transfer for Effective Training of Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08842)
*Chang Liu,Boyu Shi,Xu Yang,Xin Geng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes XPERT, a framework to extract and reuse cross-domain expert knowledge from Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models to improve training efficiency and performance of other language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE language models contain explicitly routed expert modules, and analysis shows some experts are consistently activated across diverse domains, encoding generalizable knowledge. This suggests these experts may be reusable knowledge sources, but there is no established method to practically extract and transfer this expert-level knowledge to other models.

Method: The authors introduce XPERT, which (1) identifies cross-domain experts via inference-time analysis of expert activation patterns, (2) refines the selected experts’ representations using tensor decomposition to consolidate and compress knowledge, and (3) adapts and injects this processed expert knowledge into downstream models of various scales to guide and accelerate their training.

Result: On language understanding and dialogue generation benchmarks, models that incorporate reused expert knowledge from XPERT achieve higher task performance and faster convergence than strong baseline models trained without such knowledge reuse.

Conclusion: MoE LLMs can serve as structured, reusable repositories of expert-level knowledge. By identifying, refining, and transferring cross-domain experts, XPERT demonstrates that expert-level knowledge reuse is an effective strategy to improve both the efficiency and effectiveness of training new language models.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models organize knowledge into explicitly routed expert modules, making expert-level representations traceable and analyzable. By analyzing expert activation patterns in MoE large language models (LLMs), we find that a subset of experts is consistently activated across diverse knowledge domains. These common experts encode cross-domain, generalizable knowledge that is closely related to model generalization, naturally raising the question of how such identifiable expert knowledge can be practically reused. Motivated by this observation, we propose XPERT, a framework that extracts, consolidates, and reuses expert knowledge from pre-trained MoE LLMs to support more effective training of language models across different model scales. XPERT identifies cross-domain experts via inference-only analysis, refines their representations through tensor decomposition, and adapts the extracted knowledge to reuse in downstream models. Experiments on language understanding and dialogue generation benchmarks show that models benefiting from reused expert knowledge achieve consistently stronger performance and faster convergence compared to strong baselines. These results highlight MoE LLMs as structured and reusable knowledge sources, and demonstrate the value of expert-level knowledge reuse for improving model training.

</details>


### [37] [EmoS: A High-Fidelity Multimodal Benchmark for Fine-grained Streaming Emotional Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08847)
*Pengze Guo,Jingxi Liang,Zhiwen Xie,Qifeng Wang,Derek F. Wong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents EmoS, a high-fidelity bilingual benchmark for emotion recognition and empathy modeling, addressing ecological validity and labeling noise while significantly improving MLLM performance when used for fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing emotion benchmarks struggle to balance real-world relevance (ecological validity), clear emotional signals, and reliable, fine-grained annotations, which limits progress in training and evaluating emotional and empathetic models, especially under the growing societal need for scalable emotional support systems.

Method: The authors construct EmoS, a bilingual benchmark comprising carefully filtered static emotional slices and a dynamic Streaming Monologue subset that reflects continuous emotional change. They employ a rigorous dual-layer human annotation pipeline to ensure high-quality, fine-grained, and reliable emotional labels that track temporal evolution of emotions.

Result: Fine-tuning multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on EmoS leads to substantial performance improvements compared with zero-shot baselines on emotion recognition and related tasks, demonstrating the dataset’s effectiveness and the value of its high-fidelity annotations.

Conclusion: EmoS offers a robust, ecologically valid, and finely annotated bilingual benchmark that mitigates noise issues in existing datasets and supports modeling of continuous emotional dynamics, thereby providing a strong foundation for future emotion recognition and empathy-oriented models; the resource is made publicly available to facilitate further research.

Abstract: In the context of today's high-pressure, aging society, the demand for large-scale emotional models capable of providing empathetic support is more critical than ever. However, existing benchmarks fail to simultaneously achieve ecological validity, signal clarity, and reliable fine-grained labeling. We introduce EmoS, a high-fidelity bilingual benchmark designed to resolve the limitations of ecological validity and noise in existing datasets by combining strictly filtered static slices with a dynamic Streaming Monologue subset. Supported by a rigorous dual-layer human annotation pipeline, EmoS provides trusted ground truth that captures continuous emotional evolution. Empirical results show that fine-tuning MLLMs (multimodal large language models) on EmoS yields significant gains over zero-shot baselines, laying the foundation for the training and evaluation of future emotion recognition models and empathy models. The dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/NLP2CT/EmoS.

</details>


### [38] [Architecture, Not Scale: Circuit Localization in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08853)
*Sohan Venkatesh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that the difficulty of mechanistic interpretability in transformers depends more on attention architecture than on model size, showing that grouped query attention yields more concentrated and stable circuits and exhibits a phase transition in factual recall circuits above a critical scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability often assumes that as language models grow larger, the underlying circuits become harder to analyze. Yet scaling laws and architectural variants like grouped query attention might change how features are represented. The authors want to test whether interpretability difficulty is inherently tied to parameter count or instead strongly influenced by attention design, and whether there are qualitative changes in circuits as models scale.

Method: They empirically compare three types of circuits—indirect object identification, induction heads, and factual recall—across two transformer families: Pythia (with standard multi-head attention) and Qwen2.5 (with grouped query attention). They examine how dispersed or concentrated the responsible circuits are, how stable their mechanisms remain across scale, and whether there are phase transitions in circuit structure, especially for factual recall, as model size increases.

Result: Grouped query attention models exhibit circuits that are significantly more concentrated in fewer components and more mechanistically stable across scales, contrasted with standard multi-head attention models of comparable size. For factual recall in the Qwen2.5 family, they observe a discrete phase transition at a critical scale, where the circuit collapses into a single bottleneck pathway instead of gradually spreading or degrading as size increases. The same basic pattern of concentration holds for indirect object identification and induction heads as well.

Conclusion: Interpretability difficulty is not an inevitable function of model size; instead, architectural choices—particularly the use of grouped query attention—can yield more localized and stable circuits that are easier to study mechanistically. Moreover, circuit structure can undergo sharp phase transitions with scale, implying that carefully chosen architectures may make large models more tractable for mechanistic analysis rather than less.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability assumes that circuit analysis becomes harder as models scale. We challenge this assumption by showing that the attention architecture matters more than parameter count. Studying three circuit types across Pythia and Qwen2.5, we find that grouped query attention produces circuits that are far more concentrated and mechanistically stable than standard multi-head attention at comparable scales. The same concentration pattern holds across indirect object identification, induction heads, and factual recall. Within a single architecture family (Qwen2.5), factual recall circuits undergo a discrete phase transition above a critical scale, collapsing to a single bottleneck rather than degrading gradually. These findings suggest that some architectural choices make large models more tractable to study and that interpretability difficulty is not a fixed consequence of model size.

</details>


### [39] [Max-pooling Network Revisited: Analyzing the Role of Semantic Probability in Multiple Instance Learning for Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08863)
*Shota Fujikawa,Issei Sato*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a more efficient hallucination detector for LLMs that avoids expensive semantic similarity computations while keeping performance close to state of the art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detection methods for LLMs, like HaMI, rely on both semantic consistency checks and internal model states, which are accurate but computationally expensive due to repeated sampling and semantic similarity calculations. There is a need for a method that preserves strong performance but is lightweight enough for practical deployment.

Method: They theoretically analyze HaMI through the lens of decision margins, showing that combining internal states with semantic consistency enlarges the decision margin. Using this insight, they revisit classical sentence classification architectures: they aggregate token-level internal representations using max pooling and feed the pooled representation into a small MLP to produce sentence-level hallucination scores. This design adaptively aggregates internal feature representations but does not use any explicit semantic consistency computation.

Result: Their max-pooled, MLP-based hallucination detector significantly reduces computational cost compared to hybrid approaches like HaMI because it removes the need for repeated sampling and semantic similarity calculations, while delivering performance that remains competitive with state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: A margin-enlargement perspective can guide the design of efficient hallucination detectors for LLMs. By focusing solely on adaptive aggregation of internal representations via simple architectures (max pooling + MLP), one can maintain near state-of-the-art detection quality with far better efficiency, obviating the need for explicit semantic consistency computation.

Abstract: Hallucination detection has become increasingly important for improving the reliability of large language models (LLMs). Recently, hybrid approaches such as HaMI, which combine semantic consistency with internal model states via Multiple Instance Learning (MIL), have achieved state-of-the-art performance. However, these methods incur substantial computational overhead due to repeated sampling and costly semantic similarity computations. In this work, we first provide a theoretical analysis of HaMI in terms of decision margins, revealing that scaling internal states with semantic consistency leads to an enlarged decision margin. Motivated by this insight, we revisit classical sentence classification models from a margin enlargement perspective, aggregating token-level features via max pooling and directly estimating sentence scores using a lightweight MLP. Without requiring semantic consistency computations, our approach achieves substantial efficiency improvements while maintaining competitive performance with state-of-the-art baselines through adaptive aggregation of internal feature representations.

</details>


### [40] [DocScope: Benchmarking Verifiable Reasoning for Trustworthy Long-Document Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08888)
*Xiang Feng,Jiawei Zhou,Zhangfeng Huang,Kewei Wang,Shanshan Ye,Jinxin Hu,Zulong Chen,Yong Luo,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DocScope is a benchmark for evaluating how well multimodal large language models perform structured, step-by-step reasoning over long, visually rich documents, going beyond just checking if the final answer is correct.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of multimodal LLMs on document question answering largely focus on end-to-end answer accuracy, which hides whether models actually follow correct and verifiable reasoning steps, especially on long, complex, visually rich PDFs. There is a need for a benchmark that can explicitly test and score the intermediate reasoning trajectory – from locating relevant pages and regions, to extracting facts, to verifying answers – in order to understand models’ true capabilities and failure modes.

Method: The authors build DocScope, a benchmark that reformulates long-document QA as a prediction of a full reasoning trajectory. Given a PDF and a question, models must output (1) evidence pages, (2) precise evidence regions within those pages, (3) explicit factual statements extracted from the evidence, and (4) a final answer. They design a four-stage evaluation protocol—Page Localization, Region Grounding, Fact Extraction, and Answer Verification—where each stage is evaluated independently via inter-stage decoupling. Human annotators create hierarchical evidence annotations for 1,124 questions over 273 documents, and all automatic judges used in evaluation are selected and calibrated through human alignment studies. They then benchmark 6 proprietary models, 12 open-weight models, and several domain-specific systems on this benchmark, plus perform oracle and cross-architecture analyses.

Result: Experiments show that traditional answer accuracy is a poor proxy for the quality of the reasoning trajectory: even when answers are correct, the proportion of fully correct evidence chains reaches only up to 29%. Region Grounding is identified as the weakest stage across all tested models. The most challenging cases involve aggregating evidence spread across distant pages and multiple document clusters. An oracle analysis indicates that accurate perception of visual content and faithful fact extraction are the main capability bottlenecks. Cross-architecture comparisons suggest that the number of activated parameters during inference is more predictive of performance than the overall model size.

Conclusion: DocScope provides a fine-grained, trajectory-level benchmark for long-document multimodal QA, revealing that many models arrive at correct answers without robust, verifiable reasoning chains. The work highlights Region Grounding, long-range evidence aggregation, and visual perception plus fact extraction as key open challenges. It also suggests architectural and scaling insights (emphasizing activated parameters) for improving future multimodal LLMs. The benchmark and code will be made publicly available to support further research in trustworthy, interpretable document reasoning.

Abstract: Evaluating whether Multimodal Large Language Models can produce trustworthy, verifiable reasoning over long, visually rich documents requires evaluation beyond end-to-end answer accuracy. We introduce DocScope, a benchmark that formulates long-document QA as a structured reasoning trajectory prediction problem: given a complete PDF document and a question, the model outputs evidence pages, supporting evidence regions, relevant factual statements, and a final answer. We design a four-stage evaluation protocol -- Page Localization, Region Grounding, Fact Extraction, and Answer Verification -- that audits each level of the trajectory independently through inter-stage decoupling, with all judges selected and calibrated via human alignment studies. DocScope comprises 1,124 questions derived from 273 documents, with all hierarchical evidence annotations completed by human annotators. We benchmark 6 proprietary models, 12 open-weight models, and several domain-specific systems. Our experiments reveal that answer accuracy cannot substitute for trajectory-level evaluation: even among correct answers, the highest observed rate of complete evidence chains is only 29\%. Across all models, region grounding remains the weakest trajectory stage. Furthermore, the primary difficulty stems from aggregating evidence dispersed across long distances and multiple document clusters, while an oracle study identifies faithful perception and fact extraction as the dominant capability bottleneck. Cross-architecture comparisons further suggest that activated parameter count matters more than total scale. The benchmark and code will be publicly released at https://github.com/MiliLab/DocScope.

</details>


### [41] [Fitting Is Not Enough: Smoothness in Extremely Quantized LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08894)
*Yuzhuang Xu,Xu Han,Yuxuan Li,Pengzhan Li,Wanxiang Che*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that extreme low-bit quantization of LLMs harms not just numerical precision but also the smoothness of the model’s output distribution, and that preserving this smoothness is key to maintaining generation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are expensive to deploy, so there is strong demand for aggressive quantization to reduce memory and compute costs. Existing methods mainly optimize for numerical fidelity of forward passes, but even when that is addressed, very low-bit quantization still causes quality drops, suggesting another overlooked factor. The authors hypothesize that this factor is degradation of the model’s smoothness and aim to study and address it.

Method: They introduce a smoothness proxy to measure how quantization affects the smoothness of the model’s prediction landscape, and analyze how this proxy changes as bit-width decreases. Using sequence neighborhood modeling, they study how quantization alters the distribution of likely next tokens and the structure of the decoding tree. They then incorporate a simple smoothness-preserving principle into both post-training quantization and quantization-aware training, and evaluate its impact on model performance beyond standard numerical accuracy metrics.

Result: Empirically, they find that as quantization becomes more extreme (fewer bits), the smoothness proxy degrades sharply. This corresponds to a rapid shrinkage in the set of effective token candidates in the prediction neighborhood, producing sparser decoding trees and worse generation quality. Applying their smoothness-preserving principle yields measurable performance gains beyond what can be achieved by only improving forward numerical accuracy, across both post-training quantization and quantization-aware training settings.

Conclusion: Extremely low-bit quantization harms LLM performance not only through numerical precision loss but also by degrading the smoothness of the output distribution and decoding structure. Preserving smoothness during quantization improves generation quality and should be treated as a central design goal for future extreme quantization techniques.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance but incur high deployment costs, motivating extremely low-bit but lossy quantization. Existing quantization algorithms mainly focus on improving the numerical accuracy of forward computation to eliminate performance degradation. In this paper, we show that extremely quantized LLMs suffer from systematic smoothness degradation beyond numerical precision loss. Through a smoothness proxy, we observe that such degradation becomes increasingly severe as the quantization bit-width decreases. Furthermore, based on sequence neighborhood modeling, we find that quantized models exhibit a rapid reduction of effective token candidates within the prediction neighborhood, which directly leads to a sparser decoding tree and degraded generation quality. To validate it, we introduce a simple smoothness-preserving principle in both post-training quantization and quantization-aware training, and demonstrate that preserving smoothness brings additional gains beyond numerical accuracy. The core goal of this paper is to highlight smoothness preservation as an important design consideration for future extreme quantization methods. Code is available at https://github.com/xuyuzhuang11/FINE.

</details>


### [42] [FragileFlow: Spectral Control of Correct-but-Fragile Predictions for Foundation Model Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08896)
*Zhuoyun Li,Boxuan Wang,Jinwei Hu,Xiaowei Huang,Yi Dong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and mitigates a hidden robustness failure mode in LLMs/VLMs, where predictions are correct but fragile due to probability mass leaking toward specific wrong classes, and proposes FragileFlow, a regularizer with PAC-Bayes guarantees that improves worst-class robustness without hurting clean accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard robustness metrics for LLMs and VLMs—like average accuracy or average consistency under perturbations—can be misleading because they ignore how prediction probabilities shift among classes. A model may appear robust on average while systematically pushing probability mass from true labels toward particular wrong competitors, especially near decision boundaries. This structured, class-wise fragility is not captured by existing evaluations, leaving blind spots in understanding and improving worst-class robustness.

Method: The authors formalize a new concept called margin-aware error flow: how probability mass moves from the true class to specific incorrect classes when predictions are correct but lie close to the decision boundary. They introduce FragileFlow, a plug-in regularizer that (1) uses a calibrated margin buffer to detect these correct-but-fragile predictions, and (2) aggregates their off-class probability mass into a class-wise vulnerable-risk matrix that tracks which wrong classes attract mass from which true classes. They then derive a PAC-Bayes upper bound on this margin-aware error-flow quantity, showing that controlling the spectrum (eigenvalues) of the vulnerable-risk matrix provides a conservative way to guarantee deterministic worst-class robustness under a stability condition. FragileFlow is integrated during adaptation of LLMs and CLIP by penalizing harmful error-flow patterns identified by this matrix.

Result: On multiple-choice benchmarks for LLMs and few-shot adaptation settings for CLIP (a vision-language model), FragileFlow improves the proposed risk measures that directly correspond to their theory (margin-aware error-flow and its spectral properties) compared to baseline adaptation methods. Empirically, it often increases worst-class accuracy under perturbations while maintaining or not degrading clean (unperturbed) accuracy, indicating better robustness where it matters most without sacrificing overall performance.

Conclusion: The work reveals that focusing only on average robustness hides systematic, class-structured fragility in LLMs and VLMs, and offers margin-aware error flow as a way to measure and understand this issue.
