FragileFlow operationalizes this insight as a regularizer that uses a vulnerable-risk matrix and is backed by a PAC-Bayes robustness bound. Experiments validate that this approach improves theory-aligned risk metrics and typically enhances worst-class robustness without hurting clean accuracy, suggesting it is a practical addition for more reliable adaptation of large language and vision-language models.

Abstract: Robust adaptation of LLMs and VLMs is often evaluated by average accuracy or average consistency under perturbations. However, these averages can hide a structured failure mode: a prediction may remain correct while probability mass already flows from particular true classes toward systematic wrong competitors near the decision boundary. In this paper, we formalize this phenomenon as margin-aware error flow and introduce FragileFlow, a plug-in regularizer that uses a calibrated margin buffer to identify correct-but-fragile predictions and organize their off-class probability mass into a class-wise vulnerable-risk matrix. Theoretically, we provide the first PAC-Bayes upper bound for this margin-aware error-flow object, showing how empirical spectral control yields a conservative route to deterministic worst-class robustness under a stability condition. Experiments on multiple-choice LLM benchmarks and few-shot CLIP adaptation show that FragileFlow consistently improves the proposed theory-facing risk measures over matched baselines, yields perturbed worst-class accuracy gains in most settings, and preserves clean accuracy across comparisons.

</details>


### [43] [LLM-Agnostic Semantic Representation Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08898)
*Jiawei Lian,Jianhong Pan,Lefan Wang,Yi Wang,Tairan Huang,Shaohui Mei,Lap-Pui Chau*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Semantic Representation Attack (SRA), a new, model-agnostic method for generating adversarial prompts against aligned LLMs by optimizing for malicious semantic representations instead of fixed textual patterns, achieving very high success and transferability across many models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial prompt methods focus on token-level optimization toward fixed textual responses (e.g., “Sure, here is…”), which leads to issues like slow or unstable convergence, unnatural prompts, and poor cross-model generalization. There is a need for an attack paradigm that is less brittle, more semantically grounded, and better transferable across different LLMs while maintaining stealth and interpretability.

Method: The authors reformulate the adversarial objective from matching exact textual templates to matching malicious semantic representations in a continuous space. They introduce a theoretical framework establishing a relationship between semantic coherence and optimization convergence, and derive a bound on cross-model semantic generalization. Building on this, they design the Semantic Representation Heuristic Search (SRHS) algorithm, which incrementally expands discrete token chunks while preserving interpretability and structural coherence, guided by semantic similarity to the target malicious representation rather than surface-form patterns.

Result: Their method, Semantic Representation Attack (SRA) implemented via SRHS, is evaluated on 26 open-source LLMs. Experiments show an average attack success rate of 99.71%, with strong cross-model transferability and high stealth (i.e., prompts remain natural and less likely to be detected).

Conclusion: Optimizing adversarial prompts in semantic representation space rather than for fixed textual templates yields more effective, generalizable, and stealthy jailbreak attacks on LLMs. Ensuring semantic coherence during optimization theoretically guarantees convergence and transferability, and the proposed SRHS algorithm successfully operationalizes this idea, substantially outperforming existing token-level attack approaches.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly employ alignment techniques to prevent harmful outputs. Despite these safeguards, attackers can circumvent them by crafting adversarial prompts. Predominant token-level optimization methods primarily rely on optimizing for exact affirmative templates (e.g., ``\textit{Sure, here is...}''). However, these paradigms frequently encounter bottlenecks such as suboptimal convergence, compromised prompt naturalness, and poor cross-model generalization. To address these limitations, we propose Semantic Representation Attack (SRA), a novel LLM-agnostic paradigm that fundamentally reconceptualizes adversarial objectives from exact textual targeting to malicious semantic representations. Theoretically, we establish the semantic Coherence-Convergence Relationship and derive a Cross-Model Semantic Generalization bound, proving that maintaining semantic coherence guarantees both white-box semantic convergence and black-box transferability. Technically, we operationalize this framework via the Semantic Representation Heuristic Search (SRHS) algorithm, which preserves interpretability and structural coherence of the adversarial prompts during incremental discrete token chunk expansion. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our framework achieves a 99.71% average attack success rate across 26 open-source LLMs, with strong transferability and stealth.

</details>


### [44] [Decomposing and Steering Functional Metacognition in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08942)
*Yanshi Li,Xueru Bai,Shuman Liu,Haibo Zhang,Anxiang Zeng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether large language models (LLMs) have structured, internally represented “metacognitive” states (like evaluation awareness, risk perception, and effort allocation) that influence their reasoning, and shows these states are decodable and causally manipulable via internal activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work shows that LLMs behave differently when they seem aware they are being evaluated, distorting benchmark-based performance estimates. It is unclear if this is just a superficial artifact or evidence of deeper internal structure. The authors want to understand, mechanistically, whether LLMs maintain structured internal variables related to metacognition—such as evaluation awareness, self-assessed capability, risk perception, computational effort, audience adaptation, and intentionality—and how these variables affect reasoning and benchmark outcomes.

Method: The authors analyze residual stream activations of multiple reasoning-oriented LLMs. They train linear probes to decode proposed “functional metacognitive states” (evaluation awareness, self-assessed capability, perceived risk, computational effort allocation, audience expertise adaptation, intentionality) from internal activations. They examine how decodability varies across layers to identify layer-wise profiles. Then, using the probe-derived directions in activation space, they perform activation steering: intervening along these directions during inference to test whether modifying each state causally changes behavior, measuring effects on reasoning style, verbosity, accuracy, and safety behavior across tasks.

Result: They find that the proposed functional metacognitive states are linearly decodable from residual stream activations and exhibit distinct layer-wise signatures, suggesting separable internal representations. Steering activations along each state’s direction systematically and differentially alters behavior: for example, changing verbosity, accuracy, and safety-related responses in ways consistent with the intended metacognitive manipulation. These effects are dissociable across states, indicating that the states are not just one generic artifact but a decomposable set of internal variables influencing reasoning.

Conclusion: Benchmark performance of LLMs is shaped not only by core task competence but also by internally represented functional metacognitive states, such as evaluation awareness and risk perception. These states can be mechanistically identified, decoded, and causally controlled through interventions in activation space. Understanding and manipulating such states is crucial for reliable evaluation and robust deployment of reasoning models. The paper proposes a mechanistic framework for studying functional metacognition in artificial systems and releases code and data for further work.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly exhibit behaviors suggesting awareness of their evaluation context, often adapting their reasoning strategies in benchmark settings. Prior work has shown that such evaluation awareness can distort performance measurements; however, it remains unclear whether this phenomenon reflects a single behavioral artifact or a deeper internal structure within the model.
  We propose that LLMs maintain a decomposable space of functional metacognitive states: internal variables encoding factors such as evaluation awareness, self-assessed capability, perceived risk, computational effort allocation, audience expertise adaptation, and intentionality. Through residual stream analysis across multiple reasoning models, we demonstrate that these states are linearly decodable from internal activations and exhibit distinct layer-wise profiles. Moreover, by steering model activations along probe-derived directions, we show that each functional metacognitive state causally modulates reasoning behavior in dissociable ways, affecting verbosity, accuracy, and safety-related responses across tasks.
  Our findings suggest that benchmark performance reflects not only task competence but also the activation of specific functional metacognitive states. We argue that understandi ng and controlling these internal states is essential for reliable evaluation and deployment of reasoning models, and we provide a mechanistic framework for studying functional m etacognition in artificial systems. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/xlands/meta-cognition.

</details>


### [45] [Improving Lexical Difficulty Prediction with Context-Aligned Contrastive Learning and Ridge Ensembling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08950)
*Wicaksono Leksono Muhamad,Joanito Agili Lopo,Tsamarah Rana Nugraha,Ahmad Cahyono Adi,Muhammad Oriza Nurfajri*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new contrastive-regression framework to better predict lexical difficulty across different L1 backgrounds by structuring the representation space and reducing model bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing lexical difficulty prediction models are trained only with scalar regression targets, which do not explicitly structure the embedding space. This limits their capacity to align representations across languages and to reflect the inherently ordinal nature of word difficulty. There is a need for methods that both align cross-lingual representations and encode the ordered difficulty scale, while remaining robust across difficulty levels.

Method: The authors introduce Context-Aligned Contrastive Regression, combining a Ridge regression ensemble with two additional learning objectives: (1) Cross-View Context contrastive learning to align word representations from different contextual views and across L1s while preserving language-specific signals; (2) Ordinal Soft Contrastive Learning to enforce that embeddings respect the ordinal relations of difficulty levels, using a soft contrastive objective instead of hard class boundaries. These objectives are integrated into a unified training framework for lexical difficulty prediction.

Result: On three datasets with different L1 backgrounds, the proposed method outperforms or is more stable than standard regression baselines. The contrastive components enhance cross-lingual representation alignment and ensure that the learned embeddings encode the graded nature of lexical difficulty. The Ridge ensemble reduces systematic biases present in individual base models, yielding more consistent performance across easy and hard words.

Conclusion: Structuring the representation space via context-aligned and ordinal-aware contrastive learning, combined with an ensemble regression scheme, leads to better and more robust lexical difficulty prediction across multiple L1s. This suggests that moving beyond plain scalar regression supervision and explicitly modeling both cross-lingual alignment and ordinal difficulty yields tangible benefits for language learning and readability applications.

Abstract: Lexical difficulty prediction is a fundamental problem in language learning and readability assessment, requiring models to estimate word difficulty across different first-language (L1) backgrounds. However, existing approaches rely on regression-only training with scalar supervision, which does not explicitly structure the representation space, limiting their ability to capture cross-lingual alignment and ordinal difficulty. To mitigate these issues, we propose Context-Aligned Contrastive Regression, which integrates Ridge regression ensemble with two complementary objectives, i.e., Cross-View Context and Ordinal Soft Contrastive Learning. Experiments on three L1 datasets show that (i) contrastive objectives improve cross-lingual representation alignment while preserving language-specific nuances, (ii) the learned representations capture the ordinal structure of lexical difficulty, and (iii) the ensemble effectively mitigates systematic biases of individual models, leading to more stable performance across difficulty levels.

</details>


### [46] [Dolphin-CN-Dialect: Where Chinese Dialects Matter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08961)
*Yangyang Meng,Huihang Zhong,Guodong Lin,Guanbo Wang,Hu Du,Zhiming Shao,Yukai Huang,Ke Li,Wei-Qiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Dolphin-CN-Dialect is a compact, streaming-capable ASR model optimized for Mandarin and multiple Chinese dialects, using improved data sampling and a new tokenizer to boost dialect recognition while keeping latency and model size low.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ASR systems, including earlier Dolphin versions, struggle with low-resource Chinese dialects, data imbalance between standard Mandarin and dialects, and efficient streaming deployment. There is a need for an ASR model that handles rich dialectal variation, maintains high accuracy under severe data imbalance, supports real-time inference, and remains lightweight for practical deployment.

Method: The authors enhance the previous Dolphin ASR by: (1) improving data processing and training stability; (2) introducing a temperature-based data sampling strategy to up-sample low-resource dialects relative to Mandarin; (3) redesigning the tokenizer to use character-level units for Chinese, subwords for English, and extensible special tokens for dialects; and (4) engineering support for both streaming and non-streaming inference, hotword customization, and hardware-optimized deployment.

Result: Experiments show that Dolphin-CN-Dialect substantially improves dialect recognition accuracy and reduces character error rate compared to the prior Dolphin model. It also achieves performance competitive with recent state-of-the-art open-source ASR systems while using a significantly smaller model. The model effectively handles multi-dialect scenarios in both streaming and offline modes.

Conclusion: Dolphin-CN-Dialect is an effective and practical multi-dialect ASR solution for Chinese scenarios, successfully mitigating data imbalance for dialects and achieving strong accuracy with a compact, streaming-capable model. Its flexible tokenizer, hotword mechanism, and hardware-efficient design make it suitable for real-world deployment where both latency and recognition quality matter.

Abstract: We present Dolphin-CN-Dialect, a streaming-capable ASR model with a focus on Chinese and dialect-rich scenarios. Compared to the previous version, Dolphin-CN-Dialect introduces substantial improvements in data processing, tokenization, training stability, and data sampling strategies. To address the challenges of highly imbalanced dialect data, we propose a temperature-based sampling strategy that effectively balances standard Mandarin and low-resource dialects, leading to significant gains in dialect recognition performance. In addition, we redesign the tokenizer to better align with linguistic characteristics, adopting character-level modeling for Chinese and subword modeling for English, while introducing extensible dialect tokens. Experimental results show that Dolphin-CN-Dialect achieves improvement in dialect recognition accuracy and CER reduction compared to Dolphin. Furthermore, Dolphin-CN-Dialect reaches competitive performance with recent SOTA open-source ASR models, while maintaining a significantly smaller model size. Dolphin-CN-Dialect supports both streaming and non-streaming inference, enabling a practical balance between latency and accuracy. It also provides flexible customization through hotword support and efficient deployment optimized for specialized hardware. These improvements make Dolphin-CN-Dialect a strong and practical solution for real-world multi-dialect ASR applications.

</details>


### [47] [LLiMba: Sardinian on a Single GPU -- Adapting a 3B Language Model to a Vanishing Romance Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09015)
*Luca Ballore*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build and analyze a 3B-parameter Sardinian-capable LLM (LLiMba) adapted from Qwen2.5 via continued pretraining and several fine-tuning strategies, showing large gains in Sardinian translation and insights into adapter methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sardinian, despite having about a million speakers, is almost absent from modern NLP: commercial systems don’t support it and general LLMs don’t reliably generate it. The authors want to create a capable Sardinian model and, more generally, understand how best to adapt a mid-sized Romance LLM to a low-resource Romance language on affordable hardware.

Method: They start from Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and perform continued pretraining on 11.5M Sardinian tokens (covering multiple Sardinian varieties) plus 2.4M Romance tokens as replay to reduce register blurring. They evaluate perplexity and FLORES-200 translation for multiple directions. Then they compare five supervised fine-tuning setups under matched conditions: full fine-tuning, LoRA r64, rsLoRA r128, rsLoRA r256, and DoRA r256. They analyze BLEU scores, failure modes such as script leakage, factual retention, hallucination patterns, regularization effects, and the impact of tokenization (byte fallback) on perplexity across scripts.

Result: After continued pretraining, the model attains 6.76 perplexity on held-out Sardinian and beats the base model on all six FLORES-200 directions. For SFT, rsLoRA r256 performs best for translation into Sardinian, reaching 28.5 BLEU from English vs. 17.3 after CPT and 21.0 with full fine-tuning. Rank ablation shows rsLoRA r128 is in between LoRA r64 and rsLoRA r256 on BLEU, but it uncovers qualitative failures like cross-script leakage unique to that variant. LoRA r64 preserves less factual content and produces more confident hallucinations; DoRA r256 minimizes train–eval loss gap but has the worst factual accuracy. They also observe that perplexity can be misleading across scripts due to byte-fallback tokenization lowering perplexity for non-Latin scripts.

Conclusion: Adapter capacity (rank/size) is more important than the specific LoRA variant when adapting a Romance base LLM to a low-resource Romance language like Sardinian. Stronger regularization methods are not always better, as they can harm factual accuracy or cause unique pathologies even when BLEU improves. Standard translation metrics can rank configurations smoothly yet still hide qualitatively distinct error types, so qualitative analysis remains necessary. Finally, cross-script perplexity comparisons require care because byte-fallback tokenization can artificially deflate perplexity for non-Latin scripts.

Abstract: Sardinian, a Romance language with roughly one million speakers, has minimal presence in modern NLP. Commercial services do not support it, and current language models do not produce it reliably. We present LLiMba, a 3B parameter Sardinian-ready model adapted from Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct through continued pretraining (CPT) and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a single 24 GB consumer GPU. The corpus contains 11.5 million tokens of Sardinian spanning LSC, Logudorese, and Campidanese, augmented with 2.4 million tokens of related Romance text as replay against register blurring. After CPT the model reaches a perplexity of 6.76 on held out Sardinian and outperforms the base across all six FLORES-200 directions. We compare five SFT configurations under matched conditions: full fine-tuning, LoRA r64, rsLoRA r128, rsLoRA r256, and DoRA r256. rsLoRA r256 wins on every direction into Sardinian, reaching 28.5 BLEU from English against 17.3 after CPT and 21.0 with full fine-tuning. The rank ablation places r128 between LoRA r64 and rsLoRA r256 on BLEU but reveals failure modes invisible to the metric, including leakage across scripts no other variant produces. LoRA r64 retains less factual content from SFT than configurations at higher rank and produces more confident fabrications, though all methods fabricate on content absent from training. DoRA r256 yields the smallest gap between training and evaluation but the worst factual accuracy. The findings indicate that adapter capacity matters more than the choice among LoRA variants for adapting a Romance pretrained base to a low resource Romance target, that stronger regularization is not uniformly beneficial, and that translation metrics smoothly order configurations whose qualitative behavior differs categorically. Perplexity comparisons across scripts must account for byte fallback tokenization, which deflates the metric for scripts other than Latin.

</details>


### [48] [GAMBIT: A Three-Mode Benchmark for Adversarial Robustness in Multi-Agent LLM Collectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09027)
*Alexandre Le Mercier,Chris Develder,Thomas Demeester*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GAMBIT is a benchmark and dataset for evaluating and improving imposter (deceptive agent) detection in multi-agent AI systems under adaptive, co-evolving adversaries, using chess as the testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current adversarial studies in multi-agent systems focus on simple tasks and static adversaries. In realistic deployments, a single deceptive agent can undermine the entire collective while adapting to evade detectors that were explicitly trained to catch it. There is no standard, challenging benchmark that captures this dynamic, especially one that evaluates both zero-shot robustness under distribution shift and the ability to rapidly recalibrate detectors against new attack strategies.

Method: The authors build GAMBIT, a benchmark based on chess as a deep reasoning multi-agent task, using Gemini 3.1 Pro agents as both cooperative players and imposters. They construct an evolutionary framework that co-evolves imposter strategies to be both highly harmful (collapsing task performance) and hard to detect. The benchmark defines three evaluation modes: two modes for zero-shot detection under increasing distribution shifts and a recalibration mode that tests how quickly detectors can adapt to novel imposter strategies using only 20 labeled examples. They compile a dataset of 27,804 labeled instances covering 240 distinct co-evolved imposter strategies and compare standard vs meta-learned detectors on zero-shot and few-shot adaptation behavior.

Result: The adaptive imposter generated by their evolutionary framework substantially degrades the collective’s task performance while remaining very hard to detect: a Gemini-based detector achieves only about 50.5% F1-score, near random for a balanced task. They also find that detectors with almost identical zero-shot performance can behave very differently when adapting to new attacks: one meta-learned detector adapts about 20 times faster and achieves up to 8x better performance in the few-shot recalibration regime than a non-meta-learned counterpart. These differences emerge only in the recalibration evaluation mode, not in standard zero-shot metrics.

Conclusion: GAMBIT is the first multi-agent benchmark where adversaries and defenses explicitly co-evolve, exposing key weaknesses of relying solely on zero-shot evaluation against adaptive attackers. The framework shows that adaptive imposters can remain nearly undetectable while severely harming system performance, and that meta-learning-based detectors can yield much faster and more effective recalibration from very limited labeled data. The benchmark, dataset, and code provide a reusable testbed and imposter-generation framework that can be extended beyond chess to other multi-agent domains with evolving adversaries.

Abstract: In multi-agent systems (MAS), a single deceptive agent can nullify all gains of an agentic AI collective and evade deployed defenses. However, existing adversarial studies on MAS target only shallow tasks and do not consider adaptive adversaries, which evolve their strategies to evade the very detectors trained to catch them. To address that gap, we introduce GAMBIT, a benchmark with three evaluation modes and two independent scores for evaluating imposter detectors: the first two modes measure zero-shot detection under increasing distribution shift, and a third recalibration mode measures how quickly a detector adapts to novel attacks from just 20 labeled examples. The benchmark comes with a dataset of 27,804 labeled instances spanning 240 co-evolved imposter strategies. Our contributions are threefold: (1) Using chess as a substrate deep reasoning problem and Gemini 3.1 Pro for agents, we release GAMBIT and its dataset to evaluate imposter detectors under realistic constraints against a stealthy adaptive imposter; (2) We introduce an adaptive imposter agent based on an efficient evolutionary framework, generalizable beyond chess, that collapses collective task performance while remaining essentially undetectable (50.5% F1-score with a Gemini-based detector); (3) We show that zero-shot evaluation can be highly misleading for adaptive adversaries: two detectors with near-identical zero-shot scores differ by 8x on few-shot adaptation, while the meta-learned variant converges 20x faster, a gap only visible in the recalibration mode. Altogether, GAMBIT provides the first multi-agent benchmark where adversarial attacks and defenses co-evolve, with an imposter framework generalizable beyond our use case, and promising techniques for fast recalibration in a rapidly evolving adversarial system. Code and data: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/gambit.

</details>


### [49] [A Quantum Inspired Variational Kernel and Explainable AI Framework for Cross Region Solar and Wind Energy Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09032)
*Pavan Manjunath,Thomas Prufer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Hybrid four-stage framework for short-term solar and wind power forecasting with a quantum-inspired residual corrector and a generative-AI explainability layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Short-horizon solar and wind generation forecasts are crucial for modern power systems, but most existing models are tailored to a single climate regime and focus mainly on either recurrent networks or large monolithic models that mix forecasting and explanation. There is a need for a modular, climate-robust approach that separates forecasting, residual correction, and interpretability.

Method: The paper proposes a four-stage hybrid pipeline. Stage 1 gathers hourly generation, irradiance, and surface weather data via public APIs. Stage 2 trains three classical forecasting baselines—ARIMA, gradient-boosted regression trees, and a two-layer LSTM—to obtain point forecasts and residual error series. Stage 3 applies a quantum-inspired variational kernel using a six-qubit hardware-efficient ansatz with three entangling layers to correct the residuals. Stage 4 employs generative AI purely as an explainability module, converting benchmark numbers and performance metrics into structured natural-language explanations.

Result: On three open datasets (Iberian solar, North Sea wind, and a mixed Texas trace), the proposed framework achieves forecasting accuracy within one percentage point of the best classical baseline for in-domain tasks. The quantum-inspired kernel achieves about a 15x higher Fisher discriminant ratio between calm and stormy regimes compared to a tuned radial basis function kernel, indicating much better separation of weather regimes.

Conclusion: A modular hybrid architecture that decouples data acquisition, classical forecasting, quantum-inspired residual correction, and generative-AI-based explainability can match strong classical forecasts while significantly improving regime separation. This suggests that quantum-inspired kernels and dedicated explainability layers can add value without sacrificing forecasting performance across diverse climatic regions.

Abstract: Reliable short horizon forecasting of solar and wind generation is a structural prerequisite of any modern power system yet most published forecasters are tuned and evaluated on a single climatic regime and most algorithmic novelty has been concentrated either on classical recurrent networks or on monolithic foundation models that combine forecasting and explanation We develop a four stage hybrid framework that separates these concerns The first stage acquires hourly generation irradiance and surface weather records through public application programming interfaces The second stage trains three classical baselines autoregressive integrated moving average gradient boosted regression trees and a two layer long short term memory network and produces a strong point forecast together with a residual error series The third stage corrects the residual through a quantum inspired variational kernel built on a six qubit hardware efficient ansatz with three repeated entangling layers The fourth stage uses generative artificial intelligence strictly as an explainability layer that reads the measured benchmark numbers and produces a structured natural language interpretation Across three regions drawn from open public archives Iberian solar North Sea wind and a mixed Texas trace the proposed configuration stays within one percentage point of the strongest classical baseline on the in domain forecasting task and the quantum inspired kernel separates calm and stormy weather regimes with a Fisher discriminant ratio approximately fifteen fold higher than a tuned radial basis kernel

</details>


### [50] [BiAxisAudit: A Novel Framework to Evaluate LLM Bias Across Prompt Sensitivity and Response-Layer Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09041)
*Jialing Gan,Junhao Dong,Songze Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that common bias benchmarks for large language models are unreliable because they ignore how prompt format and different parts of an answer can pull in opposite directions. It proposes BiAxisAudit, a two-axis protocol that treats bias as a distribution over prompts and separates selection from elaboration to reveal hidden inconsistencies and more accurately track real bias reductions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bias audits are now part of legal and governance frameworks (e.g., EU AI Act), so the robustness and security of bias benchmarks themselves matter. Existing benchmarks usually compress bias into a single number from one prompt format and one surface label, which misses important failure modes. Specifically, small, meaning-preserving changes in prompts can drastically change measured bias, and different parts of a model’s response (like multiple-choice selection vs free-text explanation) can conflict, hiding bias behind apparently neutral aggregate scores. This creates a "cancellation trap" and opens room for gaming or misinterpreting audits.

Method: The authors empirically probe large language models using a fixed pool of bias-relevant statements but systematically vary prompt dimensions (task format, perspective, role, sentiment) to see how bias scores change across prompts. They also decompose each response into two layers: a discrete Selection (e.g., chosen option) and a free-text Elaboration. They quantify cross-prompt instability and within-response inconsistency and then formalize BiAxisAudit, a protocol that: (1) treats bias as a distribution over a factorial grid of prompt configurations (across-prompt axis), and (2) applies Split Coding to separately code and analyze Selection and Elaboration, computing metrics like Inconsistency Rate and Divergence Net Imbalance (within-response axis). They apply this framework to eight LLMs, each with 80,200 coded responses, and examine variance explained by prompts versus models as well as cross-layer effects.

Result: Meaning-preserving changes in prompt format can shift bias endorsement by more than 0.7 on the same underlying statements, demonstrating high sensitivity to prompt design. Within individual responses, Selection and Elaboration often take opposing stances, so aggregate scores can look unbiased while hiding strong, but canceling, biases. Rankings of models based on selection-only vs elaboration-only scores are nearly uncorrelated (Spearman ρ=0.238, p=0.570); for instance, LLaMA3-70B is mid-ranked under selection-only but top-ranked under elaboration-only using the same responses. In the BiAxisAudit analysis, task format alone explains as much variance in bias signals as which model is used. A majority (63.6% pooled, up to 85.2% for some models) of bias signals appear in only one coding layer (selection or elaboration), showing strong cross-layer asymmetry. Interactions among prompt dimensions are larger than their main effects. The instrument can differentiate genuine bias reductions (where both bias endorsement rate and inconsistency drop) from cases where bias appears reduced only because it shifts between selection and elaboration layers.

Conclusion: Single-number, single-format bias benchmarks for LLMs are unreliable and can be misleading, as they overlook both prompt sensitivity and internal inconsistencies between selection and elaboration. BiAxisAudit offers a more secure and informative alternative by reporting bias together with reliability along two orthogonal axes: across-prompt variation and within-response layer separation. This approach reveals how much benchmark outcomes depend on prompt design, uncovers hidden or redistributed biases, and better distinguishes real bias mitigation from superficial changes, which is crucial for compliant and trustworthy bias auditing in regulatory contexts.

Abstract: Bias audits of large language models now operate within governance frameworks such as the EU AI Act, making benchmark reliability a security concern in its own right. Many current benchmarks, however, collapse bias into a single scalar from one prompt format and one surface label. This design misses two failure modes that can be exploited without changing model weights. Across prompts, meaning-preserving format changes shift bias endorsement by more than $0.7$ on a fixed statement pool. Within a response, the discrete Selection and free-text Elaboration can take opposing stances, so an apparently clean aggregate may hide substantial internal inconsistency (a ``cancellation trap''). Selection-only and elaboration-only rankings are therefore nearly uncorrelated across eight LLMs (Spearman $ρ= 0.238$, $p = 0.570$): LLaMA3-70B ranks in the middle under selection-only scoring but highest under elaboration-only scoring on the same responses. We introduce \textsc{BiAxisAudit}, a protocol that reports each bias score together with a reliability estimate on two orthogonal axes. The across-prompt axis evaluates each statement under a factorial grid of task format, perspective, role, and sentiment, treating bias as a distribution rather than a point estimate. The within-response axis uses Split Coding to recover Selection and Elaboration as separate signals, measured by the Inconsistency Rate and Divergence Net Imbalance. Across eight LLMs with $80{,}200$ coded responses each, task format alone explains as much variance as model choice; $63.6\%$ of pooled bias signals (up to $85.2\%$ per model) appear in only one coding layer, and prompt-dimension interactions exceed main effects. The instrument also separates real bias reductions from apparent reductions caused by cross-layer redistribution: some prompt configurations reduce both BER and IR, whereas others suppress only selection-layer bias.

</details>


### [51] [Evaluating Pragmatic Reasoning in Large Language Models: Evidence from Scalar Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09042)
*Ye-eun Cho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how different evaluation methods affect measurements of pragmatic reasoning in large language models, showing that results depend strongly on both the model and the evaluation setup.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is uncertainty about whether LLMs’ apparent pragmatic abilities reflect genuine underlying competence or are artifacts of specific prompts and tasks, especially since prompt-based judgments can diverge from models’ internal probability distributions.

Method: Using scalar diversity as a graded test of pragmatic inference and following Hu & Levy (2023), the authors compare two evaluation paradigms—direct probability measurements and metalinguistic prompting—across multiple LLM families and experimental task structures, systematically varying prompting strategies and conditions.

Result: They find that neither direct probability measurement nor metalinguistic prompting consistently yields better or more “pragmatic” behavior; pragmatic patterns differ markedly across model families, prompts, and task designs, and clear scalar diversity gradients only appear in some specific model-condition combinations.

Conclusion: Pragmatic reasoning in LLMs cannot be captured as a single, stable competence by any one evaluation paradigm; instead, it emerges from an interaction between internal probabilistic representations and the particular prompting and task context, making evaluation design crucial for interpreting LLM pragmatic abilities.

Abstract: Evaluating pragmatic reasoning in large language models (LLMs) remains challenging because model behavior can vary depending on evaluation methods. Previous studies suggest that prompt-based judgments may diverge from models' internal probability distributions, raising questions about whether observed performance reflects underlying competence or task-induced behavior. This study examines this issue using scalar diversity as a graded diagnostic for pragmatic inference. Following Hu & Levy (2023), this study compares direct probability measurement and metalinguistic prompting across multiple models and experimental settings. The results show that neither evaluation method consistently outperforms the other and that pragmatic behavior varies substantially across model families, prompting strategies, and task structures. Moreover, scalar diversity gradients emerge only in specific model-condition combinations, suggesting that pragmatic reasoning in LLMs reflects an interaction between internal probabilistic representations and task-induced prompting behavior rather than a stable competence captured by a single evaluation paradigm. These findings highlight the central role of evaluation design in interpreting pragmatic abilities in LLMs.

</details>


### [52] [Phase Transitions in Affective Meaning Divergence: The Hidden Drift Before the Break](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09043)
*Napassorn Litchiowong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces and formalizes affective meaning divergence (AMD)—a measure of how differently interlocutors emotionally interpret the same words—and shows that growing AMD can abruptly destabilize conversational repair and coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People often use the same words but interpret their emotional or affective meaning differently, which can cause conversations to derail. Existing work focuses on toxicity or sentiment but lacks a theoretically grounded, dynamic measure of how emotional interpretations diverge over time. The paper aims to formalize this phenomenon, connect it to theories of communication and coordination, and test whether it predicts when conversations go awry.

Method: The authors formalize affective meaning divergence (AMD) as the total-variation distance between two interlocutors’ affect distributions conditioned on linguistic anchors (e.g., specific words). Using speech-act theory, common-ground models, and entropy-regularized game theory, they construct a dynamic model of conversational repair and derive a logit best-response map. They then analyze real online conversations from CGA-Wiki and CGA-CMV, computing: (1) lexical divergence, (2) AMD, and (3) dialog-act repair statistics over time. They examine whether conversations that derail show critical-slowing-down (CSD) patterns—rising variance and slowing recovery—in these measures and compare AMD-based indicators to toxicity and sentiment baselines, including boundary-condition tests on a second dataset.

Result: The theoretical model shows that as AMD-driven load increases, the conversation dynamics can undergo a saddle-node bifurcation: beyond a threshold (when βα > 4), repair coordination collapses abruptly and exhibits hysteresis. Empirically, derailing conversations in CGA-Wiki display clear CSD signatures: increased variance in lexical divergence (p < 0.001, d = 0.36), AMD (p = 0.001, d = 0.26), and dialog-act repair (p = 0.016, d = 0.20), all statistically significant after multiple-comparisons correction and stronger than toxicity and sentiment baselines. AMD shows a distinct temporal pattern, with its variance peaking near the modeled bifurcation point, while toxicity variance peaks earlier. On the CGA-CMV dataset, evidence is mixed but trends align with the main findings.

Conclusion: Affective meaning divergence is a measurable, theoretically grounded construct that captures how interlocutors’ emotional interpretations of the same language diverge, and rising AMD can drive abrupt breakdowns in conversational repair and coordination. The combination of formal modeling and empirical analysis supports viewing derailing conversations as a dynamical system approaching a critical transition, with AMD offering a more precise and temporally diagnostic early-warning signal than standard toxicity or sentiment metrics. While boundary-condition results are mixed, they are directionally consistent, suggesting AMD is a promising lens for understanding and predicting conversational breakdowns.

Abstract: One partner says "Fine" meaning <i>resolution</i>; the other hears <i>surrender</i>. The word is shared; the affective uptake is not. We formalize this as <b>affective meaning divergence (AMD)</b>, the total-variation distance between interlocutors' anchor-conditioned affect distributions. Building on speech-act theory, common-ground accumulation, and entropy-regularized game theory, we derive a logit best-response map whose dynamics undergo a saddle-node bifurcation: when $βα> 4$, a monotone increase in AMD-driven load produces an abrupt, hysteretic collapse of repair coordination. On Conversations Gone Awry (CGA-Wiki; $N=652$), derailing conversations exhibit critical-slowing-down (CSD) signatures across multiple levels: lexical divergence variance ($p<0.001$, $d=0.36$), AMD variance ($p=0.001$, $d=0.26$), and dialog-act repair variance ($p=0.016$, $d=0.20$), all significant after correction and stronger than toxicity and sentiment baselines. AMD provides a distinct temporal signature, with retrospectively measured variance peaking at the bifurcation point while toxicity variance peaks earlier, and is the only indicator grounded in the theoretical framework. Boundary-condition analysis on CGA-CMV ($N=1{,}169$) yields mixed but directionally consistent evidence.

</details>


### [53] [Language-Conditioned Visual Grounding with CLIP Multilingual](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09060)
*J. de Curtò,Mauro Liz,I. de Zarzà*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates why multilingual vision-language models like CLIP perform inconsistently across languages, isolating whether the problem lies in the visual encoder, the text encoder, or their interaction, by probing models where only the multilingual text branch varies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multilingual CLIP-style models underperform or behave inconsistently across languages, especially low-resource ones, but it is unclear which part of the model causes these gaps and what the exact failure modes are. Understanding this is important for fair, efficient, and reliable deployment of such models globally, and for guiding future model and tokenizer design.

Method: The authors build a dense multilingual CLIP probing setup where the visual encoder is kept identical and only the XLM-RoBERTa-based text encoder changes across 13 typologically diverse languages. They test two CLIP variants with a large difference in visual encoder size (ViT-B/32 vs ViT-H/14, paired with XLM-R base vs large). Using 11 semantic concepts and 210 images, they densely sample text prompts and obtain model attention/activation maps, then compute several agreement metrics with English as reference: cluster-mask IoU, top-percentile IoU, and Spearman rank correlation, yielding 2,310 paired measurements per language. They analyze differences across high-resource vs low-resource languages and across backbone scales.

Result: They find: (1) Low-resource languages (Arabic, Basque, Luxembourgish) systematically underperform high-resource languages at both scales, and since the visual encoder is fixed, the deficit is localized to the text branch. The cluster-mask IoU gap between high- and low-resource languages is around 0.11–0.14 and statistically extreme (Wilcoxon p<10^-300). (2) Scaling the visual encoder by 7x does not uniformly fix gaps: it worsens structural failures for Basque and Luxembourgish while improving Arabic, suggesting different underlying causes—corpus coverage vs tokenizer fertility issues. (3) The maximum similarity scores across languages remain close to English (mean ratio ~0.94 at large scale), but cluster-mask IoU drops sharply, meaning the model still finds strong matches but localizes them to different spatial regions, revealing spatial misalignment—not signal collapse—as the main failure mode. They also estimate energy usage (3.4–3.9 Wh per 1,000 queries), showing the probing setup is compatible with practical inference budgets.

Conclusion: Cross-language performance gaps in multilingual CLIP are primarily rooted in the text encoder rather than the shared visual encoder, with different low-resource languages failing for distinct structural reasons (data coverage vs tokenization). The main issue is spatial misalignment of grounding rather than loss of semantic signal. Scaling up the visual backbone alone is insufficient and can even exacerbate some language failures. The proposed dense-CLIP probing is both diagnostic and energy-efficient, making it a useful tool for analyzing and improving multilingual grounding and for designing more equitable deployment strategies.

Abstract: Multilingual vision-language models exhibit systematic performance gaps across languages, but the mechanism remains ambiguous: cross-language divergence could arise from the visual encoder, the text branch, or their interaction. We resolve this ambiguity through a dense multilingual CLIP probe in which the visual encoder is held identical across thirteen typologically diverse languages and only the XLM-RoBERTa text branch varies. We evaluate two CLIP architectures spanning a 7x visual-encoder scale gap (XLM-R base + ViT-B/32, ~87M visual parameters; XLM-R large + ViT-H/14, ~632M) on 11 concepts and 210 images, and quantify cross-language agreement via cluster-mask IoU, top-percentile IoU, and Spearman rank correlation against an English reference (n=2,310 paired observations per language). Three findings emerge. First, low-resource languages (Arabic, Basque, Luxembourgish) incur a structural penalty at both backbone scales (Wilcoxon HR>LR p<10^-300; cluster-mask IoU gap +0.114 at base, +0.143 at large), isolating the deficit to the text branch. Second, scaling the encoder 7x widens the gap for structural failure cases (Basque Δ=-0.056, Luxembourgish Δ=-0.076) while improving Arabic (Δ=+0.033), separating corpus-coverage from tokeniser-fertility failures. Third, peak similarity is preserved across languages (mean ratio 0.94 at large scale) while cluster-mask IoU drops sharply, identifying spatial misalignment, not signal collapse, as the dominant failure mode. At 3.4-3.9 Wh per 1,000 queries, dense-CLIP grounding is competitive with high-throughput inference budgets, positioning it as a practical substrate for energy-aware multilingual deployment.

</details>


### [54] [Soohak: A Mathematician-Curated Benchmark for Evaluating Research-level Math Capabilities of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09063)
*Guijin Son,Seungone Kim,Catherine Arnett,Hyunwoo Ko,Hyein Lee,Hyeonah Kang,Jiang Longxi,Jin Yun,JungYup Lee,Kyungmin Lee,Sam Yoosuk Kim,Sang Park,Seunghyeok Hong,SeungJae Lee,Seungyeop Yi,Shinae Shin,SunHye Bok,Sunyoung Shin,Yonghoon Ji,Youngtaek Kim,Hanearl Jung,Akari Asai,Graham Neubig,Sean Welleck,Youngjae Yu,Akshelin R,Alexander B. Ivanov,Boboev Muhammadjon,Chaeyoung Han,Christian Stump,Dmitrii Karp,Dohyun Kwon,DoYong Kwon,Duk-Soon Oh,Giovanni Resta,Greta Panova,Huiyun Noh,Hyungryul Baik,Hyungsun Bae,Inomov Mashrafdzhon,Jeewon Kim,Ji Eun Lee,Jiaqi Liu,Jieui Kang,Jimin Kim,Jon-Lark Kim,Junseo Yoon,Junwoo Jo,Kibeom Kim,Kiwoon Kwon,Mario Kummer,Max Mercer,Minjun Kim,Nahyun Lee,Ng Ze-An,Rafał Marcin Łochowski,Raphaël Lachièze-Rey,Ruichen Zhang,Sejin Park,Seonguk Seo,Shin Jaehoon,Sunatullo,Taewoong Eom,Yeachan Park,Yongseok Jang,Youchan Oh,Zhaoyang Wang,Zoltán Kovács*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Soohak, a large, newly written benchmark of 439 research-level math problems (including a refusal subset) for evaluating advanced LLM reasoning beyond olympiad level, showing substantial headroom for current frontier and open-weight models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing math reasoning benchmarks for LLMs focus mainly on olympiad-style, step-by-step problem solving, and research-level benchmarks are very small (often under 50 problems), limiting reliable evaluation of next-generation models that already perform at gold-medal level on IMO-style tasks. There is also little evaluation of an important research-math skill: recognizing ill-posed or unsound problems and choosing to refrain from answering. The authors want a larger, harder, and more realistic research-math benchmark that better captures these capabilities and leaves room for progress.

Method: The authors collaborated with 64 mathematicians to author from scratch a new benchmark called Soohak, containing 439 research-level math problems. They structured the benchmark into two main subsets: (1) a Challenge subset of well-posed research-level problems designed to test high-level mathematical reasoning and problem solving; and (2) a Refusal subset consisting of ill-posed or otherwise problematic tasks that a responsible system should recognize and decline to answer confidently. They then evaluated several state-of-the-art frontier LLMs (Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5, Claude-Opus-4.5) and leading open-weight models (Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Kimi-2.5) on these subsets, measuring solution accuracy on the Challenge set and appropriate refusal behavior on the Refusal set. They also plan to delay public release of the dataset until late 2026 to avoid training contamination, offering evaluation as a service in the meantime.

Result: On the Challenge subset, strong frontier models attain only modest accuracy: Gemini-3-Pro 30.4%, GPT-5 26.4%, and Claude-Opus-4.5 10.4%, while top open-weight models such as Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, and Kimi-2.5 all score below 15%. This indicates that Soohak is substantially harder than olympiad-level benchmarks and that there remains large headroom for future model improvement. On the Refusal subset, no evaluated model exceeds 50% performance, revealing that recognizing ill-posed research problems and appropriately refusing to answer remains a major unsolved capability across current LLMs.

Conclusion: Soohak provides a substantially larger and more challenging research-level math benchmark than existing resources, enabling more reliable evaluation of frontier LLMs beyond olympiad-style reasoning. The low scores of both proprietary and open-weight models on its Challenge and Refusal subsets show that current systems fall far short of research-mathematician-level capability and particularly struggle with recognizing and declining to solve ill-posed problems. The benchmark thus establishes new performance targets for future models and highlights refusal behavior on unsound tasks as an important, under-optimized dimension of mathematical reasoning. To limit contamination and preserve its value as an evaluation resource, the authors will delay public dataset release until 2026 and provide evaluation access on request in the interim.

Abstract: Following the recent achievement of gold-medal performance on the IMO by frontier LLMs, the community is searching for the next meaningful and challenging target for measuring LLM reasoning. Whereas olympiad-style problems measure step-by-step reasoning alone, research-level problems use such reasoning to advance the frontier of mathematical knowledge itself, emerging as a compelling alternative. Yet research-level math benchmarks remain scarce because such problems are difficult to source (e.g., Riemann Bench and FrontierMath-Tier 4 contain 25 and 50 problems, respectively). To support reliable evaluation of next-generation frontier models, we introduce Soohak, a 439-problem benchmark newly authored from scratch by 64 mathematicians. Soohak comprises two subsets. On the Challenge subset, frontier models including Gemini-3-Pro, GPT-5, and Claude-Opus-4.5 reach 30.4%, 26.4%, and 10.4% respectively, leaving substantial headroom, while leading open-weight models such as Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, and Kimi-2.5 remain below 15%. Notably, beyond standard problem solving, Soohak introduces a refusal subset that probes a capability intrinsic to research mathematics: recognizing ill-posed problems and pausing rather than producing confident but unjustified answers. On this subset, no model exceeds 50%, identifying refusal as a new optimization target that current models do not directly address. To prevent contamination, the dataset will be publicly released in late 2026, with model evaluations available upon request in the interim.

</details>


### [55] [Character-Level Transformer for Tajik-Persian Transliteration with a Parallel Lexical Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09092)
*Mullosharaf K. Arabov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper builds a large Tajik–Persian parallel lexicon and uses it to train and benchmark a character-level Transformer transliteration model, releasing all resources for reproducible research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of high-quality, publicly available resources and strong baselines for automatic transliteration between Tajik (written in Cyrillic) and Persian (written in Perso-Arabic). This hinders cross-script NLP, dictionary building, and processing of related dialects. The authors aim to fill this resource gap and provide a solid, reproducible baseline model.

Method: 1) Curate and lexicographically verify a parallel corpus of 52,152 Tajik–Persian word/phrase pairs from printed dictionaries, encyclopedias, and vetted online sources. 2) Preprocess and deterministically split the corpus into train/validation/test. 3) Train a character-level sequence-to-sequence Transformer for transliteration. 4) Compare its performance—measured by Character Error Rate and exact-match accuracy—against rule-based dictionary methods and recurrent neural network baselines. 5) Analyze errors by part of speech to understand performance across lexical categories.

Result: The Transformer model achieves a Character Error Rate of 0.3216 and an exact-match accuracy of 0.3133, beating both rule-based and recurrent neural baselines. Using beam search with width 3 further improves results to a CER of 0.3182 and an accuracy of 0.3215. The authors also provide POS-based performance breakdowns and release all code, data splits, and configurations.

Conclusion: A large, carefully curated Tajik–Persian transliteration corpus combined with a character-level Transformer yields state-of-the-art performance for this task and outperforms traditional rule-based and RNN approaches. By releasing all resources and configurations, the work establishes a strong, reproducible baseline and enables further research in transliteration, cross-script NLP, and lexicographic applications involving Tajik and related Persian dialects.

Abstract: This study addresses automatic transliteration from Tajik (Cyrillic script) to Persian (Perso-Arabic script). We present a curated, lexicographically verified parallel corpus of 52,152 Tajik--Persian words and short phrases, compiled from printed dictionaries, encyclopedic sources, and manually verified online resources. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the largest publicly available word-level corpora for Tajik--Persian transliteration.
  Using this corpus, we train a character-level sequence-to-sequence Transformer model and evaluate it using Character Error Rate (CER) and exact-match accuracy. The Transformer achieves a CER of 0.3216 and an exact-match accuracy of 0.3133, outperforming both dictionary-based rule-based and recurrent neural baselines. With beam search (k=3), performance further improves to CER 0.3182 and accuracy 0.3215. We describe the data collection and preprocessing pipeline, model architecture, and experimental protocol, and report a part-of-speech analysis showing performance differences across lexical categories.
  All preprocessing scripts, deterministic splits into training, validation, and test sets, and training configurations are released to support reproducibility and further research on Tajik and related Persian dialects. The corpus supports research in character-level transliteration, cross-script NLP, and lexicographic applications.

</details>


### [56] [Dynamic Meta-Metrics: Source-Sentence Conditioned Weighting for MT Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09098)
*Luke Zhang,Justin Vasselli,Aditya Khan,York Hay Ng,En-Shiun Annie Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Dynamic Meta-Metrics (DMM) is a framework that dynamically combines existing machine translation metrics conditioned on the source sentence, improving correlation with human judgments over static ensembles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MT evaluation commonly relies on single metrics or static ensembles that do not adapt to specific properties of the source sentences or language pairs. Different metrics capture different quality aspects and may perform better on certain sentence types, but static weighting cannot exploit this variation. The authors aim to create an evaluation framework that adapts metric combinations to the characteristics of each source segment to better approximate human judgments.

Method: They propose Dynamic Meta-Metrics (DMM), which learns source-conditioned combinations of existing MT metrics. First, they cluster source sentences. For hard conditioning, they train a separate, interpretable combiner (e.g., linear or MLP) for each cluster. They also explore a soft-conditioned variant where combination weights vary smoothly with cluster responsibilities, effectively giving a weighted mixture over per-cluster combiners. They test different combiner architectures (linear, MLP, Gaussian process-based) and train them to maximize agreement with human evaluations using WMT Metrics Shared Task data.

Result: On WMT Metrics Shared Task benchmarks across multiple language pairs, MLP-based combiners consistently outperform linear and Gaussian process ensembles for both system-level and segment-level evaluation. Additionally, incorporating soft conditioning over source clusters yields further improvements over purely linear static models in terms of pairwise agreement with human judgments.

Conclusion: Dynamic, source-conditioned metric ensembles can better approximate human MT evaluation than static, global combinations. Learning cluster-specific or softly conditioned metric weights allows the framework to exploit variation in how different metrics perform across source-sentence types, and MLP-based combiners are particularly effective. This suggests that future MT evaluation systems should consider context-sensitive, data-driven ensembles instead of relying on single metrics or fixed-weight combinations.

Abstract: We propose Dynamic Meta-Metrics (DMM), a framework for machine translation evaluation that learns source-sentence conditioned combinations of existing metrics. Rather than relying on a single static ensemble or language-specific weighting, DMM adapts the metric combination based on properties of the source segment. We study hard conditioning, which fits an interpretable combiner per cluster, and an exploratory soft-conditioned extension whose weights vary continuously with source-cluster responsibilities. We evaluate DMM on the WMT Metrics Shared Task data across multiple language pairs using pairwise agreement measures at the system and segment levels. Across settings, MLP-based combinations outperform linear and Gaussian process-based ensembles, and introducing soft conditioning yields gains over linear models.

</details>


### [57] [GRC: Unifying Reasoning-Driven Generation, Retrieval and Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09100)
*Zhongtao Miao,Qiyu Wu,Yoshimasa Tsuruoka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GRC, a unified training framework that lets a single LLM do reasoning-driven generation, reasoning-enhanced embeddings, and context compression in one forward pass using meta latent tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Currently, text embedding models and generative LLMs are trained separately, which is costly in terms of data, training, and deployment. At the same time, context compression is increasingly important for long-context reasoning, RAG, agents, and continual learning, but is usually handled by separate methods. The authors want a single, efficient, and modular framework that jointly handles reasoning, representation, and compression, improving data efficiency and simplifying RAG deployment.

Method: They introduce meta latent tokens and a unified tuning approach that jointly trains an LLM to perform: (1) reasoning-driven generation, (2) reasoning-enhanced text representation, and (3) context compression, all in one forward pass. The framework, called GRC, defines generative, representative, and compressive objectives over shared latent tokens and model parameters. During inference, the model remains modular and "LEGO-style," so components can be flexibly combined. They also design hybrid paged attention to speed up inference with the additional latent mechanisms. The framework yields new paradigms: self-reason-latent embeddings, where embeddings are derived through internal reasoning over latent tokens, and latent memory-augmented generation, where an O(1)-length compressed KV cache serves as updatable memory.

Result: Models trained with GRC can simultaneously output generative text, embeddings, and compressed representations within a single forward pass. Experiments on reasoning-intensive retrieval benchmarks, generative tasks, document compression, latency, and RAG scenarios show improved or competitive performance while reducing deployment complexity and increasing data utilization by roughly 3x. The hybrid paged attention further improves inference speed under these unified tasks.

Conclusion: GRC demonstrates that reasoning-driven generation, reasoning-enhanced embeddings, and context compression can be effectively unified in one LLM framework with meta latent tokens. This yields more efficient training and deployment, higher data utilization, and new paradigms for embeddings and memory-augmented generation. The results suggest a path toward truly unified models that handle generation, retrieval, and compression seamlessly, particularly beneficial for RAG and long-context reasoning applications.

Abstract: Text embedding and generative tasks are usually trained separately based on large language models (LLMs) nowadays. This causes a large amount of training cost and deployment effort. Context compression is also a challenging and pressing task, which is vital to reasoning-driven generation, and agentic tasks requiring long context and continual learning. In this paper, we explore how to unify reasoning-driven generation, reasoning-enhanced text representation and context compression tasks in one forward pass for LLMs. Through meta latent tokens and a unified generative, representative and compressive tuning approach, we propose a training framework named GRC that bridges the three tasks. The trained models can accomplish three objectives in a single forward pass while maintaining modular, LEGO-style flexibility during inference. This design greatly reduces the deployment effort for retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and achieves efficient inference and three times data utilization during training. Furthermore, this framework design enables a new paradigm for text embedding: self-reason-latent embeds, and a new generation paradigm, latent memory-augmented generation, where compressed and internalized KV cache with O(1) length is used as the updatable memory. We also propose hybrid paged attention to speed up the inference of our models. Extensive experiments on reasoning-intensive retrieval benchmarks, generative tasks, document compression, latency evaluation, and RAG settings demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and may shed light on the truly unified model that can handle reasoning-driven generation, embedding and compression tasks seamlessly.

</details>


### [58] [Fin-Bias: Comprehensive Evaluation for LLM Decision-Making under human bias in Finance Domain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09106)
*Xiaoyu Hu,Jinman Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Fin-Bias is a benchmark to test how much LLMs ‘herd’ toward biased human analyst opinions in long financial reports, and whether prompting them to detect opinions helps them make more independent and accurate investment calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing finance LLM benchmarks mostly test stock-picking or trading skills on small datasets and do not analyze how susceptible models are to biased human context, which is crucial for real-world financial deployment. The authors want to systematically study whether LLMs follow human bias (herding) embedded in analyst reports and how to reduce such herding so that LLMs can make more reliable, independent investment decisions under uncertainty.

Method: They build Fin-Bias, a benchmark of 8,868 long firm-specific analyst reports from multiple industries, each with analyst-written qualitative analysis plus a labeled investment rating (Bullish/Neutral/Bearish). They run LLMs on these reports under several conditions: (1) with the original analyst rating, (2) without the rating, and (3) with intentionally altered (‘fake’) ratings, then collect the LLMs’ own investment ratings. They further design a procedure that asks models to detect and treat human opinions as potential bias, encouraging more independent reasoning, and then evaluate how this affects prediction performance on future stock returns.

Result: LLMs systematically herd toward the explicit analyst rating included in the context, even when that rating is manipulated (‘fake’), demonstrating strong susceptibility to contextual bias. When prompted with the proposed opinion-detection approach to recognize and discount potential human bias, some LLMs become less influenced by the explicit ratings and, in this debiased mode, achieve investment rating performance that surpasses human analysts in predicting subsequent stock returns.

Conclusion: Fin-Bias exposes a significant vulnerability of LLMs in financial applications: they tend to adopt human-biased opinions present in long, uncertain textual context instead of reasoning independently. However, by explicitly detecting and handling human opinions as potential bias, LLMs can be steered toward more independent judgments and even outperform human analysts on future return prediction. The benchmark provides a structured way to study and mitigate bias herding in financial LLM deployment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in financial contexts, raising critical concerns about reliability, alignment, and susceptibility to adversarial manipulation. While prior finance-related benchmarks assess LLMs' capabilities in stock trading, they are often restricted to small sample and fail to demonstrate LLM susceptibility to context with potential human bias. We introduce Fin-Bias (financial herding under long and uncertain financial context), a benchmark for evaluating LLM investment decision-making when faced with uncertainty and possible human-biased opinions. Fin-Bias includes 8868 long firm-specific analyst reports, including firm aspects summarized and analyzed by sophisticated analysts with investment ratings (Bullish/Neutral/Bearish) spanning from various industries. We present large language models with firm analyst reports with/without analyst investment ratings and even with 'fake' rating, to get investment ratings generated by LLMs. Our results reveal that LLMs tend to herd the explicit bias in context. We also develop a method to detect potential human opinions, which can encourage LLMs to think independently, some models even exceed human performance in predicting future stock return.

</details>


### [59] [From Traditional Taggers to LLMs: A Comparative Study of POS Tagging for Medieval Romance Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09147)
*Matthias Schöffel,Esteban Garces Arias*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates large language models for part-of-speech tagging in three Medieval Romance languages, showing LLM-based methods outperform traditional taggers, especially with fine-tuning and cross-lingual transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: POS tagging for Medieval Romance languages is difficult due to spelling variation, complex morphology, and scarce annotated data. Existing rule-based and statistical taggers struggle in this setting, and there is a lack of systematic evaluation of how modern LLMs can address these challenges for historical texts.

Method: The authors conduct an empirical comparison of traditional rule-based and statistical POS taggers against open-source LLMs on Medieval Occitan, Medieval Catalan, and Medieval French. They test several settings: zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, monolingual fine-tuning, and cross-lingual transfer (multilingual and targeted bilingual training) using historically grounded, annotated datasets.

Result: LLM-based approaches consistently outperform traditional POS taggers. The best results come from fine-tuning and multilingual training. Cross-lingual transfer particularly helps under-resourced varieties, and carefully chosen bilingual setups can surpass more general multilingual configurations for certain target languages.

Conclusion: Modern LLMs are highly effective for POS tagging in Medieval Romance languages, especially when leveraging fine-tuning and linguistically informed transfer learning strategies. Linguistic proximity and dataset properties are crucial when designing transfer setups. The work offers practical recommendations and releases all code, models, and datasets to facilitate reproducible, LLM-based POS tagging pipelines in digital humanities.

Abstract: Part-of-speech (POS) tagging for Medieval Romance languages remains challenging due to orthographic variation, morphological complexity, and limited annotated resources. This paper presents a systematic empirical evaluation of large language models (LLMs) for POS tagging across three medieval varieties: Medieval Occitan, Medieval Catalan, and Medieval French. We compare traditional rule-based and statistical taggers with modern open-source LLMs under zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, monolingual fine-tuning, and cross-lingual transfer learning settings.
  Experiments on historically grounded datasets show that LLM-based approaches consistently outperform traditional taggers, with fine-tuning and multilingual training yielding the largest improvements. In particular, cross-lingual transfer learning substantially benefits under-resourced varieties, while targeted bilingual training can outperform broader multilingual configurations for specific target languages. The results highlight the importance of linguistic proximity and dataset characteristics when designing transfer strategies for historical NLP.
  These findings provide empirical insights into the applicability of modern neural methods to medieval text processing and provide practical guidance for deploying LLM-based POS tagging pipelines in digital humanities research. All code, models, and processed datasets are released for reproducibility.

</details>


### [60] [Meow-Omni 1: A Multimodal Large Language Model for Feline Ethology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09152)
*Jucheng Hu,Zhangquan Chen,Yulin Chen,Chengjie Hong,Liang Zhou,Tairan Wang,Sifei Li,Giulio Zhu,Feng Zhou,Yiheng Zeng,Suorong Yang,Dongzhan Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces Meow-Omni 1, an open-source quad-modal multimodal LLM that fuses video, audio, physiological time-series, and text to infer animal (cat) intent, achieving SOTA on a new benchmark and releasing full pipeline and dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computational ethology struggles with semantic aliasing, where identical outward animal behaviors (like a cat’s purr) can reflect very different internal states depending on physiological context. Existing multimodal LLMs can’t ingest high-frequency biological time-series data, limiting them to shallow pattern recognition instead of reasoning about latent internal states. There is a need for models and datasets that jointly leverage behavior, sound, and physiology to better infer animal intent, with applications in veterinary care and wildlife conservation.

Method: They design Meow-Omni 1, a quad-modal MLLM that natively handles video, audio, physiological time-series, and text. The approach uses targeted architectural adaptations to integrate specialized scientific encoders (for signals like physiological time series) into a shared backbone. Intent inference is framed as a physiologically grounded cross-modal alignment problem, aligning internal-state labels with patterns across all modalities. They also build MeowBench, an expert-annotated quad-modal benchmark, and Meow-10K, a supporting dataset, and train/evaluate the model end-to-end on these resources. The entire system (model weights, training framework, data) is released open-source.

Result: On the MeowBench benchmark, Meow-Omni 1 achieves 71.16% accuracy for intent recognition, outperforming existing state-of-the-art vision-language and omni-modal baseline models by a substantial margin. This demonstrates the benefit of incorporating physiological time-series data and specialized encoders into multimodal LLMs for ethology tasks.

Conclusion: The work shows that fusing video, audio, physiological signals, and text within a dedicated quad-modal MLLM substantially improves animal intent inference compared to prior multimodal models. By releasing Meow-Omni 1, the MeowBench benchmark, and the Meow-10K dataset as a complete open-source pipeline, the authors propose a scalable framework for inter-species intent understanding and pave the way toward practical applications in veterinary diagnostics and wildlife conservation using foundation models.

Abstract: Deciphering animal intent is a fundamental challenge in computational ethology, largely because of semantic aliasing, the phenomenon where identical external signals (e.g., a cat's purr) correspond to radically different internal states depending on physiological context. Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are blind to high-frequency biological time-series data, restricting them to superficial behavioural pattern matching rather than genuine latent-state reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce Meow-Omni 1, the first open-source, quad-modal MLLM purpose-built for computational ethology. It natively fuses video, audio, and physiological time-series streams with textual reasoning. Through targeted architectural adaptation, we integrate specialized scientific encoders into a unified backbone and formalize intent inference via physiologically grounded cross-modal alignment. Evaluated on MeowBench, a novel, expert-verified quad-modal benchmark, Meow-Omni 1 achieves state-of-the-art intent-recognition accuracy (71.16%), substantially outperforming leading vision-language and omni-modal baselines. We release the complete open-source pipeline including model weights, training framework, and the Meow-10K dataset, to establish a scalable paradigm for inter-species intent understanding and to advance foundation models toward real-world veterinary diagnostics and wildlife conservation.

</details>


### [61] [Lost in Translation? Exploring the Shift in Grammatical Gender from Latin to Occitan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09156)
*Ahan Chatterjee,Matthias Schöffel,Matthias Aßenmacher,Esteban Garces Arias*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper uses an interpretable deep learning framework to study how grammatical gender changed from Latin’s three-gender system to the two-gender systems of the Romance languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latin had masculine, feminine, and neuter genders, whereas most modern Romance languages lost the neuter. How exactly gender information is distributed between word forms and their context, and how this may relate to historical change, is not well understood. Existing NLP tools and tokenizers are not well suited to low‑resource historical data such as Latin, limiting quantitative investigation of this diachronic process.

Method: The authors build an interpretable deep learning framework tailored to historical Latin and related Romance data. They first design a new tokenizer and compare it to standard tokenization methods, showing its superiority in this setting.
