Using this framework, they run two main analyses: (1) a lexical-level model that predicts grammatical gender from lemmas and their morphological features, probing which morphological cues are most informative; and (2) a contextual-level model that predicts gender from sentential context, allowing them to measure how different parts of speech contribute to inferring gender. They then compare and interpret these models to understand where gender information resides.

Result: Their custom tokenizer substantially outperforms conventional tokenization strategies on this low‑resource historical corpus. Lexical-level experiments show that specific morphological features of lemmas carry significant predictive power for grammatical gender. Contextual-level experiments reveal that certain parts of speech (e.g., surrounding determiners, adjectives, or pronouns) contribute strongly to grammatical gender prediction, quantifying how context supports gender assignment. Overall, they obtain a detailed picture of how much gender information is encoded in word forms versus in context.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that deep learning models, when made interpretable and adapted to historical data, can illuminate the restructuring of grammatical gender from Latin to Romance. Gender information is partitioned in systematic ways between lexical morphology and contextual cues, and understanding this distribution helps explain the transition from a three‑gender to a two‑gender system. The work also provides practical NLP contributions—an improved tokenizer and publicly available code, datasets, and results—for future research in historical linguistics and computational morphology.

Abstract: The diachronic evolution from Latin to the Romance languages involved a restructuring of the grammatical gender system from a tripartite configuration (masculine, feminine, neuter) to a bipartite one (masculine, feminine). In this work, we introduce an interpretable deep learning framework to investigate this phenomenon at both lexical and contextual levels. First, we show that conventional tokenization strategies are insufficiently robust for this low-resource historical setting, and that our proposed tokenizer improves performance over these baselines. At the lexical level, we evaluate the contribution of morphological features to gender prediction. At the contextual level, we quantify the contributions of different part-of-speech categories to grammatical gender prediction. Together, these analyses characterize the distribution of gender information between the lemma and its sentential context. We make our codebase, datasets, and results publicly available.

</details>


### [62] [WorldSpeech: A Multilingual Speech Corpus from Around the World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09167)
*Antonis Asonitis,Luca A. Lanzendörfer,Frédéric Berdoz,Roger Wattenhofer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WorldSpeech is a large-scale 24 kHz multilingual ASR corpus (65k hours, 76 languages) that significantly improves ASR performance when models are fine-tuned on it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ASR systems work well only for high-resource languages because most languages lack sufficient aligned audio-transcript data. The authors aim to alleviate this data scarcity problem to improve ASR accuracy for many more languages.

Method: The authors construct WorldSpeech, a multilingual speech dataset by collecting and aligning 65k hours of 24 kHz speech with transcripts from public sources such as parliamentary records, international broadcasts, and public-domain audiobooks, covering 76 languages. They then fine-tune existing ASR models on this corpus and evaluate performance.

Result: WorldSpeech contains 65k hours of aligned data spanning 76 languages; 37 languages have more than 200 hours, 28 have over 500 hours, and 24 exceed 1000 hours. Fine-tuning ASR models on WorldSpeech yields an average relative WER reduction of 63.5% across 11 typologically diverse languages.

Conclusion: A large, carefully collected multilingual corpus like WorldSpeech can dramatically boost ASR performance for many under-served languages, demonstrating that expanding high-quality aligned data coverage is an effective route to more inclusive and accurate speech recognition.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) performs well for high-resource languages with abundant paired audio-transcript data, but its accuracy degrades sharply for most languages due to limited publicly available aligned data. To this end, we introduce WorldSpeech, a 24 kHz multilingual speech corpus comprising 65k hours of aligned audio-transcript data across 76 languages, collected from diverse public sources including parliamentary proceedings, international broadcasts, and public-domain audiobooks. For 37 languages, WorldSpeech provides more than 200 hours of aligned speech, with 28 exceeding 500 hours and 24 surpassing 1k hours. Fine-tuning existing ASR models on WorldSpeech results in an average relative Word-Error-Rate reduction of 63.5% across 11 typologically diverse languages.

</details>


### [63] [Two Ways to De-Bias an LLM-as-a-Judge: A Continuous-Score Comparison of Hierarchical Bayesian Calibration and Neural-ODE Score Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09227)
*Andrea Morandi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares two methods for calibrating biased LLM-as-a-judge scores to match human (GPT-4) ratings, showing that a simple Bayesian linear corrector is better in low-data regimes, while a non-parametric neural flow (FFJORD) wins with many anchors, and offering a deployment decision rule based on label budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-as-a-judge scoring is cheap but systematically biased (leniency/strictness, compressed scale, verbosity bias). Organizations want to keep using cheap automatic raters but need their scores to approximate human ratings. Post-hoc calibration using a modest number of human-labeled anchors is a pragmatic solution, but it is unclear how best to model the mapping from biased LLM scores to human scores, especially under different data budgets.

Method: The authors study post-hoc calibration for LLM-as-a-judge using two contrasting correctors fit on paired anchor data (LLM score, human score). (1) A parametric hierarchical Bayesian linear model that yields an affine correction with per-score uncertainty. (2) A non-parametric Neural-ODE flow model (FFJORD) that directly transports the distribution of LLM scores to that of human scores. They evaluate both methods on the UltraFeedback fine_grained_score dataset with 1700 total paired examples (200 held out), and analyze calibration performance under different anchor counts (e.g., 100 vs. 1500). They decompose evaluation into three aspects: recovering the population-mean, per-item accuracy, and matching the overall score distribution shape, using metrics like mean offset, MAE, Pearson correlation, and KL divergence.

Result: Both methods substantially reduce a raw +0.71-point bias in LLM scores, achieving mean offsets within ±0.08 of GPT-4 using as few as 100 anchors. With 100 anchors, the Bayesian linear corrector outperforms or matches the flow: about 2× better by distributional KL divergence (0.031 vs. 0.058) and tied MAE. With 1500 anchors, the neural flow dominates across all metrics (MAE 0.320 vs. 0.359, Pearson 0.922 vs. 0.896, KL 0.026 vs. 0.037). The Bayesian linear model saturates earlier because it cannot represent remaining tanh-like nonlinearities, whereas the flow continues to improve as labeled data increases.

Conclusion: Calibration method choice should be driven by label budget. In low-data settings, a simple hierarchical Bayesian linear corrector is more data-efficient and yields better or comparable calibration. In high-data regimes with hundreds to thousands of anchors, a flexible neural flow model (FFJORD) yields systematically better reconstruction of human-like scores in terms of item-wise accuracy and distributional match. The authors distill these empirical findings into a practical decision rule to guide production deployment of LLM-as-a-judge calibration pipelines.

Abstract: [Abridged] Using a Large Language Model (LLM) as an automatic rater (LLM-as-a-judge) is cheap but potentially biased: some judges run lenient, others strict, the middle of the scale gets compressed, and verbose answers may be over-rewarded. A common remedy is post-hoc calibration: leave the cheap judge in place and, on a modest set of paired anchors, fit a transformation from raw judge scores to an estimate of the human rating. We compare two correctors that take opposing views on how this mapping should be modeled: a parametric, small-anchor hierarchical Bayesian linear correction with per-score uncertainty, and a non-parametric Neural-ODE (FFJORD) score-transport flow. Both are run head-to-head on UltraFeedback fine-grained_score (1700 paired examples, 200 held out), with calibration split into three operational sub-questions: population-mean recovery, per-item accuracy, and distributional-shape match.
  The headline result is that the choice between methods is primarily a data-budget question. Both correctors close the raw $+0.71$-point mean offset to within $\pm 0.08$ of the GPT-4 reference, at 100 and at 1500 anchors. Past that, the methods swap roles. With 100 anchors, the linear corrector reconstructs the human-score distribution roughly twice as well by KL divergence (0.031 vs. 0.058) and ties the flow on MAE. With 1500 anchors the flow wins on every metric (MAE 0.320 vs. 0.359, Pearson 0.922 vs. 0.896, KL 0.026 vs. 0.037). The Bayesian linear corrector saturates well below 1500 anchors: residual $\tanh$-shaped non-linearity is, by construction, structure a linear correction cannot fit. The flow keeps improving as labels grow. We translate these findings into an explicit decision rule for production deployments.

</details>


### [64] [Matching Meaning at Scale: Evaluating Semantic Search for 18th-Century Intellectual History through the Case of Locke](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09236)
*Yu Wu,Ananth Mahadevan,Filip Ginter,Michael Mathioudakis,Mikko Tolonen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well semantic search uncovers non-verbatim receptions of John Locke’s ideas in 18th‑century texts, showing gains over lexical methods but also limits due to residual dependence on word overlap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digitized historical corpora make it possible to trace the transmission of ideas at scale, but standard text reuse and quotation detection techniques mainly find exact or near‑exact wording overlaps. This misses paraphrases, indirect allusions, and more implicit forms of engagement that are crucial for intellectual history. The authors want to know whether modern semantic search methods can better detect such meaning-level connections in historical materials, using a concrete and historically important test case: the 18th‑century reception of John Locke.

Method: They construct an expert-annotated dataset of receptions of John Locke based on a semantic taxonomy that distinguishes different types and degrees of semantic engagement beyond verbatim quotation. They then apply an off‑the‑shelf semantic search pipeline to 18th‑century texts and compare its ability to retrieve passages engaging with Locke’s ideas against standard lexical text reuse baselines. Linguistic diagnostics are used to analyze what kinds of correspondences are retrieved and to what extent retrieval depends on surface vocabulary overlap.

Result: Semantic search retrieves significantly more implicit receptions of Locke’s work—cases where the ideas are engaged without close verbal overlap—than traditional lexical reuse methods. Nonetheless, detailed diagnostics show that even the semantic system is still partially constrained by lexical similarity, exhibiting a “lexical gatekeeping” effect: passages with little shared vocabulary remain relatively hard to retrieve.

Conclusion: Semantic retrieval methods offer clear advantages over purely lexical approaches for uncovering meaning-level intellectual receptions in large historical corpora, but they do not fully escape dependence on surface word overlap. Consequently, while semantic search is a powerful new tool for studying the circulation of ideas, it must be used with awareness of its lexical biases and in combination with other methods or further methodological improvements to capture the full spectrum of implicit intellectual engagement.

Abstract: While digitized corpora have transformed the study of intellectual transmission, current methods rely heavily on lexical text reuse detection, capturing verbatim quotations but fundamentally missing paraphrases and complex implicit engagement. This paper evaluates semantic search in 18th-century intellectual history through the reception of John Locke's foundational work. Using expert annotation grounded in a semantic taxonomy, we examine whether an off-the-shelf semantic search pipeline can surface meaning-level correspondences overlooked by lexical methods. Our results demonstrate that semantic search retrieves substantially more implicit receptions than lexical baselines. However, linguistic diagnostics also reveal a "lexical gatekeeping" effect, where retrieval remains partially constrained by surface vocabulary overlap. These findings highlight both the potential and the limitations of semantic retrieval for analyzing the circulation of ideas in large historical corpora. The data is available at https://github.com/COMHIS/locke-sim-data.

</details>


### [65] [Repeated-Token Counting Reveals a Dissociation Between Representations and Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09239)
*Sohan Venkatesh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that large language models actually represent token counts accurately internally, but specific MLP blocks overwrite this information with a biased, format-triggered wrong answer near the end of the network, so counting failures come from routing, not representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although large language models perform well on many reasoning tasks, they fail at simple counting of repeated tokens. This has been widely assumed to stem from an inability to internally track counts. The authors want to test this assumption directly and understand where and why the failure occurs inside the network.

Method: The authors apply linear probes to the residual stream across all layers of several LLMs (Llama-3.2 1B/3B and Qwen2.5 1.5B/3B/7B) to decode token counts at each depth. They analyze attention patterns and tokenization effects, and identify specific MLP blocks whose activations correlate with the emergence of incorrect final answers. They compare behavior for different input formats (repeated words vs digits, space-separated vs comma-separated lists) and locate the depth at which a format-triggered prior overwrites the correct count.

Result: Linear probes can decode the correct token count with near-perfect accuracy at all post-embedding layers, including the layers where the final wrong answer is produced. Attention does not collapse on repeated tokens, and tokenization artifacts do not explain the errors. Instead, an MLP block around 88–93% of the network depth injects a fixed wrong answer when the model sees repeated word tokens in a space-separated list format. This prior does not trigger for repeated digits and is reduced or suppressed by comma delimiters in larger models, but it persists in smaller ones. The pattern is consistent across the tested Llama and Qwen models at similar relative depths.

Conclusion: Counting failures in these LLMs are not due to missing or poor internal representations of counts, but to misrouting by late MLP blocks that apply a strong, format-specific prior overriding the correct representation. Therefore, improving counting requires interventions in routing and priors rather than simply enhancing representational capacity or attention over repeated tokens.

Abstract: Large language models fail at counting repeated tokens despite strong performance on broader reasoning benchmarks. These failures are commonly attributed to limitations in internal count tracking. We show this attribution is wrong. Linear probes on the residual stream decode the correct count with near-perfect accuracy at every post-embedding layer, across all model depths. This holds even at the exact layers where the wrong answer crystallizes while the model simultaneously outputs an incorrect count. Attention patterns show no evidence of collapse over repeated tokens and tokenization artifacts account for none of the failure. Instead, a format-triggered multi-layer perceptron (MLP) block overwrites the correctly-encoded count with a fixed wrong answer at roughly 88--93,% network depth. This prior fires for repeated word-tokens in space-separated list format and is absent for repeated digit-tokens. It is suppressed by comma-separated delimiters in larger models but persists in smaller ones. The finding holds across Llama-3.2 (1B and 3B) and Qwen2.5 (1.5B, 3B and 7B) at consistent relative depth. Counting failure is a failure of routing not of representation and the two require different interventions.

</details>


### [66] [LLM Agents Already Know When to Call Tools -- Even Without Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09252)
*Chung-En Sun,Linbo Liu,Ge Yan,Zimo Wang,Tsui-Wei Weng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces When2Tool, a benchmark and method to decide when LLM agents should call tools, and shows a probing-based steering method that sharply cuts unnecessary tool calls with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-augmented LLMs often call external tools even when they could answer directly. This causes extra latency and cost, and existing evaluations do not focus on the decision of whether a tool is actually needed. There is a need for a systematic benchmark capturing different reasons why tools may or may not be necessary, and for methods that can control tool use without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: 1) Construct When2Tool, a benchmark with 18 environments (15 single-hop, 3 multi-hop) grouped into three categories of tool necessity: computational scale (tasks too large to do in-context), knowledge boundaries (tasks requiring information beyond model pretraining), and execution reliability (tasks where deterministic tools are safer than the model). Each environment has calibrated difficulty levels that separate tool-necessary from tool-unnecessary instances. 2) Evaluate two training-free baselines: (a) Prompt-only approaches that modify instructions to discourage tool calls, and (b) Reason-then-Act approaches that force the model to explicitly reason about whether to call a tool before acting. 3) Probe hidden states of several LLMs to see if tool necessity is linearly decodable from pre-generation representations, comparing this to the model’s own natural-language reasoning about tool use. 4) Propose Probe&Prefill: train a lightweight linear classifier (probe) on hidden states to predict tool necessity, then use its prediction to prefill the model’s response with a steering sentence that nudges it toward or away from calling tools.

Result: The benchmark shows that existing training-free strategies give limited control over tool use. Prompt-only methods reduce tool calls but also suppress necessary ones, leading to noticeable accuracy drops. Reason-then-Act also degrades performance, especially on hard tasks. Probing experiments reveal that a simple linear classifier can predict when tools are needed with AUROC 0.89–0.96 across six models, significantly better than the model’s explicit, verbalized self-assessments. With Probe&Prefill, tool calls are reduced by about 48% on average with only ~1.7% absolute accuracy loss; by contrast, the strongest baseline achieving similar accuracy reductions only cuts tool calls by 6%, or to match the tool-call reduction it suffers roughly 5× larger accuracy loss.

Conclusion: LLMs possess an internal, linearly accessible representation of tool necessity that they do not reliably use during generation. When2Tool provides a controlled benchmark to study this phenomenon across different dimensions of tool necessity. Leveraging a simple probe on hidden states and steering the model’s initial tokens (Probe&Prefill) yields much better control over tool usage than prompt engineering or explicit reasoning alone, enabling substantial reductions in unnecessary tool calls with minimal effect on task accuracy.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents tend to call tools indiscriminately, even when the model can answer directly. Each unnecessary call wastes API fees and latency, yet no existing benchmark systematically studies when a tool call is actually needed. We propose When2Tool, a benchmark of 18 environments (15 single-hop, 3 multi-hop) spanning three categories of tool necessity -- computational scale, knowledge boundaries, and execution reliability -- each with controlled difficulty levels that create a clear decision boundary between tool-necessary and tool-unnecessary tasks. We evaluate two families of training-free baselines: Prompt-only (varying the prompt to discourage unnecessary calls) and Reason-then-Act (requiring the model to reason about tool necessity before acting). Both provide limited control: Prompt-only suppresses necessary calls alongside unnecessary ones, and Reason-then-Act still incurs a disproportionate accuracy cost on hard tasks. To understand why these baselines fail, we probe the models' hidden states and find that tool necessity is linearly decodable from the pre-generation representation with AUROC 0.89--0.96 across six models, substantially exceeding the model's own verbalized reasoning. This reveals that models already know when tools are needed, but fail to act on this knowledge during generation. Building on this finding, we propose Probe&Prefill, which uses a lightweight linear probe to read the hidden-state signal and prefills the model's response with a steering sentence. Across all models tested, Probe&Prefill reduces tool calls by 48% with only 1.7% accuracy loss, while the best baseline at comparable accuracy only reduces 6% of tool calls, or achieves a similar tool call reduction but incurs a 5$\times$ higher accuracy loss. Our code is available at https://github.com/Trustworthy-ML-Lab/when2tool

</details>


### [67] [Cornerstones or Stumbling Blocks? Deciphering the Rock Tokens in On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09253)
*Yuxuan Jiang,Runchao Li,Shubhashis Roy Dipta,Dawei Li,Zhao Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies why some tokens, dubbed Rock Tokens, keep high loss under on-policy distillation even when training seems converged, and shows they absorb lots of gradient but barely help reasoning, suggesting we should not weight all tokens equally during distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-Policy Distillation (OPD) uses a per-token KL loss to match a student model to a teacher, implicitly assuming all tokens are equally useful for learning. Meanwhile, RL with Verifiable Rewards has shown that only a small subset of tokens drive most reasoning gains. There is little understanding of which tokens matter in OPD and why some tokens remain hard to learn. The authors are motivated to understand the token-level optimization dynamics of OPD, especially the role of persistently high-loss tokens, to improve efficiency and effectiveness of large-scale model distillation.

Method: The authors perform an empirical token-level analysis during OPD training. They track per-token KL losses between teacher and student distributions over time, identifying tokens that retain high loss even after overall training saturates. They label these as Rock Tokens and measure their frequency and contribution to gradient norms. They then use causal interventions—editing or removing these tokens in generated sequences—to evaluate how they affect downstream reasoning performance. Finally, they experiment with modified training schemes that strategically bypass or reweight these tokens to test whether doing so improves distillation efficiency.

Result: They find that after OPD convergence, up to 18% of tokens in generated outputs remain as persistently high-loss Rock Tokens. These tokens occur frequently and contribute a disproportionately large fraction of total gradient norm, yet their own predictions barely improve over training. Causal interventions show that modifying or removing Rock Tokens has negligible impact on the model's reasoning performance, indicating that they mainly represent structural or discourse residuals rather than core reasoning steps. When the training objective is adjusted to de-emphasize or bypass Rock Tokens, alignment becomes more efficient without harming reasoning quality.

Conclusion: OPD spends a large fraction of optimization effort on Rock Tokens—tokens with persistently high KL loss that neither learn well from the teacher nor significantly influence reasoning performance. This reveals a mismatch between uniform per-token weighting in KL-based distillation and the actual functional importance of tokens. By recognizing and strategically bypassing these tokens, distillation can be made more efficient, suggesting that future alignment and distillation methods should adopt non-uniform token weighting focused on functionally important tokens instead of treating all tokens equally.

Abstract: While recent work in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has shown that a small subset of critical tokens disproportionately drives reasoning gains, an analogous token-level understanding of On-Policy Distillation (OPD) remains largely unexplored. In this work, we investigate high-loss tokens, a token type that--as the most direct signal of student-teacher mismatch under OPD's per-token KL objective--should progressively diminish as training converges according to existing studies; however, our empirical analysis shows otherwise. Even after OPD training reaches apparent saturation, a substantial subset of tokens continues to exhibit persistently high loss; these tokens, which we term Rock Tokens, can account for up to 18\% of the tokens in generated outputs. Our investigation reveals two startling paradoxes. First, despite their high occurrence frequency providing a disproportionately large share of total gradient norms, Rock Tokens themselves remain stagnant throughout training, resisting teacher-driven corrections. Second, through causal intervention, we find that these tokens provide negligible functional contribution to the model's actual reasoning performance. These findings suggest that a vast amount of optimization bandwidth is spent on structural and discourse residuals that the student model cannot or need not internalize. By deconstructing these dynamics, we demonstrate that strategically bypassing these ``stumbling blocks'' can significantly streamline the alignment process, challenging the necessity of uniform token weighting and offering a more efficient paradigm for large-scale model distillation.

</details>


### [68] [Beyond Continuity: Challenges of Context Switching in Multi-Turn Dialogue with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09268)
*Aditya Sinha,Harald Steck,Vito Ostuni,Matteo Rinaldi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well large language models handle topic shifts in multi-turn conversations and finds that many models fail, especially open-weight ones, then extracts lessons for improving long-term multi-turn robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real conversations with LLMs, users often change topics or refine earlier questions. Current models frequently fail to notice these shifts and instead keep using outdated or irrelevant context, causing wrong answers. There was no systematic benchmark to stress-test and quantify this behavior across different model types and domains.

Method: The authors design two subtasks: (1) classify each new user turn as a pivot (topic shift) or refinement of the prior topic, and (2) select the relevant past turns for answering the current query. They then build synthetic benchmarks from multiple real-world datasets from different domains, programmatically injecting controlled context shifts of varying difficulty. Using these benchmarks, they evaluate zero-shot performance of ten LLMs (including open-weight, closed-source, and reasoning-focused models), and analyze error patterns such as overuse of prior context and position bias.

Result: Some reasoning-oriented and strongly instruction-tuned models can reliably detect pivots and handle context selection; open-weight models generally perform poorly, often dragging in stale context even when given explicit cues to ignore it. All evaluated models show a position bias, favoring recent or particular-position context regardless of true relevance. Overall, multi-turn understanding and context management remain weak across the board, with large variability between model families.

Conclusion: Today’s LLMs are not robust at handling topic shifts and context refinement in multi-turn dialogue, especially open-weight models, which often mismanage conversational history. The findings highlight the need for better training, prompting, and architectural or tooling support (e.g., improved context selection mechanisms) to make LLMs more reliable over long, evolving conversations. The paper’s benchmarks and analysis provide guidance and a testbed for future improvements in multi-turn robustness.

Abstract: Users interacting with Large Language Models (LLMs) in a multi-turn conversation routinely refine their requests or pivot to new topics. LLMs, however, often miss these topic shifts and carry over irrelevant context from previous turns, leading to inaccurate responses. In this paper, we stress-test the multi-turn understanding of LLMs and study the following two sub-tasks: (1) detecting whether the user pivots or refines in the current turn, and (2) shortlisting relevant context from previous turns. To this end, we construct synthetic benchmarks based on real-world datasets from varied domains, as to simulate context shifts of different levels of difficulty. We then evaluate the zero-shot performance of ten LLMs (open-weight, closed-source and reasoning), and demonstrate that only some reasoning and strongly instructed LLMs are accurate in detecting pivots; open-weight LLMs struggle with the task and frequently carry stale context even with explicit cues; and all models suffer from a position bias. Based on the results, we discuss key takeaways for improving long-term robustness in multi-turn capabilities for LLMs.

</details>


### [69] [DeltaRubric: Generative Multimodal Reward Modeling via Joint Planning and Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09269)
*Rui Liu,Dian Yu,Zhenwen Liang,Yucheng Shi,Tong Zheng,Runpeng Dai,Haitao Mi,Pratap Tokekar,Leoweiliang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DeltaRubric is a two-stage, rubric-based evaluation framework for multimodal LLMs that plans visual checks and then verifies them, significantly improving reward modeling reliability over single-step evaluators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal reward models and evaluators often rely on language priors instead of carefully checking images, leading to lazy and biased judgments. While rubric-based evaluation helps in text-only domains by enforcing explicit criteria, extending this idea to vision–language tasks is hard because visual reasoning is complex and often depends on instance-specific details. There is a need for an evaluation approach that dynamically focuses on concrete visual discrepancies between candidate answers to provide more robust supervision for aligning MLLMs.

Method: DeltaRubric converts multimodal preference evaluation into a plan-and-execute pipeline within a single MLLM that plays multiple roles. In the first role, the model is a Disagreement Planner that, given the question, image, and conflicting responses, constructs a neutral, instance-specific checklist capturing key points of disagreement—especially spatial and factual aspects tied to the image. In the second role, as a Checklist Verifier, the same model uses that checklist to systematically inspect the image and question, scoring which response satisfies each item and producing an overall grounded preference judgment. This multi-role process is trained via reinforcement learning to jointly optimize both planning and verification behaviors.

Result: Applied to Qwen3-VL 4B and 8B Instruct models, DeltaRubric produces notable performance gains as a reward model. On VL-RewardBench, it increases overall accuracy by +22.6 points for the 4B model and +18.8 points for the 8B model relative to no-rubric baselines, and it consistently outperforms standard single-step evaluators. These improvements indicate better grounding in visual evidence and more fine-grained discrimination between candidate responses.

Conclusion: Structuring multimodal evaluation into an explicit plan (disagreement-focused checklist) followed by targeted verification leads to more reliable and generalizable multimodal reward models. DeltaRubric shows that a single MLLM, trained in a multi-role RL framework, can self-generate and execute instance-specific rubrics to overcome lazy judging and language-prior biases, substantially improving alignment quality for MLLMs.

Abstract: Aligning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) requires reliable reward models, yet existing single-step evaluators can suffer from lazy judging, exploiting language priors over fine-grained visual verification. While rubric-based evaluation mitigates these biases in text-only settings, extending it to multimodal tasks is bottlenecked by the complexity of visual reasoning. The critical differences between responses often depend on instance-specific visual details. Robust evaluation requires dynamically synthesizing rubrics that isolate spatial and factual discrepancies. To address this, we introduce $\textbf{DeltaRubric}$, an approach that reformulates multimodal preference evaluation as a plan-and-execute process within a single MLLM. DeltaRubric operates in two steps: acting first as a $\textit{Disagreement Planner}$, the model generates a neutral, instance-specific verification checklist. Transitioning into a $\textit{Checklist Verifier}$, it executes these self-generated checks against the image and question to produce the final grounded judgment. We formulate DeltaRubric as a multi-role reinforcement learning problem, jointly optimizing planning and verification capabilities. Validated on Qwen3-VL 4B and 8B Instruct models, DeltaRubric achieves solid empirical gains. For instance, On VL-RewardBench, it improves base model overall accuracy by $\textbf{+22.6}$ (4B) and $\textbf{+18.8}$ (8B) points, largely outperforming standard no-rubric baselines. The results demonstrate that decomposing evaluation into structured, verifiable steps leads to more reliable and generalizable multimodal reward modeling.

</details>


### [70] [BetaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Sequential Model Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09285)
*Bingqing Liu,Wei Liu,Yuhua Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes and improves null-space-based model editing methods for large language models, revealing why they leak knowledge and degrade under sequential edits, and proposes BetaEdit, a history-aware, leakage-controlled framework that achieves better performance in massive-scale sequential editing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Null-space-based editing aims to modify model knowledge while preserving original behavior by restricting updates to a null space of existing representations. However, (1) the null space is only approximate, causing knowledge leakage (unintended changes outside the targeted edit), and (2) performance degrades severely when many edits are applied sequentially. Although history-aware strategies were observed to help empirically, the reasons were not well understood. The paper seeks to diagnose these issues theoretically and empirically, and to design an editing framework that remains reliable and effective under large numbers of edits.

Method: The authors first analyze existing null-space-based editing methods to formally and empirically expose knowledge leakage due to the use of approximate null spaces. They then study why history-aware editing—where each new edit accounts for the past edits or their effects—helps sustain both targeted edit success and general capabilities in long editing sequences. Based on these insights, they propose BetaEdit, a refined null-space-based framework that (a) explicitly controls knowledge leakage during each edit, and (b) incorporates history-aware updates into the null-space construction or constraint mechanism. They evaluate BetaEdit on three large language models and two standard model-editing benchmarks under massive sequential editing scenarios, comparing against prior editing methods.

Result: Across three large language models and two standard benchmarks, BetaEdit consistently outperforms previous null-space and other model-editing methods, particularly in the challenging regime of large-scale sequential editing. It achieves better preservation of the models’ general capabilities while maintaining or improving the success of the intended edits, and reduces the accumulation of degradation typically observed with many consecutive edits.

Conclusion: Null-space-based model editing, as previously implemented, inherently suffers from knowledge leakage and severe degradation under sequential edits because the null space is only approximated and past edits are not properly accounted for. History-aware strategies help because they adapt each new edit to the evolving model state and its modified representation spaces. Building on this understanding, BetaEdit provides a principled, history-aware null-space editing framework that controls knowledge leakage and significantly improves robustness and performance in massive-scale sequential editing of large language models.

Abstract: Null-space-based methods have garnered considerable attention in model editing by constraining updates to the null space of the pre-existing knowledge representation, thereby preserving the model's original behavior. However, in practice these methods rely on an approximate null space--leading to knowledge leakage--and further suffer from severe performance degradation during sequential editing. Recent work shows that history-aware editing strategies can empirically mitigate this decline, yet the underlying reason remains unclear. In this paper, we first expose the knowledge leakage inherent in existing null-space approaches and then analyze why history-aware updates effectively preserve both editing performance and general capabilities during long-horizon editing. Building on these insights, we propose BetaEdit, a refined framework that effectively controls the knowledge leakage and integrates history-aware updates into the null-space paradigm. Extensive experiments on three large language models across two standard benchmarks show that BetaEdit consistently outperforms prior methods in the challenging regime of massive-scale sequential editing. Code is available at: https://github.com/lbq8942/BetaEdit.

</details>


### [71] [LEAF-SQL: Level-wise Exploration with Adaptive Fine-graining for Text-to-SQL Skeleton Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09295)
*Zhao Tan,Xiping Liu,Qing Shu,Qizhi Wan,Dexi Liu,Changxuan Wan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces LEAF-SQL, a coarse-to-fine tree-search-based framework that uses hierarchical SQL skeletons plus generation and evaluation agents to improve Text-to-SQL performance, especially on complex queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Text-to-SQL methods using large language models still struggle with complex, deeply nested, or multi-clause SQL queries. Current skeleton-based methods assume a single structural hypothesis and lack progressive, stepwise reasoning, limiting their ability to explore alternative query structures and refine them adaptively. The authors aim to address these shortcomings to better handle complex query logic and improve execution accuracy on challenging benchmarks.

Method: LEAF-SQL reframes SQL skeleton prediction as a coarse-to-fine tree search. It introduces: (1) a three-level skeleton hierarchy that progressively refines query structure; (2) a Skeleton Formulation Agent that generates multiple, diverse skeleton candidates at different granularities; and (3) a Skeleton Evaluation Agent that scores and prunes these candidates to keep only promising structures. This search over skeletons yields a set of structurally diverse and adaptively refined skeletons that then guide the final SQL generation by an LLM backbone.

Result: Across extensive experiments on Text-to-SQL benchmarks, LEAF-SQL consistently boosts the performance of various LLM backbones. On the official hidden test set of the difficult BIRD benchmark, LEAF-SQL attains 71.6 execution accuracy, surpassing leading search-based and skeleton-based approaches, particularly on complex queries.

Conclusion: Recasting skeleton prediction as a coarse-to-fine tree search with hierarchical skeletons and specialized generation and evaluation agents leads to more effective exploration of SQL structures. This yields stronger intermediate representations that, in turn, significantly enhance Text-to-SQL performance on complex queries, as evidenced by state-of-the-art or superior execution accuracy on challenging benchmarks like BIRD.

Abstract: Text-to-SQL translates natural language questions into executable SQL queries, enabling intuitive database access for non-experts. While large language models achieve strong performance on Text-to-SQL with prompting, they still struggle with complex queries that involve deeply nested logic or multiple clauses. A widely used approach employs SQL skeletons--intermediate representations of query logic--to streamline generation, but existing methods are limited by their reliance on a single structural hypothesis and lack of progressive reasoning. To overcome these limitations, we propose LEAF-SQL, a novel framework that reframes skeleton prediction as a coarse-to-fine tree search process. LEAF-SQL enables systematic exploration of diverse structural hypotheses with adaptive refinement. Several key techniques are employed in LEAF-SQL: (1) a three-level skeleton hierarchy to guide the search, (2) a Skeleton Formulation Agent to generate diverse candidates, and (3) a Skeleton Evaluation Agent to efficiently prune the search space. This integrated design yields skeleton candidates that are both structurally diverse and granularity-adaptive, providing a stronger foundation for the SQL generation. Extensive experiments show that LEAF-SQL consistently improves the performance of various LLM backbones. On the official hidden test set of the challenging BIRD benchmark, our method achieves 71.6 execution accuracy, which outperforms leading search-based and skeleton-based methods, affirming its effectiveness for complex queries.

</details>


### [72] [Mem-W: Latent Memory-Native GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09317)
*Guibin Zhang,Yaohui Ling,Fanci Meng,Kun Wang,Shuicheng Yan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Mem-W is a GUI agent architecture that treats memory as native latent context rather than external text, compressing past trajectories into compact memory tokens to improve long-horizon control on web and mobile benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GUI agents rely on external, human-readable memory (text summaries, structured logs) that must be re-encoded by the policy, causing a representational mismatch and limiting long-horizon performance. The authors want a memory mechanism that is native to the latent embedding space in which the GUI policy actually operates, enabling better use of historical experience and in-session context.

Method: They propose Mem-W, a family of GUI agents that integrates memory directly into the agent’s continuous embedding sequence. A shared trajectory-to-latent compressor turns both long-term experiential histories and short-term working memory segments into compact memory tokens. These tokens are concatenated with the current GUI observation and local context, so the policy reads and writes to memory via the same latent interface it uses for perception and action. Training uses self-distillation and outcome-aware supervision to ensure the compressed memory preserves decision-relevant information while filtering out irrelevant details.

Result: On four web and mobile navigation benchmarks, Mem-W, when plugged into different backbone models, consistently outperforms both non-memory and traditional memory-augmented baselines, with improvements up to +30.0 points, demonstrating the benefits of latent-native memory for long-horizon GUI tasks.

Conclusion: Treating memory as part of an agent’s continuous latent context, rather than as an external symbolic artifact, yields significant gains for GUI agents on long-horizon tasks. Latent-context-native memory like Mem-W appears to be a scalable, architecture-agnostic foundation for more capable GUI agents that can better leverage past experience and ongoing task progress.

Abstract: GUI agents are beginning to operate the web, mobile, and desktop as interactive worlds, where successful control depends on carrying forward visual, procedural, and task-level evidence beyond the fleeting present screen. Yet most agents still treat memory as an external, human-readable artifact: histories are summarized, categorized, retrieved, and reinserted as text or structured records before being encoded again by the policy. This creates a mismatch between the representational form in which experience is stored and the latent embedding sequence over which modern GUI policies actually act. We introduce Mem-W, a series of latent-memory-native GUI agents that treat memory as part of the agent's continuous context rather than as an auxiliary symbolic scaffold. Mem-W weaves both historical trajectories (as experiential memory) and in-session segments (as working memory) into compact memory tokens through a shared trajectory-to-latent compressor. These tokens are woven with the current GUI observation and local context into one continuous embedding sequence, allowing the agent to read successes, failures, and unfinished progress through the same machine-native interface. Mem-W is trained with self-distillation and outcome-aware supervision to preserve decision-relevant state while filtering memory toward evidence that truly supports task success. Across four web and mobile navigation benchmarks, Mem-W consistently improves diverse backbones and memory-enhanced baselines, with gains of up to $+30.0$, suggesting that latent-context-native memory can serve as a scalable foundation for long-horizon GUI agency.

</details>


### [73] [Test-Time Speculation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09329)
*Avinash Kumar,Sujay Sanghavi,Poulami Das*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Test-Time Speculation (TTS), an online distillation method that adapts speculative decoding draft models during inference to maintain higher acceptance lengths, significantly improving speedups for long generations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods rely on an offline-trained draft model whose acceptance length drops sharply on long generations because they are trained on short sequences but used far out-of-distribution at inference time. As generation length grows, the draft’s predictions mismatch the target model more often, reducing acceptance length close to 1 and eliminating speedup, especially for long-response tasks. There is a need for a way to preserve or improve acceptance length during long generations without incurring extra inference cost.

Method: The authors introduce Test-Time Speculation (TTS), an online distillation framework that updates the draft model during inference. In speculative decoding, each draft token is already verified by the target model; TTS interprets this as a free supervision signal and performs lightweight updates to the draft (student) to better match the target (teacher) across successive speculation rounds. This continuous adaptation is done at test time, with no extra forward passes of the target model beyond standard verification, improving the draft’s alignment with the target as the sequence grows.

Result: Across several model families (Qwen-3, Qwen-3.5, Llama3.1), TTS achieves up to 72% higher acceptance length than state-of-the-art speculators and 41% improvement on average, with the gains becoming larger as generation length increases. This leads to better practical speedups for long outputs compared to prior methods like DFlash, EAGLE-3, and PARD, whose acceptance lengths degrade toward 1 for long generations.

Conclusion: Test-Time Speculation effectively mitigates the degradation of speculative decoding performance on long sequences by treating verification calls to the target model as supervision to continually distill and adapt the draft model. This online approach maintains substantially higher acceptance lengths than existing offline-trained speculators, especially on long generations, thereby restoring and scaling inference speedups for long-form tasks.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by using a fast draft model to generate tokens and a more accurate target model to verify them. Its performance depends on the $\textit{acceptance length}$, or number of draft tokens accepted by the target. Our studies show that the acceptance length of even state-of-the-art speculators, like DFlash, EAGLE-3 and PARD degrade with generation length, reaching values close to 1 (i.e. no speedup) within just a few thousand output tokens, making speculators ineffective for long-response tasks. Acceptance lengths decline because most speculators are trained offline on short sequences, but are forced to match the target model on much longer outputs at inference, well beyond their training distribution.
  To address this issue, we propose $\textit{Test-Time Speculation (TTS)}$, an online distillation approach that continuously adapts the speculator at test-time. TTS leverages the key insight that the token verification step already invokes the target model for each draft token, providing the training signal needed to adapt the draft at no additional cost. Treating the draft as the student and the target as a teacher, TTS adjusts the draft over several speculation rounds, with each update improving the draft's accuracy as generation proceeds. Our results across multiple models from the Qwen-3, Qwen-3.5, and Llama3.1 families show that TTS improves acceptance lengths over state-of-the-art speculators by up to $72\%$ and $41\%$ on average, with the benefits scaling with increased generation lengths.

</details>


### [74] [RuPLaR : Efficient Latent Compression of LLM Reasoning Chains with Rule-Based Priors From Multi-Step to One-Step](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09346)
*Xiaocheng Luo,Kang Wang,Zaifu Zhan,Yuechi Zhou,Xiangyu Duan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes One-Model One-Step, a compressed latent chain-of-thought framework that uses rule-based priors to guide a single LLM to perform latent reasoning in one stage, improving accuracy and reducing token usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Natural-language Chain-of-Thought improves interpretability but is inefficient and limited in expressiveness. Existing latent CoT methods, which move reasoning into continuous latent space, often rely on multi-step or multi-model pipelines that introduce structural complexity, error propagation, and coordination overhead. The authors want a simpler, more efficient, and more accurate latent reasoning approach that avoids cascaded processes and heavy token usage.

Method: They design One-Model One-Step, a compression framework called RuPLaR (Latent Reasoning with Rule-Based Priors). A single LLM is trained end-to-end to autonomously generate latent reasoning tokens in one step, rather than via cascaded models or stages. Rule-based prior probability distributions guide the generation of these latent tokens. The training objective has three components: (1) cross-entropy loss to ensure the final answer matches ground truth (answer consistency), (2) a KL-divergence term that forces the soft latent tokens to align with the rule-based priors (Soft Thinking constraint), and (3) a problem-thought semantic alignment constraint that encourages the representation of the problem and the latent reasoning tokens to be semantically aligned in the model’s representation space.

Result: Experiments across relevant reasoning benchmarks show that the proposed compression framework improves accuracy by 11.1% compared to existing latent CoT methods, while using far fewer tokens. This demonstrates that the one-model, one-step latent reasoning guided by rule-based priors can be both more effective and more efficient than prior multi-step or multi-model latent CoT approaches.

Conclusion: One-Model One-Step with RuPLaR offers a simpler and more efficient latent CoT paradigm by removing cascaded processes and inter-model dependencies, instead using a single LLM trained with rule-based priors and joint objectives. The method yields higher accuracy and lower token consumption, indicating that compressed, rule-guided latent reasoning can be a powerful and extensible alternative to traditional natural-language CoT and earlier latent CoT frameworks.

Abstract: The Chain-of-Thought (CoT) paradigm, while enhancing the interpretability of Large Language Models (LLMs), is constrained by the inefficiencies and expressive limits of natural language. Latent Chain-of-Thought (latent CoT) reasoning, which operates in a continuous latent space, offers a promising alternative but faces challenges from structural complexities in existing multi-step or multi-model paradigms, such as error propagation and coordination overhead. In this paper, we introduce One-Model One-Step, a novel compression framework for Latent Reasoning with Rule-Based Priors(RuPLaR) to address this challenge. Our method trains an LLM to autonomously generate latent reasoning tokens in a single training stage, guided by rule-based prior probability distributions, thereby eliminating cascaded processes and inter-model dependencies. To ensure reasoning quality, we design a joint training objective that enforces answer consistency via cross-entropy, aligns soft tokens with rule-based priors via KL divergence (the Soft Thinking constraint), and adds a problem-thought semantic alignment constraint in the representation space. Extensive experiments show that our compression framework not only improves accuracy by 11.1% over existing latent CoT methods but also achieves this with minimal token usage, underscoring its effectiveness and extensibility. Code: https://github.com/xiaocen-luo/RuPLaR.

</details>


### [75] [HOME-KGQA: A Benchmark Dataset for Multimodal Knowledge Graph Question Answering on Household Daily Activities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09348)
*Shusaku Egami,Aoi Ohta,Tomoki Tsujimura,Masaki Asada,Tatsuya Ishigaki,Ken Fukuda,Masahiro Hamasaki,Hiroya Takamura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces HOME-KGQA, a new, more realistic and challenging multimodal knowledge-graph question answering benchmark focused on household activities, revealing current LLM-based KGQA methods perform poorly on it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KGQA benchmarks are mostly encyclopedic, single-modality, and lack detailed spatiotemporal information, making them poorly suited to real-world Embodied AI scenarios involving everyday household activities. There is a need for a dataset that better reflects these complex, multimodal, spatiotemporal reasoning demands to properly evaluate and advance KGQA systems.

Method: Construct a multimodal knowledge graph centered on daily household activities, then build a KGQA benchmark (HOME-KGQA) consisting of complex, multi-hop natural language questions aligned with graph database query languages. The questions are designed to require multi-level spatiotemporal reasoning, multimodal grounding, and aggregate operations, going beyond what existing benchmarks demand. The authors then evaluate state-of-the-art LLM-based KGQA methods on this new dataset and compare performance to that on existing benchmarks.

Result: HOME-KGQA contains more challenging questions than existing KGQA datasets, especially around spatiotemporal reasoning, multimodal grounding, and aggregation. When evaluated on HOME-KGQA, LLM-based KGQA methods perform significantly worse than they do on traditional benchmarks, failing to reach comparable performance levels.

Conclusion: The HOME-KGQA benchmark exposes substantial limitations of current LLM-based KGQA approaches when faced with realistic, multimodal, spatiotemporally rich household activity scenarios. This indicates that new methods and improvements are required before KGQA can be reliably deployed in real-world Embodied AI settings. The dataset is publicly released to foster further research.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) provide flexible natural language processing capabilities, while knowledge graphs (KGs) offer explicit and structured knowledge. Integrating these two in a complementary manner enables the development of reliable and verifiable AI systems. In particular, knowledge graph question answering (KGQA) has attracted attention as a means to reduce LLM hallucinations and to leverage knowledge beyond the training data. However, existing KGQA benchmark datasets are biased toward encyclopedic knowledge, limited to a single modality, and lack fine-grained spatiotemporal data, which limits their applicability to real-world scenarios targeted by Embodied AI. We introduce HOME-KGQA, a novel KGQA benchmark dataset built on a multimodal KG of daily household activities. HOME-KGQA consists of complex, multi-hop natural language questions paired with graph database query languages. Compared to existing benchmarks, it includes more challenging questions that involve multi-level spatiotemporal reasoning, multimodal grounding, and aggregate functions. Experimental results show that the LLM-based KGQA methods fail to achieve performance comparable to that on existing datasets when evaluated on HOME-KGQA. This highlights significant challenges that should be addressed for the real-world deployment of KGQA systems. Our dataset is available at https://github.com/aistairc/home-kgqa

</details>


### [76] [Cross-Cultural Transfer of Emoji Semantics and Sentiment in Financial Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09414)
*Ahmed Mahrous,Roberto Di Pietro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether emojis carry stable, transferable sentiment signals in financial social media and if they can support zero-shot sentiment transfer across languages, platforms, and asset communities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although emojis are pervasive in online financial discussions, it is unclear whether their usage and sentiment meaning are consistent enough across languages and communities to be useful for cross-domain or zero-shot sentiment analysis in finance. Existing sentiment models are often language- or platform-specific and may not generalize well across markets.

Method: The authors compile large multilingual corpora of Twitter and StockTwits financial posts in four languages and across different asset communities. They analyze cross-community divergence in emoji frequency, semantics, and sentiment polarity. They then train sentiment models under three input setups—emoji-only, text-only, and text+emoji—and evaluate zero-shot sentiment transfer performance across languages, platforms, and asset communities.

Result: Emoji usage frequencies vary substantially, especially across languages, but the underlying semantics and sentiment polarity of emojis remain largely stable across financial communities. Sentiment models transfer relatively well across different assets, with minimal performance loss, but cross-language transfer is much more difficult. Incorporating emojis into the models consistently narrows transfer performance gaps compared to using text alone.

Conclusion: Financial discussions share a partially common "emoji code": while specific emojis may be used at different rates, their meanings and sentiment orientations are broadly consistent across communities. Emojis therefore act as compact, language-independent sentiment carriers that enhance generalization of sentiment models across platforms and markets, making them valuable for zero-shot and cross-domain financial sentiment analysis.

Abstract: Emojis are widely used in online financial communication, but it is unclear whether they provide transferable sentiment signals across languages, platforms, and asset communities. This study examines the extent to which emoji usage, semantics, and sentiment polarity remain stable across financial communities, and how these layers influence zero-shot sentiment transfer. Using large corpora of Twitter and StockTwits posts in four languages, we measure cross-community divergence and evaluate sentiment models trained under emoji-only, text-only, and text+emoji inputs.
  We find that emoji frequencies differ across communities, especially across languages, but their semantics and sentiment polarity are largely stable. Cross-asset transferability shows minimal degradation, while cross-language transfer remains the most challenging. Including emojis consistently reduces transfer gaps relative to text-only models. These results indicate that financial communication exhibits a partially shared ``emoji code,'' and that emojis provide compact, language-independent sentiment cues that improve model generalization across markets and platforms.

</details>


### [77] [Perception Without Engagement: Dissecting the Causal Discovery Deficit in LMMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09422)
*Jiafeng Liang,Zhihao Zhu,Zihan Zhang,Baoqi Ren,Shixin Jiang,Runxuan Liu,Tao Ren,Ming Liu,See-Kiong Ng,Bing Qin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper diagnoses why large multimodal models rely too much on text instead of video for causal reasoning, introduces a perturbation-based benchmark (ProCauEval), and proposes a reinforcement-learning method (ADPO) to force models to use visual evidence rather than textual shortcuts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large multimodal models perform well on standard video understanding benchmarks but fail at robust causal discovery, often relying on textual priors instead of actual visual content. Current evaluations mostly measure end accuracy and do not reveal *how* or *why* models fail, especially their reliance on shortcuts. There is a need for a diagnostic framework that disentangles visual and textual contributions to model decisions and for training methods that actively reduce shortcut dependence.

Method: 1) ProCauEval: a perturbation-based evaluation protocol with five controlled configurations that systematically manipulate visual and textual inputs. By corrupting or altering either modality, the protocol can measure how much models depend on true visual evidence vs. textual priors and characterize failure modes. 2) Empirical study: Apply ProCauEval to 17 mainstream large multimodal models to quantify their modality usage, robustness, and fragility to perturbations. 3) ADPO (Anti-Distillation Policy Optimization): a reinforcement learning framework extending GRPO. It constructs a prior-only counterfactual teacher by corrupting visual inputs, then explicitly optimizes the policy to diverge from this teacher—maximizing the KL divergence between outputs with original vs. corrupted visuals—so that the model is pushed to ground its reasoning in visual information.

Result: Using ProCauEval, the authors find that LMMs actually perceive visual content correctly but underuse it for causal reasoning, leaning heavily on textual priors. Stronger post-training intensifies this reliance, and models with higher baseline scores are more brittle when visual or textual inputs are perturbed. With ADPO training, models show increased reliance on visual evidence (higher visual engagement under ProCauEval) without losing general comprehension ability, indicating more robust causal discovery behavior.

Conclusion: The work reveals that current LMMs’ causal reasoning deficits stem less from poor perception and more from overreliance on textual priors. ProCauEval provides a mechanism-level diagnostic tool for disentangling visual and textual contributions. ADPO demonstrates that reinforcement learning with negative teacher alignment can actively counteract shortcut learning by pushing models away from prior-only behaviors, improving visual grounding in causal reasoning. This offers an initial pathway toward more reliable causal discovery in multimodal models.

Abstract: Although Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have achieved strong performance on general video understanding, their susceptibility to textual prior shortcuts during causal discovery has been recognized as a critical deficit. The underlying mechanisms of this phenomenon remain incompletely understood, as existing benchmarks only measure response accuracy without revealing the sources and extent of the deficit. We introduce ProCauEval, a perturbation-based evaluation protocol that shifts from outcome assessment to mechanism diagnosis, probing causal discovery through five controlled configurations that systematically manipulate visual and textual modalities to decompose their respective contributions to model behavior and dissect the failure modes. Evaluating 17 mainstream LMMs, we find that models faithfully perceive video content yet systematically underexploit it during causal reasoning. We further observe that stronger post-training amplifies rather than mitigates textual prior reliance, and that higher baseline performance correlates with greater fragility under perturbation. To address these, we propose Anti-Distillation Policy Optimization (ADPO), a reinforcement learning framework built on negative teacher alignment, which augments GRPO by explicitly pushing the policy away from a prior-only counterfactual teacher induced by visual corruption. Specifically, ADPO maximizes the divergence between the policy distributions conditioned on the original and visually corrupted inputs, thereby forcing the model to ground its reasoning in visual evidence rather than textual shortcuts. Extensive experiments show that ADPO improves visual engagement without sacrificing fundamental comprehension, thus offering a preliminary step toward reliable causal discovery.

</details>


### [78] [PumpSense: Real-Time Detection and Target Extraction of Crypto Pump-and-Dumps on Telegram](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09431)
*Ahmed Mahrous,Roberto Di Pietro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper builds a labeled Telegram dataset and shows that pump-and-dump announcements and their target coins/exchanges can be detected and extracted in (near) real time using ML and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cryptocurrency pump-and-dump schemes on Telegram harm market integrity, but existing detection approaches are slow and rely on market data, and there are no public, message-level labeled datasets or benchmarks for detecting the announcements and identifying the targeted assets.

Method: The authors collect and manually label over 280,000 Telegram posts from 39 pump-organizing groups, marking pump announcements and their target coin and exchange. They define two tasks: (1) real-time detection of pump-announcement messages, and (2) extraction of the mentioned cryptocurrency and exchange. For task (1) they evaluate a LightGBM classifier and a transformer-based BGE-M3 model; for task (2) they compare rule-based extraction with LLM-based extraction.

Result: On the detection task, LightGBM achieves F1=0.79 with 9.4 s latency per sample, whereas BGE-M3 reaches F1=0.83 with 50 ms latency per sample and can be deployed to operate in microseconds per message window on commodity hardware. On the extraction task, rule-based methods perform poorly due to ticker ambiguity, while LLM-based methods reach an accuracy of 0.91, establishing a strong baseline.

Conclusion: Text-based analysis of Telegram coordination messages enables near-instant detection of pump-and-dump announcements and accurate extraction of the targeted coin and exchange, outperforming market-data-only approaches and prior rule-based extraction methods. The released labeled dataset and benchmarks provide a foundation for future research on real-time market manipulation detection.

Abstract: Cryptocurrency pump-and-dump schemes coordinated via Telegram threaten market integrity. However, existing research addressing this specific threat has not yet produced solutions that combine reliable results with fast response. This is in part due to the absence of publicly available, message-level labeled data, as well as design choices. In this paper, we address both issues. In particular, we introduce a corpus of over 280,000 Telegram posts from 39 pump-organizing groups, all manually reviewed to identify 2,246 pump announcements and their targeted cryptocurrency and exchange. Leveraging this dataset, we define two tasks: real-time pump-announcement detection and target cryptocurrency/exchange extraction. For detection, we compare two machine-learning models: a lightweight tree-based LightGBM classifier (F1=0.79, latency=9.4 s/sample) and a transformer-based BGE-M3 (F1=0.83, latency=50 ms/sample). With our proposed approach, we show that message analysis can achieve near-instant pump detection at the level of individual Telegram message windows. Unlike prior work that relies purely on market data and typically detects pumps tens of seconds after abnormal trading activity is observed, our method operates directly on the coordination messages themselves and can be evaluated in microseconds per window on commodity hardware. To our knowledge, we also establish the first benchmark for manipulated coin and exchange extraction. We demonstrate that traditional rule-based extraction methods, widely relied upon in prior literature, are ineffective due to ticker ambiguity. In contrast, LLMs achieve the highest accuracy with a score of 0.91.

</details>


### [79] [Key Coverage Matters: Semi-Structured Extraction of OCR Clinical Reports](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09440)
*Yu Wang,Yingyun Li,Ying Qin,Haiyang Qian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a key-conditioned extractive QA framework over OCRed clinical reports and shows that expanding a canonical key inventory (key coverage) is critical to reliable structured data extraction across heterogeneous hospitals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical reports are scattered across institutions, often only available as paper or scanned documents that are hard to integrate into EHRs, limiting longitudinal care, real‑world studies, and trial matching. Existing OCR and information extraction approaches struggle due to noisy text, heterogeneous formats, and the need for low-cost on‑premise deployment. There is a need for a robust, generalizable way to extract structured information from semi‑structured OCRed clinical reports without assuming a fixed schema.

Method: They cast the task as canonical key‑conditioned extractive question answering over OCR-derived text. Because important fields (keys) are open‑ended and not known a priori, they construct and maintain a canonical key inventory via iterative key mining from data, normalization, clustering of key variants/aliases, and lightweight human verification. They propose a key coverage metric to quantify how completely this inventory captures the keys present in reports. A 0.2B-parameter BERT-based model is trained to answer key-conditioned extraction queries, and its performance is evaluated as key coverage increases. The approach is compared with a fine‑tuned Qwen3-0.6B baseline. Although experiments use Chinese reports, the pipeline relies on language-agnostic key–value structure and assumes appropriate alias mapping for other languages.

Result: On real-world reports from over 20 hospitals, model performance improves monotonically as key coverage grows. With the Top-90 canonical keys covered, the BERT-based extractor attains F1 scores of 0.839 (exact match) and 0.893 (boundary-tolerant). At this Top-90 coverage level, it also outperforms a fine-tuned Qwen3-0.6B baseline under exact match, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed key-inventory and extraction framework in noisy OCR settings.

Conclusion: Key coverage of a well-constructed canonical key inventory is the primary driver of end-to-end performance for key-conditioned extraction from OCRed clinical reports. A relatively small 0.2B BERT model, combined with iterative key mining, normalization, clustering, and light human review, can reliably extract fields across heterogeneous hospitals once sufficient key coverage is achieved. Although demonstrated on Chinese data, the method is fundamentally language-agnostic for semi-structured clinical documents and can be adapted to other healthcare settings by building suitable canonical key inventories and alias mappings.

Abstract: Clinical reports are often fragmented across healthcare institutions because privacy regulations and data silos limit direct information sharing. When patients seek care at a different hospital, they often carry paper or scanned reports from prior visits. This hinders EHR integration and longitudinal review, and downstream applications that depend on more complete patient records, such as patient management, follow-up care, real-world studies, and clinical-trial matching. Although OCR can digitize such reports, reliable extraction remains challenging because clinical documents are heterogeneous, OCR text is noisy, and many healthcare settings require low-cost on-premise deployment. We formulate this problem as canonical key-conditioned extractive question answering over OCR-derived clinical reports. Because the key fields are neither fixed nor known in advance, the key space is open. We maintain a canonical key inventory through iterative key mining, normalization, clustering, and lightweight human verification, and introduce key coverage as a metric to quantify inventory completeness. Using a 0.2B BERT-based model, experiments on real-world reports from more than 20 hospitals show performance improves monotonically with key coverage. The model achieves F1 scores of 0.839 and 0.893 under exact match and boundary-tolerant matching, respectively, once the Top-90 canonical keys are covered. These results show that key coverage is a dominant factor for end-to-end performance. At Top-90 coverage, our model outperforms a fine-tuned Qwen3-0.6B baseline under exact match. Although our annotated corpus is Chinese, the method relies on the language-agnostic key-value organization of semi-structured clinical reports and can be adapted to other settings given an appropriate canonical key inventory and alias mapping.

</details>


### [80] [Beyond Position Bias: Shifting Context Compression from Position-Driven to Semantic-Driven](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09463)
*Jiwei Tang,Zhijing Huang,Xinyu Zhang,Chen Jason Zhang,Jianxing Yu,Libin Zheng,Rui Meng,Jian Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SeCo, a semantic-driven context compression method for LLMs that mitigates position bias and improves efficiency and robustness in long-context scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context use of LLMs is computationally expensive and often redundant. Existing soft prompt compression methods reduce cost but are limited by position bias, as they compress based on token positions or contiguous spans, causing unstable performance and loss of semantic coherence. The paper is motivated by the need for a context compression method that respects semantic structure rather than physical token layout, thereby improving both efficiency and reliability.

Method: The authors introduce Semantic Consistency Context Compression (SeCo), which reframes context compression as a semantic-driven process. Instead of inserting learnable tokens at fixed positions or grouping by contiguous token segments, SeCo selects query-relevant tokens as semantic centers in embedding space. Remaining tokens are then merged into these centers using a consistency-weighted scheme that emphasizes semantic similarity. This yields compact embeddings that better preserve semantic relationships while discarding redundant information and avoiding position bias.

Result: On 14 benchmarks and across two backbone LLMs, SeCo outperforms prior soft prompt and context compression approaches. It delivers better downstream task performance, lower inference latency, and stronger robustness on out-of-domain evaluations, indicating more stable and generalizable compression behavior.

Conclusion: SeCo demonstrates that context compression for LLMs is more effective when driven by semantic structure rather than positional layout. By anchoring compression in query-relevant semantic centers and merging other tokens via consistency-weighted aggregation, SeCo reduces computational overhead while preserving crucial information, leading to state-of-the-art accuracy, efficiency gains, and improved robustness in long-context settings.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across diverse tasks. However, their deployment in long-context scenarios faces high computational overhead and information redundancy. While soft prompt compression has emerged as a promising way to mitigate these costs by compressing sequences into compact embeddings, existing paradigms remain fundamentally constrained by position bias: they primarily rely on learnable tokens insertion at fixed positions or group tokens according to their physical token layout, thereby inducing performance instability and semantic fragmentation. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose Semantic Consistency Context Compression (SeCo), a method that shifts context compression from position-driven to semantic-driven. Rather than constraint by physical token layout, SeCo dynamically anchors compression directly in the semantic space by selecting query-relevant tokens as semantic centers and aggregating remaining tokens via consistency-weighted merging. This design inherently preserves semantic consistency while eliminating position bias. Extensive experiments on 14 benchmarks across two backbone models demonstrate that SeCo consistently shows superiority in downstream tasks, inference latency, and out-of-domain robustness. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/seco-EE5E.

</details>


### [81] [FinMoji: A Framework for Emoji-driven Sentiment Analysis in Financial Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09469)
*Ahmed Mahrous,Roberto Di Pietro*

Main category: cs.CL
