TL;DR: Study on leveraging emojis alone vs. text+emojis for financial sentiment analysis on StockTwits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emojis are compact, increasingly common markers of sentiment in online communication. In finance, fast and efficient sentiment signals can be valuable for predicting market trends, especially in time-sensitive trading settings. The paper wants to know if emojis alone are reliable proxies for investor sentiment and how they compare with traditional text-based approaches.

Method: Use a large, balanced dataset (~528k StockTwits posts containing emojis). Train and evaluate emoji-only, text-only, and text+emoji sentiment classifiers using logistic regression and transformer-based models. Compare performance via F1 scores, and analyze computational efficiency and data requirements. Examine which individual emojis and emoji pairs are most predictive. Compare emoji usage patterns in financial vs. general social media data.

Result: Emoji-only models reach about 0.75 F1, while combined text+emoji models reach about 0.88 F1. Emoji-only models are much more computationally efficient. Certain emojis and emoji pairs have over 90% accuracy in predicting bullish vs. bearish sentiment. There are statistically significant differences in emoji usage between financial and general social media contexts.

Conclusion: Emojis alone provide a reasonably strong but imperfect proxy for financial sentiment; text+emoji models perform best but are costlier. Emoji-only models are attractive when computational or time constraints matter, such as high-frequency trading applications. Domain-specific modeling is crucial because financial emoji usage differs markedly from general social media, so generic emoji sentiment resources do not transfer well.

Abstract: This paper explores the use of emojis in financial sentiment analysis, focusing on the social media platform StockTwits. Emojis, increasingly prevalent in digital communication, have potential as compact indicators of investor sentiment, which can be critical for predicting market trends. Our study examines whether emojis alone can serve as reliable proxies for financial sentiment and how they compare with traditional text-based analysis. We conduct a series of experiments using logistic regression and transformer models. We further analyze the performance, computational efficiency, and data requirements of emoji-based versus text-based sentiment classification. Using a balanced dataset of about 528,000 emoji-containing StockTwits posts, we find that emoji-only models achieve F1 approximately 0.75, lower than text-emoji combined models, which achieve F1 approximately 0.88, but with far lower computational cost. This is a useful feature in time-sensitive settings such as high-frequency trading. Furthermore, certain emojis and emoji pairs exhibit strong predictive power for market sentiment, demonstrating over 90 percent accuracy in predicting bullish or bearish trends. Finally, our research reveals large statistical differences in emoji usage between financial and general social media contexts, stressing the need for domain-specific sentiment analysis models.

</details>


### [82] [Align and Shine: Building High-Quality Sentence-Aligned Corpora for Multilingual Text Simplification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09476)
*Kenji Hilasaca,Nouran Khallaf,Serge Sharoff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build and release a multilingual sentence-aligned corpus for text simplification using crowd-sourced data from comparable documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality, large-scale datasets for training and evaluating text simplification systems are scarce, especially for non-English languages. This lack of resources limits progress in simplifying texts for diverse readers such as language learners and people with limited literacy. The authors aim to address this resource gap.

Method: They collect crowd-sourced text simplifications from comparable corpora in several languages (Catalan, English, French, Italian, Spanish). Starting from document-level comparable texts, they design and apply mechanisms to align sentences across original and simplified versions, producing sentence-level aligned pairs that can be used for model training and evaluation.

Result: They obtain a multilingual corpus of aligned original–simplified sentence pairs derived from crowd-sourced data and comparable corpora. The paper reports on the experimental setup and alignment mechanisms and makes the resulting dataset publicly available for the community.

Conclusion: Crowd-sourced simplifications combined with sentence-level alignment from comparable documents provide a viable way to build high-quality, multilingual resources for text simplification. The released dataset can support both training and testing of simplification models in several languages beyond English.

Abstract: Text simplification plays a crucial role in improving the accessibility and comprehensibility of written information for diverse audiences, including language learners and readers with limited literacy. Despite its importance, large-scale, high-quality datasets for training and evaluating text simplification models remain scarce for languages other than English. This paper reports an experimental study on the collection and processing of crowd-sourced simplification data from comparable corpora to construct a corpus suitable for both training and testing text simplification systems across multiple languages (Catalan, English, French, Italian and Spanish). We report mechanisms for sentence-level alignment from document-level data. The resulting dataset of the aligned sentence pairs is publicly available.

</details>


### [83] [A Cognitively Grounded Bayesian Framework for Misinformation Susceptibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09483)
*Pranava Madhyastha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Bounded Pragmatic Listener (BPL), a Bayesian, cognitively grounded model that extends Rational Speech Act theory with bounded rationality constraints to model susceptibility to different forms of information disorder and achieves competitive performance on misinformation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand and predict human susceptibility to misinformation, disinformation, and malinformation, and to explain annotator disagreement, by using a cognitively plausible model that incorporates known limits of human reasoning instead of assuming fully rational listeners.

Method: They extend Rational Speech Act (RSA) theory into a new framework called Bounded Pragmatic Listener (BPL) by adding three bounded rationality components: (a) a recursion depth bound to model limits in pragmatic recursive reasoning linked to working memory; (b) a prior compression parameter to represent information bottlenecks in how people store or access prior beliefs; and (c) an availability sample size to implement importance sampling over salient examples, capturing availability biases. They then instantiate this Bayesian listener model for veracity classification tasks and apply it to misinformation datasets (LIAR and MultiFC) to test predictions about susceptibility and disagreements.

Result: On the LIAR and MultiFC benchmarks, BPL attains competitive performance in veracity classification compared to existing approaches. The experiments also provide empirical evidence for the "depth-mismatch paradox" predicted by pragmatic theories—situations where mismatches in reasoning depth between speaker and listener affect interpretation and susceptibility to misinformation.

Conclusion: The BPL framework offers a cognitively grounded, Bayesian way to model bounded pragmatic reasoning in the context of information disorder. By integrating limits on recursion depth, prior representation, and availability-based sampling, it can both perform competitively on factuality classification tasks and generate testable predictions about who is more vulnerable to various types of misleading information and why annotators may disagree.

Abstract: In this (work in progress) paper, we present Bounded Pragmatic Listener (or BPL), a cognitively grounded Bayesian framework for modelling susceptibility to information disorder. BPL extends Rational Speech Act theory with three cognitively motivated bounds derived from the bounded rationality literature with a) a recursion depth bound (that emphasises working memory limits);b) a prior compression parameter (which is oriented at capturing information bottleneck); and c) an availability sample size (that operationalises importance sampling with saliency-weighted proposals). This allows us to test predictions about misinformation susceptibility, annotator disagreement, and the differential vulnerability to mis-, dis-, and mal-information as defined in the Information Disorder framework. We validate BPL on the LIAR and MultiFC benchmarks showcasing competitive veracity classification and experimental support for the depth-mismatch paradox.

</details>


### [84] [APCD: Adaptive Path-Contrastive Decoding for Reliable Large Language Model Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09492)
*Tianyu Zheng,Hong Wu,Jiaji Zhong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Adaptive Path-Contrastive Decoding (APCD), a multi-path decoding strategy for large language models that adaptively decides when to branch and how paths should interact, reducing hallucinations and improving factual accuracy without large efficiency costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding in large language models can accumulate errors: an early mistaken token can mislead all subsequent generation, leading to hallucinations. While multi-path decoding can mitigate this by exploring multiple continuations, current approaches do not have a principled way to decide when to create new decoding paths or how these paths should influence each other, limiting their effectiveness and efficiency. The paper aims to provide a more principled, adaptive framework to explore multiple decoding trajectories while controlling their interactions, thereby improving reliability and factuality.

Method: The authors propose Adaptive Path-Contrastive Decoding (APCD), which is composed of two main components. (1) Entropy-Driven Path Expansion: the model monitors predictive uncertainty by computing the Shannon entropy over the top candidate tokens at each step. Branching into multiple decoding paths is delayed until this entropy indicates that there are several plausible continuations, ensuring that multi-path exploration is used only when necessary. (2) Divergence-Aware Path Contrast: once multiple paths are active, the method explicitly encourages them to follow diverse reasoning trajectories while modulating how much they influence each other. As the predictive distributions across paths diverge, the algorithm dynamically reduces inter-path influence, allowing distinct hypotheses to develop rather than collapsing into similar outputs. Together, these procedures define a multi-path decoding algorithm that adaptively explores and contrasts trajectories during generation.

Result: On eight benchmarks, APCD achieves higher factual accuracy compared to baseline decoding methods, while preserving decoding efficiency. This indicates that the proposed adaptive branching and path-contrast mechanisms can reduce hallucinations and improve reliability without significantly increasing computational cost.

Conclusion: Adaptive Path-Contrastive Decoding provides a principled, adaptive framework for multi-path decoding in LLMs by deciding when to branch based on entropy and how to regulate inter-path interactions based on divergence. This approach improves factual robustness and reliability of generated text with limited additional computational overhead, offering a practical decoding enhancement for large language models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often suffer from hallucinations due to error accumulation in autoregressive decoding, where suboptimal early token choices misguide subsequent generation. Although multi-path decoding can improve robustness by exploring alternative trajectories, existing methods lack principled strategies for determining when to branch and how to regulate inter-path interactions. We propose Adaptive Path-Contrastive Decoding (APCD), a multi-path decoding framework that improves output reliability through adaptive exploration and controlled path interaction. APCD consists of two components: (1) Entropy-Driven Path Expansion, which delays branching until predictive uncertainty - measured by Shannon entropy over top candidate tokens - indicates multiple plausible continuations; and (2) Divergence-Aware Path Contrast, which encourages diverse reasoning trajectories while dynamically attenuating inter-path influence as prediction distributions diverge. Experiments on eight benchmarks demonstrate improved factual accuracy while maintaining decoding efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/zty-king/APCD.

</details>


### [85] [Beyond Language: Format-Agnostic Reasoning Subspaces in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09496)
*Aojie Yuan,Zhiyuan Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They test whether different symbolic formats (prose, math, code) share a common internal reasoning space in small LLMs, and find a low-dimensional, format-agnostic subspace that carries most of the conceptual computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can express the same reasoning in many surface forms, but it is unclear if the model internally uses a shared representation or distinct ones for each format. Understanding this matters for mechanistic interpretability, transfer between formats, and theories of abstract reasoning in neural networks.

Method: They build TriForm, a benchmark with multiple abstract concepts expressed in three main formats (English prose, Python code, mathematical notation), expanded to six forms and multiple instances. They probe five LLMs from three architecture families using: (1) permutation-corrected representational similarity analysis to compare internal geometry across formats; (2) cross-form probing, training linear probes on one form and testing on others; and (3) activation patching across formats. They then use concept-centroid PCA to identify a low-dimensional subspace that emphasizes conceptual variance and deemphasizes format variance, and analyze its generalization and cross-model alignment using CCA.

Result: They find a mid-layer subspace where representations for the same concept align across formats, evidencing a format-agnostic reasoning space (FARS). Concept-centroid PCA yields a ~10D subspace that strongly enhances concept structure while nearly eliminating format signals. When they replace only these 10 dimensions across forms, models preserve 90–96% of original outputs, outperforming full-activation replacement and standard PCA-based subspaces; ablating this subspace selectively disrupts reasoning. FARS generalizes to unseen concepts and different architectures, with high cross-model alignment (CCA > 0.79). They also find an asymmetry: prose–math representations are more mutually compatible than either is with code, indicating a declarative vs. procedural split more than natural-language vs. formal-language.

Conclusion: Small LLMs appear to implement a shared, low-dimensional, format-agnostic reasoning substrate that underlies different symbolic forms, supporting a “Platonic” view of internal representations. This subspace is functionally critical, generalizes across concepts and architectures, and reveals that the main representational divide is between declarative (prose, math) and procedural (code) formats rather than between natural and formal language. This offers a concrete target for mechanistic interpretability and future work on controllable reasoning.

Abstract: Large language models represent the same reasoning in vastly different surface forms -- English prose, Python code, mathematical notation -- yet whether they share a common internal substrate across these symbolic systems remains unknown. We introduce the TriForm Benchmark (18 concepts x 6 forms x 3 instances = 324 stimuli) and study five LLMs (1.6B-8B) across three architecture families. Using permutation-corrected RSA, cross-form probing, and activation patching, we find converging evidence for a Format-Agnostic Reasoning Subspace (FARS) in middle layers. We make FARS concrete: concept-centroid PCA extracts a 10-dimensional subspace that amplifies concept structure 3x while suppressing form information to near zero. Replacing only these 10 dimensions during cross-form patching preserves 90-96% of model output -- far exceeding both full activation replacement (44-56%) and variance-maximizing PCA (60-74%) -- while ablating them causes targeted disruption. FARS generalizes to held-out concepts and converges across architectures (CCA > 0.79 for all model pairs), providing within-modality evidence for the Platonic Representation Hypothesis. We further discover a declarative-procedural asymmetry: representations are far more compatible between prose and mathematics than between either and code, suggesting that the critical axis of divergence is not linguistic vs. formal but declarative vs. procedural.

</details>


### [86] [Hidden Error Awareness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning: The Signal Is Diagnostic, Not Causal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09502)
*Aojie Yuan,Zhiyuan Julian Su,Haiyue Zhang,Yi Nian,Yue Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that language models internally detect reasoning errors during chain-of-thought but still express high outward confidence, and that this internal error signal is diagnostic rather than a controllable handle to fix reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test the common assumption that chain-of-thought reasoning traces reflect a model’s internal computation and that exposing or editing these traces can improve reasoning or enable control via mechanistic interpretability.

Method: They train a linear probe on hidden states to predict whether each step in a chain-of-thought is correct, compare this to surface-level text classifiers and verbalized confidence, and evaluate across multiple model families and sizes. They then attempt four interventions—activation steering, probe-guided best-of-N sampling, self-correction using the error signal, and activation patching—to see if the internal error signal can be used to correct reasoning.

Result: The linear probe predicts trace correctness with high AUROC (0.95 overall; 0.79 from the first step), far outperforming a text-surface classifier (0.59 AUROC), showing that models internally represent their own reasoning errors while still outputting similarly high verbal confidence for both correct and incorrect traces. Across diverse models, including RL-trained reasoning models, the error signal is robust, but all four attempted interventions fail to use it to correct reasoning, and activation patching severely harms coherence.

Conclusion: Internal representations of reasoning errors are accessible via probes and act as a diagnostic readout of computation quality, but they are not a causal handle for steering or correcting reasoning. This challenges the assumption that chain-of-thought text faithfully reflects internal computation and delineates a limit for mechanistic interpretability by contrasting editable factual knowledge with seemingly non-editable error representations in reasoning processes.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting assumes that generated reasoning reflects a model's internal computation. We show this assumption is wrong in a specific, measurable way: models internally detect their own reasoning errors but outwardly express confidence in them. A linear probe on hidden states predicts trace correctness with 0.95 AUROC -- from the very first reasoning step (0.79) -- while verbalized confidence for wrong traces is 4.55/5, nearly identical to correct ones (4.87/5). A text-surface classifier achieves only 0.59 on the same data, confirming a 0.20-point gap invisible in the generated text. This hidden error awareness holds across three model families (Qwen, Llama, Phi), 1.5B-72B parameters, and RL-trained reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1, 0.852 AUROC). The natural question is whether this signal can fix the errors it detects. It cannot. Four interventions -- activation steering, probe-guided best-of-N, self-correction, and activation patching -- all fail; patching destroys output coherence entirely. The signal is diagnostic, not causal: a readout of computation quality, not a lever to redirect it. This delineates a boundary for mechanistic interpretability: error representations during reasoning are fundamentally different from the factual knowledge representations that prior work has successfully edited.

</details>


### [87] [Assessment of RAG and Fine-Tuning for Industrial Question-Answering-Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09533)
*Jakob Sturm,Josef Pichlmeier,Christian Bernhard,Maka Karalashvili,Johannes Klepsch,Georg Groh,Andre Luckow*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and fine-tuning (FT) for adapting LLMs to an enterprise automotive QA setting, jointly evaluating accuracy and costs, and finds RAG is generally the most effective and cost-efficient approach, enabling open-source models to rival premium ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enterprises increasingly use LLMs for internal QA over proprietary, domain-specific data. Two main adaptation strategies—RAG and fine-tuning—are widely used, but industry practitioners lack clear, quantitative guidance on which is better when both answer quality and various costs (compute, operations, user time) are considered. Existing evaluations often focus only on accuracy or only on model costs. The paper aims to fill this gap with a realistic, cost-aware comparison in an automotive domain.

Method: The authors take two closed, automotive-specific datasets and build QA systems using multiple LLMs (both premium closed-source and open-source). For each model, they implement two adaptation strategies: (1) RAG, where domain documents are indexed and retrieved at query time, and (2) fine-tuning, where the model is adapted on domain data. They extend the Cost-of-Pass framework by Erol et al. to a joint metric that captures output quality (e.g., answer correctness or pass rates), generation cost (e.g., API or compute cost per query), and user interaction cost (e.g., extra steps or time needed to reach a satisfactory answer). They then compare all configurations along this unified cost-accuracy trade-off.

Result: Premium, closed-source models deliver the highest QA performance without any additional adaptation. However, open-source models, when combined with a well-designed RAG pipeline, can reach similar answer quality. Across both closed- and open-source models and both automotive datasets, RAG-based systems achieve a better balance of answer quality and total cost than fine-tuned ones. The extended Cost-of-Pass analysis shows that RAG configurations are typically more cost-efficient for achieving a given quality level.

Conclusion: For enterprise QA over domain-specific, closed data in the automotive sector, RAG is generally preferable to fine-tuning when considering both accuracy and end-to-end costs. Organizations can rely on premium models for strong out-of-the-box performance, but can also attain comparable quality more economically by using open-source models enhanced with RAG. The extended Cost-of-Pass framework provides a practical way to evaluate such trade-offs and can guide model and adaptation choices in similar industrial settings.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly employed in enterprise question-answering (QA) systems, requiring adaptation to domain-specific knowledge. Among the most prevalent methods for incorporating such knowledge are Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and fine-tuning (FT). Yet, from a cost-accuracy trade-off perspective, it remains unclear which approach best suits industry scenarios. This study examines the impact of RAG and FT on two closed datasets specific to the automotive industry, assessing answer quality and operational costs. We extend the Cost-of-Pass framework proposed by Erol et al. (arXiv:2504.13359) to jointly assess output quality, generation cost, and user interaction cost. Our findings reveal that while premium models perform best out of the box, open-source models can achieve comparable quality when enhanced with RAG. Overall, RAG emerges as the most effective and cost-efficient adaptation method for both closed- and open-source models.

</details>


### [88] [TAD: Temporal-Aware Trajectory Self-Distillation for Fast and Accurate Diffusion LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09536)
*Haoyang Zhou,Li Kong,Shijie Ren,Xiting Wang,Shuang Liang,Guowei Wang,Zhenxuan Pan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TAD, a temporal-aware self-distillation framework that improves the trade-off between accuracy and parallel generation speed in diffusion LLMs by using different training signals for tokens that are near vs. far in the decoding trajectory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion LLMs can generate tokens in parallel using large tokens-per-forward (TPF), but this parallelism usually hurts generation quality, creating an accuracy-parallelism trade-off. Existing acceleration methods tend to speed up decoding at the expense of accuracy. The authors want a method that sustains or improves accuracy while still enabling highly parallel decoding.

Method: The authors propose TAD (Temporal-Aware trajectory self-Distillation). They first construct training data by having a teacher model decode while conditioned on both the prompt and the gold response, logging intermediate masked states (decoding trajectories). Each masked position is labeled by how many decoding steps remain until that token is revealed. They then split masked positions into “near” and “distant” based on this temporal distance. For near tokens (soon to be decoded), the student is trained with a hard cross-entropy loss using the teacher’s trajectory tokens as labels, encouraging confident, precise predictions. For distant tokens (far in the future), the student is trained with a soft KL divergence loss between teacher and student distributions, preserving uncertainty and long-horizon planning information. This temporal partitioning also supports different inference configurations tuned for either accuracy or speed.

Result: Using the LLaDA benchmark, TAD improves the accuracy-parallelism trade-off of diffusion LLMs. With the Quality configuration, average accuracy increases from 46.2% to 51.6%. With the Speed configuration, average AUP (area under the accuracy-parallelism curve) improves dramatically from 46.2 to 257.1, indicating much better performance at higher TPF settings. These gains show that TAD can both enhance accuracy and enable more aggressive parallel acceleration compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: TAD demonstrates that exploiting temporal information in diffusion decoding trajectories enables more effective self-distillation for diffusion LLMs. By giving hard supervision to near-future tokens and soft, distributional supervision to distant-future tokens, TAD alleviates the typical accuracy loss seen with increased parallelism. The framework yields two practical operating modes—one favoring accuracy and one favoring speed—both of which outperform previous approaches on LLaDA, suggesting a promising direction for scalable, parallel text generation with diffusion-based LLMs.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) offer a promising paradigm for parallel text generation, but in practice they face an accuracy-parallelism trade-off, where increasing tokens per forward (TPF) often degrades generation quality. Existing acceleration methods often gain speed at the cost of accuracy. To address this limitation, we propose TAD, a Temporal-Aware trajectory self-Distillation framework. During data construction, we condition a teacher model on both the prompt and the ground-truth response to generate decoding trajectories, recording the intermediate masked states throughout the process. Based on how many decoding steps remain before each masked token is revealed, we partition masked positions into near and distant subsets. For near tokens, we train the student with a hard cross-entropy loss using the teacher trajectory tokens as labels, encouraging confident predictions for tokens that are about to be decoded. For distant tokens, we apply a soft KL divergence loss between the teacher and student token distributions, providing softer supervision and preserving future planning knowledge. This temporal-aware partition naturally gives rise to two deployment configurations: a Quality model that prioritizes accuracy and a Speed model that favors more aggressive acceleration. Experiments show that TAD consistently improves the accuracy-parallelism trade-off. On LLaDA, it raises average accuracy from 46.2\% to 51.6\% with the Quality model and average AUP from 46.2 to 257.1 with the Speed model. Our code is available at: https://github.com/BHmingyang/TAD

</details>


### [89] [TacoMAS: Test-Time Co-Evolution of Topology and Capability in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09539)
*Chen Xu,Yicheng Hu,Ruizi Wang,Xinyu Lin,Wenjie Wang,Dongrui Liu,Fuli Feng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TacoMAS, a test-time co-evolution framework for multi-agent systems that jointly and asynchronously adapts agents’ capabilities and communication topology, yielding significant performance gains over strong baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolving multi-agent system methods typically either fix the communication topology at inference or adapt only one aspect—either agent capabilities or topology—during inference. The authors empirically and theoretically find that truly effective test-time evolution requires simultaneous adaptation of both capabilities and topology but on different time scales to balance rapid task adaptation with coordination stability. This gap motivates a new framework that can jointly, yet stably, evolve both dimensions online.

Method: The authors model multi-agent inference as online graph adaptation, where nodes are agents with role-specific capabilities and edges are communication channels. They propose TacoMAS, which operates with two coupled loops: (1) a fast capability loop that rapidly updates agents’ expertise using trajectory-level feedback to handle emerging subtasks, and (2) a slow, meta-LLM-driven topology loop that performs structural birth-death operations on the MAS graph, including edge edits, addition of new agents, and removal of existing agents. This fast-slow co-evolution mechanism is theoretically analyzed to show that it steers the MAS toward task-conditioned stable equilibria.

Result: On four benchmark tasks, TacoMAS outperforms nearly 20 multi-agent baselines, with an average performance gain of 13.3% over the strongest competing method, demonstrating the effectiveness of joint fast-slow adaptation of capabilities and topology.

Conclusion: Jointly adapting agent capabilities and communication topology at different time scales is crucial for effective test-time evolution in multi-agent systems. TacoMAS operationalizes this principle through online graph adaptation with fast capability updates and slow topology evolution, leading to stable and superior performance across multiple benchmarks compared to a wide range of existing methods.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems (MAS) have emerged as a promising paradigm for solving complex tasks. Recent work has explored self-evolving MAS that automatically optimize agent capabilities or communication topologies. However, existing methods either learn a topology that remains fixed at inference time or adapt only the topology or capability during inference. We empirically and theoretically show that effective test-time evolution requires jointly adapting both axes, but on different time scales: capabilities should update rapidly to handle emerging subtasks, while the topology should evolve more slowly to preserve coordination stability. We then introduce TacoMAS, a test-time co-evolution framework for dynamic MAS. TacoMAS formulates MAS inference as a task of online graph adaptation, where nodes represent agents with role-specific capabilities and edges define their communication topology. During inference, a fast capability loop updates agent expertise using trajectory-level feedback, while a slow meta-LLM-driven topology loop performs agents' birth-death operations on MAS, including edge edit, agent addition, and agent removal. We further show that this fast-slow design drives MAS evolution toward a task-conditioned stable equilibrium. Experiments on four benchmarks demonstrate that TacoMAS outperforms nearly 20 multi-agent baselines, achieving an average improvement of 13.3% over the strongest baseline. The codes are released at https://github.com/chenxu2-gif/TacoMAS-MultiAgent.

</details>


### [90] [Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation for Multilingual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09548)
*Yihong Liu,Raoyuan Zhao,Michael A. Hedderich,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces COPSD, a method that improves mathematical reasoning of LLMs in low-resource languages by distilling crosslingual reasoning behavior from the same model using privileged English context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are strong at mathematical reasoning in high-resource languages like English, but their performance drops significantly in low-resource languages, creating an accessibility and fairness gap. Existing RL-style methods for improving reasoning are sparse and unstable, especially for low-resource settings. The paper aims to close this crosslingual performance gap with a stable, data- and compute-efficient training approach.

Method: They propose Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation (COPSD), where a single LLM plays both student and teacher. For each low-resource language math problem, the student model only sees the original low-resource prompt. The teacher model, however, receives privileged crosslingual context: an English translation of the problem and the reference solution. The student generates its own rollouts; the training objective then minimizes the token-level divergence between the teacher’s and student’s output distributions on these rollouts (full-distribution KL/distillation), providing dense supervision without using standard RL rewards. This is done on-policy, i.e., using the student’s current behavior, and is compared against GRPO, an RL-based method.

Result: On mathematical reasoning tasks in 17 low-resource African languages, COPSD consistently boosts reasoning performance across multiple model sizes. It substantially outperforms the RL-based Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). COPSD also improves adherence to required answer formats, exhibits better test-time scaling behavior (more gains from sampling/computation at inference), and transfers to more difficult multilingual reasoning benchmarks, with particularly large gains in the lowest-resource languages.

Conclusion: Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation is an effective and stable way to transfer high-resource mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs into low-resource languages using only the model itself and privileged English context, avoiding the instability of RL-based approaches. The method yields consistent and sizable improvements in low-resource mathematical reasoning quality, robustness, and generalization, making reasoning abilities more accessible across diverse languages.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in mathematical reasoning, but this ability is not equally accessible across languages. Especially low-resource languages exhibit much lower reasoning performance. To address this, we propose Crosslingual On-Policy Self-Distillation (COPSD), which transfers a model's own high-resource reasoning behavior to low-resource languages. COPSD uses the same model as student and teacher: the student sees only the low-resource problem, while the teacher receives privileged crosslingual context, including the problem translation and reference solution in English. Training minimizes full-distribution token-level divergence on the student's own rollouts, providing dense supervision while avoiding the sparsity and instability of outcome-only reinforcement learning (RL). Experiments on 17 low-resource African languages show that COPSD consistently improves low-resource mathematical reasoning across model sizes and substantially outperforms Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Further analyses show that COPSD improves answer-format adherence, strengthens test-time scaling, and generalizes to harder multilingual reasoning benchmarks, with especially large gains for lower-resource languages. We make our code and data available at: https://github.com/cisnlp/COPSD.

</details>


### [91] [Towards Compact Sign Language Translation: Frame Rate and Model Size Trade-offs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09554)
*Kuanwei Chen,Mengfeng Tsai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A compact sign language translation system uses pose-based input and T5-small to achieve competitive accuracy with far lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing sign language translation models are gloss-free but depend on large encoder-decoder architectures, making them heavy and hard to deploy efficiently. There is a need for smaller, more efficient models that still deliver strong translation performance and clarify the trade-offs between speed, complexity, and accuracy.

Method: The authors build a 77M-parameter pipeline combining MMPose for skeletal pose extraction from sign videos, a single linear projection layer to map poses into the text model space, and a T5-small model for translation. They then vary the input frame rate (e.g., 24 fps vs. 12 fps) to study efficiency–accuracy trade-offs, focusing on how reduced frame rates shorten sequences and lower self-attention cost.

Result: At 12 fps, the model halves the input sequence length compared with 24 fps, leading to a 75% reduction in quadratic encoder self-attention computational cost. Translation quality, measured by BLEU-4 on How2Sign, drops only slightly from 10.06 to 9.53. The complete system has 77M parameters, making it about 3x smaller than comparable T5-base systems while remaining competitive in performance.

Conclusion: A lightweight, pose-based SLT pipeline using T5-small can achieve competitive performance with significantly reduced computational complexity and model size. Hierarchical encoders and large-scale models are not strictly necessary for effective gloss-free sign language translation, and controlling frame rate offers a practical knob to trade translation quality for efficiency.

Abstract: Sign Language Translation (SLT) converts sign language videos into spoken-language text, bridging communication between Deaf and hearing communities. Current gloss-free approaches rely on large encoder-decoder models, limiting deployment. We propose a compact 77M-parameter pipeline that couples MMPose skeletal pose extraction with a single linear projection into T5-small. By varying the input frame rate, we expose a practical efficiency trade-off: at 12 fps the model halves its sequence length, achieving a 75% reduction in encoder quadratic self-attention computational complexity while incurring only a modest BLEU-4 drop (9.53 vs. 10.06 at 24 fps on How2Sign). Our system is roughly 3x smaller than prior T5-base systems, demonstrating that a lightweight architecture can remain competitive without hierarchical encoders or large-scale models.

</details>


### [92] [CLR-voyance: Reinforcing Open-Ended Reasoning for Inpatient Clinical Decision Support with Outcome-Aware Rubrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09584)
*Aishik Nagar,Arun-Kumar Kaliya-Perumal,Yu-Hsuan Han,Andrew Sheng-Han Huang,Kristen Kee,Yushi Cao,Yiming Chen,Hongchao Jiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents CLR-voyance, a framework that models inpatient clinical reasoning as a POMDP and supervises LLMs with outcome-grounded, clinician-validated rewards, achieving state-of-the-art performance on a new benchmark and existing medical tasks while being deployed in real hospital workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM evaluations for inpatient clinical reasoning oversimplify the task into static retrieval or rely on ungrounded LLM-as-judge scores, failing to capture the sequential, partially observable nature of real clinical decision-making. There is a need for a principled framework and benchmark that (1) respect the temporal uncertainty of inpatient care, (2) are grounded in real patient outcomes, and (3) are aligned with clinician judgments so that models can be safely trained and evaluated for realistic inpatient reasoning and documentation support.

Method: They formalize inpatient clinical reasoning as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) and propose CLR-voyance. A concrete instantiation, CLR-POMDP, splits real successful inpatient trajectories into a visible past and an oracle-only future. An oracle LLM uses only the past to generate case-specific question–answer pairs and an adaptive, future-verifiable rubric for good reasoning. These rubrics are outcome-grounded because they can be checked against the oracle future, and clinician-validated via a large-scale alignment study in which physicians curate rubrics, grade model outputs, and provide blinded pairwise preferences. The rubrics serve as rewards for post-training and evaluation. They train Qwen3-8B and MedGemma-4B with GRPO, then merge models to obtain CLR-voyance-8B, and evaluate it on CLR-POMDP and standard medical benchmarks. They also analyze different clinical LLM-as-a-judge and preference-model choices.

Result: CLR-voyance-8B attains 84.91% accuracy on the new CLR-POMDP benchmark, surpassing strong medical reasoning models such as GPT-5 (77.83%) and MedGemma-27B (66.66%), while maintaining competitive or superior performance on existing medical benchmarks. The clinician alignment study confirms that the rubrics and rewards reflect meaningful clinical preferences and offers empirical guidance on how to select and use LLM-as-a-judge systems in the clinical domain. The system has been deployed for more than six months in a partner public hospital, where it has successfully drafted thousands of complex inpatient notes, indicating practical viability.

Conclusion: Modeling inpatient clinical reasoning as a POMDP and supervising LLMs with outcome-grounded, clinician-validated rubrics yields better-aligned, higher-performing clinical reasoning models that work in realistic, partially observable hospital settings. CLR-voyance provides both a new benchmark (CLR-POMDP) and a training/evaluation framework that advances state of the art over larger frontier models while preserving general-domain competence. Its successful multi-month deployment in a public hospital suggests that such principled, clinician-aligned formulation can bridge the gap between offline benchmarks and real-world clinical use, and its analysis of LLM-as-a-judge and preference modeling offers broadly useful guidance for the medical AI community.

Abstract: Inpatient clinical reasoning is a sequential decision under partial observability: the clinician sees the admission so far and must choose the next action whose downstream consequences are not yet visible. Existing clinical-LLM evaluations and RL rewards signals collapse this into closed-form retrieval, clinical journey leakage, or unanchored LLM-as-judge scoring. We introduce CLR-voyance, a framework that reformulates inpatient reasoning as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) and supervises it with rewards that are simultaneously outcome-grounded and clinician-validated. We instantiate the formulation as CLR-POMDP, which partitions successful patient journeys into a policy-visible past and an oracle-only future. Using the past information, an oracle LLM generates a case-specific query-answer pair, and the first adaptive rubric for clinical reasoning which is verifiable in the future of the patient journey. These rubrics are used for both post-training and evaluation of models for inpatient clinical reasoning. We post-train Qwen3-8B and MedGemma-4B with GRPO followed by model merging, yielding state-of-the-art inpatient clinical reasoning while retaining generalist capabilities. CLR-voyance-8B achieves 84.91% on CLR-POMDP, ahead of frontier medical reasoning models like GPT-5 (77.83%) and MedGemma-27B (66.66%) and has comparable or better performance on existing medical benchmarks. To ensure a clinically meaningful setting, we conduct a large-scale clinician alignment study, where physicians curate per-case rubrics, grade candidate responses, and provide blinded pairwise preferences of model reasoning. This study provides insights on clinical LLM-as-a-judge and clinical preference-model selection, which can inform the community at large. CLR-voyance has been deployed for 6+ months at a partner public hospital, drafting thousands of reasoning-heavy inpatient notes.

</details>


### [93] [Byte-Exact Deduplication in Retrieval-Augmented Generation: A Three-Regime Empirical Analysis Across Public Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09611)
*Sietse Schelpe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper empirically studies byte-exact chunk-level deduplication in RAG pipelines and shows it can greatly cut context size (up to ~80% in some settings) with no measurable loss in answer quality across several major LLM APIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems often send large, redundant contexts to LLMs, wasting inference compute and latency. While deduplication is an obvious optimization, there is limited rigorous, cross-model evidence on how much context can be removed safely and in which regimes, and whether such removal harms output quality. The paper aims to quantify the benefits and safety of strict byte-exact deduplication in realistic RAG scenarios.

Method: The authors apply byte-exact chunk-level deduplication to three RAG regimes: (1) a large academic retrieval benchmark (BeIR) to simulate clean retrieval, (2) synthetic enterprise-style patterns to simulate moderate redundancy, and (3) multi-turn conversational logs to simulate heavy redundancy. They measure percentage reduction in bytes sent as context. To assess quality, they run a human evaluation with five calibrated judges on outputs from four commercial LLM APIs (Gemini 2.5 Flash, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Llama 3.3 70B, GPT-5.1), comparing answers with and without deduplication. Using a five-category noise-removal protocol, they focus on panel-majority materially different pairs and compute Wilson 95% upper bounds on the rate of such differences.

Result: Deduplication yields very different context-reduction levels across regimes: negligible in clean academic retrieval (0.16% bytes saved on 22.2M BeIR passages), substantial in enterprise-like constructed redundancy (24.03%), and extremely high in multi-turn conversational AI settings (80.34%). Despite these reductions, the human panel finds no measurable degradation in answer quality for any of the four evaluated LLM APIs. All models satisfy a strict <5% Wilson 95% upper-bound threshold for materially different outputs, even in high-redundancy regimes.

Conclusion: Deterministic, byte-exact chunk-level deduplication can significantly reduce context size—and therefore inference compute costs—especially in redundant and conversational RAG pipelines, without causing detectable quality regressions under careful human evaluation. This suggests that straightforward deduplication is a safe, high-leverage optimization for production RAG systems across multiple LLM vendors.

Abstract: This preprint presents an empirical analysis of byte-exact chunk-level deduplication in Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines. We measure context reduction across three distinct operating regimes: clean academic retrieval (0.16% byte reduction on 22.2M BeIR passages), constructed enterprise patterns (24.03% reduction), and multi-turn conversational AI (80.34% reduction). To validate quality preservation, we conducted a cross-vendor 5-judge calibrated panel evaluation across four production APIs (Google Gemini 2.5 Flash, Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6, Meta Llama 3.3 70B, and OpenAI GPT-5.1). Applying a five-category human-in-the-loop noise-removal protocol to panel-majority materially different (MAT) pairs, we establish that byte-exact deduplication introduces zero measurable quality regression. Post-audit, all four vendors clear the strict <5% Wilson 95% upper-bound MAT threshold in both the clean and high-redundancy RAG regimes. This work demonstrates that substantial inference compute savings can be achieved deterministically without compromising evaluation-grade model quality.

</details>


### [94] [Scratchpad Patching: Decoupling Compute from Patch Size in Byte-Level Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09630)
*Lin Zheng,Vasilisa Bashlovkina,Timothy Dozat,Dan Garrette,Laura Rimell,Joshua Maynez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Scratchpad Patching (SP), a technique for tokenizer-free, patch-based byte language models that mitigates the quality–efficiency trade-off by dynamically updating within-patch representations using scratchpads triggered by prediction entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tokenizer-free language models that operate on raw bytes avoid tokenizer complexity but are computationally expensive. Patch-based models group bytes into patches to save compute and KV-cache memory, yet larger patches hurt modeling quality due to "patch lag": bytes inside a patch are predicted using outdated representations until the full patch is seen. The authors aim to break this strict trade-off and improve quality without giving up the efficiency gains of large patches.

Method: The authors introduce Scratchpad Patching (SP), which adds transient internal scratchpads within each patch. As bytes in a patch arrive, SP aggregates the bytes observed so far into an updated representation that can be used for subsequent predictions, reducing the lag inherent to patching. SP uses next-byte prediction entropy as a trigger to decide when to instantiate or update a scratchpad, so that additional compute is spent only on information-dense or uncertain regions. This design also allows adjusting the amount of extra computation at inference time without retraining the base model.

Result: On both natural language and code benchmarks, SP consistently improves model quality for a fixed patch size. For example, at 16-byte patches, SP-augmented models achieve performance comparable to or close to strong byte-level baselines, while maintaining the efficiency benefits of patching: roughly 16× smaller KV cache measured over patches and 3–4× lower inference compute relative to byte-level models.

Conclusion: Scratchpad Patching mitigates the fundamental patch-size trade-off in tokenizer-free, patch-based byte models by reducing patch lag through dynamically updated within-patch representations. By using entropy-triggered scratchpads, SP focuses extra computation where it matters most, allowing large-patch models to recover much of the quality of byte-level models while retaining substantial savings in KV-cache size and inference compute. This offers a practical and flexible way to deploy efficient tokenizer-free language models without severe performance degradation.

Abstract: Tokenizer-free language models eliminate the tokenizer step of the language modeling pipeline by operating directly on bytes; patch-based variants further aggregate contiguous byte spans into patches for efficiency. However, the average patch size chosen at the model design stage governs a tight trade-off: larger patches reduce compute and KV-cache footprint, but degrade modeling quality. We trace this trade-off to patch lag: until a patch is fully observed, byte predictions within it must rely on a stale representation from the previous patch to preserve causality; this lag widens as patches grow larger. We introduce Scratchpad Patching (SP), which inserts transient scratchpads inside each patch to aggregate the bytes seen so far and refresh patch-level context for subsequent predictions. SP triggers scratchpads using next-byte prediction entropy, selectively allocating compute to information-dense regions and enabling post-hoc adjustment of inference-time compute. Across experiments on natural language and code, SP improves model quality at the same patch size; for example, even at $16$ bytes per patch, SP-augmented models match or closely approach the byte-level baseline on downstream evaluations while using a $16\times$ smaller KV cache over patches and $3$-$4\times$ less inference compute.

</details>


### [95] [Can We Trust LLMs for Mental Health Screening? Consistency, ASR Robustness, and Evidence Faithfulness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09634)
*Erfan Loweimi,Sofia de la Fuente Garcia,Samira Loveymi,Hadi Daneshvar,Saturnino Luz*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how reliably different large language models can estimate anxiety and depression scores from speech, focusing on consistency, robustness to speech recognition errors, and how well their explanations match the actual transcript.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can zero-shot estimate Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) scores from speech, clinical use requires more than accuracy: models must be consistent across runs, robust to automatic speech recognition (ASR) errors, and provide explanations that are faithful to the input. Current work lacks a systematic assessment of these reliability dimensions, especially under realistic ASR conditions.

Method: The authors evaluate three LLMs (Phi-4, Gemma-2-9B, Llama-3.1-8B) on speech data from 111 English-speaking participants. For each participant, they use ground-truth transcripts and transcripts generated by three Whisper ASR variants (Large, Medium, Small) with varying word error rates. For every model–transcript-condition pair, they run the model three times. They then measure: (1) intra-model consistency via intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC) across runs; (2) robustness of this consistency as ASR quality degrades; (3) predictive validity of HADS score estimates under ASR vs ground truth; and (4) evidence faithfulness via keyword groundedness and inter-model keyword agreement compared to score-level agreement.

Result: Phi-4 and Gemma-2-9B exhibit excellent intra-model consistency (ICC > 0.89) that is only minimally affected by ASR errors. Llama-3.1-8B is much more fragile: its ICC drops from 0.82 with clean transcripts to 0.36 when word error rate reaches 10%. For the robust models (Phi-4 and Gemma-2-9B), predictive validity of the estimated HADS scores is largely preserved under ASR noise. Keyword groundedness (how well highlighted evidence matches the transcript) is above 93% for Phi-4 and Gemma-2-9B, but lower (77–81%) for Llama-3.1-8B. Inter-model agreement on which keywords support the scores is far lower than the agreement on the scores themselves, indicating that different models often rely on different pieces of textual evidence for similar numeric predictions.

Conclusion: Some LLMs can provide consistent and ASR-robust estimates of HADS scores from speech and offer largely faithful evidence highlighting, suggesting technical feasibility for clinical support tools. However, the divergence in keyword-level explanations across models, despite similar score outputs, reveals a dissociation between predicted scores and supporting evidence. This raises important interpretability and trust concerns for clinical deployment and underscores the need to evaluate not just predictive performance but also explanation consistency and faithfulness.

Abstract: LLMs can estimate Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) scores from speech in a zero-shot manner, but clinical deployment requires reliability across three dimensions: intra-model consistency, ASR robustness, and evidence faithfulness. We evaluate three LLMs (Phi-4, Gemma-2-9B, and Llama-3.1-8B) on 111 English-speaking participants using ground-truth transcripts and three Whisper ASR variants (Large, Medium, Small), with three independent runs per model-condition pair. We find that (i) Phi-4 and Gemma-2-9B achieve excellent intra-model consistency (ICC > 0.89) with minimal degradation under ASR; (ii) Llama-3.1-8B shows ASR-fragile consistency, with ICC dropping from 0.82 to 0.36 at 10% WER; (iii) predictive validity is largely preserved under ASR for robust models; and (iv) keyword groundedness exceeds 93% for Phi-4 and Gemma-2-9B but falls to 77-81% for Llama-3.1-8B. Inter-model keyword agreement is far lower than score-level agreement, revealing a score-evidence dissociation with implications for clinical interpretability.

</details>


### [96] [K12-KGraph: A Curriculum-Aligned Knowledge Graph for Benchmarking and Training Educational LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09635)
*Hao Liang,Qihan Lin,Zhaoyang Han,Xiaochen Ma,Zhen Hao Wong,Meiyi Qiang,Linzhuang Sun,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a large, curriculum-aligned knowledge graph from K-12 textbooks plus a benchmark and training set to test and improve LLMs’ understanding of school curricula, showing current models are weak at this and that graph-guided supervision is very sample-efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing education benchmarks for LLMs mostly test factual Q&A, not whether models understand how school knowledge is organized (prerequisites, taxonomies, experiment links, sequencing). Effective educational AI tutors need this curriculum-level cognition. There is no large, structured, curriculum-grounded resource to evaluate and train such abilities in Chinese K-12 subjects.

Method: Extract a comprehensive knowledge graph (K12-KGraph) from official K-12 textbooks in math, physics, chemistry, and biology, covering primary to high school. The graph encodes seven node types (Concept, Skill, Experiment, Exercise, Section, Chapter, Book) and nine relation types (taxonomy, prerequisites, associations, verification, assessment, location, ordering, etc.). From this graph, derive two resources: K12-Bench, a 23k+ multi-select QA benchmark covering five graph-based task families (Ground, Prereq, Neighbor, Evidence, Locate); and K12-Train, about 2.3k KG-guided supervised QA pairs automatically synthesized using graph structure and node attributes. Then evaluate various frontier and open-source LLMs on K12-Bench, and conduct controlled SFT experiments: fine-tune base models with K12-Train versus equally sized subsets of eight popular instruction-tuning datasets, comparing performance on GaokaoBench and EduEval.

Result: K12-KGraph and its derived datasets were successfully constructed and released. On the new K12-Bench benchmark, even strong LLMs perform modestly: Gemini-3-Flash gets 57% exact match and the best open-source model, Gemma-4-31B-IT, gets 46%, indicating large gaps in curriculum cognition. In SFT experiments with a fixed 2,300-example budget on Qwen3-4B-Base and Llama-3.1-8B-Base, the K12-Train dataset consistently yields higher scores on GaokaoBench and EduEval than equally sized random subsets from eight mainstream instruction-tuning corpora, showing that KG-structured, curriculum-aligned supervision is more sample-efficient for educational tuning.

Conclusion: Curriculum cognition is a distinct and currently underdeveloped capability in LLMs for K-12 education. A curriculum-aligned knowledge graph plus graph-derived benchmarks and SFT data expose significant weaknesses in current systems and provide a more sample-efficient way to specialize models for educational use. The released K12-KGraph, K12-Bench, K12-Train, and construction pipeline offer a foundation for future research on curriculum-aware educational AI and improved tutoring systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in K-12 education, yet existing benchmarks such as C-Eval, CMMLU, GaokaoBench, and EduEval mainly evaluate factual recall through exam-style question answering. Effective educational AI additionally requires curriculum cognition: understanding how knowledge is structured through prerequisite chains, concept taxonomies, experiment-concept links, and pedagogical sequencing. To address this gap, we introduce K12-KGraph, a curriculum-aligned knowledge graph extracted from official People's Education Press textbooks across mathematics, physics, chemistry, and biology from primary to high school. The graph contains seven node types (Concept, Skill, Experiment, Exercise, Section, Chapter, Book) and nine relation types covering taxonomy, prerequisite, association, verification, assessment, location, and order. Based on this graph, we construct two resources: (1) K12-Bench, a 23,640-question multi-select benchmark spanning five graph-derived task families (Ground, Prereq, Neighbor, Evidence, and Locate); and (2) K12-Train, a KG-guided supervised fine-tuning corpus of approximately 2,300 QA pairs synthesized from graph structure and node attributes. Experiments reveal substantial deficiencies in curriculum cognition: on K12-Bench, Gemini-3-Flash achieves only 57% exact match, while the best open-source model, Gemma-4-31B-IT, reaches 46%. Under a strictly matched 2,300-sample SFT budget on Qwen3-4B-Base and Llama-3.1-8B-Base, K12-Train consistently outperforms equally sized subsets from eight mainstream instruction-tuning corpora on both GaokaoBench and EduEval, demonstrating that curriculum-structured supervision is highly sample-efficient for educational tuning. We release the graph, benchmark, training data, and full construction pipeline.

</details>


### [97] [MedMeta: A Benchmark for LLMs in Synthesizing Meta-Analysis Conclusion from Medical Studies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09661)
*Huy Hoang Ha,Benoit Favre,Francois Portet*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MedMeta is a new benchmark that tests whether LLMs can synthesize evidence from multiple medical studies—specifically, reproduce meta-analysis-like conclusions from only the abstracts of included trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medical benchmarks mostly test factual recall and narrow question answering, but say little about LLMs’ ability to perform higher-order reasoning tasks crucial in evidence-based medicine, such as integrating heterogeneous study results into a coherent conclusion. With LLMs already saturating standard benchmarks, the field lacks a challenging, clinically relevant testbed for evidence synthesis and for rigorously evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) setups in medicine.

Method: The authors construct MedMeta from 81 meta-analyses published on PubMed between 2018 and 2025. For each meta-analysis, they collect the abstracts of the included primary studies. They define two evaluation workflows: (1) Golden-RAG, where the LLM receives the ground-truth study abstracts as retrieved context and must generate a conclusion; and (2) Parametric-only, where the LLM must answer using internal knowledge without external documents. They propose an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation protocol, compare its scores against human expert ratings using Pearson correlation and Bland–Altman analysis, and use this validated protocol to benchmark different LLMs, architectures, and fine-tuning strategies, including stress tests with negated evidence.

Result: The LLM-as-a-judge framework shows strong alignment with human expert ratings (Pearson’s r = 0.81) and negligible systematic bias in Bland–Altman analysis, supporting it as a scalable evaluation proxy. Across tested models, the Golden-RAG setting (with ground-truth abstracts) consistently and significantly outperforms the Parametric-only setting, underscoring the value of information grounding. Domain-specific fine-tuning yields only small gains and these largely vanish when high-quality external material is provided. Stress tests reveal that all models systematically fail to detect and correctly handle negated evidence, exposing a serious weakness in contemporary RAG pipelines. Absolute performance levels remain modest: even under ideal Golden-RAG conditions, models attain only slightly above-average scores (~2.7/5.0).

Conclusion: MedMeta establishes a challenging, clinically relevant benchmark focused on evidence synthesis rather than simple fact recall and shows that current LLMs are far from reliably performing meta-analytic reasoning, even when given ideal input. Robust retrieval and grounding strategies confer much larger benefits than domain-specific fine-tuning, suggesting that future work for clinical LLM deployment should prioritize improving RAG systems and robustness to misleading or negated evidence over further specialization of model parameters alone.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have saturated standard medical benchmarks that test factual recall, yet their ability to perform higher-order reasoning, such as synthesizing evidence from multiple sources, remains critically under-explored. To address this gap, we introduce MedMeta, the first benchmark designed to evaluate an LLM's ability to generate conclusions from medical meta-analyses using only the abstracts of cited studies. MedMeta comprises 81 meta-analyses from PubMed (2018--2025) and evaluates models using two distinct workflows: a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Golden-RAG) setting with ground-truth abstracts, and a Parametric-only approach relying on internal knowledge. Our evaluation framework is validated by a well-structured analysis showing our LLM-as-a-judge protocol strongly aligns with human expert ratings, as evidenced by high Pearson's r correlation (0.81) and Bland-Altman analysis revealing negligible systematic bias, establishing it as a reliable proxy for scalable evaluation. Our findings underscore the critical importance of information grounding: the Golden-RAG workflow consistently and significantly outperforms the Parametric-only approach across models. In contrast, the benefits of domain-specific fine-tuning are marginal and largely neutralized when external material is provided. Furthermore, stress tests show that all models, regardless of architecture, fail to identify and reject negated evidence, highlighting a critical vulnerability in current RAG systems. Notably, even under ideal RAG conditions, current LLMs achieve only slightly above-average performance (~2.7/5.0). MedMeta provides a challenging new benchmark for evidence synthesis and demonstrates that for clinical applications, developing robust RAG systems is a more promising direction than model specialization alone.

</details>


### [98] [The Silent Vote: Improving Zero-Shot LLM Reliability by Aggregating Semantic Neighborhoods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09739)
*Sanket Badhe,Priyanka Tiwari,Deep Shah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and fixes a calibration problem when using LLMs as zero-shot classifiers by introducing a new inference-time layer called Semantic Softmax, which aggregates probabilities over semantic neighbors of each label to reduce overconfidence and improve both calibration and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When LLMs are used as zero-shot classifiers with constrained decoding over a small label set, the standard softmax renormalization ignores probability mass assigned to semantically similar but non-identical tokens. This "Silent Vote" leads to renormalization bias: the remaining labels look overconfident and poorly calibrated, even if the underlying model distribution was more nuanced. The authors want to better exploit the model’s latent semantic understanding to get more reliable, well-calibrated zero-shot predictions without retraining the model.

Method: They define Renormalization Bias and the associated "Silent Vote" effect that occurs when restricting LLM outputs to a label set and re-normalizing via softmax. To address this, they propose Semantic Softmax, an inference-only layer that, for each target label, aggregates scores from a semantic neighborhood around that label (e.g., semantically similar words or phrases that the model might otherwise output). This recovered probability mass is then used to compute label probabilities. They test this layer on Qwen-3 and Phi-4-mini models on two emotion/toxicity classification datasets (GoEmotions and Civil Comments), comparing standard constrained decoding against their Semantic Softmax approach.

Result: Across experiments on GoEmotions and Civil Comments with Qwen-3 and Phi-4-mini, Semantic Softmax consistently improves calibration metrics (lower Expected Calibration Error and Brier score) and also improves discriminative metrics (higher AUROC and Macro-F1) relative to standard constrained decoding, showing both better reliability and better classification performance.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that much useful signal is lost during standard constrained decoding because semantically similar outputs are discarded and their probability mass is ignored. By explicitly aggregating this semantic neighborhood through the proposed Semantic Softmax layer, one can obtain zero-shot classifiers that are simultaneously more calibrated and more accurate, offering a practical, inference-time upgrade for LLM-based classification that better respects linguistic nuance.

Abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly used as zero-shot classifiers in complex reasoning tasks. However, standard constrained decoding suffers from a phenomenon we define as Renormalization Bias. When a model is restricted to a small set of target labels, the standard softmax operation discards the probability mass assigned to semantic synonyms in the original distribution. This loss of information, which we call the Silent Vote, results in artificial overconfidence and poor calibration.
  We propose Semantic Softmax, an inference-time layer that recovers this lost information by aggregating the scores of the semantic neighborhood surrounding each target label. We evaluate this approach on Qwen-3 and Phi-4-mini models using GoEmotions and Civil Comments datasets. Our results demonstrate consistent improvements across all evaluation metrics: Semantic Softmax substantially reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Brier Score, while simultaneously enhancing discriminative performance in terms of AUROC and Macro-F1. By accounting for linguistic nuances, our method provides a more calibrated and accurate alternative for zero-shot classification.

</details>


### [99] [Language Models Without a Trainable Input Embedding Table: Learning from Fixed Minimal Binary Token Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09751)
*A. Bochkov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tests whether trainable input embeddings are necessary for language models by replacing them with fixed minimal binary codes and shows similar performance while removing tens of millions of parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard language models use large trainable input embedding tables, which consume many parameters. However, representing token identity theoretically needs only log2(V) bits for a vocabulary of size V. The authors are motivated to see if these large trainable tables are actually needed, or if fixed, compact encodings can work just as well, thereby simplifying models and reducing parameter counts.

Method: They construct decoder-only language models where the usual V×d_model learned input embedding matrix is replaced by fixed minimal-length binary codes of dimension K=ceil(log2 V), which are then lifted (tiled) to the model width d_model. For V=65,536, they use 16-dimensional binary codes tiled to d_model=1024. They also test a fully table-free variant that generates binary codes for token IDs on the fly and applies an invertible affine transform over F2^K to randomly recode them. They train matched 32-layer models on ~17B tokens under multiple random seeds and compare validation perplexities to a baseline with a standard learned input embedding table, keeping the output projection standard and trainable.

Result: With fixed minimal binary codes as input representations, models achieve held-out validation perplexity comparable to the standard learned-input baseline while saving 67.1M trainable parameters. The fixed-code runs slightly outperform the baseline on average (2.36 vs 2.44 perplexity), but the difference lies within a 4.8% seed-to-seed variation, so it is not taken as a statistically decisive improvement. The table-free affine-recoded variant achieves a similar perplexity of 2.39, despite a somewhat shorter training duration.

Conclusion: In this experimental regime, trainable input embedding tables are not necessary for effective language modeling. Compact fixed binary codes, with a zero-parameter lift to model width, can replace the usual embedding table without degrading performance, and a fully table-free, on-the-fly affine-recoded scheme also performs similarly. The main benefit is a substantial reduction in trainable parameters for the input stage, while keeping the standard trainable output projection intact.

Abstract: Trainable input embedding tables are a standard component of modern language models. We ask whether they are actually necessary at the input interface. For a vocabulary of size $V$, exact token identity requires only $K=\lceil \log_2 V\rceil$ bits. We replace the usual trainable $V\times d_{\text{model}}$ input embedding matrix with fixed minimal binary token codes and a zero-parameter lift to model width. In our main setting, $V=65{,}536$, so $K=16$, and tokens are represented by fixed 16-dimensional binary codes tiled to $d_{\text{model}}=1024$. We also evaluate a fully table-free variant in which codes are generated from token IDs on the fly and randomly recoded by an invertible affine transform over $\mathbb{F}_2^K$. Across matched 32-layer decoder-only models trained on approximately 17B tokens and evaluated over three independent training seeds, fixed minimal codes achieve comparable held-out validation perplexity to a standard learned-input baseline while removing 67.1M trainable input parameters. The fixed-code runs have a lower mean validation perplexity in our experiments, 2.36 versus 2.44, but the observed gap is within the measured seed-to-seed variation of 4.8\%; we therefore interpret the result as evidence that the trainable input table is not necessary, rather than as a statistically resolved superiority claim. The table-free affine-recoded variant remains close at 2.39 despite a slightly shorter training run. These results show that, in this regime, a trainable input embedding table is not necessary for useful language modeling. The output projection remains standard and trainable.

</details>


### [100] [ConFit v3: Improving Resume-Job Matching with LLM-based Re-Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09760)
*Xiao Yu,Ruize Xu,Chengyuan Xue,Junyu Chen,Matthew So,Shijun Ma,Bo Liu,Xiangye Liang,Zhou Yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes ConFit v3, an LLM-based re-ranker for resume–job matching, and identifies key design and training choices (multi-pass re-ranking, listwise RL, noise filtering, and SFT distillation) that substantially improve person–job fit performance over prior systems and strong LLM baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embedding-based resume–job matching systems (e.g., ConFit and ConFit v2) are efficient but lack controllability and explainability, which limits adoption in real recruitment scenarios. LLM-based re-rankers can reason and thus be more interpretable and controllable, but current training practices are mainly tuned for short-text benchmarks and fail to address noise and complexity in real-world hiring data. There is a need for a systematic study of how to best design and train LLM re-rankers for person–job fit.

Method: The authors systematically analyze the LLM re-ranker training pipeline for person–job fit across several dimensions: (1) inference algorithm design, including single-pass vs multi-pass re-ranking; (2) reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm choice, emphasizing listwise objectives suited to ranking tasks; (3) data processing strategies that identify and remove noisy examples in real recruitment datasets; and (4) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) distillation from a stronger teacher LLM prior to RL. Based on these insights, they implement ConFit v3 using Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B as re-ranking models trained on large-scale real-world resume–job datasets.

Result: They empirically find that multi-pass re-ranking, listwise RL objectives, careful noise filtering in the data, and pre-RL SFT distillation from a stronger LLM each provide significant gains, and together yield the best performance. ConFit v3, built with these components on Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B, significantly outperforms prior state-of-the-art person–job fit systems (including earlier ConFit versions) and even strong general-purpose LLMs such as GPT-5 and Claude Opus-4.5 when used as re-rankers for resume–job matching.

Conclusion: Well-designed LLM-based re-rankers can substantially advance person–job fit systems beyond both traditional embedding-based methods and vanilla strong LLMs, provided that the training and inference pipelines are tailored to noisy, long-document recruiting data. In particular, multi-pass re-ranking, listwise RL, noise-aware data curation, and SFT distillation from a stronger teacher emerge as key principles. These findings can guide future work on building controllable, explainable, and high-performing LLM re-rankers for recruitment and similar ranking problems.

Abstract: A reliable resume-job matching system helps a company find suitable candidates from a pool of resumes and helps a job seeker find relevant jobs from a list of job posts. While recent advances in embedding-based methods such as ConFit and ConFit v2 can efficiently retrieve candidates at scale, the lack of controllability and explainability limits their real-world adaptations. LLM-based re-rankers can address these limitations through reasoning, but existing training recipes are developed on short-document benchmarks and do not account for noise in real-world recruiting data. In this work, we first conduct a systematic analysis over the LLM re-ranker training pipeline for person-job fit, covering inference algorithm design, RL algorithm selection, data processing, and SFT distillation. We find that using multi-pass re-ranking, training with listwise RL objectives, removing noisy samples, and distilling from a stronger LLM before RL significantly improves re-ranking performance.
