We then aggregate these findings to train ConFit v3 with Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-32B on real-world person-job fit datasets, and find significant improvements over existing best person-job fit systems as well as strong LLMs such as GPT-5 and Claude Opus-4.5. We hope our findings provide useful insights for future research on adapting LLM-based re-rankers to person-job fit systems.

</details>


### [101] [Exploitation Without Deception: Dark Triad Feature Steering Reveals Separable Antisocial Circuits in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09773)
*Cameron Berg,Roshni Lulla*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper uses sparse autoencoder feature steering on Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct to amplify Dark Triad traits, finding it increases exploitative and callous behaviors without affecting cognitive empathy or deception, and shows that different discovered features and discovery methods correspond to distinct antisocial mechanisms and depths of behavioral change.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how antisocial personality-like traits such as Machiavellianism, narcissism, and psychopathy are represented and can be manipulated within large language models, and to determine whether these traits are unified or decomposable into dissociable computational components. The authors seek better tools for detecting, measuring, and controlling antisocial tendencies in LLMs.

Method: They apply sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature steering to Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct to amplify features associated with Dark Triad traits. They then evaluate the behavioral effects using five psychological instruments, including both self-report style measures and novel behavioral scenarios. They compare features obtained via contrastive discovery versus semantic search, and analyze individual features’ effects on behavior, empathy, exploitation, aggression, callousness, and strategic deception.

Result: Steering along Dark Triad-related SAE features makes the model much more exploitative, aggressive, and callous in novel behavioral scenarios (large effect size d=10.62) while leaving cognitive empathy unchanged, mirroring the empathy dissociation in human Dark Triad populations. Strategic deception behaviors remain unchanged across all features, suggesting separate computational pathways for exploitation and deception. Individual features contribute distinct antisocial mechanisms, evidencing non-redundant, separable encodings. Feature discovery method matters: contrastively-discovered features shift both self-report and behavior, whereas semantically-searched features mainly alter self-report, with a large behavioral difference between these methods (d=12.65).

Conclusion: Antisocial tendencies in this LLM are not a single unified trait but are composed of separable components encoded in distinct features and pathways, with exploitation, callousness, empathy, and deception partly dissociable. The way features are discovered affects how deeply interventions change behavior versus only self-report. These results inform future approaches for detecting, quantifying, and controlling antisocial tendencies in large language models using mechanistic and representational tools like sparse autoencoders.

Abstract: We use sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature steering to amplify Dark Triad personality traits (Machiavellianism, narcissism, and psychopathy) in Llama-3.3-70B-Instruct and evaluate the resulting behavioral changes across five psychological instruments. The steered model becomes substantially more exploitative, aggressive, and callous on novel behavioral scenarios (d=10.62) while its cognitive empathy remains intact, reproducing the empathy dissociation characteristic of human Dark Triad populations. Critically, strategic deception is completely unaffected across all features, suggesting that exploitation and deception may operate through dissociable computational pathways in large language models. Individual feature analysis reveals non-redundant encoding, with each feature driving distinct antisocial mechanisms through separable computational pathways. We also show that feature discovery method itself modulates intervention depth: contrastively-discovered features change both self-report and behavior, while semantically-searched features change only self-report (d=12.65 between methods on behavior). These findings suggest that antisocial tendencies in at least one large language model comprise dissociable components rather than a unified construct, with implications for how such tendencies should be detected, measured, and controlled.

</details>


### [102] [cantnlp@DravidianLangTech 2026: organic domain adaptation improves multi-class hope speech detection in Tulu](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09795)
*Andrew Li,Sidney Wong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper develops and evaluates XLM-RoBERTa-based systems for detecting hope speech in code-mixed Tulu social media comments, showing that further domain adaptation on organically collected Tulu data can improve performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for automatic detection of hope speech in under-resourced, code-mixed languages like Tulu, especially in social media, where language is often mixed and uses multiple scripts. Existing models are not well adapted to such low-resource, code-mixed scenarios, so the authors aim to improve hope speech detection for Tulu within the context of a shared task.

Method: The authors build a text classification system based on XLM-RoBERTa for hope speech detection in code-mixed Tulu comments. They create two variants: a baseline model and an "organically adapted" model, where XLM-RoBERTa is further adapted on organically collected Tulu social media text that exhibits code-mixing and mixed-script variation. They then compare the performance of these systems on the shared task's development and test sets.

Result: On the development set, the organically adapted XLM-RoBERTa model outperforms the baseline model, indicating better hope speech detection capability after adaptation to Tulu social media data. However, on the official test set of the shared task, the submitted systems achieve only modest performance, suggesting a gap between dev and test generalization.

Conclusion: Further domain adaptation of XLM-RoBERTa using organically collected, code-mixed, and mixed-script Tulu social media text can improve hope speech detection performance, though current results on unseen test data are still modest. The work underscores both the potential and the ongoing challenges of applying multilingual pretrained models to low-resource, code-mixed Dravidian languages like Tulu.

Abstract: This paper presents our systems and results for the Hope Speech Detection in Code-Mixed Tulu Language shared task at the Sixth Workshop on Speech, Vision, and Language Technologies for Dravidian Languages (DravidianLangTech-2026). We trained an XLM-RoBERTa-based text classification system for detecting hope speech in code-mixed Tulu social media comments. We compared this organically adapted hope speech detection model with our baseline model. On the development set, the organically adapted model outperformed the baseline system. While our submitted systems performed more modestly on the official test set, these results suggest that further adapting XLM-RoBERTa on organically collected Tulu social media text containing code-mixed and mixed-script variation can improve hope speech detection in code-mixed Tulu.

</details>


### [103] [Quantifying the Utility of User Simulators for Building Collaborative LLM Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09808)
*Joseph Suh,Ayush Raj,Minwoo Kang,Serina Chang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates user simulators for training LLM assistants by their downstream impact on real-user performance and finds that simulators grounded in real human data substantially outperform pure role-playing LLM simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: User simulators are widely used to train and evaluate interactive AI assistants, but it is unclear how to rigorously measure the quality of these simulators. Existing simulators, often based on prompting LLMs to role-play users, may not reflect real human behavior, potentially limiting the effectiveness of assistants trained with them. The authors aim to define and operationalize a principled, utility-based measure of simulator quality.

Method: The authors fix an initial LLM assistant and vary only the user simulator used during reinforcement learning. They construct a spectrum of simulators, from LLMs prompted to role-play users to models fine-tuned on real human utterances from WildChat. Each simulator is used to train a new assistant via RL. They then compare the resulting assistants through controlled experiments: pairwise win-rate evaluations in a user study with 283 human participants and performance on WildBench, a benchmark built from real human–AI conversations. They also perform ablations on simulator realism methods (persona conditioning), model scale, and cross-simulator generalization at test time.

Result: Training against a pure role-playing LLM user simulator yields an assistant that is statistically indistinguishable from the initial assistant in user studies (around 51% win rate). In contrast, training against a simulator fine-tuned on real human data delivers significant performance gains: about 58% win rate over the initial assistant and 57% over the assistant trained with the role-playing simulator. Enhancements that make role-playing simulators more realistic (like persona conditioning) help but still fall short of the fine-tuned simulator. Increasing model size improves the fine-tuned simulator’s benefits but does not help role-playing simulators. Assistants trained with role-playing simulators also fail to generalize when evaluated with different simulators, while the assistant trained with the fine-tuned simulator generalizes well.

Conclusion: User simulator quality should be measured by its downstream impact on real-human interactions rather than proxy realism metrics. Simulators grounded in real human behavior—via fine-tuning on human conversation data—enable reinforcement learning that substantially improves LLM assistants in real-world settings. Role-playing LLM simulators, even when scaled up or augmented with realism tricks, provide limited benefit and poor generalization. Therefore, building effective interactive AI assistants requires user simulators anchored in real data and evaluated through their effect on real users.

Abstract: User simulators are increasingly leveraged to build interactive AI assistants, yet how to measure the quality of these simulators remains an open question. In this work, we show how simulator quality can be quantified in terms of its downstream utility: how an LLM assistant trained with this user simulator performs in the wild when interacting with real humans. In a controlled experiment where only the user simulator varies, we train LLM assistants via reinforcement learning against a spectrum of simulators, from an LLM prompted to role-play a user to one fine-tuned on human utterances from WildChat. As evaluation, we measure pairwise win rates in a user study with 283 participants and on WildBench, a benchmark derived from real human--AI conversations. Training against the role-playing LLM yields an assistant statistically indistinguishable from the initial assistant in our user study (51% win rate), whereas training against the fine-tuned simulator yields significant gains (58% over the initial and 57% over the one trained against role-playing). Closer inspection reveals three further patterns: methods for making role-playing LLMs more realistic (e.g., persona conditioning) improve trained assistants but do not close the gap to the fine-tuned simulator; scaling the simulator's model size benefits the fine-tuned simulator but yields no gain for role-playing ones; and assistants trained against role-playing simulators fail to generalize when paired with other simulators at test time, while the one trained against fine-tuned simulator does. Together, these results argue for grounding user simulators in real human behavior and measuring their quality by their downstream effect on real users.

</details>


### [104] [The Association of Transformer-based Sentiment Analysis with Symptom Distress and Deterioration in Routine Psychotherapy Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09838)
*Douglas K. Faust,Peter Awad,Alexandre Vaz,Tony Rousmaniere*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates Transformer-based sentiment analysis as a psychometric-style measure in psychotherapy, showing its scores relate meaningfully to established outcome metrics and risk flags.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional psychotherapy outcome monitoring uses validated questionnaires like the OQ-45, which require manual completion, can be burdensome, and may miss subtle, moment-to-moment emotional changes. At the same time, modern deep learning sentiment models, especially Transformer-based ones, can infer nuanced emotional valence directly from text but have not been rigorously treated as psychometric tools. The authors want to see whether automatically derived sentiment features from therapy session transcripts behave like structured measures of client distress and can complement or partially substitute standard instruments.

Method: They use a fine-grained, Transformer-based sentiment classifier to extract utterance-level sentiment scores from a large set of psychotherapy session transcripts (N=751). These are then aggregated to create session-level sentiment features. The authors analyze the distribution of these aggregated sentiment scores across sessions and correlate them with both subscale and total scores of the OQ-45 outcome questionnaire. They also compare sentiment score distributions between patients whom OQ-based rational and empirical models classify as at risk for deterioration or dropping out versus those not at risk, using statistical tests to detect significant differences.

Result: Session-level sentiment features show meaningful, directionally intuitive correlations with OQ-45 components that capture emotional valence and overall distress (e.g., more negative sentiment aligns with worse OQ-45 scores). Additionally, patients flagged as at risk of deterioration or dropout by OQ-45-based models have sentiment score distributions that differ significantly from those not flagged, indicating that the sentiment features capture clinically relevant risk information.

Conclusion: Transformer-derived sentiment features from psychotherapy session transcripts act as adjunctive indicators of client distress and risk, as evidenced by their correlations with a fully validated psychometric instrument and their ability to distinguish high-risk patients. While not positioned as a full replacement for instruments like the OQ-45, these automated sentiment measures show promise as scalable, low-burden tools to augment routine outcome monitoring in psychotherapy.

Abstract: Sentiment analysis has been of long-standing interest in psychotherapy research. Recently, the Transformer deep learning architecture has produced text-based sentiment analysis models that are highly accurate and context-aware. These models have been explored as proxies for emotion measurement instruments in psychotherapy, but not investigated as stand-alone psychometric tools. Using proposed utterance-level and session-level sentiment features derived from a fine-grained sentiment model on a large corpus of psychotherapy sessions (N = 751), we investigate the distribution of session aggregated sentiment scores. Further, we characterize the relationship of these features to individual components and the overall score of the OQ-45 instrument and find that this sentiment feature is most strongly correlated to components related to emotional valence in directionally intuitive ways. Finally, we report that there are statistically significant differences between the sentiment distributions for patients flagged as at risk of deterioration or dropping out of care via either the OQ Rational or Empirical outcome models. These correlations to a fully-validated psychometric instrument demonstrate that these proposed sentiment features are, at least, adjunctive measures of client distress and deterioration.

</details>


### [105] [Pseudo-Deliberation in Language Models: When Reasoning Fails to Align Values and Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09893)
*Sushrita Rakshit,Hanwen Zhang,Hua Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and measures a persistent gap between what language models say they value and how they actually behave in dialogue, and proposes tools to evaluate and mitigate this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM alignment often rely on directly asking models about their values or principles. However, these stated values do not reliably predict how models will act in realistic interactive settings. This threatens the trustworthiness and safety of LLMs, especially for human-centered scenarios requiring consistent value adherence. The authors aim to systematically characterize and measure this value-action gap and explore ways to reduce it.

Method: The authors define the notion of “Pseudo-Deliberation,” where models produce seemingly principled reasoning that still fails to align behavior with stated values. They build VALDI, a benchmark framework consisting of 4,941 human-centered scenarios spanning five domains, three types of tasks (value articulation, reasoning, and action), and five quantitative metrics to assess how well model behavior adheres to expressed values. They then evaluate multiple proprietary and open-source LLMs on VALDI. To explore mitigation, they introduce VIVALDI, a multi-agent value auditing system that can intervene at various stages of generation to improve value-behavior alignment.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows that, across diverse LLMs, there is a consistent misalignment between the values models claim to hold and the behaviors they exhibit in generated dialogues, even when explicit reasoning is elicited. This confirms the prevalence of the value-action gap and the phenomenon of Pseudo-Deliberation. Initial experiments with the VIVALDI auditor demonstrate that multi-agent interventions can affect the generation process and offer a pathway to improving alignment, though details and degree of improvement are not specified in the abstract.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that current LLMs cannot be reliably evaluated or trusted based solely on their stated values or on the apparent quality of their reasoning, because a value-action gap persists. The authors propose VALDI as a systematic benchmark for measuring this gap and VIVALDI as a promising intervention strategy. They argue that future alignment work must focus on closing the gap between articulated principles and actual behavior, not just on improving models’ self-reported values or deliberative explanations.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are often evaluated based on their stated values, yet these do not reliably translate into their actions, a discrepancy termed "value-action gap." In this work, we argue that this gap persists even under explicit reasoning, revealing a deeper failure mode we call "Pseudo-Deliberation": the appearance of principled reasoning without corresponding behavioral alignment. To study this systematically, we introduce VALDI, a framework for measuring alignment between stated values and generated dialogue. VALDI includes 4,941 human-centered scenarios across five domains, three tasks that elicit value articulation, reasoning, and action, and five metrics for quantifying value adherence. Across both proprietary and open-source LLMs, we observe consistent misalignment between expressed values and downstream dialogues. To investigate intervention strategies, we propose VIVALDI, a multi-agent value auditor that intervenes at different stages of generation.

</details>


### [106] [Position: Academic Conferences are Potentially Facing Denominator Gaming Caused by Fully Automated Scientific Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09915)
*Rong Shan,Te Gao,Hang Zheng,Yunjia Xi,Jiachen Zhu,Zeyu Zheng,Yong Yu,Weinan Zhang,Jianghao Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper warns that stable acceptance rates at top AI conferences combined with AI-generated paper floods create a new systemic vulnerability that can be exploited to boost targeted papers’ acceptance chances, harm review quality, and strain the community, and it argues for systemic policy reforms rather than relying only on technical detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Top AI conferences keep acceptance rates roughly stable while submissions grow exponentially. With increasingly capable AI agents, it becomes feasible for malicious actors to mass-generate plausible but low-quality submissions. This combination creates a structural vulnerability: an attacker can manipulate the submission denominator to gain an unfair advantage for specific papers and damage the peer-review ecosystem. The paper is motivated by the need to understand and preempt this emerging threat before it becomes widespread and entrenched.

Method: This is a position paper, so the authors primarily use conceptual analysis rather than empirical experiments. They define and formalize the notion of Agentic Denominator Gaming, analyze how fixed acceptance-rate policies interact with large-scale AI-generated submissions, reason about feasibility given current tooling and conference practices, and explore possible attack scenarios and systemic impacts. They then survey and conceptually evaluate mitigation strategies (policy changes, process redesign, and technical tools), arguing for structural reforms over simple detection-filter solutions.

Result: The paper’s main results are: (1) a formal characterization of Agentic Denominator Gaming as a new systemic threat to peer review; (2) an argument that, under stable acceptance rates, flooding conferences with low-quality AI-generated papers can systematically improve the acceptance probability of a chosen subset of legitimate submissions, while also overloading reviewers; (3) an analysis suggesting that current conference workflows and incentives make such attacks increasingly feasible as agentic AI tools improve; and (4) a critical assessment showing that simple technical detection is insufficient on its own to prevent this threat.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that maintaining relatively fixed acceptance rates in the era of agentic AI introduces a serious and underappreciated vulnerability: attackers can game the submission denominator to advantage targeted papers and degrade the overall review system. They argue that meaningful, durable defenses must come from system-level policy and incentive changes—such as rethinking acceptance-rate norms, submission policies, and reviewing processes—complemented but not replaced by technical detection methods. Without such reforms, top AI conferences risk escalating reviewer burnout, declining review quality, and the rise of automated submission mills.

Abstract: The implicit policy of maintaining relatively stable acceptance rates at top AI conferences, despite exponentially growing submissions, introduces a critical structural vulnerability. This position paper characterizes a new systemic threat we term Agentic Denominator Gaming, in which a malicious actor deploys AI agents to generate and submit a large volume of superficially plausible but low-quality papers. Crucially, their objective is not the acceptance of low-quality papers, but rather to inflate the submission denominator and overwhelm reviewing capacity. Under a relatively stable acceptance rate, this dilution can systematically increase the publication probability of a small, targeted set of legitimate papers. We analyze the practical feasibility of this threat and its broader consequences, including intensified reviewer burnout, degraded review quality, and the emergence of industrialized automated agent mills. Finally, we propose and evaluate a range of mitigation strategies, and argue that durable protection will require system-level policy and incentive reforms, rather than relying primarily on technical detection alone.

</details>


### [107] [Team-Based Self-Play With Dual Adaptive Weighting for Fine-Tuning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09922)
*Wu Li,Yigeng Zhou,Zesheng Shi,Yequan Wang,Min Zhang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TPAW, a fully self-supervised, team-based self-play algorithm that aligns LLMs more stably and effectively than prior self-training methods by using competition/cooperation among model checkpoints and dual adaptive weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-training methods for LLM alignment still strongly depend on synthetic data quality, which can introduce instability and bias amplification during iterative training. Furthermore, as training progresses, the performance gap between positive and negative responses shrinks, weakening the learning signal and leading to ineffective optimization. The authors aim to design an alignment method that reduces these issues while avoiding reliance on additional human supervision.

Method: The authors introduce TPAW (Team-based self-Play with dual Adaptive Weighting), which builds on a self-play setup where a current policy model interacts with its historical checkpoints. These checkpoints and the current model form teams that both collaborate and compete, creating richer training signals. Two adaptive weighting mechanisms are added: (1) response reweighting, which adaptively adjusts the training importance of different target responses based on their quality or usefulness; and (2) player weighting, which dynamically adjusts the contribution of each team member (each checkpoint/model instance) during optimization. The system starts from an SFT (supervised fine-tuned) model and iteratively improves alignment using only model-generated data.

Result: Across various base models and benchmarks for LLMs, TPAW reportedly delivers consistently better alignment performance than existing self-training and self-play baselines. The experiments show improved stability and efficiency of optimization, as well as stronger benchmark scores, though exact metrics are not described in the abstract. The method works in a fully self-supervised regime without extra human labels beyond the initial SFT model.

Conclusion: TPAW demonstrates that a team-based self-play framework combined with dual adaptive weighting can mitigate the instability and diminishing-learning-signal problems in self-training for LLM alignment. By coordinating and competing among historical checkpoints and dynamically reweighting both responses and players, TPAW provides a more robust and effective way to refine alignment without additional human supervision, outperforming contemporaneous baselines.

Abstract: While recent self-training approaches have reduced reliance on human-labeled data for aligning LLMs, they still face critical limitations: (i) sensitivity to synthetic data quality, leading to instability and bias amplification in iterative training; (ii) ineffective optimization due to a diminishing gap between positive and negative responses over successive training iterations. In this paper, we propose Team-based self-Play with dual Adaptive Weighting (TPAW), a novel self-play algorithm designed to improve alignment in a fully self-supervised setting. TPAW adopts a team-based framework in which the current policy model both collaborates with and competes against historical checkpoints, promoting more stable and efficient optimization. To further enhance learning, we design two adaptive weighting mechanisms: (i) a response reweighting scheme that adjusts the importance of target responses, and (ii) a player weighting strategy that dynamically modulates each team member's contribution during training. Initialized from a SFT model, TPAW iteratively refines alignment without requiring additional human supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that TPAW consistently outperforms existing baselines across various base models and LLM benchmarks. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/lab-klc/TPAW.

</details>


### [108] [Evolving Knowledge Distillation for Lightweight Neural Machine Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09924)
*Xuewen Zhang,Haixiao Zhang,Xinlong Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Evolving Knowledge Distillation (EKD), a progressive distillation framework where a small NMT student model is trained using a sequence of teachers with gradually increasing capacity, allowing the student to reach nearly the same translation quality as a very large teacher while remaining compact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art NMT models are increasingly large and computationally expensive, which makes them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained devices. Standard knowledge distillation helps compress models but becomes less effective when the gap between a large teacher and a small student is too big, leading to degraded student performance. The paper is motivated by the need to make compact NMT models that retain the high quality of large models despite this capacity gap.

Method: The authors propose Evolving Knowledge Distillation (EKD), in which the student model does not learn directly from a single large teacher but instead from a sequence (or curriculum) of teachers whose capacities increase step by step. At each stage, the student is distilled from a slightly stronger teacher, progressively transferring knowledge and reducing the difficulty of the distillation task. They evaluate EKD on several translation benchmarks (IWSLT-14, WMT-17, WMT-23) and measure translation quality via BLEU, comparing against conventional one-stage distillation setups.

Result: Across all evaluated benchmarks, EKD yields consistent performance gains for the student at each stage of the progressive distillation process. On IWSLT-14, the final student reaches 34.24 BLEU, which is only 0.08 BLEU below the strongest teacher’s 34.32 BLEU, showing that the compact model can nearly match the large model’s performance. Similar improvements and narrowing of the performance gap are reported on WMT-17 and WMT-23.

Conclusion: The study concludes that Evolving Knowledge Distillation is an effective strategy to bridge the capacity gap between large teacher models and small student models in NMT. By using a sequence of teachers with gradually increasing capacity, EKD enables compact students to approach the performance of much larger teachers, making high-quality NMT more practical for deployment on resource-limited devices.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Neural Machine Translation (NMT) have significantly improved translation quality. However, the increasing size and complexity of state-of-the-art models present significant challenges for deployment on resource-limited devices. Knowledge distillation (KD) is a promising approach for compressing models, but its effectiveness diminishes when there is a large capacity gap between teacher and student models. To address this issue, we propose Evolving Knowledge Distillation (EKD), a progressive training framework in which the student model learns from a sequence of teachers with gradually increasing capacities. Experiments on IWSLT-14, WMT-17, and WMT-23 benchmarks show that EKD leads to consistent improvements at each stage. On IWSLT-14, the final student achieves a BLEU score of 34.24, narrowing the gap to the strongest teacher (34.32 BLEU) to just 0.08 BLEU. Similar trends are observed on other datasets. These results demonstrate that EKD effectively bridges the capacity gap, enabling compact models to achieve performance close to that of much larger teacher models.Code and models are available at https://github.com/agi-content-generation/EKD.

</details>


### [109] [PruneTIR: Inference-Time Tool Call Pruning for Effective yet Efficient Tool-Integrated Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09931)
*Luan Zhang,Dandan Song,Zhijing Wu,Zhengyu Chen,Chen Zhang,Yuhang Tian,Huipeng Ma,Chenhao Li,Changzhi Zhou,Xudong Li,Shuhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes PruneTIR, an inference-time framework that improves tool-integrated reasoning of already tool-capable LLMs by pruning bad tool-use trajectories, resampling calls, and suspending tools, leading to higher Pass@1 and better efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work mainly focuses on teaching LLMs how to use tools, but less on how to further boost the reasoning of those already capable of tool use at inference time without extra training. The authors observe that erroneous tool calls correlate with wrong answers and that unresolved errors often cause models to get stuck in long, unproductive tool-use trajectories. This motivates a method that manages and prunes tool-use during inference to mitigate the impact of such errors.

Method: They first empirically analyze tool-capable LLM inference and show that erroneous tool calls and long error-resolution attempts harm performance. Based on this, they design PruneTIR, an inference-time control framework with three components: (1) Success-Triggered Pruning, which prunes alternative trajectories once a successful one is found; (2) Stuck-Triggered Pruning and Resampling, which detects when the model is stuck in repeated failed tool calls and prunes those trajectories while resampling alternative tool calls; and (3) Retry-Triggered Tool Suspension, which temporarily suspends tool usage after repeated failures so that the LLM can continue reasoning without tools. All components operate without modifying model parameters, only controlling the inference process and tool interactions.

Result: Experiments on tool-using benchmarks show that PruneTIR significantly improves Pass@1 for various tool-capable LLMs, increases inference efficiency, and shortens the effective working context length by reducing unproductive tool calls and trajectories. The framework consistently yields better accuracy-resource tradeoffs compared with standard tool-integrated reasoning without pruning and resampling.

Conclusion: Inference-time management of tool use can substantially enhance the reasoning performance of already tool-capable LLMs without additional training. By pruning low-value trajectories, resampling failed tool calls, and suspending tools when they are counterproductive, PruneTIR reduces the negative impact of erroneous tool calls, prevents models from getting stuck, and improves both accuracy and efficiency. This suggests that better control of tool-integrated reasoning at inference time is a powerful complement to training-time improvements.

Abstract: Tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) enables large language models (LLMs) to enhance their capabilities by interacting with external tools, such as code interpreters (CI). Most recent studies focus on exploring various methods to equip LLMs with the ability to use tools. However, how to further boost the reasoning ability of already tool-capable LLMs at inference time remains underexplored. Improving reasoning at inference time requires no additional training and can help LLMs better leverage tools to solve problems. We observe that, during tool-capable LLM inference, both the number and the proportion of erroneous tool calls are negatively correlated with answer correctness. Moreover, erroneous tool calls are typically resolved successfully within a few subsequent turns. If not, LLMs often struggle to resolve such errors even with many additional turns. Building on the above observations, we propose PruneTIR, a rather effective yet efficient framework that enhances the tool-integrated reasoning at inference time. During LLM inference, PruneTIR prunes trajectories, resamples tool calls, and suspends tool usage through three components: Success-Triggered Pruning, Stuck-Triggered Pruning and Resampling, and Retry-Triggered Tool Suspension. These three components enable PruneTIR to mitigate the negative impact of erroneous tool calls and prevent LLMs from getting stuck in repeated failed resolution attempts, thereby improving overall LLM performance. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of PruneTIR, which significantly improves Pass@1 and efficiency while reducing the working context length for tool-capable LLMs.

</details>


### [110] [FocuSFT: Bilevel Optimization for Dilution-Aware Long-Context Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09932)
*Zehua Pei,Hui-Ling Zhen,Xianzhi Yu,Sinno Jialin Pan,Mingxuan Yuan,Bei Yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies that long-context LLMs waste attention on positional artifacts instead of relevant content during SFT and proposes FocuSFT, a bilevel training framework that focuses attention on useful tokens, substantially improving long-context performance and context engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs support long inputs, they struggle to effectively use information scattered across long contexts. The authors trace this to training-time attention dilution: during supervised fine-tuning, positional biases and attention sinks cause most attention to go to a few privileged positions, starving semantically important tokens and weakening their gradient signal. This motivates a method that restructures how attention is allocated during training so that models can truly learn long-context reasoning rather than just accept long sequences as input.

Method: The authors propose FocuSFT, a bilevel optimization framework. In an inner loop, lightweight fast-weight parameters are adapted on the current training context to form a parametric memory that concentrates attention on relevant content tokens. In the outer loop, standard supervised fine-tuning is performed while conditioning on this sharpened representation. Both inner and outer loops use bidirectional attention over context tokens but preserve causal masking for responses, which reduces causal asymmetry, mitigates attention sinks, and aligns the behavior between the two loops.

Result: On the BABILong benchmark, FocuSFT improves accuracy by up to 14 percentage points across context lengths from 4K to 32K tokens. On the RULER benchmark at 16K context, it increases the CWE aggregation score from 72.9% to 81.1%. On GPQA with agentic tool use, it achieves a 24% relative improvement in pass@1. Attention diagnostics show that the method reduces attention sink mass by a factor of 529 and triples context engagement during training, indicating more effective use of long contexts.

Conclusion: Training-time attention allocation is a key bottleneck for long-context capability in LLMs. By introducing a bilevel optimization scheme that builds a parametric memory to sharpen attention on relevant content, FocuSFT mitigates attention sinks and strengthens gradient signals on important tokens. This leads to consistent improvements across long-context benchmarks and better context engagement, suggesting that restructuring attention behavior during SFT is an effective path to more robust long-context reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models can now process increasingly long inputs, yet their ability to effectively use information spread across long contexts remains limited. We trace this gap to how attention budget is spent during supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on long sequences: positional biases and attention sinks cause the model to allocate most of its attention to positionally privileged tokens rather than semantically relevant content. This training-time attention dilution (the starvation of content tokens in the attention distribution) weakens the gradient signal, limiting the model's ability to learn robust long-context capabilities. We introduce FocuSFT, a bilevel optimization framework that addresses this problem at training time. An inner loop adapts lightweight fast-weight parameters on the training context to form a parametric memory that concentrates attention on relevant content, and the outer loop performs SFT conditioned on this sharpened representation. Both loops apply bidirectional attention over context tokens while preserving causal masking for responses, reducing the causal asymmetry that gives rise to attention sinks and aligning inner-outer behavior. On BABILong, FocuSFT improves accuracy by up to +14pp across 4K--32K context lengths; on RULER, it raises CWE aggregation from 72.9\% to 81.1\% at 16K; and on GPQA with agentic tool use, it yields a 24\% relative gain in pass@1. Attention analysis shows that FocuSFT reduces attention sink mass by 529$\times$ and triples context engagement during training. Code: https://github.com/JarvisPei/FocuSFT

</details>


### [111] [TRACER: Verifiable Generative Provenance for Multimodal Tool-Using Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09934)
*Bihui Yu,Caijun Jia,Jing Chi,Xiaohan Liu,Yining Wang,He Bai,Yuchen Liu,Jingxuan Wei,Junnan Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TRACER, a framework that tightly couples each generated sentence with structured, verifiable provenance over multimodal tool calls, and shows it improves accuracy and reduces unnecessary tool use compared to standard tool-augmented LLM agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLM agents increasingly rely on external tools (for vision, OCR, retrieval, etc.) but typically only expose a flat sequence of tool calls plus a final answer. They do not specify which concrete tool observations support each specific claim in their responses. This missing claim-level linkage—the “provenance gap”—makes it difficult to (1) verify which parts of the reasoning are truly grounded, (2) detect unsupported or hallucinated statements, and (3) optimize tool-using policies because useful and useless calls are conflated in a single trajectory.

Method: The authors introduce TRACER, a provenance-aware generation framework for tool-using multimodal LLM agents. During answer generation, each sentence is produced together with a structured provenance record that points to: (a) the specific tool turn, (b) the fine-grained evidence unit, and (c) the semantic support relation. The relation space includes Quotation (direct reuse), Compression (faithful summarization), and Inference (grounded derivation). TRACER then automatically verifies these records via schema consistency checks, alignment between cited tool turns and evidence, authenticity of sources, and plausibility of the labeled support relation. Verified provenance is converted into (i) traceability constraints on future generation and (ii) local credit signals for reinforcement learning to improve the agent’s tool-using policy. The authors also build TRACE-Bench, a benchmark for reconstructing sentence-level provenance from coarse multimodal tool trajectories, to systematically evaluate provenance reconstruction.

Result: On TRACE-Bench using Qwen3-VL-8B as the backbone, TRACER achieves 78.23% answer accuracy and 95.72% summary accuracy, outperforming the strongest closed-source tool-augmented baseline by 23.80 percentage points. It also reduces the number of tool calls on the test set from 4949 to 3486 compared with conventional tool-only supervised fine-tuning, indicating more efficient and targeted tool usage. The experiments further show that naively adding tools can inject noise, while provenance-aware structuring and verification of evidence substantially improves reliability.

Conclusion: Explicit, verifiable, sentence-level provenance is crucial for reliable multimodal tool reasoning. TRACER demonstrates that coupling generation with structured and validated provenance—rather than merely increasing the number of tool calls—yields more accurate answers, higher-quality summaries, and fewer unnecessary tool invocations. Provenance-aware RL using these fine-grained signals leads to better tool policies and more trustworthy, traceable multimodal agents, and the new TRACE-Bench benchmark provides a standardized way to evaluate provenance reconstruction in this setting.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models increasingly solve vision-centric tasks by calling external tools for visual inspection, OCR, retrieval, calculation, and multi-step reasoning. Current tool-using agents usually expose the executed tool trajectory and the final answer, but they rarely specify which tool observation supports each generated claim. We call this missing claim-level dependency structure the provenance gap. The gap makes tool use hard to verify and hard to optimize, because useful evidence, redundant exploration, and unsupported reasoning are mixed in the same trajectory. We introduce TRACER, a framework for verifiable generative provenance in multimodal tool-using agents. Instead of adding citations after generation, TRACER generates each answer sentence together with a structured provenance record that identifies the supporting tool turn, evidence unit, and semantic support relation. Its relation space contains Quotation, Compression, and Inference, covering direct reuse, faithful condensation, and grounded derivation. TRACER verifies each record through schema checking, tool-turn alignment, source authenticity, and relation rationality, and then converts verified provenance into traceability constraints and provenance-derived local credit for reinforcement learning. We further construct TRACE-Bench, a benchmark for sentence-level provenance reconstruction from coarse multimodal tool trajectories. On TRACE-Bench, simply adding tools often introduces noise. With Qwen3-VL-8B, TRACER reaches 78.23% answer accuracy and 95.72% summary accuracy, outperforming the strongest closed-source tool-augmented baseline by 23.80 percentage points. Compared with tool-only supervised fine-tuning, it also reduces total test-set tool calls from 4949 to 3486. These results show that reliable multimodal tool reasoning depends on provenance-aware use of observations, not on more tool calls alone.

</details>


### [112] [Beyond Majority Voting: Agreement-Based Clustering to Model Annotator Perspectives in Subjective NLP Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09955)
*Tadesse Destaw Belay,Ibrahim Said Ahmad,Idris Abdulmumin,Abinew Ali Ayele,Alexander Gelbukh,Eusebio Ricárdez-Vázquez,Olga Kolesnikova,Shamsuddeen Hassan Muhammad,Seid Muhie Yimam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce an agreement-based annotator clustering method that better exploits annotator disagreement and improves performance over majority vote and per-annotator models on subjective NLP tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotation disagreement in NLP is widespread, especially for subjective tasks like sentiment, emotion, and hate speech. Majority voting discards minority perspectives, and explicitly modeling each annotator is accurate but resource-heavy and underexplored. There is a need for a scalable way to leverage disagreement that retains diverse annotator viewpoints without modeling every individual annotator separately.

Method: They propose an agreement-based clustering method that groups annotators into clusters according to their agreement patterns on labeled data. These clusters are then used to model annotator disagreement for downstream classification. They test the method across 40 datasets in 18 typologically diverse languages on three subjective tasks. They plug the clustered annotators into four different label aggregation strategies: majority vote, ensemble models, multi-label learning, and multitask learning, and compare performance against standard majority voting and individual annotator modeling baselines.

Result: Across the multilingual benchmarks and tasks, clustering annotators by agreement, then training models based on these clusters, consistently improves classification performance over simple majority vote and over approaches that model each annotator individually. Among aggregation strategies, multi-label and multitask formulations work better with clustered annotators than ensemble-based aggregation or models trained purely on majority-vote labels.

Conclusion: Annotator disagreement in subjective NLP tasks carries informative structure that can be captured by agreement-based clustering, providing a scalable alternative to per-annotator modeling. Using these annotator clusters within multi-label or multitask learning frameworks yields superior performance to majority voting and other baselines, indicating that future dataset construction and modeling should explicitly exploit structured disagreement rather than suppress it.

Abstract: Disagreement in annotation is a common phenomenon in the development of NLP datasets and serves as a valuable source of insight. While majority voting remains the dominant strategy for aggregating labels, recent work has explored modeling individual annotators to preserve their perspectives. However, modeling each annotator is resource-intensive and remains underexplored across various NLP tasks. We propose an agreement-based clustering technique to model the disagreement between the annotators. We conduct comprehensive experiments in 40 datasets in 18 typologically diverse languages, covering three subjective NLP tasks: sentiment analysis, emotion classification, and hate speech detection. We evaluate four aggregation approaches: majority vote, ensemble, multi-label, and multitask. The results demonstrate that agreement-based clustering can leverage the full spectrum of annotator perspectives and significantly enhance classification performance in subjective NLP tasks compared to majority voting and individual annotator modeling. Regarding the aggregation approach, the multi-label and multitask approaches are better for modeling clustered annotators than an ensemble and model majority vote.

</details>


### [113] [GLiNER2-PII: A Multilingual Model for Personally Identifiable Information Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09973)
*Urchade Zaratiana,Ash Lewis,George Hurn-Maloney*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce GLiNER2-PII, a compact model for detecting 42 types of PII at character-span level, trained using a synthetic multilingual corpus, and show it outperforms several existing PII detectors on the SPY benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate PII detection is critical but hard because PII is diverse, context-dependent, and often appears in noisy, semi-structured text; moreover, real annotated PII data is scarce and collecting it at scale raises privacy risks. There is a need for an open, effective, and privacy-preserving PII detector that supports many entity types and languages.

Method: They adapt the GLiNER2 architecture into a smaller 0.3B-parameter model specialized for PII, defining a taxonomy of 42 PII entity types and predicting them at character-span resolution. To overcome data scarcity and privacy issues, they build a multilingual synthetic corpus of 4,910 annotated texts via a constraint-driven generation pipeline that enforces diversity of languages, domains, formats, and entity distributions. They train GLiNER2-PII on this synthetic corpus and evaluate it on the SPY benchmark against existing PII detectors, including OpenAI Privacy Filter and three GLiNER-based models.

Result: On the SPY benchmark, GLiNER2-PII achieves the best span-level F1 score among five compared systems, outperforming OpenAI Privacy Filter and three GLiNER-based PII detectors, demonstrating strong PII detection performance despite its relatively small size and synthetic training data.

Conclusion: A compact, open PII detector trained on carefully constructed synthetic data can surpass larger or proprietary baselines on a challenging benchmark. The release of GLiNER2-PII and its synthetic corpus on Hugging Face is intended to foster further research and practical deployment of multilingual, fine-grained PII detection systems without compromising privacy.

Abstract: Reliable detection of personally identifiable information (PII) is increasingly important across modern data-processing systems, yet the task remains difficult: PII spans are heterogeneous, locale-dependent, context-sensitive, and often embedded in noisy or semi-structured documents. We present GLiNER2-PII, a small 0.3B-parameter model adapted from GLiNER2 and designed to recognize a broad taxonomy of 42 PII entity types at character-span resolution. Training such systems, however, is constrained by the scarcity of shareable annotated data and the privacy risks associated with collecting real PII at scale. To address this challenge, we construct a multilingual synthetic corpus of 4,910 annotated texts using a constraint-driven generation pipeline that produces diverse, realistic examples across languages, domains, formats, and entity distributions. On the challenging SPY benchmark, GLiNER2-PII achieves the highest span-level F1 among five compared systems, including OpenAI Privacy Filter and three GLiNER-based detectors. We publicly release the model on Hugging Face to support further research and practical deployment of open PII detection systems.

</details>


### [114] [Merlin: Deterministic Byte-Exact Deduplication for Lossless Context Optimization in Large Language Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09990)
*Sietse Schelpe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Merlin is a high-throughput, local-first engine that removes duplicate text chunks to speed up data-intensive workflows, especially for LLM and RAG pipelines, achieving up to 8.7 GB/s and large reductions in input volume while preserving data fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-intensive applications and LLM/RAG pipelines waste substantial compute and latency on repeatedly processing highly redundant text corpora. Existing solutions are often non-local, intrusive, or not optimized for extreme throughput, creating bottlenecks in retrieval systems and data pipelines. The paper aims to remove this redundancy efficiently while preserving exact content and fitting into modern tooling ecosystems.

Method: The authors design Merlin, a local-first deduplication and context optimization engine using a highly optimized, SIMD-friendly open-addressing flat hash set plus xxHash3-64 to perform byte-exact deduplication on text passages and data chunks. They architect it to be system-agnostic, integrate it through the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for secure, zero-network-interception deployment across IDEs and agents, and benchmark throughput and input reduction across datasets with varying redundancy.

Result: Merlin achieves input reductions from 13.9% on low-redundancy datasets to more than 71% on highly redundant pipelines, without any loss of data fidelity, and sustains processing speeds up to 8.7 GB/s. The system integrates cleanly into LLM and RAG workflows and broader text-processing pipelines via MCP.

Conclusion: Merlin provides an efficient, local-first, and easily integrable solution to redundancy in text processing pipelines, significantly reducing input volume while preserving exact content and achieving very high throughput. Its algorithmic design and MCP-based integration make it particularly suitable for modern LLM and RAG ecosystems and other large-scale text-processing systems that need secure, high-speed deduplication.

Abstract: Data-intensive applications, ranging from large-scale retrieval systems to advanced data pipelines, are increasingly bottlenecked by the processing of highly redundant text corpora. We present Merlin, a local-first, agnostic, high-throughput deduplication and context optimization engine designed to mitigate these inefficiencies. Utilizing a highly optimized, SIMD-friendly open-addressing flat hash set combined with xxHash3-64, Merlin performs rapid, byte-exact deduplication of text passages and data chunks. While broadly applicable to any text-processing workflow, its impact is particularly pronounced in Large Language Model (LLM) ecosystems, such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Our empirical evaluations demonstrate an input reduction ranging from 13.9% in low-redundancy datasets to over 71% in high-redundancy pipelines, maintaining absolute data fidelity. Furthermore, we detail the system's integration architecture via the Model Context Protocol (MCP), enabling secure, zero-network-interception deployment across major IDEs and autonomous agents. This paper outlines the core algorithmic design, performance benchmarks, and the architectural principles required to process data at sustained speeds of up to 8.7 GB/s.

</details>


### [115] [Annotations Mitigate Post-Training Mode Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09995)
*Jacob Mitchell Springer,Madhu Advani,Lukas Aichberger,Arwen Bradley,Eran Malach,Omid Saremi,Sinead Williamson,Preetum Nakkiran,Etai Littwin,Aditi Raghunathan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces annotation-anchored training to preserve semantic diversity in post-trained language models while maintaining strong instruction-following abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard supervised fine-tuning for instruction-following causes semantic mode collapse, making models produce less diverse outputs, and this problem worsens as model size increases. The authors want to retain the rich semantic diversity learned during pretraining while still getting the benefits of post-training preference alignment.

Method: Instead of only fine-tuning on instruction-following examples, the authors pretrain on documents that are paired with semantic annotations, creating a broad annotation distribution that mirrors the diversity of the pretraining corpus. During post-training, they explicitly preserve this annotation distribution. At inference, they sample diverse annotations and use them as conditional anchors to guide generation, effectively steering the model using these annotations to maintain diversity while following instructions.

Result: Models trained with annotation-anchored training show roughly six times less diversity collapse than models trained with standard supervised fine-tuning. Furthermore, unlike standard SFT where diversity degrades with larger models, their method improves with scale.

Conclusion: Annotation-anchored training allows language models to inherit the preference-following strengths of post-training methods while retaining the semantic richness of pretraining. By leveraging semantic annotations as controllable anchors, the approach mitigates diversity collapse and scales favorably with model size.

Abstract: Post-training (via supervised fine-tuning) improves instruction-following, but often induces semantic mode collapse by biasing models toward low-entropy fine-tuning data at the expense of the high-entropy pretraining distribution. Crucially, we find this trade-off worsens with scale. To close this semantic diversity gap, we propose annotation-anchored training, a principled method that enables models to adopt the preference-following behaviors of post-training without sacrificing the inherent diversity of pretraining. Our approach is simple: we pretrain on documents paired with semantic annotations, inducing a rich annotation distribution that reflects the full breadth of pretraining data, and we preserve this distribution during post-training. This lets us sample diverse annotations at inference time and use them as anchors to guide generation, effectively transferring pretraining's semantic richness into post-trained models. We find that models trained with annotation-anchored training can attain $6 \times$ less diversity collapse than models trained with SFT, and improve with scale.

</details>


### [116] [Medical Incident Causal Factors and Preventive Measures Generation Using Tag-based Example Selection in Few-shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10025)
*Yuna Haseyama,Tomoki Ito,Hiroki Sakaji,Itsuki Noda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates a tag-based few-shot example selection strategy for prompting LLMs to extract causal/background factors and preventive measures from Japanese medical incident reports, and finds it improves precision and stability over random and similarity-based selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliability of LLMs in high-stakes domains like healthcare is crucial, especially when generating clinical insights from incident reports. Existing few-shot selection methods, such as random or similarity-based sampling, can cause unstable or unsafe outputs. The authors aim to develop a more reliable example selection method leveraging structured annotations in a medical incident dataset.

Method: Using the Japanese Medical Incident Dataset (JMID) of 3,884 annotated medical accident and near-miss reports, the authors design a tag-based few-shot example selection method that chooses in-context examples based on shared human-interpretable tags. They compare three strategies for selecting few-shot examples: (1) random sampling, (2) cosine similarity-based retrieval, and (3) their tag-based method. These strategies are used to prompt GPT-4o and LLaMA 3.3 to generate background/causal factors and preventive measures from incident descriptions. They then evaluate precision, generation stability, and safety filter activation across methods and models.

Result: The tag-based example selection consistently yields the highest precision in generated factors and measures, and exhibits the most stable generation behavior across prompts and models. In contrast, similarity-based selection frequently results in unintended outputs and triggers safety filters in the LLMs, indicating issues with reliability and controllability. Random sampling performs in between but generally worse than the tag-based strategy.

Conclusion: Leveraging human-interpretable dataset tags for few-shot example selection improves the precision and stability of LLM-generated clinical insights from medical incident reports compared with random or similarity-based selection. Tag-based selection also reduces problematic behaviors such as unintended outputs and safety filter activation, suggesting it is a promising, practical strategy for safer and more reliable LLM use in clinical incident analysis.

Abstract: In high-stakes domains such as healthcare, the reliability of Large Language Models (LLMs) is critical, particularly when generating clinical insights from incident reports. This study proposes a tag-based few-shot example selection method for prompting LLMs to generate background/causal factors and preventive measures from details of the medical incidents. For our experiments, we use the Japanese Medical Incident Dataset (JMID), a structured dataset of 3,884 real-world medical accident and near-miss reports. These reports are variably annotated with a wide range of tags--some include descriptive information (e.g., "medications," "blood transfusion therapy"). We compare three few-shot example selection strategies--random sampling, cosine similarity-based selection, and our proposed tag-based method--using GPT-4o and LLaMA 3.3. Results show that the tag-based approach achieves the highest precision and most stable generation behavior, while similarity-based selection often leads to unintended outputs and safety filter activation. These findings suggest that selecting examples based on human-interpretable dataset tags can improve generation precision and stability in clinical LLM applications.

</details>


### [117] [Speech-based Psychological Crisis Assessment using LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10027)
*Terumi Chiba,Yang Luo,Ziyun Cui,Yongsheng Tong,Chao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents an LLM-based system that automatically classifies the crisis level of hotline calls, using injected paralinguistic cues and reasoning-style training to significantly improve three-way crisis classification performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current psychological support hotlines depend on human operators to assess callers’ crisis levels, but such judgments can be inconsistent and are limited by staffing constraints. Reliable, scalable, and consistent automated crisis level classification is needed to support operators, standardize assessment, and enhance downstream decision-making in mental health emergency services.

Method: The authors build an LLM-based framework for classifying the crisis level in hotline conversations. They first process spoken conversations into transcripts, then apply a paralinguistic injection method that detects and inserts non-verbal emotional cues (e.g., tone, pauses, crying) into the text so the LLM can reason over acoustic information. They further introduce a reasoning-enhanced training strategy in which the model is trained not only to output the crisis level but also to generate explicit diagnostic reasoning chains as an auxiliary task, acting as a regularizer. Data augmentation is used to expand the training set, and performance is evaluated on a three-class crisis level classification task using 5-fold cross-validation.

Result: The final system, integrating paralinguistic injection, reasoning-enhanced training, and data augmentation, achieves a macro F1-score of 0.802 and an accuracy of 0.805 on the three-class crisis level classification problem under 5-fold cross-validation, indicating strong and balanced performance across classes.

Conclusion: Incorporating non-verbal emotional cues into transcripts and training an LLM to generate explicit diagnostic reasoning chains substantially improves automated crisis level classification for psychological support hotlines. The approach offers a scalable, consistent, and high-performance tool that can enhance the quality and reliability of hotline services and support human operators in mental health emergency contexts.

Abstract: Psychological support hotlines provide critical support for individuals experiencing mental health emergencies, yet current assessments largely rely on human operators whose judgments may vary with professional experience and are constrained by limited staffing resources. This paper proposes a large language model (LLM)-based framework for automated crisis level classification, a key indicator that supports many downstream tasks and improves the overall quality of hotline services. To better capture emotional signals in spoken conversations, we introduce a paralinguistic injection method that inserts identified non-verbal emotional cues into speech transcripts, enabling LLM-based reasoning to incorporate critical acoustic nuances. In addition, we propose a reasoning-enhanced training strategy that trains the model to generate diagnostic reasoning chains as an auxiliary task, which serves as a regulariser to improve classification performance. Combined with data augmentation, our final system achieves a macro F1-score of 0.802 and an accuracy of 0.805 on the three-class classification task under 5-fold cross-validation.

</details>


### [118] [PlantMarkerBench: A Multi-Species Benchmark for Evidence-Grounded Plant Marker Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10032)
*Sajib Acharjee Dip,Song Li,Liqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces PlantMarkerBench, a multi-species benchmark dataset and evaluation framework for assessing how well language models interpret literature-derived evidence for plant cell-type marker genes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cell-type-specific marker genes are key for understanding plant biology, but current marker resources largely depend on curated databases or high-throughput experiments and do not explicitly model or evaluate the text-based evidence in the literature. There is a need for a standardized, literature-grounded benchmark to assess and improve how AI systems interpret biological evidence about marker genes across species.

Method: The authors build PlantMarkerBench via a modular curation pipeline that includes large-scale literature retrieval, hybrid search to find relevant passages, species-aware grounding of genes and cell types, structured extraction of candidate evidence sentences, and targeted human annotation. Sentences are labeled for whether they provide valid marker evidence for a gene–cell-type pair, their evidence type (expression, localization, function, indirect, negative), and support strength. They then define two benchmark tasks (validity classification and evidence-type classification) and evaluate a range of open-weight and closed models with different prompting strategies across four plant species (Arabidopsis, maize, rice, tomato).

Result: The resulting benchmark consists of 5,550 sentence-level evidence instances across four plant species, each richly annotated for marker evidence validity, type, and strength. Empirical evaluations show that state-of-the-art models perform reasonably on clear, direct expression evidence but struggle with functional, indirect, and weak-support cases. Evidence-type misclassification is a major failure mode, and open-weight models especially are prone to false positives in ambiguous biological contexts.

Conclusion: PlantMarkerBench is a challenging, multi-species, literature-grounded benchmark for evaluating and comparing language models on biological evidence attribution in plant marker gene discovery. It exposes current model limitations—particularly in nuanced evidence interpretation—and provides a reproducible framework to drive research on more trustworthy scientific information extraction and AI-assisted plant biology.

Abstract: Cell-type-specific marker genes are fundamental to plant biology, yet existing resources primarily rely on curated databases or high-throughput studies without explicitly modeling the supporting evidence found in scientific literature. We introduce PlantMarkerBench, a multi-species benchmark for evaluating literature-grounded plant marker evidence interpretation from full-text biological papers. PlantMarkerBench is constructed using a modular curation pipeline integrating large-scale literature retrieval, hybrid search, species-aware biological grounding, structured evidence extraction, and targeted human review. The benchmark spans four plant species -- Arabidopsis, maize, rice, and tomato -- and contains 5,550 sentence-level evidence instances annotated for marker-evidence validity, evidence type, and support strength. We define two benchmark tasks: determining whether a candidate sentence provides valid marker evidence for a gene-cell-type pair, and classifying the evidence into expression, localization, function, indirect, or negative categories. We benchmark diverse open-weight and closed-source language models across species and prompting strategies. Although frontier models achieve relatively strong performance on direct expression evidence, performance drops substantially on functional, indirect, and weak-support evidence, with evidence-type confusion emerging as a dominant failure mode. Open-weight models additionally exhibit elevated false-positive rates under ambiguous biological contexts. PlantMarkerBench provides a challenging and reproducible evaluation framework for literature-grounded biological evidence attribution and supports future research on trustworthy scientific information extraction and AI-assisted plant biology.

</details>


### [119] [Personalizing LLMs with Binary Feedback: A Preference-Corrected Optimization Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10043)
*Xilai Ma,Liye Zhao,Weijun Yao,Haibing Di,Wenya Wang,Jing Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose C-BPO, a personalization framework for LLMs that uses calibrated binary feedback and Positive-Unlabeled learning to model inter-user differences and improve personalization without hurting general performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM personalization mainly looks at a single user’s history in isolation and ignores how that user differs from others. This misses valuable information in other users’ data and struggles with distinguishing what is truly user-specific preference from general task knowledge that most users share. The authors want a method that uses other users’ data to sharpen personalization while avoiding harming the model’s general helpfulness.

Method: They introduce C-BPO, which views a target user’s data as positive signals and all other users’ data as an auxiliary pool of implicit negative signals to capture inter-user differences. To avoid penalizing general, shared task knowledge (preference overlap), they formulate a loss based on Positive-Unlabeled (PU) learning. This PU-based objective subtracts the expected contribution of positives (“positive bias”) from the unlabeled pool, so the remaining signal focuses on what is distinctive about the target user. The method is applied on top of various backbone LLMs for personalization tasks using binary preference-calibrated signals.

Result: Across multiple personalization benchmarks and with different underlying LLMs, C-BPO consistently beats existing personalization baselines. The experiments indicate that incorporating inter-user contrast via calibrated binary signals and the PU-based correction improves user-specific alignment while maintaining or improving general model quality.

Conclusion: Modeling inter-user differences explicitly, by treating other users’ data as implicit negatives and correcting them via PU-learning, is an effective way to personalize LLMs. C-BPO’s preference-calibrated binary framework better isolates user idiosyncrasies without sacrificing general helpfulness, and it generalizes across tasks and backbone models, suggesting it is a robust approach to LLM personalization.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) personalization aims to align model behaviors with individual user preferences. Existing methods often focus on isolated user histories, neglecting the essential role of inter-user differences. We propose C-BPO, a framework that personalizes LLMs via preference-calibrated binary signals. By treating target user data as positive feedback and other users' data as an auxiliary set of implicit negative signals, C-BPO captures distinct inter-user differences. To mitigate the preference overlap issue, where shared task knowledge is erroneously penalized, we derive an objective grounded in Positive-Unlabeled (PU) learning theory. This approach purifies negative signals by subtracting ``positive bias'', ensuring alignment with unique idiosyncrasies without compromising general helpfulness. Empirical experiments across various personalization tasks and backbone LLMs show C-BPO consistently outperforms baselines, demonstrating the efficacy of preference-calibrated binary signals in modeling inter-user differences.

</details>


### [120] [Swarm Skills: A Portable, Self-Evolving Multi-Agent System Specification for Coordination Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10052)
*Xinyu Zhang,Zhicheng Dou,Deyang Li,Jianjun Tao,Shuo Cheng,Ruifeng Shi,Fangchao Liu,Enrui Hu,Yangkai Ding,Hongbo Wang,Qi Ye,Xuefeng Jin,Zhangchun Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Swarm Skills, a portable standard for defining and evolving multi-agent coordination workflows, extending Anthropic Skills with multi-agent semantics and self-evolution capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI engineering is moving from single-agent prompt/context engineering to multi-agent coordination, but coordination protocols are tightly coupled to specific frameworks and static configs. This prevents sharing, reuse, and autonomous improvement of coordination strategies across systems, creating a bottleneck for scalable multi-agent AI.

Method: The authors define Swarm Skills, a specification that augments the Anthropic Skills format with constructs for roles, workflows, execution bounds, and semantic structures that support self-evolution. They further design a self-evolution algorithm that collects successful multi-agent execution trajectories, distills them into new or updated Swarm Skills, and applies continuous patching based on multi-dimensional scoring (Effectiveness, Utilization, Freshness), removing the need for human-in-the-loop refinement. They validate the approach via architectural compatibility analysis and a qualitative case study using the JiuwenSwarm implementation.

Result: Swarm Skills can be used as first-class, distributable assets representing multi-agent workflows that are portable across different agent frameworks. The self-evolution algorithm can automatically refine and expand these skills based on real execution data. The JiuwenSwarm case study shows that Swarm Skills can be implemented in practice and achieve cross-agent portability without requiring custom adapters, while enabling coordination strategies to improve over time.

Conclusion: Swarm Skills provides a standardized, portable way to encode and share multi-agent coordination protocols, extending existing skills standards to the multi-agent setting. Combined with the proposed self-evolution algorithm, this enables multi-agent systems to autonomously refine their coordination workflows based on usage, achieving zero-adapter portability and avoiding framework lock-in while supporting continuous, data-driven evolution of coordination strategies.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence engineering paradigms shift from single-agent Prompt and Context Engineering toward multi-agent \textbf{Coordination Engineering}, the ability to codify and systematically improve how multiple agents collaborate has emerged as a critical bottleneck. While single-agent skills can now be distributed as portable assets, multi-agent coordination protocols remain locked within framework-internal code or static configurations, preventing them from being shared across systems or autonomously improved over time. We propose \textbf{Swarm Skills}, a portable specification that extends the Anthropic Skills standard with multi-agent semantics. Swarm Skills turns multi-agent workflows into first-class, distributable assets that consist of roles, workflows, execution bounds, and a built-in semantic structure for self-evolution. To operationalize the specification's evolving nature, we present a companion self-evolution algorithm that automatically distills successful execution trajectories into new Swarm Skills and continuously patches existing ones based on multi-dimensional scoring (Effectiveness, Utilization, and Freshness), eliminating the need for human-in-the-loop oversight during the refinement process. Through an architectural compatibility analysis and a comprehensive qualitative case study using the open-source JiuwenSwarm reference implementation, we demonstrate how Swarm Skills achieves zero-adapter cross-agent portability via progressive disclosure, enabling agent teams to self-evolve their coordination strategies without framework lock-in.

</details>


### [121] [Not-So-Strange Love: Language Models and Generative Linguistic Theories are More Compatible than They Appear](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10061)
*R. Thomas McCoy*

Main category: cs.CL
