TL;DR: The paper argues that neural language models can embody not only gradient, usage-based linguistic theories but also formal, generative-style theories, thereby broadening what kinds of linguistic theories can be empirically tested and potentially reconciled.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent work (Futrell & Mahowald, 2025) interprets the success of neural language models as evidence favoring gradient, usage-based theories of language. However, this risks prematurely excluding theories grounded in formal structures, such as those in the generative tradition. There is a need to clarify whether and how current LMs can also support or instantiate these more formal, structure-based theories, in order to avoid a false dichotomy and to fully leverage LMs as tools for linguistic theory testing.

Method: Conceptual and theoretical analysis: the author examines how neural LMs can be interpreted as implementing formal, structure-based linguistic theories, likely by mapping properties of model architectures, internal representations, and training regimes to key commitments of generative theories and contrasting this with usage-based interpretations offered by Futrell & Mahowald.

Result: The author provides arguments and examples suggesting that LMs are compatible with, and can be seen as instantiating, formal generative-style linguistic theories, not just gradient usage-based ones. This shows that the empirical success of LMs underdetermines the choice between these theoretical frameworks.

Conclusion: LMs should not be taken as straightforward evidence only for usage-based, gradient theories. Instead, they can also be interpreted as realizing or testing generative, structure-based theories. Recognizing this broadens the theoretical space that LMs can inform and opens the door to possible reconciliations or hybrid accounts between usage-based and generative approaches.

Abstract: Futrell and Mahowald (2025) frame the success of neural language models (LMs) as supporting gradient, usage-based linguistic theories. I argue that LMs can also instantiate theories based on formal structures - the types of theories seen in the generative tradition. This argument expands the space of theories that can be tested with LMs, potentially enabling reconciliations between usage-based and generative accounts.

</details>


### [122] [NCO: A Versatile Plug-in for Handling Negative Constraints in Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10065)
*Hyundong Jin,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces NCO, a decoding strategy for large language models that efficiently enforces multiple hard and regex-based content constraints during generation, avoiding state explosion and large automata while remaining compatible with standard inference methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for preventing undesirable content in LLM outputs (e.g., profanity, PII) either rely on post-processing/resampling or constrained decoding with large combined automata. These approaches can be computationally expensive, degrade output quality, and suffer from state explosion when many hard or regex constraints are combined. There is a need for an efficient decoding strategy that can enforce multiple constraints during generation without large computational overhead and that integrates smoothly with common inference strategies.

Method: The authors propose NCO, a decoding strategy that performs online pattern matching over a set of finite hard constraints and regex constraints. Instead of building a single large automaton that combines all forbidden patterns (which can cause state explosion), NCO incrementally tracks constraint satisfaction or violation during decoding. It works with standard search procedures such as common sampling methods and beam search, and additionally supports soft masking, allowing probabilistic rather than strictly deterministic suppression of tokens linked to forbidden patterns.

Result: Experiments on practical use cases, including suppression of PII and profanity in generated text, show that NCO effectively prevents the appearance of forbidden patterns while keeping computational overhead lower than automaton-based constrained decoding baselines. It maintains compatibility with standard decoding strategies and preserves generation quality, demonstrating that online pattern matching can scale to realistic constraint sets.

Conclusion: NCO provides a practical and efficient decoding framework for enforcing multiple hard and regex-based constraints in LLM generation. By avoiding the construction of a monolithic automaton and instead performing online pattern matching, it mitigates state explosion and computational costs. Its compatibility with existing inference strategies and support for both hard and soft suppression make it a versatile tool for content control tasks such as PII and profanity filtering.

Abstract: Controlling Large Language Models (LLMs) to prevent the generation of undesirable content, such as profanity and personally identifiable information (PII), has become increasingly critical. While earlier approaches relied on post-processing or resampling, recent research has shifted towards constrained decoding methods that control outputs during generation to mitigate high computational costs and quality degradation. However, preventing multiple forbidden hard constraints or regex constraints from appearing anywhere in the output is computationally challenging. A straightforward solution is to convert these constraints into a single automaton that tracks all forbidden patterns during decoding, but this often becomes impractically large. Standard regex engines also do not readily support the operations needed to build such a constraint, such as complement and intersection. In order to address these limitations, we propose NCO, a decoding strategy that performs online pattern matching over finite hard constraints and regex constraints, reducing computational overhead without inducing state explosion. NCO is fully compatible with standard inference strategies, including various sampling methods and beam search, while also supporting soft masking for probabilistic suppression. We empirically demonstrate its effectiveness across practical tasks, including PII and profanity suppression. Our implementation is available at https://github.com/hyundong98/NCO-Decoding.git .

</details>


### [123] [PHAGE: Patent Heterogeneous Attention-Guided Graph Encoder for Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10073)
*Yongmin Yoo,Qiongkai Xu,Zhangkai Wu,Longbing Cao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose PHAGE, a patent encoder that explicitly models the hierarchical dependency structure among patent claims instead of treating them as flat text, leading to better performance on several downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing patent encoders ignore the directed dependency structure of patent claims, which form a hierarchy where dependent claims refine the scope of earlier ones. Simply linearizing claims as text loses this rich intra-document topology, and naively injecting structure into Transformers is non-trivial because (1) different dependency types vary in semantics and reliability, and (2) the dependency graph is defined over claims, whereas Transformer attention operates over tokens.

Method: They design PHAGE, which constructs a heterogeneous claim-level dependency graph via a deterministic pipeline that distinguishes reliable legal citation edges from noisier rule-based technical relations. This graph is injected into a Transformer using (a) a connectivity mask and (b) learnable, relation-aware attention biases that transfer claim-level topology to token-level self-attention, so the model can treat edge types differently. On top of this encoder, they add a dual-granularity contrastive learning objective that aligns representations with both inter-patent taxonomy (across documents) and intra-patent topology (within a document).

Result: PHAGE achieves superior performance compared with prior baselines on multiple patent-related tasks: classification, retrieval, and clustering. Empirical analysis indicates that explicitly modeling intra-document claim dependency structure yields a stronger inductive bias than relying on inter-document structures alone.

Conclusion: Explicitly encoding the heterogeneous dependency structure among patent claims into Transformer attention, and training with a dual-granularity contrastive objective, significantly improves patent representation learning. The intra-document claim topology provides a strong and persistent inductive bias that is reflected in the trained encoder weights and is more beneficial than solely focusing on inter-document relationships.

Abstract: Patent claims form a directed dependency structure in which dependent claims inherit and refine the scope of earlier claims; however, existing patent encoders linearize claims as text and discard this hierarchy. Directly encoding this structure into self-attention poses two challenges: claim dependencies mix relation types that differ in semantics and extraction reliability, and the dependency graph is defined over claims while Transformers attend over tokens. PHAGE addresses the first challenge through a deterministic graph construction pipeline that separates near-deterministic legal citations from noisier rule-based technical relations, preserving type distinctions as heterogeneous edges. It addresses the second through a connectivity mask and learnable relation-aware biases that lift claim-level topology into token-level attention, allowing the encoder to differentially weight each relation type. A dual-granularity contrastive objective then aligns representations with both inter-patent taxonomy and intra-patent topology. PHAGE outperforms all baselines on classification, retrieval, and clustering, showing that intra-document claim topology is a stronger inductive bias than inter-document structure and that this bias persists in the encoder weights after training.

</details>


### [124] [FERA: Uncertainty-Aware Federated Reasoning for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10082)
*Ruhan Wang,Chengkai Huang,Zhiyong Wang,Junda Wu,Rui Wang,Tong Yu,Julian McAuley,Lina Yao,Dongruo Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces FERA, a training-free federated framework that improves multi-step reasoning of large language models by coordinating multiple clients holding private demonstrations, using uncertainty-aware iterative refinement and aggregation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality reasoning demonstrations greatly enhance LLM reasoning, but such data is often siloed across organizations that cannot share raw data due to privacy and regulatory constraints. Existing federated learning approaches mainly focus on model training and assume homogeneous or uniformly reliable clients, which is unrealistic when clients hold different and query-dependent quality of reasoning traces. There is a need for a method that can leverage distributed, private reasoning data to improve LLM reasoning without centralizing data or retraining models, and that can deal with heterogeneous and unreliable client contributions.

Method: The authors propose Uncertainty-Aware Federated Reasoning (FERA), a training-free, iterative server-client co-refinement protocol. In each communication round, the server sends a query and context to clients. Clients generate multi-step reasoning traces along with lightweight uncertainty estimates about their own reasoning. The server then aggregates these traces using Uncertainty-Aware Self-Critique Aggregation (UA-SCA), which assigns query-dependent trust weights to each client based on their uncertainty, performs structured cross-client verification to identify conflicts and agreements, and does not simply discard low-quality traces but revises flawed steps to salvage useful information. The improved, synthesized reasoning from the server is then redistributed as enhanced context to clients in the next round. This iterative process continues over multiple rounds, with theoretical analysis showing convergence and the benefit of uncertainty-aware weighting on convergence speed.

Result: Theoretical results show that the iterative FERA protocol converges and that incorporating uncertainty-aware trust weighting leads to faster convergence compared to uniform aggregation. Empirically, on several multi-step reasoning benchmarks, FERA consistently outperforms both standard federated training methods and other training-free baselines. Performance improves progressively across communication rounds, with gains in accuracy while maintaining reasonable communication costs and computational overhead on both server and clients.

Conclusion: FERA demonstrates that federated, training-free coordination among heterogeneous clients with private reasoning demonstrations can substantially improve LLM multi-step reasoning without sharing raw data or retraining models. Uncertainty-aware aggregation and iterative co-refinement effectively manage query-dependent client reliability, leading to better accuracy and efficiency than existing federated and training-free approaches. This framework provides a practical path to leverage distributed proprietary reasoning data under real-world privacy and regulatory constraints.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning capabilities when guided by high-quality demonstrations, yet such data is often distributed across organizations that cannot centralize it due to regulatory, proprietary, or institutional constraints. We study federated reasoning, where a server improves multi-step reasoning by coordinating with heterogeneous clients holding private demonstrations, without centralized training or raw data sharing. The key challenge is that client reliability is query-dependent, while the server cannot inspect client data to determine which contributions are trustworthy. To address this, we propose Uncertainty-Aware Federated Reasoning (FERA), a training-free framework based on iterative server-client co-refinement. Across communication rounds, clients generate reasoning traces with lightweight uncertainty estimates, and the server synthesizes them into improved reasoning that is redistributed as context for the next round, progressively improving both server outputs and client-side reasoning. Within each round, Uncertainty-Aware Self-Critique Aggregation (UA-SCA) resolves conflicts among heterogeneous client traces through query-dependent trust weighting and structured cross-client verification. Rather than simply discarding low-quality traces, UA-SCA revises flawed reasoning steps to recover useful information. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that the proposed iterative protocol converges and that uncertainty-aware weighting accelerates convergence. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks show that FERA consistently outperforms both federated training and training-free baselines, achieving progressively higher accuracy across rounds while maintaining communication and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [125] [GLiNER-Relex: A Unified Framework for Joint Named Entity Recognition and Relation Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10108)
*Ihor Stepanov,Oleksandr Lukashov,Mykhailo Shtopko,Vivek Kalyanarangan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents GLiNER-Relex, a unified, zero-shot-capable model that performs both named entity recognition and relation extraction in a single architecture using a shared transformer encoder and label embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches typically treat named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE) as separate tasks, requiring distinct models and limiting flexibility and efficiency. Moreover, most RE systems are closed-set, constrained to a fixed inventory of entity and relation types defined at training time, which restricts their applicability in dynamic knowledge graph construction scenarios. There is a need for a single, efficient model that can jointly perform NER and RE in a zero-shot fashion, handling arbitrary user-defined labels at inference without retraining.

Method: The authors extend the GLiNER framework to create GLiNER-Relex, a unified architecture for joint NER and RE. A shared bidirectional transformer encoder simultaneously encodes the text, entity type labels, and relation type labels into a common representation space. First, entity spans are recognized using GLiNER-style label embeddings. Then, entity pair representations are constructed from the recognized spans. A dedicated relation scoring module computes compatibility scores between each entity pair representation and the embeddings of candidate relation types. The whole system supports zero-shot extraction by conditioning on textual descriptions of entity and relation types provided at inference time.

Result: On four standard relation extraction benchmarks—CoNLL04, DocRED, FewRel, and CrossRE—the proposed model achieves competitive performance compared with specialized relation extraction architectures and large language models. At the same time, it preserves the computational efficiency associated with the GLiNER family, indicating a favorable trade-off between accuracy, flexibility (zero-shot capability), and efficiency.

Conclusion: GLiNER-Relex successfully unifies NER and RE into a single, label-conditioned model that can perform zero-shot extraction of arbitrary entity and relation types. It offers competitive accuracy on multiple RE benchmarks while being computationally efficient. The authors release the model as an open-source Python package with a simple inference API, enabling users to specify custom entity and relation labels at inference and obtain both entity mentions and relation triplets in one call, thereby facilitating practical knowledge graph construction from unstructured text.

Abstract: Joint named entity recognition (NER) and relation extraction (RE) is a fundamental task in natural language processing for constructing knowledge graphs from unstructured text. While recent approaches treat NER and RE as separate tasks requiring distinct models, we introduce GLiNER-Relex, a unified architecture that extends the GLiNER framework to perform both entity recognition and relation extraction in a single model. Our approach leverages a shared bidirectional transformer encoder to jointly represent text, entity type labels, and relation type labels, enabling zero-shot extraction of arbitrary entity and relation types specified at inference time. GLiNER-Relex constructs entity pair representations from recognized spans and scores them against relation type embeddings using a dedicated relation scoring module. We evaluate our model on four standard relation extraction benchmarks: CoNLL04, DocRED, FewRel, and CrossRE, and demonstrate competitive performance against both specialized relation extraction models and large language models, while maintaining the computational efficiency characteristic of the GLiNER family. The model is released as an open-source Python package with a simple inference API that allows users to specify arbitrary entity and relation type labels at inference time and obtain both entities and relation triplets in a single call. All models and code are publicly available.

</details>


### [126] [SkillRAE: Agent Skill-Based Context Compilation for Retrieval-Augmented Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10114)
*Xiangcheng Meng,Shu Wang,Yixiang Fang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillRAE is a two-stage Retrieval-Augmented Execution framework that focuses on organizing and compiling retrieved skills into a compact, grounded, and directly usable context for LLM-based agents, significantly improving performance on artifact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based agents rely more on large reusable skill libraries to solve complex, artifact-rich tasks, simple retrieval and execution is no longer sufficient. Existing work optimizes which skills to retrieve and how to execute them, but largely ignores how to structure and compile the retrieved skills into an effective, compact context that downstream executors can use immediately. There is a gap in making the retrieved skill evidence organized, grounded, and efficient for real task execution.

Method: SkillRAE introduces a two-stage Retrieval-Augmented Execution approach with (1) an offline indexing stage and (2) an online retrieval stage. Offline, it builds a multi-level skill graph capturing relationships among skill communities, individual skills, and reusable subunits. Online, it performs skill-ranked retrieval on this graph while exporting selected subunit-level evidence, and then applies a rescue-aware compact compilation procedure to recover and organize key evidence into a compact, task-specific context that can be fed to LLM executors.

Result: On two public RAE benchmarks, including SkillsBench, SkillRAE significantly outperforms baseline methods. On SkillsBench, it improves performance by 11.7% over the previous state-of-the-art. Ablation studies demonstrate that the proposed context compilation mechanism is essential to the gains, indicating that the improvement is not simply due to adding more prompt content but to the structured way evidence is compiled.

Conclusion: SkillRAE shows that focusing on how retrieved skills are organized and compiled into context is crucial for LLM-based agents on artifact-rich tasks. Its multi-level skill graph and rescue-aware compact compilation enable transforming a coarse-ranked set of skills into a compact, grounded, and directly usable task-specific context, yielding substantial performance improvements over existing RAE approaches.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents (e.g., OpenClaw) increasingly rely on reusable skill libraries to solve artifact-rich tasks such as document-centric workflows and data-intensive analysis. As these libraries grow, a few works have attempted to study the Retrieval-Augmented Execution (RAE), which often first retrieves some external skills and other knowledge, then compiles the context using retrieved skills, and finally executes the task. Existing works mainly focus on optimizing skill retrieval and task execution, and they pay little attention to how to effectively organize the selected skill evidence in a form that is compact, grounded, and immediately usable for the downstream executors to complete tasks. To fill this gap, we propose SkillRAE, a two-stage RAE approach focusing on skill-based context compilation, which consists of the offline and online stages. Specifically, in the offline indexing stage, it builds a multi-level skill graph over skill communities, skills, and reusable subunits, for capturing their relationships. In the online retrieval stage, it first performs skill-ranked retrieval with selected-subunit evidence export in the graph, and then applies rescue-aware compact compilation to recover the key evidence. Together, these components compile a coarse-ranked skill set into a task-specific context that is compact, grounded, and immediately usable. Experiments on two public benchmarks show that SkillRAE achieves a significant improvement over baselines for RAE. For example, on SkillsBench, it achieves an improvement of 11.7% over the SOTA method. Ablation studies further show that our context compilation is crucial, instead of a mere prompt addition.

</details>


### [127] [Synthetic Pre-Pre-Training Improves Language Model Robustness to Noisy Pre-Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10129)
*Xu Guo,Runyu Peng,Jian Tong,Yunhua Zhou,Haijun Lv,Zhihui Lu,Qipeng Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a short, synthetic pre-pre-training stage that teaches temporal structure, making LLMs more robust to noisy data during subsequent pre-training and reducing the amount of natural-text data needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Web-scale pre-training data for LLMs is inherently noisy. Even with curation, noise remains and harms model performance. The authors want a way to make models more robust to such noise and to reduce reliance on massive amounts of natural-text data, without heavily modifying the main pre-training pipeline.

Method: Before standard pre-training on natural text, they add a lightweight "pre-pre-training" stage using synthetic data with explicitly learnable temporal (formal-language-like) structure. They then pre-train models under different levels and types of noise corruption in the natural-text data, comparing models with and without this synthetic PPT. They also conduct mechanistic analyses of attention patterns to understand how PPT affects the model’s handling of noisy tokens over training.

Result: Across various noise corruption settings, models that undergo synthetic PPT consistently show better robustness to noise, with especially large relative gains when the noise level is high. For a 1B-parameter model, adding only 65M synthetic PPT tokens allows achieving the same final loss as a baseline model but with up to 49% fewer noisy natural-text pre-training tokens. Attention-level analyses show that PPT-initialized models do not immediately ignore noisy tokens, but over training they increasingly downweight attention pathways between corrupted tokens.

Conclusion: A small synthetic pre-pre-training stage that teaches temporal structure can significantly improve LLM robustness to noisy pre-training data and reduce data requirements. Rather than directly suppressing noise from the start, PPT changes the optimization trajectory so that the model gradually avoids self-modeling noise, leading to more efficient and robust pre-training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) rely on web-scale corpora for pre-training. The noise inherent in these datasets tends to obscure meaningful patterns and ultimately degrade model performance. Data curation mitigates but cannot eliminate such noise, so pre-training corpora remain noisy in practice. We therefore study whether a lightweight pre-pre-training (PPT) stage based on synthetic data with learnable temporal structure helps resist noisy data during the pre-training (PT) stage. Across various corruption settings, our method consistently improves robustness to noise during PT, with larger relative gains at higher noise levels. For a 1B-parameter model, a synthetic PPT stage with only 65M tokens achieves the same final loss as the baseline while using up to 49\% fewer natural-text PT tokens across different noise levels. Mechanistic analyses suggest PPT does not immediately suppress attention to noisy tokens. Rather, PPT-initialized models gradually downweight attention between corrupted tokens during noisy PT. This indicates that synthetic PPT inhibits noise self-modeling and shapes the subsequent optimization trajectory. Code is available at https://github.com/guox18/formal-language-prepretraining.

</details>


### [128] [NyayaAI: An AI-Powered Legal Assistant Using Multi-Agent Architecture and Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10155)
*Deepanshu,Divi Saxena,Deepali Rana,Ayesha Varshney,Sahinur Rahman Laskar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NyayaAI is an AI-powered, multi-agent legal assistant for Indian law that uses LLMs plus retrieval over a curated legal corpus to make legal information more accessible and to automate common legal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal information in India is hard to access because of complex language and large, fragmented document collections. There is a need for tools that help lawyers, students, and laypersons efficiently research, understand, and draft legal documents while ensuring reliability and grounding in authoritative sources.

Method: The authors build NyayaAI, a multi-agent system implemented with the Mastra TypeScript framework. It combines Large Language Models with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline over a curated Indian legal knowledge base (constitution, statutes, case law, precedents). A main agent delegates to specialized sub-agents for legal research, summarization, case law retrieval, and drafting. A compliance module validates responses, and the system’s performance is evaluated via domain classification, RAG retrieval, and end-to-end accuracy metrics.

Result: NyayaAI attains 70% precision on domain classification, 74% precision for RAG retrieval, and 72% overall response accuracy on test samples, indicating effective grounding and reasonable reliability for its intended tasks.

Conclusion: Structured, multi-agent LLM systems with RAG over curated legal corpora can substantially improve accessibility to Indian legal information and enhance efficiency of legal workflows. The publicly released code supports further research and development in AI for law.

Abstract: Legal information in India remains largely inaccessible due to the complexity of legal language and the sheer volume of legal documentation involved in research and case analysis. This paper presents NyayaAI, an AI-powered legal assistant that automates and simplifies legal workflows for lawyers, law students, and general users. The system combines Large Language Models with a Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline grounded in a curated Indian legal knowledge base comprising constitutional provisions, statutes, case laws, and judicial precedents. A multi-agent architecture orchestrated through the Mastra TypeScript framework coordinates a main agent with specialized sub-agents handling legal research, document summarization, case law retrieval, and drafting assistance. A compliance module validates all responses before delivery. Domain classification achieved 70\% precision across test samples, with RAG retrieval precision at 74\% and overall response accuracy at 72\%, demonstrating that structured multi-agent LLM systems can meaningfully improve legal accessibility and workflow efficiency. The code\footnote{https://github.com/B97784/NyayaAI} is made publicly available for the benefit of the research community.

</details>


### [129] [ASTRA-QA: A Benchmark for Abstract Question Answering over Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10168)
*Shu Wang,Shansong Zhou,Xinyang Wang,Shiwei Wang,Hulong Wu,Yixiang Fang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ASTRA-QA is a new benchmark for evaluating abstract, multi-point question answering over long and multi-document inputs, with structured annotations enabling fine-grained, scalable evaluation of coverage and hallucination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing document-based QA benchmarks and evaluation methods are inadequate for abstract questions that require synthesizing dispersed information, because they lack stable abstract reference answers and rely on coarse similarity metrics or unstable head-to-head model comparisons. This makes it difficult to reliably measure coverage of key points and hallucinations when answers need to integrate information across long or multiple documents.

Method: The authors construct ASTRA-QA, a dataset of 869 question–answer instances over academic and news documents. The questions span five types of abstract information needs and three controlled retrieval scopes. For each instance, they provide explicit evaluation annotations: sets of required answer topics, sets of curated unsupported topics (things that should not appear), and aligned evidential spans in the source documents. They then define evaluation procedures that score system answers by measuring coverage of annotated key topics and penalizing inclusion of curated unsupported content. They run experiments using several representative Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) configurations, including vanilla, graph-based, and hierarchical retrieval, to demonstrate the benchmark’s diagnostic capabilities.

Result: ASTRA-QA yields structured, reference-grounded metrics that quantify how well system answers cover required key points, how much hallucinated or unsupported content they contain, and how robust they are to different retrieval scopes. Experiments with multiple RAG methods show that the benchmark can differentiate methods and offer nuanced diagnostics about coverage, hallucination behavior, and the impact of retrieval strategies.

Conclusion: ASTRA-QA fills a gap in QA evaluation for abstract, synthesis-oriented questions over long or multiple documents by providing a benchmark with explicit topic-level and evidence annotations. Its evaluation framework supports scalable, fine-grained measurement of coverage and hallucination without relying on unstable head-to-head comparisons, and the experiments indicate it can serve as a practical diagnostic tool for developing and analyzing RAG systems. The dataset and code are publicly released to support further research.

Abstract: Document-based question answering (QA) increasingly includes abstract questions that require synthesizing scattered information from long documents or across multiple documents into coherent answers. However, this setting is still poorly supported by existing benchmarks and evaluation methods, which often lack stable abstract references or rely on coarse similarity metrics and unstable head-to-head comparisons. To alleviate this issue, we introduce ASTRA-QA, a benchmark for AbSTRAct Question Answering over documents. ASTRA-QA contains 869 QA instances over academic papers and news documents, covering five abstract question types and three controlled retrieval scopes. Each instance is equipped with explicit evaluation annotations, including answer topic sets, curated unsupported topics, and aligned evidence. Building on these annotations, ASTRA-QA assesses whether answers cover required key points and avoid unsupported content by directly scoring topic coverage and curated unsupported content, enabling scalable evaluation without exhaustive head-to-head comparisons. Experiments with representative Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) methods spanning vanilla, graph-based, and hierarchical retrieval settings show that ASTRA-QA provides reference-grounded diagnostics for coverage, hallucination, and retrieval-scope robustness. Our dataset and code are available at https://xinyangsally.github.io/astra-benchmark.

</details>


### [130] [When Reviews Disagree: Fine-Grained Contradiction Analysis in Scientific Peer Reviews](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10171)
*Sandeep Kumar,Yash Kamdar,Abid Hossain,Bharti Kumari,Tanik Saikh,Asif Ekbal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a fine-grained framework and benchmark for detecting and scoring contradictions between full scientific peer reviews, plus models to perform this task efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific peer reviews often contain conflicting expert opinions, but current NLP methods treat disagreement coarsely as binary contradiction between isolated sentence pairs, ignoring full-review context and variation in conflict severity. This makes it hard for Area Chairs and editors to interpret disagreements at scale and to see precisely where and how strongly reviewers disagree.

Method: 1) Formulate reviewer-contradiction analysis at the review level by explicitly marking evidence spans and assigning graded disagreement intensity scores. 2) Build RevCI, an expert-annotated benchmark consisting of pairs of peer reviews with span-level contradiction annotations and graded intensity labels. 3) Propose IMPACT, a structured multi-agent framework that uses aspect-conditioned evidence extraction, deliberative reasoning, and adjudication to detect contradictions and estimate their intensity. 4) Distill IMPACT into TIDE, a compact language model that performs joint prediction of contradiction evidence and intensity in a single forward pass for efficient deployment.

Result: IMPACT substantially outperforms strong single-agent and generic multi-agent baselines on two dimensions: (a) correctly identifying contradiction evidence spans between reviews, and (b) matching human judgments of disagreement intensity. TIDE, the distilled smaller model, achieves performance close to IMPACT while greatly reducing inference cost.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, review-level modeling of contradictions between peer reviews is both feasible and beneficial. By providing the RevCI dataset, the IMPACT multi-agent framework, and the efficient TIDE model, the work advances automated support tools that help meta-reviewers understand not just whether but where and how strongly reviewers disagree.

Abstract: Scientific peer reviews frequently contain conflicting expert judgments, and the increasing scale of conference submissions makes it challenging for Area Chairs and editors to reliably identify and interpret such disagreements. Existing approaches typically frame reviewer disagreement as binary contradiction detection over isolated sentence pairs, abstracting away the review-level context and obscuring differences in the severity of evaluative conflict. In this work, we introduce a fine-grained formulation of reviewer contradiction analysis that operates over full peer reviews by explicitly identifying contradiction evidence spans and assigning graded disagreement intensity scores. To support this task, we present RevCI, an expert-annotated benchmark of peer-review pairs with evidence-level contradiction annotations with graded intensity labels. We further propose IMPACT, a structured multi-agent framework that integrates aspect-conditioned evidence extraction, deliberative reasoning, and adjudication to model reviewer contradictions and their intensity. To support efficient deployment, we distill IMPACT into TIDE, a small language model that predicts contradiction evidence and intensity in a single forward pass. Experimental results show that IMPACT substantially outperforms strong single-agent and generic multi-agent baselines in both evidence identification and intensity agreement, while TIDE achieves competitive performance at significantly lower inference cost.

</details>


### [131] [LegalCiteBench: Evaluating Citation Reliability in Legal Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10186)
*Sijia Chen,Hang Yin,Shunfan Zhou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LegalCiteBench is a benchmark to evaluate how well legal LLMs handle case-law citations without external tools, revealing that current models perform extremely poorly and often hallucinate plausible but wrong authorities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are being used in legal drafting and research, where incorrect or fabricated case citations can cause serious harm. Existing legal benchmarks focus on statutory reasoning or general QA and miss a key common-law failure mode: generating plausible but incorrect case citations when asked for authorities in a closed-book setting. The authors want a way to systematically study and quantify this specific problem.

Method: The authors build LegalCiteBench, a benchmark derived from about 1,000 real U.S. judicial opinions (24K instances) from the Case Law Access Project. They define five citation-centric tasks—citation retrieval, citation completion, citation error detection, case matching, and case verification and correction—and evaluate 21 different LLMs in a strictly closed-book setting. They also measure a Misleading Answer Rate (MAR) to quantify how often models give concrete but wrong authorities, and run an experiment with prompts encouraging abstention / uncertainty expressions to see if that reduces hallucinations or improves correctness.

Result: Across 21 LLMs, exact citation recovery for case law is very poor in closed-book conditions: even the best models score below 7/100 on citation retrieval and completion tasks. Larger or legally fine-tuned models show only limited improvement and do not solve the problem. Models frequently produce specific but incorrect or low-overlap citations, with MAR above 94% for 20 of 21 models on tasks that rely heavily on citation retrieval. Prompting models to express uncertainty slightly reduces confident fabrication but does not significantly raise citation accuracy.

Conclusion: LegalCiteBench demonstrates that current LLMs, even large or legally specialized ones, are highly unreliable at generating or recovering accurate case citations without external grounding. Hallucinated but plausible-looking authorities are pervasive, and simple prompt-level uncertainty instructions are insufficient to fix this. The benchmark is proposed as a diagnostic tool for studying and eventually mitigating authority-generation failures, verification behavior, and abstention strategies in legal LLMs, especially in scenarios where external legal databases are unavailable or ignored.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into legal drafting and research workflows, where incorrect citations or fabricated precedents can cause serious professional harm. Existing legal benchmarks largely emphasize statutory reasoning, contract understanding, or general legal question answering, but they do not directly study a central common-law failure mode: when asked to provide case authorities without external grounding, models may return plausible-looking but incorrect citations or cases. We introduce LegalCiteBench, a benchmark for studying closed-book citation recovery, citation verification, and case matching in legal language models. LegalCiteBench contains approximately 24K evaluation instances constructed from 1,000 real U.S. judicial opinions from the Case Law Access Project. The benchmark covers five citation-centric tasks: citation retrieval, citation completion, citation error detection, case matching, and case verification and correction. Across 21 LLMs, exact citation recovery remains highly challenging in this closed-book setting: even the strongest models score below 7/100 on citation retrieval and completion. Within the evaluated models, scale and legal-domain pretraining provide limited gains and do not resolve this difficulty. Models also frequently provide concrete but incorrect or low-overlap authorities under our evaluation protocol, with Misleading Answer Rates (MAR) exceeding 94% for 20 of 21 evaluated models on retrieval-heavy tasks. A prompt-only abstention experiment shows that explicit uncertainty instructions reduce some confident fabrication but do not improve citation correctness. LegalCiteBench is intended as a diagnostic framework for studying authority generation failures, verification behavior, and abstention when external grounding is absent, incomplete, or bypassed.

</details>


### [132] [How Should LLMs Listen While Speaking? A Study of User-Stream Routing in Full-Duplex Spoken Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10199)
*Hui Lu,Xueyuan Chen,Huimeng Wang,Shuhai Peng,Shiyin Kang,Xixin Wu,Zhiyong Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to route users’ live speech into an LLM for full-duplex dialogue, comparing directly mixing it into the prompt vs. keeping it as separate cross-attended memory, and shows a core tradeoff between better grounding and better robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable full-duplex spoken dialogue where the system keeps listening while speaking, which standard LLMs are not architected for. A central unsolved challenge is how to feed continuously arriving user audio/text into an LLM that was designed for a single, linear text sequence without mid-generation input. The authors aim to clarify and empirically evaluate architectural choices for routing the user stream under a unified framework, to guide future system designs.

Method: Start from a text-only LLM and extend it into a full-duplex spoken dialogue system. Define and implement two routing strategies for the live user stream: (1) channel fusion, where the user input is injected into the LLM’s main token sequence, and (2) cross-attention routing, where the user input is stored in an external memory accessed via cross-attention adapters. Train both variants under a shared pipeline and evaluate on spoken question answering tasks and specialized full-duplex interaction benchmarks with overlapping speech and interruptions. Compare semantic grounding, QA accuracy, and robustness to context corruption when user speech overlaps system generation.

Result: Channel fusion improves semantic grounding and yields consistently better performance on spoken question answering across benchmarks. However, when user and system speech overlap (e.g., interruptions, semantically similar content), channel fusion models are prone to context corruption: if they fail to stop output quickly enough, the newly arriving user tokens can disrupt the ongoing generation, yielding incoherent or derailed responses. Cross-attention routing performs worse on QA and semantic grounding but preserves the LLM’s generation context better and is substantially more robust to overlapping conditions and this corruption failure mode.

Conclusion: The way the user stream is routed into an LLM is a crucial architectural axis for full-duplex spoken dialogue. Direct channel fusion maximizes semantic integration and QA performance but is fragile under overlapping, interruptive scenarios; cross-attention routing trades away some grounding and accuracy for stronger protection of the generation context and robustness to interference. Designers of full-duplex dialogue systems must therefore choose or balance between these two desiderata, and the paper’s empirical findings provide concrete guidance on that tradeoff. A demo is provided for qualitative exploration of behaviors.

Abstract: Full-duplex spoken dialogue requires a model to keep listening while generating its own spoken response. This is challenging for large language models (LLMs), which are designed to extend a single coherent sequence and do not naturally support user input arriving during generation. We argue that how the user stream is routed into the LLM is therefore a key architectural question for full-duplex modeling. To study this question, we extend a text-only LLM into a unified full-duplex spoken dialogue system and compare two routing strategies under a shared training pipeline: (i) channel fusion, which injects the user stream directly into the LLM input, and (ii) cross-attention routing, which keeps the user stream as external memory accessed through cross-attention adapters. Experiments on spoken question answering and full-duplex interaction benchmarks reveal a clear tradeoff. Channel fusion yields stronger semantic grounding and consistently better question-answering performance. However, under semantically overlapping conditions such as user interruptions, it is more vulnerable to context corruption: if the model fails to stop in time, the overlapping user stream can interfere with ongoing generation and lead to semantically incoherent continuations. Cross-attention routing underperforms on question answering, but better preserves the LLM generation context and is more robust to this failure mode. These results establish user-stream routing as a central design axis in full-duplex spoken dialogue and offer practical guidance on the tradeoff between semantic integration and context robustness. We provide a demo page for qualitative inspection.

</details>


### [133] [To Redact, or not to Redact? A Local LLM Approach to Deliberative Process Privilege Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10211)
*Maik Larooij,David Graus*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how to automatically classify whether government documents contain sensitive deliberative content (FOIA Exemption 5) using a small, locally deployable language model, and shows that specific prompting strategies can match or beat prior larger or cloud-based models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Government transparency laws allow citizens to request documents, but some parts must be redacted when they involve sensitive deliberative or privileged material. Existing automatic sensitivity classifiers often rely on large cloud-based models, which pose legal and political issues because unreleased documents cannot be sent to third-party servers. There is thus a need for effective sensitivity classification using smaller models that can run locally on consumer hardware, while still achieving high recall (catching as much sensitive content as possible).

Method: The authors use a small local LLM (Qwen3.5 9B) for sentence-level classification of deliberative content under FOIA Exemption 5. They systematically compare eight prompting variants for using LLMs as classifiers, including standard prompting, few-shot prompting, and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting. They particularly test combinations of CoT with few-shot examples selected based on previous model errors (error-based examples). Performance is evaluated against earlier classification models and a commercial cloud model (Gemini 2.5 Flash) using metrics emphasizing recall and F2-score.

Result: The combination of Chain-of-Thought prompting with few-shot prompting using error-based examples yields the best performance among local-model approaches. It outperforms earlier classification models on recall and F2-score and achieves performance close to that of the commercial Gemini 2.5 Flash model, which serves as a strong, cost-efficient cloud baseline. Linguistic analysis of predicted deliberative sentences shows they contain more opinion-expressing verbs and first-person phrasing than non-deliberative ones, and that deliberativeness is usually signaled by multiple such indicators together.

Conclusion: Effective sensitivity classification for FOIA deliberative process privilege can be performed with a relatively small, locally deployable LLM, avoiding the legal and political complications of cloud processing. Carefully designed prompting—especially combining CoT reasoning with error-informed few-shot examples—substantially improves recall-focused performance and approaches that of strong commercial models. Deliberative content is linguistically characterized not by a single cue but by combinations of indicators, particularly first-person language paired with opinion-expressing verbs, which can inform future model and feature design.

Abstract: Government transparency laws, like the Freedom of Information (FOIA) acts in the United States and United Kingdom, and the Woo (Open Government Act) in the Netherlands, grant citizens the right to directly request documents from the government. As these documents might contain sensitive information, such as personal information or threats to national security, the laws allow governments to redact sensitive parts of the documents prior to release. We build on prior research to perform automatic sensitivity classification for the FOIA Exemption 5 deliberative process privilege using Large Language Models (LLMs). However, processing documents not yet cleared for review via third-party cloud APIs is often legally or politically untenable. Therefore, in this work, we perform sensitivity classification with a small, local model, deployable on consumer-grade hardware (Qwen3.5 9B). We compare eight variants of applying LLMs for sentence classification, using well-known prompting techniques, and find that a combination of Chain-of-Thought prompting and few-shot prompting with error-based examples outperforms classification models of earlier work in terms of recall and F2 score. This method also closely approaches the performance of a widely-used, cost-efficient commercial model (Gemini 2.5 Flash). In an additional analysis, we find that sentences that are predicted as deliberative contain more verbs that indicate the expression of opinions, and are more often phrased in in first-person. Above all, deliberativeness seems characterized by the presence of a combination of multiple indicators, in particular the combination of first-person words with a verb for expressing opinion.

</details>


### [134] [The Impact of Editorial Intervention on Detecting Native Language Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10216)
*Ahmet Yavuz Uluslu,Mark Gales,Kate Knill,Gerold Schneider*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how well native language identification (NLI) models can still detect an author’s L1 when their non‑native texts are increasingly rewritten by AI tools (grammar correction and paraphrasing). It finds that light corrections preserve L1 signals and accuracy, but heavy fluency edits and paraphrasing remove these traces and strongly hurt NLI performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NLI systems rely on characteristic errors and stylistic patterns in second‑language writing to infer the author’s L1. However, in realistic settings, non‑native writers now frequently use AI tools for grammatical error correction and rewriting, which may erase or alter these cues. The paper aims to understand whether NLI is still feasible under such conditions, what kinds of L1 traces persist after editing, and how different levels of AI intervention affect profiling accuracy.

Method: The authors take 450 learner essays from the Write & Improve 2024 corpus and process each essay with different levels of automatic rewriting: from minimal grammatical error correction through more extensive fluency edits to full paraphrasing. They then apply NLI models to the original and edited texts, comparing classification performance across conditions. Through performance patterns and qualitative analysis, they identify which linguistic properties (surface errors vs. deeper semantic/pragmatic features) the models exploit at each editing level.

Result: NLI models maintain relatively high accuracy under minimal grammatical corrections, indicating that their decisions are not solely driven by overt errors. Instead, models rely on deeper L1‑related cues such as atypical word choices, pragmatics, and culture-inflected perspectives. However, as editing becomes more aggressive—especially fluency-oriented correction and paraphrasing—these deeper traces are progressively normalized, causing a sharp drop in NLI performance.

Conclusion: L1 attribution is more robust than error-based accounts suggest, because NLI models also leverage deeper lexico-semantic and pragmatic transfer cues that survive light editing. Nevertheless, strong AI intervention via fluency edits and paraphrasing largely removes these structural traces, severely limiting the effectiveness of current NLI approaches on AI-mediated learner texts. This has implications for both the design of NLI systems and the interpretation of profiling in an era of widespread AI writing assistance.

Abstract: Native Language Identification (NLI) is the task of determining an author's native language (L1) from their non-native writings. With the advent of human-AI co-authorship, non-native texts are routinely corrected and rewritten by large language models, fundamentally altering the linguistic features NLI models depend on. In this paper, we investigate the robustness of L1 traces across increasing degrees of editorial intervention. By processing 450 essays from the Write & Improve 2024 corpus through varying levels of grammatical error correction (GEC) and paraphrasing, we demonstrate that L1 attribution does not entirely depend on surface-level errors. Instead, the detection models leverage deeper L1 features: unidiomatic lexico-semantic choices, pragmatic transfer, and the author's underlying cultural perspective. We find that minimal edits preserve these structural traces and maintain high profiling accuracy. In contrast, fluency edits and paraphrasing normalize these L1 features, leading to a severe degradation in performance.

</details>


### [135] [Relative Score Policy Optimization for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10218)
*Zichao Yu,Shengze Xu,Bingqing Jiang,Wenyi Zhang,Difan Zou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose RSPO, a new reinforcement learning method to improve reasoning in diffusion LLMs by using verifiable rewards to better calibrate noisy likelihood estimates, leading to stronger planning and competitive math performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion LLMs can generate text in parallel and efficiently, but their reasoning skills lag and standard RL with verifiable rewards is hard to apply because diffusion models lack tractable sequence-level log-probabilities. Existing approaches rely on ELBO-based approximations that are high variance and become unstable when strong rewards magnify errors in the score estimates. A new method is needed to stably and effectively apply RL with verifiable rewards to dLLMs.

Method: They introduce Relative Score Policy Optimization (RSPO), an RL with verifiable rewards algorithm tailored to diffusion LLMs. RSPO interprets the reward advantage not just as a gradient direction but as defining a target relative log-ratio between current and reference policies. The algorithm obtains a noisy estimate of this relative log-ratio from the diffusion model, then calibrates it by comparing the observed reward advantage with the implicit target log-ratio. The policy is updated based on the discrepancy between the noisy estimate and the reward-implied target, rather than directly on the raw advantage, thereby correcting for noise in likelihood estimation.

Result: On empirical benchmarks for mathematical reasoning and planning, RSPO significantly improves performance of diffusion LLMs. The gains are particularly strong on planning tasks, while mathematical reasoning results are competitive with existing methods, demonstrating that RSPO can effectively harness verifiable rewards despite intractable exact log-likelihoods.

Conclusion: RSPO provides a simple and stable way to apply reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to diffusion LLMs by treating reward advantages as targets for relative log-ratios and using them to calibrate noisy likelihood estimates. This mitigates instability from ELBO-based approximations and yields notable performance improvements, especially for planning, indicating that diffusion-based text models can be effectively post-trained for reasoning-heavy tasks.

Abstract: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) offer a promising route to parallel and efficient text generation, but improving their reasoning ability requires effective post-training. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a natural choice for this purpose, yet its application to dLLMs is hindered by the absence of tractable sequence-level log-ratios, which are central to standard policy optimization. The lack of tractable sequence-level log-ratios forces existing methods to rely on high-variance ELBO-based approximations, where high verifier rewards can amplify inaccurate score estimates and destabilize RL training. To overcome this issue, we propose \textbf{R}elative \textbf{S}core \textbf{P}olicy \textbf{O}ptimization (RSPO), a simple RLVR method that uses verifiable rewards to calibrate noisy likelihood estimates in dLLMs. The core of our algorithm relies on a key observation: a reward advantage can be interpreted not only as an update direction, but also as a target for the relative log-ratio between the current and reference policies. Accordingly, RSPO calibrates this noisy relative log-ratio estimate by comparing its reward advantage with the reward-implied target relative log-ratio, updating the policy according to the gap between the current estimate and the target rather than the raw advantage alone. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and planning benchmarks show that RSPO yields especially strong gains on planning tasks and competitive mathematical-reasoning performance.

</details>


### [136] [Route Before Retrieve: Activating Latent Routing Abilities of LLMs for RAG vs. Long-Context Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10235)
*Yiwen Chen,Kuan Li,Fuzhen Zhuang,Deqing Wang,Zhao Zhang,Liwen Zhang,Yong Jiang,Shuai Wang,Minhao Cheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Pre-Route, a proactive routing framework that decides when to use retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) vs. long-context (LC) processing for LLMs, using lightweight metadata and structured reasoning to make explainable, cost-efficient choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With LLMs now able to handle very long contexts, practitioners must choose between RAG (efficient but limited by retrieval quality) and LC (better global reasoning but costly and position-sensitive). Existing fallback methods from RAG to LC are reactive, inefficient, and hard to interpret, creating a need for a principled, proactive, and explainable routing mechanism that can decide the best strategy before answering.

Method: Pre-Route performs structured reasoning prior to answering a query, using lightweight metadata like document type, length, and an initial snippet. It guides LLMs through task analysis, coverage estimation, and prediction of information needs to decide whether to use RAG, LC, or combinations. The authors analyze routing ability via prompting (guidelines), use linear probes on internal representations to identify an “optimal routing dimension” and how structured prompts sharpen it, and distill the routing behavior into smaller models for efficient deployment. They evaluate on LaRA and LongBench-v2 benchmarks.

Result: Pre-Route consistently chooses better strategies than baselines such as always using RAG, always using LC, or using Self-Route’s failure-driven fallback. The paper shows that single-sample routing with proper guidelines nearly matches Best-of-N routing performance, that linear probes can clearly distinguish routing decisions when using structured prompts, and that smaller distilled models can replicate the routing behavior effectively. Overall, it improves task performance under a fixed cost budget.

Conclusion: LLMs have a latent, exploitable ability to reason about how they should be used (routing between RAG and LC). By eliciting this through structured guidelines and leveraging simple metadata, Pre-Route delivers explainable and cost-effective routing that generalizes across domains, outperforming reactive and naive strategies while being amenable to distillation into smaller models.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have expanded the context window to beyond 128K tokens, enabling long-document understanding and multi-source reasoning. A key challenge, however, lies in choosing between retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and long-context (LC) strategies: RAG is efficient but constrained by retrieval quality, while LC supports global reasoning at higher cost and with position sensitivity. Existing methods such as Self-Route adopt failure-driven fallback from RAG to LC, but remain passive, inefficient, and hard to interpret. We propose Pre-Route, a proactive routing framework that performs structured reasoning before answering. Using lightweight metadata (e.g., document type, length, initial snippet), Pre-Route enables task analysis, coverage estimation, and information-need prediction, producing explainable and cost-efficient routing decisions. Our study shows three key findings: (i) LLMs possess latent routing ability that can be reliably elicited with guidelines, allowing single-sample performance to approach that of multi-sample (Best-of-N) results; (ii) linear probes reveal that structured prompts sharpen the separability of the "optimal routing dimension" in representation space; and (iii) distillation transfers this reasoning structure to smaller models for lightweight deployment. Experiments on LaRA (in-domain) and LongBench-v2 (OOD) confirm that Pre-Route outperforms Always-RAG, Always-LC, and Self-Route baselines, achieving superior overall cost-effectiveness.

</details>


### [137] [Building Korean linguistic resource for NLU data generation of banking app CS dialog system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10241)
*Jeongwoo Yoon,On-yu Park,Changhoe Hwang,Gwanghoon Yoo,Eric Laporte,Jeesun Nam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a Korean financial NLU training dataset (FIAD) using linguistic patterns and local grammar graphs, and show it effectively trains intent/entity models for banking customer service.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Task-oriented dialog systems in banking need robust NLU, but collecting and annotating enough diverse Korean utterances is expensive and difficult. There was a lack of domain-specific, linguistically grounded annotated resources for Korean banking CS, limiting model performance and coverage of user requests.

Method: They mine banking app reviews to empirically identify common structures of Korean request utterances, capturing them as three patterns: TOPIC (ENTITY, FEATURE), EVENT, and DISCOURSE MARKER. These patterns are encoded as Local Grammar Graphs (LGGs) that can systematically generate and annotate diverse request utterances reflecting different intents and entities in the financial domain. Using FIAD-generated data, they train several NLU models (DIET alone and DIET combined with different Korean BERT variants: HANBERT, KoBERT, KorBERT) for intent classification and topic/entity-feature extraction, then compare their performance.

Result: Using FIAD-generated training data, all evaluated models achieve high performance: the DIET-only model reaches 0.91 F1 (or accuracy) on intent and 0.83 on topic (entity+feature). Adding language models slightly improves results: DIET+HANBERT achieves 0.94/0.85, DIET+KoBERT 0.94/0.86, and DIET+KorBERT 0.95/0.84 for intent/topic, respectively, demonstrating both the usefulness of FIAD and small performance differences among BERT variants.

Conclusion: A linguistically constructed resource based on Local Grammar Graphs (FIAD) can effectively generate high-quality annotated Korean banking utterances for NLU, reducing manual labeling needs while still supporting strong intent and entity extraction performance. This approach is practical for building domain-specific NLU datasets and works well with modern transformer-based models in task-oriented dialog systems.

Abstract: Natural language understanding (NLU) is integral to task-oriented dialog systems, but demands a considerable amount of annotated training data to increase the coverage of diverse utterances. In this study, we report the construction of a linguistic resource named FIAD (Financial Annotated Dataset) and its use to generate a Korean annotated training data for NLU in the banking customer service (CS) domain. By an empirical examination of a corpus of banking app reviews, we identified three linguistic patterns occurring in Korean request utterances: TOPIC (ENTITY, FEATURE), EVENT, and DISCOURSE MARKER. We represented them in LGGs (Local Grammar Graphs) to generate annotated data covering diverse intents and entities. To assess the practicality of the resource, we evaluate the performances of DIET-only (Intent: 0.91 /Topic [entity+feature]: 0.83), DIET+ HANBERT (I:0.94/T:0.85), DIET+ KoBERT (I:0.94/T:0.86), and DIET+ KorBERT (I:0.95/T:0.84) models trained on FIAD-generated data to extract various types of semantic items.

</details>


### [138] [MemReread: Enhancing Agentic Long-Context Reasoning via Memory-Guided Rereading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10268)
*Baibei Ji,Xiaoyang Weng,Juntao Li,Zecheng Tang,Yihang Lou,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemReread is a long-context reasoning framework that reads a document in a streaming, linear-time fashion and selectively rereads parts of it—guided by question decomposition and reinforcement learning—to recover discarded but needed evidence without using intermediate retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention in large language models scales quadratically with context length, making very long-context reasoning computationally expensive. Agent-memory approaches process documents chunk by chunk with linear complexity, but they risk losing important latent evidence when overwriting memory. Retrieval-augmented methods try to recall discarded information, yet they still suffer from evidence loss during memory formation and from noisy or invalid retrieval queries. The paper aims to design a long-context reasoning mechanism that preserves linear-time complexity while reducing evidence loss and avoiding the brittleness of retrieval-based recall.

Method: The authors introduce MemReread, a streaming reading framework that processes documents sequentially and maintains an evolving memory. Unlike prior methods, it does not rely on intermediate retrieval. After reading, if the final memory is judged insufficient to answer the question, MemReread performs question decomposition to break the task into sub-questions and then selectively rereads relevant parts of the document to reconstruct indirect or previously discarded facts. This supports non-linear reasoning (through decomposition and rereading) while keeping the natural reading order. Additionally, they develop a reinforcement learning training framework that: (1) improves length extrapolation to longer contexts, and (2) learns a policy to adaptively choose how many rereading passes to perform based on task difficulty, trading off accuracy and computation.

Result: Across a range of long-context reasoning benchmarks, MemReread achieves better performance than existing baseline frameworks that use memory-based streaming or retrieval-based recall, while maintaining linear time complexity with respect to context length. The experiments also show that the RL-based policy effectively adjusts the number of rereads, controlling computational cost without sacrificing much accuracy.

Conclusion: MemReread provides an efficient and more robust alternative to retrieval-based long-context reasoning. By combining streaming reading with on-demand question decomposition and targeted rereading, it can recover evidence that would otherwise be lost in linear-memory systems, enabling non-linear reasoning while preserving linear-time complexity. The reinforcement learning component further enhances scalability and adaptability, making MemReread a practical solution for long-context tasks.

Abstract: To tackle long-context reasoning tasks without the quadratic complexity of standard attention mechanisms, approaches based on agent memory have emerged, which typically maintain a dynamically updated memory when linearly processing document chunks. To mitigate the potential loss of latent evidence in this memorize-while-reading paradigm, recent works have integrated retrieval modules that allow agents to recall information previously discarded during memory overwriting. However, retrieval-based recall suffers from both evidence loss during memory formation and interference induced by invalid queries. To overcome these limitations, we propose MemReread. Built upon streaming reading, MemReread circumvents intermediate retrieval. It triggers question decomposition and rereading when the final memory is insufficient, enabling the recovery of indirect facts that were prematurely discarded. This design supports non-linear reasoning while preserving the inherent logical flow of document comprehension. To further enhance practicality, we introduce a reinforcement learning framework that enhances length extrapolation capability while dynamically determining the number of rereading passes based on task complexity, thereby flexibly controlling computational overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MemReread consistently outperforms baseline frameworks on long-context reasoning tasks, while maintaining linear time complexity with respect to context length.

</details>


### [139] [Qwen Goes Brrr: Off-the-Shelf RAG for Ukrainian Multi-Domain Document Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10296)
*Anton Bazdyrev,Ivan Bashtovyi,Ivan Havlytskyi,Oleksandr Kharytonov,Artur Khodakovskyi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Pipeline system for multi-domain Ukrainian PDF QA with retrieval, reranking, and constrained answer generation, achieving top leaderboard scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To build an effective and competition-compliant system for answering multiple-choice questions over large collections of Ukrainian PDFs, while also localizing supporting documents and pages, and to understand which components most improve performance under tight constraints.

Method: Design a retrieval-augmented pipeline consisting of: (1) contextual chunking that preserves PDF structure; (2) question-aware dense retrieval using Qwen3-Embedding-8B; (3) a fine-tuned Qwen3-Reranker-8B that conditions on both the question and answer options to rerank passages; and (4) answer generation constrained to a small set of top reranked passages using Qwen3-32B. Evaluate via Recall@1 for retrieval and answer accuracy, and compare pipelines with and without reranking and with different numbers of top passages.

Result: Reranking increases retrieval quality, with Recall@1 rising from 0.6957 to 0.7935. Using the top-2 reranked passages instead of only the top-1 improves answer accuracy from 0.9348 to 0.9674 on a held-out split. The submitted system achieved 0.9452 on the public leaderboard and 0.9598 on the private leaderboard for the UNLP shared task.

Conclusion: Within the constraints of a strict code competition, the most effective improvements come from preserving document structure in chunking and making retrieval/reranking explicitly aware of the answer options, rather than from adding complex downstream heuristics. Structured, answer-aware retrieval-augmented pipelines can achieve strong performance on multi-domain document understanding tasks.

Abstract: We participated in the Fifth UNLP shared task on multi-domain document understanding, where systems must answer Ukrainian multiple-choice questions from PDF collections and localize the supporting document and page. We propose a retrieval-augmented pipeline built around three ideas: contextual chunking of PDFs, question-aware dense retrieval and reranking conditioned on both the question and answer options, and constrained answer generation from a small set of reranked passages. Our final system uses Qwen3-Embedding-8B for retrieval, a fine-tuned Qwen3-Reranker-8B for passage ranking, and Qwen3-32B for answer selection. On a held-out split, reranking improves Recall@1 from 0.6957 to 0.7935, while using the top-2 reranked passages raises answer accuracy from 0.9348 to 0.9674. Our best leaderboard run reached 0.9452 on the public leaderboard and 0.9598 on the private leaderboard. Our results suggest that, under strict code-competition constraints, preserving document structure and making relevance estimation aware of the answer space are more effective than adding complex downstream heuristics.

</details>


### [140] [Extending Confidence-Based Text2Cypher with Grammar and Schema Aware Filtering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10318)
*Makbule Gulcin Ozsoy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how to use simple structural constraints during test-time inference to make LLM-generated Cypher queries more reliable, showing that grammar and schema-aware filtering improve syntactic validity and execution correctness, though at the cost of coverage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can translate natural language into database queries (e.g., Text2SQL, Text2SPARQL, Text2Cypher), most existing approaches rely on prompting, fine-tuning, or iterative refinement without explicitly enforcing syntactic and schema constraints. This leads to unreliable outputs because generated queries must satisfy both syntax and database schema rules to be executable. The authors want to understand and improve reliability by leveraging structured constraints at inference time.

Method: The authors study Text2Cypher and propose a test-time inference framework based on post-generation validation. They extend a confidence-based inference approach with a sequential filtering pipeline that applies: (1) confidence scoring of candidate queries, (2) grammar validation to ensure syntactic correctness, and (3) schema-aware checks to ensure consistency with the database structure. After filtering, they aggregate the remaining candidates to produce final predictions. They then empirically analyze how each type of constraint (grammar vs schema) affects query quality.

Result: Experiments with two instruction-tuned LLMs show that grammar-based filtering effectively removes syntactically invalid Cypher queries, improving overall syntactic validity. Adding schema-aware filtering further boosts execution quality by ensuring that queries respect the database schema (e.g., correct labels, properties, relationships). However, as filtering becomes stricter, more candidate queries are rejected, leading to a higher rate of empty outputs and lower execution coverage.

Conclusion: Simple structural checks applied at test time—specifically grammar validation and schema-aware filtering—can significantly improve the reliability of LLM-generated Cypher queries in Text2Cypher tasks. Grammar constraints mainly help with syntactic validity, while schema constraints primarily enhance execution correctness, though both introduce a trade-off between quality and coverage. The work clarifies the distinct contributions of syntax and schema constraints and suggests that constraint-based post-processing is a practical way to make LLM-based query generation more dependable.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) allow users to query databases using natural language by translating questions into executable queries. Despite strong progress on tasks such as Text2SQL, Text2SPARQL, and Text2Cypher, most existing methods focus on better prompting, fine-tuning, or iterative refinement. However, they often do not explicitly enforce structural constraints, such as syntactic validity and schema consistency. This can reduce reliability, since generated queries must satisfy both syntax rules and database schema constraints to be executable. In this work, we study how structured constraints can be used in test-time inference for Text2Cypher. We focus on post-generation validation to improve query correctness. We extend a confidence-based inference framework with a sequential filtering process that combines confidence scoring, grammar validation, and schema constraints before final aggregation. This lets us analyze how different constraint types affect generated queries. Our experiments with two instruction-tuned models show that grammar-based filtering improves syntactic validity. Schema-aware filtering further improves execution quality by enforcing consistency with the database structure. However, stronger filtering also increases the number of empty predictions and reduces execution coverage. Overall, we show that adding simple structural checks at test time improves the reliability of Text2Cypher generation, and we provide a clearer view of how syntax and schema constraints contribute differently.

</details>


### [141] [ANCHOR: Abductive Network Construction with Hierarchical Orchestration for Reliable Probability Inference in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10328)
*Wentao Qiu,Guanran Luo,Zhongquan Jian,Jingqi Gao,Meihong Wang,Qingqiang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL
