While classical CPT techniques like data replay have become the standard paradigm, the mechanisms underlying how LMs acquire and retain facts over time, termed as continual Factual Knowledge Acquisition (cFKA), remain unclear. In this work, we present a theoretical framework that characterizes the training dynamics of cFKA using a single-layer Transformer, offering a unified explanation for the behavior of representative CPT methods. Our analysis reveals that regularization-based methods merely adjust the convergence rate of parameters without altering the inherent forgetting tendency, whereas data replay methods succeed in shifting convergence dynamics and stabilizing pretrained knowledge. Building on these insights, we propose a novel generative data replay approach, called \textbf{S}electing \textbf{T}okens via attenti\textbf{O}n \textbf{C}ontribution~(STOC), which identifies influential factual snippets to guide replay data generation. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets validate our findings and demonstrate that STOC effectively enhances cFKA by mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

</details>


### [163] [A Single-Layer Model Can Do Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10643)
*Zanmin Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Grounded Prediction Networks (GPN), a language model architecture that uses a single recurrent state vector instead of many stacked layers, achieving competitive perplexity while enabling interpretable state geometry analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing language models scale performance mainly by stacking many layers, each with its own state or memory (e.g., per-layer KV cache in Transformers, per-layer matrices in Mamba, GDN, RWKV, xLSTM). Biological neural systems, in contrast, rely heavily on recurrence with relatively shallow depth. The authors want to explore how far a more biologically inspired, recurrent, shallow architecture with a single shared state can go on language modeling, and whether such a design offers interpretability advantages.

Method: They propose Grounded Prediction Networks (GPN), where the model maintains one global state vector that is updated at each step by passing through a single recurrent block. This block consists of one feed-forward network (FFN) and one shared matrix memory, reused across time instead of stacked in depth. They benchmark 1-layer and 2-layer GPN variants on the FineWeb-Edu language modeling task and compare perplexity against deeper baselines such as a 12-layer Transformer++ and a 10-layer Gated DeltaNet (GDN). They also inspect the geometry of the single working-state vector to understand its functional structure.

Result: At around 130M parameters, a 1-layer GPN+M model attains a FineWeb-Edu perplexity of 18.06. This is within 13% of a 12-layer Transformer++ baseline (perplexity 16.05) and within 18% of a 10-layer GDN (perplexity 15.34). A 2-layer GPN variant further improves performance, reducing the perplexity gap to 6% relative to Transformer++ and 11% relative to GDN. Although GPN does not surpass or fully match the deep baselines, it achieves relatively competitive performance with much less depth and a simpler recurrent structure. Analysis of the state vector reveals distinct structural components such as a default-token direction, a short-horizon content region spanning tens of tokens, and memory heads differentiating into fast and slow retention pools.

Conclusion: GPN demonstrates that a shallow, recurrent architecture with a single shared state vector can approach the performance of much deeper, layer-stacked language models at a similar parameter count, though it does not yet fully close the gap. The compact state representation offers a clear window into the internal dynamics of the model, revealing interpretable geometric structures and memory behaviors. This suggests that strongly recurrent, low-depth designs are a viable and scientifically informative alternative to conventional deep transformers, particularly when interpretability and simplicity of the working memory are priorities.

Abstract: Modern language models scale depth by stacking layers, each holding its own state - a per-layer KV cache in transformers, a per-layer matrix in Mamba, Gated DeltaNet (GDN), RWKV, and xLSTM. Biological systems lean heavily on recurrence rather than on stacking. We ask how far that shape can go on language modeling. We propose Grounded Prediction Networks (GPN): one state vector revisited at every step through a single recurrent block - one FFN, one shared matrix memory. At 130M parameters, a 1-layer GPN+M reaches FineWeb-Edu perplexity 18.06, within 13% of a 12-layer Transformer++ (16.05) and 18% of a 10-layer GDN (15.34); a 2-layer variant closes the gap to 6%/11%. We do not match the deep baselines. Because the working context is a single vector, we can directly inspect its geometry: a persistent default-token direction, a content-bearing horizon of tens of tokens, and memory heads that split spontaneously into fast and slow retention pools.

</details>


### [164] [When Can Digital Personas Reliably Approximate Human Survey Findings?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10659)
*Mumin Jia,Yilin Chen,Divya Sharma,Jairo Diaz-Rodriguez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well LLM-powered digital personas can stand in for human survey respondents, showing they can match aggregate distributions in some cases but are weak at predicting individual answers and complex respondent structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers and practitioners want to use LLM-based digital personas to cheaply and quickly simulate survey respondents instead of running real human surveys, but it is unclear under which conditions these personas actually approximate human responses accurately and when they fail.

Method: Using the LISS panel, the authors build digital personas from respondents’ background variables and pre-2023 survey data, then compare persona-generated answers to the same respondents’ post-2023 held-out responses. They test four persona architectures, including retrieval-augmented variants, across three different LLMs and two prediction tasks, and evaluate performance at multiple levels: question-level accuracy, respondent-level prediction, distributional alignment, equity across subgroups, and ability to recover multivariate clustering structure among respondents.

Result: Digital personas substantially improve alignment with human response distributions, particularly for questions linked to stable traits and values, but perform poorly at predicting specific individuals’ answers and cannot reproduce the multivariate structure of respondents (e.g., clusters). Retrieval-augmented persona designs yield the largest gains, yet overall performance is driven more by the underlying structure of human responses (variability, common vs rare patterns) than by the choice of LLM or architecture.

Conclusion: LLM-based digital personas can be useful for approximating aggregate survey distributions in domains with stable, low-variability responses and common respondent patterns, but they are unreliable for individual-level prediction, complex respondent structure, or domains with subjective, heterogeneous, or rare responses. Researchers should use digital personas selectively and maintain human validation where precision or nuanced structure matters.

Abstract: Digital personas powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed as substitutes for human survey respondents, yet it remains unclear when they can reliably approximate human survey findings. We answer this question using the LISS panel, constructing personas from respondents' background variables and pre-2023 survey histories, then testing them against the same respondents' held-out post-cutoff answers. Across four persona architectures, three LLMs, and two prediction tasks, we assess performance at the question, respondent, distributional, equity, and clustering levels. Digital personas improve alignment with human response distributions, especially in domains tied to stable attributes and values, but remain limited for individual prediction and fail to recover multivariate respondent structure. Retrieval-augmented architectures provide the clearest gains, but performance depends more on human response structure than on model choice: personas perform best for low-variability questions and common respondent patterns, and worst for subjective, heterogeneous, or rare responses. Our results provide practical guidance on when digital personas could be appropriate for survey research and when human validation remains necessary.

</details>


### [165] [Prompt-Activation Duality: Improving Activation Steering via Attention-Level Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10664)
*Diancheng Kang,Zheyuan Liu,Ningshan Ma,Yue Huang,Zhaoxuan Tan,Meng Jiang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GCAD, a new activation steering method that avoids KV-cache contamination and thus maintains long-horizon coherence while preserving persona control in dialogue models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard activation steering in the residual stream works for single turns but breaks down in multi-turn, stateful dialogue because the modified hidden states are stored in the KV cache. This leads to repeated reuse of perturbed states, causing cumulative coherence degradation over long conversations. The authors aim to make steering more reliable and stable in realistic, multi-turn dialogue settings.

Method: They analyze failure modes of residual-stream steering and identify KV-cache contamination as central. To fix this, they propose Gated Cropped Attention-Delta (GCAD) steering. GCAD extracts steering signals from the parts of self-attention driven by the system prompt (prompt-mediated pathways), then injects these signals during generation. It uses token-level gating to control when and where the steering is applied, and it limits intervention to attention contributions rather than globally perturbing the residual stream, thereby reducing harmful accumulation through the KV cache.

Result: In persona-steering experiments and a main multi-turn dialogue benchmark, GCAD maintains strong trait (persona) control while improving long-horizon coherence. Quantitatively, it improves average coherence drift from -18.6 to -1.9 and increases trait expression at turn 10 from 78.0 to 93.1, indicating both better alignment with the intended persona and much lower degradation in conversation quality over many turns.

Conclusion: Activation steering can be made substantially more reliable in multi-turn dialogue if interventions are aligned with the model’s existing prompt-based control mechanisms. By steering via prompt-derived attention deltas with gated, token-level application, GCAD mitigates KV-cache contamination and yields better coherence and trait control over long conversations.

Abstract: Activation steering controls language model behavior by adding directions to internal representations at inference time, but standard residual-stream steering can fail in stateful dialogue. We identify KV-cache contamination as a key failure mode: steered token states are stored and repeatedly reused, turning a local perturbation into cumulative coherence degradation. To address this challenge, we propose Gated Cropped Attention-Delta steering (GCAD), which extracts steering signals from system-prompt contributions to self-attention and applies them with token-level gating. Across persona-steering experiments, GCAD preserves trait control while substantially improving long-horizon coherence. On the main multi-turn benchmark, GCAD improves average coherence drift from -18.6 to -1.9 and raises turn-10 trait expression from 78.0 to 93.1. These results suggest that activation steering becomes more reliable when interventions follow the prompt-mediated pathways that models already use for behavioral control.

</details>


### [166] [Why Low-Resource NLP Needs More Than Cross-Lingual Transfer: Lessons Learned from Luxembourgish](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10714)
*Fred Philippy,Siwen Guo,Jacques Klein,Tegawendé F. Bissyandé*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how cross-lingual transfer and language-specific resources interact in low-resource NLP, using Luxembourgish as a case study, and argues they are complementary rather than competing approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource languages like Luxembourgish lack sufficient data for training high-performing NLP models. While cross-lingual transfer from high-resource languages is a dominant strategy, it is unclear whether it can replace the need for language-specific data collection and development. The authors aim to clarify this relationship to design more sustainable NLP pipelines for low-resource languages.

Method: The authors synthesize prior research findings and new data collection results focused on Luxembourgish. They compare performance patterns of multilingual models under different degrees of cross-lingual transfer and amounts/qualities of language-specific, task-aligned Luxembourgish data, examining how these factors jointly affect NLP task performance.

Result: They find a strong interdependence: cross-lingual transfer greatly boosts performance but only when there is sufficiently high-quality, task-aligned target-language data. Conversely, available Luxembourgish resources are too small to yield strong results by themselves, but they become highly effective when integrated into cross-lingual setups. Neither cross-lingual transfer nor language-specific data alone is sufficient for optimal performance.

Conclusion: Cross-lingual transfer and language-specific efforts should be treated as complementary pillars of low-resource NLP, not as substitutes. Effective pipelines for languages like Luxembourgish require carefully balancing multilingual transfer with targeted collection of high-quality, task-aligned resources. The paper distills these insights into practical guidelines for building sustainable NLP pipelines in low-resource settings.

Abstract: Cross-lingual transfer has become a central paradigm for extending natural language processing (NLP) technologies to low-resource languages. By leveraging supervision from high-resource languages, multilingual language models can achieve strong task performance with little or no labeled target-language data. However, it remains unclear to what extent cross-lingual transfer can substitute for language-specific efforts. In this paper, we synthesize prior research findings and data collection results on Luxembourgish, which, despite its typological proximity to high-resource languages and its presence in a multilingual context, remains insufficiently represented in modern NLP technologies. Across findings, we observe a fundamental interdependence between cross-lingual transfer and language-specific efforts. Cross-lingual transfer can substantially improve target-language performance, but its success depends critically on the availability of sufficiently high-quality, task-aligned target-language data. At the same time, such resources, particularly in low-resource settings, are typically too limited in scale to drive strong performance on their own. Instead, such resources reach their full potential only when leveraged within a cross-lingual framework. We therefore argue that cross-lingual transfer and language-specific efforts should not be viewed as competing alternatives. Instead, they function as complementary components of a sustainable low-resource NLP pipeline. Based on these insights, we provide practical guidelines for integrating and balancing cross-lingual transfer with language-specific development in sustainable low-resource NLP pipelines.

</details>


### [167] [Towards On-Policy Data Evolution for Visual-Native Multimodal Deep Search Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10832)
*Shijue Huang,Hangyu Guo,Chenxin Li,Junting Lu,Xinyu Geng,Zhaochen Su,Zhenyu Li,Shuang Chen,Hongru Wang,Yi R. Fung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new multimodal agent framework and training pipeline that reuse intermediate images via an image bank and evolve training data on-policy, significantly boosting deep search performance over text–image benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal deep-search agents suffer from two bottlenecks: (1) images produced during tool use (search, browsing, transformations) are treated as transient and cannot be reused by subsequent tools, which wastes visual evidence and weakens multi-step reasoning; (2) training data is generated by static curation pipelines that do not adapt to the agent’s changing strengths and weaknesses, leading to suboptimal supervision and reinforcement learning signals as the model improves.

Method: The authors design a visual-native agent harness built around an image bank reference protocol: every image produced by tools is registered with an addressable reference so later tools and reasoning steps can re-consume and refine it. On this infrastructure, they introduce On-policy Data Evolution (ODE), a closed-loop data generator that uses rollouts from the current policy to create and refine training data round by round. ODE targets what the current policy still needs to learn and supports both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL) data generation, covering the full model training lifecycle.

Result: Using ODE with the Qwen3-VL models, the 8B variant improves its average score from 24.9% to 39.0% across 8 multimodal deep search benchmarks, outperforming Gemini-2.5 Pro (37.9%) in standard agent-workflow settings. The 30B variant’s average score improves from 30.6% to 41.5%. Ablation and analysis show that image-bank reuse is particularly beneficial for complex, iterative visual refinement tasks, and that rollout-based evolution produces more grounded SFT traces and better policy-aligned RL tasks compared with static data synthesis.

Conclusion: A visual-native agent harness with reusable image-bank references plus On-policy Data Evolution provides an effective way to train multimodal deep search agents. Reusing intermediate visual evidence enables stronger multi-step reasoning, while on-policy evolving data better matches the agent’s current capabilities and learning needs. Together, these components substantially improve performance across multimodal deep search benchmarks and yield higher-quality, policy-aware training data than traditional static pipelines.

Abstract: Multimodal deep search requires an agent to solve open-world problems by chaining search, tool use, and visual reasoning over evolving textual and visual context. Two bottlenecks limit current systems. First, existing tool-use harnesses treat images returned by search, browsing, or transformation as transient outputs, so intermediate visual evidence cannot be re-consumed by later tools. Second, training data is usually built by fixed curation recipes that cannot track the target agent's evolving capability. To address these challenges, we first introduce a visual-native agent harness centered on an image bank reference protocol, which registers every tool-returned image as an addressable reference and makes intermediate visual evidence reusable by later tools. On top of this harness, On-policy Data Evolution (ODE) runs a closed-loop data generator that refines itself across rounds from rollouts of the policy being trained. This per-round refinement makes each round's data target what the current policy still needs to learn. The same framework supports both diverse supervised fine-tuning data and policy-aware reinforcement learning data curation, covering the full training lifecycle of the target agent. Across 8 multimodal deep search benchmarks, ODE improves the Qwen3-VL-8B agent from 24.9% to 39.0% on average, surpassing Gemini-2.5 Pro in standard agent-workflow setting (37.9%). At 30B, ODE raises the average score from 30.6% to 41.5%. Further analyses validate the effectiveness of image-bank reuse, especially on complex tasks requiring iterative visual refinement, while rollout-feedback evolution yields more grounded SFT traces and better policy-matched RL tasks than static synthesis.

</details>


### [168] [Training-Free Cultural Alignment of Large Language Models via Persona Disagreement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10843)
*Huynh Trung Kiet,Dao Sy Duy Minh,Tuan Nguyen,Chi-Nguyen Tran,Phu-Hoa Pham,Nguyen Lam Phu Quy,The Anh Han,Long Tran-Thanh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DISCA, an inference-time method to culturally align black-box large language models using disagreement across sociodemographic personas derived from public survey data, improving cultural alignment without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models influence morally relevant decisions but exhibit culturally biased preferences. Existing cultural alignment approaches often require per-country preference datasets and fine-tuning, or white-box access to model internals unavailable for commercial APIs. There is a need for a scalable, black-box, public-data-only method to better align models with diverse global moral preferences, especially capturing within-country variation rather than assuming a single cultural consensus.

Method: The authors introduce DISCA (Disagreement-Informed Steering for Cultural Alignment), an inference-time steering method. They construct, for each country, a panel of persona agents grounded in the World Values Survey, representing different sociodemographic groups. During inference, they measure disagreement among these personas and translate it into a bounded, loss-averse logit correction applied to the base model’s outputs. This is done without modifying model weights and is compatible with black-box access constraints.

Result: Evaluated across 20 countries and 7 open-weight LLM backbones ranging from 2B to 70B parameters, DISCA reduces cultural misalignment on the MultiTP benchmark by 10–24% for the six backbones with at least 3.8B parameters, and by 2–7% on open-ended moral scenarios. These gains are achieved purely via inference-time calibration, with no fine-tuning or additional proprietary data.

Conclusion: The study concludes that leveraging within-country sociodemographic disagreement as a steering signal enables effective cultural alignment of LLMs under realistic black-box conditions. DISCA demonstrates that inference-time calibration can serve as a scalable alternative or complement to fine-tuning for adapting models to the “long tail” of global moral preferences while avoiding the need for per-country training data or access to model internals.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly mediate decisions that turn on moral judgement, yet a growing body of evidence shows that their implicit preferences are not culturally neutral. Existing cultural alignment methods either require per-country preference data and fine-tuning budgets or assume white-box access to model internals that commercial APIs do not expose. In this work, we focus on this realistic black-box, public-data-only regime and observe that within-country sociodemographic disagreement, not consensus, is the primary steering signal. We introduce DISCA (Disagreement-Informed Steering for Cultural Alignment), an inference-time method that instantiates each country as a panel of World-Values-Survey-grounded persona agents and converts their disagreement into a bounded, loss-averse logit correction. Across 20 countries and 7 open-weight backbones (2B--70B), DISCA reduces cultural misalignment on MultiTP by 10--24% on the six backbones >=3.8B, and 2--7% on open-ended scenarios, without changing any weights. Our results suggest that inference-time calibration is a scalable alternative to fine-tuning for serving the long tail of global moral preferences.

</details>


### [169] [Grounded Satirical Generation with RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10853)
*Oona Itkonen,Yuxin Su,Linyao Du,Ona De Gibert*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a retrieval-augmented pipeline to generate Finnish satirical dictionary-style definitions grounded in current news, plus a new evaluation framework; it finds that models become good at political relevance but not reliably humorous, and LLM judges mirror humans on politics but not humor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humor, and especially satire, is hard for LLMs because it is subjective and highly context-dependent. Existing work lacks grounded, culturally specific setups (like Finnish news satire) and robust evaluation methods for assessing both humor and political relevance. The authors want to understand whether adding contextual retrieval and different word selection strategies can improve satirical text generation and how well LLM-based evaluators match human judgments in this domain.

Method: They build a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline that pulls up-to-date Finnish news related to a chosen source word, then prompts an LLM to write satirical dictionary-style definitions grounded in that news. They experiment with different conditions, including topic-based vs. other word selection, with and without RAG, and evaluate 100 generated definitions using six human annotators along several dimensions (including humor and political relevance). They also design a task-specific evaluation framework and run an LLM-as-a-judge study with five state-of-the-art models, comparing their scores to human annotations.

Result: Human annotators rate the definitions as clearly more political than humorous. Using topic-based word selection and RAG improves how politically relevant the outputs are, but these changes do not produce clear improvements in perceived humor. In the LLM-as-a-judge setup, the tested models show good correlation with human judgments for political relevance, but their scores correlate poorly with human assessments of humor.

Conclusion: Grounding satire generation in current news and tuning input word selection can make LLM outputs more politically on-point, but this does not translate into reliably funnier content. Humor, particularly satire, remains hard for LLMs to capture, and existing automatic evaluation via LLM judges is much more reliable for political relevance than for humor itself. The authors provide code and an annotated dataset to facilitate future research on better methods and evaluations for grounded satire generation.

Abstract: Humor generation remains challenging task for Large Language Models (LLMs), due to their subjective nature. We focus on satire, a form of humor strongly shaped by context. In this work, we present a novel pipeline for grounded satire generation that uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over current news to produce satirical dictionary definitions in the Finnish context. We also introduce a new task-specific evaluation framework and annotate 100 generated definitions with six human annotators, enabling analysis across multiple experimental conditions, including cultural background, source-word type, and the presence or absence of RAG. Our results show that the generated definitions are perceived as more political than humorous. Both topic-based word selection and RAG improve the political relevance of the outputs, but neither yields clear gains in humor generation. In addition, our LLM-as-a-judge evaluation of five state-of-the-art models indicates that LLMs correlate well with human judgments on political relevance, but perform poorly on humor. We release our code and annotated dataset to support further research on grounded satire generation and evaluation.

</details>


### [170] [Learning More from Less: Exploiting Counterfactuals for Data-Efficient Chart Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10855)
*Jianzhu Bao,Haozhen Zhang,Kuicai Dong,Bozhi Wu,Sarthak Ketanbhai Modi,Zi Pong Lim,Yon Shin Teo,Wenya Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes ChartCF, a data-efficient framework to train vision-language models for chart understanding by enhancing their sensitivity to counterfactual visual changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language models for chart understanding rely heavily on scaling supervised fine-tuning with large synthetic datasets, which is inefficient and fails to exploit the programmatic nature of charts where small code-driven visual changes can drastically alter semantics and answers. There is a need to explicitly teach models counterfactual sensitivity to fine-grained visual differences.

Method: ChartCF introduces three main components: (1) a counterfactual data synthesis pipeline that programmatically modifies chart-generating code to create visually similar but semantically different charts; (2) a chart similarity-based data selection mechanism that removes overly difficult counterfactual pairs to improve training efficiency; and (3) a multimodal preference optimization procedure that jointly optimizes model behavior across textual and visual modalities using these counterfactual pairs.

Result: Across five chart-understanding benchmarks, ChartCF-trained VLMs achieve better or comparable performance to strong chart-specific baselines while requiring significantly less supervised training data.

Conclusion: Explicitly incorporating counterfactual sensitivity through code-based chart perturbations, similarity-aware data selection, and multimodal preference optimization enables more data-efficient training of chart-understanding VLMs without sacrificing accuracy, suggesting a more scalable path for future chart reasoning systems.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable progress in chart understanding, largely driven by supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on increasingly large synthetic datasets. However, scaling SFT data alone is inefficient and overlooks a key property of charts: charts are programmatically generated visual artifacts, where small, code-controlled visual changes can induce drastic shifts in semantics and correct answers. Learning this counterfactual sensitivity requires VLMs to discriminate fine-grained visual differences, yet standard SFT treats training instances independently and provides limited supervision to enforce this behavior. To address this, we introduce ChartCF, a data-efficient training framework designed to enhance counterfactual sensitivity. ChartCF consists of: (1) a counterfactual data synthesis pipeline via code modification, (2) a chart similarity-based data selection strategy that filters overly difficult samples for improved training efficiency, and (3) multimodal preference optimization across both textual and visual modalities. Experiments on five benchmarks show that ChartCF achieves superior or comparable performance to strong chart-specific VLMs while using significantly less training data.

</details>


### [171] [RUBEN: Rule-Based Explanations for Retrieval-Augmented LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10862)
*Joel Rorseth,Parke Godfrey,Lukasz Golab,Divesh Srivastava,Jarek Szlichta*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents RUBEN, an interactive tool to find minimal rule sets that explain outputs of retrieval-augmented LLMs and use them for safety analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM behavior in retrieval-augmented settings is complex and opaque, making it hard to understand why certain outputs occur or to systematically test safety and robustness.

Method: RUBEN interactively mines rules from model inputs/contexts and outputs, then applies novel pruning strategies to derive a minimal set of rules that subsume others, making explanations compact and efficient to explore.

Result: The tool efficiently finds minimal explanatory rule sets and enables new analyses of LLM safety, including evaluating the robustness of safety training and the impact of adversarial prompt injections.

Conclusion: Minimal rule-based explanations can make retrieval-augmented LLM behavior more interpretable and provide a practical mechanism to probe and stress-test safety mechanisms and adversarial robustness.

Abstract: This paper demonstrates RUBEN, an interactive tool for discovering minimal rules to explain the outputs of retrieval-augmented large language models (LLMs) in data-driven applications. We leverage novel pruning strategies to efficiently identify a minimal set of rules that subsume all others. We further demonstrate novel applications of these rules for LLM safety, specifically to test the resiliency of safety training and effectiveness of adversarial prompt injections.

</details>


### [172] [DGPO: Beyond Pairwise Preferences with Directional Consistent Groupwise Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10863)
*Mengyi Deng,Zhiwei Li,Xin Li,Tingyu Zhu,Yulan Yuan,Zhijiang Guo,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DGPO, a group-wise preference optimization framework that improves directional consistency and reasoning diversity in LLM alignment by using multi-candidate, direction-aware comparisons, leading to notable accuracy gains across benchmarks and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing preference optimization methods for aligning LLMs often focus on pairwise comparisons and struggle to ensure that the model’s reasoning remains directionally consistent (e.g., forward vs. reverse questions) while still allowing diverse valid reasoning paths. There is a need for a method that better captures relative quality among multiple candidate answers and enforces coherent reasoning across differently phrased but logically equivalent tasks.

Method: The authors propose Directional-Groupwise Preference Optimization (DGPO), which: (1) constructs structured groups of forward and reverse question-answer instances; (2) performs multi-candidate comparisons within each group; and (3) optimizes a margin-based likelihood objective that pushes coherent reasoning paths away from inconsistent ones. By aggregating supervision at the group level instead of only in pairs, DGPO explicitly models direction-aware alignment and leverages richer relative preference information across several candidate solutions.

Result: Experiments show that using reverse data alone yields an average 3.2% performance improvement across five benchmarks. Adding DGPO on top of this provides further consistent gains across various datasets and model families, with average accuracy improvements up to 3.6%, indicating that the method effectively improves alignment and reasoning quality.

Conclusion: DGPO’s group-wise, direction-aware preference optimization leads to better directional consistency and maintains reasoning diversity in LLMs compared with traditional pairwise objectives. The empirical gains across multiple benchmarks and model sizes suggest that modeling multi-candidate, direction-aware preferences is a promising direction for improving LLM alignment and reasoning robustness.

Abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress, current preference optimization methods still struggle to align directional consistency while preserving reasoning diversity. To address this limitation, we propose Directional-Groupwise Preference Optimization (DGPO), a lightweight framework that aggregates supervision signals at the group level and explicitly models direction-aware alignment through multi-candidate comparisons. DGPO organizes forward and reverse question-answer instances into structured sets and optimizes a margin-based likelihood objective that separates coherent reasoning paths from inconsistent alternatives. This group-wise formulation captures richer relative information than pairwise objectives and reinforces consistency across diverse reasoning pathways. Empirical results show that our constructed reverse data yields a 3.2% average improvement across five benchmarks, while DGPO further delivers consistent gains across multiple datasets and model families, achieving average accuracy improvements of up to 3.6%.

</details>


### [173] [Neural at ArchEHR-QA 2026: One Method Fits All: Unified Prompt Optimization for Clinical QA over EHRs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10877)
*Abrar Majeedi,Viswanatha Reddy Gajjala,Sai Prasanna Teja Reddy Bogireddy,Siddhant Rai*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Neural1.5 is a modular, prompt-optimized system for multi-step QA over EHRs that achieves strong performance without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate question answering over electronic health records needs not just good answers, but also precise evidence retrieval and explicit grounding in clinical notes. Existing approaches often rely on model fine-tuning or monolithic pipelines, which can be costly and inflexible for complex, multi-stage clinical QA tasks. The shared task ArchEHR-QA 2026 defines four subtasks that together reflect this end-to-end challenge.

Method: The authors build Neural1.5 as a pipeline decomposed into four independent stages: question interpretation, evidence identification, answer generation, and evidence alignment. For each stage, they use DSPy’s MIPROv2 optimizer to automatically search for effective prompts, jointly tuning instructions and few-shot examples. During inference, they run models multiple times with stochasticity and use self-consistency voting to select stable outputs. They further add stage-specific verification strategies, such as self-reflection and chain-of-verification in the alignment stage, to refine and validate intermediate and final results.

Result: Across all teams that submitted systems for all four subtasks, Neural1.5 ranks second overall with a mean rank of 4.00, including subtask rankings of 4th in question interpretation, 1st in evidence identification, 4th in answer generation, and 7th in evidence alignment.

Conclusion: Systematic, per-stage prompt optimization combined with self-consistency and verification mechanisms can deliver competitive performance on complex, multi-step clinical QA without requiring expensive model fine-tuning. This modular, prompt-engineering-based approach is presented as a cost-effective alternative for multifaceted EHR QA tasks.

Abstract: Automated question answering (QA) over electronic health records (EHRs) demands precise evidence retrieval, faithful answer generation, and explicit grounding of answers in clinical notes. In this work, we present Neural1.5, our method for the ArchEHR-QA 2026 shared task at CL4Health@LREC 2026, which comprises four subtasks: question interpretation, evidence identification, answer generation, and evidence alignment. Our approach decouples the task into independent, modular stages and employs DSPy"s MIPROv2 optimizer to automatically discover high-performing prompts, jointly tuning instructions and few-shot demonstrations for each stage. Within every stage, self-consistency voting over multiple stochastic inference runs suppresses spurious errors and improves reliability, while stage-specific verification mechanisms (e.g., self-reflection and chain-of-verification for alignment) further refine output quality. Among all teams that participated in all four subtasks, our method ranks second overall (mean rank 4.00), placing 4th, 1st, 4th, and 7th on Subtasks 1-4, respectively. These results demonstrate that systematic, per-stage prompt optimization combined with self-consistency mechanisms is a cost-effective alternative to model fine-tuning for multifaceted clinical QA.

</details>


### [174] [Grounded or Guessing? LVLM Confidence Estimation via Blind-Image Contrastive Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10893)
*Reza Khanmohammadi,Erfan Miahi,Simerjot Kaur,Charese H. Smiley,Ivan Brugere,Kundan Thind,Mohammad M. Ghassemi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces BICR, a confidence estimation framework for large vision-language models that distinguishes whether predictions are grounded in the image or driven by language priors, improving calibration and discrimination without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large vision-language models often answer based purely on language priors, ignoring the image, yet still appear confident and fluent. Existing confidence estimation methods cannot tell if the image actually influenced the prediction because they only observe standard inference behavior. This visual ungroundedness is problematic in high-stakes or reliability-critical settings, motivating a method that can explicitly assess whether a model’s confidence is truly visually grounded.

Method: The authors propose Blind-Image Contrastive Ranking (BICR), a model-agnostic framework that operates on frozen LVLMs. For each image-question pair, they extract hidden states twice: once with the real image and once with the image blacked out while keeping the question fixed. A lightweight probe is trained on the real-image hidden states to predict confidence, with a ranking loss that penalizes cases where confidence on the blacked-out input exceeds that on the real-image input. This contrastive regularization incentivizes the probe to treat evidence of visual grounding as a reliability signal. Importantly, BICR adds no overhead at inference time since only the real-image features are used.

Result: Across five modern LVLMs and seven baseline confidence methods, evaluated on tasks including VQA, hallucination detection, medical imaging, and financial document understanding, BICR achieves the best average performance in both calibration and discrimination. The gains in discrimination are statistically significant and robust under cluster-aware analysis, and BICR attains these improvements with 4–18x fewer parameters than the strongest probing-based baseline.

Conclusion: BICR effectively detects and downweights visually ungrounded predictions in LVLMs by explicitly contrasting real and blacked-out image inputs during training. It yields better-calibrated and more discriminative confidence estimates across diverse tasks and models, without extra inference cost and with a much smaller probe than competing methods, indicating a practical path toward more reliable vision-language systems.

Abstract: Large vision-language models suffer from visual ungroundedness: they can produce a fluent, confident, and even correct response driven entirely by language priors, with the image contributing nothing to the prediction. Existing confidence estimation methods cannot detect this, as they observe model behavior under normal inference with no mechanism to determine whether a prediction was shaped by the image or by text alone. We introduce BICR (Blind-Image Contrastive Ranking), a model-agnostic confidence estimation framework that makes this contrast explicit during training by extracting hidden states from a frozen LVLM twice: once with the real image-question pair, and once with the image blacked out while the question is held fixed. A lightweight probe is trained on the real-image hidden state and regularized by a ranking loss that penalizes higher confidence on the blacked-out view, teaching it to treat visual grounding as a signal of reliability at zero additional inference cost. Evaluated across five modern LVLMs and seven baselines on a benchmark covering visual question answering, object hallucination detection, medical imaging, and financial document understanding, BICR achieves the best cross-LVLM average on both calibration and discrimination simultaneously, with statistically significant discrimination gains robust to cluster-aware analysis at 4-18x fewer parameters than the strongest probing baseline.

</details>


### [175] [RubricEM: Meta-RL with Rubric-guided Policy Decomposition beyond Verifiable Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10899)
*Gaotang Li,Bhavana Dalvi Mishra,Zifeng Wang,Jun Yan,Yanfei Chen,Chun-Liang Li,Long T. Le,Rujun Han,George Lee,Hanghang Tong,Chen-Yu Lee,Tomas Pfister*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RubricEM is a rubric-guided reinforcement learning framework for training deep research agents that plan, search, and write long-form reports without ground-truth answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep research agents operate in settings where rewards are not easily verifiable, trajectories are long and tool-augmented, and standard RL/post-training cannot effectively reuse past attempts or provide good feedback. There is a need for a structured interface that can guide policy execution, feedback, and memory in such open-ended research tasks.

Method: RubricEM uses self-generated rubrics as a central interface. First, it decomposes research into stages (planning, evidence gathering, review, synthesis) and conditions each stage’s policy on the rubric. It introduces Stage-Structured GRPO, which uses rubric-based judgments at each stage to provide denser, semantically meaningful reward signals for long-horizon optimization. In parallel, it trains a reflection meta-policy with a shared backbone that learns from judged trajectories and distills them into reusable rubric-grounded guidance for future tasks.

Result: An 8B-parameter model trained with RubricEM (RubricEM-8B) attains strong results on four long-form research benchmarks, outperforming similarly sized open-source models and approaching the performance of proprietary deep-research systems. Additional ablations and analyses identify which components of RubricEM are most important for these gains.

Conclusion: Treating rubrics as a central interface—rather than just final-answer evaluators—enables more effective reinforcement learning for deep research agents. Stagewise rubric-conditioned policies, stage-structured credit assignment, and reflection-based meta-learning together yield significant improvements on long-form research tasks and offer insights into how to train non-verifiable, long-horizon research systems.

Abstract: Training deep research agents, namely systems that plan, search, evaluate evidence, and synthesize long-form reports, pushes reinforcement learning beyond the regime of verifiable rewards. Their outputs lack ground-truth answers, their trajectories span many tool-augmented decisions, and standard post-training offers little mechanism for turning past attempts into reusable experience. In this work, we argue that rubrics should serve not merely as final-answer evaluators, but as the shared interface that structures policy execution, judge feedback, and agent memory. Based on this view, we introduce RubricEM, a rubric-guided reinforcement learning framework that combines stagewise policy decomposition with reflection-based meta-policy evolution. RubricEM first makes research trajectories stage-aware by conditioning planning, evidence gathering, review, and synthesis on self-generated rubrics. It then assigns credit with Stage-Structured GRPO, which uses stagewise rubric judgments to provide denser semantic feedback for long-horizon optimization. In parallel, RubricEM trains a shared-backbone reflection meta-policy that distills judged trajectories into reusable rubric-grounded guidance for future attempts. The resulting RubricEM-8B achieves strong performance across four long-form research benchmarks, outperforming comparable open models and approaching proprietary deep-research systems. Beyond final performance, we perform thorough analyses to understand the key ingredients of RubricEM.

</details>


### [176] [WildClawBench: A Benchmark for Real-World, Long-Horizon Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10912)
*Shuangrui Ding,Xuanlang Dai,Long Xing,Shengyuan Ding,Ziyu Liu,Yang JingYi,Penghui Yang,Zhixiong Zhang,Xilin Wei,Xinyu Fang,Yubo Ma,Haodong Duan,Jing Shao,Jiaqi Wang,Dahua Lin,Kai Chen,Yuhang Zang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces WildClawBench, a realistic, native-runtime benchmark for evaluating long-horizon CLI agents powered by large language and vision-language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks largely use simplified, synthetic setups with short tasks, mock tools, and final-answer-only evaluation, which fails to reflect how agents are actually deployed on real command-line environments for extended work. There is a need for a benchmark that measures whether frontier models can successfully execute realistic, long-horizon tasks end-to-end in native runtimes with real tools and complex side effects.

Method: Design a benchmark (WildClawBench) containing 60 human-authored tasks that are bilingual and multimodal, organized into six thematic categories. Tasks are long-horizon, averaging ~8 minutes of wall-clock execution and >20 tool calls each. They are executed inside reproducible Docker containers that host actual CLI agent harnesses (OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, Hermes Agent) and expose real tools instead of mock services. Evaluation uses a hybrid grading scheme combining deterministic rule-based checks, inspection of environment state for side effects, and an LLM/VLM judge for semantic correctness.

Result: The benchmark is used to evaluate 19 frontier language and vision-language models. The strongest model, Claude Opus 4.7 under the OpenClaw harness, achieves only 62.2% overall success, and all other models score below 60%. Changing only the agent harness for a fixed model can cause performance differences of up to 18 percentage points, underscoring the sensitivity of performance to runtime integration details.

Conclusion: Current frontier LLM/VLM agents are far from reliably solving realistic, long-horizon CLI tasks when evaluated in native runtimes with real tools. Agent performance is not just a function of the base model but is highly dependent on the harness and runtime setup. The authors provide the benchmark tasks, code, and containerized tooling to enable reproducible, more realistic agent evaluation and to drive future progress on robust long-horizon autonomous agents.

Abstract: Large language and vision-language models increasingly power agents that act on a user's behalf through command-line interface (CLI) harnesses. However, most agent benchmarks still rely on synthetic sandboxes, short-horizon tasks, mock-service APIs, and final-answer checks, leaving open whether agents can complete realistic long-horizon work in the runtimes where they are deployed. This work presents WildClawBench, a native-runtime benchmark of 60 human-authored, bilingual, multimodal tasks spanning six thematic categories. Each task averages roughly 8 minutes of wall-clock time and over 20 tool calls, and runs inside a reproducible Docker container hosting an actual CLI agent harness (OpenClaw, Claude Code, Codex, or Hermes Agent) with access to real tools rather than mock services. Grading is hybrid, combining deterministic rule-based checks, environment-state auditing of side effects, and an LLM/VLM judge for semantic verification. Across 19 frontier models, the best, Claude Opus 4.7, reaches only 62.2% overall under OpenClaw, while every other model stays below 60%, and switching harness alone shifts a single model by up to 18 points. These results show that long-horizon, native-runtime agent evaluation remains a far-from-resolved task for current frontier models. We release the tasks, code, and containerized tooling to support reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [177] [ELF: Embedded Language Flows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10938)
*Keya Hu,Linlu Qiu,Yiyang Lu,Hanhong Zhao,Tianhong Li,Yoon Kim,Jacob Andreas,Kaiming He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Embedded Language Flows (ELF), a continuous-time diffusion/flow model operating in embedding space for language modeling, which outperforms prior discrete and continuous diffusion language models with fewer sampling steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion and flow-based models are very successful for continuous data like images and videos, but when applied to language they mostly operate on discrete tokens, losing some benefits of continuous formulations and making it harder to transfer powerful techniques from vision diffusion models. The authors aim to design an effective language model that remains continuous as long as possible to leverage those advantages and close the performance gap between diffusion LMs and standard autoregressive models.

Method: They propose Embedded Language Flows (ELF), which performs diffusion/flow in a continuous embedding space using continuous-time Flow Matching. Text tokens are embedded into a continuous space, evolved through a flow process, and only at the final step are mapped back to discrete tokens via a shared-weight network. This continuous formulation allows straightforward use of image-domain diffusion tricks such as classifier-free guidance (CFG).

Result: Empirically, ELF substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art discrete and continuous diffusion language models on generation quality metrics while requiring fewer sampling steps to produce good text, demonstrating both efficiency and quality gains.

Conclusion: Staying in the continuous embedding space for most of the generative process and using flow matching enables more effective diffusion-based language models. ELF demonstrates that continuous DLMs can be competitive and even superior to existing discrete approaches, offering a promising direction for future non-autoregressive language modeling research.

Abstract: Diffusion and flow-based models have become the de facto approaches for generating continuous data, e.g., in domains such as images and videos. Their success has attracted growing interest in applying them to language modeling. Unlike their image-domain counterparts, today's leading diffusion language models (DLMs) primarily operate over discrete tokens. In this paper, we show that continuous DLMs can be made effective with minimal adaptation to the discrete domain. We propose Embedded Language Flows (ELF), a class of diffusion models in continuous embedding space based on continuous-time Flow Matching. Unlike existing DLMs, ELF predominantly stays within the continuous embedding space until the final time step, where it maps to discrete tokens using a shared-weight network. This formulation makes it straightforward to adapt established techniques from image-domain diffusion models, e.g., classifier-free guidance (CFG). Experiments show that ELF substantially outperforms leading discrete and continuous DLMs, achieving better generation quality with fewer sampling steps. These results suggest that ELF offers a promising path toward effective continuous DLMs.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [178] [Where Reliability Lives in Vision-Language Models: A Mechanistic Study of Attention, Hidden States, and Causal Circuits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08200)
*Logan Mann,Ajit Saravanan,Ishan Dave,Shikhar Shiromani,Saadullah Ismail,Yi Xia,Emily Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper tests whether sharp attention maps in vision-language models actually signal reliable answers, and finds that attention is a very poor predictor of correctness compared to hidden-state geometry and late-layer circuits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many practitioners trust VLM outputs more when attention is sharply focused on the relevant image regions, assuming this reflects confidence and calibration. This assumption guides debugging, interpretability, and safety efforts, but had not been systematically tested across models using a unified framework. The authors want to rigorously evaluate whether attention-based interpretability is a good proxy for reliability, and to identify better internal or behavioral indicators of when a VLM is likely to be correct.

Method: They introduce the VLM Reliability Probe (VRP), a mechanistic analysis pipeline applied to three open-weight VLM families (LLaVA-1.5, PaliGemma, Qwen2-VL, 3–7B parameters). VRP aligns multiple internal signals—attention maps, hidden states, and generation dynamics—with ground-truth correctness labels. They: (1) quantify how well attention sharpness and attention statistics predict correctness; (2) test causal importance of attended patches via masking; (3) train linear probes on hidden states to predict correctness and compute AUROC; (4) evaluate behavioral predictors such as self-consistency via multiple sampled generations; and (5) perform neuron-level ablations to study where and how reliability-related information is encoded across network layers and architectures (late-fusion vs early-fusion).

Result: (i) Attention structure is almost uncorrelated with correctness: point-biserial correlations between various attention sharpness metrics and correctness are near zero with tight confidence intervals, even though masking top-attended patches significantly reduces accuracy, confirming attention’s causal role in feature extraction. (ii) Reliability signals emerge more strongly in later computation stages: a linear probe on select hidden states can predict correctness with AUROC > 0.95 on the POPE benchmark for two of the three model families, and self-consistency with K=10 samples is the strongest behavioral predictor of correctness among those tested, although it is about 10x more expensive computationally. (iii) Neuron-level ablation reveals distinct reliability-coding patterns across architectures: in late-fusion LLaVA, reliability is concentrated in a narrow late bottleneck and ablation of a few probe-identified neurons yields large accuracy drops, whereas in early-fusion PaliGemma and Qwen2-VL, reliability is distributed more diffusely and these models tolerate ablation of roughly half of the peak-layer hidden units with minimal performance loss.

Conclusion: Sharp attention maps should not be treated as reliable indicators of VLM answer correctness: attention is necessary for processing but not a good predictor of confidence or calibration. Instead, internal hidden-state geometry, margin formation across layers, and sparse late-layer circuits, along with behavioral measures like self-consistency, carry much stronger signals about reliability. Architectural choices (late vs early fusion) also shape how fragile or robust these reliability representations are. For interpreting and monitoring 3–7B VLMs, tools should focus less on attention map visualization and more on probing and tracking hidden-state structure and late-layer circuits.

Abstract: A pervasive intuition holds that vision-language models (VLMs) are most trustworthy when their attention maps look sharp: concentrated attention on the queried region should imply a confident, calibrated answer. We test this Attention-Confidence Assumption directly. We instrument three open-weight VLM families (LLaVA-1.5, PaliGemma, Qwen2-VL; 3-7B parameters) with a unified mechanistic pipeline -- the VLM Reliability Probe (VRP) -- that compares attention structure, generation dynamics, and hidden-state geometry against a single correctness label. Three results emerge. (i) Attention structure is a near-zero predictor of correctness (R_pb(C_k,y)=0.001, 95% CI [-0.034,0.036]; R_pb(H_s,y)=-0.012, [-0.047,0.024] on a pooled n=3,090 split), even though attention remains causally necessary for feature extraction (top-30% patch masking drops accuracy by 8.2-11.3 pp, p<0.001). (ii) Reliability becomes legible later in the computation: a single hidden-state linear probe reaches AUROC>0.95 on POPE for two of three families, and self-consistency at K=10 is the strongest behavioral predictor we measure at 10x inference cost (R_pb=0.43). (iii) Causal neuron-level ablations expose a sharp architectural split with direct monitor-design implications: late-fusion LLaVA concentrates reliability in a fragile late bottleneck (-8.3 pp object-identification accuracy after top-5 probe-neuron ablation), whereas early-fusion PaliGemma and Qwen2-VL distribute it widely and absorb destruction of ~50% of their peak-layer hidden dimension with <=1 pp degradation. The takeaway is narrow but consequential: in 3-7B VLMs, reliability is read more reliably off hidden-state geometry, layer-wise margin formation, and sparse late-layer circuits than off attention-map sharpness.

</details>


### [179] [Auto-Rubric as Reward: From Implicit Preferences to Explicit Multimodal Generative Criteria](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08354)
*Juanxi Tian,Fengyuan Liu,Jiaming Han,Yilei Jiang,Yongliang Wu,Yesheng Liu,Haodong Li,Furong Xu,Wanhua Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Auto-Rubric as Reward (ARR) and Rubric Policy Optimization (RPO) to better align multimodal generative models with human preferences by explicitly decomposing judgments into interpretable rubric criteria instead of opaque scalar rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLHF methods for multimodal generative models collapse rich, multi-dimensional human preferences into scalar or pairwise labels. This loses compositional structure, makes reward models opaque, and introduces vulnerabilities such as reward hacking and positional bias. Recent rubric-based approaches help but struggle with reliability, scalability, and data efficiency in generating good rubrics. The authors want a way to expose and use the model’s internalized preference structure in a more interpretable, robust, and data-efficient way.

Method: They introduce Auto-Rubric as Reward (ARR), which first asks a vision-language model (VLM) to generate prompt-specific rubrics: explicit, factorized criteria (quality dimensions) that capture the holistic intent of a prompt. Human or model comparisons are then made along these independent dimensions, turning implicit preferences into interpretable constraints and reducing evaluation biases. To train generators, they propose Rubric Policy Optimization (RPO), which converts ARR’s multidimensional rubric-based evaluations into a robust binary reward signal. Instead of regressing to a single scalar, RPO uses rubric-conditioned preference decisions to guide policy gradients in generative training.

Result: On text-to-image generation and image editing benchmarks, ARR combined with RPO achieves better alignment and performance than standard pairwise reward models and vision-language model judges. It shows improved robustness, reduced evaluation bias, and better data efficiency in aligning multimodal generative models with human preferences.

Conclusion: Explicitly externalizing a model’s internalized preference knowledge into structured rubrics, and using those rubrics for both evaluation and policy optimization, yields more reliable and data-efficient multimodal alignment than traditional scalar reward modeling. The key limitation in prior approaches is not lack of knowledge in models but the absence of a factorized, interpretable interface to that knowledge.

Abstract: Aligning multimodal generative models with human preferences demands reward signals that respect the compositional, multi-dimensional structure of human judgment. Prevailing RLHF approaches reduce this structure to scalar or pairwise labels, collapsing nuanced preferences into opaque parametric proxies and exposing vulnerabilities to reward hacking. While recent Rubrics-as-Reward (RaR) methods attempt to recover this structure through explicit criteria, generating rubrics that are simultaneously reliable, scalable, and data-efficient remains an open problem. We introduce Auto-Rubric as Reward (ARR), a framework that reframes reward modeling from implicit weight optimization to explicit, criteria-based decomposition. Before any pairwise comparison, ARR externalizes a VLM's internalized preference knowledge as prompt-specific rubrics, translating holistic intent into independently verifiable quality dimensions. This conversion of implicit preference structure into inspectable, interpretable constraints substantially suppresses evaluation biases including positional bias, enabling both zero-shot deployment and few-shot conditioning on minimal supervision. To extend these gains into generative training, we propose Rubric Policy Optimization (RPO), which distills ARR's structured multi-dimensional evaluation into a robust binary reward, replacing opaque scalar regression with rubric-conditioned preference decisions that stabilize policy gradients. On text-to-image generation and image editing benchmarks, ARR-RPO outperforms pairwise reward models and VLM judges, demonstrating that explicitly externalizing implicit preference knowledge into structured rubrics achieves more reliable, data-efficient multimodal alignment, revealing that the bottleneck is the absence of a factorized interface, not a deficit of knowledge.

</details>


### [180] [Embeddings for Preferences, Not Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08360)
*Carter Blair,Ariel D. Procaccia,Milind Tambe*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to better aggregate free-form text opinions for collective decision-making by learning embeddings that reflect people’s preferences rather than just semantic similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In modern collective decision-making and online deliberation, people express opinions in natural language instead of selecting from predefined options. To use tools from facility location and fair clustering, opinions are embedded into vectors. However, standard text embeddings focus on semantic similarity (topic/meaning) instead of how much a person actually agrees with a statement. Because semantic features like wording and style are correlated with preferences, existing embeddings can look correct while actually relying on the wrong signal, which harms preference prediction and fairness.

Method: The authors formalize the gap between semantic similarity and preference similarity as an invariance problem: embeddings mix preference-relevant signals (stance, values) with nuisance variables (style, wording). They construct synthetic training data that deliberately breaks the natural correlation between stance/values and style/wording. This forces a learned scorer or embedding to become invariant to nuisance features and to rely more on preference-relevant information rather than default cosine similarity on off-the-shelf embeddings. They then learn and evaluate this adjusted scoring geometry for text opinions.

Result: Using the synthetic data to decorrelate preference and nuisance signals provably shifts the optimal scoring function away from nuisance-dominated cosine similarity. Empirically, the resulting method significantly improves the accuracy of predicting participants’ preferences and agreements across 11 real-world online deliberation datasets, compared to standard text embeddings and cosine-based similarity.

Conclusion: Preference-aware text embeddings can be learned by explicitly addressing the invariance problem between semantic and preferential similarity. By training on synthetic data that breaks the correlation between stance/values and style/wording, one can obtain a scoring geometry that better captures true agreement. This leads to more accurate and fair aggregation of free-form text opinions in collective decision-making settings than standard semantic embeddings and cosine similarity provide.

Abstract: Modern AI is opening the door to collective decision-making in which participants express their views as free-form text rather than voting on a fixed set of candidates. A natural idea is to embed these opinions in a vector space so that the substantial literature on facility location problems and fair clustering can be brought to bear. But standard text embeddings measure semantic similarity, whereas distances in facility location problems and fair clustering require what we call \textit{preferential similarity}: a participant's agreement with a piece of text should be inversely related to their distance from it. Off-the-shelf embeddings inherit a coarse preference signal through a correlation between semantic and preferential similarity, but fail to capture preferences when the correlation breaks. We formalize this as an invariance problem: text embedding models encode both a preference-relevant signal (stance and values) and semantic nuisance (style and wording), and the two are observationally correlated, so a geometry that relies on nuisance can appear preference-correct even when it is not. We show that synthetic training data designed to break this correlation provably shifts the optimal scorer away from nuisance-dominated cosine and significantly improves preference prediction across 11 online deliberation datasets.

</details>


### [181] [On Distinguishing Capability Elicitation from Capability Creation in Post-Training: A Free-Energy Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08368)
*Yuhao Li,Shengchao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper distinguishes between two kinds of post‑training for large language models: eliciting existing capabilities versus creating new ones, formalized using the concept of accessible support and a free‑energy view of SFT and RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing debates oversimplify post‑training by framing supervised fine‑tuning as mere imitation and reinforcement learning as genuine discovery, which obscures what actually changes in a model’s behavior and capabilities. The authors want a more precise way to talk about how post‑training affects what models can do in practice.

Method: They introduce the concept of accessible support—the set of behaviors a pretrained model can practically produce under finite budgets—and analyze SFT and RL through a free‑energy perspective, viewing both as reweighting a pretrained reference distribution according to external signals (demonstrations for SFT, rewards for RL).

Result: They show conceptually that when post‑training updates stay close to the base model, SFT and RL primarily perform local reweighting within the model’s existing accessible support (capability elicitation) rather than expanding that support (capability creation). They also identify mechanisms—like search, interaction, tool use, and adding new information—through which post‑training can genuinely expand the reachable behavioral space.

Conclusion: The important distinction in post‑training is not SFT versus RL, but whether a procedure merely reweights behaviors that were already practically reachable or instead expands the model’s accessible support to create new practical capabilities. This framework can better guide research and evaluation of post‑training methods for large language models.

Abstract: Debates about large language model post-training often treat supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as imitation and reinforcement learning (RL) as discovery. But this distinction is too coarse. What matters is whether a training procedure increases the probability of behaviors the pretrained model could already produce, or whether it changes what the model can practically reach. We argue that post-training research should distinguish between capability elicitation and capability creation. We make this distinction operational by introducing the notion of accessible support: the set of behaviors that a model can practically produce under finite budgets. Post-training that reweights behaviors within this support is capability elicitation; whereas changing the support itself corresponds to capability creation. We develop this argument through a free-energy view of post-training. SFT and RL can both be seen as reweighting a pretrained reference distribution, only with different external signals. Demonstration signals define low-energy behavior for SFT, and reward signals define low-energy behavior for RL. When the update remains close to the base model, the main effect is local reweighting, not capability creation. Within this framework, the central question is no longer whether post-training is framed as SFT or RL, but whether it reweights behaviors already within reach, or instead expands the model's reachable behavioral space through search, interaction, tool use, or the incorporation of new information.

</details>


### [182] [MemQ: Integrating Q-Learning into Self-Evolving Memory Agents over Provenance DAGs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08374)
*Junwei Liao,Haoting Shi,Ruiwen Zhou,Jiaqian Wang,Shengtao Zhang,Wei Zhang,Weinan Zhang,Ying Wen,Zhiyu Li,Feiyu Xiong,Bo Tang,Muning Wen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MemQ, a method that improves how LLM agents learn from and use episodic memory by propagating credit through chains of related memories rather than treating each memory in isolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing episodic memory systems for LLM agents judge and update each memory independently, ignoring how earlier retrieved memories enable the creation of later ones. This fails to properly assign credit in multi-step tasks where success depends on long chains of dependent memories. The authors want a principled way to propagate learning signals through these dependency structures so that the memory system better supports complex, multi-step reasoning and interaction.

Method: They introduce MemQ, which assigns Q-values to memories and updates them using TD(λ) eligibility traces over a provenance DAG. This DAG records which existing memories were retrieved when a new memory was formed. Instead of using temporal distance, credit for success is propagated backward through the DAG, with the weight decaying as (γλ)^d with graph depth d. They formalize the problem as an Exogenous-Context MDP (EC-MDP), where the environment’s task stream is exogenous and the memory store is endogenous, and use this structure to analyze and tune γ and λ. MemQ is then evaluated across multiple benchmarks involving different agent tasks.

Result: On six benchmarks (OS interaction, function calling, code generation, multimodal reasoning, embodied reasoning, and expert-level QA), MemQ achieves the best performance in both generalization and runtime learning. The gains are especially pronounced on multi-step tasks that generate deep and relevant provenance chains, with improvements up to +5.7 percentage points, while improvements are smaller (+0.77 percentage points) on simple single-step classification tasks where traditional one-step updates are already adequate.

Conclusion: Modeling episodic memory as a Q-valued structure and propagating credit along provenance DAGs via TD(λ) yields better use of stored experience for LLM agents, especially on complex, multi-step problems. The EC-MDP formulation clarifies how to set and interpret γ and λ in this setting and offers a foundation for future work on principled memory mechanisms in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Episodic memory allows LLM agents to accumulate and retrieve experience, but current methods treat each memory independently, i.e., evaluating retrieval quality in isolation without accounting for the dependency chains through which memories enable the creation of future memories. We introduce MemQ, which applies TD($λ$) eligibility traces to memory Q-values, propagating credit backward through a provenance DAG that records which memories were retrieved when each new memory was created. Credit weight decays as $(γλ)^d$ with DAG depth $d$, replacing temporal distance with structural proximity. We formalize the setting as an Exogenous-Context MDP, whose factored transition decouples the exogenous task stream from the endogenous memory store. Across six benchmarks, spanning OS interaction, function calling, code generation, multimodal reasoning, embodied reasoning, and expert-level QA, MemQ achieves the highest success rate on all six in generalization evaluation and runtime learning, with gains largest on multi-step tasks that produce deep and relevant provenance chains (up to +5.7~pp) and smallest on single-step classification (+0.77~pp) where single-step updates already suffice. We further study how $γ$ and $λ$ interact with the EC-MDP structure, providing principled guidance for parameter selection and future research. Code will be available soon.

</details>


### [183] [SkillLens: Adaptive Multi-Granularity Skill Reuse for Cost-Efficient LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08386)
*Yongliang Miao,Ziyang Yu,Liang Zhao,Bowen Zhu,Hasibul Haque*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillLens is a hierarchical framework that lets LLM agents reuse and adapt skills at multiple granularities, improving effectiveness and efficiency on downstream tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill libraries for LLM agents store skills as flat, monolithic prompts. When reused, these coarse skills either add irrelevant or misleading context if included wholesale, or require rewriting entire skills, which is costly and often unnecessary. There is a need for a method that can selectively reuse only the relevant parts of a skill while locally adapting mismatched components, balancing relevance with inference cost and enabling continual improvement over time.

Method: The paper introduces SkillLens, which structures skills into a four-layer hierarchical graph: policies, strategies, procedures, and primitives. For a new task, it (1) retrieves semantically relevant skill seeds, (2) expands these via a degree-corrected random walk over the skill graph, and (3) uses a verifier to decide for each visited skill unit whether to accept as-is, decompose into finer-grained components, rewrite/adapt, or skip. The system also includes an evolutionary update process that refines skills at multiple granularities and improves the verifier’s routing decisions over time. The authors accompany the method with theoretical analysis of its cost under sparse mismatch and the convergence behavior of the evolutionary update rule.

Result: On MuLocBench (bug localization) and ALFWorld (embodied agent tasks), SkillLens outperforms strong existing skill-based baselines. It yields up to a 6.31 percentage-point improvement in Acc@1 for bug localization and increases success rate from 45.00% to 51.31% on ALFWorld, demonstrating more effective skill reuse and adaptation in practice.

Conclusion: Hierarchical, mixed-granularity skill reuse with SkillLens lets LLM agents adapt only where necessary while reusing compatible subskills directly, achieving better performance with sublinear adaptation cost under sparse mismatch assumptions. The evolutionary refinement of both skills and verifier provably improves a validation objective until a local optimum and empirically delivers consistent gains over flat skill libraries on diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Skill libraries have become a practical way for LLM agents to reuse procedural experience across tasks. However, existing systems typically treat skills as flat, single-resolution prompt blocks. This creates a tension between relevance and cost: injecting coarse skills can introduce irrelevant or misleading context, while rewriting entire skills is expensive and often unnecessary. We propose SkillLens, a hierarchical skill-evolution framework that organizes skills into a four-layer graph of policies, strategies, procedures, and primitives, and retrieves them at mixed granularity. Given a task, SkillLens first retrieves semantically relevant skill seeds, expands them through degree-corrected random walk over the skill graph, and then uses a verifier to decide whether each visited unit should be accepted, decomposed, rewritten, or skipped.
