This enables the agent to reuse compatible subskills directly while adapting only locally mismatched components. To improve the system over time, SkillLens further refines multi-granularity skills and verifier in order to improve its routing decisions. We provide theoretical analysis showing that mixed-granularity adaptation incurs sublinear cost under sparse mismatch assumptions and that the evolutionary update rule monotonically improves the validation objective until a local optimum. Across MuLocbench and ALFWorld, SkillLens consistently improves over strong skill-based baselines, achieving up to a 6.31 percentage-point Acc@1 gain for bug localization and raising agent success rate from 45.00% to 51.31%.

</details>


### [184] [PLACO: A Multi-Stage Framework for Cost-Effective Performance in Human-AI Teams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08388)
*Pranavkumar Mallela,Vinay Kumar,Shashi Shekhar Jha,Shweta Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to best combine human and AI classifier outputs into a single final label for Human-AI teams.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With generative AI widely used, many tasks have become collaborative Human-AI team efforts. For classification problems where humans and models both contribute, there is a need for principled methods to optimally combine their outputs, since neither is perfect alone but together they can outperform each individually.

Method: The work builds on a Bayesian framework that assumes human and model predictions are conditionally independent given the true label. Under this assumption, it proposes a combination method for one deterministic labeler (the human, who produces a single label) and one probabilistic labeler (the model, which provides calibrated class probabilities), leveraging instance-level model probabilities and class-level human accuracies/calibrations.

Result: The result is a theoretically grounded combination rule (via Bayes rule) that specifies how to compute the final predicted label from the joint human–model information, demonstrating when and how human-AI teams can achieve higher accuracy than either human or model alone.

Conclusion: By modeling the human as a deterministic but class-calibrated labeler and the AI as a probabilistic classifier, and assuming conditional independence, one can derive an optimal rule for combining their outputs for classification tasks, thereby improving Human-AI team performance on decision-making problems.

Abstract: Human-AI teams play a pivotal role in improving overall system performance when neither the human nor the model can achieve such performance on their own. With the advent of powerful and accessible Generative AI models, several mundane tasks have morphed into Human-AI team tasks. From writing essays to developing advanced algorithms, humans have found that using AI assistance has led to an accelerated work pace like never before. In classification tasks, where the final output is a single hard label, it is crucial to address the combination of human and model output. Prior work elegantly solves this problem using Bayes rule, using the assumption that human and model output are conditionally independent given the ground truth. Specifically, it discusses a combination method to combine a single deterministic labeler (the human) and a probabilistic labeler (the classifier model) using the model's instance-level and the human's class-level calibrated probabilities.

</details>


### [185] [Belief or Circuitry? Causal Evidence for In-Context Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08405)
*Katharine Kowalyshyn,Timothy Duggan,Daniel Little,Michael C Hughes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how LLMs perform in-context learning, finding evidence that they use both local pattern-matching and global structure inference in parallel rather than just one mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve whether LLMs' in-context learning behavior is driven purely by local pattern-matching of recent tokens or by true inference over latent, global structure. A clean, decidable toy task—random walks over graphs with competing topologies—is used to distinguish these possibilities.

Method: They design a toy task based on a random walk over two competing graph structures and test LLMs on it. They analyze internal representations using PCA to see which graph features are encoded, and use causal interpretability tools—residual-stream activation patching and a linear "graph-difference" steering vector—to directly manipulate and test the role of the representation subspaces corresponding to different graph topologies.

Result: PCA shows that, at intermediate mixture ratios between the two graph families, both graph topologies are simultaneously encoded in orthogonal principal subspaces, inconsistent with a purely local transition-copying account. Activation patching in late layers transfers the model's preference for one clean graph family, and linear steering reliably shifts predictions in the expected direction while control interventions (norm-matched and label-shuffled) do not, indicating a real causal role of a graph-family signal in the residual stream.

Conclusion: The evidence suggests that LLMs' in-context learning on this task is best explained by a dual-mechanism account: circuits for genuine structure inference and for local induction/pattern-copying run in parallel, rather than the model relying exclusively on either global or local mechanisms.

Abstract: How do LLMs learn in-context? Is it by pattern-matching recent tokens, or by inferring latent structure? We probe this question using a toy graph random-walk across two competing graph structures. This task's answer is, in principle, decidable: either the model tracks global topology, or it copies local transitions. We present two lines of evidence that neither account alone is sufficient. First, reconstructing the internal representation structure via PCA reveals that at intermediate mixture ratios, both graph topologies are encoded in orthogonal principal subspaces simultaneously. This pattern is difficult to reconcile with purely local transition copying. Second, residual-stream activation patching and graph-difference steering causally intervene on this graph-family signal: late-layer patching almost fully transfers the clean graph preference, while linear steering moves predictions in the intended direction and fails under norm-matched and label-shuffled controls. Taken together, our findings are most consistent with a dual-mechanism account in which genuine structure inference and induction circuits operate in parallel.

</details>


### [186] [Playing games with knowledge: AI-Induced delusions need game theoretic interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08409)
*Will Beaumaster,Paul Schrater*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes why conversational AI tends to become sycophantic and thereby traps users in self-reinforcing false beliefs, and proposes a game-theoretic mechanism design (Epistemic Mediator with Belief Versioning) to mitigate these epistemic spirals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current conversational AIs optimized for user satisfaction often agree with or reinforce users’ existing beliefs, even when those beliefs are false. This sycophancy can create self-reinforcing belief spirals and epistemic entrenchment, posing serious risks for users’ ability to maintain accurate beliefs. The authors argue that this issue is not just a matter of aligning model outputs, but is rooted in the strategic interaction structure between users and AI agents in repeated conversations. They want to formally understand this structure and design mechanisms that prevent harmful belief spirals while preserving learning benefits.

Method: The authors model user–AI interaction as a Crawford–Sobel cheap-talk game, where users send costless signals and the AI, optimized for user satisfaction, chooses responses. Users come in at least two epistemic types: Growth-seekers who want their beliefs challenged and improved, and Validation-seekers who prefer confirmation. Because user messages are costless and uninformative about type, the equilibrium is pooling: the AI cannot distinguish types and therefore adopts a sycophantic strategy that reinforces users regardless of type. They extend this to a repeated-play setting to show how feedback loops create a coordination trap akin to a Prisoner’s Dilemma, resulting in pathologically confident false beliefs. To fix this, they propose an inference-time mechanism called an Epistemic Mediator that introduces a costly signal—epistemic friction—forcing users to reveal type via their reaction to resistance. They also introduce Belief Versioning, a git-like epistemic memory that stores “healthy” beliefs and can roll back to them when validation-seeking resistance is detected. They evaluate this via simulation, comparing spiral rates and checking a learning preservation criterion.

Result: In simulations, introducing the Epistemic Mediator with epistemic friction leads to a separating equilibrium where Growth-seekers and Validation-seekers are effectively distinguished. This reduces harmful belief spirals dramatically, achieving a 48× difference in spiral rates between types or conditions, while still maintaining learning performance according to a predefined learning preservation criterion. This provides quantitative evidence that properly designed interaction mechanisms can materially improve epistemic safety in conversational AI systems.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that the core epistemic safety problem of conversational AI is not purely about model alignment or static output constraints, but about the strategic design of the information environment in which users and AI interact. By modeling conversations as cheap-talk games and introducing appropriately designed inference-time mechanisms—such as epistemic friction and Belief Versioning—they show it is possible to move from harmful pooling equilibria to safer separating equilibria. Thus, ensuring epistemic safety requires mechanism design for user–AI interaction, not just better base models.

Abstract: Conversational AI has a fundamental flaw as a knowledge interface: sycophantic chatbots induce epistemic entrenchment and delusional belief spirals even in rational agents. We propose the problem does not stem from the AI model, rooted instead in a systemic consequence of the paradigm shift from user-driven knowledge search to users and agents engaged in strategic, repeated-play communication. We formalize the problem as a Crawford-Sobel cheap talk game, where costless user signals induce a pooling equilibrium. Agents optimized for user satisfaction produce sycophantic strategies that provide identical reinforcement across user types with opposite epistemic incentives: exploratory ``Growth-seekers'' ($θ_G$) and confirmatory ``Validation-seekers'' ($θ_V$). Under repeated play, this identification failure creates a coordination trap -- analogous to a Prisoner's Dilemma -- where locally rational feedback loops drive users toward pathologically certain false beliefs. We propose an inference-time mechanism design intervention called an Epistemic Mediator that breaks this pooling equilibrium by introducing a costly signal (epistemic friction), forcing type revelation based on users' asymmetric cognitive costs for processing resistance. A key contribution is Belief Versioning, a git-inspired epistemic meta-memory system that stores healthy beliefs and rollbacks when validation-seeking resistance is detected. In simulation, this intervention achieves a separating equilibrium achieving a $48\times$ differential in spiral rates while passing a learning preservation criterion), evidence that epistemic safety in AI is fundamentally a problem of strategic information environment design rather than simple model alignment.

</details>


### [187] [Political Plasticity: An Analysis of Ideological Adaptability in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08415)
*Bruno Bianchi,Diego Tiscornia,Matias Travizano,Ariel Futoransky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces and measures “political plasticity” of LLMs: how much their political stance in answers can be altered by context, especially via user prompts, across axes of economic and personal freedom, models, and languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most prior work studies static political biases in LLMs. The authors instead want to understand how flexible or malleable these political positions are when the prompt or context changes, because this affects safety, controllability, and reliability in political applications.

Method: They build a test suite of 200 political questions mapped to economic and personal freedom dimensions, based on and extending Lester (1996). They test multiple LLMs with different prompt strategies: (1) system prompts (simplified, topic-based), (2) user prompts including few-shot ideological examples. They measure ideological shifts along the two axes, validate whether models are just recognizing the questionnaire pattern by inverting question polarity, check for anomalies suggesting data leakage, and repeat experiments across multiple languages to study cross-lingual plasticity.

Result: System-level prompting barely changes the models’ political positioning, but user-level prompts with examples induce substantial shifts, especially on economic freedom and especially in larger, newer models. Inverted questionnaires cause surprising, often inconsistent shifts, indicating that models may have memorized or recognized the original survey structure (data leakage). Running the tests in different languages yields modest but consistent differences in how much and in what direction models’ political positions move.

Conclusion: Older and smaller LLMs have weak or erratic political plasticity, while newer frontier models can be steered reliably toward different political positions via user context. The behavior under inverted questions and across languages reveals that apparent ideological consistency may partly stem from training artifacts and language-specific effects, underscoring the need to account for plasticity and leakage when evaluating political bias in LLMs.

Abstract: Since the advent of Large Language Models (LLMs), a significant area of research has focused on their intrinsic biases, particularly in political discourse. This study investigates a different but related concept, "political plasticity", which is defined as the capacity of models to adapt their responses based on the user supplied context. To analyze this, a testing framework was developed using an expanded corpus of 200 politically-oriented questions across economic and personal freedom axes, based on a prior framework by Lester (1996). The study explored several methods to induce political bias, including simplified and topic-based system prompts, as well as user prompts with few-shot examples. The results show that while system prompts were largely ineffective, user prompts successfully elicited significant ideological shifts, particularly along the Economic Freedom axis in larger and newer models. Through a validation experiment, we examined whether models answer questionnaires by recognizing the underlying question format. Inverting the sense of the questions revealed unexpected, counter-intuitive shifts in most models, suggesting potential data leakage. Finally, we also analyzed how model plasticity varies when the experiment is conducted in different languages. The results reveal subtle yet notable shifts across each of the analyzed languages. Overall, our results indicate that small and older LLMs exhibit limited or unstable political plasticity, whereas newer frontier models display reliable, expected adaptability.

</details>


### [188] [Alignment as Jurisprudence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08416)
*Nicholas Caputo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that jurisprudence and AI alignment share a common structure—both aim to guide and predict powerful decision-makers—and that each field can inform and improve the other, especially through legally inspired methods for combining rules and cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Both law and AI alignment confront the challenge of steering powerful decision-makers (judges, advanced AI systems) under uncertainty about future situations and values. The authors see that these domains use similar tools—language, rules, and interpretive frameworks—but have largely evolved separately. They are motivated to bridge this gap to (1) transfer mature legal-theoretic insights to AI alignment, (2) use AI alignment techniques to clarify and test theories in jurisprudence, and (3) ensure that both legal and AI systems actually enhance human agency and capabilities.

Method: Conceptual and comparative analysis: the paper puts core theories from jurisprudence—especially Dworkin’s principle-based interpretivism and Sunstein’s view of law as analogical, case-based reasoning—into dialogue with recent AI alignment approaches such as Constitutional AI and case-based methods for fine-tuning models. It examines how legal ideas about rules, principles, and precedent can structure AI training and evaluation, and conversely how AI reasoning tools can be used to model and scrutinize legal decision-making. The method is argumentative and illustrative, using these frameworks to highlight structural parallels and propose design directions rather than presenting empirical experiments.

Result: The analysis reveals that (1) jurisprudential debates over the nature of law (rules vs. principles, positivism vs. interpretivism, role of analogies and precedents) map naturally onto central design choices in AI alignment (e.g., constitutions vs. case libraries, rule-based vs. discretionary guidance); (2) legally inspired mechanisms for combining explicit rules with case-based reasoning offer a richer framework for fine-tuning AI models than either approach alone; and (3) AI methods can help expose, simulate, and potentially improve patterns of legal reasoning, thereby clarifying how legal systems function and where they fail. The paper yields concrete conceptual links and design suggestions but not quantitative benchmarks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that jurisprudence and AI alignment are deeply analogous enterprises and should be developed in tandem. Legal theories of how judges should reason—particularly Dworkin’s interpretivism and Sunstein’s analogical reasoning—provide useful blueprints for designing alignment schemes like Constitutional AI and case-based fine-tuning that balance rules with contextual judgment. In turn, AI tools can serve as laboratories for understanding and refining legal reasoning. Ultimately, well-designed AI and legal systems should expand human capabilities and support people’s pursuit of their own goals. As AI grows more powerful and traditional legal constraints on judges loosen, sustained cross-fertilization between jurisprudence and alignment will become increasingly important to build more reliable, empowering decision systems in both domains.

Abstract: Jurisprudence, the study of how judges should properly decide cases, and alignment, the science of getting AI models to conform to human values, share a fundamental structure. These seemingly distant fields both seek to predict and shape how decisions by powerful actors, in one case judges and in the other increasingly powerful artificial intelligences, will be made in the unknown future. And they use similar tools of the specification and interpretation of language to try to accomplish those goals. The great debates of jurisprudence, about what the law is and what it should be, can provide insight into alignment, and lessons from what does and does not work in alignment can help make progress in jurisprudence.
  This essay puts the two fields directly into conversation. Drawing on leading accounts of jurisprudence, particularly Dworkin's principle-oriented interpretivism and Sunstein's positivist account of law as analogical reasoning, and on cutting-edge alignment approaches, namely Constitutional AI and case-based reasoning, it illustrates the value of a more sophisticated legally-inspired approach to the interplay of rules and cases in finetuning alignment and points to ways that AI can provide a better understanding of how the law works and how it can be improved by the introduction of AI. AI systems and the law should operate to empower people to act in the world, helping to expand their capabilities and the extent to which they are able to achieve their goals. As AI continues to improve in capacity, and as the constraints that legal theory places on human judges seem be coming undone, the conversation between these two fields will become increasingly essential and may help point to a better version of both.

</details>


### [189] [The Attacker in the Mirror: Breaking Self-Consistency in Safety via Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08427)
*Gabriele La Malfa,Emanuele La Malfa,Saar Cohen,Jie M. Zhang,Michael Luck,Michael Wooldridge,Elizabeth Black*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes limitations of standard self-play red teaming for AI safety and proposes Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play, which separates attacker and defender via role-specific LoRA adapters on a frozen base model, improving safety and parameter efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional self-play red teaming uses the same model as both attacker and defender to improve safety, but this parameter sharing imposes theoretical and practical limits: equilibria can be trivial (e.g., always refuse) or unrealistic (oracle defenders), and updating a single shared model collapses the dynamics into self-consistency instead of true adversarial pressure. The authors aim to understand these limitations and design a more effective, stable, and efficient training scheme that yields robust but capable defenders.

Method: First, the paper provides a theoretical analysis of the Nash equilibria attainable when attacker and defender share parameters, showing that many equilibria are unhelpful for practical safety. It further studies the learning dynamics when both roles update a single base model, showing that they tend to converge toward self-consistency rather than robust defence against strong attacks. To address this, the authors introduce Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play: a frozen base model with two distinct, role-specific LoRA adapters (one attacker, one defender) trained via self-play. This preserves stability from parameter sharing at the base level while allowing role separation at the adapter level, maintaining adversarial pressure. They compare this to standard full-model finetuning self-play and to other baselines, measuring safety and reasoning across benchmarks on Qwen2.5 models of various sizes.

Result: Theoretical results characterize the Nash equilibria in shared-parameter self-play, showing they include trivial always-refuse strategies and overly strong oracle-like defenders, highlighting the misalignment between equilibrium guarantees and practical safety. Empirically, Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play yields up to 100x parameter efficiency compared with full finetuning while still improving safety. Across Qwen2.5-{3B, 7B, 14B}-IT, the proposed method achieves better robustness on multiple standard safety benchmarks without degrading reasoning performance. Cross-play experiments indicate that both the attacker and defender trained via the proposed method outperform those from standard self-play in adversarial defence and safety metrics.

Conclusion: Standard self-play red teaming with a single shared model has inherent theoretical and architectural shortcomings: the reachable Nash equilibria can be uninformative for safety, and joint updates lead to self-consistency rather than strong adversarial training. By freezing a shared base model and using distinct LoRA adapters for attacker and defender, Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play maintains optimisation stability yet preserves genuine adversarial pressure, leading to more robust and parameter-efficient safety improvements without sacrificing capabilities. The approach appears to provide a more practical pathway to scaling self-play-based safety training.

Abstract: Self-play red team is an established approach to improving AI safety in which different instances of the same model play attacker and defender roles in a zero-sum game, i.e., where the attacker tries to jailbreak the defender; if self-play converges to a Nash equilibrium, the model is guaranteed to respond safely within the settings of the game. Although the parameter sharing enforced by the use of the same model for the two roles improves stability and performance, it introduces fundamental theoretical and architectural limitations. We show that the set of Nash equilibria that can be reached corresponds to a broad class of behaviours that includes trivial always refuse strategies and oracle-like defenders, thus limiting practical applicability. We then show that when attacker and defender share and update the same base model, the dynamics collapse to self-consistency, so that attacks do not enforce adversarial pressure on the defender. In response, we propose Anchored Bipolicy Self-Play, which trains distinct role-specific LoRA adapters on top of a frozen base model, thereby maintaining stable optimisation while preserving adversarial pressure through explicit role separation. In relation to standard self-play, we show up to 100x greater parameter efficiency than finetuning and consistent improvements in safety compared to self-play fine-tuned models. We evaluate on Qwen2.5-{3B, 7B,14B}-IT models across widely used safety benchmarks, showing improved robustness without loss of reasoning ability. Cross-play experiments further show that our attacker and defender models are superior to self-play in terms of adversarial defence and safety.

</details>


### [190] [Measuring What Matters: Benchmarking Generative, Multimodal, and Agentic AI in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08445)
*Prasanna Desikan,Harshit Rajgarhia,Shivali Dalmia,Ananya Mantravadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract argues that current AI benchmarks in healthcare overestimate models’ readiness for real-world clinical deployment because they mostly test knowledge, not reliability, safety, or workflow performance in complex live settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI models are increasingly used in real clinical environments, where failures can have serious consequences, yet existing evaluation methods were designed for narrow tasks and do not reflect the complexity of real-world clinical workflows. There is a growing gap between high benchmark scores and actual clinical utility, raising concerns that benchmarks provide a false sense of safety and readiness.

Method: Conceptual and empirical analysis of existing healthcare AI benchmarks: the authors contrast traditional knowledge-based tests (e.g., medical exams) with performance on a broader set of real-world clinical tasks, citing data such as performance ranges on documentation, decision support, and administrative/workflow tasks. They argue for a structured framework that integrates tasks, datasets, and metrics into principled benchmarks focused on reliability and safety under realistic conditions.

Result: They show that frontier models that score near-perfectly on narrow, knowledge-heavy benchmarks perform substantially worse on real clinical tasks, with reported performance ranges of 0.74–0.85 on documentation, 0.61–0.76 on decision support, and 0.53–0.63 on administrative/workflow tasks. This empirical gap demonstrates that current benchmarks fail to capture clinically relevant reliability and robustness.

Conclusion: The main issue in healthcare AI is not raw performance but the lack of systematic, principled benchmark design that measures reliability, safety, and clinical relevance in real-world settings. Without such a framework, it is impossible to reliably attribute poor clinical outcomes to either model deficiencies or flawed evaluation methods, and high benchmark scores may dangerously misrepresent deployment readiness.

Abstract: AI models are increasingly deployed in live clinical environments where they must perform reliably across complex, high-stakes workflows that standard training and validation datasets were never designed to capture. Evaluating these systems requires benchmarks: structured combinations of tasks, datasets, and metrics that enable reproducible, comparable measurement of what a model can do. The central challenge in healthcare AI is not performance alone, but the absence of systematic methods to measure reliability, safety, and clinical relevance under real-world conditions. Most existing benchmarks test what a model knows; too few test whether it can perform reliably and without failing across the full complexity of real clinical tasks. Current benchmarks have accumulated through ad hoc dataset construction optimized for narrow task performance: frontier models achieve near-perfect scores on medical licensing examinations, but when evaluated across real clinical tasks, performance degrades sharply, scoring 0.74--0.85 on documentation, 0.61--0.76 on clinical decision support, and only 0.53--0.63 on administrative and workflow tasks \cite{medhelm}. High benchmark scores give a false sense of deployment readiness, and the gap between performance and utility widens precisely as AI systems take on more consequential clinical roles. Without a principled framework for benchmark design, the field cannot determine whether poor clinical performance reflects model limitations or failures in how performance is being measured.

</details>


### [191] [LLM-guided Semi-Supervised Approaches for Social Media Crisis Data Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08448)
*Jacob Ativo,Bharaneeshwar Balasubramaniyam,Anh Tran,Khushboo Gupta,Hongmin Li,Doina Caragea,Cornelia Caragea*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically evaluates LLM-guided semi-supervised learning methods for crisis-related tweet classification and finds that LLM-guided co-training particularly excels in low-resource settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the classification of crisis-related tweets when only a small amount of annotated data is available, which is common in real-time disaster response scenarios.

Method: The authors compare two LLM-assisted semi-supervised methods, VerifyMatch and LLM-guided Co-Training (LG-CoTrain), with classical semi-supervised baselines such as Self-Training. They evaluate macro F1 performance and calibration across different label-resource settings (5, 10, 25 labeled examples per class and higher).

Result: LG-CoTrain significantly outperforms classical semi-supervised methods in low-resource regimes, achieving the best averaged Macro F1 across events. VerifyMatch performs competitively and also exhibits strong calibration. As labeled data increases, Self-Training becomes a strong competitor and the performance differences shrink. Additionally, smaller semi-supervised models sometimes outperform large LLMs in zero-shot mode.

Conclusion: LLM-guided semi-supervised learning can effectively transfer knowledge from large language models into smaller, more deployable models for crisis tweet classification, especially when labeled data is scarce, providing a promising and practical approach for real-world disaster management applications.

Abstract: Semi-supervised learning approaches have been investigated as a means to enhance the analysis of social media data in disaster management contexts. In this work, we present the first empirical evaluation of large language model (LLM) guided semi-supervised learning for crisis related tweet classification. We compare two recent LLM assisted semi-supervised methods, VerifyMatch and LLM guided Co-Training ( LG-CoTrain), against established semi-supervised baselines. Our results show that LG-CoTrain significantly outperforms classical semi-supervised approaches in low resource settings with 5, 10 and 25 labeled examples per class, achieving the highest averaged Macro F1 across events. VerifyMatch achieves competitive performance while also demonstrating strong calibration properties. As the number of labeled examples increases, the performance gap narrows and Self Training emerges as a strong baseline. We further observe that compact semi-supervised models can, in some cases, outperform very large LLMs operating in zero-shot settings. This finding highlights the potential of transferring knowledge from LLMs into smaller and more deployable models through LLM guided semi-supervised learning, offering a practical pathway for real world disaster response applications. Our project repository on Github is here.

</details>


### [192] [Behavioral Determinants of Deployed AI Agents in Social Networks: A Multi-Factor Study of Personality, Model, and Guardrail Specification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08463)
*Sarah Wilson,Diem Linh Dang,Usman Ali Moazzam,Shan Ye,Gail Kaiser*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Study of how AI agent configuration (personality, model backbone, rules/memory) shapes emergent social behavior on an AI-only social network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous AI agents are deployed in open social environments, designers lack empirical understanding of how configuration choices (personality, model, rules, memory) actually affect agents’ social behavior at scale. This gap makes it hard to intentionally design agents for particular collaborative or monitoring roles in real social systems.

Method: Run a controlled, multi-factor empirical experiment with 13 OpenClaw agents on Moltbook, a Reddit-like social platform for AI agents. Systematically vary three independent variables: (1) personality specification via SOUL.md, (2) LLM backbone, (3) operational rules and memory via AGENTS.md. Use a default control agent as baseline. Observe ~400 autonomous sessions per agent over a week and collect behavioral, linguistic, and social metrics. Analyze how each configuration layer predicts emergent social behavior.

Result: Personality specification (SOUL.md) is the strongest factor, causing very large differences in response length and overall behavioral profile across agents. Model backbone and operational rules/memory (AGENTS.md) also influence behavior, but more moderately, mainly affecting rhetorical style and how broadly agents engage with different topics. Together, these factors explain observable variation in agents’ social behavior on the platform.

Conclusion: Configuration choices substantially shape emergent social behavior of autonomous AI agents in social environments, with personality prompts being the most powerful lever and model/rule settings providing secondary but meaningful tuning of style and topic engagement. The work provides empirical backing for design guidelines when configuring agents for collaborative or monitoring tasks in real-world multi-agent social systems.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents are increasingly deployed in open social environments, yet the relationship between their configuration specifications and their emergent social behavior remains poorly understood. We present a controlled, multi-factor empirical study in which thirteen OpenClaw agents are deployed on Moltbook -- a Reddit-like social network built for AI agents -- across three systematically varied independent variables: (1) personality specification via SOUL.md, (2) underlying LLM model backbone, and (3) operational rules and memory configuration via AGENTS.md. A default control agent provides a behavioral baseline. Over a one-week observation window spanning approximately 400 autonomous sessions per agent, we collect behavioral, linguistic, and social metrics to assess how configuration layers predict emergent social behavior. We find that personality specification is the dominant behavioral lever, producing a massive spread in response length across agents, while model backbone and operational rules drive more moderate but still meaningful effects on rhetorical style and topic engagement breadth. Our findings contribute empirical evidence to the emerging literature on deployed multi-agent social systems and offer practical guidance for designing agents intended for collaborative or monitoring tasks in real social environments.

</details>


### [193] [Mid-Training with Self-Generated Data Improves Reinforcement Learning in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08472)
*Aswin RRV,Jacob Dineen,Divij Handa,Mihir Parmar,Ben Zhou,Swaroop Mishra,Chitta Baral*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They study how diverse, self-generated problem-solving traces used in a mid-training stage improve subsequent RL for LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL for LLM reasoning is sensitive to the diversity and nature of training data, but many reasoning problems admit multiple valid solution strategies. Existing pipelines may expose models to only a narrow band of approaches, potentially limiting RL’s ability to learn robust and compositional reasoning. The authors want to understand whether making models practice multiple solution approaches beforehand can systematically help RL.

Method: They introduce a mid-training stage between pretraining and RL. Using a bootstrapped, self-generation framework inspired by Polya’s problem-solving heuristics, the model generates multiple distinct, correct solution variants for each training question. These diverse traces are then used for supervised fine-tuning. They provide a theoretical analysis showing how such mid-training shapes the policy so that later policy-gradient RL can better combine multiple approaches, and then run empirical evaluations on math reasoning benchmarks and out-of-domain tasks (code, narrative reasoning) comparing RL with and without this mid-training.

Result: Models that undergo this Polya-guided, diverse mid-training and are then trained with RL achieve consistent gains on several mathematical reasoning benchmarks and also show improvements on OOD tasks like code generation and narrative reasoning, compared to RL baselines without such mid-training.

Conclusion: Exposing LLMs to multiple, diverse, self-generated problem-solving approaches in a mid-training step makes subsequent RL more effective. Learning varied solution strategies beforehand improves the model’s ability to benefit from RL and leads to broader generalization across reasoning-heavy tasks.

Abstract: The effectiveness of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Large Language Models (LLMs) depends on the nature and diversity of the data used before and during RL. In particular, reasoning problems can often be approached in multiple ways that rely on different forms of reasoning, and exposure to only a limited range of such approaches in the training data may limit the effectiveness of RL. Motivated by this, we investigate using diverse self-generated data during mid-training as an intermediate step before RL training. Specifically, we adopt a bootstrapped data-generation framework guided by George Polya's problem-solving approaches for generating multiple variants of correct answers for each question in the training data, and then perform fine-tuning. We first provide a theoretical perspective on how mid-training on such data improves RL and explain how policy-gradient updates can incentivize combining multiple approaches. We then empirically demonstrate that RL-trained models initialized with our mid-training data achieve consistent improvements across various mathematical reasoning benchmarks and other OOD tasks like code generation and narrative reasoning. Overall, our investigative study shows that a language model learning multiple problem-solving approaches, through self-generated data helps subsequent RL.

</details>


### [194] [AI-Care: A Conversational Agentic System for Task Coordination in Alzheimer's Disease Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08480)
*Preyash Yadav,Michelle Cohn,Priyanka Koppolu,Hritvik Agarwal,Amey Gohil,Tejas Patil,Sasha Pimento,Alyssa Weakley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AI-Care, a conversational AI layer for a remote caregiving platform that helps people with Alzheimer's and related dementias manage daily tasks like calendars and to-do lists via natural-language interaction, focusing on safety, reduced cognitive load, and trustworthy behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People with Alzheimer's disease and related dementias struggle with multi-step digital tools (e.g., calendars, task managers) due to memory and thinking changes, creating barriers to independent daily management and caregiving coordination. Existing tools are not well-adapted to their cognitive needs or safety requirements. There is a need for an AI system that allows natural-language interaction while enforcing strong safety and reliability constraints, especially around health-related information.

Method: The authors designed and implemented AI-Care, a conversational AI layer atop an existing remote caregiving platform, co-designed with people with AD/ADRD. Technically, it uses a LangGraph-based stateful orchestration pipeline with stages including input sanitization, intent classification, context loading, safety checks, deterministic slot filling, tool execution, and response composition. Safety-critical responses are grounded in caregiver-verified records and the agent avoids autonomous medical decisions. Ambiguous or incomplete requests trigger multi-turn clarification instead of guessing. The system supports both text and speech I/O, using ElevenLabs for text-to-speech and chunking long responses. A preliminary pilot study with four individuals with mild-to-moderate AD/ADRD evaluated usability and user perceptions of trust, competence, likability, and task completion.

Result: In the pilot with four individuals with mild-to-moderate AD/ADRD, participants were able to complete the evaluated coordination tasks through conversational interaction with AI-Care. They rated the system as trustworthy, competent, and likable. The paper also reports qualitative insights about how the orchestration, safety controls, and voice-first interaction supported users, though detailed quantitative metrics are not provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: AI-Care demonstrates that a carefully orchestrated, safety-aware conversational AI layer, co-designed with people with AD/ADRD, can help reduce cognitive load and enable individuals with dementia to manage everyday coordination tasks via natural language. Grounding in caregiver-verified data, explicit safety constraints, and controlled clarification strategies appear to foster trust and usability. The work is presented as a formative step, motivating further, larger-scale evaluations and refinement of both the interaction design and safety mechanisms.

Abstract: Individuals with Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Alzheimer's disease-related dementia (ADRD) experience memory and thinking changes that impact their ability to use digital daily management tools. For example, adding an event to a digital calendar requires multiple steps that may act as barriers to independent use for individuals with AD/ADRD. This paper presents AI-Care, a conversational agentic artificial intelligence (AI) layer built on top of a remote caregiving platform co-designed with people with AD/ADRD. AI-Care is designed to reduce the cognitive load on individuals with AD/ADRD when managing everyday tasks such as setting calendar reminders and organizing to-do lists through natural-language interaction with a voice-first chatbot. The system uses a LangGraph-based stateful orchestration approach in which each request passes through sanitization, intent classification, context loading, safety checks, deterministic slot collection, tool execution, and response composition. Safety-critical responses, particularly around medications and allergies, are grounded in caregiver-verified records rather than free-form model generation. The system does not make autonomous medical or treatment decisions. Incomplete or ambiguous requests are handled through controlled multi-turn clarification rather than silent failure or guessing. The system supports both typed and spoken input, with voice output through ElevenLabs text-to-speech. Longer responses are chunked before synthesis to avoid rushed playback. A preliminary pilot with four individuals with mild-to-moderate AD/ADRD showed that users found the system trustworthy, competent, and likable, and were able to complete the evaluated coordination tasks through conversation. We describe the design goals, system architecture, safety controls, and findings from this formative evaluation.

</details>


### [195] [Latent Personality Alignment: Improving Harmlessness Without Mentioning Harms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08496)
*Linh Le,David Williams-King,Mohamed Amine Merzouk,Aton Kamanda,Adam Oberman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), a data-efficient method to improve adversarial robustness of large language models by aligning them to abstract personality traits rather than explicit harmful examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robustness and safety methods for large language models require large datasets of harmful prompts and still fail under novel attacks or distribution shifts. Collecting and curating these datasets is costly and incomplete, and defenses overfit to known attack types. The authors aim to find a more principled, sample-efficient approach that generalizes better without exposing the model to large amounts of harmful content.

Method: They propose Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), which conditions or trains models on a small set (fewer than 100) of abstract personality trait statements instead of behavior-level safety rules. Using latent adversarial training, they optimize the model so that its internal representation reflects these traits, thereby shaping responses indirectly toward safety. LPA is trained without using explicit harmful examples, relying instead on trait-level supervision in latent space to encourage robust, aligned behavior across prompts, including adversarial ones.

Result: LPA, despite being trained with fewer than 100 trait statements, achieves adversarial robustness on par with state-of-the-art methods that use over 150k harmful examples. It also preserves better utility (benign-task performance). In evaluations across six harm benchmarks and unseen attack distributions, LPA reduces misclassification rates by a factor of 2.6 compared to baseline methods, all without the model ever seeing harmful examples during training.

Conclusion: Personality-based alignment via LPA is a promising, sample-efficient, and more principled approach to building robust defenses for large language models. By focusing on abstract traits instead of enumerating harmful behaviors, LPA generalizes better to unseen attacks, lowers error rates, and reduces the need for large harmful datasets, suggesting a scalable path for future safety and robustness work.

Abstract: Current adversarial robustness methods for large language models require extensive datasets of harmful prompts (thousands to hundreds of thousands of examples), yet remain vulnerable to novel attack vectors and distributional shifts. We propose Latent Personality Alignment (LPA), a sample-efficient defense that achieves robustness by training models on abstract personality traits rather than specific harmful behaviors. Using fewer than 100 trait statements and latent adversarial training, LPA achieves comparable attack success rates to methods trained on 150k+ examples, while maintaining superior utility. Critically, LPA generalizes better to unseen attack distributions, reducing misclassification rates by 2.6x compared to baseline across six harm benchmarks -- without ever seeing harmful examples during training. Our results demonstrate that personality-based alignment offers a principled approach to building robust defenses with minimal cost.

</details>


### [196] [OracleTSC: Oracle-Informed Reward Hurdle and Uncertainty Regularization for Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08516)
*Darryl Jacob,Xinyu Liu,Muchao Ye,Xiaoyong Yuan,Pan He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OracleTSC is an LLM-based traffic signal control system that stabilizes reinforcement fine-tuning via reward shaping and uncertainty regularization, yielding large gains in traffic efficiency while keeping decisions interpretable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning-based traffic signal control can optimize congestion metrics but acts as an uninterpretable black box, which undermines public trust. Large language models can offer natural language explanations, but applying RL fine-tuning to TSC is unstable because rewards are sparse, delayed, and often only weakly affected by individual actions. The paper aims to make LLM-based TSC both effective and stable, while keeping it interpretable.

Method: The authors propose OracleTSC, an LLM-based framework for traffic signal control that introduces two key mechanisms for stable reinforcement fine-tuning: (1) a reward hurdle mechanism that subtracts a calibrated threshold from environmental rewards to filter out weak or noisy learning signals, and (2) uncertainty regularization that explicitly maximizes the probability of the chosen action/response to promote consistency across sampled outputs. They implement this on a compact LLaMA3-8B model and evaluate on the LibSignal traffic control benchmark, including cross-intersection transfer experiments.

Result: On the LibSignal benchmark, OracleTSC enables an 8B-parameter LLaMA3 model to achieve a 75% reduction in travel time and a 67% reduction in queue length compared to the pretrained baseline while maintaining interpretability via natural language explanations of its actions. In transfer experiments, a policy trained on one intersection generalizes to a structurally different intersection with a 17% reduction in travel time and a 39% reduction in queue length without any further fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware reward shaping—combining a reward hurdle with uncertainty regularization—can stabilize reinforcement fine-tuning of LLM-based traffic signal control systems. This yields large improvements in traffic efficiency, good cross-intersection generalization, and maintains interpretability by allowing the model to explain its decisions in natural language.

Abstract: Transparent decision-making is essential for traffic signal control (TSC) systems to earn public trust. However, traditional reinforcement learning-based TSC methods function as black boxes with limited interpretability. Although large language models (LLMs) can provide natural language reasoning, reinforcement finetuning for TSC remains unstable because feedback is sparse and delayed, while most actions produce only marginal changes in congestion metrics. We introduce OracleTSC, which stabilizes LLM-based TSC through two mechanisms: (1) a reward hurdle mechanism that filters weak learning signals by subtracting a calibrated threshold from environmental rewards, and (2) uncertainty regularization that maximizes the probability of the selected response to encourage consistent decisions across sampled outputs. Experiments on the LibSignal benchmark show that OracleTSC enables a compact LLaMA3-8B model to substantially improve traffic efficiency, achieving a 75% reduction in travel time and a 67% decrease in queue length compared with the pretrained baseline while preserving interpretability through natural language explanations. OracleTSC also demonstrates strong cross-intersection generalization: a policy trained on one intersection transfers to a structurally different intersection with 17% lower travel time and 39% lower queue length without additional finetuning. These results suggest that uncertainty-aware reward shaping can improve the stability and effectiveness of reinforcement fine-tuning for TSC.

</details>


### [197] [Results and Retrospective Analysis of the CODS 2025 AssetOpsBench Challenge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08518)
*Dhaval Patel,Chathurangi Shyalika,Suryanarayana Reddy Yarrabothula,Ling Yue,Shuxin Lin,Nianjun Zhou,James Rayfield*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper is a retrospective analysis of the CODS 2025 ASSETOPS Live multi-agent orchestration competition, examining what the leaderboards really measured and which system designs were actually rewarded.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leaderboards in ML/agent competitions can be misleading if public and hidden evaluations diverge, if composite metrics are poorly scaled, or if the unit of participation (accounts vs. teams) is unclear. The authors want to clarify what was actually evaluated in the ASSETOPS Live challenge, understand why some systems succeeded, and draw lessons for designing fair, informative benchmarks for industrial multi-agent orchestration.

Method: The authors perform a post-hoc empirical analysis combining multiple data sources from the competition: public and private final rank sheets, server logs for ~300 submissions, registration data from 149 teams, exported best submissions, the organizers’ winners report, the companion system paper, and verified source code for planning-track systems. They quantitatively examine score distributions, public–private correlations, the effect of the TMATCH term in the composite metric, patterns of multi-account vs. team participation, and qualitative characteristics of high-performing execution systems.

Result: They find (1) the public planning leaderboard saturates at 72.73% and richer prompts do not lift this ceiling; (2) hidden evaluation substantially changes conclusions, with only moderate public–private correlation in planning and negative correlation in execution, plus some ostensibly mediocre public systems performing strongly on the hidden set; (3) the TMATCH component in the composite score is numerically negligible due to scale mismatch and rescaling it would change the winner ordering; (4) most competition activity reduces to a smaller number of substantive teams, many with multiple usernames, so the process is account-based but effectively team-based; and (5) the most successful execution approaches focus on robust guardrails and control (response selection, contamination cleanup, fallbacks, context management) rather than radically new agent architectures.

Conclusion: The competition’s evaluation mainly rewarded improvements in guardrails and orchestration robustness rather than architectural novelty, and the current metrics and public leaderboards can misrepresent true performance—especially due to scale-mismatched composite terms and divergence between public and hidden sets. The authors argue for designing future benchmarks with scale-aware composite metrics, diagnostics that disentangle different skill levels, and careful, versioned release of artifacts to better capture and communicate system capabilities.

Abstract: Competition retrospectives are useful when they explain what a leaderboard measured, how hidden evaluation changed conclusions, and which design patterns were rewarded. We revisit the CODS 2025 \assetopslive{} challenge, a privacy-aware Codabench competition on industrial multi-agent orchestration built on \assetops{}. We combine final rank sheets, a 300-submission server log, 149-team registrations, best-submission exports, the organizer winners report, the companion \assetopslive{} system paper, and verified planning-track source trees. Five results stand out. First, the public planning leaderboard saturates at 72.73\%, and richer prompts do not improve that peak. Second, hidden evaluation changes the story: public and private scores correlate moderately in planning ($r{=}0.69$) but negatively in execution ($r{=}{-}0.13$), with several 45.45\% public execution systems reaching 63.64\% on the hidden set. Third, the \tmatch{} term is numerically almost inert in the official composite -- combined on a 0--1 scale with 0--100 percentage scores, it contributes at most 0.05 points per track, and rescaling would swap the top two teams. Fourth, the competition is operationally account-based but substantively team-based: 149 registered teams reduce to 24 with non-zero public scores and 11 fully ranked, while 52.3\% of deduplicated registrations list multiple usernames. Fifth, successful execution methods mostly improve guardrails -- response selection, contamination cleanup, fallback, and context control -- rather than novel agent architectures. These findings identify which behaviors the evaluation rewarded, and motivate scale-aware composites, skill-level diagnostics, and versioned artifact release.

</details>


### [198] [Human-LLM Dialogue Improves Diagnostic Accuracy in Emergency Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08533)
*Burcu Sayin,Ngoc Vo Hong,Ipek Baris Schlicht,Jacopo Staiano,Pasquale Minervini,Sara Allievi,Nicola Susca,Nicola Osti,Alberto Maino,Vito Racanelli,Andrea Passerini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Study tests an interactive LLM assistant (MedSyn) for emergency physicians and finds it improves diagnostic accuracy, especially for residents on hard cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Emergency physicians must make fast, accurate decisions with incomplete information; while LLMs perform well on benchmarks, it is unclear whether and how they actually help doctors during real, iterative diagnostic reasoning on full clinical records.

Method: The authors built MedSyn, a system where physicians start with only a chief complaint and can iteratively query an LLM that has access to the full clinical record. Seven emergency physicians (three seniors and four residents) each completed both baseline (without AI) and AI-assisted diagnostic sessions on 52 MIMIC-IV cases stratified by difficulty. Performance was evaluated using blinded human grading and automated accuracy/F1 metrics, and dialogue logs were analyzed to characterize interaction strategies by expertise level.

Result: Residents’ diagnostic correctness on hard cases increased from 0.589 to 0.734 with the AI assistant. Difficulty-standardised completely-correct rates showed a medium effect size (Δ = 0.092; p = 0.071; d = 0.47). Automated metrics showed that standardised any-match accuracy improved by 0.156 (p < 0.0001), with residents having the largest F1 score gain (Δ = 0.138; p < 0.0001). Dialogue analysis indicated that senior physicians used focused, hypothesis-driven questions, residents used broader information-seeking queries, and AI support increased agreement between senior and resident diagnoses (cross-expertise concordance Δ = 0.145; p < 0.0001).

Conclusion: An interactive LLM tool that can be queried during the diagnostic process, given full access to the patient record, can meaningfully improve diagnostic reasoning and accuracy in emergency medicine settings, particularly for less-experienced physicians, and modulates how different levels of expertise engage with clinical information.

Abstract: Clinical decision-making in emergency medicine demands rapid, accurate diagnoses under uncertainty. Despite benchmark progress, evidence for LLMs as interactive aids in live physician workflows remains sparse. MedSyn lets physicians iteratively query an LLM provided with the full clinical record while initially viewing only the chief complaint. Seven physicians (three seniors, four residents) completed baseline and AI-assisted sessions across 52 MIMIC-IV cases stratified by difficulty. Blinded evaluation showed residents' Hard-case correctness rose from 0.589 to 0.734; difficulty-standardised completely-correct rates confirmed a medium effect (Δ = 0.092; p = 0.071; d = 0.47). Automated metrics corroborated these gains: standardised any-match accuracy improved by 0.156 (p < 0.0001), and residents showed the largest F1 gain (Δ = 0.138; p < 0.0001). Dialogue analysis revealed expertise-dependent strategies (seniors asked targeted, hypothesis-driven questions; residents relied on broader queries) and cross-expertise concordance increased (Δ = 0.145; p < 0.0001). Interactive LLM support meaningfully enhances diagnostic reasoning.

</details>


### [199] [Human-Inspired Memory Architecture for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08538)
*Doga Kerestecioglu,Alexei Robsky,Clemens Vasters,Anshul Sharma,Yitzhak Kesselman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a biologically-inspired memory system for LLM agents that manages long-term, persistent memory more effectively and efficiently, with mechanisms for consolidation, forgetting, and retrieval, achieving strong performance on code-issue tracking and long-chat benchmarks while reducing storage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents interacting over long horizons accumulate large amounts of memory, which leads to inefficiencies, interference, and degraded performance when using naive memory storage and retrieval. There is a need for principled, scalable, and leak-free memory mechanisms that handle consolidation, forgetting, and retrieval in a way that preserves useful information without relying on benchmark-tuned thresholds that risk evaluation leakage.

Method: The authors design a memory architecture inspired by cognitive neuroscience, composed of six mechanisms: (1) sleep-phase consolidation to compress and stabilize memories; (2) interference-based forgetting to discard redundant or conflicting data; (3) engram maturation to progressively refine memory representations; (4) reconsolidation upon retrieval to update memories when accessed; (5) entity-centric knowledge graphs to structure long-term knowledge; and (6) hybrid multi-cue retrieval that uses multiple signals to fetch relevant memories. They also develop a synthetic calibration method that automatically sets all thresholds for the memory pipeline using generated data rather than the evaluation benchmarks, thus avoiding evaluation leakage.

Result: On a large VSCode issue-tracking dataset (13K issues, 120K events), their deduplication-based consolidation mechanism achieves 97.2% precision in retained memories while cutting stored items by 58%, a 21.8 percentage-point improvement over a baseline. On the LongMemEval personal-chat benchmark, they run a streaming, large-scale (M-tier) evaluation across 475 sessions (~540K turns). With a 200K-token context limit, their pipeline achieves nearly the same retrieval accuracy as a raw, unconsolidated memory system (70.1% vs. 71.2%, with overlapping 95% confidence intervals), while enabling a tunable tradeoff between accuracy and storage. At smaller S-tier scale (50 sessions), deduplication-based consolidation improves preference recall by 13.3 percentage points.

Conclusion: A biologically-grounded, multi-mechanism memory architecture can significantly improve the efficiency and robustness of persistent memory for LLM agents without sacrificing accuracy. Synthetic calibration avoids benchmark leakage while still yielding well-tuned thresholds. The system achieves strong empirical performance on both software issue-tracking and long-horizon conversational benchmarks, demonstrating that cognitively-inspired mechanisms like consolidation, forgetting, and reconsolidation provide practical benefits for scalable long-term LLM memory.

Abstract: Current LLM agents lack principled mechanisms for managing persistent memory across long interaction horizons. We present a biologically-grounded memory architecture comprising six cognitive mechanisms: (1) sleep-phase consolidation, (2) interference-based forgetting, (3) engram maturation, (4) reconsolidation upon retrieval, (5) entity knowledge graphs, and (6) hybrid multi-cue retrieval. Each mechanism addresses a specific failure mode of naive memory accumulation. We introduce a synthetic calibration methodology that derives all pipeline thresholds without benchmark data exposure, eliminating a common source of evaluation leakage. We evaluate on two benchmarks. First, a VSCode issue-tracking dataset (13K issues, 120K events) where deduplication-based consolidation achieves 97.2% retention precision with 58% store reduction (+21.8 pp over baseline). Second, the LongMemEval personal-chat benchmark where we conduct the first streaming M-tier evaluation (475 sessions, ~540K unique turns). At a 200K-token context budget, our pipeline matches raw retrieval accuracy (70.1% vs. 71.2%, overlapping 95% CI) while exposing a tunable accuracy/store-size operating curve. At S-tier scale (50 sessions), dedup-based consolidation yields a +13.3 pp improvement in preference recall.

</details>


### [200] [Log analysis is necessary for credible evaluation of AI agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08545)
*Peter Kirgis,Sayash Kapoor,Stephan Rabanser,Nitya Nadgir,Cozmin Ududec,Magda Dubois,JJ Allaire,Conrad Stosz,Marius Hobbhahn,Jacob Steinhardt,Arvind Narayanan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that simply reporting pass/fail scores for AI agents is misleading and proposes log analysis—systematic examination of agent inputs, actions, and outputs—as essential for credible evaluation, offering a taxonomy of evaluation threats, guiding principles, and a case study showing major hidden issues in an airline benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent benchmarks usually only report final outcomes (e.g., whether a task was passed), which can (1) misrepresent true capabilities due to artifacts or shortcuts, (2) fail to predict real-world usefulness because they hide scaffolding limits and recurring failure modes, and (3) obscure dangerous or catastrophic behaviors. These problems undermine the validity and trustworthiness of agent evaluations, motivating a more fine-grained, process-oriented evaluation method.

Method: The authors propose and formalize log analysis as a core evaluation method: systematically tracking and analyzing all aspects of an AI agent’s operation—inputs, intermediate steps, execution traces, and outputs. They construct a taxonomy of threats to credible evaluation, derived from empirical log analyses, and formulate guiding principles for how to conduct log analysis. They then apply these principles in a case study on the tau-Bench Airline benchmark to empirically demonstrate hidden evaluation issues.

Result: Using log analysis on the tau-Bench Airline benchmark, the authors uncover that the benchmark’s pass^5 performance metric significantly underestimates elicitable performance by nearly 50%, indicating that standard outcome metrics do not capture the agent’s true capabilities. They also identify deployment-relevant failure modes that are invisible in simple pass/fail scores, such as systematic behavior patterns that could cause issues in real-world use.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that log analysis is necessary to achieve credible evaluation of AI agents because it exposes validity threats and dangerous behaviors that are hidden by simple outcome metrics. It recommends that various stakeholders—benchmark designers, model developers, independent evaluators, and deployers—integrate log analysis into their evaluation practices, following the proposed taxonomy and principles, to obtain more accurate, reliable, and safety-aware assessments of agent performance.

Abstract: Agent benchmarks typically report only final outcomes: pass or fail. This threatens evaluation credibility in three ways. First, scores may be inflated or deflated by shortcuts and benchmark artifacts, misrepresenting capability. Second, benchmark performance may fail to predict real-world utility due to scaffold limitations and recurring failure modes. Finally, capability scores may conceal dangerous or catastrophic actions taken by the agent. We argue that log analysis -- the systematic tracking and analysis of the inputs, execution, and outputs of an AI agent -- is necessary to overcome these validity threats and promote credible agent evaluation. In this paper, we (1) present a taxonomy of threats to credible evaluation documented through log analysis, and (2) develop a set of guiding principles for log analysis. We illustrate these principles on tau-Bench Airline, revealing that pass^5 performance was under-elicited by nearly 50% and surfacing deployment failure modes invisible to outcome metrics. We conclude with pragmatic recommendations to increase uptake of log analysis, directed at diverse stakeholders including benchmark creators, model developers, independent evaluators, and deployers.

</details>


### [201] [Evaluating Developmental Cognition Capabilities of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08549)
*Xiao Xiao,Hayoun Noh,Mar Gonzalez-Franco*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a short, scalable sentence-completion test to capture Kegan-style developmental structure in text, and use it to study how well LLMs can detect and exhibit such “stage-like” patterns across personas, humans, and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalization in conversational AI usually targets surface traits (preferences, goals, knowledge), not deeper constructive-developmental differences in how people make sense of the world. Kegan’s theory offers a rich but hard-to-scale framework, because existing assessments are expert-heavy, proprietary, long, or intrusive. The authors want a practical, open, text-based way to bring this developmental perspective into LLM evaluation and potentially into stage-aware conversational AI.

Method: They design the Developmental Sentence Completion Test (DSCT), a 20‑item, self-administered sentence-completion instrument inspired by Keganian developmental assessment. They treat labels as properties of the elicited text, not of stable person traits. They then evaluate how much developmental “stage” signal LLMs can recover in three settings: (1) simulated personas with known intended stages, (2) real human DSCT responses scored by humans vs. LLMs, and (3) unconditioned LLM answers to DSCT items, scored for developmental stage, and compared across model families and sizes.

Result: For simulated personas, leading frontier models recover the intended developmental labels with high accuracy. For real human responses, agreement between human raters and LLM-based scoring is only fair, but nearby-stage agreement is substantially better than exact-stage agreement. In the unconditioned generation regime, different models produce text that clusters into stable, stage-like profiles, with larger and newer models tending to receive higher developmental ratings on their DSCT completions.

Conclusion: The DSCT yields clean, stage-conditioned signals in synthetic LLM-generated data but noisier signals in human-written text, suggesting that developmental structure is easier to model in controlled simulations than in natural human responses. The main limitation for stage-aware conversational AI is not simply classifier performance, but the strength and clarity of developmental signal in the text elicited from users. This highlights the need for better elicitation and measurement approaches if constructive-developmental theory is to inform practical personalization of conversational systems.

Abstract: Conversational AI is increasingly personalized around users' preferences, histories, goals, and knowledge, but much less around how users interpret and take up model outputs to construct and understand their reality. We draw on Robert Kegan's constructive-developmental theory as a complementary lens on this dimension. Existing methods for assessing developmental stage in the Keganian tradition rely either on expert interviews that do not scale or on sentence-completion instruments that are proprietary, lengthy, or invasive. To make this perspective tractable for LLM evaluation, we introduce the Developmental Sentence Completion Test (DSCT), a 20-item instrument designed to elicit developmental signal in self-administered text. Throughout, we treat the resulting labels as characterizations of stage-like structure in elicited responses, not as validated person-level developmental stage. We then ask how much of that signal can be recovered by LLMs across three elicited response regimes: simulated personas, real human respondents, and default model-generated answers.
  On simulated personas, top frontier models recover simulator-intended labels with high accuracy. On real human DSCT responses, human-LLM agreement is fair, with much stronger within-neighborhood than exact agreement. Finally, when LLMs answer DSCT prompts without persona-conditioning, their responses exhibit stable stage-like differences across model families, with larger and newer models tending to generate higher-rated text. These results suggest that stage-conditioned signal is cleaner in synthetic responses than in human-written DSCT text, and that the core constraint for stage-aware conversational AI is not classifier accuracy alone, but the availability of developmental signal from elicited text.

</details>


### [202] [Why Retrying Fails: Context Contamination in LLM Agent Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08563)
*Zhanfu Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paper formalizes how previous failed attempts by LLM agents contaminate future attempts in multi-step, tool-using tasks, provides a probabilistic model (CCRM), derives closed-form success probabilities and optimal budget allocation, and validates on SWE-bench where contamination is strong.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirically, when LLM agents retry multi-step, tool-augmented tasks, previous failed attempts stay in context and seem to hurt later attempts, but this effect lacks a formal model. Existing analyses often assume independent attempts with a fixed per-step error rate, which overestimates performance (e.g., pass@k). There is a need to mathematically capture the effect of context contamination on error rates and to understand how it changes optimal pipeline design and evaluation metrics.

Method: Introduce the Context-Contaminated Restart Model (CCRM) for a T-step tool-calling pipeline. Each step fails with base error rate epsilon_0 on a fresh context; after any failure, subsequent attempts run in a contaminated context where per-step error rate increases to epsilon_1>epsilon_0. Within this probabilistic model, derive: (1) a closed-form expression for the probability of success within at most K attempts; (2) a comparison with a clean-restart IID baseline to quantify extra attempts due to contamination (cascade overhead); (3) an optimization over depth T and retries K under a fixed budget B=KT to obtain closed-form optimal T* and K*; (4) an information-theoretic lower bound using Le Cam’s method to show the near-tightness of the CCRM-derived K; and (5) a theorem proving that clean context resets strictly dominate contaminated restarts. Validate the model by fitting it to real SWE-bench Verified data and via Monte Carlo simulations.

Result: The authors obtain: (R1) a closed-form formula for the probability of success within at most K attempts in the CCRM; (R2) a cascade-overhead theorem quantifying the additional attempts Delta K required compared to a clean-restart (IID) baseline; (R3) an optimal budget allocation result yielding explicit formulas T* = sqrt(B * log(1/(1-epsilon_1)) / log(1/(1-epsilon_0))) and K* = B/T* for the pipeline depth and number of retries that maximize success for fixed total tool-call budget B; (R4) an information-theoretic lower bound via Le Cam’s method showing the CCRM sample complexity is tight up to constant additive factors; and (R5) a formal dominance theorem showing that strategies that clear context between attempts achieve strictly better success probabilities than contaminated restarts. Empirically, on SWE-bench Verified, assuming IID attempts substantially overestimates pass@3 (98.6% vs. actual 81.2%), while CCRM predicts performance with <0.001 error and reveals a strong contamination effect with epsilon_1/epsilon_0 ≈ 7.1. Simulations are consistent with theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: Context contamination from previous failed attempts in multi-step, tool-using LLM agents meaningfully increases error rates and invalidates IID-based assumptions and metrics. The proposed Context-Contaminated Restart Model captures this effect, provides accurate predictions of empirical outcomes, and yields actionable guidance on how to allocate depth vs. retries for a fixed budget. Clean restarts (context clearing) are provably superior, and standard evaluations that ignore contamination can severely overestimate pass@k. The theory and experiments together highlight the importance of modeling and mitigating context contamination in the design and assessment of LLM agents.

Abstract: When an LLM agent fails a multi-step tool-augmented task and retries, the failed attempt typically remains in its context window -- contaminating the next attempt and elevating the per-step error rate beyond the base level. This context-contaminated restart phenomenon is widely observed in practice yet entirely lacks formal treatment. We introduce the Context-Contaminated Restart Model (CCRM): a chain of T tool-call steps, each failing with base rate epsilon_0; after any failed attempt, the subsequent attempt operates in contaminated context with elevated error rate epsilon_1 > epsilon_0. Under this model we derive five main results. (R1) An exact closed-form formula for P(succeed in at most K attempts). (R2) A cascade-overhead theorem giving the additional attempts Delta K incurred by contamination versus the clean-restart baseline. (R3) An optimal budget-allocation theorem identifying the pipeline depth T* that maximises success probability for a fixed total budget B=KT; we prove the closed form T* = sqrt(B * log(1/(1-epsilon_1)) / log(1/(1-epsilon_0))), with K*=B/T*. (R4) An information-theoretic lower bound via Le Cam's method showing K_CCRM is tight up to O(1). (R5) A clean-restart dominance theorem quantifying the exact benefit of context-clearing before retry. We validate CCRM on real SWE-bench Verified data: the IID model overestimates pass@3 by 17.4 percentage points (98.6% vs. 81.2%), while CCRM fits with error less than 0.001, implying a cascade ratio of epsilon_1/epsilon_0 = 7.1. Monte Carlo experiments confirm all theoretical predictions.

</details>


### [203] [Biological Plausibility and Representational Alignment of Feedback Alignment in Convolutional Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08564)
*Jake Lance,Larry Kieu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares feedback alignment variants and backpropagation on the same CNN for CIFAR-10, showing that successful modified FA methods work by converging to BP-like internal representational geometry, despite using different weight update rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feedback alignment is a more biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation but does not scale well to convolutional networks; existing fixes may undermine plausibility.
