The authors want to systematically evaluate these algorithms side-by-side and understand why some modified FA methods work, particularly in terms of biological plausibility, interpretability of learned representations, and computational cost.

Method: They implement a single convolutional architecture trained on CIFAR-10 under five different learning rules: standard backpropagation and several modified feedback alignment algorithms. They run controlled experiments and perform a tripartite analysis: (1) biological plausibility (e.g., reliance on weight symmetry or global signals), (2) interpretability (analyzing and comparing internal representations/feature geometry across layers), and (3) computational complexity (time and resource demands). They compare the structure and geometry of internal representations produced by each algorithm to those from BP.

Result: Modified feedback alignment algorithms can successfully train the CNN on CIFAR-10 and produce internal representations that are structurally and geometrically similar to those obtained with backpropagation, even though their learning rules differ. The representational geometry—how examples are arranged in feature space—under modified FA tends to align closely with that from BP.

Conclusion: The effectiveness of modified feedback alignment for convolutional networks likely arises from its ability to approximate the representational geometry induced by backpropagation. That is, although the weight update rules are different and more biologically plausible, the resulting internal feature spaces converge to BP-like structures. This suggests that matching BP’s internal representation geometry may be a key requirement for biologically plausible alternatives to BP to achieve competitive performance.

Abstract: The feedback alignment (FA) algorithm offers a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation (BP) for training neural networks yet notably fails to scale to convolutional architectures. Modifications have been proposed to address this limitation, but at questionable cost to biological plausibility. In this paper, we evaluate five learning algorithms including modified FA and standard BP, applied to the same convolutional architecture with the CIFAR-10 dataset. We provide a tripartite comparative analysis focusing on biological plausibility, interpretability, and computational complexity. Our results indicate that modified FA algorithms converge on internal representations that are structurally similar to those produced by backpropagation. In particular, it appears the functional success of modified FA algorithms may be rooted in their ability to mimic the representational geometry of backpropagation, converging on similar representations despite relying on fundamentally different weight update mechanisms.

</details>


### [204] [What Will Happen Next: Large Models-Driven Deduction for Emergency Instances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08599)
*Zhengqing Hu,Dong Chen,Junkun Yuan,Liang Liu,Hua Wang,Zhao Jin,Yingchaojie Feng,Wei Chen,Mingliang Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes WLDS, a large-model-driven system that generates diverse, calibrated simulations of emergency events to support risk assessment and decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing emergency simulation systems rely on preset, previously observed scenarios, which limits randomness, diversity, and coverage of potential risks, especially when real emergency instances are scarce. There is a need for a method that can explore more possible evolutions of emergencies while maintaining factual and logical reliability to improve risk assessment and decision-making.

Method: The authors design the LMs-driven World Line Divergence System (WLDS). WLDS uses large models to generate and deduce multiple possible development paths ("world lines") of emergency events across domains. It incorporates two key calibration mechanisms: factual calibration to align generated content with real-world facts and data, and logical calibration to ensure consistency and reasoning soundness. An interactive module lets users choose or adjust deduction directions, helping steer away from hard-to-detect hallucinations. A visualization module combines text and images to present simulated emergency trajectories more intuitively. They also construct an Emergency Instances Deduction (EID) benchmark to evaluate the system.

Result: On the EID benchmark, WLDS achieves high precision and fidelity in simulating and deducing emergency instances across several specific domains. Experiments show that WLDS can generate a larger volume of meaningful emergency deduction data than traditional systems and that its outputs maintain factual accuracy and logical rigor. Additional experiments indicate that these generated scenarios support more informed and potentially better decision-making for future similar emergencies.

Conclusion: WLDS demonstrates that large models, when equipped with factual and logical calibration plus interactive control and visualization, can overcome the limitations of traditional preset-based emergency simulators. The system produces diverse, high-quality emergency scenarios across domains, enriching data for analysis and supporting improved risk assessment and emergency decision-making. The proposed EID benchmark provides a basis for future research and comparison in emergency instance deduction.

Abstract: Traditional simulation methods reproduce occurred emergency instances through presetting to assist people in risk assessment and emergency decision-making. However, due to the lack of randomness and diversity, existing simulation systems struggle to fully explore the potential risk as emergency instances are scarce. In contrast, Large Models (LMs) can dynamically adjust generation strategies to introduce controllable randomness, while also possessing extensive prior knowledge and cross-domain knowledge transfer capabilities. Inspired by it, we propose the LMs-driven World Line Divergence System (WLDS), which enables diversified visualization and deduction of emergency instances in different domains. WLDS leverages LMs to deduce emergency instances in various development directions, and introduces the factual calibration and logical calibration mechanism to ensure factual accuracy and logical rigor during the deduction process. The interactive module can independently select deduction directions to avoid potential hallucinations that are difficult for the system to identify. Furthermore, by introducing the visualization module, WLDS forms simulation and deduction that combine text and images, which enhances interpretability. Extensive experiments conducted on the proposed Emergency Instances Deduction (EID) benchmark dataset demonstrate that WLDS achieves high-precision and high-fidelity simulation and deduction of emergency instances in multiple specific domains. Relevant experiments further demonstrate that WLDS can generate more emergency instances deduction data for users and provide support for better decision-making in similar emergency instances in the future.

</details>


### [205] [The Echo Amplifies the Knowledge: Somatic Marker Analogues in Language Models via Emotion Vector Re-Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08611)
*Jared Glover*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They add an emotion-like, feature-based memory “echo” to a language model so it can later partially re-experience how situations felt, not just what happened, and show this affects its threat appraisals and decisions only when combined with semantic knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing language model memories are purely semantic: they store factual summaries of past interactions but not the associated affective state, unlike humans, who have both semantic and episodic/affective memories. Psychological work (Tulving’s noetic vs autonoetic consciousness and Damasio’s somatic marker hypothesis) suggests that emotion-linked markers are important for guiding decisions. The authors want to see whether adding an explicit, reusable representation of emotional state to LM memory will change its later appraisals and choices in a way that parallels human findings.

Method: They work with Gemma 3 1B-IT plus pretrained Gemma Scope 2 sparse autoencoders. At an internal layer (22) they identify 310 features that are “emotion-exclusive” and whose geometry corresponds to psychologically meaningful emotion structure. During an experience, they construct emotion vectors from these features, store them, and later partially re-inject this vector at recall time, when triggered by context similarity computed at an earlier layer (7). They compare four conditions: A) no memory, B) semantic labels only, C) emotion echo only, and BC) both semantic labels and emotion echo, evaluating emotional orientation (threat vs safety gradient) and decision quality under each condition, with statistical testing (permutation tests, z-tests).

Result: The emotion echo alone (condition C) increases the sensitivity of the model’s threat ratings to contextual similarity compared with no memory (A): the slope of threat rating vs similarity rises from 0.56 to 0.80 with a statistically significant difference (p=0.011). For decision-making, adding the echo to semantic knowledge (BC) substantially improves the rate of good choices (80%) compared with semantic knowledge alone (B, 52%), with z=+2.60 and p<0.01. However, the echo alone (C) does not improve decision quality (22%, not significant). Thus, the echo alters emotional appraisals by itself, but only affects actions when combined with semantic information.

Conclusion: They demonstrate a concrete mechanism for augmenting language models with affect-like episodic traces based on interpretable internal features. These emotion echoes measurably change how the model evaluates threat and safety and, when paired with semantic knowledge, improve downstream decisions, mirroring Damasio’s claim that emotional markers amplify the impact of knowledge on action rather than substituting for it. The work suggests that affective memory components can be added to LMs to better align their behavior with human-like appraisal and decision processes.

Abstract: Current language model memory systems store what happened but not how it felt. This distinction -- between semantic memory (knowing about a past event) and episodic memory (re-experiencing it) -- was identified by Tulving as the difference between noetic and autonoetic consciousness. Damasio demonstrated that humans with intact knowledge but absent emotional markers exhibit impaired decision-making.
  We bridge this gap for language models. Using Gemma 3 1B-IT with pretrained Gemma Scope 2 sparse autoencoders, we identify 310 emotion-exclusive features at layer 22 with psychologically valid geometry. We construct distinctive-feature emotion vectors during experience and partially re-inject them during recall, triggered by context similarity at layer 7.
  We test four conditions paralleling Damasio's framework: A (no memory), B (semantic labels), C (emotion echo), and BC (semantic + echo). For emotional orientation, the echo alone steepens the threat-safety gradient: the regression slope of threat rating on contextual similarity is 0.80 for C vs 0.56 for A ($p$=0.011, permutation test). For decisions, the echo amplifies knowledge into action: BC=80% good choices vs B=52% ($z$=+2.60, $p$<0.01), while the echo alone has no effect (C=22%, n.s.). The echo changes how the model feels independently, but changes what it does only when combined with knowledge -- replicating Damasio's core finding.
  The echo amplifies knowledge. It does not replace it.

</details>


### [206] [Generalization Bounds of Emergent Communications for Agentic AI Networking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08613)
*Yong Xiao,Jingxuan Chai,Guangming Shi,Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an information-theoretic emergent communication framework for 6G agentic AI networks, jointly learning agents’ decisions and communication protocols under bandwidth/complexity constraints, and shows better generalization than prior work on hardware tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional, rigid, predefined communication protocols are ill-suited for 6G agentic AI networks where heterogeneous autonomous agents must coordinate under real networking constraints (bandwidth, computation). Existing emergent communication approaches ignore these constraints and lack a solid information-theoretic basis, providing no guarantees on efficiency or generalization. The paper is motivated by the need for a task-aware, AI-native communication paradigm that is theoretically grounded and practically viable in constrained physical networks.

Method: The authors design an emergent communication framework in which heterogeneous agents learn to solve tasks collaboratively while simultaneously learning their own signaling protocol. Central to the method is a new joint loss that optimizes both the agents’ decision-making policies and their communication messages. This loss is derived from multi-agent, multi-task distributed information bottleneck (DIB) theory, explicitly modeling the trade-off between preserving task-relevant information and limiting representation complexity (linked to bandwidth and computation). They also derive theoretical generalization bounds for decentralized inference when agents face unseen environment states. The approach is implemented and tested on a real-world hardware prototype.

Result: Theoretical results provide generalization bounds for the learned emergent communication protocol under decentralized inference, demonstrating its robustness to unseen environmental states. Empirically, experiments on a hardware prototype show that agents trained with the proposed DIB-based joint objective achieve significantly better generalization performance than state-of-the-art emergent communication or protocol-learning baselines, under realistic networking constraints.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that emergent communication grounded in distributed information bottleneck theory is a viable and effective foundation for 6G agentic AI-native networking. By jointly optimizing decision-making and communication under explicit information–complexity trade-offs, their framework yields emergent protocols that generalize better and respect physical networking constraints, representing a promising direction for future AgentNet systems.

Abstract: The evolution of 6G networking toward agentic AI networking (AgentNet) systems requires a shift from traditional data pipelines to task-aware, agentic AI-native communication solutions. Emergent communication, a novel communication paradigm in which autonomous agents learn their own signaling protocols through interaction, is increasingly viewed as a promising solution to address the challenges posed by existing rigid, predefined protocol-based networking architecture. However, most existing emergent communication frameworks fail to account for physical networking constraints, such as bandwidth and computational complexity, and often lack a rigorous information-theoretical foundation. To address these challenges, this paper introduces a novel emergent communication framework that facilitates collaborative task-solving among heterogeneous agents through an information-theoretic lens. We propose a novel joint loss function that unifies the optimization of decision-making functions and the learning of communication signaling. Our proposed solution is grounded on the multi-agent and multi-task distributed information bottleneck (DIB) theory, which allows the quantification of the fundamental trade-off between task-relevant information representation and computational complexity. We further provide theoretical generalization bounds of the emergent communication protocol during decentralized inference across unseen environmental states. Experimental validation on a real-world hardware prototype confirms that our proposed framework significantly improves generalization performance, compared to the state-of-the-art solutions.

</details>


### [207] [DiagnosticIQ: A Benchmark for LLM-Based Industrial Maintenance Action Recommendation from Symbolic Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08614)
*Devin Yasith De Silva,Dhaval Patel,Christodoulos Constantinides,Shuxin Lin,Nianjun Zhou,Paul J Adams,Sal Rosato,Nicolas Constantinides,Deborah L. McGuinness,Jayant Kalagnanam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark to test whether large language models can translate industrial monitoring rules into concrete maintenance actions and shows that current top models are capable but brittle and poorly calibrated when rules are perturbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In industrial settings, engineers write symbolic rules tied to sensor readings that signal when maintenance is needed, but the real bottleneck is converting these rules into correct, asset-specific maintenance actions. This translation requires deep, specialized expertise that technicians gain over years. The authors want to know if LLMs can reliably assist in this decision-support step, and they argue that existing benchmarks do not capture the nuances of mapping symbolic conditions to operational actions in complex industrial assets.

Method: They build \ours{}, a benchmark of 6,690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions derived from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 types of industrial assets. They design a symbolic-to-MCQA generation pipeline that first normalizes the original rules into Disjunctive Normal Form, then uses embedding-based methods to sample plausible distractor options. They create five benchmark variants—Pro, Pert, Verbose, Aug, and Rationale—each targeting different potential failure modes such as increased distractor complexity, structural perturbations, verbosity, and reasoning requirements. They evaluate 29 large language models and 4 embedding-based baselines on these variants, and additionally run a human study with 9 practitioners to calibrate task difficulty and required expertise.

Result: Human practitioners achieved a mean accuracy of 45%, indicating that the tasks demand specialized domain knowledge beyond general operational experience. Among LLMs, the top three models perform within one macro-accuracy point of each other, suggesting a saturated frontier, with Bradley-Terry Elo ranking claude-opus-4-6 roughly 30 Elo points above the next best model. However, all models show significant brittleness: in the Pro variant with expanded distractor sets, every model’s relative accuracy drops by 13–60%; in the Aug variant with inverted conditions, frontier models still incorrectly prefer the original answer 49–63% of the time, revealing strong pattern-matching biases.

Conclusion: LLMs are sufficiently capable to handle template-like fault-detection and rule interpretation in industrial monitoring, but their reliability is undermined by poor calibration and structural brittleness. The main obstacle to deployment is not raw ability but how models behave under expanded options and rule perturbations: they tend to overfit superficial patterns rather than robustly reasoning over the symbolic conditions. The \ours{} benchmark surfaces these weaknesses and can guide future work on making LLM-based decision support more robust and trustworthy for industrial maintenance workflows.

Abstract: Monitoring complex industrial assets relies on engineer-authored symbolic rules that trigger based on sensor conditions and prompt technicians to perform corrective actions. The bottleneck is not detection but response: translating rules into maintenance steps requires asset-specific knowledge gained through years of practice. We investigate whether LLMs can serve as decision support for this rule-to-action step and introduce \ours{}, a benchmark of 6{,}690 expert-validated multiple-choice questions from 118 rule-action pairs across 16 asset types. We contribute (i) a symbolic-to-MCQA pipeline normalizing rules to Disjunctive Normal Form with embedding-based distractor sampling, (ii) five variants probing distinct failure modes (Pro, Pert, Verbose, Aug, Rationale), and (iii) a benchmark of 29 LLMs and 4 embedding baselines. A human evaluation (9 practitioners, mean 45.0\%) confirms \ours{} requires specialist knowledge beyond operational experience. Three findings stand out. The frontier has closed: the top three LLMs lie within one Macro point, with Bradley-Terry Elo placing claude-opus-4-6 30 points above the next model. Yet \ours{}\,Pro exposes brittleness, with every model losing 13--60\% relative accuracy under distractor expansion. \ours{}\,Aug exposes pattern-matching: under condition inversion, frontier models still select the original answer 49--63\% of the time. The deployment bottleneck is not capability but calibration: frontier models handle template-style fault detection but break under structural perturbation.

</details>


### [208] [C2L-Net: A Data-Driven Model for State-of-Charge Estimation of Lithium-Ion Batteries During Discharge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08653)
*Khoa Tran,T. Nguyen-Thoi,Vin Nguyen-Thai,Duong Tran Anh,Hung-Cuong Trinh,Tri Le*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes C2L-Net, a data-driven framework for online lithium-ion battery SOC estimation that uses only a short historical window, achieves state-of-the-art accuracy, and is up to 60× faster with fewer parameters than recent baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data-driven SOC estimators either require long historical sequences, causing high computational cost and padding-induced bias, or have limited temporal modeling capacity when constrained to short receptive fields. There is a need for an approach that can perform accurate, realistic online SOC estimation with short history, high efficiency, and robust generalization to varying operating conditions.

Method: Introduce C2L-Net, a context-to-latest framework that explicitly separates contextual encoding from latest-measurement updating. It uses chunk-based feature extraction with Theta Attention Pooling and a Fourier-based Seasonality Basis to compress and capture local temporal patterns. A causal context encoder combines GRU and Causal Cosine Attention to learn temporal dependencies without future leakage. A latest-measurement decoder, inspired by recursive filtering, updates the latent contextual state using the newest measurement to rapidly adapt to dynamic battery states.

Result: On a public lithium-ion battery drive-cycle dataset at multiple fixed temperatures, C2L-Net attains state-of-the-art or competitive SOC estimation accuracy while being far more efficient. It achieves up to 60× faster inference and uses fewer parameters than recent data-driven baselines, while maintaining robust performance on unseen driving profiles.

Conclusion: Separating context encoding from latest-measurement updating in a data-driven architecture enables accurate, efficient, and responsive online SOC estimation from short historical windows. C2L-Net offers a practical, computationally efficient solution for real-world BMS applications, outperforming or matching existing methods in accuracy while substantially reducing inference time and model size and generalizing well across diverse driving conditions.

Abstract: Accurate state-of-charge (SOC) estimation is critical for the safe and efficient operation of lithium-ion batteries in battery management systems (BMS). Although data-driven approaches can effectively capture nonlinear battery dynamics, many existing methods rely on long historical input sequences, resulting in high computational cost and introducing padding-induced positional bias at the beginning of drive cycles. To address these limitations, we propose C2L-Net, a novel context-to-latest data-driven framework for realistic online SOC estimation using only a short historical window (20 s). Unlike existing short-receptive-field or long-history models, the proposed framework explicitly separates contextual encoding from latest-measurement updating, enabling both efficient temporal modeling and rapid adaptation to dynamic battery states. The proposed model incorporates a chunk-based feature extraction mechanism that combines Theta Attention Pooling with a Fourier-based Seasonality Basis to capture local temporal patterns while reducing sequence length. A causal context encoder, integrating a gated recurrent unit (GRU) with Causal Cosine Attention, models temporal dependencies without information leakage. Furthermore, a latest-measurement decoder, inspired by recursive filtering, updates the contextual state using the most recent measurement, enhancing responsiveness to dynamic operating conditions. Extensive experiments on a public lithium-ion battery drive-cycle dataset under multiple fixed-temperature conditions demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art or competitive accuracy while significantly improving computational efficiency. In particular, C2L-Net achieves up to 60 times faster inference and requires fewer parameters than recent data-driven baselines, while maintaining robust performance across unseen driving profiles.

</details>


### [209] [MIND-Skill: Quality-Guaranteed Skill Generation via Multi-Agent Induction and Deduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08670)
*Yixuan Li,Mingshu Cai,Ziyang Xiao,Wanyuan Wang,Yanchen Deng,Bo An*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MIND-Skill, a framework that automatically learns reusable procedural skills from successful LLM agent trajectories, and shows it improves performance on complex tasks compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents are promising for autonomous problem solving but struggle with complex, multi-step, real-world tasks that require domain-specific procedural knowledge. Reusable skills could help by letting agents reuse successful strategies, but current skill design is manual and expert-driven. The paper aims to automate the extraction of such skills from prior successful trajectories while ensuring their quality and generalizability.

Method: The authors propose MIND-Skill, a multi-agent framework with two LLM-based components: an induction agent that abstracts reusable skills from successful trajectories, and a deduction agent that tries to reconstruct the original trajectories by following the induced skills. To enforce skill quality, they define three textual loss terms: (1) reconstruction loss, comparing original and reconstructed trajectories; (2) outcome loss, ensuring the reconstructed trajectories still solve the tasks correctly; and (3) rubric loss, which scores documentation quality and enforces an appropriate abstraction level according to predefined criteria. These losses are jointly optimized via TextGrad. After training, the learned skills are tested on held-out tasks not seen during optimization.

Result: On the AppWorld and BFCL-v3 benchmarks, MIND-Skill achieves consistently better performance than concurrent automatic skill generation approaches, indicating that its induced skills are more effective and generalizable for guiding LLM agents on complex tasks.

Conclusion: MIND-Skill demonstrates that multi-agent induction–deduction with textual loss optimization can automatically derive high-quality, reusable skills from successful trajectories, reducing the need for manual expert curation and improving LLM agents’ ability to handle complex, multi-step tasks. The approach offers a principled way to both learn and validate procedural knowledge that transfers to new tasks.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) powered AI agents have emerged as a promising paradigm for autonomous problem-solving, yet they continue to struggle with complex, multi-step real-world tasks that demand domain-specific procedural knowledge. Reusable agent skills, which encapsulate successful problem-solving strategies, offer a natural remedy by enabling agents to build on prior experience. However, curating such skills has largely remained a manual endeavor, requiring human experts to distill rich domain knowledge into actionable guidelines. In this work, we present $\textbf{M}$ulti-agent $\textbf{IN}$duction and $\textbf{D}$eduction for $\textbf{Skill}$s ($\textbf{MIND-Skill}$), a framework that automatically induces generalizable skills from successful trajectories with robust quality guarantees. MIND-Skill consists of an induction agent which is tasked to abstract reusable skills from successful trajectories, and a deduction agent which aims to reconstruct trajectories by following the induced skills. To guarantee the quality of the generated skills, we introduce a reconstruction loss that compares input and reconstructed trajectories, an outcome loss that enforces the correctness of the reconstructed trajectories, and a rubric loss that assesses the documentation quality and regularizes the abstraction level of the generated skills according to predefined criteria. These textual losses are jointly optimized with TextGrad, and the resulting skills are evaluated on held-out tasks unseen during optimization. Experiments on AppWorld and BFCL-v3 show that MIND-Skill consistently outperforms concurrent skill generation methods.

</details>


### [210] [Iterative Critique-and-Routing Controller for Multi-Agent Systems with Heterogeneous LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08686)
*Wenzhi Fang,Liangqi Yuan,Guangchen Lan,Dong-Jun Han,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new controller for multi-agent LLM systems that can both critique intermediate drafts and choose which model to call next, modeled as a sequential decision problem, leading to better performance with fewer calls to the strongest (and likely most expensive) agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM controllers usually do one-shot routing: they pick a single model once, pass the query, and directly return its output. This lacks mechanisms to inspect or critique intermediate answers or to iteratively refine them, and it cannot manage trade-offs between accuracy and cost when agents have different strengths. The authors aim to design a controller that can actively manage multi-step collaboration between heterogeneous LLM agents, improving quality while respecting constraints on using expensive strong agents.

Method: They introduce a critique-and-routing controller that treats multi-agent coordination as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process. At each step, the controller: (1) critiques the current draft solution, (2) decides whether to terminate or continue, and (3) if continuing, chooses which agent to invoke next. They encode explicit constraints on how often each agent can be used, define a composite reward over multiple turns capturing solution quality, cost, and constraint satisfaction, and then train the controller with policy gradient methods under a Lagrangian-relaxed objective that enforces utilization constraints.

Result: Across several heterogeneous multi-agent LLM setups and seven reasoning benchmarks, their controller outperforms state-of-the-art baseline controllers. It achieves performance close to that of the strongest individual agent while calling that strongest agent in fewer than 25% of total model invocations, demonstrating improved efficiency and better quality–cost trade-offs.

Conclusion: Modeling multi-agent LLM coordination as a sequential decision process with a critique-and-routing controller enables iterative refinement and better use of heterogeneous agents. This approach both improves reasoning performance over existing one-shot routing controllers and significantly reduces reliance on the strongest (and presumably most expensive) agent, indicating that learned controllers can effectively orchestrate multi-step collaboration among LLMs under explicit resource constraints.

Abstract: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems often rely on a controller to coordinate a pool of heterogeneous models, yet existing controllers are typically limited to one-shot routing: they select a model once and return its output directly. Such routing-only designs provide no mechanism to critique intermediate drafts or support iterative refinement. To address this limitation, we propose a critique-and-routing controller that casts multi-agent coordination as a sequential decision problem. At each turn, the controller evaluates the current draft, decides whether to stop or continue, and, if needed, selects the next agent for further refinement. We formulate this process as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process (MDP) with explicit agent-utilization constraints, design a composite reward for controller decisions across turns, and optimize the controller via policy gradients under a Lagrangian-relaxed objective. Extensive experiments across multiple heterogeneous multi-agent systems and seven reasoning benchmarks show that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines and substantially narrows the gap to the strongest agent, while using it for fewer than 25% of total calls.

</details>


### [211] [Reconciling Consistency-Based Diagnosis with Actual-Causality-Based Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08688)
*Leopoldo Bertossi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper links Consistency-Based Diagnosis with Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility in the context of Explainable AI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Consistency-Based Diagnosis has been underused in Explainable AI; understanding its relationship with causal notions could enhance explainability methods and data management.

Method: The authors conceptually and/or formally analyze and establish theoretical connections between Consistency-Based Diagnosis, Actual Causality, and Causal Responsibility.

Result: They identify and formalize relationships between these frameworks, showing how ideas from CBD and causal reasoning can inform one another in XAI settings.

Conclusion: Bridging CBD with causal notions opens new avenues for more robust and principled XAI and explainable data management techniques.

Abstract: We establish, from the point of view of Explainable AI (XAI), connections between Consistency-Based Diagnosis (CBD), on one side, and Actual Causality and Causal Responsibility, on the other. CBD has received little attention from the XAI community. Connections between these two areas could have a fruitful impact on XAI and Explainable Data Management.

</details>


### [212] [SkillMaster: Toward Autonomous Skill Mastery in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08693)
*Min Yang,Jinghua Piao,Xu Xia,Xiaochong Lan,Jiaju Chen,Yongshun Gong,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillMaster is a training framework that lets LLM agents autonomously create, refine, and select reusable skills, turning skills from external tools into internal, self-improving capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent frameworks rely on externally provided or manually curated skills. These skills are not truly learned, adapted, or managed by the agents themselves; instead, teachers or auxiliary modules decide what skills exist and how they are used. This limits agents’ ability to improve over time, to fix failing behaviors, and to transfer procedural knowledge across tasks. The paper aims to make LLM agents capable of autonomously mastering their skills: learning when new skills are needed, how to refine them from experience, and how to deploy them effectively during complex, multi-step tasks.

Method: The authors propose SkillMaster, a reinforcement-style training framework with three main components: (1) Trajectory-informed skill review: after each task episode, the agent analyzes its trajectories to decide whether to create, update, or keep existing skills, turning trajectory evidence into proposed skill edits. (2) Counterfactual evaluation of skill edits: each candidate skill edit is assessed via its counterfactual utility on related probe tasks, yielding a direct learning signal for which edits actually help performance. (3) DualAdv-GRPO: a learning algorithm that separately estimates advantages for task-solving actions and for skill-editing decisions, stabilizing the joint optimization of solving tasks while managing and improving a bank of skills. This framework is applied to LLM agents operating in ALFWorld and WebShop environments.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks, SkillMaster improves success rates by 8.8% and 9.3% over state-of-the-art baselines, respectively. It achieves the best overall performance among all compared methods. Qualitative and quantitative analyses show that SkillMaster-trained agents can detect when existing skills fail, refine procedural knowledge based on trajectory evidence, and generalize these refined skills to new tasks while requiring only limited changes to the skill bank.

Conclusion: SkillMaster enables LLM agents to move from passively using externally defined skills to actively managing and improving their own skill repertoires. By combining trajectory-based skill review, counterfactual evaluation of skill edits, and a dual-advantage optimization scheme, the framework supports autonomous skill creation, refinement, and selection. This leads to better performance on complex tasks and demonstrates that LLM agents can internalize and continually improve reusable skills via experience, rather than relying solely on external supervision or hand-crafted skill libraries.

Abstract: Skills provide an effective mechanism for improving LLM agents on complex tasks, yet in existing agent frameworks, their creation, refinement, and selection are typically governed by external teachers, hand-designed rules, or auxiliary modules. As a result, skills remain external resources to be invoked, rather than capabilities that agents can develop, adapt, and internalize through experience. To endow LLM agents with autonomous skill mastery, we propose SkillMaster, a training framework that teaches agents to create new skills, refine existing skills, and select accumulated skills during task solving. This capability is achieved through three key designs. First, we train agents through trajectory-informed skill review, teaching agents to propose, update, or retain skills based on evidence from completed episodes. Second, each candidate skill edit is designed to be evaluated by its counterfactual utility on related probe tasks, providing a direct learning signal for training skill-editing decisions. Third, we introduce DualAdv-GRPO, which separately estimates advantages for task-solving actions and skill-editing decisions, stabilizing joint training across task solving and skill management. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that SkillMaster improves the overall success rate over state-of-the-art baselines by 8.8% and 9.3%, respectively, achieving the best performance among all compared methods. Further analysis reveals a marked shift in agent capability: agents trained with SkillMaster can identify skill failures, refine procedural knowledge from trajectory evidence, and transfer improvements to future tasks with limited skill-bank edits. Overall, SkillMaster moves LLM agents beyond mere skill use toward self-improving agents capable of developing, adapting, and applying their own skill repertoires.

</details>


### [213] [MBP-KT: Learning Global Collaborative Information from Meta-Behavioral Pattern for Enhanced Knowledge Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08697)
*Yuhao Jia,Duantengchuan Li,Jinsong Chen,Zhongjie Mao,Mingwen Tong,Yue Li,Xiaoguang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose MBP-KT, a general framework that converts raw learner interaction sequences into meta-behavioral patterns and uses them to extract global collaborative information that can be plugged into many KT models to boost performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing collaborative information-based KT methods rely directly on raw interaction sequences and model-specific modules, which limits their ability to capture deep behavioral patterns and to generalize across models. There is a need for a universal way to represent and exploit collaborative behavioral information so that it is reusable and preserves rich learning patterns.

Method: They design MBP-KT, which (1) constructs meta-behavioral sequences by transforming raw interaction logs into combinations of predefined meta-behavioral patterns, thereby preserving higher-level learning behaviors; (2) introduces a parameter-free module that aggregates these sequences across learners to obtain global collaborative representations; and (3) proposes general strategies to inject this collaborative representation into diverse downstream KT architectures without modifying them heavily.

Result: On multiple real-world KT datasets, integrating MBP-KT with a variety of baseline KT models consistently improves their prediction performance compared to the same models without MBP-KT and to prior collaborative KT methods.

Conclusion: Representing learner behavior at the meta-behavioral pattern level and extracting global collaborative information in a model-agnostic, parameter-free way provides a universal, plug-and-play enhancement for KT models, leading to better knowledge state prediction and demonstrating good generality across datasets and architectures.

Abstract: The emerging collaborative information-based knowledge tracing (KT) has been a promising way to enhance modeling of learners' knowledge states. The core idea is to extract the collaborative information from interaction sequences of other learners to assist the prediction on the target one. Despite effectiveness, existing methods are built on the raw interaction sequences with tailored modules, which inevitably limits their capacity in deeply capturing learning behavioral patterns and generalization. To this end, we propose a general meta-behavioral pattern-aware framework (MBP-KT) for KT. Specifically, MBP-KT introduces a novel meta-behavioral sequence construction to transform the raw interaction sequences into the combinations of different meta-behavioral patterns. In this way, the learning behavioral patterns of learners can be effectively preserved. Then, MBP-KT develops a parameter-free module to extract the global collaborative representations from the constructed meta-behavioral sequences. Moreover, MBP-KT provides general injection strategies to introduce the extracted global collaborative information into various downstream KT models, ensuring the universality of the collaborative information. Extensive results on real-world datasets demonstrate that MBP-KT can consistently boosts the performance of a wide range of KT models.

</details>


### [214] [RewardHarness: Self-Evolving Agentic Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08703)
*Yuxuan Zhang,Penghui Du,Bo Li,Cong Wei,Junwen Miao,Huaisong Zhang,Songcheng Cai,Yubo Wang,Dongfu Jiang,Yuyu Zhang,Ping Nie,Wenhu Chen,Changqian Yu,Kelsey R. Allen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RewardHarness, an agentic, self-evolving reward framework for evaluating instruction-guided image edits that learns human-like preference judgments from very few annotated examples by evolving a library of tools and reasoning skills rather than training a new reward model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward models for evaluating image edits require large-scale human preference annotations and extensive model training, while humans can infer evaluation criteria from only a few examples. This mismatch leads to poor data efficiency and makes reward modeling expensive and inflexible. The paper aims to close this data-efficiency gap by designing a reward system that can rapidly align with human preferences with minimal supervision and without costly retraining.

Method: RewardHarness reframes reward modeling as context evolution instead of weight optimization. It maintains a library of tools and skills and uses two main components: (1) an Orchestrator that, given a source image, edited candidates, and an instruction, selects a relevant subset of tools/skills and supervises the reasoning process, and (2) a frozen Sub-Agent that uses these tools and skills to build explicit reasoning chains and outputs preference judgments between candidate edits. After each prediction, the system compares its judgments with ground-truth human preferences; by analyzing successful and failed reasoning traces, the Orchestrator iteratively refines and expands the tool/skill library—effectively self-evolving its context—without additional human labels or gradient-based training.

Result: Using only about 100 preference demonstrations (0.05% of the EditReward dataset), RewardHarness attains 47.4% average accuracy on image-edit evaluation benchmarks, outperforming GPT-5 by 5.3 percentage points under the same conditions. When its judgments are used as a reward signal for GRPO-based reinforcement learning fine-tuning, the resulting RL-tuned models reach a score of 3.52 on ImgEdit-Bench, demonstrating that its learned reward signal is strong enough to guide downstream policy optimization.

Conclusion: RewardHarness demonstrates that an agentic, tool- and skill-centric approach to reward modeling can substantially reduce reliance on large-scale preference annotations and retraining, achieving competitive or superior performance using only a tiny fraction of data. By framing reward learning as iterative context (tool/skill) evolution rather than parameter optimization, the framework can self-improve its reasoning and alignment with human preferences in a label-efficient way and provide effective reward signals for RL-based image editing systems.

Abstract: Evaluating instruction-guided image edits requires rewards that reflect subtle human preferences, yet current reward models typically depend on large-scale preference annotation and additional model training. This creates a data-efficiency gap: humans can often infer the target evaluation criteria from only a few examples, while models are usually trained on hundreds of thousands of comparisons. We present RewardHarness, a self-evolving agentic reward framework that reframes reward modeling as context evolution rather than weight optimization. Instead of learning from large-scale annotations, RewardHarness aligns with human preferences by iteratively evolving a library of tools and skills from as few as 100 preference demonstrations. Given a source image, candidate edited images, and an editing instruction, an Orchestrator selects the most relevant subset of tools and skills from the maintained library, and a frozen Sub-Agent uses them to construct a reasoning chain that produces a preference judgment. By comparing predicted judgments with ground-truth preferences and analyzing successes and failures in the reasoning process, the Orchestrator automatically refines its library of tools and skills without additional human annotation. Using only 0.05% of the EditReward preference data, RewardHarness achieves 47.4% average accuracy on image-editing evaluation benchmarks, surpassing GPT-5 by 5.3 points. When used as a reward signal for GRPO fine-tuning, RL-tuned models achieve 3.52 on ImgEdit-Bench. Project page: https://rewardharness.com.

</details>


### [215] [AgentPSO: Evolving Agent Reasoning Skill via Multi-agent Particle Swarm Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08704)
*Hyunmin Hwang,Jaemin Kim,Choonghan Kim,Hangeol Chang,Jong Chul Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes AgentPSO, a particle-swarm-inspired framework that evolves natural-language reasoning skills of multiple LLM agents, improving individual and collective reasoning without changing model parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM reasoning often uses debate or aggregation only at inference time, which can suffer from incorrect peer influence and biased consensus, and the agents’ reasoning skills remain static across tasks. There is a need for a method that can systematically improve and reuse agents’ reasoning behaviors over time without retraining the base model.

Method: AgentPSO models each agent as a PSO-like particle whose state is a natural-language “skill” (prompt/instruction) and whose velocity is a semantic update direction in text space. Over training iterations, each agent updates its skill by combining four components: its previous velocity, its personal-best skill, the population’s global-best skill, and a self-reflective direction extracted from peer reasoning trajectories. This iterative process moves agents toward better reasoning skills while keeping backbone LLM parameters fixed.

Result: On mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, AgentPSO outperforms (1) static single-agent prompting and (2) test-time-only multi-agent reasoning methods that do not evolve skills. The learned skills generalize to new benchmarks and to a different backbone model, showing robustness and transferability.

Conclusion: AgentPSO successfully adapts particle swarm optimization ideas to multi-agent LLM reasoning by evolving natural-language skills instead of model parameters. It yields better and more transferable reasoning behaviors than static or purely inference-time multi-agent schemes, indicating that the framework captures reusable reasoning procedures rather than overfitting to specific benchmarks.

Abstract: Multi-agent reasoning has shown promise for improving the problem-solving ability of large language models by allowing multiple agents to explore diverse reasoning paths. However, most existing multi-agent methods rely on inference-time debate or aggregation, which can be vulnerable to incorrect peer influence and biased consensus. Moreover, the agents themselves remain static, as their underlying reasoning skills do not evolve across tasks. In this paper, we introduce AgentPSO, a particle-swarm-inspired framework for evolving multi-agent reasoning skills. AgentPSO treats each agent as a particle-like reasoner whose state is a natural-language skill and whose velocity is a semantic update direction, iteratively moving agents toward stronger skill states to improve both individual and collective reasoning performance. Across training iterations, each agent updates its skill by combining its previous velocity, personal-best skill, global-best skill, and a self-reflective direction derived from peer reasoning trajectories. This enables agents to learn reusable reasoning behaviors from both their own experiences and the strongest skills discovered by the population, without updating the parameters of the backbone language model. Experiments on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks show that AgentPSO improves over static single-agent skills and test-time-only multi-agent reasoning baselines. The evolved skills further transfer across benchmarks and to another backbone model, suggesting that AgentPSO captures reusable reasoning procedures rather than merely optimizing benchmark-specific prompts. Code is open-sourced at https://github.com/HYUNMIN-HWANG/AgentPSO/.

</details>


### [216] [When Can Human-AI Teams Outperform Individuals? Tight Bounds with Impossibility Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08710)
*Dongxin Guo,Jikun Wu,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops a theory for when human-AI teams can outperform their best member using confidence-based aggregation rules, showing this depends on the correlation of their errors and metacognitive sensitivity differences, and validates the theory on vision benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirical studies show that human-AI teams often fail to outperform the better of the two, but there is no general theory explaining when such complementarity is possible or how large the gains can be. This paper aims to provide principled, quantitative conditions and bounds for complementarity to guide the design of effective human-AI teaming strategies.

Method: The authors integrate signal detection theory with information-theoretic analysis to derive tight theoretical bounds for a broad class of confidence-based aggregation rules. They analytically characterize the conditions on error correlations and metacognitive sensitivity under which teams can beat the best individual, derive minimax bounds on achievable gains, prove impossibility results beyond a correlation threshold, and extend the framework to multi-class settings. They then empirically validate the predictions against human and model confidence/accuracy data on datasets like ImageNet-16H and CIFAR-10H, including tests of robustness to non-Gaussian assumptions.

Result: (1) They prove a complementarity theorem: confidence-based human-AI teams outperform the best individual if and only if the error correlation between human and model, ρ_HM, falls below a critical threshold ρ*, which in a symmetric near-chance regime is approximately equal to a. (2) They show via minimax bounds that the performance gains from teaming scale on the order of Θ(√(Δd)), where Δd captures differences in metacognitive sensitivity between team members. (3) They establish an impossibility result: no confidence-based aggregation rule can achieve complementarity when ρ_HM is at or above the threshold ρ*. (4) They generalize their results to multi-class classification, deriving a class-dependent threshold ρ*_K ≈ ρ*/√(K-1). Empirically, their theoretical predictions align closely with observed team accuracies on ImageNet-16H (R = 0.94) and CIFAR-10H (R = 0.91), and the predicted scaling of the multi-class threshold is supported by human data (R = 0.93 for K = 16), with robustness to deviations from Gaussian assumptions.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that human-AI complementarity under confidence-based aggregation is fundamentally constrained by the correlation structure of errors and differences in metacognitive sensitivity, which explains why empirical gains from human-AI teaming are often modest or absent. Their theory yields explicit formulas and bounds that can be used to design and evaluate aggregation schemes and select partners (humans or models) with suitably decorrelated errors. However, the results apply only to non-interactive aggregation and do not cover richer forms of collaboration involving deliberation or generation of new answers.

Abstract: Human-AI teams fail to outperform their best member in 70% of studies, yet no theory specifies when complementarity is achievable. We derive tight bounds for the broad class of confidence-based aggregation rules by integrating signal detection theory with information-theoretic analysis, yielding four results: (1) a complementarity theorem (teams outperform individuals iff error correlation $ρ_{HM} < ρ^*$, with $ρ^* \approx a$ in the symmetric near-chance regime); (2) minimax bounds showing gains scale as $Θ(\sqrt{Δd})$ with metacognitive sensitivity difference; (3) an impossibility result proving no confidence-based aggregation rule achieves complementarity when $ρ_{HM} \geq ρ^*$; and (4) multi-class generalization $ρ^*_K \approx ρ^*/\sqrt{K-1}$. Predictions match observed team accuracy ($R = 0.94$ on ImageNet-16H, $R = 0.91$ on CIFAR-10H) and the multi-class threshold scaling holds on human data ($R = 0.93$, $K = 16$), with robustness under non-Gaussian distributions. The framework explains why complementarity is rare and provides actionable design formulas; results apply to aggregation, not to interactive deliberation that generates novel answers.

</details>


### [217] [Bias by Necessity: Impossibility Theorems for Sequential Processing with Convergent AI and Human Validation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08716)
*Jikun Wu,Dongxin Guo,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that certain cognitive biases are inevitable in autoregressive language models and humans due to constraints of sequential information processing, and supports this with theoretical proofs, LLM experiments, and human studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether well-known cognitive biases such as primacy effects, anchoring, and order-dependence are avoidable flaws or mathematically necessary outcomes of sequential processing architectures like autoregressive language models, and to connect these findings to human cognition.

Method: The authors prove three impossibility theorems showing that primacy, anchoring, and order dependence necessarily arise from causal masking and sequential conditioning in autoregressive models. They then empirically validate model-level predictions on 12 frontier LLMs using model comparisons (R^2 and BIC) and conduct two pre-registered human experiments examining how anchor position and working memory load/capacity affect these biases.

Result: The theoretical results show that primacy bias stems from asymmetric attention accumulation, anchoring from information bounds in sequential conditioning, and that exact debiasing via permutation marginalization is computationally intractable though approximable via Monte Carlo. Empirically, the theory fits LLM behavior well (R^2 = 0.89, strong BIC preference) and human experiments confirm that later anchors have weaker effects and that increased working memory load strengthens primacy bias while higher working memory capacity reduces it.

Conclusion: Cognitive biases like primacy, anchoring, and order effects are not arbitrary errors but predictable, resource-rational byproducts of systems constrained to process information sequentially—whether in LLMs or human cognition—implying fundamental limits on debiasing through architecture or computation alone.

Abstract: Are certain cognitive biases mathematically inevitable consequences of sequential information processing? We prove that primacy effects, anchoring, and order-dependence are architecturally necessary in autoregressive language models due to causal masking constraints. Our three impossibility theorems establish: (1) primacy bias arises from asymmetric attention accumulation; (2) anchoring emerges from sequential conditioning with provable information bounds; and (3) exact debiasing by permutation marginalization requires factorial-time computation, with Monte Carlo approximation feasible at constant per-tolerance overhead. We validate these bounds across 12 frontier LLMs ($R^2 = 0.89$; $Δ$BIC $= 16.6$ vs. next-best alternative). We then derive quantitative predictions from the framework and test them in two pre-registered human experiments ($N = 464$ analyzed). Study 1 confirms anchor position modulates anchoring magnitude ($d = 0.52$, BF$_{10} = 847$). Study 2 shows working memory load amplifies primacy bias ($d = 0.41$, BF$_{10} = 156$), with WM capacity predicting bias reduction ($r = -.38$). These convergent findings reframe cognitive biases as resource-rational responses to sequential processing.

</details>


### [218] [Done, But Not Sure: Disentangling World Completion from Self-Termination in Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08747)
*Ying Chen,Rui Jiang,Lihuang Fang,Mingxu Wang,Zhifeng Gu,Lei Yi,Jie Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces VIGIL, a framework to separately evaluate whether an embodied agent both completes a task and correctly decides to stop, a capacity called terminal commitment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied AI benchmarks conflate distinct failure modes at episode termination—never completing the task, completing it but failing to stop, or falsely reporting success—into a single failure signal. This prevents researchers from diagnosing whether models can correctly recognize and commit to task completion. The authors aim to design an evaluation protocol that isolates and measures this terminal commitment ability independently from raw task execution.

Method: They propose VIGIL, an evaluation framework in which agents operate with only egocentric RGB observations, receive no action-success feedback, and must terminate each episode by issuing a semantic success report that is deterministically checked against the hidden world state. The framework defines two metrics: world-state completion (W), measuring whether the goal state was actually achieved, and benchmark success (B), which additionally requires that the final semantic report is correct. This setup yields four distinct outcome categories—missed execution, post-attainment drift, unsupported commitment, and verified success. They also run experiments on 20 models across 1,000 fixed episodes, and conduct an intervention where action-feedback signals are added to examine their effects on execution vs. terminal commitment.

Result: The experiments show that models with similar world-state completion W can differ by up to 19.7 percentage points in benchmark success B, revealing large variation in terminal commitment despite comparable execution performance. In some cases, one model reliably converts achieved goal states into correct stopping reports, while another with near-identical execution behavior overshoots the goal and fails to terminate properly. The action-feedback intervention improves execution (W) broadly but does not eliminate commitment failures in models that do not inherently ground their terminal reports in the achieved state.

Conclusion: VIGIL successfully makes terminal commitment a separately visible and quantifiable capability in embodied agent evaluation. By decoupling world-state completion from correct episode closure and semantic reporting, the framework exposes qualitatively different failure modes at termination and shows that improving execution alone does not guarantee robust terminal commitment. This protocol can thus serve as a diagnostic tool and benchmark component for developing agents that not only achieve goals but also know when to stop and accurately report success.

Abstract: Standard embodied evaluations do not independently score whether an agent correctly commits to task completion at episode closure, a capacity we call terminal commitment. Behaviorally distinct failures--never completing the task, completing it but failing to stop, and reporting success without sufficient evidence--collapse into the same benchmark failure. We introduce VIGIL, an evaluation framework that makes terminal commitment independently measurable. Under VIGIL's default protocol, agents observe only egocentric RGB, receive no action-success signals, and must end each episode with a semantic report checked deterministically against hidden world state. This yields two separate scores: world-state completion (W) and benchmark success (B), where B additionally requires a correct terminal report. This decoupling makes four outcome categories distinguishable: missed execution, post-attainment drift, unsupported commitment, and verified success. Across 20 models on 1,000 frozen episodes, systems with comparable W differ by up to 19.7 pp in B: one model converts achieved states into correct reports, while another with near-identical execution drifts past the goal without closing. An action-feedback intervention further tests the separation: execution-oriented signals improve W broadly, yet commitment failures persist in models that do not already ground terminal reports in the achieved state. VIGIL provides a protocol that makes terminal commitment independently visible and scorable.

</details>


### [219] [Value-Decomposed Reinforcement Learning Framework for Taxiway Routing with Hierarchical Conflict-Aware Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08754)
*Shizhong Zhou,Haifeng Liu,Zheng Zhang,Shiyu Zhang,Bo Yang,Yi Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CaTR, a conflict-aware, real-time reinforcement learning framework for multi-aircraft taxiway routing that improves safety–efficiency trade-offs over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Taxiway routing and on-surface conflict avoidance at airports are tightly coupled, safety-critical problems. Existing planning/optimization methods are computationally expensive online, and prior reinforcement learning approaches have difficulty capturing downstream conflicts and balancing multiple, often sparse, safety and efficiency objectives. There is a need for a method that can operate in real time, reason about future conflicts, and explicitly trade off safety and efficiency.

Method: The paper proposes Conflict-aware Taxiway Routing (CaTR), a reinforcement learning framework for real-time routing of multiple aircraft on airport taxiways. CaTR models the airport surface as a grid-based environment and uses action masking to enforce feasibility and reduce the action space. It introduces a hierarchical foresight traffic representation that encodes both current and downstream conflict-related traffic conditions. To handle multiple sparse, safety-critical objectives, CaTR uses a value-decomposed reinforcement learning strategy that decomposes the global value function, enabling prioritized learning of safety-related outcomes while still considering efficiency.

Result: In experiments using a realistic simulation of Changsha Huanghua International Airport under varying traffic densities, CaTR is compared against representative planning, optimization, and RL baselines. The results demonstrate that CaTR achieves superior safety–efficiency trade-offs, i.e., fewer conflicts and good taxi efficiency, while also maintaining computational runtimes suitable for practical, real-time deployment.

Conclusion: CaTR effectively addresses the coupled problems of taxiway routing and conflict avoidance by combining conflict-aware state representations, constrained action selection, and value-decomposed reinforcement learning. The framework can operate in real time and outperforms existing planning, optimization, and RL approaches in terms of balancing safety and efficiency, indicating its potential for deployment in real-world airport surface operations.

Abstract: Taxiway routing and on-surface conflict avoidance are coupled safety-critical decision problems in airport surface operations. Existing planning and optimization methods are often limited by online computational cost, while reinforcement learning methods may struggle to represent downstream traffic conflicts and balance multiple objectives. This paper presents Conflict-aware Taxiway Routing (CaTR), a reinforcement learning framework for real-time multi-aircraft taxiway routing. CaTR constructs a grid-based airport surface environment with action masking, introduces a hierarchical foresight traffic representation to encode current and downstream conflict-related traffic conditions, and adopts a value-decomposed reinforcement learning strategy to prioritize sparse but safety-critical objectives. Experiments are conducted on a realistic environment based on Changsha Huanghua International Airport under multiple traffic density levels. Results show that CaTR achieves better safety--efficiency trade-offs than representative planning, optimization, and reinforcement learning baselines while maintaining practical runtime.

</details>


### [220] [AHD Agent: Agentic Reinforcement Learning for Automatic Heuristic Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08756)
*Haoze Lv,Ning Lu,Ziang Zhou,Shengcai Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build an LLM-based agent that actively decides when to design new heuristics and when to query tools/environment, and train it via reinforcement learning with synthetic environments to automatically create strong heuristics for combinatorial optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automatic heuristic design with LLMs treats the model as a passive text generator inside a fixed pipeline, using limited, mostly static context. This misses important state-dependent information like where heuristics fail, making exploration inefficient and requiring many trial-and-error evaluations. The authors want a more autonomous, interactive system where the LLM can strategically gather information from the solving environment and design better heuristics with fewer evaluations, and that can be trained efficiently on smaller models while still generalizing across domains.

Method: They propose AHD Agent, a tool-integrated multi-turn framework where the LLM acts as an agent: at each step it chooses either to (1) generate or refine heuristics or (2) call environment tools to obtain targeted feedback or evidence (e.g., performance stats, failure cases). To train this dynamic decision-maker, they build an agentic reinforcement learning system with an environment synthesis pipeline: they construct synthetic but representative optimization environments, then train a compact 4B-parameter model via RL to maximize heuristic performance and data efficiency across tasks. The agent learns policies over when to query tools, how to interpret feedback, and how to update heuristic designs over multiple turns.

Result: Across eight combinatorial optimization domains, including four held-out tasks not seen during training, the 4B-parameter AHD Agent matches or outperforms state-of-the-art automatic heuristic design baselines that rely on much larger LLMs. It also requires significantly fewer heuristic evaluations to reach comparable or better performance, evidencing greater data and sample efficiency. Scaling experiments varying model size and inference budget show that the agentic framework scales favorably and that improved interaction and decision-making yield substantial gains beyond raw model size increases.

Conclusion: AHD Agent demonstrates that turning LLMs into interactive, tool-using agents for automatic heuristic design yields better and more evaluation-efficient heuristics than passive text generation in fixed workflows, even with much smaller models. The agentic RL training with synthetic environments successfully imparts generalizable heuristic-design skills across diverse and unseen COP domains. This framework outlines a scalable path toward genuinely autonomous heuristic design systems that can flexibly explore, diagnose failures, and iteratively refine heuristics with minimal human intervention.

Abstract: Automatic heuristic design (AHD) has emerged as a promising paradigm for solving NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems (COPs). Recent works show that large language models (LLMs), when integrated into well-designed frameworks (i.e., LLM-AHD), can autonomously discover high-performing heuristics. However, existing LLM-AHD frameworks typically treat LLMs as passive generators within fixed workflows, where the model generates heuristics from manually designed, limited context. Such context may fail to capture state-dependent information (e.g., specific failure modes), leading to inefficient trial-and-error exploration. To overcome these limitations, we propose AHD Agent, a novel tool-integrated, multi-turn framework that empowers LLMs to proactively decide whether to generate heuristics or invoke tools to retrieve targeted evidence from the solving environment. To effectively train such a dynamic decision-making agent, we introduce an agentic reinforcement learning (RL) system, which leverages a novel environment synthesis pipeline to optimize a compact model's generalizable AHD capabilities. Experiments across eight diverse domains, including four held-out tasks, demonstrate that our 4B-parameter agent matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines using much larger models, while requiring significantly fewer evaluations. Model and inference scaling analysis further reveals that AHD Agent offers an effective trajectory toward truly autonomous heuristic design.

</details>


### [221] [From Holo Pockets to Electron Density: GPT-style Drug Design with Density](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08767)
*Jiahao Chen,Letian Gao,Yanhao Zhu,Wenbiao Zhou,Bing Su,Zhi John Lu,Bo Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes EDMolGPT, a generative model that designs drug-like molecules directly from low‑resolution electron density (ED) point clouds around protein binding sites, leveraging both calculated and experimental ED to better capture binding environments and flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structure-based drug design methods usually condition molecule generation on idealized, empty binding pockets from holo structures, ignoring the physical signal from ligands, solvent, and conformational variability. The authors aim to ground molecular generation in experimentally or computationally derived electron density that more faithfully represents the actual binding environment, including flexibility, and to unify the use of calculated and cryo-EM/X-ray data for training and application.

Method: They introduce EDMolGPT, a decoder-only autoregressive generative framework that takes low-resolution ED point clouds as input conditions and generates molecules token by token while simultaneously producing associated 3D conformations. The model is trained on both calculated ED (simulated from known structures) and experimental ED (from cryo-EM/X-ray), allowing unified pre-training and downstream integration with experimental data. The ED representation captures the filler (ligands, solvent) and pocket flexibility, serving as a physically grounded conditioning signal for generation.

Result: On a benchmark of 101 biological targets, EDMolGPT is shown to be effective at structure-based de novo drug design, producing molecules consistent with the ED-derived binding environments and exhibiting 3D conformations, while mitigating structural bias inherent in rigid-pocket-based approaches. Specific metrics are not given in the abstract, but the evaluations demonstrate improved or competitive performance across these targets.

Conclusion: Grounding molecular generation in low-resolution electron density around binding sites is a powerful alternative to rigid pocket conditioning. EDMolGPT leverages both calculated and experimental ED to model realistic binding environments and conformational flexibility, leading to more physically consistent, 3D-aware de novo drug designs across many targets.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative modeling have enabled significant progress in structure-based drug design (SBDD). Existing methods typically condition molecule generation on empty binding pockets from holo complexes, overlooking informative components such as the filler (ligands and solvent). Here, we leverage low-resolution electron density (ED) derived from the filler as a physically grounded condition for \textit{de novo} drug design. We consider two types of ED, calculated and cryo-EM/X-ray, obtainable from computational or experimental sources, supporting unified pre-training and experimental integration. Compared with rigid pocket representations, experimental ED naturally captures conformational flexibility and provides a more faithful description of the binding environment. Based on this, we introduce EDMolGPT, a decoder-only autoregressive framework that generates molecules from low-resolution ED point clouds. By grounding generation in physically meaningful density signals, EDMolGPT mitigates structural bias and produces molecules with 3D conformations. Evaluations on 101 biological targets verify the effectiveness. Our project page: https://jiahaochen1.github.io/EDMolGPT_Page/.

</details>


### [222] [EvoMAS: Learning Execution-Time Workflows for Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08769)
*Chengdong Xu,Kaiqiang Ke,Ziheng Liu,Jiaqi Wei,Zibo Shao,Weile Guo,Chao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EvoMAS is a framework that dynamically constructs and adapts multi-agent LLM workflows during execution, improving performance on long, complex tasks compared to static, predesigned workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems usually rely on a static, one-shot workflow design that is fixed before task execution. This is ineffective for long-horizon, evolving tasks where subgoals, available evidence, and information needs change over time. There is a need for a principled way to adapt the composition and coordination of agents online, based on the evolving task state, using reliable learning signals rather than brittle heuristics.

Method: EvoMAS treats multi-agent workflow construction as a meta-level sequential decision-making problem along a single task trajectory. For each execution stage, it (1) builds an explicit representation of the current task state via a Planner–Evaluator–Updater pipeline; (2) uses a learned Workflow Adapter to select and arrange a stage-specific layered workflow from a fixed pool of specialized candidate agents. The Workflow Adapter is trained with policy gradient RL using sparse, verifiable terminal task success as the primary reward signal. The authors also investigate adding evaluator-based process rewards under challenging sparse-reward conditions, analyzing how these intermediate signals affect learning and performance.

Result: On benchmarks including GAIA, HLE, and DeepResearcher, EvoMAS achieves higher performance than strong single-agent baselines and recent automated multi-agent workflow design methods. Ablations and analyses show that (a) explicit task-state construction and (b) learned workflow adaptation each bring gains, and together they are complementary. Additional experiments reveal that process rewards help most when terminal success signals are extremely sparse. Qualitative case studies further demonstrate that EvoMAS adapts its agent coordination patterns in response to changes in the task state over time.

Conclusion: Dynamic, execution-time construction and adaptation of multi-agent LLM workflows is more effective for complex, long-horizon tasks than static, precomputed workflows. EvoMAS offers a practical framework for treating workflow design as a sequential decision problem, leveraging explicit task states and RL-trained workflow adaptation. Explicit state modeling and adaptive coordination jointly improve performance, and carefully designed process rewards can further assist learning when terminal feedback is very sparse.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems have shown strong potential on complex tasks through agent specialization, tool use, and collaborative reasoning. However, most automated multi-agent system design methods still follow a one-shot paradigm: a workflow is optimized or selected before execution and then reused unchanged throughout the task. This static coordination strategy is ill-suited for long-horizon tasks whose subgoals, intermediate evidence, and information needs evolve over multiple execution stages. We propose EvoMAS, a framework for execution-time multi-agent workflow construction. EvoMAS formulates workflow construction as a meta-level sequential decision problem along a single task trajectory. At each stage, it constructs an explicit task state through a Planner-Evaluator-Updater pipeline and uses a learned Workflow Adapter to instantiate a stage-specific layered workflow from a fixed pool of candidate agents. The adapter is trained with policy gradients using sparse, verifiable terminal task success as the main supervision signal, while evaluator-based process reward is analyzed separately under very-hard sparse-reward settings.
