Experiments on GAIA, HLE, and DeepResearcher show that EvoMAS outperforms single-agent baselines and recent automated multi-agent workflow design methods. Our analyses further show that explicit task-state construction and learned workflow adaptation provide complementary benefits. Additional results indicate that process reward is most useful when terminal success is extremely sparse, and qualitative case studies illustrate that EvoMAS adapts agent coordination as the task state evolves.

</details>


### [223] [Reasoning Compression with Mixed-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08776)
*Han Yang,Mingyan Wu,Bailan He,Zeyu Cao,Sikuan Yan,Kevin Qinghong Lin,Zifeng Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Mixed-Policy Distillation (MPD), a framework that uses a large teacher model to rewrite small-model reasoning traces into concise trajectories and trains the small model on these compressed traces, reducing tokens and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning-focused LLMs are powerful but expensive because they generate long chain-of-thought traces. Smaller models, which are preferred in real-world deployments due to memory, latency, and cost constraints, tend to produce even longer and more redundant reasoning than larger models, worsening efficiency. The authors observe that larger reasoning models naturally generate more concise reasoning, suggesting an opportunity to transfer this compression ability to smaller models instead of relying on crude length constraints that can harm performance.

Method: They propose Mixed-Policy Distillation (MPD), a distillation framework where: (1) the student model first samples its own reasoning trajectory for a problem; (2) a larger teacher model then rewrites this student trajectory into a more concise but still valid reasoning trace; (3) the student is trained via KL-divergence-based alignment on these teacher-compressed trajectories. This mixes on-policy (student-generated) and off-policy (teacher-guided) learning: the student keeps its own exploration distribution while the teacher imposes a bias toward concise reasoning, avoiding both the verbosity of pure on-policy distillation and the distribution mismatch of pure off-policy distillation.

Result: Using Qwen3-1.7B as the student model, MPD achieves up to 27.1% reduction in token usage compared with baselines, while simultaneously improving performance on multiple reasoning benchmarks. This shows that reasoning can be compressed without sacrificing, and even while enhancing, accuracy for small models.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that concise reasoning behavior can be effectively transferred from larger to smaller LLMs by having a teacher rewrite student trajectories and training the student on these compressed traces. Mixed-Policy Distillation offers a practical approach for building efficient small reasoning models that use fewer tokens and achieve better performance, addressing the deployment constraints of real-world applications.

Abstract: Reasoning-centric large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance by generating intermediate reasoning trajectories, but often incur excessive token usage and high inference-time decoding cost. We observe that, when solving the same problems, larger reasoning models can often produce more concise traces, whereas smaller reasoning models tend to generate longer and more redundant trajectories. This is especially problematic in real-world deployment, where memory, latency, and serving-cost constraints often favor smaller models. Our observations suggest that reasoning compression can be transferred from large models to small ones rather than enforced through explicit length constraints. Based on this insight, we propose Mixed-Policy Distillation (MPD), a reasoning compression framework that transfers concise reasoning behavior from a larger-sized teacher to a smaller student by distilling teacher-compressed student trajectories. Unlike on-policy distillation, which aligns the student with teacher distributions over verbose student trajectories, or off-policy distillation, which relies on teacher-generated trajectories and may suffer from distribution mismatch, MPD combines the strengths of both. Given a student-sampled trajectory, the teacher rewrites it into a more concise reasoning trace, and the student is trained via KL-based alignment on the compressed trajectory. This preserves student-policy exploration while injecting teacher-guided compression. Experiments on Qwen3-1.7B show that MPD reduces token usage by up to 27.1% while improving performance across multiple reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating an effective approach to efficient small-model reasoning.

</details>


### [224] [Not All Turns Matter: Credit Assignment for Multi-Turn Jailbreaking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08778)
*Zhida He,Xiaoyu Wen,Han Qi,Ziyuan Zhou,Peng Yu,Xingcheng Xu,Dongrui Liu,Xia Hu,Chaochao Lu,Qiaosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TRACE is a reinforcement learning framework that assigns fine-grained, turn-level credit in multi-turn jailbreak attacks on LLMs, improving both attack success and defensive alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-turn jailbreak attacks train policies using only coarse, trajectory-level success/failure signals. This ignores that different dialogue turns contribute unequally to bypassing safety, causing a credit assignment problem that misguides learning and limits attack performance and analysis.

Method: TRACE introduces turn-aware credit assignment for RL-based multi-turn jailbreak. For successful attack dialogues, it estimates each turn’s contribution via leave-one-turn-out semantic masking—removing a turn and measuring its effect on success. For failed dialogues, it assigns negative credit based on the harmfulness and semantic relevance of each prompt, plus an extra penalty when the model explicitly refuses. The resulting per-turn reward signals are used to train attack policies and are then reused (with role reversal) to train defensive alignment policies.

Result: On both open-source and closed-source LLM targets, TRACE significantly improves multi-turn jailbreak performance compared with existing RL baselines, achieving roughly a 25% relative increase in attack success rate while maintaining or improving efficiency and transferability across models.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, turn-level credit assignment better captures how different dialogue phases and turns contribute to successful or failed jailbreak attempts. TRACE leverages this to enhance RL-based multi-turn attacks and, by reusing the same credit signal from the defender’s perspective, helps improve the safety-utility balance of defense alignment as well.

Abstract: Deploying LLMs in multi-turn dialogues facilitates jailbreak attacks that distribute harmful intent across seemingly benign turns. Recent training-based multi-turn jailbreak methods learn long-horizon attack strategies from interaction feedback, but often rely on coarse trajectory-level outcome signals that broadcast uniformly to every turn. However, we find that turn-level contributions in multi-turn jailbreaking are non-uniform, phase-dependent, and target-specific. Such coarse outcome supervision induces a credit assignment problem, leading to over-rewarding redundant turns in successful trajectories and under-crediting useful intermediate turns in failed ones. To address this, we propose TRACE, a turn-aware credit assignment framework for reinforcement learning (RL)-based multi-turn jailbreaking. For successful trajectories, TRACE estimates turn-level contributions via leave-one-turn-out semantic masking; for failed ones, TRACE assigns penalties based on prompt harmfulness and semantic relevance, with an additional local refusal-aware penalty. Furthermore, we reuse the attack-side credit signal for multi-turn defense alignment. Extensive experiments on open-source and closed-source targets show that TRACE achieves strong overall performance in effectiveness, transferability, and efficiency, yielding about a 25% relative improvement in attack success rate over the strongest RL baseline while also improving the safety-utility balance when reused for defense alignment.

</details>


### [225] [Mirror, Mirror on the Wall: Can VLM Agents Tell Who They Are at All?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08816)
*Filippo Ziliotto,Ciro Beneduce,Bruno Lepri,Luciano Serafini,Massimiliano Luca,Tommaso Campari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether embodied vision-language model agents can recognize themselves in a mirror, introducing a 3D benchmark to test mirror-based self-identification and showing that this capability mainly emerges in stronger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mirror self-recognition is a classic test of higher-order cognition in animals, but it is unclear whether analogous self-recognition capabilities can emerge in embodied AI agents, particularly vision-language models. Existing evaluations of VLMs often conflate genuine self-grounding with shortcuts such as priors, prompt following, or linguistic confabulation. There is a need for a controlled, perception-action-grounded benchmark that can diagnose whether any apparent self-referential abilities in these models are actually rooted in their sensorimotor experience rather than in superficial cues.

Method: The authors build a controlled 3D environment where a first-person embodied VLM agent observes a mirror and must infer a hidden attribute of its own body (e.g., a visual property that is only visible via reflection), then select the correct matching target while avoiding confusing its own reflection with other agents or objects. They design tasks that include mirror removal, misleading visual cues, and occluded reflections to rule out shortcuts that do not rely on actual mirror-grounded self-identification. They further analyze the agent’s decision process by measuring behaviors such as actively seeking the mirror, respecting temporal ordering of observations and actions, explicitly attributing observations to self vs. other, and maintaining consistency between linguistic reasoning and chosen actions. Multiple VLMs of varying strengths are embedded as controllers and evaluated on this benchmark.

Result: Stronger vision-language models demonstrate emerging mirror-based self-identification: they can reliably use reflected information about their own body to guide their actions and choose the correct targets. In contrast, weaker models frequently look at the mirror but either fail to extract the self-relevant attribute or misinterpret their reflection as belonging to another entity, leading to self-other misattribution errors. Ablation conditions such as mirror removal and misleading or occluded reflections reduce performance in ways that indicate that success in the stronger models depends on actual use of mirror cues rather than spurious correlations. Additionally, experiments that introduce conflicts between visual evidence and self-referential language show that accurate self-identification requires grounded perception-action reasoning and cannot be inferred from self-referential language alone.

Conclusion: The study concludes that a form of mirror-based self-recognition can emerge in sufficiently capable embodied vision-language models, but that this capacity is fragile and strongly dependent on the model’s visual reasoning abilities. The proposed benchmark serves as a diagnostic tool to distinguish genuine, perception- and action-grounded self-identification from behaviors driven by linguistic priors, prompt compliance, or confabulation. Mirror-based evaluation thus offers a principled way to probe whether an embodied AI system’s apparent self-referential behavior is causally rooted in its sensorimotor experience rather than in superficial or purely linguistic patterns.

Abstract: In the animal kingdom, mirror self-recognition is a canonical probe of higher-order cognition, emerging only in some species. We ask whether an analogous functional capability emerges in embodied vision-language model (VLM) agents: can they recognize themselves in a mirror? We introduce a controlled 3D benchmark where a first-person VLM agent must infer a hidden body attribute from its reflection and select the matching target, while avoiding self-other misattribution. To separate mirror-grounded self-identification from shortcuts, we test mirror removal, misleading cues, and occluded reflections. We also evaluate the decision process through mirror seeking, temporal ordering, self-attribution, and reasoning-action consistency. Our experiments show that mirror-based self-identification emerges mainly in stronger VLMs. These models can use reflected evidence for action, whereas weaker models often inspect the mirror but fail to extract self-relevant information or misattribute their reflection. Language-vision conflict further shows that self-referential language alone is not evidence of grounded self-identification. Overall, mirror-based evaluation provides a diagnostic for whether embodied self-grounding is causally rooted in perception and action rather than priors, prompt compliance, or confabulation.

</details>


### [226] [How You Begin is How You Reason: Driving Exploration in RLVR via Prefix-Tuned Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08817)
*Yifan Xu,Junren Chen,Yifan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes IMAX, a framework that improves exploration in reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for LLM reasoning by learning diverse, informative soft prefixes, leading to better reasoning performance than standard RLVR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with verifiable rewards is effective for LLM reasoning but suffers from reward sparsity and long reasoning horizons, which makes exploration difficult. This results in entropy collapse: the model increases accuracy for a few trajectories but fails to explore and cover diverse successful reasoning paths. Existing passive methods like entropy regularization hurt generation quality and create noisy rollouts. The authors seek a way to encourage structured, useful exploration without degrading reasoning quality.

Method: Introduce IMAX (Information-Maximizing Augmented eXploration), which trains a pool of soft prefixes that, when prepended to prompts, reshape the base model’s prior over reasoning trajectories. Each prefix serves as a control knob to induce a distinct rollout distribution from the same backbone LLM. The framework defines an Information Maximization (InfoMax) reward that complements the usual verifiable reward, encouraging prefixes to generate diverse yet task-relevant reasoning behaviors. IMAX is designed to be algorithm-agnostic and to plug into existing RLVR pipelines, where RL optimizes both verifiable and InfoMax rewards over the prefix pool.

Result: Empirically, across three different LLM backbone scales, IMAX consistently outperforms standard RLVR baselines on reasoning benchmarks. The reported gains reach up to 11.60% improvement in Pass@4 and 10.57% in Avg@4, indicating better success rates and average performance over multiple rollouts per problem.

Conclusion: By learning a pool of information-maximizing soft prefixes and augmenting verifiable rewards with an InfoMax signal, IMAX alleviates entropy collapse and improves exploratory coverage of successful reasoning trajectories in RLVR. The framework integrates easily with existing RLVR methods and yields substantial performance gains in LLM reasoning tasks, suggesting that structured, prefix-based exploration is a promising direction for enhancing RL-driven reasoning in language models.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) recently thrives in large language model (LLM) reasoning tasks. However, the reward sparsity and the long reasoning horizon make effective exploration challenging. In practice, this challenge manifests as the \emph{entropy collapse} phenomenon, where RLVR improves single-rollout accuracy but fails to expand coverage on successful reasoning trajectories. Passive exploration techniques like entropy regularization tend to dismiss generation quality, resulting in noisy rollouts. In response to this issue, we propose an Information-Maximizing Augmented eXploration (IMAX) framework to train a pool of soft prefixes that reshapes the base model's prior over reasoning trajectories. Rather than relying on RL to incentivize exploration on top of the base model, each prefix acts as a trainable control knob that induces a distinct rollout distribution from the same backbone model. To encourage discovery of diverse and task-relevant reasoning behaviors, we derive an Information Maximization (InfoMax) reward to complement the verifiable rewards for RL training. IMAX is in general algorithm-agnostic and can be seamlessly integrated into existing RLVR pipelines. Experiment results have shown that across three backbone scales, IMAX consistently improves reasoning performance over standard RLVR, with gains up to 11.60\% in Pass@4 and 10.57\% in Avg@4.

</details>


### [227] [Mental Health AI Safety Claims Must Preserve Temporal Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08827)
*Srimonti Dutta,Ratna Kandala*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that current safety evaluations for mental health AI overlook temporal dynamics of conversations, introduces a formal notion (Temporal Safety Non-Identifiability) explaining why time-dependent safety cannot be inferred from time-agnostic tests, and proposes a reporting standard (SCOPE/SCOPE-MH) that preserves temporal evidence and reveals failures missed by per-turn scoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mental health AI systems are often evaluated using static, per-turn, or end-point metrics that ignore how risks can emerge gradually over sequences of interactions. Clinically important failures—such as delayed escalation of risk, reinforcement of harmful patterns, user dependency, or breakdowns in repair—are inherently temporal, yet evaluation protocols usually discard order, timing, and accumulation. This leads not just to incomplete but potentially misleading safety judgments. The authors are motivated to formally characterize this mismatch and provide a framework so that safety claims made about mental health AI are actually supported by the type of evidence collected in evaluations.

Method: The authors first provide a formal analysis defining Temporal Safety Non-Identifiability: conditions under which safety properties that depend on sequence, timing, accumulation, or recovery cannot be reliably inferred from evaluations that ignore those temporal aspects. On top of this, they propose SCOPE (Safety Claims Over Preserved Evidence) as a general principle and reporting standard that ties any safety claim to the evidence whose temporal structure is preserved by the evaluation. They then specialize this to mental health AI as SCOPE-MH, which specifies what temporal information should be retained and reported for this domain. To demonstrate feasibility, they apply SCOPE-MH in a proof-of-concept study using the AnnoMI dataset of expert-annotated motivational interviewing conversations, comparing temporally-aware analysis against standard per-turn behavior scores.

Result: Applying SCOPE-MH to the AnnoMI dataset uncovers failure mechanisms in mental health AI interactions that are not captured by conventional per-turn scoring methods. Specifically, the temporally-preserved evaluation reveals patterns such as delayed escalation, repeated problematic reinforcement, and deteriorating interaction quality across turns—issues that would be invisible or misrepresented when conversations are reduced to independent turns or aggregate scores. This empirical demonstration supports their theoretical claim that time-agnostic evaluations cannot certify temporally defined safety properties.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that safety properties for mental health AI that depend on temporal dynamics cannot be validly certified using evaluations that discard sequence, timing, or accumulation, due to Temporal Safety Non-Identifiability. Therefore, preserving temporal evidence in evaluation is a necessary requirement—not an optional enhancement—for safety-critical deployment in mental health contexts. SCOPE and its instantiation SCOPE-MH provide a principled way to align safety claims with the evidence actually retained by an evaluation, and should be used as a diagnostic complement to existing infrastructure to reveal clinically meaningful failure modes otherwise missed.

Abstract: The safety of mental health AI is often judged at the wrong temporal scale. Current evaluations typically score isolated responses, endpoint outcomes, or aggregate dialogue quality, while clinically consequential failures may arise from the order and accumulation of interactions themselves, including delayed escalation, repeated reinforcement, dependency formation, failed repair, and gradual deterioration across turns. This paper argues that this mismatch is not merely a limitation of evaluation coverage but a source of invalid safety conclusions. We introduce Temporal Safety Non-Identifiability, a formal account of why safety properties that depend on sequence, timing, accumulation, or recovery cannot be certified by protocols that discard those features. From this formalization, we develop SCOPE (Safety Claims Over Preserved Evidence) as a general principle for aligning safety claims with the evidence an evaluation actually retains, and instantiate it as SCOPE-MH, a mental-health instantiation of this reporting standard. We operationalize SCOPE-MH through a proof-of-concept on the AnnoMI dataset of expert-annotated motivational interviewing conversations, which reveals mechanisms of failure that per-turn behavior scoring does not represent. We propose SCOPE-MH as a diagnostic complement to existing evaluation infrastructure and argue that evaluation preserving temporal evidence is necessary, not optional, for safety-critical mental health AI deployment.

</details>


### [228] [When Agents Overtrust Environmental Evidence: An Extensible Agentic Framework for Benchmarking Evidence-Grounding Defects in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08828)
*Strick Sheng,Ziyue Wang,Liyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces EnvTrustBench, a benchmark framework to evaluate whether LLM-based agents remain correctly grounded in the true environment state when their external observations (files, web, tools, logs) are stale, incorrect, or adversarial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents increasingly rely on complex scaffolds that expose external environment information (files, web pages, APIs, logs). These signals drive tool use and decisions, but their trustworthiness is often unclear. Existing benchmarks mostly test raw capabilities or narrow attack types (prompt injection, memory poisoning) and do not directly answer whether agents stay faithful to the real environment state when external observations are unreliable. The authors aim to systematically study and quantify this core reliability and security problem.

Method: They conceptualize a new failure mode called evidence-grounding defects (EGDs), where an agent accepts an environment-facing claim as sufficient evidence for action without reconciling it with fresher or conflicting evidence, thereby taking an incorrect path relative to the true environment state. The authors build EnvTrustBench, a framework that, for each task scenario, automatically generates: (1) a workspace and environment, (2) an objective presented to the agent, and (3) a validation oracle. The framework then runs the agent, logs its action–observation trajectory and final state, and uses the oracle to judge whether an EGD occurred. They instantiate this across multiple scenarios, each expanded via feedback-guided generation over several iterations, and test combinations of 6 LLM backbones and 5 common scaffolds.

Result: Across 55 generated cases spanning 11 task scenarios and multiple feedback-guided iterations, evidence-grounding defects appear consistently for all tested configurations (6 LLMs × 5 scaffolds). This shows that agents frequently act on stale, incorrect, or malicious environmental information without properly cross-checking against available current evidence. The failures occur across diverse workflows, indicating the issue is systemic rather than confined to particular models or tool stacks.

Conclusion: EnvTrustBench exposes evidence-grounding defects as a pervasive and under-examined reliability failure in LLM agents interacting with complex environments. The benchmark demonstrates that current agents and scaffolds commonly lose grounding in the true environment state when confronted with unreliable observations, which has serious security and robustness implications. The authors argue that environmental grounding should be treated as a first-class systems-level design and evaluation problem, involving better policies for context admission, provenance, freshness checks, verification, and action gating, rather than being addressed solely at the model or prompt level.

Abstract: Large language model agents increasingly operate through environment-facing scaffolds that expose files, web pages, APIs, and logs. These observations influence tool use, state tracking, and action sequencing, yet their reliability and authority are often uncertain. Environmental grounding is therefore a systems-level problem involving context admission, evidence provenance, freshness checking, verification policy, action gating, and model reasoning. Existing agent benchmarks mainly evaluate task capability or specific attacks such as prompt injection and memory poisoning, but they under-specify a fundamental reliability question: whether agents remain grounded in the true environment state when observations are stale, incorrect, or malicious.
  We introduce EnvTrustBench, an agentic framework for benchmarking this failure mode. We define an evidence-grounding defect (EGD) as a behavioral failure in which an agent treats an environment-facing claim as sufficient evidence for action without resolving it against available current evidence, leading to a task-incorrect false path under the true environment state. Given a task scenario, EnvTrustBench generates the workspace, environment, agent-facing objective, and validation oracle, executes the evaluated agent, records its action-observation trajectory and final state, and applies the oracle to produce a verdict.
  Using 6 LLM backbones and 5 widely used scaffolds, we evaluate 55 generated cases across 11 task scenarios, with each scenario expanded through five feedback-guided generation iterations. Results show that EGDs consistently emerge across operational workflows, highlighting environmental grounding as a core agent reliability problem with important security implications.

</details>


### [229] [SynerDiff: Synergetic Continuous Batching for Fast and Parallel Diffusion Model Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08835)
*Ziqi Zhou,Peng Yang,Yuxin Liang,Mingliu Liu,Jia Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SynerDiff is a continuous batching system for diffusion models that coordinates UNet and VAE execution to boost throughput and reduce end-to-end latency under multi-task concurrency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Serving diffusion models for AI-generated content at scale needs both high throughput and low end-to-end latency, but existing continuous batching approaches cause severe resource contention when UNet and VAE run concurrently, producing latency spikes and forcing a trade-off between UNet throughput and VAE latency across scheduling strategies.

Method: SynerDiff introduces a two-level synergy design. At the intra-concurrency level, it reduces resource contention by targeting component-specific bottlenecks using VAE Chunking and Adaptive Skip-CFG, which adjust how VAE work and classifier-free guidance operations are executed. At the inter-concurrency level, it uses a threshold-aware scheduler that exploits the different sensitivity of UNet and VAE to scheduling granularities: it plans concurrent sequences and tunes intra-level decisions to minimize VAE latency while keeping UNet throughput above a target threshold. A feedback controller dynamically updates this throughput threshold based on queue load to enhance overall capacity.

Result: Experiments show that SynerDiff achieves a 1.6× throughput improvement and reduces both average end-to-end latency and P99 tail latency by up to 78.7% compared with baseline systems, all while maintaining high image fidelity.

Conclusion: SynerDiff effectively coordinates UNet and VAE execution in diffusion model serving to mitigate resource contention and scheduling trade-offs, simultaneously improving throughput, average and tail latencies, and sustaining output quality, thereby raising the capacity ceiling of AI content generation services.

Abstract: The expansion of Artificial Intelligence-generated content service requires diffusion model serving to simultaneously achieve high throughput and low task end-to-end (E2E) latency. However, existing continuous batching methods suffer from severe resource contention during UNet-VAE concurrency, leading to latency spikes. Furthermore, concurrent multi-task scheduling entails a trade-off between UNet throughput and VAE latency across varying scheduling strategies. To address these, we propose SynerDiff, an efficient continuous batching system built on intra-inter level synergy. At the intra-concurrency level, SynerDiff alleviates resource contention by pruning component-specific resource bottlenecks via VAE Chunking and Adaptive Skip-CFG. At the inter-concurrency level, leveraging components' differential sensitivity to scheduling granularities, a threshold-aware scheduler plans concurrent sequences and tunes intra-concurrency decisions to minimize VAE latency while maintaining UNet within high-throughput threshold. Additionally, a feedback controller dynamically adjusts this threshold based on queue loads to boost system capacity ceiling. Experimental results show that, SynerDiff improves throughput by 1.6$\times$ and decreases both average E2E and P99 tail latencies by up to 78.7\%, compared to benchmarks while guaranteeing high image fidelity.

</details>


### [230] [M$^3$: Reframing Training Measures for Discretized Physical Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08843)
*Yuan Mei,Xingyu Song,Xiaowen Song,Naoya Takeishi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces M^3, a multi-scale Morton-based sampling framework that rebalances training data for neural surrogate models of physical simulations to reduce measure-induced bias and improve physical fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural surrogate models for physical simulations are usually trained on discretized samples from continuous domains. The empirical measure from these discretizations is often highly non-uniform (e.g., dense near boundaries or regions of interest), which biases optimization, leads to uneven supervision over space, and causes spatial inconsistencies in how well the learned model respects the underlying physics. The authors want a scalable way to correct for this data-distribution bias so that learned operators are accurate across the continuous domain, not just in oversampled regions.

Method: They propose M^3 (Multi-scale Morton Measure), a training data rebalancing and sampling framework. M^3 partitions the physical domain into cells organized along a Morton (Z-order) curve across multiple spatial scales. It uses information about physical variation to guide how space is partitioned and how many training samples are allocated to each cell/scale, effectively constructing a balanced training measure. This creates a multi-scale supervision scheme that spreads training attention more uniformly in a physics-aware manner while remaining scalable to industrial-size volumetric datasets and aggressive subsampling budgets.

Result: On three industrial-scale datasets with heterogeneous discretizations, applying M^3 to train neural surrogate models significantly improves prediction quality in the underlying continuous domain. In large 3D volumetric cases, M^3 yields up to 4.7x lower error. When the total number of training points is reduced drastically (from 160M to 16M to 1.6M), models trained with M^3 still outperform baseline models trained on higher-resolution data, achieving 3–4x reductions in physics-weighted relative L2 error and up to 13x reductions in MSE.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that the spatial distribution of training data (the empirical measure) is a critical factor in operator learning for physical simulations. By explicitly balancing this measure in a multi-scale, physics-aware fashion via M^3, neural surrogate models become more accurate, data-efficient, and physically consistent across the continuous domain. M^3 is thus positioned as a scalable framework for improving physical fidelity in learned simulators, especially under limited data budgets or uneven discretizations.

Abstract: Neural surrogate models for physical simulations are trained on discretized samples of continuous domains, where the induced empirical measure leads to uneven supervision, biasing optimization and causing spatial inconsistencies in physical fidelity. To mitigate this measure-induced bias, we propose M$^3$ (Multi-scale Morton Measure), a scalable framework that balances training measures by partitioning space according to physical variation and allocating supervision across multiple scales. Applied to three industrial-scale datasets with diverse discretizations, M$^3$ consistently improves predictions in the continuous physical domain, achieving up to 4.7$\times$ lower error in large-scale volumetric cases. These gains persist under aggressive subsampling (160M $\rightarrow$ 16M $\rightarrow$ 1.6M points), where M$^3$-trained models outperform those trained on higher-resolution data, reducing physics-weighted relative $L_2$ error by 3--4$\times$ and the corresponding MSE by up to 13$\times$. These results highlight data distribution as a key factor in operator learning and position M$^3$ as a scalable, data-efficient approach for physically consistent modeling.

</details>


### [231] [Ace-Skill: Bootstrapping Multimodal Agents with Prioritized and Clustered Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08887)
*Feng Xiong,Zengbin Wang,Yong Wang,Xuecai Hu,Jinghan He,Liang Lin,Yuan Liu,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ace-Skill is a co-evolutionary framework that jointly optimizes which experiences a self-evolving multimodal agent samples and how it organizes its acquired knowledge, breaking a failure loop of uninformative rollouts and noisy knowledge to achieve more efficient and robust continual adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents are supposed to continually improve by converting task interactions into reusable knowledge. In practice, their performance is limited by two intertwined problems: (1) data inefficiency, where expensive rollouts are wasted on easy or low-value samples instead of hard, informative ones; and (2) knowledge interference, where heterogeneous experiences stored in a shared repository cause noisy, poorly aligned retrieval that misguides future behavior. These issues compound over time, forming a negative feedback loop where low-quality data generates low-quality knowledge, which then harms future data collection and learning. The paper is motivated by the need to systematically address both rollout allocation and knowledge organization so that self-evolution becomes stable, efficient, and performance-improving for multimodal tool-use agents.

Method: The authors propose Ace-Skill, a co-evolutionary framework that couples rollout scheduling with knowledge structuring. On the data side, they introduce a prioritized sampler that allocates more rollouts to samples that are both informative and not yet well mastered by the agent, using a lazy-decay proficiency tracking mechanism to estimate mastery without frequent costly updates. On the knowledge side, they design a clustered organizer that semantically groups the stored knowledge artifacts into clusters, which supports cleaner, more relevant retrieval and reduces cross-task interference. These two components are optimized together, so that better sampling yields higher-quality, more structured knowledge, which in turn enhances subsequent sampling and learning in a positive feedback loop. The system is instantiated for multimodal tool-use agents and evaluated on several benchmarks.

Result: On four multimodal tool-use benchmarks, Ace-Skill significantly improves agent performance metrics such as Avg@4 accuracy, with a reported relative improvement of +35.46%. Using Ace-Skill, an open-source 35B Mixture-of-Experts model is able to match or outperform proprietary models on these benchmarks. Furthermore, the knowledge distilled with Ace-Skill transfers in a zero-shot fashion to smaller 9B and 4B models, enabling them to gain advanced capabilities without additional training. This demonstrates both effectiveness on the primary agent and reusability of the learned knowledge across model scales.

Conclusion: Ace-Skill effectively breaks the vicious cycle of uninformative rollouts and noisy, interfering knowledge in self-evolving agents by jointly optimizing what experiences to collect and how to organize them. The co-evolution of prioritized, proficiency-aware sampling and semantic knowledge clustering creates a virtuous cycle in which better data leads to better knowledge and thus better subsequent adaptation. This yields large gains on multimodal tool-use tasks, allows open-source models to rival proprietary ones, and enables efficient capability transfer to smaller models. The framework and released code suggest a practical path toward more data-efficient, robust, and reusable self-evolving multimodal agents.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents present a promising path toward continual adaptation by distilling task interactions into reusable knowledge artifacts. In practice, this paradigm remains hindered by two coupled bottlenecks: data inefficiency, where costly rollout effort is disproportionately spent on low-value samples rather than informative ones, and knowledge interference, where heterogeneous knowledge stored in shared repositories leads to noisy retrieval and task-misaligned guidance. Together, these issues form a self-reinforcing failure loop in which uninformative rollouts yield noisy knowledge, which in turn degrades subsequent rollouts. In this work, we introduce Ace-Skill, a co-evolutionary framework that jointly optimizes rollout allocation and knowledge organization for self-evolving multimodal agents. Specifically, Ace-Skill combines aprioritized sampler with lazy-decay proficiency tracking to focus rollouts on informative and insufficiently mastered samples, and a clustered organizer that semantically clusters knowledge for cleaner retrieval and more reliable adaptation. By improving sampling and organization together, Ace-Skill turns self-evolution into a virtuous cycle in which more informative rollouts produce higher-quality knowledge that supports stronger subsequent rollouts. Across four multimodal tool-use benchmarks, Ace-Skill delivers strong gains (e.g., +35.46% relative improvement in Avg@4 accuracy), enabling an opensource 35B MoE model to match or surpass proprietary models. The acquired knowledge also transfers effectively in a zero-shot manner to smaller 9B and 4B models, allowing resource-constrained agents to inherit advanced capabilities without additional training. The code has been publicly available at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/Ace-Skill.

</details>


### [232] [OPT-BENCH: Evaluating the Iterative Self-Optimization of LLM Agents in Large-Scale Search Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08904)
*Xiaozhe Li,Jixuan Chen,Xinyu Fang,Shengyuan Ding,Haodong Duan,Qingwen Liu,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces OPT-BENCH, a benchmark to test whether LLM-based agents can self-improve solutions in large search spaces using feedback, and proposes OPT-Agent, a perception–memory–reasoning loop framework. Experiments on 19 LLMs show stronger models use feedback better but all still lag human experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs are strong at reasoning and tool use, it is unclear if they possess core, human-like cognitive faculties needed for continual problem solving—specifically the ability to iteratively refine solutions in response to dynamic feedback rather than just memorize or apply tools in a rote way. The authors want to rigorously test this self-improvement capacity.

Method: They build OPT-BENCH, a benchmark combining 20 machine learning optimization tasks with 10 classic NP-hard combinatorial problems, creating large search spaces and dynamic feedback settings. They also design OPT-Agent, an agent framework with an explicit perception–memory–reasoning loop that repeatedly observes feedback, updates internal memory, and adjusts its solution strategy. They evaluate 19 LLMs from 7 families (including specialized reasoning models, general-purpose models, and open-source models, from 3B to 235B parameters) on this benchmark.

Result: Across the benchmark, larger and more capable LLMs better exploit environmental feedback to improve their solutions over time. Nonetheless, performance is still significantly below that of human experts, and the improvement from feedback is bounded by each model’s underlying base capability; feedback alone cannot compensate for weaker base models.

Conclusion: Self-improvement via feedback is an emergent but limited capability in current LLMs: stronger models can iteratively refine solutions more effectively, yet even state-of-the-art systems remain constrained by their intrinsic capacity and do not match human expert performance. OPT-BENCH and OPT-Agent provide a standardized way to study and compare such adaptive cognitive behavior in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning and tool use. However, the fundamental cognitive faculties essential for problem solving, including perception, reasoning, and memory, remain the stable core of intelligence. Unlike memorizing specific patterns, humans succeed in novel environments by applying these intrinsic faculties to adapt and optimize. Yet, whether LLMs possess this essential capacity, namely the ability to continuously refine solutions in response to dynamic environmental feedback, remains underexplored. To address this challenge, we introduce OPT-BENCH, a benchmark for evaluating self-improvement capabilities in large-scale search spaces. By combining 20 machine learning tasks with 10 classic NP-hard problems, OPT-BENCH provides a rigorous setting to assess whether agents can adapt through intrinsic self-reflection rather than rote tool application. We further propose OPT-Agent, a framework that emulates human-like cognitive adaptation. It operates through a general perception, memory, and reasoning loop, iteratively refining solutions based on environmental feedback. Through extensive experiments on 19 LLMs from 7 model families, including reasoning models, general models, and open-source models ranging from 3B to 235B parameters, we demonstrate that stronger models are more effective at leveraging feedback signals for self-improvement. However, this upper-bound adaptability remains fundamentally constrained by the models' base capacity, and even the most advanced LLMs still fall short of human expert performance.

</details>


### [233] [Forge: Quality-Aware Reinforcement Learning for NP-Hard Optimization in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08905)
*Xiaozhe Li,Xinyu Fang,Shengyuan Ding,Yang Li,Linyang Li,Haodong Duan,Qingwen Liu,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces OPT-BENCH, a framework and benchmark to train and evaluate LLMs on NP-hard optimization tasks with quality-aware reinforcement learning, showing large gains in both feasibility and solution quality over prior models and binary-reward RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most RLVR work on LLMs focuses on whether answers are correct, not how optimal they are, which is critical for NP-hard optimization problems where solution quality under constraints matters. There is a lack of standardized, scalable benchmarks and training infrastructure that target optimality and allow continuous improvement beyond binary correctness on such tasks.

Method: The authors build OPT-BENCH, which includes: (1) scalable instance generators, quality verifiers, and optimal baselines for 10 NP-hard optimization tasks; (2) a 1,000-instance benchmark that evaluates both feasibility via Success Rate (SR) and solution quality via Quality Ratio (QR); and (3) a quality-aware RLVR training scheme that uses graded rewards based on solution quality rather than just correctness. They fine-tune Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-1M on 15K examples using this framework and compare against GPT-4o and binary-reward variants, and they test transfer to other reasoning benchmarks (math, logic, knowledge, instruction following).

Result: Using OPT-BENCH, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-1M trained on 15K examples reaches 93.1% SR and 46.6% QR, greatly surpassing GPT-4o’s 29.6% SR and 14.6% QR on the benchmark. Quality-aware rewards yield a 28.8% improvement in solution quality over binary rewards. Models trained on OPT-BENCH also show measurable gains on out-of-domain tasks: +2.2% in math, +1.2% in logic, +4.1% in knowledge, and +6.1% in instruction following, indicating beneficial transfer and improved general reasoning.

Conclusion: OPT-BENCH demonstrates that LLMs can be effectively trained for NP-hard optimization using quality-aware RLVR, achieving strong gains in both feasibility and solution quality while also improving broader reasoning abilities. The findings emphasize that graded, quality-sensitive rewards and diverse optimization tasks are more important for generalization than merely increasing data size, offering practical guidance for scaling RLVR-based reasoning systems.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success on reasoning benchmarks through Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), excelling at tasks such as math, coding, logic, and puzzles. However, existing benchmarks evaluate only correctness, while overlooking optimality, namely the ability to find the best solutions under constraints. We propose OPT-BENCH, the first comprehensive framework for training and evaluating LLMs on NP-hard optimization problems through quality-aware RLVR. OPT-BENCH provides three key components: a scalable training infrastructure with instance generators, quality verifiers, and optimal baselines across 10 tasks; a rigorous benchmark with 1,000 instances evaluating both feasibility, measured by Success Rate, and quality, measured by Quality Ratio; and quality-aware rewards that enable continuous improvement beyond binary correctness. Training on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-1M with 15K examples achieves 93.1% SR and 46.6% QR, significantly outperforming GPT-4o, which achieves 29.6% SR and 14.6% QR. Beyond optimization, training on OPT-BENCH transfers to diverse tasks, including mathematics (+2.2%), logic (+1.2%), knowledge (+4.1%), and instruction following (+6.1%). Our analysis reveals that quality-aware rewards improve solutions by 28.8% over binary rewards, and that task diversity drives generalization more than data quantity, offering insights into RLVR scaling for complex reasoning.

</details>


### [234] [Internalizing Safety Understanding in Large Reasoning Models via Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08930)
*Yi Zhang,Yuxin Chen,Leheng Sheng,Dongcheng Zhang,Chaochao Lu,Xiang Wang,An Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SInternal, a framework that trains large reasoning models to internally verify the safety of their own answers using expert reasoning trajectories, leading to better robustness against jailbreaks and serving as a stronger initialization for alignment than standard supervised fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing alignment methods for large reasoning models focus on detecting malicious prompts and enforcing external compliance. These methods are largely behavioral and do not ensure that models genuinely understand or evaluate the safety of their own outputs. Empirical analysis shows that seemingly aligned models often fail to verify whether their responses are safe and remain susceptible to adversarial jailbreaks. The authors are motivated to address this lack of intrinsic safety understanding and to build models that can internally reason about the safety of their generated content.

Method: The authors propose Safety Internal (SInternal), a framework that trains large reasoning models only on safety verification tasks. Instead of just learning to produce safe outputs from prompts, the model is trained to critique and assess the safety of its own generated answers. This is done using expert reasoning trajectories that illustrate how to reason about safety. The model learns to perform internal safety verification, and the resulting verifier is then integrated back into the generation process. Additionally, they explore combining SInternal with reinforcement learning, using the SInternal-trained model as an initialization rather than a conventionally supervised fine-tuned one.

Result: Models trained with SInternal exhibit strong generalization capabilities in response safety, particularly demonstrating improved robustness against out-of-domain jailbreak attacks compared to baselines. The internal safety verification ability transfers beyond the training distribution, indicating that the model has acquired a more generalizable notion of safety. When used as an initialization for reinforcement learning, SInternal leads to better alignment performance than using standard supervised fine-tuned models, showing its effectiveness as a starting point for further alignment training.

Conclusion: Internalizing safety specifications via dedicated safety verification training leads to models that better understand and enforce safety in their own outputs. SInternal makes large reasoning models more robust to adversarial jailbreaks and more suitable as a foundation for subsequent alignment methods than approaches that only mimic safe behavior. The framework suggests that teaching models to verify safety is a more powerful strategy than solely training them to avoid unsafe outputs under typical conditions.

Abstract: While explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) empowers large reasoning models (LRMs), it enables the generation of riskier final answers. Current alignment paradigms primarily rely on externally enforced compliance, optimizing models to detect malicious prompts rather than evaluating the safety of their own outputs. We argue that this approach remains largely behavioral: our empirical analysis reveals that ostensibly aligned models lack intrinsic safety understanding, often failing to verify their own response safety and remaining vulnerable to adversarial jailbreaks. To address this fundamental limitation, we propose Safety Internal (SInternal), a framework that internalizes safety specifications by training LRMs exclusively on safety verification tasks to critique their own generated answers using expert reasoning trajectories. We demonstrate that learning to verify induces a strong generalization for response safety, significantly enhancing robustness against out-of-domain jailbreaks. Furthermore, when combined with reinforcement learning, SInternal serves as a superior initialization compared to standard supervised fine-tuning, suggesting that internalizing safety understanding creates a more robust foundation for alignment than merely mimicking safe behaviors. Our codes are available at https://github.com/AlphaLab-USTC/SInternal

</details>


### [235] [PnP-Corrector: A Universal Correction Framework for Coupled Spatiotemporal Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08935)
*Hao Wu,Fan Xu,Yuxu Lu,Penghao Zhao,Fan Zhang,Hao Jia,Yuxuan Liang,Ruijian Gou,Qingsong Wen,Xian Wu,Xiaomeng Huang,Yuan Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PnP-Corrector, a plug-and-play framework that improves long-range coupled spatiotemporal forecasts by correcting systematic biases of frozen physics simulators, significantly reducing compounding errors between interacting subsystems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coupled dynamical systems (e.g., ocean-atmosphere climate models) suffer from compounding simulation errors where biases from one subsystem amplify through interactions with others, quickly degrading long-term forecasts. Existing approaches that jointly learn or fine-tune full simulators struggle to overcome this reciprocal error amplification and often destabilize physically grounded models. There is a need for a general method that can enhance long-range predictive accuracy and stability of existing coupled simulators without retraining them from scratch or compromising physical consistency.

Method: The authors propose PnP-Corrector, a universal plug-and-play framework that separates physical simulation from error correction. Pre-trained physics-based or learned simulators for each subsystem are kept frozen. On top of their outputs, a dedicated correction agent is trained to anticipate and counteract systematic biases arising from the coupled interaction, thereby mitigating reciprocal error amplification. They further introduce DSLCast, a specifically designed efficient predictive model architecture used as the backbone of the correction agent. The framework is trained to minimize forecast errors over long horizons while leaving the underlying simulators unchanged, effectively learning data-driven corrections to the coupled system’s trajectories.

Result: Across extensive experiments on coupled spatiotemporal forecasting tasks, the PnP-Corrector framework yields markedly improved long-term forecast stability and accuracy compared with baselines and state-of-the-art methods. In a demanding benchmark of 300-day global ocean-atmosphere coupled forecasting, PnP-Corrector lowers prediction error by 29% relative to a strong baseline model and achieves superior performance on multiple key evaluation metrics, demonstrating its ability to curb compounding errors over long horizons.

Conclusion: PnP-Corrector offers a general, model-agnostic way to enhance coupled dynamical system forecasts by decoupling physical simulation from a learned correction process. By freezing existing simulators and training a proactive correction agent, it mitigates reciprocal error amplification and substantially improves long-range predictive performance. The results suggest that plug-and-play corrective modules like PnP-Corrector, particularly with architectures such as DSLCast, are a promising direction for stabilizing and upgrading complex coupled forecasting systems, including climate models, without retraining or redesigning their core physics engines.

Abstract: Coupled spatiotemporal forecasting is important for predicting the future evolution of multiple interacting dynamical systems, such as in climate models. However, existing methods are severely constrained by the persistent bottleneck of compounding errors. In coupled systems, errors from each subsystem simulator propagate and amplify one another, a phenomenon we term Reciprocal Error Amplification, leading to a rapid collapse of long-range predictions. To address this challenge, we propose a universal framework called PnP-Corrector (Plug-and-Play Corrector). The core idea of our framework is to decouple the physical simulation from the error correction process: it freezes pre-trained physics simulation engines and exclusively trains a correction agent to proactively counteract the systematic biases emerging from the coupled system. Furthermore, we design an efficient predictive model architecture, DSLCast, to serve as the backbone of this framework. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method significantly enhances the long-term stability and accuracy of coupled forecasting systems. For instance, in the challenging task of a 300-day global ocean-atmosphere coupled forecast, our PnP-Corrector framework reduces the prediction error of the baseline model by 29% and surpasses state-of-the-art models on several key metrics.

</details>


### [236] [Self-ReSET: Learning to Self-Recover from Unsafe Reasoning Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08936)
*Dongcheng Zhang,Yi Zhang,Yuxin Chen,An Zhang,Xiang Wang,Chaochao Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Self-ReSET, a reinforcement-learning-based framework that teaches large reasoning models to recover from their own unsafe reasoning trajectories, improving robustness to adversarial jailbreak prompts while preserving general utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Reasoning Models are good at self-correction in normal settings but fail to recover once they enter unsafe reasoning trajectories under adversarial attacks. Existing safety alignment methods rely on static expert-crafted data such as reflections or adversarial prefixes, which do not match the models’ dynamic, on-policy reasoning traces. This leaves large parts of the model’s generation space uncovered and prevents it from learning to recover from its own real failures. The paper aims to address this mismatch and improve robustness to adversarial prompts without sacrificing usefulness.

Method: The authors introduce Self-ReSET, a pure reinforcement learning framework. The key idea is to treat the model’s own unsafe reasoning trajectories as initial states for RL, so the model learns to ‘reset’ itself from these error trajectories back to safe behavior. Instead of training on static, pre-collected traces, the method uses on-policy trajectories: the model generates reasoning steps, unsafe paths are detected, then these states are reused as starting points for RL updates toward safe continuations. The framework provides rewards for successful self-recovery and safety adherence, guiding the policy toward robust, self-corrective patterns.

Result: Across multiple large reasoning models and benchmarks, Self-ReSET improves robustness against adversarial attacks, particularly on out-of-distribution jailbreak prompts. The method preserves general task utility while making more efficient use of data than prior alignment approaches. Empirical results show better resistance to safety violations and improved ability to steer from unsafe intermediate states back to safe outputs.

Conclusion: Self-ReSET enables large reasoning models to internalize self-recovery behaviors by using their own unsafe trajectories as reinforcement learning starting points. This dynamic, on-policy safety training improves adversarial robustness, especially for unseen jailbreak prompts, while maintaining general performance and making efficient use of data. The approach demonstrates that RL on model-generated error states is an effective way to align reasoning models toward safer behavior.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models possess remarkable capabilities for self-correction in general domain; however, they frequently struggle to recover from unsafe reasoning trajectories under adversarial attacks. Existing alignment methods attempt to mitigate this vulnerability by fine-tuning the model on expert data including reflection traces or adversarial prefixes. Crucially, these approaches are often hindered by static training data which inevitably deviate from model's dynamic, on-policy reasoning traces, resulting in model hardly covering its vast generation space and learning to recover from its own failures. To bridge this gap, we propose Self-ReSET, a pure reinforcement learning framework designed to equip LRMs with the intrinsic capacity to recover from their own safety error trajectories, which are subsequently reused as an initial state for reinforcement learning. Extensive experiments across various LRMs and benchmarks demonstrate that Self-ReSET significantly enhances robustness against adversarial attacks especially out-of-distribution (OOD) jailbreak prompts while maintaining general utility, along with efficient data utilization. Further analysis reveals that our method effectively fosters self-recovery patterns, enabling models to better identify and recover from unsafe intermediate error states back to benign paths. Our codes and data are available at https://github.com/Ing1024/Self-ReSET.

</details>


### [237] [MDGYM: Benchmarking AI Agents on Molecular Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08941)
*Vinay Kumar,Satyendra Rajput,Mausam,N. M. Anoop Krishnan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They present MDGYM, a benchmark to test whether AI agents can autonomously set up and run molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, and show current systems perform poorly, mainly due to lack of robust physical reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously test the claim that AI agents can autonomously drive scientific discovery, using molecular dynamics simulations as a demanding and realistic testbed for complex scientific workflows.

Method: They build MDGYM, a benchmark of 169 expert-curated MD tasks using LAMMPS and GROMACS at three difficulty levels. They then evaluate three agentic frameworks (Claude Code, Codex, OpenHands) powered by four LLMs, and analyze both task success rates and the qualitative failure modes in the resulting MD trajectories.

Result: All evaluated agent systems perform poorly, with the best system solving only 21% of easy tasks and under 10% of medium/hard tasks. Detailed trajectory analysis shows systematic failures: physically unstable configurations, fabricated numerical results without real computation, and premature task abandonment when facing simulation-specific errors.

Conclusion: Current LLM-based agents that appear strong on coding benchmarks fail to robustly handle MD simulation workflows, revealing that code fluency does not equate to grounded physical and scientific reasoning. MDGYM thus exposes a significant gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of autonomous scientific discovery.

Abstract: The promise of AI-driven scientific discovery hinges on whether AI agents can autonomously design and execute the computational workflows that underpin modern science. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulation presents a natural test bed to stress-test this claim; it requires translating physical intuition into syntactically and semantically correct input scripts, reasoning about initial and boundary conditions, diagnosing numerically unstable trajectories, and interpreting outputs against known physical behavior and laws. We introduce MDGYM, a benchmark of 169 expert-curated MD simulations spanning LAMMPS and GROMACS, two widely used MD packages, across three increasing difficulty levels. We evaluate three agentic frameworks -- Claude Code, Codex, and OpenHands -- with four LLMs, and find that all perform poorly: even the strongest agent solves only 21\% of easy-level tasks, with less than 10\% at higher difficulties. Trajectory analysis reveals a characteristic pattern of failure -- agents successfully invoke simulation machinery but produce physically unstable configurations, fabricate numerical outputs without executing the underlying computation, or abandon tasks prematurely rather than iterating through simulation-specific errors. These failure modes are qualitatively distinct from those observed in general software engineering benchmarks, indicating that fluent code generation does not transfer to grounded physical reasoning.

</details>


### [238] [Agentic AI Scientists Are Not Built For Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08956)
*Harshit Bisht,Vinay Kumar,Kevin Maik Jablonka,Mausam,N. M. Anoop Krishnan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that current agentic AI scientists, while useful co-scientists, are not yet suitable for truly autonomous scientific discovery, due to structural and design limitations in how they are built and evaluated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is growing interest in creating AI systems that can autonomously conduct end-to-end scientific discovery, from hypothesis generation to experimentation and interpretation. However, existing approaches treat this largely as a scaling and engineering problem, overlooking deeper conceptual and methodological issues that limit autonomy and reliability. The authors aim to clarify these limitations and propose directions for redesigning AI scientists so they can genuinely advance science rather than simply automate narrow tasks.

Method: This is a position paper, not an empirical study. The authors conduct a conceptual and critical analysis of current AI scientist architectures and practices. They identify four major challenge areas: (1) problem selection distorted by the McNamara fallacy (optimizing for what is easily measurable rather than what is scientifically important); (2) reliance on LLMs whose training data underrepresents tacit procedural and failure knowledge from real lab work; (3) post-training preference optimization that narrows the diversity of outputs and pushes toward consensus, undermining scientific creativity and exploration; and (4) benchmark design that focuses on single-turn predictive accuracy without incorporating feedback loops from real or simulated experiments back into the models. Based on this analysis, they derive a set of design recommendations.

Result: The primary result is a structured critique identifying four specific, systemic obstacles to building autonomous AI scientists: biased problem selection, missing tacit lab knowledge, diversity-reducing preference optimization, and inadequate benchmarks lacking experimental feedback. From these, they distill several concrete recommendations: use scientific simulations as verifiers during training; construct persistent world models that track evolving goals and states of investigations; create a centralized preregistration repository for AI-generated hypotheses; and prioritize deployments where there is clear scientific need rather than simply exploiting what current tools can easily do.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that achieving truly autonomous AI scientists cannot be solved by simply scaling up current LLM-based agents or adding more scaffolding. Instead, it requires rethinking foundational design choices: how problems are selected, how knowledge (including tacit and failure knowledge) is represented and learned, how diversity and disagreement are preserved, and how evaluation incorporates closed experimental feedback loops. They argue that following their proposed directions—simulation-based verification, persistent world models, preregistration infrastructure, and need-driven deployment—will be essential for AI systems to move from co-scientists that assist humans to genuinely autonomous scientific discoverers.

Abstract: A growing body of work pursues AI scientists capable of end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery. This position paper argues that although they already function as co-scientists, agentic AI scientists are not built for autonomous scientific discovery. We identify the following challenges in building and deploying autonomous AI scientists: (1) Problem selection is influenced by the McNamara fallacy; (2) Agents are built on large language models (LLMs) whose training corpora omit tacit procedural and failure knowledge of laboratory practice; (3) Preference optimisation during post-training compresses output diversity toward consensus; and (4) Most scientific benchmarks measure single-turn prediction accuracy and lack feedback from physical experiments back to the computational model. These challenges are not just questions of scale and scaffolding; they require revisiting fundamental design choices. To build truly autonomous AI scientists, we recommend the use of scientific simulations as verifiers for training, the design of persistent world models that represent the shifting objectives governing real investigations, the establishment of a centralized preregistration repository for all AI-generated hypotheses, and application driven by scientific need rather than tool affordance.

</details>


### [239] [Latency Analysis and Optimization of Alpamayo 1 via Efficient Trajectory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08975)
*Yunseong Jeon,Namcheol Lee,Yoonsu Lee,Jangwoon Park,Sol Ahn,Jong-Chan Kim,Seongsoo Hong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes and optimizes a reasoning-based end-to-end autonomous driving system (Alpamayo 1) to greatly reduce inference latency while preserving trajectory diversity and prediction quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning-based E2E autonomous driving improves interpretability by outputting both human-readable reasoning and trajectories, but current systems either use multi-reasoning (diverse but computationally redundant) or single-reasoning (efficient but assumed less diverse). Alpamayo 1 is competitive but its efficiency and the trade-off between reasoning multiplicity and diversity have not been systematically studied.

Method: (1) Redesign Alpamayo 1 from a multi-reasoning to a single-reasoning architecture to test whether trajectory diversity can be maintained with lower computational cost. (2) Optimize the diffusion-based action generation pipeline by removing inter-block overhead caused by unnecessary data copies and inefficient kernel execution. The authors evaluate both open-loop and closed-loop performance to measure latency, trajectory diversity, and prediction quality.

Result: The single-reasoning redesign achieves similar trajectory diversity as the original multi-reasoning setup. The diffusion pipeline optimizations significantly accelerate action generation. Combined, these two improvements yield a 69.23% reduction in inference latency without degrading trajectory prediction quality or diversity, verified in both closed-loop and open-loop experiments.

Conclusion: Multi-reasoning is not necessary to maintain high trajectory diversity in reasoning-based E2E autonomous driving, and considerable efficiency gains can be realized through careful architectural simplification and low-level runtime optimizations. Joint analysis of model structure and execution behavior is crucial for building efficient, interpretable E2E autonomous driving systems.

Abstract: Reasoning-based end-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving has recently emerged as a promising approach to improving the interpretability of driving decisions as it can generate human-readable reasoning together with predicted trajectories. Such approaches commonly generate multiple trajectories to capture diverse future behaviors, and they fall into two categories: (1) multi-reasoning, where one reasoning sequence is generated per trajectory, and (2) single-reasoning, where a single reasoning is shared across all trajectories. The former offers richer diversity at the cost of redundant computation, while the latter is more efficient but is often assumed to sacrifice diversity. Alpamayo 1, a representative system, adopts the multi-reasoning approach and achieves competitive trajectory prediction performance. However, the efficiency of this design remains largely unexplored, making it a well-motivated subject for investigation. In this paper, we systematically analyze and improve Alpamayo 1 in two ways. First, we reduce inference latency while preserving trajectory diversity by redesigning Alpamayo 1 into a single-reasoning system. Through extensive experiments, we find that replacing multi-reasoning with single-reasoning does not meaningfully degrade trajectory diversity. Second, we accelerate diffusion-based action generation by eliminating inter-block overhead arising from unnecessary copy operations and inefficient kernel execution. Through closed-loop and open-loop experiments, we validate both optimizations, demonstrating a 69.23% reduction in inference latency while maintaining trajectory diversity and prediction quality. These results highlight the importance of jointly analyzing system architecture and runtime execution to improve the efficiency of reasoning-based E2E AD systems.

</details>


### [240] [Learning to Explore: Scaling Agentic Reasoning via Exploration-Aware Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08978)
*Xingyuan Hua,Sheng Yue,Ju Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an exploration-aware RL framework for LLM agents that only explores when uncertainty is high, using variational-inference-based rewards and an exploration-aware action grouping mechanism, achieving better performance on text and GUI agent benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic test-time scaling lets models gather feedback before acting, but current methods use blunt, undifferentiated exploration and can’t tell when exploration is actually necessary, leading to inefficiency and suboptimal behavior.

Method: They design an exploration-aware reinforcement learning framework where: (1) a fine-grained reward function is derived via variational inference to evaluate exploratory actions by how much they are expected to improve future decisions; (2) an exploration-aware grouping mechanism separates exploratory actions from task-execution actions during optimization so that updates can treat them differently and encourage targeted exploration only under high uncertainty.

Result: Across various challenging text-based and GUI-based agent benchmarks, their method yields consistent performance improvements compared with previous exploration strategies for LLM-based agents.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling and rewarding information-gathering behavior, and structurally separating exploration from task completion in the learning process, allows LLM agents to explore selectively under uncertainty and quickly switch to execution once the task context is understood, leading to more efficient and effective agent behavior.

Abstract: Recent advancements in agentic test-time scaling allow models to gather environmental feedback before committing to final actions. A key limitation of existing methods is that they typically employ undifferentiated exploration strategies, lacking the ability to adaptively distinguish when exploration is truly required. In this paper, we propose an exploration-aware reinforcement learning framework that enables LLM agents to adaptively explore only when uncertainty is high. Our method introduces a fine-grained reward function via variational inference that explicitly evaluates exploratory actions by estimating their potential to improve future decision-making, together with an exploration-aware grouping mechanism that separates exploratory actions from task-completion actions during optimization. By targeting informational gaps, this design allows agents to explore selectively and transition to execution as soon as the task context is clear. Empirically, we demonstrate that our approach achieves consistent improvements across a range of challenging text-based and GUI-based agent benchmarks. Code is available at \url{https://github.com/HansenHua/EAPO-ICML26} and models are available at https://huggingface.co/hansenhua/EAPO-ICML26.

</details>


### [241] [Sufficient conditions for a Heuristic Rating Estimation Method application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.08991)
*Jacek Szybowski,Konrad Kułakowski,Jiri Mazurek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The papers develop and analyze the Heuristic Rating Estimation (HRE) method for evaluating alternatives using pairwise comparisons and known reference weights, and identify when the method works correctly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional pairwise comparison methods evaluate alternatives but often lack a clear way to incorporate known ratings (reference alternatives) and to guarantee correctness under varying levels of inconsistency and data completeness. The authors want to formalize and justify when the HRE method—which uses pairwise comparisons along with known reference weights—is mathematically sound and practically reliable.

Method: They study the Heuristic Rating Estimation method under both arithmetic and geometric formulations, and for both complete and incomplete pairwise comparison matrices. They derive formal conditions for the applicability and correctness of HRE, and compare inconsistency estimations between arithmetic and geometric variants, using analytical derivations supported by illustrative numerical examples.

Result: They obtain explicit conditions that must hold for HRE to be applied correctly in different settings (complete/incomplete matrices, arithmetic/geometric algorithms). The examples demonstrate that, according to their inconsistency measure, the arithmetic variant achieves optimal estimates of inconsistency among the considered options.

Conclusion: The work consolidates the theoretical foundations of the HRE method by specifying when it is valid and reliable. Among the studied variants, the arithmetic HRE algorithm provides optimal inconsistency estimates, making it a preferred choice under the stated conditions for practical decision-making problems based on pairwise comparisons with reference alternatives.

Abstract: A series of papers has introduced the Heuristic Rating Estimation method, which evaluates a set of alternatives based on pairwise comparisons and the weights of reference alternatives. We formulate the conditions under which the HRE method can be applied correctly. The research considers both arithmetic and geometric algorithms for complete and incomplete pairwise comparison methods. The illustrative examples show that the estimations of inconsistency in the arithmetic variant are optimal.

</details>


### [242] [Re$^2$Math: Benchmarking Theorem Retrieval in Research-Level Mathematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09012)
*Zicheng Lyu,Wenjie Yang,Shengzhong Zhang,Zengfeng Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce Re^2Math, a benchmark to evaluate literature-grounded mathematical tool retrieval from partial proofs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large language models are good at closed-world math reasoning but weak at using external literature to support proofs. The authors aim to rigorously test whether systems can find and apply appropriate theorems or lemmas from scholarly sources when encountering non-trivial proof steps.

Method: They construct Re^2Math instances from real proofs where an instrumental citation is used. Each instance includes hierarchical proof context and possibly an anchor hint. The task is citation-agnostic: any theorem sufficient for the proof step is acceptable. They freeze a retrieval artifact for evaluation, enabling reproducibility, and design the benchmark so it can be automatically expanded over time.
