This motivates the use of white-box monitors like linear probes, which can read the model internals directly. Currently, such probes can fail under distribution shift, limiting their usefulness in real settings. We study whether there exists a low-dimensional subspace of the model internals that captures harmful behaviors more robustly, while leaving out spuriously correlative features. Inspired by the Assistant Axis and Persona Selection Model, we construct persona axes for deception and sycophancy using contrastive persona prompts. The first principal components, obtained by unsupervised PCA of the persona-specific vectors, cleanly separate harmful and harmless personas. Across 10 evaluation datasets, we show that persona-derived directions transfer non-trivially and probes trained on persona-PC projections generalize better than probes trained on raw activations. We also find that a unified axis consisting of multiple harmful and harmless behaviors improves generalization across behaviors and datasets. Overall, persona vectors provide a useful inductive bias for building more transferable behavior probes.

</details>


### [283] [Empowering VLMs for Few-Shot Multimodal Time Series Classification via Tailored Agentic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09395)
*Lin Li,Jiawei Huang,Qihao Quan,Dan Li,Boxin Li,Xiao Zhang,Erli Meng,Wenjie Feng,Jian Lou,See-Kiong Ng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MarsTSC is a visual-language model based multimodal time-series classifier that uses agentic reasoning and a self-evolving knowledge bank to improve few-shot performance and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Few-shot multimodal time series classification is difficult for existing methods, including both classical models and foundation models, which struggle with limited labeled data, distribution shift, and lack of interpretability. There is a need for a framework that can adapt its knowledge at test time, reduce few-shot bias, and provide human-understandable rationales for its decisions.

Method: The authors design MarsTSC, an agentic reasoning framework built on vision-language models for multimodal time series classification. It introduces a self-evolving knowledge bank that acts as dynamic context. Three coordinated agents operate over this bank: (i) a Generator that performs classification via chain-of-thought style reasoning; (ii) a Reflector that analyzes incorrect or weak reasoning to identify overlooked temporal-discriminative features; and (iii) a Modifier that validates and incorporates updates into the knowledge bank to avoid context collapse. Additionally, a test-time update mechanism cautiously refines the bank during inference to handle few-shot bias and distribution shifts.

Result: On 12 standard time series benchmarks and across 6 different VLM backbones, MarsTSC consistently outperforms both traditional time series methods and newer foundation-model-based baselines in few-shot settings. It shows substantial accuracy gains while maintaining robustness under distribution shifts and provides interpretable, feature-grounded rationales for its predictions.

Conclusion: Agentic reasoning combined with a self-evolving knowledge bank is an effective way to improve few-shot multimodal time series classification. By iteratively reflecting on and updating its own reasoning context, MarsTSC achieves better performance, adaptability, and interpretability than existing approaches, suggesting a promising direction for time series modeling with VLMs.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose the first VL$\underline{\textbf{M}}$ $\underline{\textbf{a}}$gentic $\underline{\textbf{r}}$easoning framework for few-$\underline{\textbf{s}}$hot multimodal $\underline{\textbf{T}}$ime $\underline{\textbf{S}}$eries $\underline{\textbf{C}}$lassification ($\textbf{MarsTSC}$), which introduces a self-evolving knowledge bank as a dynamic context iteratively refined via reflective agentic reasoning. The framework comprises three collaborative roles: i) Generator conducts reliable classification via reasoning; ii) Reflector diagnoses the root causes of reasoning errors to yield discriminative insights targeting the temporal features overlooked by Generator; iii) Modifier applies verified updates to the knowledge bank to prevent context collapse. We further introduce a test-time update strategy to enable cautious, continuous knowledge bank refinement to mitigate few-shot bias and distribution shift. Extensive experiments across 12 mainstream time series benchmarks demonstrate that $\textbf{MarsTSC}$ delivers substantial and consistent performance gains across 6 VLM backbones, outperforming both classical and foundation model-based time series baselines under few-shot conditions, while producing interpretable rationales that ground each classification decision in human-readable feature evidence.

</details>


### [284] [Strategic commitments shape collective cybersecurity under AI inequality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09415)
*Adeela Bashir,Zia Ush Shamszaman,Zhao Song,The Anh Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper uses evolutionary game theory to study how unequal access to AI-enabled cyber defence affects security, and finds that subsidising a small set of always-secure “committed defenders” can greatly improve overall system resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI tools become central to cyber offence and defence, not all defenders can afford or access high-capability AI defences. This creates structural vulnerabilities where resource-poor defenders remain weak points, potentially undermining overall security. Policymakers and system designers need principled guidance on whether and how targeted support or subsidies for AI defences can shift the system toward more secure equilibria.

Method: The authors build an evolutionary game-theoretic model with a finite population of attackers and defenders. Defenders can choose between low- and high-capability AI-based protections with different costs and effectiveness. They model social learning dynamics where strategies spread based on payoffs, include a fraction of ‘committed defenders’ who always adopt strong defence, and then introduce targeted subsidies that eliminate the cost disadvantage for these committed defenders. They analyse equilibria and dynamics and complement theory with simulations over a wide parameter space and social-welfare analysis.

Result: Without intervention, costly strong defence leads the population to converge toward cheaper but weak defence, keeping successful attacks frequent and degrading long-run security. Adding a small group of committed defenders increases strong defence but cannot stabilise secure outcomes if strong defence remains expensive. When committed defenders receive targeted subsidies that remove their cost disadvantage, the adoption of strong defence rises sharply, attack success rates drop, and overall system resilience improves. Simulations show that subsidy-augmented commitment consistently outperforms commitment alone across many parameter settings, and welfare analysis indicates better defender payoffs with limited gains for attackers.

Conclusion: Unequal access to advanced AI defences can structurally bias cybersecurity systems toward weak protection and persistent vulnerability. Simply relying on a few highly committed defenders is insufficient when strong defence is too costly. However, targeted subsidies that neutralise the cost disadvantage for key committed defenders can shift evolutionary dynamics toward widespread strong defence, reducing successful attacks and improving resilience and welfare. The work suggests that carefully designed support for pivotal defenders is a promising policy lever linking cybersecurity strategy, AI governance, and the allocation of defensive AI capabilities.

Abstract: The growing integration of AI into cybersecurity is reshaping the balance between attackers and defenders. When access to advanced AI-enabled defence tools is uneven, resource-limited defenders may be unable to adopt effective protection, creating persistent system vulnerabilities. We study the impact of differential AI access using an evolutionary game-theoretic model in a finite population. We first show that when high-capability defence is costly, the population is driven toward low-cost, weak-defence behaviour, sustaining attacks and weakening long-run security. To address this problem, we introduce differential access to AI defence tools by allowing defenders to choose between low- and high-capability protection based on their resources. We then examine the role of a small group of committed defenders who always adopt strong defence and influence others through social learning. Although commitment increases the prevalence of strong defence, it alone cannot stabilise secure outcomes due to high defence costs. We therefore incorporate a targeted subsidy to remove the cost disadvantage from committed defenders. Our analysis shows that subsidised commitment significantly increases strong defence adoption, suppresses successful attacks, and improves overall system resilience. Simulations across a broad parameter space confirm that subsidies consistently outperform commitment alone. In addition, social-welfare analysis shows improved defender outcomes while keeping attacker gains low. These findings suggest that targeted support for key defenders can be an effective mechanism for stabilising cybersecurity in AI-driven environments and provide a theoretical bridge between cybersecurity policy, AI governance, and strategic allocation of defensive AI capabilities.

</details>


### [285] [From Passive Reuse to Active Reasoning: Grounding Large Language Models for Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09419)
*Yanan Xiao,Yixiang Tang,Zechen Feng,Lu Jiang,Minghao Yin,Pengyang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay (NSER) enhances reinforcement learning by converting replay buffers into active knowledge constructors via neuro-symbolic rule induction and reweighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard experience replay in RL is data-efficient but passive, prioritizing samples by numeric errors, unlike humans who form abstract behavioral rules from experience. There is a gap between numerical optimization and linguistic/symbolic reasoning that prevents replay buffers from constructing and using higher-level knowledge.

Method: NSER introduces a neuro-symbolic grounding pipeline that: (1) uses LLMs in a zero-shot way to induce candidate behavioral rules from stored trajectories; (2) grounds these rules into differentiable first-order logic representations; and (3) uses these symbolic structures to dynamically reweight the replay buffer’s sampling distribution, allowing rules to influence which experiences are replayed during training.

Result: Across multiple benchmarks (reactive, rule-based, and procedural tasks), NSER consistently improves sample efficiency and accelerates convergence compared to standard replay-based RL baselines.

Conclusion: By integrating LLM-based rule induction and differentiable logic with experience replay, NSER turns replay from passive memory into an active knowledge-construction engine, enabling abstract symbolic knowledge to directly guide policy optimization for more efficient and faster-learning RL agents.

Abstract: While experience replay is essential for data efficiency in reinforcement learning (RL), standard methods treat the replay buffer as a passive memory system, prioritizing samples based on numerical prediction errors rather than their semantic significance. This approach stands in contrast to human learning, which accelerates mastery by actively abstracting fragmented experiences into behavioral rules. To bridge this gap, we propose Neuro-Symbolic Experience Replay (NSER), a framework that transforms experience replay from a passive sample reuse mechanism into an active engine for knowledge construction. Specifically, NSER addresses the incompatibility between linguistic reasoning and numerical optimization through a novel neuro-symbolic grounding pipeline. It leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) in a zero-shot manner to induce candidate behavioral rules from accumulated trajectories, grounds these insights into differentiable first-order logic representations, and utilizes the resulting symbolic structures to dynamically reweight the replay distribution. By allowing abstract knowledge to directly shape policy optimization, NSER achieves consistent superior sample efficiency and convergence speed across reactive, rule-based, and procedural benchmarks.

</details>


### [286] [SimWorld Studio: Automatic Environment Generation with Evolving Coding Agent for Embodied Agent Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09423)
*Haoqiang Kang,Xiaokang Ye,Yuhan Liu,Siddhant Hitesh Mantri,Lingjun Mao,James Fleming,Drishti Regmi,Lianhui Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimWorld Studio is an Unreal Engine 5-based platform that uses a self-improving coding agent (SimCoder) to automatically generate and evolve 3D embodied learning environments, co-evolving them with embodied agents to improve generalization and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital agents using LLMs/VLMs have benefited from scalable, automatically generated sandboxes for coding and web tasks, but embodied agents still lack abundant and diverse 3D environments with verifiable tasks and standard interfaces. Existing simulators depend on manual design or limited procedural templates, and current LLM-based 3D generation mainly yields static scenes that are not directly usable for embodied learning. There is a need for a system that can automatically create interactive, physics-grounded 3D environments at scale and adapt them to learner progress.

Method: The authors develop SimWorld Studio, an open-source platform built on Unreal Engine 5. Its core component, SimCoder, is a tool- and skill-augmented coding agent that generates and executes engine-level code to build 3D worlds from language or image instructions. SimCoder iteratively self-improves by using verifier feedback (compilation errors, physics validity checks, VLM critiques) to refine generated environments and expand its library of reusable tools and skills. The resulting environments are exported as Gym-style interfaces. The platform supports co-evolution: performance signals from embodied agents are fed back to SimCoder so it can generate adaptive curricula, tuning environment difficulty to the agent’s current capabilities. Three embodied navigation case studies are used to evaluate the framework.

Result: Self-evolution in SimCoder increases the reliability of environment generation. The automatically generated environments significantly boost embodied agent performance and enable generalization to unseen benchmarks. When using co-evolution between environment generation and agent learning, the system delivers an 18-point improvement in success rate compared to training on fixed environments and a 40-point improvement compared to an untrained agent.

Conclusion: Automatically generated, evolving 3D environments can close the gap between digital and embodied agents by providing scalable, adaptive training grounds. SimWorld Studio, via its self-improving SimCoder agent and Gym-style export, offers a practical way to create deployable, verifiable embodied learning environments and adaptive curricula. The co-evolution paradigm leads to more capable and generalizable embodied agents than training on manually fixed environments.

Abstract: LLM/VLM-based digital agents have advanced rapidly thanks to scalable sandboxes for coding, web navigation, and computer use, which provide rich interactive training grounds. In contrast, embodied agents still lack abundant, diverse, and automatically generated 3D environments for interactive learning. Existing embodied simulators rely on manually crafted scenes or procedural templates, while recent LLM-based 3D generation systems mainly produce static scenes rather than deployable environments with verifiable tasks and standard learning interfaces. We introduce SimWorld Studio, an open-source platform built on Unreal Engine 5 for generating evolving embodied learning environments. At its core is SimCoder, a tool/skill-augmented coding agent that writes and executes engine-level code to construct physically grounded 3D worlds from language/image instructions. SimCoder self-evolves by using verifier feedback (e.g., compilation errors, physics checks, VLM critiques) to revise environments and autonomously add reusable tools and skills to its library. Generated worlds are exported as Gym-style environments for embodied agent learning. SimWorld Studio further enables co-evolution between environment generation and embodied learning: agent performance feedback guides SimCoder to generate adaptive curricula near the learner's capability frontier, so that environments become increasingly challenging as the embodied agent improves. Three case studies on embodied navigation show that self-evolution improves generation reliability, generated environments substantially improve embodied agent performance that generalizes to unseen benchmarks, and co-evolution yields an 18-point success-rate gain over fixed-environment learning and a 40-point gain over an untrained agent.

</details>


### [287] [VulTriage: Triple-Path Context Augmentation for LLM-Based Vulnerability Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09461)
*Wenxin Tang,Xiang Zhang,Junliang Liu,Jingyu Xiao,Xi Xiao,Jinlong Yang,Yuehe Ma,Zhenyu Liu,Zhengheng Li,Zicheng Wang,Wang Luo,Qing Li,Lei Wang,Peng Xiangli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VulTriage is a framework that augments LLMs with structured code analysis, vulnerability knowledge, and semantic summaries to significantly improve automated vulnerability detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing learning-based and LLM-based vulnerability detectors often miss bugs or raise false alarms because they fail to fully capture structural dependencies in code, incorporate domain-specific vulnerability knowledge, and distinguish subtle semantic differences between vulnerable and benign functions. The authors want to systematically enhance LLM inputs so they can reason more accurately about vulnerabilities, especially in challenging paired settings where code variants differ only slightly.

Method: The authors propose VulTriage, a triple-path context augmentation framework for LLM-based vulnerability detection. It constructs three complementary input paths for the LLM: (1) a Control Path that extracts abstract syntax tree (AST), control-flow graph (CFG), and data-flow graph (DFG) information and verbalizes them to expose control and data dependencies; (2) a Knowledge Path that retrieves relevant CWE-based vulnerability patterns and examples using a hybrid dense–sparse retrieval strategy; and (3) a Semantic Path that summarizes the functional behavior of the target code. These three forms of context are integrated into a unified instruction prompt that guides the LLM’s vulnerability reasoning process.

Result: On the PrimeVul pair test set, VulTriage achieves state-of-the-art performance and surpasses both traditional deep learning and prior LLM-based baselines in pair-wise comparison and classification metrics. Ablation studies show that removing any of the three paths degrades performance, confirming their complementary value. Additional experiments on a Kotlin vulnerability dataset show that VulTriage maintains strong performance and generalizes well even in low-resource and class-imbalanced scenarios.

Conclusion: Augmenting LLMs with structured program analysis, explicit vulnerability knowledge, and semantic summaries markedly improves automated vulnerability detection. VulTriage’s triple-path design yields more reliable vulnerability judgments, outperforms existing baselines, and generalizes across languages and data regimes, suggesting that carefully engineered contextualization is key to unlocking LLMs’ potential in software security tasks.

Abstract: Automated vulnerability detection is a fundamental task in software security, yet existing learning-based methods still struggle to capture the structural dependencies, domain-specific vulnerability knowledge, and complex program semantics required for accurate detection. Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong code understanding ability, but directly prompting them with raw source code often leads to missed vulnerabilities or false alarms, especially when vulnerable and benign functions differ only in subtle semantic details. To address this, we propose VulTriage, a triple-path context augmentation framework for LLM-based vulnerability detection. VulTriage enhances the LLM input through three complementary paths: a Control Path that extracts and verbalizes AST, CFG, and DFG information to expose control and data dependencies; a Knowledge Path that retrieves relevant CWE-derived vulnerability patterns and examples through hybrid dense--sparse retrieval; and a Semantic Path that summarizes the functional behavior of the code before the final judgment. These contexts are integrated into a unified instruction to guide the LLM toward more reliable vulnerability reasoning. Experiments on the PrimeVul pair test set show that VulTriage achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing deep learning and LLM-based baselines on key pair-wise and classification metrics. Further ablation studies verify the effectiveness of each path, and additional experiments on the Kotlin dataset demonstrate the generalization ability of VulTriage under low-resource and class-imbalanced settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/vinsontang1/VulTriage

</details>


### [288] [Don't Click That: Teaching Web Agents to Resist Deceptive Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09497)
*Yilin Zhang,Yingkai Hua,Chunyu Wei,Xin Wang,Yueguo Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes DUDE, a deception-aware defense framework for vision-language web agents that reduces susceptibility to deceptive UI elements while preserving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language model web agents can autonomously interact with GUIs but are easily fooled by deceptive or misleading interface components, and existing work either only detects deception in isolation or documents attacks without defenses. There is a need for a principled, task-integrated defense that makes agents robust to UI deception.

Method: Formalize the problem of deception-aware defense for web agents and present DUDE, a two-stage framework. DUDE uses hybrid-reward learning with asymmetric penalties to discourage deceptive interactions and employs experience summarization to extract failure patterns, which are then distilled into reusable guidance for the agent. Additionally, the authors construct RUC (Real UI Clickboxes), a benchmark of 1,407 deceptive scenarios across four domains and deception categories, to evaluate the system.

Result: On the RUC benchmark, DUDE reduces the agent’s susceptibility to deceptive UI elements by 53.8% compared to baselines, while preserving overall task completion performance.

Conclusion: DUDE provides an effective, generalizable defense mechanism for vision-language web agents against deceptive UI elements, supported by a new benchmark (RUC). This establishes a practical foundation for deploying more robust and trustworthy web agents in real-world environments.

Abstract: Vision-language model (VLM) based web agents demonstrate impressive autonomous GUI interaction but remain vulnerable to deceptive interface elements. Existing approaches either detect deception without task integration or document attacks without proposing defenses. We formalize deception-aware web agent defense and propose DUDE (Deceptive UI Detector & Evaluator), a two-stage framework combining hybrid-reward learning with asymmetric penalties and experience summarization to distill failure patterns into transferable guidance. We introduce RUC (Real UI Clickboxes), a benchmark of 1,407 scenarios spanning four domains and deception categories. Experiments show DUDE reduces deception susceptibility by 53.8% while maintaining task performance, establishing an effective foundation for robust web agent deployment.

</details>


### [289] [EpiGraph: A Knowledge Graph and Benchmark for Evidence-Intensive Reasoning in Epilepsy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09505)
*Yuyang Dai,Zheng Chen,Jathurshan Pradeepkumar,Yasuko Matsubara,Jimeng Sun,Yasushi Sakurai,Yushun Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces EpiGraph, a large-scale epilepsy knowledge graph, and EpiBench, a benchmark for testing how well LLMs perform knowledge-augmented clinical reasoning tasks in epilepsy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Epilepsy care demands complex, evidence-based reasoning over diverse clinical information sources (EEG patterns, genetics, pharmacogenomics, treatments, outcomes). Current LLMs struggle to reliably integrate such heterogeneous, specialized knowledge. There is a need for structured domain knowledge resources and standardized benchmarks to evaluate and improve knowledge-augmented clinical reasoning for epilepsy.

Method: The authors construct EpiGraph, a heterogeneous epilepsy knowledge graph by integrating 48,166 peer-reviewed papers and seven clinical resources. The resulting graph has 24,324 entities and 32,009 evidence-grounded triples organized into five clinical layers. On top of this, they design EpiBench, which defines five clinically motivated tasks: clinical decision-making, EEG report generation, pharmacogenomic precision medicine, treatment recommendation, and deep research planning. They then evaluate six large language models both in standard settings and with Graph-RAG, where models retrieve and use information from EpiGraph.

Result: Across all five EpiBench tasks, incorporating EpiGraph via Graph-RAG improves LLM performance compared to standard use without the graph. The largest performance gains (around +30–41%) occur in pharmacogenomic reasoning tasks, indicating that structured knowledge is particularly beneficial where detailed, mechanistic evidence is crucial.

Conclusion: Structured, evidence-grounded epilepsy knowledge in the form of a large-scale knowledge graph significantly enhances clinical reasoning of LLMs, especially for pharmacogenomics. EpiGraph and EpiBench together provide a practical framework and benchmark for developing and evaluating knowledge-augmented LLMs in real-world neurological applications. The resources (including code) are publicly available for further research and development.

Abstract: Epilepsy diagnosis and treatment require evidence-intensive reasoning across heterogeneous clinical knowledge, including biosignal patterns, genetic mechanisms, pharmacogenomics, treatment strategies, and patient outcomes. In this work, we present \textsc{EpiGraph}, a large-scale epilepsy knowledge graph and benchmark for evaluating knowledge-augmented clinical reasoning. \textsc{EpiGraph} integrates 48,166 peer-reviewed papers and seven clinical resources into a heterogeneous graph containing 24,324 entities and 32,009 evidence-grounded triplets across five clinical layers. Built upon this graph, \textsc{EpiBench} defines five clinically motivated tasks spanning clinical decision-making, EEG report generation, pharmacogenomic precision medicine, treatment recommendation, and deep research planning. We evaluate six LLMs under both standard and Graph-RAG settings. Results show that integrating \textsc{EpiGraph} consistently improves performance across all tasks, with the largest gains observed in pharmacogenomic reasoning (+30--41\%). Our findings demonstrate that structured epilepsy knowledge substantially enhances evidence-grounded clinical reasoning and provides a practical benchmark framework for evaluating knowledge-augmented LLMs in real-world neurological settings. Our code is available at: https://github.com/LabRAI/EEG-KG.

</details>


### [290] [WindINR: Latent-State INR for Fast Local Wind Query and Correction in Complex Terrain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09511)
*Yi Xiao,Qilong Jia,Hang Fan,Pascal Fua,Robert Jenssen,Xiaosong Ma,Wei Xue*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: WindINR is a neural implicit representation framework that provides continuous, high-resolution wind estimates at arbitrary locations and efficiently corrects them using sparse observations by updating only a compact latent state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operational applications in complex terrain (e.g., aviation, UAS operations, wind energy, hazard forecasting) often need fast, accurate wind estimates at specific locations and heights, not full dense forecast fields. Existing high-resolution models are computationally expensive, and online correction with new observations via full-network fine-tuning is slow. There is a need for a method that can (1) leverage coarse background forecasts and terrain information, (2) provide continuous, query-based high-resolution wind estimates, and (3) quickly assimilate sparse local observations.

Method: The authors develop WindINR, an implicit neural representation that maps static terrain descriptors, a low-resolution background wind field, and continuous spatial coordinates to a high-resolution local wind state via a latent-conditioned decoder. Training uses three components: (1) a privileged encoder that, using high-resolution supervision, infers a reference latent state; (2) a deployable latent predictor that estimates an initial latent state from only inference-time inputs; and (3) a mechanism that summarizes discrepancies between these two latent states into a Gaussian prior over latent corrections, adapted to the dataset. At inference, the network weights are frozen and only the latent state is updated by minimizing a regularized objective that uses sparse wind observations and their uncertainty, effectively performing fast latent-space data assimilation. The learned representation is continuously queryable at arbitrary coordinates.

Result: In observing system simulation experiments (OSSEs) over the Senja region, including scenarios with UAV-aided observations and tests with randomly placed observations, WindINR improves local high-resolution wind estimates by optimizing only the latent state. Compared to full-network fine-tuning, this latent-only correction maintains or improves accuracy while providing roughly a 2.6× speedup in online correction on CPU. The corrected wind field remains continuous and queryable at arbitrary spatial locations.

Conclusion: WindINR demonstrates that separating reusable representation learning from sample-specific latent-state correction enables fast, observation-informed, high-resolution wind estimates in complex terrain. By updating only a compact latent state conditioned on sparse observations, the framework offers a practical and efficient interface between coarse background forecasts, limited local measurements, and continuous wind queries, making it suitable for real-time or near–real-time applications in complex terrain.

Abstract: Many downstream decisions in complex terrain require fast wind estimates at a small number of user-specified locations and heights for a given forecast valid time, rather than another dense forecast field on a fixed grid. We present WindINR, a latent-state implicit neural representation framework for continuous high-resolution local wind query and sparse-observation correction. WindINR maps static terrain descriptors, a low-resolution background field, and continuous query coordinates to a high-resolution wind state through a latent-conditioned decoder. To enable rapid inference-time correction, WindINR separates reusable representation learning from sample-specific latent-state correction. During training, a privileged encoder infers a reference latent state from high-resolution supervision, a deployable latent predictor estimates an initial latent state from inference-time inputs alone, and their discrepancies are summarized into a dataset-adaptive Gaussian prior over latent corrections. At inference time, within the WindINR module, network weights remain fixed and only the latent state is updated by minimizing a regularized correction objective using sparse observations and their uncertainty. In controlled OSSEs over the Senja region, including a UAV-aided approach scenario and random-observation robustness tests, WindINR improves local high-resolution wind estimates by updating only a compact latent state rather than the full network. The corrected representation remains continuously queryable at arbitrary coordinates and, in our CPU benchmark, yields about a $2.6\times$ online-correction speedup over full-network fine-tuning, suggesting a practical interface between kilometer-scale background products, sparse local observations, and wind queries in complex terrain.

</details>


### [291] [Weighted Rules under the Stable Model Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09519)
*Joohyung Lee,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper extends stable model semantics with weighted rules inspired by Markov Logic, enabling probabilistic and preference-based reasoning over answer sets and comparing this approach to other probabilistic logic frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical stable model semantics for answer set programming is deterministic, which limits its ability to handle inconsistency, uncertainty, preferences, and probabilistic reasoning. There is a need for a unified framework that combines answer set programming with statistical and probabilistic reasoning as in Markov Logic.

Method: The authors introduce weighted rules under stable model semantics, following log-linear models similar to those used in Markov Logic. They define how weights affect stable models, enabling ranking and probabilistic interpretation of models. They further develop methods for applying statistical inference over these weighted stable models and give a formal comparison of their framework with existing approaches such as standard ASP, Markov Logic, ProbLog, and P-log.

Result: The proposed framework allows one to: (1) resolve inconsistencies in answer set programs by using weights; (2) rank stable models according to rule weights; (3) associate probabilities to stable models through a log-linear semantics; and (4) apply statistical inference techniques over weighted stable models. They also clarify formally how their approach relates to and differs from ASP, Markov Logic, ProbLog, and P-log.

Conclusion: Weighted rules under stable model semantics provide a flexible and expressive way to introduce probabilistic and preference-based reasoning into ASP. This unifies aspects of logical and statistical inference, supports handling inconsistency and uncertainty, and situates the approach in relation to other probabilistic logic formalisms through formal comparisons.

Abstract: We introduce the concept of weighted rules under the stable model semantics following the log-linear models of Markov Logic. This provides versatile methods to overcome the deterministic nature of the stable model semantics, such as resolving inconsistencies in answer set programs, ranking stable models, associating probability to stable models, and applying statistical inference to computing weighted stable models. We also present formal comparisons with related formalisms, such as answer set programs, Markov Logic, ProbLog, and P-log.

</details>


### [292] [Cplus2ASP: Computing Action Language C+ in Answer Set Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09528)
*Joseph Babb,Joohyung Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Version 2 of Cplus2ASP, a faster system for reasoning in action language C+ using modern answer set programming techniques and an incremental tool chain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a more efficient and feature-rich implementation of the action language C+ than the earlier Causal Calculator, leveraging modern ASP solvers and supporting extensible translations for multiple action languages.

Method: Implements a translation pipeline from C+ (and other action languages) to answer set programs using tools f2lp, clingo, iclingo, and as2transition, with an incremental execution mode that targets iclingo and relies on an extended module theorem for programs with nested expressions; adds features like external atoms through Lua and interactive user mode.

Result: A significantly faster, modular, and extensible system (Cplus2ASP Version 2) that is fully compatible with Causal Calculator V2 input, supports incremental grounding, and enables multi-modal translations for action languages C+, B, and BC, along with practical extensions such as Lua-based external atoms.

Conclusion: Cplus2ASP Version 2 realizes recent theoretical advances in translating action languages to ASP, achieving substantial performance improvements over older systems while maintaining compatibility and adding extensibility and practical features for advanced reasoning tasks in multiple action languages.

Abstract: We present Version 2 of system Cplus2ASP, which implements the definite fragment of action language C+. Its input language is fully compatible with the language of the Causal Calculator Version 2, but the new system is significantly faster thanks to modern answer set solving techniques. The translation implemented in the system is a composition of several recent theoretical results. The system orchestrates a tool chain, consisting of f2lp, clingo, iclingo, and as2transition. Under the incremental execution mode, the system translates a C+ description into the input language of iclingo, exploiting its incremental grounding mechanism. The correctness of this execution is justified by the module theorem extended to programs with nested expressions. In addition, the input language of the system has many useful features, such as external atoms by means of Lua calls and the user interactive mode. The system supports extensible multi-modal translations for other action languages, such as B and BC, as well.

</details>


### [293] [LLM-Guided Monte Carlo Tree Search over Knowledge Graphs: Composing Mechanistic Explanations for Drug-Disease Pairs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09542)
*Rishabh Jakhar,Michel Dumontier,Remzi Celebi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TESSERA, a neuro-symbolic framework that combines LLM guidance, knowledge graphs, and Monte Carlo Tree Search to extract multi-step explanatory paths, demonstrated on drug mechanism discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-step explanation extraction from knowledge graphs is combinatorially hard because candidate paths explode with depth and it is difficult to assign credit to intermediate steps when quality depends on long sequences. While LLMs are strong on knowledge and reasoning, they lack guarantees and struggle with long compositional chains, so there is a need for a method that uses LLM strengths without relying on them for full multi-step generation.

Method: TESSERA is a three-part neuro-symbolic framework. It (1) uses a knowledge graph to define a constrained hypothesis space of candidate explanatory paths; (2) employs Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to manage long-horizon search and perform principled credit assignment via backpropagation; and (3) places LLMs in a restricted, discriminative role: they act as a prior policy to bias MCTS exploration and as a comparative state evaluator to provide reward signals for partial paths, rather than generating entire reasoning chains autonomously.

Result: On the task of elucidating drug mechanisms using two different biomedical knowledge graphs, TESSERA produces explanations that align well with curated biological knowledge while also proposing plausible alternative mechanisms. Ablation studies show that removing or altering either of the LLM-based components (policy prior or state evaluation) degrades performance, indicating that both contribute meaningfully to the system’s effectiveness.

Conclusion: The framework successfully leverages LLMs for local discriminative judgments while preserving structural guarantees via knowledge graphs and effective long-range credit assignment via MCTS. This yields faithful, coherent multi-step explanations in the drug-mechanism domain and suggests a generally applicable paradigm for compositional reasoning over structured knowledge resources.

Abstract: Extracting multi-step explanations from knowledge graphs poses a combinatorial challenge requiring both heuristic guidance (as candidates proliferate with depth) and credit assignment (as path quality emerges over extended sequences). Frontier LLMs, strong on knowledge/reasoning benchmarks, offer a compelling source of such heuristics, yet their knowledge comes sans guarantees and compositional performance degrades as chains lengthen. We thus present TESSERA, a 3-part neuro-symbolic framework that uses LLMs in a circumscribed role: for local discriminative judgement rather than autonomous multi-step generation; the knowledge graph then defines the hypothesis space enforcing hard structural constraints, and MCTS coordinates the long-horizon search with principled credit assignment via backpropagation. LLMs perform dual roles as a prior policy biasing exploration and a comparative state evaluator supplying reward signals. Evaluation on drug mechanism elucidation across two complementary knowledge graphs demonstrates fidelity to curated biology while surfacing coherent alternative mechanisms, with ablations confirming discriminative contribution from both LLM components. Beyond its current application, our framework offers a general paradigm for compositional reasoning over structured knowledge.

</details>


### [294] [TIDE-Bench: Task-Aware and Diagnostic Evaluation of Tool-Integrated Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09544)
*Yize Li,Junzhi Li,Jason Song,Chuxiong Sun,Rui Wang,Changwen Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TIDE-Bench, a unified, high-quality benchmark to systematically evaluate tool-integrated reasoning (TIR) methods for large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for tool-integrated reasoning are fragmented and limited: they use mixed-quality datasets, cover few task types, lack detailed diagnostics beyond final accuracy, and are expensive to run at scale. This makes it hard to fairly compare TIR methods or pinpoint their weaknesses, especially in complex tool usage and coordination scenarios.

Method: The authors design TIDE-Bench, a benchmark that: (1) integrates multiple task categories, including standard math reasoning and knowledge-intensive QA plus two new categories—tool-grounded experimental design and dynamic interactive tasks—to stress complex tool use and multi-tool coordination; (2) defines a task-aware evaluation protocol that simultaneously assesses final answer correctness, reliability of the reasoning process, tool-use efficiency (e.g., number and quality of calls), and computational cost across different task types; and (3) curates compact but challenging evaluation sets by systematically filtering out low-discrimination examples from existing datasets, thereby keeping only instances that distinguish among models while reducing evaluation cost.

Result: Running extensive experiments with several base models and TIR approaches on TIDE-Bench, the authors obtain rich diagnostic results, showing that even strong systems struggle notably with aspects of tool grounding and coordinated tool use. The benchmark effectively highlights performance differences and bottlenecks that are not obvious under simpler or less discriminative evaluations.

Conclusion: TIDE-Bench serves as a holistic, efficient, and discriminative benchmark for tool-integrated reasoning, enabling more reliable comparison of methods and deeper analysis of their strengths and weaknesses. The findings underscore that tool grounding remains a key unsolved challenge, and the benchmark provides a foundation for driving future research on more robust, efficient TIR systems.

Abstract: Tool-integrated reasoning has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing large language models with external computation, retrieval, and execution capabilities. However, the field still lacks a high-quality and unified evaluation benchmark, and existing TIR evaluations remain limited in dataset quality, task diversity, diagnostic comprehensiveness, and evaluation efficiency. In this work, we introduce TIDE-Bench, a holistic and efficient benchmark for evaluating TIR methods, featuring three key advantages. First, it provides diverse task settings, combining widely used mathematical reasoning and knowledge-intensive QA tasks with two newly designed tasks, namely the tool-grounded experimental design task and the dynamic interactive task, to probe models' abilities in complex tool invocation and multi-tool coordination. Second, TIDE-Bench adopts a comprehensive yet task-aware evaluation protocol, jointly measuring final answer quality, process reliability, tool-use efficiency, and inference cost across heterogeneous task settings. Third, TIDE-Bench constructs high-quality and discriminative evaluation sets by filtering low-discrimination instances from existing datasets, substantially reducing evaluation cost while focusing on more challenging samples. Extensive experiments on multiple foundation models and TIR methods reveal persistent bottlenecks in tool grounding, offering insights for future TIR research.

</details>


### [295] [PDEAgent-Bench: A Multi-Metric, Multi-Library Benchmark for PDE Solver Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09636)
*Zhen Hang,Yushan Yashengjiang,Junhui Li,Huanshuo Dong,Yang Wei,Zhezheng Hao,Jiangtao Ma,Songlin Bai,Haozhong Kai,Xihang Yue,Gangzong Si,Dongming Jiang,Chao Yao,Zhanhua Hu,Jiangqing Zhang,Pengwei Liu,Yaomin Shen,Xingyu Ren,Lei Liu,Zikang Xu,Han Li,Qingsong Yao,Hande Dong,Hong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PDEAgent-Bench, a new benchmark to evaluate automatic code generation of numerical PDE solvers, with multi-step checks for executability, accuracy, and efficiency across several FEM libraries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is growing interest in automatically generating numerical solvers for PDEs from high-level problem descriptions using LLMs and code agents. However, existing benchmarks either focus on generic code-generation or only check for syntactic correctness and simple test cases, failing to capture the core numerical challenges of PDE solving. In particular, there is no public benchmark tailored to PDE-to-solver code generation that assesses whether generated codes are accurate, efficient, and compatible with professional FEM libraries. This gap makes it difficult to rigorously measure progress or compare systems for this important scientific-computing application.

Method: The authors construct PDEAgent-Bench, a benchmark collection of 645 PDE problem instances categorized into 6 mathematical types and 11 PDE families. For each instance, they provide: (1) a textual, agent-facing PDE problem specification; (2) a reference numerical solution on a fixed evaluation grid; and (3) instance-specific targets for solution accuracy and runtime. The benchmark spans three widely used finite-element method libraries (DOLFINx, Firedrake, deal.II). They define a staged evaluation pipeline: first verify that generated code compiles and runs, then check its numerical accuracy against the reference solution, and finally measure computational efficiency against runtime thresholds. They then test representative large language models and code agents using this pipeline.

Result: The evaluation shows that current LLMs and code agents can frequently generate solver code that is syntactically valid and executable for the benchmark problems. However, when stricter requirements are imposed—matching the reference solution within given accuracy thresholds and satisfying runtime constraints—the success rates drop sharply. This reveals a significant gap between producing runnable code and producing numerically reliable and efficient PDE solvers. The benchmark itself is demonstrated to run reproducibly across the selected FEM libraries and problem instances.

Conclusion: PDEAgent-Bench fills a missing need for a dedicated, realistic benchmark for PDE-to-solver code generation that goes beyond syntax and example-based testing to address real numerical performance criteria. The poor performance of current agents under full evaluation suggests that existing LLM-based systems are not yet ready to reliably automate PDE solver development in practice. The authors propose PDEAgent-Bench as a standardized, reproducible testbed to drive future research on agents that better understand PDE structure, choose appropriate discretizations and solver settings, and generate accurate, efficient, and library-compatible numerical solvers.

Abstract: PDE-to-solver code generation aims to automatically synthesize executable numerical solvers from partial differential equation (PDE) specifications. This task requires not only understanding the mathematical structure of PDEs, but also selecting appropriate discretization schemes and solver configurations, and correctly implementing the resulting formulations in finite-element method (FEM) libraries. Existing code generation benchmarks mainly evaluate syntactic correctness, or success on predefined test cases. To our knowledge, there is currently no publicly available benchmark specifically for PDE-to-solver code generation, and general-purpose code benchmarks do not fully capture the unique challenges of numerical PDE solution, such as ensuring solver accuracy, efficiency, and compatibility with professional FEM libraries. We introduce PDEAgent-Bench, to the best of our knowledge, the first multi-metric, multi-library benchmark for PDE-to-solver code generation. PDEAgent-Bench contains 645 instances across 6 mathematical categories and 11 PDE families, with common FEM libraries for DOLFINx, Firedrake, and deal.II. Each instance provides an agent-facing problem specification, a reference solution on a prescribed evaluation grid, and case-specific accuracy and runtime targets. PDEAgent-Bench adopts a staged evaluation framework in which generated solvers must sequentially pass executability, numerical accuracy, and computational efficiency checks. Experiments with representative LLMs and code agents show that models can often produce runnable code, but their pass rate drops substantially once accuracy and efficiency requirements are enforced. These results indicate that current agents remain limited in producing numerically reliable and efficient PDE solvers, and that PDEAgent-Bench provides a reproducible testbed grounded in the practical requirements of numerical PDE solving.

</details>


### [296] [Workspace Optimization: How to Train Your Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09650)
*Elad Sarafian,Gal Kaplun,Ron Banner,Daniel Soudry,Boris Ginsburg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'workspace optimization'—training an LLM-based agent not by updating its weights, but by improving the external structured workspace it uses to think and act—demonstrated via a multi-agent system (DreamTeam) that achieves new SOTA on ARC-AGI-3 with fewer actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frontier language models used as agents are often frozen and cannot have their weights fine-tuned in deployed environments. Many challenging tasks, especially multi-turn interactive ones like ARC-AGI-3 puzzles, require learning and adaptation beyond a single forward pass. The authors ask what can still be trained if weights are fixed, and propose that the external reasoning substrate—the workspace the agent manipulates—can be systematically optimized to improve performance and efficiency.

Method: The authors formalize 'workspace optimization' as an analogue to weight-space training: artifacts correspond to parameters, evidence to data, counterexamples to losses, and textual feedback to gradients. They design DreamTeam, a structured multi-agent system for ARC-AGI-3 where specialized roles (world-model builder, planner, hypothesis generator, probe designer, strategist, failure router, etc.) iteratively write, read, and refine an executable world model and solution hypotheses in a shared workspace. Through interaction with the environment and textual feedback loops, the workspace evolves to encode better problem decompositions and solution strategies, without changing the underlying LLM weights.

Result: On the public 25-game ARC-AGI-3 benchmark using the official scoring protocol, DreamTeam improves the score of the previous protocol-matched state-of-the-art agent from 36% to 38.4%. It also reduces environment interactions, using 31% fewer actions per game on average, based on results averaged over two independent runs.

Conclusion: The study shows that substantial gains on hard multi-turn reasoning tasks can be achieved by optimizing the agent’s external workspace rather than its model weights. The proposed workspace optimization framework, instantiated in the DreamTeam multi-agent system, yields state-of-the-art performance and improved sample efficiency on ARC-AGI-3. This supports the view that carefully designed and trainable external structures, roles, and interaction patterns are a powerful complement—or alternative—to traditional weight fine-tuning for frontier LLM agents.

Abstract: Modern agents built on frontier language models often cannot adapt their weights. What, then, remains trainable? We argue it is the agent's \emph{workspace}, the structured external substrate it reads, writes, and tests; we call its evolution workspace optimization. Workspace optimization targets hard multi-turn environments where a frontier model has strong priors but cannot solve the task in a single shot, so the agent must learn through interaction. We propose a principled way to evolve the workspace, mirroring the structure of weight-space training: artifacts in place of parameters, evidence in place of data, counterexamples in place of losses, and textual feedback in place of gradients. We instantiate the idea in DreamTeam, a multi-agent harness for ARC-AGI-3 whose roles build an executable world model, plan, hypothesize, probe, strategize, and route failures. On the current 25-game ARC-AGI-3 public set under the official scoring protocol and averaged over two independent runs, DreamTeam improves the SOTA protocol-matched agent's score from 36% to 38.4%, while using 31% fewer environment actions per game.

</details>


### [297] [CodeClinic: Evaluating Automation of Coding Skills for Clinical Reasoning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09675)
*Timothy Ossowski,Xinchi Liu,Danyal Maqbool,Vaibhav Dhanuka,Sheng Zhang,Hoifung Poon,Majid Afshar,Tyler Bradshaw,Junjie Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CodeClinic is a benchmark and framework to test whether LLM agents can synthesize reusable clinical reasoning skills for ICU monitoring and EHR-based tasks, instead of relying on fixed, manually curated toolboxes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based clinical agents depend on manually built collections of tools/skills (e.g., sepsis detection, organ failure scoring). These are costly to maintain, brittle across institutions and policies, and zero-shot LLM reasoning or code generation is often inefficient and unreliable. There is a need to systematically evaluate and improve LLMs’ ability to automatically create robust, reusable clinical skills from guidelines and data.

Method: The authors construct CodeClinic, a benchmark on MIMIC-IV with two tasks: (1) longitudinal ICU surveillance, where agents make structured decisions every four hours over patient trajectories on 25 findings across eight clinical families; and (2) compositional information seeking, a 63k-instance suite of 259 tasks across nine domains, stratified by dependency depth to test multi-step, compositional reasoning. They also design an offline autoformalization pipeline that transforms natural-language clinical guidelines into verified Python skill libraries via iterative LLM refinement, and compare these libraries against zero-shot code generation baselines.

Result: The autoformalized Python skill libraries produced by the pipeline yield more consistent reasoning behavior than zero-shot code generation and reduce per-query token usage by up to 40%. On the benchmark, these reusable skills support more reliable clinical reasoning and more efficient LLM-agent operation under varied, institution-specific policies.

Conclusion: LLM agents can benefit substantially from autoformalized, reusable clinical skill libraries rather than ad hoc zero-shot code generation or fixed, manually curated tools. CodeClinic provides a systematic way to evaluate such agents on longitudinal monitoring and compositional information seeking, and the proposed pipeline shows that clinical guidelines can be converted into efficient, verifiable code that improves both consistency and token efficiency in clinical reasoning workflows.

Abstract: Clinical reasoning agents based on large language models (LLMs) aim to automate tasks such as intensive care unit (ICU) monitoring and patient state tracking from electronic health records (EHRs). Existing systems typically rely on manually curated clinical tools or skills for concepts such as sepsis detection and organ failure assessment. However, maintaining these tool libraries requires substantial expert effort, while zero-shot querying or code generation often produces inefficient and unreliable reasoning chains, especially under institution-specific clinical policies. We introduce CodeClinic, a benchmark built on MIMIC-IV for evaluating whether LLM agents can synthesize and compose reusable clinical skills instead of relying on fixed toolboxes. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: longitudinal ICU surveillance and compositional information seeking. The longitudinal setting simulates monitoring patient trajectories with structured decisions every four hours across 25 findings and eight clinical families, while the compositional setting spans 63k instances across 259 tasks in nine domains and is stratified by compositional dependency depth to evaluate increasingly complex multi-step reasoning. We further propose an offline autoformalization pipeline that converts natural-language clinical guidelines into reusable and verified Python skill libraries through iterative LLM refinement. Compared with zero-shot code generation, the resulting libraries improve consistency while reducing per-query token usage by up to 40%.

</details>


### [298] [Absurd World: A Simple Yet Powerful Method to Absurdify the Real-world for Probing LLM Reasoning Capabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09678)
*Ryan Albright,Golam Md Muktadir,Zarif Ikram,S M Jubaer,Mehrab Hossain,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Absurd World, a benchmark that tests whether LLMs can do simple, logically coherent reasoning when real‑world patterns are systematically altered.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing work mainly stresses LLMs with ever more complex or adversarial reasoning tasks, but pays less attention to whether models are robust on simple logical reasoning once surface real‑world patterns are changed. The authors want to know if LLMs can truly reason abstractly rather than just mimic familiar real‑world correlations, and thus need a way to decouple reasoning skill from real‑world knowledge and pattern matching.

Method: They design Absurd World, a benchmarking framework that takes real‑world scenarios and decomposes them into symbols, actions, sequences, and events. These elements are automatically altered to form "absurd" but still logically coherent worlds where the underlying reasoning structure of tasks is unchanged. The benchmark then evaluates many LLMs under different prompting strategies, measuring how well they can track and apply the original logic despite the unfamiliar, unrealistic surface details.

Result: Experiments on a wide range of LLMs and prompting methods show that Absurd World reliably exposes whether models can maintain correct logical reasoning when real‑world priors are no longer usable. The benchmark differentiates models and prompts in terms of their robustness to altered realism, highlighting notable performance gaps that are not apparent on conventional benchmarks tied closely to real‑world content.

Conclusion: Absurd World is an effective tool for assessing LLMs’ genuine logical reasoning ability, independent of memorized real‑world patterns. It enables systematic, large‑scale testing of a model’s robustness to task variations derived from the same underlying logical structure, helping practitioners verify and improve the reasoning reliability of LLMs in practical applications.

Abstract: While extremely powerful and versatile at various tasks, the thinking capabilities of large language models (LLMs) are often put under scrutiny as they sometimes fail to solve problems that humans can systematically solve. However, recent literature focuses on breaking LLM reasoning with increasingly complex problems, and whether an LLM is robust in simple logical reasoning remains underexplored. This paper proposes Absurd World, a benchmarking framework, to test LLMs against altered realism, where scenarios are logically coherent, and humans can easily solve the tasks. Absurd World breaks a real-world model into symbols, actions, sequences, and events, which are automatically altered to create absurd worlds where the logic to solve the tasks remains the same. It evaluates a large collection of models with simple and advanced prompting techniques, and proves that it is an effective tool to determine LLMs' ability to think logically, ignoring the patterns learned from the real world. One can use this framework to extensively test an LLM against a real-world problem to verify whether the LLM's reasoning capability is robust against variations of the task.

</details>


### [299] [Unpredictability dissociates from structured control in language agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09692)
*Jia Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that unpredictable, stochastic behavior in language agents does not reproduce or substitute for structured, reason-linked action control, based on lesion studies and behavioral evaluations across multiple models and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: People sometimes treat randomness or unpredictability in AI agents as if it were evidence of genuine control, agency, or reasoning. The authors want to test whether simply increasing stochasticity (randomness) in a language agent can stand in for carefully designed control mechanisms that link reasons, memory, internal state, and inhibition to action selection.

Method: They implement a language-agent architecture whose internal control components (reasoning module, veto/inhibition, etc.) can be selectively disabled (lesioned). They compare variants: a structured-control agent, a high-stochasticity comparator, and various lesion or control conditions (reason lesion, veto lesion, no-fields, scrambled-context, distribution-matched, post-hoc, verbosity controls). They run extensive evaluations across multiple datasets, task families, models (including Qwen2.5 and Mistral-7B variants), and scaffolds. Behavioral tests include removing free-form traces from evaluation to avoid bias and using blinded human annotation. They also attempt strict entropy and token/compute matching and a counterfactual-flip stress test, but these do not fully pass their own criteria and are listed as limitations.

Result: Across a large number of runs and datasets, the structured-control agent consistently shows stronger coupling between reasons, memory, self-state, inhibition, and actions than the high-stochasticity or lesioned/ablated variants. High stochasticity produces more unpredictable behavior but fails to recreate structured action control. Controls that remove or scramble context or try to match output distributions similarly fail to recover the structured behavioral profiles. Human annotators show high agreement in judging these differences. Some more demanding matching tests could not be fully satisfied and are marked as limitations.

Conclusion: Stochastic unpredictability alone cannot substitute for structured, reason-based action control in the tested language agents. Genuine action-coupled control requires explicit, structured mechanisms rather than relying on randomness, even when randomness is finely tuned or matched by distributional metrics. The work suggests that evaluations of AI control and agency should look for structured coupling between internal reasons and actions, not just unpredictability or entropy levels.

Abstract: Unpredictable behavior is often taken as evidence of control, yet stochastic dispersion and structured action control need not coincide. This paper tests whether stochastic sampling can substitute for structured mechanisms that couple reasons, memory, self-state and inhibition to action selection in a language-agent implementation whose control components can be selectively disabled. In a seven-dataset baseline lesion matrix comprising 74,352 calls, the high-stochasticity comparator was more unpredictable than the structured-control variant in 7/7 datasets, whereas targeted reason and veto lesions reduced the expected structured-control profiles in 7/7 datasets each. In a matched-interface control spanning 26,946 generations, the structured agent maintained stronger action-field coupling than all stochastic, post-hoc, scrambled and verbosity controls across every dataset. The primary behavioral test removed free-form trace wording from the evaluation: 57,816 scored records showed the structured-control variant exceeding the high-stochasticity comparator or the reason/veto lesions in 7/7 datasets for all predefined behavioral components. Later open-weight runs extended the no-context controls to Qwen2.5 7B, 14B and 32B and to an independent Mistral-7B family across 20 task families and three agent scaffolds; no-fields, scrambled-context and distribution-matched controls failed to recover structured action control. A three-annotator blinded audit over 1,200 overlap items preserved high agreement. Strict entropy matching, strict token/compute matching and a formal counterfactual-flip stress test did not meet their gates and are treated as limitations. Stochastic unpredictability did not reproduce structured, action-coupled control in this implemented agent family.

</details>


### [300] [Ambig-DS: A Benchmark for Task-Framing Ambiguity in Data-Science Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09698)
*Josefa Lia Stoisser,Marc Boubnovski Martell,Sidsel Boldsen,Kaspar Märtens,Robert Kitchen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Ambig-DS, diagnostic benchmarks showing that data-science agents often silently commit to incorrect task framings when problem specifications are ambiguous, and that recognizing ambiguity—not executing pipelines—is a key bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As data-science agents become more autonomous, they can fail by silently misinterpreting under-specified tasks while still producing seemingly valid, executable pipelines. Existing benchmarks mostly check whether code runs and scores well, not whether the agent correctly understood the prediction target or evaluation objective. The authors aim to systematically expose and measure these framing failures.

Method: The authors build two diagnostic suites from existing DS-agent benchmarks: Ambig-DS-Target (51 tabular modeling tasks from DSBench with ambiguous prediction targets) and Ambig-DS-Objective (61 tasks from MLE-bench with ambiguous evaluation objectives). For each original, fully specified task, they create an ambiguous variant by controlled edits. A combined human-and-LLM verification pipeline ensures each ambiguous task genuinely supports multiple reasonable interpretations with materially different decisions. They then evaluate five data-science agents of varying capability on both the original and ambiguous tasks, measuring performance drops and characterizing failure types. They also test an intervention where agents are allowed to ask one clarifying question, under different prompting styles (permissive vs conservative).

Result: Ambiguity reliably reduces performance on both suites across all five agents. Failures rarely appear as execution errors; instead, agents submit clean but misframed solutions: wrong prediction targets in Ambig-DS-Target and wrong or overly generic evaluation choices in Ambig-DS-Objective. When agents are allowed to ask a single clarifying question under idealized conditions, they can recover much of the performance loss, implying that missing framing information is a key driver of degradation. However, agents do not accurately detect when clarification is needed: permissive prompting leads to unnecessary questions on clear tasks, while conservative prompting causes them to silently proceed on ambiguous ones.

Conclusion: The main bottleneck for current data-science agents lies not in building or executing ML pipelines but in recognizing when the prediction target or evaluation objective is under-specified. Ambig-DS exposes a silent misframing failure mode that existing benchmarks overlook and shows that even capable agents struggle to decide when to seek clarification. Improving agents’ ability to detect and resolve task ambiguity is essential for safe, reliable auto-pilot-style data-science systems and should be a core focus of future evaluation and method development.

Abstract: As data-science agents shift from co-pilots to auto-pilots, silent misframing becomes a critical failure mode. Agents quietly commit to plausible but unintended task framings, producing clean, executable artifacts that hide their incorrect assessment of the task. Existing benchmarks score whether the pipeline runs, ignoring whether the agent recognized the task was underspecified. We introduce Ambig-DS, two diagnostic suites: one for prediction-target ambiguity (Ambig-DS-Target, 51 tasks built on DSBench, a tabular modeling benchmark) and one for evaluation-objective ambiguity (Ambig-DS-Objective, 61 tasks built on MLE-bench, a Kaggle-style ML competition benchmark), constructed so that scoring uses each source benchmark's original evaluator. For every task we pair the original, fully specified version with an ambiguous variant produced by controlled edits; a human-and-LLM verification pipeline confirms each variant admits multiple plausible interpretations with decision-relevant consequences. The suites are analyzed independently and ambiguity lowers performance in both. Across five agents spanning efficient to frontier-class models, we find in our controlled diagnostic setting: (i) failures are silent commitments: wrong-target submissions on Target, wrong-metric or non-committal baseline submissions on Objective, rather than execution errors; (ii) allowing the agent to ask one clarifying question recovers much of the loss under idealized conditions, suggesting missing framing information drives a substantial part of the observed degradation; but (iii) agents cannot reliably tell when to use it: permissive prompts induce over-asking on clear tasks, while conservative prompts induce silent defaulting on ambiguous ones. Recognizing target and objective underspecification, not pipeline execution, is the bottleneck missing from standard DS-agent evaluations.

</details>


### [301] [Medical Model Synthesis Architectures: A Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09716)
*Katherine M. Collins,Marlene Berke,Ilia Sucholutsky,Ayman Ali,Adrian Weller,Timothy J. O'Donnell,Tyler Brooke-Wilson,Lionel Wong,Joshua B. Tenenbaum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MedMSA, a framework that combines language models with explicit probabilistic modeling to make transparent, calibrated clinical predictions under uncertainty, demonstrated on differential diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical decision-making involves high-stakes uncertainty, and current AI systems are both poorly calibrated under uncertainty and opaque in their reasoning. There is a need for AI tools that provide clinically useful support while making their reasoning process and uncertainty explicit, auditable, and trustworthy.

Method: The authors propose MedMSA, which first uses language models to retrieve and organize relevant medical knowledge for a given clinical case, then encodes this knowledge into a formal probabilistic model. This model supports Bayesian-style inference to quantify and propagate uncertainty, generating predictions (e.g., diagnoses) with associated probabilities and making the structure of the reasoning process explicit and verifiable.

Result: A proof-of-concept implementation of MedMSA is demonstrated on differential diagnosis tasks. Given patient symptoms, the system generates a ranked list of possible diagnoses with explicit uncertainty weights (probabilities), showing that the framework can produce calibrated and interpretable diagnostic suggestions, at least on example cases discussed in the paper.

Conclusion: MedMSA illustrates that combining language models with explicit probabilistic modeling can yield AI systems that are both practically useful and formally transparent in clinical settings. The authors argue that this approach offers a promising direction for safe, trustworthy human-AI collaboration in medicine and outline potential extensions to broader clinical tasks beyond differential diagnosis.

Abstract: Medicine is rife with high-stakes uncertainty. Doctors routinely make clinical judgments and decisions that juggle many fundamental unknowns, like predictions about what might be causing a patients' symptoms or decisions about what treatment to try next. Despite increasing interest in developing AI systems that aid or even replace doctors in clinical settings, current systems struggle with calibrated reasoning under uncertainty, and are often deeply opaque about their reasoning. We propose a framework for AI systems that can make practically useful but formally transparent clinical predictions under uncertainty. Given a clinical situation, our framework (MedMSA) uses language models to retrieve relevant prior knowledge, but constructs a formal probabilistic model to support calibrated and verifiable inferences under uncertainty. We show how an initial proof-of-concept of this framework can be used for differential diagnosis, producing an uncertainty-weighted list of potential diagnoses that could explain a patients' symptoms, and discuss future applications and directions for applying this framework more generally for safe clinical collaborations.

</details>


### [302] [Primal-Dual Guided Decoding for Constrained Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09749)
*Federico Tomasi,Dmitrii Moor,Alice Wang,Mounia Lalmas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce a decoding method for discrete diffusion models that enforces global constraints during generation using a primal-dual optimization framework with KL regularization, improving constraint satisfaction without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing discrete diffusion models can generate structured sequences but struggle to reliably satisfy global property constraints (e.g., topic relevance, molecular properties, playlist attributes) during generation, and current solutions often require retraining, extra model evaluations, or lack guarantees on constraint violation.

Method: They formulate constrained sequence generation as a KL-regularised optimization problem and solve it online using a primal-dual algorithm with adaptive Lagrangian multipliers. During each denoising step of the discrete diffusion process, they add a constraint-dependent bias to token logits. The Lagrange multipliers are updated via mirror descent based on the observed constraint violations, leading to an additive correction that corresponds to the optimal KL-regularised projection of the constrained distribution onto the model’s original distribution. The method works at inference time only, needs no retraining, no extra model evaluations, handles multiple constraints, and offers theoretical bounds on constraint violations.

Result: On three tasks—topical text generation, molecular design, and music playlist generation—the method improves the satisfaction rate of specified constraints while maintaining comparable or better task-specific quality metrics relative to baselines that do not use this constrained decoding scheme.

Conclusion: Primal-dual guided decoding is an effective, general-purpose inference-time technique for enforcing global constraints in discrete diffusion models. By posing constrained generation as a KL-regularised optimisation and solving it with adaptive Lagrangian multipliers, it offers a principled way to bias sampling toward constraint-satisfying sequences without retraining the model or sacrificing output quality, and it can be instantiated across domains via appropriate scoring functions.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models generate structured sequences by progressively unmasking tokens, but enforcing global property constraints during generation remains an open challenge. We propose primal-dual guided decoding, an inference-time method that formulates constrained generation as a KL-regularised optimisation problem and solves it online via adaptive Lagrangian multipliers. At each denoising step, the method modifies token logits through an additive, constraint-dependent bias, with multipliers updated by mirror descent based on constraint violation. The bias arises as the optimal KL-regularised projection of the constraint, so the constrained distribution remains as close as possible to the model's unconstrained distribution while still satisfying the constraint. The method requires no retraining and no additional model evaluations beyond standard sampling, supports multiple simultaneous constraints, and provides formal bounds on constraint violation. We evaluate our approach on topical text generation, molecular design, and music playlist generation, showing that a single algorithm instantiated via domain-specific scoring functions improves constraint satisfaction while preserving relevant domain-specific quality metrics.

</details>


### [303] [UTS at PsyDefDetect: Multi-Agent Councils and Absence-Based Reasoning for Defense Mechanism Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09769)
*Dima Galat,Marian-Andrei Rizoiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a multi-agent LLM system plus small fine-tuned models to detect psychological defense mechanisms in support chats, getting 2nd place in a shared task and analyzing key error patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
