Motivation: Automatically identifying psychological defense mechanisms in emotional support dialogues is hard because defenses are subtle, often defined by what is missing (affect, cognition, contact with reality). Existing methods underuse clinical theory and struggle with imbalanced, fine-grained DMRS categories. The authors want a system that is both accurate and psychologically interpretable for this benchmark task.

Method: They represent defense mechanisms along an affect–cognition integration spectrum, then encode this as explicit, prompt-level clinical rules. The core architecture is a multi-phase “deliberative council” of Gemini 2.5 agents where each class has an advocate that rates evidence strength instead of casting a single vote. This yields strong performance even without fine-tuning. They then diagnose systematic errors: especially a bias where emotional content collapses into a majority class (“L7 attractor”). To fix this, they add a targeted override ensemble using three fine-tuned Qwen3.5 models. A structured multi-agent system (builder, critic, regression guard) designs and selects 16 high-value overrides that are triggered only in specific error-prone situations.

Result: Their system places 2nd out of 64 teams with an F1 score of 0.406. Prompt-level clinical rules tied to the affect–cognition spectrum provide the single biggest improvement (+11.4 percentage points F1). The Gemini-based deliberative council alone reaches F1 0.382 without fine-tuning, ranking in the top 5 systems. Error analysis shows that 59–80% of stable predictions on minority classes are wrong due to a strong bias toward a dominant “L7” emotional class. The targeted Qwen3.5 override ensemble corrects some of these cases, adding +2.4 percentage points F1, and the multi-agent design loop yields larger gains in one cycle than eight previous attempts.

Conclusion: Clinically grounded prompt rules and multi-agent LLM deliberation can perform competitively on complex psychological labeling tasks, even without fine-tuning. However, such systems exhibit systematic biases, particularly against minority classes, which require targeted architectural interventions. A small, carefully designed override ensemble of fine-tuned models—constructed and guarded by a multi-agent design process—can effectively mitigate these biases and yield further performance gains. The work highlights the value of modeling what is absent in the text (missing affect, blocked cognition, denial of reality) and of using structured, role-based LLM agents for both prediction and system design.

Abstract: This paper describes our system for classifying psychological defense mechanisms in emotional support dialogues using the Defense Mechanism Rating Scales (DMRS), placing second (F1 0.406) among 64 teams.1 A central insight is that defense mechanisms are defined by what is absent: missing affect, blocked cognition, denied reality. We encode this as an affect-cognition integration spectrum in prompt-level clinical rules, which account for the largest single gain (+11.4pp F1). Our architecture is a multi-phase deliberative council of Gemini 2.5 agents where class-specific advocates rate evidence strength rather than voting, achieving F1 0.382 with no fine-tuning - a top-5 result on its own. We find, however, that the council is confidently wrong about minority classes: 59-80% of stable minority predictions are incorrect, driven by a systematic "L7 attractor" in which emotional content defaults to the majority class. A targeted override ensemble from three fine-tuned Qwen3.5 models applies 16 overrides (+2.4pp), selected by a structured multi-agent system (builder, critic, regression guard) that produced a larger F1 gain in one iteration than 8 prior attempts combined.

</details>


### [304] [Marrying Generative Model of Healthcare Events with Digital Twin of Social Determinants of Health for Disease Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09771)
*Ziquan Wei,Tingting Dan,Guorong Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a generative diffusion-based model that links multi-organ imaging traits, clinical event data, and ICD-coded social determinants of health proxies to better simulate and predict disease trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing disease prediction generative models mainly rely on hospital and registry event-level data and typically ignore explicit modeling of social determinants of health (SDoH), which are crucial for understanding multifactorial human diseases. This omission limits personalization and clinical decision support, especially since current use of SDoH is often restricted to coarse ICD-coded proxies without integrated modeling alongside rich sensor-derived data like imaging and plasma biomarkers.

Method: The authors propose a conditioned latent diffusion framework that connects multi-organ sensor data (e.g., imaging traits of brain, heart, liver, kidney) with tokenized healthcare events, explicitly incorporating ICD-coded SDoH proxies. A novel geometric diffusion model is designed to capture the temporal evolution of complex structured data such as brain connectivity graphs, complemented by diffusion models for tabular traits from other organs. These components are integrated with digitized SDoH proxies in a unified generative model, termed \modelname{}, for simulating disease trajectories and interventions in silico.

Result: Using the UK Biobank dataset, which includes tens of thousands of organ-specific imaging samples and nearly 500k longitudinal medical history sequences, the proposed \modelname{} significantly outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive models for human disease prediction and existing imaging trait generative baselines, indicating superior capability in modeling multi-organ traits and disease progression when SDoH proxies are included.

Conclusion: Incorporating ICD-coded proxies for social determinants of health into a conditioned latent diffusion framework that models both complex geometric brain networks and tabular organ traits yields more powerful generative models for disease reasoning and trajectory prediction. The approach demonstrates that jointly modeling sensor data, clinical events, and SDoH can enhance personalized disease modeling and enable simulated intervention analysis, suggesting a promising direction for future clinical decision support systems.

Abstract: Despite the central role of sensor-derived measurements such as imaging traits and plasma biomarkers in biomedical research and clinical practice, existing generative models for disease prediction largely depend on event-level representations from hospital and registry data. Given the multi-factorial nature of human disease, the absence of explicit modeling of social determinants of health (SDoH), even in the limited form of ICD-coded proxies (chapters Z and V--Y in ICD-10), limits the capacity for personalized disease modeling and clinical decision support. To address this limitation, we propose a generative model with ICD-coded proxies of SDoH for \textit{in silico} modeling of disease reasoning, a conditioned latent diffusion framework that establishes the connection between multi-organ sensor data with tokenized healthcare events. Specifically, we introduce a novel geometric diffusion model to characterize the temporal evolution of complex data representation such as brain networks (region-to-region connectivity encoded in a graph), in parallel with diffusion models for tabular data from other organ systems. Together, we integrate the generative model with digitalized SDoH proxies (coined \modelname{}) for simulated intervention and reasoning of future disease trajectories. We conduct extensive experiments on the UK Biobank (UKB) dataset, which contains organ-specific imaging traits, including brain (44,834), heart (23,987), liver (28,722), and kidney (32,155), along with nearly 500k medical history sequences (age range: 25$\sim$89 years). Our \modelname{} achieves significant improvements over state-of-the-art human disease autoregressive models and imaging trait generative baselines.

</details>


### [305] [Attribution-based Explanations for Markov Decision Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09780)
*Paul Kobialka,Andrea Pferscher,Francesco Leofante,Erika Ábrahám,Silvia Lizeth Tapia Tarifa,Einar Broch Johnsen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They extend attribution methods to Markov Decision Processes, defining and computing importance scores for states and paths to explain sequential decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attribution methods explain AI model outputs for static inputs, but they do not handle sequential decision-making where outcomes depend on sequences of states and actions (like in MDPs). There is a need for explainability tailored to such settings.

Method: They formally define what attributions should mean in MDPs and propose techniques that assign importance scores to individual states and entire execution paths. These scores are computed using strategy synthesis methods that can efficiently handle the MDP’s nondeterminism.

Result: They successfully compute state and path importance scores in MDPs across five case studies, showing that their method scales and can be applied in practice.

Conclusion: Attribution-based explanations can be extended to MDPs by formally defining state and path importance and computing these via strategy synthesis, yielding interpretable insights into how sequential decision-making agents operate.

Abstract: Attribution techniques explain the outcome of an AI model by assigning a numerical score to its inputs. So far, these techniques have mainly focused on attributing importance to static input features at a single point in time, and thus fail to generalize to sequential decision-making settings. This paper fills this gap by introducing techniques to generate attribution-based explanations for Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). We give a formal characterization of what attributions should represent in MDPs, focusing on explanations that assign importance scores to both individual states and execution paths. We show how importance scores can be computed by leveraging techniques for strategy synthesis, enabling the efficient computation of these scores despite the non-determinism inherent in an MDP. We evaluate our approach on five case-studies, demonstrating its utility in providing interpretable insights into the logic of sequential decision-making agents.

</details>


### [306] [EnactToM: An Evolving Benchmark for Functional Theory of Mind in Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09826)
*Gurusha Juneja,Dylan Lu,Saaket Agashe,Parth Diwane,Edward Gunn,Jayanth Srinivasa,Gaowen Liu,William Yang Wang,Yali Du,Xin Eric Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces EnactToM, a benchmark for testing AI agents' functional Theory of Mind in embodied multi-agent 3D environments, revealing that current frontier models fail at practical epistemic coordination despite doing moderately well on explicit belief questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Theory of Mind benchmarks mainly evaluate literal ToM through explicit belief questions, which do not capture the ability to use beliefs for effective coordination and action in complex, partially observable, multi-agent settings. There is a need to measure and challenge AI agents' capacity to act on implicit beliefs—functional ToM—especially in realistic, embodied environments.

Method: The authors design EnactToM, a suite of 300 multi-agent tasks in a 3D household environment with partial observability, private information, and limited communication. Tasks are formally verified for solvability and for the level of epistemic reasoning (epistemic depth) required. The benchmark is adaptive: as models improve, harder tasks are automatically generated. They evaluate seven state-of-the-art models on both functional task completion and explicit theory-of-mind style belief probes, and conduct manual failure analysis of a sample of episodes.

Result: On the hardest subset of tasks, all seven frontier models achieve 0.0% Pass^3 (success across three independent runs) on functional task completion, while reaching an average of 45.0% accuracy on explicit belief questions. Manual inspection attributes 93% of sampled failures to epistemic coordination issues, such as agents not sharing crucial information, disregarding their partner's limitations, or using communication channels inefficiently.

Conclusion: The study shows a large gap between AI models' performance on literal ToM probes and their ability to deploy Theory of Mind in embodied, collaborative tasks. EnactToM exposes systematic failures in epistemic coordination and offers a formally grounded, evolving benchmark to drive future research toward practical, functional Theory of Mind in multi-agent AI systems.

Abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM), the ability to track others epistemic state, makes humans efficient collaborators. AI agents need the same capacity in multi agent settings, yet existing benchmarks mostly test literal ToM by asking direct belief questions. The ability act optimally on implicit beliefs in embodied environments, called functional ToM, remains largely untested. We introduce EnactToM, an evolving benchmark of 300 embodied multi-agent tasks set in a 3D household with partial observability, private information, and constrained communication. Each task is formally verified for solvability and required epistemic depth, and new tasks are generated increase difficulty as models improve. On the hard split, all seven evaluated frontier models score 0.0% Pass^3 on functional task completion, while averaging 45.0% on literal belief probes. Manual analysis traces 93% of sampled failures to epistemic coordination breakdowns such as withheld information, ignored partner constraints, and misallocated messages, providing a concrete target for future work.

</details>


### [307] [Yield Curve Forecasting using Machine Learning and Econometrics: A Comparative Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09842)
*Aman Singh,Tokunbo Ogunfunmi,Sanjiv Das*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper compares econometric, classical ML, and deep learning methods for forecasting the U.S. Treasury yield curve over 47 years and finds that traditional ARIMA and naive econometric models generally outperform modern ML and deep learning approaches, with some exceptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning and deep learning have transformed areas like NLP and computer vision, but their advantage in time-series forecasting—especially for financial series like the U.S. Treasury yield curve—remains unclear and debated. The authors aim to rigorously test whether advanced ML and deep learning models actually outperform standard econometric methods on a long, real-world financial dataset that is central to bond markets.

Method: Using 47 years of daily U.S. Treasury yield curve data, the authors set up a forecasting comparison across multiple model families: econometric/time-series models (e.g., ARIMA and its extensions, naive benchmarks), classical machine learning models (including ensemble methods such as LGBM), and deep learning models (RNNs and various transformer-based forecasting models, including TimeGPT). They evaluate these models’ forecasting performance over different time blocks and also investigate how using stationary versus nonstationary versions of the data affects deep learning model performance.

Result: Overall, ARIMA and naive econometric benchmarks outperform all other models across most of the sample, with the exception of one time block where other approaches do better. Among the machine learning and deep learning approaches, TimeGPT, LGBM, and RNNs deliver the strongest performance, but still do not consistently beat the best econometric models. The study also provides empirical evidence about how data stationarity choices influence the performance of deep learning models on this task.

Conclusion: Traditional econometric models, particularly ARIMA-type and simple naive benchmarks, remain very competitive—and often superior—to more complex machine learning and deep learning models for forecasting the U.S. Treasury yield curve. While some advanced models like TimeGPT, LGBM, and RNNs perform relatively well, they do not clearly dominate. Modelers should therefore be cautious about assuming that deep learning will automatically improve financial time-series forecasts, and should pay attention to data transformations such as enforcing stationarity when using deep learning.

Abstract: While machine learning has revolutionized many fields such as natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision, its impact on time-series forecasting is still widely disputed, especially in the finance domain. This paper compares forecasting performance on U.S. Treasury yield curve data across econometrics/time-series analysis, classical machine learning, and deep learning methods, using daily data over 47 years. The Treasury yield curve is important because it is widely used by every participant in the bond markets, which are larger than equity markets. We examine a variety of methods that have not been tested on yield curve forecasting, especially deep learning algorithms. The algorithms include the Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model and its extensions, naive benchmarks, ensemble methods, Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), and multiple transformers built for forecasting. ARIMA and naive econometric models outperform other models overall, except in one time block. Of the machine learning methods, TimeGPT, LGBM and RNNs perform the best. Furthermore, the paper explores whether stationary or nonstationary data are more appropriate as input to deep learning models.

</details>


### [308] [The Metacognitive Probe: Five Behavioural Calibration Diagnostics for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09844)
*Rafael C. T. Oliveira*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Metacognitive Probe, a multi-task diagnostic to analyze how language models handle confidence, revealing nuanced strengths and weaknesses beyond standard accuracy benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks mainly test whether models give correct answers, not whether they know when they might be wrong. This leaves hidden pockets of overconfidence or poor self-knowledge in LLMs. Drawing on metacognition theory, the authors want a structured way to decompose and measure different aspects of models’ confidence behavior and compare them with humans.

Method: They design a five-task, 15-slot diagnostic called the Metacognitive Probe, targeting: (T1-CC) confidence calibration, (T2-EV) epistemic vigilance, (T3-KB) knowledge boundary, (T4-CR) calibration range, and (T5-RCV) reasoning-chain validation. The probe measures confidence–correctness alignment rather than internal states and is applied to 8 frontier LLMs and 69 human participants. They also pre-register a human developmental hypothesis and test it, which ends up being falsified. Performance is summarized per task using metrics like Spearman correlation and confidence dispersion.

Result: The probe exposes substantial within-model variation in metacognitive behavior that composite benchmarks miss. A key example is Gemini 2.5 Flash, which shows excellent within-task calibration (T1-CC score of 88, Spearman rho = 0.551 with a statistically significant p-value) yet very poor cross-task difficulty prediction (T4-CR score of 41, with high confidence variability across factoids). This demonstrates that a single model can be simultaneously strong and weak on different metacognitive dimensions.

Conclusion: The Metacognitive Probe is a useful exploratory tool to decompose and inspect LLM confidence behaviors along several distinct dimensions, revealing failures that aggregate benchmarks cannot capture. Although inspired by human metacognition theories, it is not a validated cross-species metacognition scale, and the initial developmental hypothesis about humans was not supported. Nonetheless, it shows that detailed confidence diagnostics are necessary to understand and improve model metacognition beyond overall accuracy or coarse calibration scores.

Abstract: The Metacognitive Probe is an exploratory five-task, 15-slot diagnostic that decomposes an LLM's confidence behaviour into five behaviourally-distinct dimensions: confidence calibration (T1-CC), epistemic vigilance (T2-EV), knowledge boundary (T3-KB), calibration range (T4-CR), and reasoning-chain validation (T5-RCV). It is evaluated on N=8 frontier models and N=69 humans. The instrument is motivated by Flavell (1979) and Nelson and Narens (1990) but operates on observable confidence-correctness alignment; it is not a validated cross-species metacognition scale, and the pre-specified human developmental hypothesis was falsified.
  Composite benchmarks (MMLU, BIG-Bench, HELM, GPQA) ask whether a model produces a correct response. They are silent on whether the model knows when its response is wrong. A model can score 80 on a composite calibration benchmark and still be wildly overconfident in narrow pockets the aggregate cannot surface. The Metacognitive Probe surfaces those pockets.
  Our headline is a 47-point within-model dissociation in Gemini 2.5 Flash: panel-best within-task calibration (T1-CC = 88; Spearman rho = +0.551, 95% CI [+0.14, +0.80], p = 0.005) and panel-worst cross-task difficulty prediction (T4-CR = 41; sigma_conf = 1.4 across twelve factoids).

</details>


### [309] [Fairness of Explanations in Artificial Intelligence (AI): A Unifying Framework, Axioms, and Future Direction toward Responsible AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09852)
*Gideon Popoola,John Sheppard*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper surveys and formalizes the problem of unfairness in the reasoning processes of machine learning models, even when their outputs satisfy standard fairness criteria, and proposes a conditional invariance framework and practical workflow for auditing explanation fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning is used in high-stakes domains where fairness and transparency are critical. Existing work has largely treated algorithmic fairness (fair outcomes) and explainable AI (interpretable reasoning) separately. However, a model may produce fair-looking outputs while relying on discriminatory reasoning paths—a phenomenon the authors call procedural bias. There is a need to conceptualize and systematically study the fairness of explanations themselves, beyond outcome fairness and beyond ad-hoc XAI tools.

Method: The authors conduct a theoretical and literature survey at the intersection of algorithmic fairness and XAI, define the concept of procedural bias, and analyze why post-hoc explanation methods are insufficient to guarantee fairness. They introduce a conditional invariance framework that formalizes explanation fairness as the requirement that the distribution of explanations be invariant to protected attributes when conditioning on task-relevant features. Based on this, they derive a taxonomy of explanation fairness dimensions, identify generative mechanisms that lead to unfair explanations, and codify a multi-step workflow for auditing explanation fairness in practice.

Result: The paper presents: (1) a unified conceptual framework for explanation fairness based on conditional invariance; (2) a mapping showing how existing explanation fairness metrics can be understood as partial instantiations of this framework; (3) a seven-dimensional taxonomy of explanation fairness aspects; (4) the identification of three core mechanisms that generate explanation inequity (representation-driven, explanation-model mismatch, actionability-driven); and (5) a canonical six-step evaluation workflow for conducting explanation fairness audits. These results systematize scattered work and reveal the limitations of relying on standard post-hoc explainers to assess fairness.

Conclusion: Explanation fairness is a distinct and necessary dimension of responsible ML that cannot be guaranteed by checking standard outcome fairness metrics or by using off-the-shelf post-hoc explanation tools. The conditional invariance framework provides a unifying principle for defining and evaluating explanation fairness, and the proposed taxonomy, mechanisms, and workflow offer practitioners a structured approach to diagnosing and mitigating procedural bias in AI systems deployed in high-stakes settings.

Abstract: Machine learning algorithms are being used in high-stakes decisions, including those in criminal justice, healthcare, credit, and employment. The research community has responded with two largely independent research fields: \emph{algorithmic fairness}, which targets equitable outcomes, and \emph{explainable AI} (XAI), which targets interpretable reasoning. This survey identifies and maps a novel blind spot at their intersection, which is a model that can satisfy every standard fairness criterion in its outputs while being profoundly unfair in its \emph{reasoning process}. We refer to this as the procedural bias, and mitigating it requires treating the fairness of explanations as a distinct object of scientific study.
  To our knowledge, we provide the first unified theoretical and literature review of this emerging field and elucidate the drawbacks of post-hoc explainers in certifying explanation fairness. Our central contribution is a \emph{conditional invariance framework} formalizing explanation fairness as the requirement that explanations should be indifferent regardless of the protected attributes $ P(E(X) \in \cdot \mid X_\text{rel} = x_\text{rel},\, A = a) = P(E(X) \in \cdot \mid X_\text{rel} = x_\text{rel},\, A = b)$ for all task-relevant $x$, a single principle from which all existing explanation fairness metrics emerge as partial operationalizations. We introduce a seven-dimensional taxonomy, identify three generative mechanisms of explanation inequity (representation-driven, explanation-model mismatch, actionability-driven), and propose a canonical six-step evaluation workflow for operationalizing explanation fairness audits in practice.

</details>


### [310] [When to Re-Commit: Temporal Abstraction Discovery for Long-Horizon Vision-Language Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09860)
*Chen Li,Zhantao Yang,Fangyi Chen,Han Zhang,Anudeepsekhar Bolimera,Marios Savvides*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce and learn a state-dependent “commitment depth” (how many actions to run before replanning) inside a vision–language policy, which improves both success rate and efficiency on long-horizon reasoning games compared to any fixed-depth strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-horizon reasoning and planning systems typically fix how often they replan (the commitment depth) as a hand-tuned constant. This ignores the trade-off between the computational cost of frequent replanning and the risk of compounding errors when acting open-loop for too long. The authors aim to make this trade-off adaptive and state-aware, to improve performance and efficiency in complex sequential tasks.

Method: They formalize commitment depth as the number of primitive actions executed open-loop between replanning steps and make this a learnable, state-conditioned output of the policy rather than a fixed hyperparameter. They implement this idea in a model-native vision–language policy that jointly predicts the next actions and the depth (how long to execute them) based on the current state. They then compare adaptive commitment against a range of fixed-depth baselines on long-horizon puzzle domains (Sliding Puzzle and Sokoban) and analyze the approach theoretically under a standard surrogate objective for commitment depth.

Result: On Sliding Puzzle and Sokoban, the adaptive commitment-depth policy Pareto-dominates all non-degenerate fixed-depth baselines: it attains up to 12.5% higher solve rate while using about 25% fewer primitive actions per episode. With a 7B-parameter backbone, their method outperforms much larger or more capable general models (GPT-5.5 and Claude Sonnet) on these tasks, while all tested open-weight vision–language models fail (0% zero-shot success). Theoretical results show that when the locally optimal commitment depth varies with state, any state-conditioned commitment policy is strictly better than any globally fixed depth under the considered surrogate.

Conclusion: Learning a state-conditioned commitment depth within the policy is superior to using a hand-designed fixed depth for long-horizon reasoning. It resolves the trade-off between computation and compounding error in a data-driven, adaptive way, achieving better success rates with fewer actions and enjoying theoretical advantages whenever optimal depth is state-dependent. This suggests that future long-horizon agents should treat planning frequency as a learned, context-sensitive control variable rather than a static hyperparameter.

Abstract: Long-horizon reasoning requires deciding not only what actions to take, but how deeply to commit before the next observation. We formalize this as \emph{commitment depth}: the number of primitive actions executed open-loop between replans. Commitment depth induces a trade-off between replanning cost and compounding execution error, yet most existing long-horizon systems fix it as a hand-designed scalar. In this work, we instead treat commitment depth as a learnable, state-conditioned variable of the policy itself. We instantiate this within a model-native vision--language policy that jointly predicts both what to execute and for how long. Across Sliding Puzzle and Sokoban, the resulting adaptive policy Pareto-dominates every non-degenerate fixed-depth baseline, achieving up to 12.5 percentage points higher solve rate while using approximately 25\% fewer primitive actions per episode. Despite using a 7B backbone, our method outperforms GPT-5.5 and Claude Sonnet on both tasks, while every tested open-weight vision--language model achieves 0\% zero-shot success. We further present a theoretical analysis showing that, under the standard commitment-depth surrogate, state-conditioned commitment strictly dominates any fixed depth whenever the locally optimal depth varies across states.

</details>


### [311] [Cross-Family Universality of Behavioral Axes via Anchor-Projected Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09875)
*Su-Hyeon Kim,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a way to map internal representations of different language models into a shared coordinate space so that behavioral directions (like politeness, refusal, etc.) can be compared, averaged, and transferred across model families without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Different LLM families have incompatible internal spaces (dimensions, tokenizers, training), which makes it hard to compare or reuse discovered “behavioral directions” (e.g., safety, verbosity) between models. The authors want a method that exposes shared structure across model families and enables zero-shot transfer of these directions without per-model interpretability work or fine-tuning.

Method: They build an anchor-projection framework: (1) define an anchor coordinate space (ACS) using anchor representations common across models (e.g., activations on a shared anchor set); (2) map each model’s hidden representations into this ACS; (3) extract behavioral directions in the source models, project those directions into ACS, and average them into a canonical direction per behavioral axis; (4) for a new target model, reconstruct the canonical direction back into that model’s native hidden space using only its anchor activations, without fine-tuning or retraining; (5) evaluate across five instruction-tuned model families and ten behavioral axes on detection and steering tasks under distribution shift; (6) run sensitivity analyses varying the number of source models and the size of the anchor pool.

Result: They find that for the Llama, Qwen, Mistral, and Phi models (the LQMP cluster), behavioral directions for the same axis align closely in ACS, revealing strong shared representation structure. Using canonical directions, held-out target models achieve around 0.83 accuracy on ten-way behavioral detection and 0.95 mean AUROC on binary classification tasks. Canonical steering can reliably shift refusal rates by up to about 0.46 percentage points even under distribution shift. Sensitivity experiments show that only two source models and relatively small anchor sets already suffice to approximate useful transferable directions.

Conclusion: The proposed anchor coordinate space enables robust, representation-level transfer of behavioral directions across different LLM families without model-specific fine-tuning. Models in the LQMP cluster share highly aligned structure along interpretable behavioral axes, and these shared directions can be used both for detecting and steering behaviors in new models. This offers a new, practical cross-family interpretability framework and suggests that internal representations of different LLMs are more compatible than their architectural and training differences imply.

Abstract: Large language models from different families use different hidden dimensions, tokenizers, and training procedures, making behavioral directions difficult to compare or transfer across models. We introduce an anchor-projection framework that maps hidden representations from each model into a shared anchor coordinate space (ACS). Behavioral directions extracted from source models are projected into ACS and averaged into a canonical direction. For a new model, the canonical direction is reconstructed into its native hidden space using only anchor activations, without fine-tuning or target-specific direction extraction. We evaluate five instruction-tuned model families and ten behavioral axes. We find that same-axis directions align tightly across the Llama-Qwen-Mistral-Phi (LQMP) cluster in ACS. This shared structure transfers to downstream tasks. For the aligned LQMP cluster, held-out targets achieve (0.83) ten-way detection accuracy and (0.95) mean binary AUROC, while canonical steering induces refusal-rate shifts of up to +0.46% under distribution shift. Sensitivity analyses show that two source models and small anchor pools already suffice to approximate transferable directions. Overall, ACS provides a novel perspective on cross-family interpretability, revealing that representation-level transfer remains robust across model families.

</details>


### [312] [M2A: Synergizing Mathematical and Agentic Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09879)
*Junjian Wang,Xin Zhou,Qiran Xu,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes M2A, a parameter-space model-merging method that injects strong mathematical reasoning into an agentic LLM without harming its interactive behavior, substantially boosting coding-agent performance without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning in LLMs differs across tasks: mathematical reasoning is single-shot and logic-heavy, while agentic reasoning requires multi-step interaction with tools and environments. Standard multi-task training often causes interference between these skills, leading to unstable behavior and modest gains. The authors aim to find a way for mathematical and agentic reasoning to reinforce each other without mutual degradation, particularly for real-world coding agents.

Method: M2A works by merging models directly in parameter space rather than via joint optimization. It first identifies the parameter subspace that is critical for agentic behavior. Then it computes a task vector capturing mathematical reasoning and injects it only along the null space of the agent-critical subspace. This preserves the parameters essential to agent behavior while adding math reasoning capacity where it minimally interferes. The merging coefficient is an exposed hyperparameter that controls how strongly the mathematical reasoning vector is injected, effectively tuning reasoning depth/length. No additional gradient-based fine-tuning (SFT or RL) is needed once the task vectors and subspaces are defined.

Result: In a demanding coding-agent environment, applying M2A to a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B significantly improves performance. On SWE-Bench Verified, the resolved bug-fix rate increases from 44.0% to 51.2% without any retraining of the model. Empirically, the agent exhibits deeper and more stable agentic reasoning behavior while benefiting from enhanced mathematical-style logical reasoning.

Conclusion: M2A demonstrates that mathematical and agentic reasoning can be synergistically combined through parameter-space model merging, avoiding the interference common in multi-task learning. By injecting mathematical reasoning only in the null space of agent-critical features, M2A extends reasoning depth, is simple to control via a single coefficient, and yields substantial performance gains for coding agents without additional training. This suggests a promising direction for modularly composing specialized reasoning abilities in LLMs.

Abstract: While reasoning has become a central capability of large language models (LLMs), the reasoning patterns required for different scenarios are often misaligned. Mathematical reasoning typically relies on intrinsic logic to solve closed-world problems in a single response, whereas agentic reasoning requires not only internal reasoning but also multi-turn interaction with external environments, interleaving thought and action. This misalignment prevents mathematical and agentic reasoning from effectively benefiting from each other, often yielding unstable reasoning behavior and only limited performance gains under multi-task learning. In this paper, we propose M2A, a novel paradigm that synergizes mathematical and agentic reasoning via model merging. To avoid overfitting to superficial reasoning patterns under joint training, M2A operates directly in parameter space: it identifies the feature subspace critical for agent behavior, and merges the mathematical reasoning task vector only along its null space, thereby injecting reasoning capability along directions that do not perturb agent behavior. Unlike SFT or RL, M2A requires no additional gradient-update and exposes the merging coefficient as a simple knob for controlling reasoning length. Experiments in a challenging real-world coding agent setting show that our method effectively extends agentic reasoning depth and delivers substantial performance improvements. Applied to a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B, M2A improves its SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate from 44.0% to 51.2% without retraining the model. Code is available at https://github.com/laplucky/M2A.git.

</details>


### [313] [The Gordian Knot for VLMs: Diagrammatic Knot Reasoning as a Hard Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09900)
*Hao Liu,Jicheng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces KnotBench, a benchmark for testing whether vision-language models truly understand knot diagrams, showing that current models mostly fail beyond surface-level perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While vision-language models can describe knot diagrams, it is unclear whether they can reason about and manipulate the underlying mathematical structures (knots) rather than just perceiving them. The authors want a rigorous, automatically checkable way to test structural understanding and reasoning on nontrivial diagrams.

Method: They build KnotBench, a large dataset of 858,318 images derived from 1,951 prime knot types (with crossing numbers from 3 to 19), and define 14 tasks in four categories: equivalence judgment, move prediction, identification, and cross-modal grounding. Answers are validated using Regina's canonical knot signatures. They design an image-versus-symbol split to separate perceptual understanding from operational reasoning and evaluate Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5, with and without chain-of-thought-style 'thinking' under a matched 64K output-token budget.

Result: Across 56 combinations of task and model, 15 perform at or below random chance, and for 8 of the 14 tasks, even the best model achieves less than 1.5 times random baseline performance. On diagram-to-symbol transcription, none of the evaluated models ever produce a perfectly correct representation; when decoding more permissively with Regina, the correct knot is recovered in only 0–4 out of 100 cases. Thinking-mode reasoning improves accuracy slightly (by 1.65 points for Claude and 9.25 points for GPT-5), but the overall gap in strong performance remains.

Conclusion: KnotBench reveals that current vision-language models can capture some visual features of knot diagrams but generally lack the ability to perform the operations and transformations (such as moves and equivalence reasoning) required for true structural understanding. Even with extended reasoning modes, models struggle to simulate knot moves on internal representations, highlighting a perception-operation gap that future model and benchmark designs must address.

Abstract: A vision-language model can look at a knot diagram and report what it sees, yet fail to act on that structure. KnotBench pairs an 858,318-image corpus from 1,951 prime-knot prototypes (crossing numbers 3 to 19) with a protocol whose answers are checked against Regina's canonical knot signature. Its 14 tasks span four families, equivalence judgment, move prediction, identification, and cross-modal grounding; an image-versus-symbol split locates failures along the perception-operation gap. We score Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5, each with and without thinking, under a 64K output-token budget matched on both vendors. Across 56 (task, model) cases, 15 sit at or below a random baseline and 8 of 14 tasks have a best score under 1.5x random. On diagram-to-symbol transcription, no model produces a strictly correct string, and permissive Regina decoding recovers the knot in 0 to 4 of 100 items. Thinking-mode reasoning lifts overall accuracy by 1.65 points for Claude and 9.25 points for GPT-5, narrowing the gap only modestly. Read together, the four families suggest current vision-language models hold features of a diagram but lack apparatus to simulate moves on those features.

</details>


### [314] [Separate First, Fuse Later: Mitigating Cross-Modal Interference in Audio-Visual LLMs Reasoning with Modality-Specific Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09906)
*Xuanchen Li,Yuheng Lu,Chenrui Cui,Tianrui Wang,Zikang Huang,Yu Jiang,Long Zhou,Longbiao Wang,Jianwu Dang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new framework, Separate First, Fuse Later (SFFL), to reduce cross-modal interference and hallucinations in audio-visual question answering by enforcing separate audio and visual reasoning before fusing evidence, achieving better accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing audio-visual large language models for question answering suffer from cross-modal interference, where one modality (audio or vision) incorrectly influences the interpretation of the other, causing hallucinated answers. This problem is attributed to uncontrolled cross-modal interactions during intermediate reasoning. There is a need for a method that better manages how modalities interact so that each can contribute reliably without misleading the overall reasoning process.

Method: They introduce SFFL (Separate First, Fuse Later), an audio-visual reasoning framework that performs modality-specific chain-of-thought reasoning: it first generates separate reasoning traces for audio and visual inputs, maintaining modality isolation during this stage. They create modality-preference labels via a data pipeline that operates under varying modality input conditions. These labels serve as an auxiliary reward in reinforcement learning, guiding the model to adopt instance-dependent preferences for which modality to rely on when answering. Finally, they add a modality-specific reasoning mechanism that keeps the modalities separate during reasoning but allows full cross-modal access at a later fusion stage where evidence is integrated to produce the final answer.

Result: SFFL achieves consistent gains in both answer accuracy and robustness across audio-visual QA benchmarks. Quantitatively, they report an average relative performance improvement of 5.16% on general audio-visual QA benchmarks and 11.17% on a specialized benchmark designed to evaluate cross-modal hallucination, indicating a significant reduction in hallucination errors.

Conclusion: Carefully structuring audio-visual reasoning to separate modality-specific inference before fusing evidence reduces cross-modal interference and hallucinations in audio-visual question answering. The SFFL framework, reinforced with modality-preference rewards and a modality-specific reasoning mechanism, leads to more accurate and robust AVQA performance, demonstrating the benefits of controlled, staged cross-modal interaction.

Abstract: Audio and vision provide complementary evidence for audio-visual question answering, yet current audio-visual large language models may suffer from cross-modal interference: information from one modality misguides the interpretation of another, thereby inducing hallucinations. We attribute this issue to uncontrolled cross-modal interactions during intermediate reasoning. To mitigate this, we propose Separate First, Fuse Later (SFFL), an audio-visual reasoning framework designed to reduce cross-modal interference. SFFL enforces modality-specific chain-of-thought reasoning, producing separate audio and visual reasoning traces and integrating evidence for answering. We construct modality-preference labels via a data pipeline under different modality input settings. We use these labels as an auxiliary reward in reinforcement learning to encourage a instance-dependent preference for modality cues when answering. We further introduce a modality-specific reasoning mechanism that preserves modality isolation during the separated reasoning stage while enabling full access to cross-modal information at the evidence fusion stage. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements in both accuracy and robustness, yielding an average relative gain of 5.16\% on general AVQA benchmarks and 11.17\% on a cross-modal hallucination benchmark.

</details>


### [315] [RADAR: Redundancy-Aware Diffusion for Multi-Agent Communication Structure Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09907)
*Zhen Zhang,Wanjing Zhou,Juncheng Li,Hao Fei,Jun Wen,Wei Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RADAR is a redundancy-aware, query-adaptive framework that dynamically generates communication topologies for LLM-based multi-agent systems, reducing token overhead while improving accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems use fixed or one-shot generated communication topologies, which limits fine-grained structural control. This leads to unnecessary token usage on simple tasks and insufficient coordination on complex tasks. A more adaptive and efficient communication structure is needed.

Method: Formulate the design of the communication topology as a step-by-step, conditional generation process using ideas from discrete graph diffusion models. RADAR adaptively constructs the multi-agent communication graph based on redundancy awareness and query characteristics, with the process guided by the effective size of the graph to control communication overhead.

Result: On six benchmarks, RADAR surpasses recent baseline multi-agent communication schemes in terms of task accuracy, token efficiency (lower token consumption), and robustness across varied scenarios.

Conclusion: Adaptive, redundancy-aware, and incrementally generated communication topologies can significantly enhance the performance and efficiency of LLM-based multi-agent systems. RADAR demonstrates that treating communication design as a generative graph process yields better accuracy, lower communication cost, and stronger robustness than fixed or one-shot topologies.

Abstract: Compared with individual agents, large language model based multi-agent systems have shown great capabilities consistently across diverse tasks, including code generation, mathematical reasoning, and planning, etc. Despite their impressive performance, the effectiveness and robustness of these systems heavily rely on their communication topology, which is often fixed or generated in a single step. This restricts fine-grained structural exploration and flexible composition, resulting in excessive token utilization on simple tasks while limiting capability on complicated tasks. To mitigate this challenge, we introduce RADAR, a redundancy-aware and query-adaptive generative framework that actively reduce communication overhead. Motivated by recent progress in conditional discrete graph diffusion models, we formulate communication topology design as a step-by-step generation process, guided by the effective size of the graph. Comprehensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate that RADAR consistently outperforms recent baselines, achieving higher accuracy, lower token consumption, and greater robustness across diverse scenarios. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/cszhangzhen/RADAR.

</details>


### [316] [expo: Exploration-prioritized policy optimization via adaptive kl regulation and gaussian curriculum sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09923)
*Mingxiong Lin,Zhangquan Gong,Maowen Tang,Qian Li,Chuangchuang Wang,Jian Ma,Sutian Huang,Kai Tang,Haonan Lu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes EXPO, a new RL-based training method for mathematical reasoning LLMs that improves exploration and sample efficiency over standard GRPO using adaptive KL scaling and difficulty-aware curriculum sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO-based RLVR is effective for math reasoning LLMs but has two inefficiencies: (1) a fixed KL penalty coefficient that can overconstrain exploration when the policy must deviate from the reference model, and (2) uniform sampling of questions that underutilizes moderately difficult problems, which provide the most useful gradient signal for learning. The authors aim to design a more exploration-friendly and curriculum-aware optimization scheme to push reasoning performance under a fixed inference budget.

Method: They introduce Exploration-Prioritized Policy Optimization (EXPO), a plug-in modification to GRPO with two components: (1) Accuracy-Conditioned KL Scaling (AKL), which adjusts the KL regularization coefficient as a smooth nonlinear function of batch average accuracy—reducing the KL penalty when accuracy is low to encourage exploration, and increasing it when accuracy is high to maintain alignment; (2) Gaussian Curriculum Sampling (GCS), which assigns sampling probabilities to training questions based on a Gaussian distribution over difficulty (proxied by accuracy), peaking around 50% accuracy to focus on moderately hard problems near the model's learning frontier. These modules are lightweight and integrate into existing RLVR pipelines.

Result: On DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and Qwen3-8B-Base across six math reasoning benchmarks, EXPO consistently outperforms vanilla GRPO. For example, it boosts AIME 2025 pass@32 from 63.33% to 76.67% (a 13.34 absolute gain) and yields an average pass@32 increase of 2.66 points on the 8B model. The improvements are substantially larger for pass@32 than for pass@1, indicating better exploration without increasing inference cost.

Conclusion: EXPO addresses key exploration and curriculum inefficiencies in GRPO-based RLVR via adaptive KL scaling and difficulty-targeted sampling. The approach yields notable gains in mathematical reasoning benchmarks, especially for pass@32, suggesting it more effectively expands the model's exploration space under fixed inference budgets while remaining easy to integrate into existing training pipelines.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the standard paradigm for LLM mathematical reasoning, where Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) serves as the mainstream algorithm. We point out two understudied inefficiencies existing in GRPO. First, the fixed KL penalty coefficient overly restricts policy exploration at stages where the model requires significant deviation from the reference policy. Second, uniform sampling of training questions ignores that moderately difficult problems provide the most informative gradient signals for optimization. We propose Exploration-Prioritized Policy Optimization (EXPO) with two lightweight plug-in modules. The Accuracy-Conditioned KL Scaling (AKL) dynamically adjusts KL regularization strength through a smooth nonlinear function of batch average accuracy, relaxing the penalty when the model underperforms and strengthening it when the model achieves good results. The Gaussian Curriculum Sampling (GCS) assigns sampling weights to questions following a Gaussian distribution centered at moderate accuracy around 0.5, focusing training on the model's learning frontier. We conduct extensive experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B and Qwen3-8B-Base over six mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The results show EXPO steadily surpasses vanilla GRPO. It obtains an absolute gain of 13.34 on AIME 2025 pass@32, rising from 63.33 percent to 76.67 percent, and achieves an average pass@32 improvement of 2.66 on the 8B model. The much larger performance gains on pass@32 compared with pass@1 demonstrate that EXPO effectively enlarges the model's exploration boundary under a fixed inference cost budget.

</details>


### [317] [HAGE: Harnessing Agentic Memory via RL-Driven Weighted Graph Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09942)
*Dongming Jiang,Yi Li,Guanpeng Li,Qiannan Li,Bingzhe Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces HAGE, a weighted multi-relational memory framework that turns retrieval in agentic LLM systems into a dynamic, query-conditioned traversal over a relational memory graph, improving long-horizon reasoning and efficiency over prior systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory retrieval in agentic LLMs typically relies on static mechanisms such as flat vector search or fixed binary relational graphs. These approaches fail to capture that relationships between events can vary in strength, confidence, and relevance depending on the query. As a result, systems struggle with long-horizon reasoning and may follow suboptimal or noisy memory paths. The authors are motivated to build a more flexible, relational, and query-aware memory retrieval framework that can adaptively prioritize useful connections while down-weighting weak or noisy ones, yielding better performance and efficiency in agentic LLM settings.

Method: HAGE organizes memory as a unified graph of shared memory nodes with multiple relation-specific views. Each edge carries a trainable relation feature vector that encodes different relational signals (e.g., strength, type, confidence). For a given query, an LLM-based classifier first infers the relational intent (which types of relations are relevant). A routing network then dynamically modulates specific dimensions of the edge embeddings based on this intent, effectively reweighting relation features. Retrieval is performed as sequential traversal: traversal scores combine semantic similarity between nodes and the modulated edge representations, allowing the system to score and follow promising paths. Additionally, the framework uses reinforcement learning to jointly train the routing network and edge representations based on downstream task performance, so that traversal behavior is optimized end-to-end for reasoning quality and efficiency.

Result: Empirical evaluations show that HAGE improves long-horizon reasoning accuracy relative to existing agentic memory and retrieval systems, while also achieving a better trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. The adaptive, query-conditioned traversal over the relational memory graph enables the system to more effectively exploit useful relational paths and avoid noisy or irrelevant connections, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on the tested benchmarks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that memory retrieval for agentic LLMs should be treated as dynamic, query-conditioned traversal over a structured, weighted multi-relational graph rather than as static lookup. HAGE demonstrates that representing memory as relation-specific graph views with trainable edge features, combined with an LLM-guided routing mechanism and reinforcement learning-based optimization, leads to superior long-horizon reasoning and efficiency. This validates the proposed weighted multi-relational memory framework as a promising direction for future agentic LLM systems.

Abstract: Memory retrieval in agentic large language model (LLM) systems is often treated as a static lookup problem, relying on flat vector search or fixed binary relational graphs. However, fixed graph structures cannot capture the varying strength, confidence, and query-dependent relevance of relationships between events. In this paper, we propose HAGE, a weighted multi-relational memory framework that reconceptualizes retrieval as sequential, query-conditioned traversal over a unified relational memory graph. Memory is organized as relation-specific graph views over shared memory nodes, where each edge is associated with a trainable relation feature vector encoding multiple relational signals. Given a query, an LLM-based classifier identifies the relational intent, and a routing network dynamically modulates the corresponding dimensions of the edge embedding. Traversal scores are computed via a learned combination of semantic similarity and these query-conditioned edge representations. This allows memory traversal to prioritize high-utility relational paths while softly suppressing noisy or weakly relevant connections. Beyond adaptive traversal, HAGE further introduces a reinforcement learning-based training framework that jointly optimizes routing behavior and edge representations using downstream tasks. Finally, empirical results demonstrate improved long-horizon reasoning accuracy and a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to state-of-the-art agentic memory systems. Our code is available at https://github.com/FredJiang0324/HAGE_MVPReview.

</details>


### [318] [LoopVLA: Learning Sufficiency in Recurrent Refinement for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09948)
*Boyang Shen,Kaixiang Yang,Hao Wang,Qiuyu Yu,Qiang Xie,Qiang Li,Zhiwei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoopVLA is a recurrent vision-language-action model that learns when its internal representation is already good enough to choose an action, improving efficiency without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models rely on the deepest backbone features for action prediction, which is wasteful for closed-loop robotic manipulation that mostly needs fine-grained spatial control and not always maximal semantic abstraction. Early-exit heuristics exist but they do not provide a principled, learned notion of when the current representation is sufficient to decide on an action, limiting both efficiency and reliability.

Method: The authors propose LoopVLA, a recurrent architecture that repeatedly applies a shared Transformer block to refine multimodal (vision-language) tokens. After each refinement step, the model outputs both a candidate action and a learned sufficiency score estimating whether further refinement is needed. To train sufficiency without explicit labels, they introduce a self-supervised distribution alignment loss: intermediate confidence scores are optimized to reflect the relative quality of actions across refinement steps, tying the sufficiency estimates to actual policy performance signals rather than to fixed layer depths or handcrafted rules.

Result: On the LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLA-Arena benchmarks, LoopVLA attains task success rates that match or surpass strong VLA baselines, while using about 45% fewer parameters and achieving up to 1.7× higher inference throughput. This demonstrates that the learned sufficiency mechanism can substantially improve computational efficiency without degrading—and sometimes improving—control performance.

Conclusion: LoopVLA shows that learning sufficiency directly over evolving representations is an effective way to decouple action quality from fixed network depth in VLA policies. By iteratively refining a shared set of parameters and deciding online when to stop, the model pushes the efficiency–performance frontier for robotic manipulation. The work suggests that future VLA systems can benefit from principled, self-supervised stopping criteria instead of rigidly relying on the deepest layers or hand-designed early-exit heuristics.

Abstract: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models typically treat the deepest representation of a vision-language backbone as universally optimal for action prediction. However, robotic manipulation is composed of many frequent closed-loop spatial adjustments, for which excessive abstraction may waste computation and weaken low-level geometric cues essential for precise control. Existing early-exit strategies attempt to reduce computation by stopping at predefined layers or applying heuristic rules such as action consistency, but they do not directly answer when a representation is actually sufficient for action. In this paper, we present LoopVLA, a recurrent VLA architecture that jointly learns representation refinement, action prediction, and sufficiency estimation. LoopVLA iteratively applies a shared Transformer block to refine multimodal tokens, and at each iteration produces both a candidate action and a sufficiency score that estimates whether further refinement is necessary. By sharing parameters across iterations, LoopVLA decouples refinement from absolute layer indices and grounds sufficiency estimation in the evolving representation itself. Since sufficiency has no direct supervision, we introduce a self-supervised distribution alignment objective, where intermediate confidence scores are trained to match the relative action quality across refinement steps, thereby linking sufficiency learning to policy optimization signals. Experiments on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and VLA-Arena show that LoopVLA pushes the efficiency-performance frontier of VLA policies, reducing parameters by 45% and improving inference throughput by up to 1.7 times while matching or outperforming strong baselines in task success.

</details>


### [319] [Learning the Interaction Prior for Protein-Protein Interaction Prediction: A Model-Agnostic Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09964)
*Ziqi Gao,Chenyi Zi,Zijing Liu,Ziqiao Meng,Yu Li,Jia Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They design a biologically-motivated classification head for PPI prediction based on the L3 rule, using a graph prompt module to inject length-3 path priors and improve existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most learning-based PPI predictors invest in better protein sequence/structure encoders but use simple, biologically-naive heads (concatenation, dot-product) for deciding if two proteins interact. However, biological evidence suggests that the presence and number of multiple length-3 paths between proteins in an interaction network (the L3 rule) is predictive of interaction. There is a gap between this known graph-theoretic biological insight and how PPI prediction models perform classification.

Method: They empirically validate that popular PPI benchmarks obey the L3 rule, i.e., interaction likelihood correlates with the count of length-3 paths. Then they propose L3-PPI, an L3-path-regularized graph prompt learning module. Given protein embeddings from any base PPI predictor, L3-PPI constructs a prompt graph that includes virtual L3 paths whose number can be controlled. The PPI decision is reframed as a graph-level classification problem over this generated prompt graph. The module is lightweight, plug-and-play, and trained so that the instantiated virtual L3 paths encode a biologically meaningful notion of complementarity.

Result: On standard PPI datasets, integrating L3-PPI with a variety of backbone PPI predictors consistently and significantly improves predictive performance compared to those backbones with conventional classification heads, and outperforms other advanced competitors. Their experiments also support that datasets follow the L3 rule, providing empirical backing for the method’s biological prior.

Conclusion: Encoding biologically-inspired interaction priors—in particular the L3 rule about multiple length-3 paths—directly into the classification head for PPI prediction yields better performance than generic aggregation heads. The proposed L3-PPI module can be seamlessly attached to existing PPI predictors, reformulating pairwise classification as graph-level classification over a prompt graph with virtual L3 paths, and empirically delivers superior results across benchmarks.

Abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are fundamental to cellular function and disease mechanisms. Current learning-based PPI predictors focus on learning powerful protein representations but neglect designing specialized classification heads. They mainly rely on generic aggregating methods like concatenation or dot products, which lack biological insight. Motivated by the biological "L3 rule", where multiple length-3 paths between a pair of proteins indicate their interaction likelihood, our study addresses this gap by designing a biologically informed PPI classifier. In this paper, we provide empirical evidence that popular PPI datasets strongly support the L3 rule. We propose an L3-path-regularized graph prompt learning method called L3-PPI, which can generate a prompt graph with virtual L3 paths based on protein representations and controls the number of paths. L3-PPI reformulates the classification of protein embedding pairs into a graph-level classification task over the generated prompt graph. This lightweight module seamlessly integrates with PPI predictors as a plug-and-play component, injecting the interaction prior of complementarity to enhance performance. Extensive experiments show that L3-PPI achieves superior performance enhancements over advanced competitors.

</details>


### [320] [Prospective Compression in Human Abstraction Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09985)
*Leonardo Hernandez Cano,Ivan Zareski,Luisa El Amouri,Pinzhe Zhao,Max Mascini,Emanuele Sansone,Yewen Pu,Bonan Zhao,Marta Kryven*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how humans learn reusable programming abstractions over time in changing environments, showing they plan for future tasks (prospective compression) rather than only compressing past tasks (retrospective compression), and that standard algorithms and LLM-based methods fail to capture this behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most program synthesis and library learning algorithms assume a fixed, stationary task distribution and treat library learning as compressing a static corpus of past programs. But real-world tasks arrive from evolving, non-stationary processes, and humans seem to learn abstractions that anticipate future needs. The authors want to understand and model how humans acquire reusable abstractions in such non-stationary settings, and to test whether humans learn libraries prospectively (aimed at future compression) rather than purely retrospectively (aimed at past compression).

Method: The authors introduce the Pattern Builder Task, where human participants build increasingly complex geometric patterns by composing primitives, transformations, and custom helper functions that persist across trials. They design two experiments with different latent curricula (hidden structures in how tasks evolve) that allow them to distinguish behaviors predicted by prospective compression from those predicted by alternative accounts (e.g., retrospective compression). They then compare human behavior to six computational models representing different online library learning strategies, including standard retrospective compression-based algorithms and LLM-based program synthesis with inductive biases.

Result: Participants’ choices of helper abstractions and how they reuse them are systematically influenced by the evolving, latent task structure, indicating sensitivity to non-stationarity in the task-generating process. Across the two experiments, human behavior aligns with models that perform prospective compression—optimizing libraries for anticipated future tasks—rather than models that only compress the past or rely on static inductive biases. Existing retrospective compression algorithms and LLM-based program synthesis fail to reproduce the observed human abstraction patterns.

Conclusion: Human library learning in program synthesis tasks appears prospective: people choose abstractions that support efficient solutions to future, not just past, tasks, and they track latent changes in the underlying task distribution. This prospective compression behavior cannot be accounted for by standard retrospective compression frameworks or current LLM-based program synthesis biases, implying that effective models of human-like program synthesis and abstraction must incorporate explicit reasoning about non-stationary environments and future task demands.

Abstract: A core challenge in program synthesis is online library learning: the incremental acquisition of reusable abstractions under uncertainty about future task demands. Existing algorithms treat library learning as retrospective compression over a static task distribution, where the learned library is determined by the corpus of past tasks. However, real-world learning domains are often non-stationary, with tasks arising from a generative process that evolves over time. We propose and test the hypothesis that in non-stationary domains human library learning selects abstractions prospectively: targeting compression of future tasks. We study this question using the Pattern Builder Task, a visual program synthesis paradigm in which participants construct increasingly complex geometric patterns from a small set of primitives, transformations, and custom helpers that carry forward across trials. Using this task, we conduct two experiments with complementary latent curricula, designed to dissociate between behaviors consistent with prospective compression, and alternative library learning accounts. Using six computational models spanning online library learning strategies, we show that human abstraction behavior reflects sensitivity to latent, non-stationary structure in the task-generating process. This behavior is consistent with prospective compression, and cannot be captured by existing retrospective compression-based algorithms, or inductive biases modeled by LLM-based program synthesis.

</details>


### [321] [Optimizer-Induced Mode Connectivity: From AdamW to Muon](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.09991)
*Fangzhao Zhang,Sungyoon Kim,Erica Zhang,Yiqi Jiang,Mert Pilanci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Studies how different optimizers shape mode connectivity, showing optimizer-specific connected regions and interactions in neural network loss landscapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While mode connectivity is known to exist between many trained neural networks, most prior work ignores how the choice of optimizer constrains which solutions are reachable in practice. The authors aim to understand the structure of solutions that are actually found by specific optimizers and how these optimizer-induced solution sets interact.

Method: The authors analyze two-layer ReLU networks theoretically, focusing on solutions obtained by particular optimizers such as AdamW, Muon, and the Lion-𝒦 family. They study the connectivity of these optimizer-specific solution sets at large width and construct small-width examples where optimizers converge to distinct components. They complement theory with empirical experiments on GPT-2 pretraining, where they examine mode connectivity paths between models trained with the same vs. different optimizers and analyze spectral properties along these paths.

Result: They prove that, for sufficiently wide two-layer ReLU networks, all solutions reachable by a fixed optimizer (AdamW, Muon, Lion-𝒦, etc.) form a connected set, a property not guaranteed by previous mode connectivity results. They further show that, at large width, solution regions induced by different optimizers can either overlap or be disjoint depending on regularization choices. In a constructed small-width case, AdamW and Muon converge to zero-loss solutions that lie in disconnected components separated by a provable loss barrier. Empirically, GPT-2 models trained with the same optimizer can be connected by paths that preserve each model’s spectrum, whereas paths between different-optimizer models show a smooth spectral transition.

Conclusion: Optimizer choice induces distinct geometric and spectral structures in the space of solutions: solutions reachable by a single optimizer form connected regions under suitable conditions, while regions associated with different optimizers may intersect or be separated by loss barriers. These findings extend classical mode connectivity by revealing an optimizer-dependent organization of the loss landscape, highlighting that implicit regularization from the optimizer is crucial to understanding which modes are practically reachable.

Abstract: Mode connectivity has been widely studied, yet the role of the optimizer remains underexplored. We revisit it through optimizer-induced implicit regularization, asking how connectivity behaves when restricted to solutions constrained by a given optimizer. For two-layer ReLU networks, we show that solutions from a single optimizer -- AdamW, Muon, or others in the Lion-$\mathcal{K}$ family -- form a connected set at sufficiently large width, a result not implied by prior work. We then characterize how optimizer-induced regions interact: at large width two different regions can be disjoint or overlap depending on regularization, while in our small-width example AdamW and Muon converge to disconnected zero-loss components separated by a provable loss barrier. Empirically, in GPT-2 pretraining, we observe same-optimizer paths preserve each model's spectrum while cross-optimizer paths traverse a smooth transition. Our results reveal optimizer-dependent structure beyond classical mode connectivity literature.

</details>


### [322] [TimeClaw: A Time-Series AI Agent with Exploratory Execution Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10038)
*Hangchen Liu,Dongyuan Li,Renhe Jiang,Jiewen Deng,Weiwei Ye,Yoshihide Sekimoto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TimeClaw, a framework that converts exploratory executions of time-series tasks into reusable hierarchical experience to improve LLM-based forecasting and reasoning without modifying the base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based and foundation-model-based approaches for time series tasks are largely execution-centric: they solve each instance individually and do not effectively learn from exploratory executions. This is problematic in verifiable numeric domains like finance and weather, where there can be many valid solution paths with varying quantitative quality, and where early success can lead to a collapse in tool usage and reduced exploration. The authors are motivated to create a method that can systematically compare and reuse exploratory runs so that models can improve over time without online adaptation.

Method: The authors propose TimeClaw, an exploratory execution learning framework organized as a four-stage loop: Explore, Compare, Distill, and Reinject. In the Explore stage, the system generates multiple candidate executions and tool-use procedures for time series tasks. In Compare, these executions are evaluated with respect to quantitative metrics. Distill converts the best exploratory executions into hierarchical distilled experience, capturing useful patterns and procedures. Reinject uses this distilled experience at inference time as additional guidance, without updating the base model’s parameters. The framework incorporates metric-supervised exploratory execution learning, task-aware tool dropout to encourage exploration, and hierarchical experience structures for later reuse.

Result: On an MTBench-aligned benchmark comprising 17 tasks across finance and weather prediction and reasoning, TimeClaw outperforms baseline systems consistently. The gains demonstrate that the framework effectively improves both numerical accuracy and contextual reasoning in time series tasks when compared to execution-centric baselines.

Conclusion: The study concludes that in scientific and numeric domains, the key bottleneck is not only the raw execution capability of models but also how exploratory experience is compared, distilled, and reused. TimeClaw shows that turning exploratory execution into reusable hierarchical knowledge, without changing the base model or relying on online test-time adaptation, can yield substantial benefits for time series forecasting and reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Time series analysis underpins forecasting, monitoring, and decision making in domains such as finance and weather, where solving a task often requires both numerical accuracy and contextual reasoning. Recent progress has moved from specialized neural predictors to approaches built on LLMs and foundation models that can reason over time series inputs and use external tools. However, most such systems remain execution-centric: they focus on solving the current instance but learn little from exploratory execution. This is especially limiting in verifiable numeric settings, where multiple candidate executions and tool-use procedures may all be task-valid yet differ sharply in quantitative quality, and where early success can trigger tool-prior collapse that suppresses further exploration. To address this limitation, we present TimeClaw, an exploratory execution learning framework that turns exploratory execution into reusable hierarchical distilled experience through a four-stage loop: Explore, Compare, Distill, and Reinject. TimeClaw combines metric-supervised exploratory execution learning, task-aware tool dropout, and hierarchical distilled experience for inference-time reinjection, while keeping the base model frozen and avoiding online test-time adaptation. In an MTBench-aligned evaluation with 17 tasks that span finance and weather prediction and reasoning tasks, TimeClaw delivers consistent gains over the baselines. These results suggest that, for scientific systems, the bottleneck is not only execution-time capability, but how exploratory experience is compared, distilled, and reused.

</details>


### [323] [Route by State, Recover from Trace: STAR with Failure-Aware Markov Routing for Multi-Agent Spatiotemporal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10057)
