Moreover, models trained with VPR show improved performance on general and agentic reasoning benchmarks outside the training environments, indicating that the learned reasoning skills transfer beyond the specific oracle-verified tasks.

Conclusion: Verifiable Process Rewards (VPR) is an effective approach for training LLM agents on long-horizon reasoning tasks when reliable intermediate verification is available. By providing dense, localized learning signals, VPR improves credit assignment and yields reasoning skills that generalize to broader benchmarks. However, its effectiveness is contingent on the quality and reliability of the oracle, and extending this approach to less structured, open-ended domains without clear intermediate verifiers remains an open challenge.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has improved the reasoning abilities of large language models (LLMs), but most existing approaches rely on sparse outcome-level feedback. This sparsity creates a credit assignment challenge in long-horizon agentic reasoning: a trajectory may fail despite containing many correct intermediate decisions, or succeed despite containing flawed ones. In this work, we study a class of densely-verifiable agentic reasoning problems, where intermediate actions can be objectively checked by symbolic or algorithmic oracles. We propose Verifiable Process Rewards (VPR), a framework that converts such oracles into dense turn-level supervision for reinforcement learning, and instantiate it in three representative settings: search-based verification for dynamic deduction, constraint-based verification for logical reasoning, and posterior-based verification for probabilistic inference. We further provide a theoretical analysis showing that dense verifier-grounded rewards can improve long-horizon credit assignment by providing more localized learning signals, with the benefit depending on the reliability of the verifier. Empirically, VPR outperforms outcome-level reward and rollout-based process reward baselines across controlled environments, and more importantly, transfers to both general and agentic reasoning benchmarks, suggesting that verifiable process supervision can foster general reasoning skills applicable beyond the training environments. Our results indicate that VPR is a promising approach for enhancing LLM agents whenever reliable intermediate verification is available, while also highlighting its dependence on oracle quality and the open challenge of extending VPR to less structured, open-ended environments.

</details>


### [343] [EmbodiSkill: Skill-Aware Reflection for Self-Evolving Embodied Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10332)
*Ruofei Ju,Xinrui Wang,Xin Ding,Yifan Yang,Hao Wu,Shiqi Jiang,Qianxi Zhang,Hao Wen,Xiangyu Li,Weijun Wang,Kun Li,Yunxin Liu,Haipeng Dai,Wei Wang,Ting Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EmbodiSkill is a training-free framework that lets embodied agents automatically refine their own skills from task trajectories by distinguishing between genuine skill issues and execution mistakes, significantly improving task success in simulated embodied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodied agents operate in diverse, dynamic environments where layouts, object states, and execution factors vary, so they need reusable skills for object search and action execution that can adapt and improve over time. Existing self-evolving skill methods mainly come from digital or purely textual environments and treat trajectories in a coarse, undifferentiated way, often assuming that failure always indicates that the underlying skill is wrong. In embodied settings this is inadequate, because a task failure can also stem from the agent not following otherwise correct guidance (an execution lapse), so naïvely updating skills from failures can corrupt good procedural knowledge rather than improving it. The paper aims to address this gap by designing a way for embodied agents to interpret their own trajectories more carefully and evolve skills appropriately without additional training.

Method: The authors introduce EmbodiSkill, a training-free, reflection-based framework for self-evolving skills in embodied agents. For each task trajectory, EmbodiSkill compares what actually happened with the current skill specification and interprets the trajectory in a skill-aware way. It separates evidence into two types: (1) skill-changing evidence, which indicates that the skill’s content or structure (its “body”) should be updated, and (2) execution-lapse evidence, which shows that the underlying skill guidance is valid but was not properly followed during execution. EmbodiSkill then revises only the relevant parts of the skill body based on skill-changing evidence, while preserving and highlighting valid guidance when the failure is attributed to execution lapses. This mechanism lets the system refine procedural skills over time purely from trajectories, without gradient-based retraining of the base model. The framework is instantiated with large language model executors (e.g., Qwen3.5-27B) operating in embodied benchmarks like ALFWorld and EmbodiedBench.

Result: On the ALFWorld benchmark, EmbodiSkill applied to a frozen Qwen3.5-27B executor achieves a task success rate of 93.28%. This performance substantially exceeds that of a strong baseline where GPT-5.2 is used directly as an agent without explicit skills, with EmbodiSkill delivering a 31.58% absolute improvement in task success. Across both ALFWorld and EmbodiedBench, EmbodiSkill consistently improves embodied task performance, demonstrating the effectiveness of distinguishing skill errors from execution lapses and performing targeted skill revision. These empirical results indicate that the approach enables practical skill self-evolution in embodied environments without additional training of the underlying model.

Conclusion: EmbodiSkill shows that embodied agents can self-evolve their procedural skills in a training-free manner by performing skill-aware reflection on their own trajectories. By explicitly separating failures due to flawed skill content from those due to execution lapses, the framework can selectively revise skills while preserving and reinforcing valid guidance, leading to substantial gains in task success on embodied benchmarks. The findings suggest that careful interpretation of trajectories as evidence about skills, rather than treating all failures uniformly, is key to accumulating reusable, high-quality procedural knowledge in embodied agents. This opens a path toward more robust, continually improving embodied systems that learn from their own experience without expensive retraining.

Abstract: Embodied agents can benefit from skills that guide object search, action execution, and state changes across diverse environments. Since embodied environments vary across layouts, object states, and other execution factors, these skills must self-evolve from trajectories generated during task execution. However, existing skill self-evolution methods are mainly developed in digital environments and often convert trajectories into coarse skill updates. Directly applying this paradigm to embodied settings is problematic, because a failed task execution may reflect not only incorrect skill content, but also an execution lapse in which the agent fails to follow valid guidance. We propose EmbodiSkill, a training-free framework for embodied skill self-evolution through skill-aware reflection and targeted revision. EmbodiSkill interprets each trajectory with respect to the current skill, uses skill-changing evidence to update the skill body, and uses execution-lapse evidence to preserve and emphasize valid guidance. Experiments on ALFWorld and EmbodiedBench show that EmbodiSkill consistently improves embodied task success. On ALFWorld, EmbodiSkill enables a frozen Qwen3.5-27B executor to reach 93.28% task success, outperforming GPT-5.2 used as a direct agent without skills by 31.58%. These results show that skill-aware self-evolution helps embodied agents accumulate reusable procedural knowledge from their own trajectories.

</details>


### [344] [CORTEG: Foundation Models Enable Cross-Modality Representation Transfer from Scalp to Intracranial Brain Recordings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10337)
*Liuyin Yang,Qiang Sun,Bob Van Dyck,Eva Calvo Merino,Marc M. Van Hulle*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes CORTEG, a method that transfers large pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models to intracranial ECoG to enable cross-patient, data-efficient brain-computer interface decoding, achieving competitive or superior performance on movement and audio regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ECoG provides high-quality neural signals for brain-computer interfaces but each patient yields limited data, leading most models to be trained separately per subject and failing to exploit knowledge shared across patients. Meanwhile, large scalp-EEG foundation models exist but are modality-mismatched. The authors aim to overcome data scarcity and subject-specificity in ECoG by seeing if knowledge from EEG foundation models can be effectively transferred to ECoG, enabling cross-patient generalization and fast calibration for new patients.

Method: They introduce CORTEG, a cross-modality transfer framework that uses a large pretrained scalp-EEG foundation model as a backbone and adapts it to ECoG. CORTEG includes: (1) an electrode-aware KNNSoftFourier spatial adapter to map from ECoG electrode layouts to the EEG-pretrained representation; (2) a dual-stream tokenizer that separately encodes low-frequency and high-gamma ECoG activity; and (3) a leave-one-subject-out fine-tuning strategy to train on multiple patients while holding one out for adaptation. The system is fine-tuned for 10–30 minutes per new patient on a single GPU for task-specific regression decoding.

Result: On two regression tasks—public finger trajectory regression with 9 subjects and a private audio envelope regression with 16 subjects—CORTEG matches or outperforms strong task-specific ECoG baselines. It attains the highest mean correlation on the finger trajectory benchmark, although gains are not statistically significant given n=9, and shows larger, statistically significant improvements on the audio task and under low-data calibration regimes. Analyses of learned features show patterns consistent with known neurophysiology, and the latent spaces organize finger movements into low-dimensional manifolds that reflect their structure.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that scalp-EEG foundation model pretraining can be successfully repurposed for intracranial ECoG decoding. CORTEG enables data-efficient, cross-patient brain-computer interfaces that can be calibrated rapidly to new individuals while achieving competitive or superior decoding performance. This provides a systematic proof-of-concept for cross-modality transfer from noninvasive EEG to invasive ECoG in neural decoding applications.

Abstract: Intracranial electrocorticography (ECoG) offers high-signal-to-noise access to cortical activity for brain-computer interfaces, yet limited per-patient data has led most prior work to rely on small, subject-specific decoders that neglect information shared across patients. We investigate whether large pretrained scalp-EEG foundation models (EEG FMs) can be adapted to ECoG, enabling cross-patient learning and competitive decoding performance while calibrating to a held-out patient in 10-30 minutes on a single GPU. We introduce CORTEG, a cross-modality transfer framework that combines a pretrained EEG FM backbone, an electrode-aware KNNSoftFourier spatial adapter, a dual-stream tokenizer for low-frequency and high-gamma activity, and a leave-one-subject-out fine-tuning strategy. We evaluate CORTEG on two challenging regression tasks: public finger trajectory regression (n=9) and private audio envelope regression (n=16). CORTEG matches or exceeds the strongest task-specific baselines on both tasks: it reaches the highest mean correlation among compared methods on the public finger benchmark (gain not statistically significant on n=9 subjects), with larger and statistically significant gains on the audio task and in low-data per-patient calibration. Feature analyses align with neurophysiology, and latent manifolds capture low-dimensional finger-movement structure. CORTEG provides systematic evidence that scalp-EEG pretraining can be repurposed for ECoG decoding, enabling data-efficient intracranial BCIs that can adapt to new patients.

</details>


### [345] [PaperFit: Vision-in-the-Loop Typesetting Optimization for Scientific Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10341)
*Bihui Yu,Xinglong Xu,Junjie Jiang,Jiabei Cheng,Caijun Jia,Siyuan Li,Conghui He,Jingxuan Wei,Cheng Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Visual Typesetting Optimization (VTO) and a vision-in-the-loop agent, PaperFit, that iteratively analyzes rendered PDFs and edits LaTeX source to fix visual defects and meet page-budget constraints, substantially improving over existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compilable LaTeX manuscripts often contain visual and layout problems—misplaced floats, overflowing equations, bad table scaling, widows/orphans, and poor page balance—that require tedious manual trial-and-error. Existing rule-based tools only see source and logs, while text-only LLMs cannot reason about or verify two-dimensional layout effects of edits. There is thus a need for an automated, visually aware system that can close the loop between source edits and rendered PDF quality.

Method: The authors formalize Visual Typesetting Optimization (VTO) as transforming a compilable LaTeX document into a visually polished, page-budget-compliant PDF via iterative visual verification and source-level revision. They define a five-category taxonomy of typesetting defects to guide diagnosis. They propose PaperFit, a vision-in-the-loop agent that repeatedly renders pages, visually inspects them to detect categorized defects, and then applies constrained LaTeX repairs before re-rendering. To evaluate, they construct PaperFit-Bench, a benchmark of 200 LaTeX papers spanning 10 venue templates and 13 defect types at varying difficulty levels.

Result: On the newly created PaperFit-Bench, PaperFit significantly outperforms all baseline systems (rule-based tools and text-only LLM approaches) across the considered defect types and difficulty settings, demonstrating superior ability to produce visually polished, page-budget-compliant PDFs.

Conclusion: Effective typesetting optimization requires a visual closed-loop process that incorporates rendered PDF feedback, which existing rule-based and text-only LLM tools lack. The proposed Visual Typesetting Optimization task and the PaperFit agent fill this gap, and empirical results on PaperFit-Bench show that vision-in-the-loop optimization is a crucial missing stage in automated document preparation pipelines.

Abstract: A LaTeX manuscript that compiles without error is not necessarily publication-ready. The resulting PDFs frequently suffer from misplaced floats, overflowing equations, inconsistent table scaling, widow and orphan lines, and poor page balance, forcing authors into repetitive compile-inspect-edit cycles. Rule-based tools are blind to rendered visuals, operating only on source code and log files. Text-only LLMs perform open-loop text editing, unable to predict or verify the two-dimensional layout consequences of their changes. Reliable typesetting optimization therefore requires a visual closed loop with verification after every edit. We formalize this problem as Visual Typesetting Optimization (VTO), the task of transforming a compilable LaTeX paper into a visually polished, page-budget-compliant PDF through iterative visual verification and source-level revision, and introduce a five-category taxonomy of typesetting defects to guide diagnosis. We present PaperFit, a vision-in-the-loop agent that iteratively renders pages, diagnoses defects, and applies constrained repairs. To benchmark VTO, we construct PaperFit-Bench with 200 papers across 10 venue templates and 13 defect types at different difficulty. Extensive experiments show that PaperFit outperforms all baselines by a large margin, establishing that bridging the gap from compilable source to publication-ready PDF requires vision-in-the-loop optimization and that VTO constitutes a critical missing stage in the document automation pipeline.

</details>


### [346] [TMAS: Scaling Test-Time Compute via Multi-Agent Synergy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10344)
*George Wu,Nan Jing,Qing Yi,Chuan Hao,Ming Yang,Feng Chang,Yuan Wei,Jian Yang,Ran Tao,Bryan Dai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TMAS, a multi-agent, memory-augmented test-time scaling framework that coordinates multiple reasoning trajectories and refinement iterations to improve large language model reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time scaling techniques improve reasoning by spending more computation at inference, but they either loosely coordinate parallel reasoning paths or reuse past information in an unstructured, noisy way. This makes it hard to balance exploring new reasoning strategies with exploiting reliable partial results. The authors want a more principled, structured mechanism to coordinate multiple reasoning trajectories and iterations so that the model can reuse good intermediate insights while avoiding redundant or unproductive reasoning patterns.

Method: They propose TMAS, which frames inference as collaboration among specialized agents. These agents interact via structured information flow across trajectories and refinement rounds, supported by hierarchical memories: (1) an experience bank that stores reliable low-level intermediate conclusions and local feedback for reuse; and (2) a guideline bank that records higher-level strategies previously explored, guiding future rollouts away from redundant reasoning. On top of this, they design a hybrid-reward reinforcement learning scheme that both maintains base reasoning skills and optimizes for better use of experience and exploration of novel strategies under the TMAS framework.

Result: On difficult reasoning benchmarks, TMAS shows stronger performance gains as computation (iterations) increases, compared with existing test-time scaling baselines. The hybrid reward RL training further improves how well performance scales with more iterations and makes the iterative process more stable.

Conclusion: Structuring test-time scaling as a multi-agent collaborative process with hierarchical memories and a tailored hybrid-reward RL scheme leads to more effective and stable improvements in LLM reasoning as additional inference-time computation is used. TMAS better balances exploration and exploitation than prior methods and yields stronger iterative scaling on challenging reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Test-time scaling has become an effective paradigm for improving the reasoning ability of large language models by allocating additional computation during inference. Recent structured approaches have further advanced this paradigm by organizing inference across multiple trajectories, refinement rounds, and verification-based feedback. However, existing structured test-time scaling methods either weakly coordinate parallel reasoning trajectories or rely on noisy historical information without explicitly deciding what should be retained and reused, limiting their ability to balance exploration and exploitation. In this work, we propose TMAS, a framework for scaling test-time compute via multi-agent synergy. TMAS organizes inference as a collaborative process among specialized agents, enabling structured information flow across agents, trajectories, and refinement iterations. To support effective cross-trajectory collaboration, TMAS introduces hierarchical memories: the experience bank reuses low-level reliable intermediate conclusions and local feedback, while the guideline bank records previously explored high-level strategies to steer subsequent rollouts away from redundant reasoning patterns. Furthermore, we design a hybrid reward reinforcement learning scheme tailored to TMAS, which jointly preserves basic reasoning capability, enhances experience utilization, and encourages exploration beyond previously attempted solution strategies. Extensive experiments on challenging reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that TMAS achieves stronger iterative scaling than existing test-time scaling baselines, while hybrid reward training further improves scaling effectiveness and stability across iterations. Code and data are available at https://github.com/george-QF/TMAS-code.

</details>


### [347] [How Mobile World Model Guides GUI Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10347)
*Weikai Xu,Kun Huang,Yunren Feng,Jiaxing Li,Yuhan Chen,Yuxuan Liu,Zhizheng Jiang,Heng Qu,Pengzhi Gao,Wei Liu,Jian Luan,Xiaolin Hu,Bo An*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies different types of world models for mobile GUI agents, comparing text, image, and code-based future-state representations, and evaluates how they help downstream mobile control tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile GUI agents powered by vision-language models can see screens and follow instructions, but they struggle with reliably predicting the consequences of their actions, which is crucial for long, complex, or high-risk tasks. Existing mobile world models predict future states either as text or as images, but it is unclear which representation works best, whether synthetic rollouts can substitute for real environment interactions, and how to best use world models at test time for agents with different levels of competence.

Method: The authors curate and relabel a mobile world-model dataset and train multiple world models, each predicting future states in a different modality: (1) delta text (only changes), (2) full text descriptions, (3) diffusion-based images, and (4) renderable code that can reconstruct the GUI. They evaluate these models on MobileWorldBench and Code2WorldBench benchmarks, then measure how useful each type is for downstream mobile-agent tasks (AITZ, AndroidControl, AndroidWorld). They analyze in-distribution vs OOD performance, the impact of using model-generated trajectories as training data, and the effects of using world models for test-time guidance and self-reflection in agents with different action entropy (confidence levels).

Result: The trained world models reach state-of-the-art performance on MobileWorldBench and Code2WorldBench. Renderable code achieves high-fidelity reconstructions of in-distribution mobile interfaces and serves as strong multimodal supervision for building datasets, while text-based feedback proves more robust when executing in OOD environments. Synthetic trajectories produced by the world models can effectively augment training, giving agents additional interaction experience and boosting end-to-end performance, despite not matching the original data distribution. For strong, low-entropy (overconfident) agents, using the world model for posterior self-reflection yields only marginal benefits.

Conclusion: Renderable code is particularly powerful for accurate in-distribution reconstruction and supervision, whereas text-based outputs are better suited for robust OOD execution. World models are valuable for generating training data and as prior perception modules rather than as general post-hoc verifiers, especially for already strong, low-entropy agents. Overall, learned mobile world models across multiple modalities can significantly aid the training and performance of mobile GUI agents, but their role at test time must be carefully chosen depending on agent characteristics and deployment conditions.

Abstract: Recent advances in vision-language models have enabled mobile GUI agents to perceive visual interfaces and execute user instructions, but reliable prediction of action consequences remains critical for long-horizon and high-risk interactions. Existing mobile world models provide either text-based or image-based future states, yet it remains unclear which representation is useful, whether generated rollouts can replace real environments, and how test-time guidance helps agents of different strengths. To answer the above questions, we filter and annotate mobile world-model data, then train world models across four modalities: delta text, full text, diffusion-based images, and renderable code. These models achieve SoTA performance on both MobileWorldBench and Code2WorldBench. Furthermore, by evaluating their downstream utility on AITZ, AndroidControl, and AndroidWorld, we obtain three findings. First, renderable code reconstruction achieves high in-distribution fidelity and provides effective multimodal supervision for data construction, while text-based feedback is more robust for online out-of-distribution (OOD) execution. Second, world-model-generated trajectories can provide transferable interaction experience in the training process and improve agents' end-to-end task performance, although these data do not preserve the original distribution. Last, for overconfident mobile agents with low action entropy, posterior self-reflection provides limited gains, suggesting that world models are more effective as prior perception or training supervision than as universal post-hoc verifiers.

</details>


### [348] [Agent-ValueBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Agent Values](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10365)
*Haonan Dong,Qiguan Feng,Kehan Jiang,Haoran Ye,Xin Zhang,Guojie Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benchmarks agent values, showing they differ from base LLM values, and introduces Agent-ValueBench to study and steer them via harness and skill alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous LLM-based agents are widely deployed but their underlying value systems are poorly understood, and current value benchmarks only assess raw LLMs, not agents in interactive environments.

Method: Design Agent-ValueBench: 394 executable environments in 16 domains, with 4,335 value-conflict tasks spanning 28 value systems and 332 dimensions. Each task has curated golden trajectories and a rubric-based judge. Use it to evaluate 14 leading models across 4 agent harnesses, analyzing how agent values emerge and shift under different harnesses and skill steering.

Result: Empirically show that agent values differ from those of the base LLM, identify a pervasive "Value Tide" of shared value tendencies across models, and demonstrate that harness design and embedded skills can non-additively bend these value patterns more than basic prompt or model alignment.

Conclusion: Agent values are an emergent property of the full agent stack, not just the base model. Effective alignment for autonomous agents should focus less on only model-level tuning and prompting, and more on harness-level design and skill steering, guided by benchmarks like Agent-ValueBench.

Abstract: Autonomous agents have rapidly matured as task executors and seen widespread deployment via harnesses such as OpenClaw. Safety concerns have rightly drawn growing research attention, and beneath them lie the values silently steering agent behavior. Existing value benchmarks, however, remain confined to LLMs, leaving agent values largely uncharted. From intuitive, empirical, and theoretical vantage points, we show that an agent's values diverge from those of its underlying LLM, and the agentic modality further introduces dataset-, evaluation-, and system-level challenges absent from text-only protocols. We close this gap with Agent-ValueBench, the first benchmark dedicated to agent values. It features 394 executable environments across 16 domains, offering 4,335 value-conflict tasks that cover 28 value systems and 332 dimensions. Every instance is co-synthesized through our purpose-built end-to-end pipeline and curated per-instance by professional psychologists. Each task ships with two pole-aligned golden trajectories whose checkpoints anchor a trajectory-level rubric-based judge. Benchmarking 14 frontier proprietary and open-weights models across 4 mainstream harnesses, we uncover three concerted findings. Agent values first manifest as a Value Tide of cross-model homogeneity beneath interpretable counter-currents. This tide bends non-additively under harness pull, and yet more decisively under deliberate steering via embedded skills. Together these results signal that the agent-alignment lever is shifting from classical model alignment and prompt steering toward harness alignment and skill steering.

</details>


### [349] [EGL-SCA: Structural Credit Assignment for Co-Evolving Instructions and Tools in Graph Reasoning Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10366)
*Zike Yuan,Yukun Cao,Han Zhang,Jianzhi Yan,Le Liu,Cai ke,Yue Yu,Hui Wang,Ming Liu,Bing Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework (EGL-SCA) for graph reasoning agents that jointly optimizes natural-language reasoning instructions and executable tools, using verifier-guided structural credit assignment to decide what to update after failures, achieving strong state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph reasoning from natural language requires more than just good prompts or powerful tools: agents must (1) infer graph structure from text, (2) decide if available tools suffice, (3) interact with tools under strict protocols, and (4) pass a structured verifier. Existing work typically focuses on either improving instructions (reasoning policies) or the toolset in isolation, leaving a gap in how to systematically determine, after an error, whether to revise the prompting / reasoning strategy or to modify the underlying algorithmic tools. The paper aims to address this credit-assignment problem for graph reasoning agents.

Method: The authors propose EGL-SCA, a dual-space, verifier-centric framework with: (1) an instruction-side policy space governing natural-language reasoning strategies, and (2) a tool-side program space containing executable graph algorithms. A structural credit assignment mechanism analyzes trajectories and verifier feedback to route updates: failures caused by reasoning lead to prompt/policy optimization, while failures tied to algorithmic limitations trigger program synthesis, repair, or expansion of the toolset. Training tasks are organized into families, and a Pareto-style retention strategy is used to keep a balanced set of solutions that trade off success rate, generality across tasks, and parsimony (simplicity/compactness of instructions and tools).

Result: On four graph reasoning benchmarks, EGL-SCA reaches an average success rate of 92.0%, which is reported as state-of-the-art. The framework substantially outperforms baselines that rely only on improved prompting (pure instruction-side optimization) or that assume a fixed toolbox of graph algorithms without adaptive tool learning or repair.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling and updating both the instruction-side reasoning policy and the tool-side program space, guided by a structural verifier and explicit credit assignment, leads to significantly better graph reasoning performance than optimizing either side alone. Verifier-centric structural credit assignment and carefully designed training/retention strategies enable effective co-evolution of language instructions and algorithmic tools, suggesting a promising direction for building more robust, adaptable graph reasoning agents.

Abstract: Graph reasoning agents operating from natural-language inputs must solve a coupled problem: they must reconstruct a structured graph instance from text, decide whether existing computational assets are sufficient, interact with tools under a strict execution protocol, and satisfy an external verifier that checks structured correctness rather than textual plausibility. Existing approaches usually improve either the instruction side or the tool side in isolation, which leaves unclear what should be updated after failure. We propose EGL-SCA, a verifier-centric dual-space framework that models a graph reasoning agent using two collaborative components: an instruction-side policy space for reasoning strategies, and a tool-side program space for executable algorithmic tools. Our central mechanism is structural credit assignment, which maps trajectory evidence to conditional updates, precisely routing failures to either prompt optimization or tool synthesis and repair. To provide sufficient learning signals for dual-space adaptation, we introduce a training distribution stratified by task family, coupled with a Pareto-style retention strategy to balance success, generality, and parsimony. Experiments on four graph reasoning benchmarks show that EGL-SCA achieves a state-of-the-art 92.0\% average success rate. By effectively co-evolving instructions and tools, our framework significantly outperforms both pure-prompting and fixed-toolbox baselines.

</details>


### [350] [Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects: From Passive Assertions to Active Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10370)
*Zeyd Boukhers,Oya Beyan,Cong Yang,Christoph Lange*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes and operationalizes Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs), which can actively manage and reconcile scientific knowledge over time using Semantic Web standards, reputation, and consensus mechanisms, and evaluates them on real rare-disease datasets under both normal and adversarial conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific knowledge on the Web is currently represented as static, passive assertions that cannot autonomously validate new evidence, handle contradictions, or update confidence as more data become available. Knowledge curation is heavily dependent on centralized institutions and registries; when these close or lose funding, stewardship of the data effectively stops even if the data remain online, threatening long-term reliability and sustainability. The authors aim to create a standards-based, automated framework that allows scientific knowledge objects to maintain, update, and defend their own integrity over time, in a way that is less dependent on specific institutions and better aligned with FAIR and Semantic Web principles.

Method: The authors extend the concept of FAIR Digital Objects into Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs) by adding three key capabilities, all grounded in Semantic Web standards. (1) A policy layer over RDF-star, aligned with PROV-O, SHACL, and ODRL, expresses portable condition–action rules for how an object should react to events and evidence. (2) An announcement layer based on ActivityStreams 2.0 enables structured event announcements with bounded per-announcement evaluation cost. (3) An agreement layer implements a consensus mechanism that resolves multi-source contradictions using reputation- and confidence-weighted agreement under a bounded adversarial model. They give a formal specification that separates policy logic, event-handler behavior, and communication interfaces, and implement an open reference system. They then evaluate it on 4,305 FAIR Digital Objects derived from rare-disease resources (ClinVar, HPO, Orphanet) plus synthetic observations, testing conflict resolution against known expert adjudications and subjecting the consensus layer to various adversarial attacks (Sybil, collusion, poisoning) to observe behavior within and beyond a defined Byzantine tolerance (f < n/5).

Result: On 4,305 aFDO instances grounded in rare-disease ontologies and data (ClinVar, HPO, Orphanet) with controlled synthetic inputs, the proposed consensus mechanism correctly resolves 56.3% of 3,914 real-world ClinVar conflicts where submitters disagree and an expert panel has later issued a decision, indicating substantial though not perfect alignment with expert curation. Under adversarial conditions including Sybil attacks, collusion, and data poisoning, the system’s performance degrades gradually rather than catastrophically as long as the fraction of malicious actors remains below the designed Byzantine fault tolerance threshold (f < n/5); beyond this threshold, the mechanism fails in ways consistent with the formal model’s predictions. This demonstrates both empirical effectiveness in realistic biomedical curation conflicts and predictable behavior under adversarial stress.

Conclusion: Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs) provide a concrete, standards-based pathway from static, institution-dependent scientific publications toward self-governing digital objects that can apply policies, communicate, and reach consensus about conflicting evidence over time. The formal model and reference implementation show that aFDOs can meaningfully reconcile many real-world conflicts in biomedical variant interpretation and maintain robust behavior under bounded adversarial conditions. This supports the feasibility of long-lived, accountable automation in scientific knowledge management that is less dependent on the continuity of specific registries or institutions, and suggests that future infrastructures could embed such autonomous capabilities directly into digital scientific artifacts.

Abstract: Scientific knowledge on the Web is published as passive assertions and cannot decide when to validate evidence, reconcile contradictions, or update confidence as findings accumulate. Curation depends on centralised middleware and institutional continuity, but when registries close, active stewardship stops even when data remain online. We advance the concept of Autonomous FAIR Digital Objects (aFDOs) from an abstract idea to an operational model, to offer a route from passive scientific publication toward accountable, standards-aligned automation that can outlive its publishing institutions. aFDO augments FDOs with three capabilities anchored in Semantic Web standards, namely 1) a policy layer over RDF-star aligned with PROV-O, SHACL, and ODRL for portable condition-action rules, 2) an announcement layer over ActivityStreams 2.0 that bounds per-announcement evaluation cost, and 3) an agreement layer that resolves multi-source contradictions through reputation and confidence weighted agreement under a bounded adversarial model. We provide a formal definition that distinguishes policy specifications, event handlers, and communication interfaces. We evaluate an open reference implementation on 4,305 FDOs grounded in rare-disease ontologies, namely ClinVar, HPO, and Orphanet, combined with controlled synthetic observations. The consensus mechanism resolves 56.3% of 3,914 naturally occurring ClinVar conflicts where multiple submitters disagree and an expert panel has subsequently adjudicated. Under Sybil, collusion, and poisoning attacks, the mechanism degrades gracefully within its design Byzantine-tolerance bound (f < n/5), and fails as predicted beyond that bound.

</details>


### [351] [Agent-X: Full Pipeline Acceleration of On-device AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10380)
*Jinha Chung,Byeongjun Shin,Jiin Kim,Minsoo Rhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent-X is a software-only framework that speeds up on-device LLM-based agents without hurting accuracy by optimizing both prefill and decode latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents perform well but suffer from high end-to-end latency on edge and on-device deployments, limiting their practicality. Existing work has not systematically characterized or removed these latency bottlenecks in the specific context of agentic workloads.

Method: The paper proposes Agent-X, which introduces two main techniques: (1) prompt rewriting to better exploit prefix caching based on common, agent-specific input token patterns; and (2) LLM-free speculative decoding to generate tokens quickly with minimal overhead. The framework targets both the prefill and decode stages of the inference pipeline and is designed as software-only so it can be dropped into current on-device agents.

Result: On representative on-device agent workloads, Agent-X delivers a 1.61× end-to-end speedup in real systems while maintaining the same accuracy. It can be integrated into existing on-device AI agents without architectural changes.

Conclusion: Agent-X effectively removes key latency bottlenecks in on-device LLM-based agents, accelerating both prefill and decode while preserving accuracy. The authors claim it is the first systematic characterization and optimization of latency in on-device agentic workloads, and it offers a practical, deployable path to faster edge-based LLM agents.

Abstract: LLM-based agents deliver state-of-the-art performance across tasks but incur high end-to-end latency on edge devices. We introduce Agent-X, a software-only, accuracy-preserving framework that accelerates both the prefill and decode stages of on-device agent workloads. Agent-X's two key components rewrite prompts to leverage prefix caching tailored to agent-specific input-token patterns and enable LLM-free speculative decoding for fast token generation with minimal overhead. On representative agentic workloads, Agent-X achieves a 1.61x end-to-end speedup in real systems with no accuracy loss and can be seamlessly integrated into existing on-device AI agents. To the best of our knowledge, ours is the first to systematically characterize and eliminate latency bottlenecks in on-device agents.

</details>


### [352] [Agentic Performance at the Edge: Insights from Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10384)
*Shiqiang Wang,Herbert Woisetschläger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically studies how well smaller, edge-deployable AI agents perform on agentic tasks, showing that quality is not determined only by parameter count and depends strongly on how models are paired with tools and workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Edge and IoT environments often cannot host very large AI models due to memory, power, and latency constraints, yet they are prime candidates for agentic AI. The authors want to understand how much capability is lost when using smaller models and how to best design edge agents under such constraints.

Method: They run an empirical study comparing different model sizes (focused on edge-feasible scales), general-purpose versus code-specialized models, and tool-augmented execution, all under a fixed interaction protocol. They use a domain-conditioned evaluation framework and analyze logs of model-tool interactions to understand behavior and failure modes.

Result: They find that agent performance at the edge does not monotonically track parameter count: smaller or specialized models can sometimes be competitive when well matched with tools and workflows. They identify distinct patterns of semantic versus execution failures across model types and characterize accuracy-latency trade-offs, yielding Pareto fronts for different domains.

Conclusion: Successful edge-agent deployment requires co-design of model selection and tool workflows rather than relying on model size alone. Their domain-conditioned evaluation and analysis framework can guide practitioners to choose model-tool combinations that lie on favorable accuracy-latency Pareto fronts aligned with operational priorities.

Abstract: Agentic artificial intelligence (AI) is a natural fit for Internet of Things (IoT) and edge systems, but edge deployments are often constrained to models around 8 billion parameters or smaller. An important question is: How much agentic-task quality is lost when model size is constrained by memory, power, and latency budgets? To address this question, in this paper, we provide an initial empirical study considering edge-focused model scaling, general-purpose versus coder-oriented model effects, and tool-enabled execution under a fixed protocol. We introduce a domain-conditioned evaluation methodology, an implementation-grounded analysis of model-tool interactions, practical guidance for model selection under constraints, and an analysis of failure modes that reveals distinct semantic versus execution failure patterns across model families. Our core finding is that edge-agent quality is not a simple function of parameter count. Robust deployment depends on the joint design of model choice and tool workflow. Domain-conditioned analysis reveals Pareto fronts in the accuracy-latency space that can guide strategy selection based on operational priorities.

</details>


### [353] [GuardAD: Safeguarding Autonomous Driving MLLMs via Markovian Safety Logic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10386)
*Tianyuan Zhang,Peng Yue,Zihao Peng,Jiangfan Liu,Zonghao Ying,Jiakai Wang,Tianlin Li,Jian Yang,Yaodong Yang,Aishan Liu,Xianglong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GuardAD is a model-agnostic neuro-symbolic safeguard that monitors and revises actions of multimodal LLM-based autonomous driving systems using evolving Markovian logical safety states, significantly cutting accidents while slightly improving task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal large language model (MLLM) based autonomous driving systems are vulnerable to safety threats, especially in complex, accident-prone scenarios. Current safeguard mechanisms mainly use static logical constraints that do not capture temporal evolution of traffic and interactions between participants, leading to weak robustness in dynamic environments. There is a need for a safeguard that reasons about safety over time, detects emerging and latent hazards, and improves safety without retraining or modifying the underlying MLLMs.

Method: The paper proposes GuardAD, a model-agnostic safety safeguard that formalizes autonomous driving safety as an evolving Markovian logical state. It introduces a Neuro-Symbolic Logic Formalization to define safety predicates over heterogeneous traffic participants, and uses n-th order Markovian Logic Induction to continuously update these predicates based on temporal observations. This enables reasoning about hazards over multiple time steps. Instead of only blocking unsafe actions, GuardAD performs Logic-Driven Action Revision: inferred safety states are used to refine and correct the suggested actions from the base MLLM, all without modifying the MLLM itself.

Result: In experiments across multiple autonomous driving benchmarks and various AD-focused MLLMs, GuardAD significantly reduces accident rates by 32.07% on average, while also slightly increasing overall task performance by 6.85%. Additional closed-loop simulation experiments and real-world vehicle tests further demonstrate that GuardAD effectively enhances safety and maintains or improves driving performance in practice.

Conclusion: GuardAD offers an effective, model-agnostic neuro-symbolic safeguard for MLLM-based autonomous driving systems by turning safety into an evolving Markovian logical state and using this to revise actions rather than only vetoing them. This approach enables detection of emerging and latent hazards over time and leads to substantially fewer accidents with improved task performance. The combination of benchmark, simulation, and real-world evaluations suggests that GuardAD is a practical and promising direction for enhancing the safety of MLLM-driven autonomous driving without changing the underlying models.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly integrated into autonomous driving (AD) systems; however, they remain vulnerable to diverse safety threats, particularly in accident-prone scenarios. Recent safeguard mechanisms have shown promise by incorporating logical constraints, yet most rely on static formulations that lack temporally grounded safety reasoning over evolving traffic interactions, resulting in limited robustness in dynamic driving environments. To address these limitations, we propose GuardAD, a model-agnostic safeguard that formulates AD safety as an evolving Markovian logical state. GuardAD introduces Neuro-Symbolic Logic Formalization, which represents safety predicates over heterogeneous traffic participants and continuously induces them via n-th order Markovian Logic Induction. This design enables the inference of emerging and latent hazards beyond single-step observations. Rather than simply vetoing unsafe actions, GuardAD performs Logic-Driven Action Revision, where inferred safety states actively guide action refinement without modifying the underlying MLLM. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks and AD-MLLMs demonstrate that GuardAD substantially reduces accident rates (-32.07%) while slightly improving task performance (+6.85%). Moreover, closed-loop simulation evaluations, together with physical-world vehicle studies, further validate the effectiveness and potential of GuardAD.

</details>


### [354] [LLM4Branch: Large Language Model for Discovering Efficient Branching Policies of Integer Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10401)
*Zhinan Hou,Xingchen Li,Yankai Zhang,Tianxun Li,Keyou You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM4Branch uses large language models to automatically generate and tune branching policies for MILP solvers, achieving state-of-the-art CPU performance without relying on expert demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing effective branching policies for MILP solvers is critical for performance but traditionally depends on manually crafted heuristics or expensive expert data. Existing learning-based approaches struggle due to reliance on expert demonstrations and a mismatch between their training objectives and actual end-to-end solver performance. The paper aims to remove these bottlenecks and automate branching policy design in a more direct, performance-driven manner.

Method: The authors introduce LLM4Branch, a framework that uses a Large Language Model to generate a program skeleton representing a branching policy. This skeleton includes a parameterized structure whose parameters are not learned via gradients but are optimized with a zeroth-order optimization method. The optimization is performed on a small set of MILP instances using only end-to-end performance feedback from the solver, effectively closing the loop between policy design and final performance.

Result: On standard MILP benchmarks, the discovered branching policies produced by LLM4Branch achieve a new state-of-the-art among CPU-based methods and reach performance levels comparable to sophisticated GPU-based models, demonstrating the effectiveness and efficiency of the approach.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that LLMs can be used not just for text generation but as automated designers of algorithmic policies. By coupling LLM-generated policy programs with black-box performance optimization, LLM4Branch can discover competitive branching strategies without expert demonstrations, offering a scalable and effective route to improving MILP solvers.

Abstract: Efficient branching policies are essential for accelerating Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) solvers. Their design has long relied on hand-crafted heuristics, and now machine learning has emerged as a promising paradigm to automate this process. However, existing learning-based methods are often hindered by their dependence on expensive expert demonstrations and the gap between training objectives and the solver's end-to-end performance. In this work, we propose LLM4Branch, a novel framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate the discovery of efficient branching policies. Specifically, the discovered policy is an executable program with a program skeleton generated by the LLM and a parameter vector, which is optimized via a zeroth-order method over a few instances with their end-to-end performance feedback. Extensive experiments on standard MILP benchmarks demonstrate that LLM4Branch establishes a new state-of-the-art among CPU-based methods and achieves performance competitive with advanced GPU-based models. Codes are available at https://github.com/hzn18/LLM4Branch.

</details>


### [355] [Can Agent Benchmarks Support Their Scores? Evidence-Supported Bounds for Interactive-Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10448)
*Shanshan Gao,Liyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces an “outcome evidence reporting layer” for interactive agent benchmarks to make success/failure judgments more reliable and transparent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing interactive agent benchmarks often label runs as successes based on superficial outcome checks (e.g., whether a button was clicked) that don’t actually confirm the intended state change or the correctness of the full action path. This makes benchmark scores misleading and obscures different failure modes. There is a need for a way to systematically capture and expose the uncertainty in outcome detection without redesigning benchmarks or agents.

Method: The authors add a separate reporting layer on top of existing benchmarks that does not alter tasks, agents, or evaluators. This layer (1) specifies, in advance, which stored artifacts (logs, screenshots, state dumps, etc.) are needed to verify each claimed outcome; (2) applies a fixed, “locked” checklist to every completed run and assigns one of three evidence labels: Evidence Pass, Evidence Fail, or Unknown, depending on whether artifacts sufficiently support the claimed outcome; and (3) computes and reports evidence-aware score bounds that explicitly account for runs labeled Unknown, instead of folding them invisibly into a single success rate.

Result: Applied to five public benchmarks—ANDROIDWORLD, AGENTDOJO, APPWORLD, tau3 bench retail, and MINIWOB—the outcome evidence layer produced reports that break down runs by their evidence labels and reveal several distinct failure modes that are not visible in traditional single-number success metrics. This demonstrates that many runs previously counted as ‘successful’ are actually uncertain or unsupported by strong evidence.

Conclusion: Benchmark quality depends critically on how outcomes are detected and evidenced, not just on task design. The proposed outcome evidence reporting layer makes benchmark evaluations more reliable and interpretable by (a) requiring explicit verification artifacts, (b) labeling runs by evidence strength, and (c) propagating uncertainty into reported score bounds. This clarifies where benchmarks and agents truly succeed or fail and surfaces uncertainty instead of hiding it in aggregate metrics.

Abstract: Interactive agent benchmarks map an agent run to a binary outcome through outcome checks. When these checks rely on surface level signals or fail to capture the agent's actual action path, they cannot reliably determine whether the run succeeded. For example, a benchmark task may ask whether Alice's shipping address was changed, while the outcome check only verifies that the agent clicked "Save." This does not guarantee that the intended state change occurred, since the agent may have modified the wrong record. Treating such a run as successful therefore makes the reported score misleading. Benchmark quality thus depends not only on task design, but also on the reliability of outcome detection.
  We address this problem by introducing an outcome evidence reporting layer for existing benchmarks, without modifying their tasks, agents, or evaluators. The layer performs three functions. First, before scoring, it specifies which stored artifacts are required to verify the claimed outcome for each case. Second, it applies a locked checklist to each completed run and assigns one of three evidence labels: Evidence Pass, Evidence Fail, or Unknown. Third, it reports evidence supported score bounds that quantify uncertainty arising from Unknown cases. Rather than silently counting, discarding, or hiding uncertain cases inside a single aggregate success rate, the framework keeps them explicitly visible.
  We evaluate the outcome evidence layer on five public benchmarks: ANDROIDWORLD, AGENTDOJO, APPWORLD, tau3 bench retail, and MINIWOB. The resulting reports separate several empirically distinct failure modes.

</details>


### [356] [ASIA: an Autonomous System Identification Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10480)
*Dario Piga,Marco Forgione*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ASIA, an autonomous large-language-model-driven framework that automates the trial-and-error process in system identification by iteratively proposing, implementing, and evaluating model architectures from natural language problem descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong theoretical foundations in system identification, practical model-building still depends heavily on manual trial-and-error in choosing model classes, training algorithms, and hyperparameters. This is time-consuming and requires significant expert knowledge. At the same time, recent progress in agentic AI shows that large language models can act as autonomous coding and experimentation agents, motivating their use to automate the system identification workflow.

Method: The authors develop ASIA, an agentic framework that leverages a large language model as an autonomous coding agent. Given a plain-English description of a system identification task, ASIA iteratively generates hypotheses (model architectures and training strategies), implements corresponding code via an agentic platform, runs experiments, and evaluates performance, thereby closing the loop from hypothesis to evaluation without human intervention. They empirically evaluate ASIA on two standard system identification benchmarks and log/analyze the agent's search behavior.

Result: On two benchmark system identification tasks, ASIA is able to automatically discover viable architectures, training strategies, and hyperparameters that yield competitive dynamical models. The empirical study provides data on the quality of the obtained models and insights into how the agent explores the design space during search.

Conclusion: ASIA demonstrates that an LLM-based autonomous agent can effectively automate much of the system identification workflow, reducing the need for manual experimentation and expert intervention. However, the approach raises important issues, including risks of implicit test leakage, reduced transparency in methodology, and challenges for reproducibility, which must be addressed for robust scientific and engineering use.

Abstract: Over the years, research in system identification has provided a rich set of methods for learning dynamical models, together with well-established theoretical guarantees. In practice, however, the choice of model class, training algorithm, and hyperparameter tuning is still largely left to empirical trial-and-error, requiring substantial expert time and domain experience. Motivated by recent advances in agentic artificial intelligence, we present ASIA, a framework that delegates this iterative search to a large language model acting as an autonomous coding agent. Building on existing agentic platforms, ASIA closes the loop between hypothesis, implementation, and evaluation without human intervention, requiring only a plain-English description of the identification problem. We conduct an empirical study of ASIA on two system identification benchmarks and analyse the agent's search behaviour, the architectures and training strategies it discovers, and the quality of the resulting models. We also discuss the potential of the approach and its current limitations, including implicit test leakage, reduced methodological transparency, and reproducibility concerns.

</details>


### [357] [SkillEvolver: Skill Learning as a Meta-Skill](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10500)
*Genrui Zhang,Erle Zhu,Jinfeng Zhou,Caiyan Jia,Hongning Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillEvolver is a meta-skill that automatically writes, deploys, and iteratively improves agent skills from real usage feedback, without retraining model weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent skills are static: they are created once by humans or one-shot generation and then never improved, so they cannot learn from deployment failures or adapt to new domains. There is a need for a general, lightweight mechanism that lets skills continuously improve based on how agents actually use them, while remaining portable across agents and models.

Method: Introduce SkillEvolver, a meta-skill that treats skills as editable artifacts (prose plus code). SkillEvolver: (1) authors an initial domain-specific skill, (2) deploys it into an agent, (3) observes failures that arise when that separate agent uses the skill in real tasks, and (4) refines the skill text/code accordingly. The meta-skill itself is loaded like any other skill via a CLI-agent interface. Learning is done on the skill specification, not on model parameters. A “fresh-agent overfit audit” with a new agent instance checks each refinement to detect overfitting, leakage, and issues like silent-bypass (skills that look correct but are never called at runtime). This process iterates online during actual use, distinct from offline trace distillation which optimizes from stored traces alone.

Result: On 83 SkillsBench tasks across more than 15 domains, skills produced and refined with SkillEvolver achieve 56.8% accuracy, outperforming curated human skills at 43.6% and a no-skill baseline at 29.9%. On three GPU kernel optimization tasks from KernelBench, SkillEvolver-improved skills increase mean runtime speedup from 1.16× to 1.51× compared to baseline.

Conclusion: Online, artifact-level skill learning via a meta-skill can substantially improve agent performance over static, manually curated skills without modifying model weights. SkillEvolver shows that treating skills as editable prose+code and refining them from real deployment failures yields better accuracy and efficiency across diverse domains, while remaining plug-and-play for existing CLI-based agents.

Abstract: Agent skills today are static artifact: authored once -- by human curation or one-shot generation from parametric knowledge -- and then consumed unchanged, with no mechanism to improve from real use. We propose \textbf{SkillEvolver}, a lightweight, plug-and-play solution for online skill learning, in which a single meta-skill iteratively authors, deploys, and refines domain-specific skills. The learning target of SkillEvolver is the skill's prose and code, not model weights, so that the resulting artifact drops into any agent without retraining; and the meta-skill itself is just another skill, loaded through the same interface by any protocol-compliant CLI-agent. Unlike trace-distillation, the meta-skill refines only after deploying the learnt skill, such that the learning signal comes from failures another agent encounters while using it -- not from exploratory traces alone. Refinement iterations are governed by a fresh-agent overfit audit that catches possible leakage as well as deployed-skill-specific failures, including the silent-bypass mode in which a skill appears valid in content but is never invoked at runtime. On $83$ SkillsBench tasks spanning $15^{+}$ domains, SkillEvolver reaches $56.8\%$ accuracy versus $43.6\%$ for curated human skills and $29.9\%$ for the no-skill baseline; on three GPU kernel optimization tasks from KernelBench, it also raises mean speedup from $1.16$ to $1.51$ on average.

</details>


### [358] [SLASH the Sink: Sharpening Structural Attention Inside LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10503)
*Yiming Liu,Bin Lu,Xinbing Wang,Chenghu Zhou,Meng Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper finds that LLMs already internally reconstruct graph structure but this is weakened by attention sinks; it formalizes this as a representation bottleneck and proposes a training-free attention-redistribution method (Slash) that sharpens structural attention and improves graph and molecular prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are good at semantic understanding but weak at structural reasoning on graphs serialized as text. Existing fixes (adapters, fine-tuning) are costly and hurt generalizability. The authors want to know whether LLMs inherently understand graph topology and, if so, how to better exploit this capability without extra training.

Method: The authors analyze LLM attention maps when processing serialized graphs and discover a sawtooth attention pattern that aligns with token-level adjacency, indicating implicit reconstruction of graph topology. They formalize how this structural signal is diluted by attention sinks as a representation bottleneck caused by anisotropic bias vs. local aggregation needs. Based on this, they introduce StructuraL Attention SHarpening (Slash), a training-free, plug-and-play attention redistribution scheme that boosts attention along structurally relevant patterns and reduces sink effects.

Result: Empirical evaluations on pure graph reasoning tasks and molecular property prediction show that Slash consistently improves performance of diverse LLMs on these structurally intensive problems, demonstrating that leveraging and sharpening existing structural attention is effective without model retraining.

Conclusion: LLMs naturally encode graph topology internally, but this capability is obscured by attention sink–induced bottlenecks linked to anisotropic language biases. By redistributing attention via the Slash mechanism, one can expose and amplify this latent structural understanding, yielding substantial gains on graph and molecular tasks without additional training or specialized adapters.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) show remarkable semantic understanding but often struggle with structural understanding when processing graph topologies in a serialized format. Existing solutions rely on training external graph-based adapters or fine-tuning, which incur high costs and lost generalizability. In this work, we investigate the internal mechanisms of LLMs and present a critical finding: LLMs spontaneously reconstruct the graph's topology internally, evidenced by a distinct "sawtooth" pattern in their attention maps that structurally aligns with the "token-level adjacency matrix". However, this intrinsic structural understanding is diluted by the attention sink. We theoretically formalize this dilution as a representation bottleneck, stemming from a fundamental conflict: the model's anisotropic bias, essential for language tasks, suppresses the topology-aware local aggregation required for graph reasoning. To address this, we propose a training-free solution, named StructuraL Attention SHarpening (Slash), which amplifies this internal structural understanding via a plug-and-play attention redistribution. Experiments on pure graph tasks and molecular prediction validate Slash delivers significant and consistent performance gains across diverse LLMs.

</details>


### [359] [Consistency as a Testable Property: Statistical Methods to Evaluate AI Agent Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10516)
*Harsh Raj,Niranjan Orkat,Suvrorup Mukherjee,Aritra Guha,Cheryl Flynn,Subhabrata Majumdar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a formal, quantitative framework to measure how reliably AI agents behave when their inputs or environments are perturbed in ways that should not change task semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite strong average performance, AI agents can fail unpredictably when facing small, semantically equivalent changes in prompts, environments, or task details. Existing metrics like pass@1 mostly measure whether an agent ever solves a task, not whether it does so consistently and stably. This unreliability is problematic for deploying agents in high-stakes settings. The paper aims to build a rigorous measurement science that distinguishes an agent’s underlying capability from its robustness of execution.

Method: The authors define two complementary notions of reliability: (1) output-level reliability, quantified using U-statistics computed over agent outputs under semantically preserving perturbations of tasks; and (2) trajectory-level stability, captured via kernel-based metrics that compare entire action trajectories across perturbed runs. They design a framework to generate semantically equivalent task variations, apply these metrics across diverse operating conditions, and analyze how agent performance changes. This allows them to separate knowledge/capability from robustness to minor variations and to localize where failures arise in the decision process.

Result: On three agentic benchmarks, trajectory-level consistency metrics show much higher diagnostic power than standard pass@1. The experiments reveal cases where agents know how to solve tasks in principle but still exhibit brittle or collapsing strategies when minor task variations are introduced. The proposed metrics successfully expose such hidden instabilities and pinpoint specific stages or components in the agent architecture that drive inconsistency.

Conclusion: A mathematically grounded reliability framework based on U-statistics and kernel methods can more accurately characterize AI agent robustness than traditional success rates. Distinguishing core capability from execution robustness uncovers failure modes that are invisible to pass@1 and provides actionable insights for improving agent architectures. This framework is positioned as a prerequisite for trustworthy deployment of agents in sensitive real-world applications.

Abstract: This paper establishes a rigorous measurement science for AI agent reliability, providing a foundational framework for quantifying consistency under semantically preserving perturbations. By leveraging $U$-statistics for output-level reliability and kernel-based metrics for trajectory-level stability, we offer a principled approach to evaluating agents across diverse operating conditions. Our proposal highlights the important distinction between the core capability and execution robustness of an agent, showing that minor task-level variations can induce complete strategy breakdowns despite the agent possessing the requisite knowledge for the task. We validate our framework through extensive experiments on three agentic benchmarks, demonstrating that trajectory-level consistency metrics provide far greater diagnostic sensitivity than traditional pass@1 rates. By providing the mathematical tools to isolate where and why agents deviate, we enable the identification and rectification of architectural concerns that hinder the deployment of agents in high-stakes, real-world environments.

</details>


### [360] [PrimeKG-CL: A Continual Graph Learning Benchmark on Evolving Biomedical Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10529)
*Yousef A. Radwan,Yao Li,Qing Qing,Ziqi Xu,Xingtong Yu,Jiaxing Huang,Renqiang Luo,Xikun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PrimeKG-CL, a realistic continual-learning benchmark for biomedical knowledge graphs, built from real ontology updates, and shows how different continual learning strategies and decoders interact, while providing open data, code, and splits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continual graph learning is typically evaluated on simplistic, synthetic temporal splits of static and generic knowledge graphs, which do not reflect the complex, asynchronous, and structured evolution of real biomedical knowledge graphs whose underlying ontologies add and deprecate large numbers of edges between releases. There is a need for a realistic benchmark that captures these real-world dynamics to properly evaluate and compare continual learning methods for biomedical applications such as drug repurposing and clinical decision support.

Method: The authors construct PrimeKG-CL from nine authoritative biomedical databases, yielding a heterogeneous KG with 129K+ nodes, 8.1M+ edges, 10 node types, and 30 relation types, and two real temporal snapshots (June 2021 and July 2023) that differ by millions of added and removed edges. They define 10 entity-type-grouped tasks with multimodal node features and a stratified test split distinguishing persistent, added, and removed knowledge. They then benchmark six continual learning strategies across four KG embedding decoders on three tasks (biomedical relationship prediction, entity classification, KGQA), and compare them with LKGE, an LLM-based retrieval-augmented agent, and CMKL. They also test scalability of the IncDE continual KG embedding framework on the largest base task.

Result: Empirical results show strong interactions between the choice of KG decoder and continual learning strategy: no single continual learning approach is universally best across all decoders, and poor combinations can significantly harm performance. DistMult uniquely shows a clear distinction between performance on persistent versus deprecated edges, revealing that common evaluation metrics may conflate remembering valid facts with failing to forget outdated ones; this separation is not observed with RotatE. Multimodal node features substantially improve entity-level tasks, by up to 60%. The IncDE framework fails to scale to the 5.67M-triple base task, exhausting up to 350GB of RAM across multiple attempts.

Conclusion: PrimeKG-CL provides a realistic, large-scale benchmark for continual learning on biomedical knowledge graphs, exposing limitations of current methods and metrics when faced with genuine temporal KG evolution. The study highlights that continual learning performance is highly dependent on the interaction between KG decoders and learning strategies, that evaluation should distinguish retention of valid knowledge from forgetting deprecated facts, and that multimodal features are valuable for entity-level tasks. The benchmark’s open data, pipeline, baselines, and carefully stratified splits aim to support more robust and scalable future research in continual biomedical KG learning.

Abstract: Biomedical knowledge graphs underwrite drug repurposing and clinical decision support, yet the upstream ontologies they depend on update on independent cycles that add millions of edges and deprecate hundreds of thousands more between releases. Yet existing continual graph learning has been studied almost exclusively on synthetic random splits of static, generic KGs, a regime that cannot reproduce the asynchronous, structured evolution real biomedical KGs undergo. To this end, we introduce PrimeKG-CL, a CGL benchmark built from nine authoritative biomedical databases (129K+ nodes, 8.1M+ edges, 10 node types, 30 relation types) with two genuine temporal snapshots (June 2021, July 2023; 5.83M edges added, 889K removed, 7.21M persistent), 10 entity-type-grouped tasks, multimodal node features, and a per-task persistent/added/removed test stratification. On three tasks (biomedical relationship prediction, entity classification, KGQA), we evaluate six CL strategies across four KGE decoders, plus LKGE, an LLM-RAG agent, and CMKL. We find that decoder choice and continual learning strategy interact strongly: no single strategy performs best across all decoders, and mismatched combinations can significantly degrade performance. Moreover, only DistMult exhibits a clear separation between persistent and deprecated knowledge, indicating that standard metrics conflate retention of still-valid facts with failure to forget outdated ones; this effect is absent under RotatE. In addition, multimodal features improve entity-level tasks by up to 60%, and a recent CKGE framework (IncDE) failed to scale to our 5.67M-triple base task across five attempts up to 350GB RAM. Data, pipeline, baselines, and the stratified split are released openly. Dataset:huggingface.co/datasets/yradwan147/PrimeKGCL|Code:github.com/yradwan147/primekg-cl-neurips2026

</details>


### [361] [A Reflective Storytelling Agent for Older Adults: Integrating Argumentation Schemes and Argument Mining in LLM-Based Personalised Narratives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10531)
*Jayalakshmi Baskar,Vera C. Kaelin,Kaan Kilic,Helena Lindgren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper designs and evaluates a knowledge-grounded, argument-aware LLM storytelling agent to support purposeful, health-related narrative interactions with older adults, showing that argument mining and hallucination-risk indicators meaningfully relate to users’ perceptions of clarity, meaning, and inconsistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Older adults can benefit from digital companions that support reflection on health-promoting behaviors, but standard LLM storytelling suffers from hallucinations, low transparency, and weak alignment with users’ real motivations and contexts. The authors aim to create a narrative agent that is both purposeful (health-oriented) and inspectable, so that its generated stories are grounded in user models and can be evaluated against human judgments.

Method: They build a reflective storytelling agent that integrates: (1) knowledge graphs and structured user models of health-promoting activities and motivations; (2) argumentation theory and argument mining to identify and structure purposes and justifications in narratives; and (3) hallucination-risk indicators to flag potentially unreliable content. The work is evaluated in two phases: Phase I, a participatory design study with 11 domain experts to iteratively refine the system; Phase II, a user study with 55 older adults who read persona-based narratives varying by prompt and creativity level, and rated perceived purpose, usefulness, cultural relatability, inconsistencies, clarity, and meaningfulness. System-derived argument-quality and hallucination-risk indicators were compared with user evaluations.

Result: The final system successfully generated narratives grounded in structured user models. In Phase II, older adult participants identified personally relevant purposes in about two thirds of narratives, and specifically argument-based purposes in about half of these. Cultural recognisability had a strong positive effect on willingness to use the system. Users tolerated minor inconsistencies when stories were still understandable and personally meaningful. Narratives marked with higher hallucination-risk indicators were more likely to be perceived as inconsistent by participants, while narratives with higher argument-quality scores tended to receive higher ratings for clarity and meaningfulness.

Conclusion: Integrating argument mining and structured knowledge into LLM-based storytelling provides a practical inspection and grounding mechanism for health-oriented digital companions for older adults. Argument-derived signals and hallucination-risk indicators align in informative ways with human evaluations, suggesting that such reflective mechanisms can help manage hallucinations, improve perceived purpose and clarity, and guide the design of transparent, user-aligned narrative agents in health-support contexts.

Abstract: This work investigates whether knowledge-driven large language model (LLM)-based storytelling can support purposeful narrative interaction with a digital companion for older adults.
