To address known limitations of LLMs, including hallucinations and limited transparency, we present a reflective storytelling agent integrating knowledge graphs, user modelling, argumentation theory, and argument mining to guide and inspect narrative generation.
  The study consisted of two phases. Phase I employed participatory design involving 11 domain experts in a formative evaluation that informed iterative refinement. The resulting system generates narratives grounded in structured user models representing health-promoting activities and motivations. Phase II involved 55 older adults evaluating persona-based narratives across four prompts and two creativity levels. Participants assessed perceived purpose, usefulness, cultural relatability, and inconsistencies. The system additionally computed hallucination-risk indicators to evaluate generated narratives.
  Participants recognised personally relevant purposes in roughly two thirds of narratives, while argument-based purposes were identified in around half of these cases. Cultural recognisability strongly influenced willingness to use the functionality, whereas minor inconsistencies were often tolerated when narratives remained understandable and personally relevant. Narratives with higher hallucination-risk indicators were more often perceived as inconsistent, while higher argument-quality indicators tended to co-occur with higher clarity and meaningfulness ratings.
  Overall, the study positions argument mining as a reflective inspection mechanism for comparing formal grounding signals with human evaluations in health-oriented LLM storytelling for older adults.

</details>


### [362] [Bridging Sequence and Graph Structure for Epigenetic Age Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10541)
*Yao Li,Xikun Zhang,Xiaotao Shen,Sonika Tyagi,Xin Zheng,Jiaxing Huang,Feng Xia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new epigenetic age predictor that combines CpG co-methylation graphs with handcrafted DNA sequence features, improving accuracy over prior graph-based models and yielding biologically interpretable feature importance patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DNA methylation-based epigenetic clocks are powerful but current machine learning models treat methylation sites largely in isolation or only exploit co-methylation structure, ignoring the rich sequence context around each CpG. No prior framework jointly integrates co-methylation graph structure and site-specific DNA sequence information in a principled, unified model. The authors aim to close this gap to improve prediction accuracy and biological interpretability of epigenetic age estimators.

Method: They build a unified sequence–graph integration framework for epigenetic age prediction. Methylation sites are nodes in a co-methylation graph processed by a graph neural network. For each site, they compute eight-dimensional, hand-engineered statistical features from the local DNA sequence. A lightweight gated modulation mechanism uses these sequence features to adaptively scale each site's methylation signal according to its estimated biological relevance before graph convolution. They also compare this approach to alternatives that use CNN-based sequence encoders instead of handcrafted features and conduct post-hoc interpretability analysis of feature importance as a function of age.

Result: On 3,707 blood methylation samples, their model achieves a test mean absolute error of 3.149 years, representing a 12.8% improvement over the strongest existing graph-based baseline. Handcrafted, biologically informed sequence statistics outperform CNN-based end-to-end sequence encoders in this data regime. Interpretability analysis highlights CpG density and local adenine frequency as features whose importance shifts with age, aligning with known patterns of age-related hypermethylation in CpG-dense promoter regions.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling co-methylation graph structure and local DNA sequence context via gated modulation leads to more accurate and biologically meaningful epigenetic age prediction. In moderately sized methylation datasets, simple, domain-informed sequence statistics can be more effective than deep, learned sequence embeddings. The identified sequence features and their age-dependent importance patterns support existing mechanistic hypotheses about how local sequence context shapes age-related methylation changes.

Abstract: Epigenetic clocks based on DNA methylation have emerged as powerful tools for estimating biological age, with broad applications in aging research, age-related disease studies, and longevity science. Despite advances across machine learning approaches to epigenetic age prediction, spanning penalised linear regression, deep feedforward networks, residual architectures, and graph neural networks, no existing method jointly models co-methylation graph structure and site-specific DNA sequence context within a unified framework. We propose a unified sequence--graph integration framework for epigenetic age prediction that addresses this gap, integrating eight-dimensional DNA sequence statistical features through a lightweight gated modulation mechanism that adaptively scales each site's methylation signal according to its sequence-determined biological relevance prior to graph convolution. Evaluated on 3,707 blood methylation samples against a comprehensive set of baselines, our method achieves a test MAE of 3.149 years, a 12.8\% improvement over the strongest graph-based baseline. Biologically informed statistical features outperform CNN-based sequence encoding, demonstrating that handcrafted sequence features are more effective than end-to-end learned representations in this data regime. Post-hoc interpretability analysis identifies CpG density and local adenine frequency as features with age-dependent importance shifts, consistent with known mechanisms of age-related hypermethylation at CpG-dense promoter regions. Our code is at https://github.com/yaoli2022/graphage-seq.

</details>


### [363] [Agent-First Tool API: A Semantic Interface Paradigm for Enterprise AI Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10555)
*Kai Pan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an Agent-First Tool API paradigm that redesigns tool interfaces for autonomous AI agents, addressing five core mismatches between traditional CRUD APIs and agent needs, and shows substantial gains in task success and autonomy on a production SaaS platform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional enterprise APIs are designed for humans and CRUD workflows, not for autonomous AI agents that must reason, plan, iterate, and recover from errors with minimal human oversight. This leads to architectural mismatches—such as reliance on exact identifiers, UI-oriented payloads, single-shot assumptions, human-like permissions, and poor error semantics—that limit reliability, safety, and autonomy of agents in real production systems. The authors want to systematically characterize these mismatches and introduce a principled API design paradigm tailored to agent cognition and control requirements.

Method: The authors first analyze and categorize five fundamental architectural mismatches between conventional CRUD-style APIs and the needs of autonomous AI agents. They then propose an Agent-First Tool API paradigm with three main components: (1) a Six-Verb Semantic Protocol that structures tool usage into six phases (search, resolve, preview, execute, verify, recover); (2) a Normalized Tool Contract (NTC) that enriches tool responses with structured decision-support metadata such as confidence scores, evidence chains, and recommended next actions; and (3) a dual-layer governance pipeline that combines static capability policies with dynamic, context-aware risk escalation. They implement this paradigm in a multi-tenant SaaS platform integrating 85 tools across 6 domains, and run comparative experiments on 50 real operational tasks against optimized CRUD-style baseline APIs.

Result: In the production deployment, Agent-First Tool APIs integrated 85 tools in 6 business domains. On 50 realistic operational tasks, the new paradigm achieved an 88% end-to-end task success rate compared to 64% with well-tuned CRUD baselines, corresponding to a 37.5% relative improvement. It also reduced the need for human interventions by 72.7% and improved autonomous error recovery effectiveness by a factor of 5.8, indicating better robustness and agent self-correction capabilities.

Conclusion: The Agent-First Tool API paradigm effectively addresses key architectural gaps between human-centric CRUD APIs and the needs of autonomous AI agents, substantially improving task success, autonomy, and error recovery in a real SaaS production environment. The authors argue that their approach is complementary to existing transport/discovery standards like MCP, functioning as a semantic application layer on top of such protocols, and suggest that future enterprise tooling for agents should adopt similar semantic, metadata-rich, governance-aware API contracts to fully unlock agent capabilities.

Abstract: As AI agents transition from research prototypes to enterprise production systems, the tool interfaces they consume remain rooted in human-oriented CRUD paradigms. This paper identifies five fundamental architectural mismatches between conventional APIs and autonomous agent requirements: exact-identifier dependence, rendering-oriented responses, single-shot interaction assumptions, user-equivalent authorization, and opaque error semantics. We propose the Agent-First Tool API paradigm, comprising three integrated mechanisms: (1) a Six-Verb Semantic Protocol that decomposes tool interactions into search, resolve, preview, execute, verify, and recover phases; (2) a Normalized Tool Contract (NTC) providing structured decision-support metadata including confidence scores, evidence chains, and suggested next actions; and (3) a dual-layer governance pipeline combining static capability policies with dynamic risk escalation. The paradigm is implemented and validated in a production multi-tenant SaaS platform serving 85 registered tools across 6 business domains. Comparative experiments on 50 real operational tasks demonstrate that Agent-First APIs achieve 88% end-to-end task success rate versus 64% for optimized CRUD baselines (+37.5%), while reducing required human interventions by 72.7% and improving autonomous error recovery by 5.8x. We establish that the paradigm is orthogonal and complementary to transport-layer standards such as MCP, operating as the semantic application layer above existing tool discovery and invocation protocols.

</details>


### [364] [Deep Arguing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10569)
*Adam Gould,Francesca Toni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Deep Arguing, a neurosymbolic method where a neural network builds an argumentation graph between data points and class labels, enabling competitive classification accuracy with explicit, case-based argumentative explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models are highly accurate but difficult to interpret because they contain many parameters, entangle feature extraction with task objectives, and lack explicit reasoning structures. Existing interpretability methods often provide post-hoc or incomplete insights and do not explain predictions as structured reasoning. The authors want a model that both classifies well and produces faithful, human-understandable explanations grounded in explicit argumentative relations between examples and labels, across different data modalities.

Method: They introduce Deep Arguing, a neurosymbolic framework that integrates deep neural networks with formal argumentation theory. A neural network encodes data points and constructs an argumentation graph in which each point forms an argument that supports its assigned class label and attacks alternative labels. Differentiable argumentation semantics are used to perform reasoning over this graph, enabling end-to-end training that jointly learns feature representations and the strengths of supporting/attacking relations. Structural constraints are imposed on the argumentation graph during training to encourage interpretable, well-formed argumentative structures, which also regularize learning.

Result: On both tabular and image datasets, Deep Arguing achieves predictive performance competitive with standard deep learning baselines. At the same time, it produces argumentation structures that show which data points support or attack a given prediction, yielding case-based explanations aligned with the learned decision process. The structure constraints improve both the readability of the argumentation graphs and the classification performance compared to unconstrained variants or purely subsymbolic baselines.

Conclusion: Deep Arguing demonstrates that integrating deep learning with differentiable argumentation reasoning can deliver models that are both accurate and inherently interpretable. By framing classification as a process of constructing and evaluating arguments between data points and labels, the approach yields faithful, case-based explanations rather than post-hoc justifications. The authors conclude that neurosymbolic argumentation with structural constraints is a promising direction for interpretable AI across multiple data modalities.

Abstract: Deep learning has become the dominant approach for creating high capacity, scalable models across diverse data modalities. However, because these models rely on a large number of learned parameters, tightly couple feature extraction with task objectives, and often lack explicit reasoning mechanisms, it is difficult for humans to understand how they arrive at their predictions. Understanding what representations emerge and why they arise from the training data remains an open challenge. We introduce Deep Arguing, a novel neurosymbolic approach that integrates deep learning with argumentation construction and reasoning for interpretable classification with different data modalities. In our approach deep neural networks construct an argumentation structure wherein data points support their assigned label and attack different ones. Using differentiable argumentation semantics for reasoning, the model is trained end-to-end to jointly learn feature representation and argumentative interactions. This results in argumentation structures providing faithful case-based explanations for predictions. Structure constraints over the argumentation graph guide learning, improving both interpretability and predictive performance. Experiments with tabular and imaging datasets show that Deep Arguing achieves performance competitive with standard baselines whilst offering interpretable argumentative reasoning.

</details>


### [365] [LLM Jaggedness Unlocks Scientific Creativity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10574)
*Shray Mathur,J. Anibal Boscoboinik,Esther H. R. Tsai,Kevin G. Yager*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SciAidanBench to measure and analyze the uneven, “jagged” scientific creativity of LLMs and shows that combining models can outperform any single one.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI progress is not smooth; models improve unevenly across tasks and domains. Existing creativity benchmarks focus mainly on general creativity and do not capture how well LLMs generate novel scientific ideas. The authors want to systematically study this jagged, uneven progress in the specific and socially important context of scientific idea generation, and to see whether the irregular strengths of different models can be exploited rather than seen only as a weakness.

Method: They build SciAidanBench, a benchmark consisting of open-ended scientific questions, and ask LLMs to produce as many distinct, coherent ideas per question as possible. The number of valid ideas is used as a proxy for scientific creative potential. They evaluate 19 base LLMs (30 variants including reasoning-optimized versions) from 8 providers. They analyze performance along several axes: (1) comparison between general creativity tasks and scientific creativity tasks; (2) variability across prompts within a model; (3) variability across scientific subfields (domains) within a model. Finally, they test inference-time methods such as increased compute, knowledge pooling, and cross-model brainstorming to construct ensembles (“meta-models”) that aggregate the jagged strengths of individual models.

Result: They find that: (1) Gains in general creativity do not map cleanly to gains in scientific creativity, so different models have divergent capability profiles. (2) Within a single model, performance is highly variable across prompts, with some questions triggering bursts of creativity and others yielding poor output. (3) Each model shows uneven strengths across scientific subdomains, indicating fragmented internal capabilities. (4) By using inference-time techniques and ensembles that combine multiple models, they can systematically exploit this jaggedness and build meta-models that outperform any single model on SciAidanBench.

Conclusion: Jagged, uneven progress is a fundamental structural property of current LLM development. In the context of scientific idea generation, this jaggedness manifests across tasks, prompts, and domains. However, rather than treating this as a flaw, it can be turned into an asset: by understanding where different models excel and combining them via ensemble and brainstorming-style methods, it is possible to achieve higher levels of scientific creativity than any individual model can reach alone.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence advances, models are not improving uniformly. Instead, progress unfolds in a jagged fashion, with capabilities growing unevenly across tasks, domains, and model scales. In this work, we examine this dynamic jaggedness through the lens of scientific idea generation. We introduce SciAidanBench, a benchmark of open-ended scientific questions designed to measure the scientific creativity of large language models (LLMs). Given a scientific question, models are asked to generate as many unique and coherent ideas as possible, with the total number of valid responses serving as a proxy for creative potential. Evaluating 19 base models across 8 providers (30 total variants including reasoning versions), we find that jaggedness manifests both across models and within models. First, in a cross-task comparison between general and scientific creativity, improvements in general creativity do not translate uniformly to scientific creativity, revealing divergent capability profiles across models. Second, at the prompt level, stronger models do not improve uniformly; instead, they exhibit high variability, with bursts of creativity on some questions and limited performance on others. Third, at the domain level, individual models display uneven strengths across scientific subfields, reflecting fragmented internal capability profiles. Finally, we show that this jaggedness can be harnessed. We explore mechanisms of inference-time compute, knowledge pooling, and brainstorming to combine models effectively and construct meta-model ensembles that outperform any single model. Our results position jaggedness not as a limitation, but as a resource, a structural feature of AI progress that, when understood and leveraged, can amplify LLM-driven scientific creativity.

</details>


### [366] [A Resilient Solution for Sewer Overflow Monitoring across Cloud and Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10592)
*Vipin Singh,Tianheng Ling,Peter Ghaly,Felix Grimmeisen,Gregor Schiele,Felix Biessmann*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Web-based system using deep learning to forecast overflow basin filling in combined sewer systems, supporting CSO prevention and working in both cloud and edge setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Historical cities have aging combined sewer systems that cannot cope well with increasingly frequent extreme rainfall, leading to combined sewer overflows that harm the environment and public health. Operators need accurate, timely forecasts of overflow basin filling levels to anticipate when capacity will be exceeded and take preventive measures, but existing monitoring and forecasting tools may not be sufficiently predictive, integrated, or robust to network outages.

Method: Design and implementation of a web-based demonstrator that integrates deep learning forecasting models for predicting filling dynamics of sewer overflow basins. The system is deployed in both cloud and edge computing environments and connected to an interactive monitoring dashboard. The architecture is designed to remain functional and provide forecasts even during network outages by leveraging edge-side capabilities.

Result: A functioning demonstrator (accessible via a public URL) and an accompanying video showcase that illustrate how deep learning-based forecasts of basin filling are visualized within an interactive dashboard, and how the system operates across cloud and edge infrastructures to support overflow monitoring. The abstract implies successful integration and robustness but does not provide quantitative performance metrics.

Conclusion: Deep learning forecasting methods can be effectively integrated into a web-based monitoring dashboard for combined sewer overflow basins, with deployment across cloud and edge settings enhancing resilience to network outages. This approach supports proactive CSO management by improving the ability to anticipate basin capacity exceedance and enabling timely preventive interventions.

Abstract: Aging combined sewer systems in many historical cities are increasingly stressed by extreme rainfall events, which can trigger combined sewer overflows (CSO) with significant environmental and public health impacts. Forecasting the filling dynamics of overflow basins is critical for anticipating capacity exceedance and enabling timely preventive actions for CSO. We present a web-based demonstrator (https://riwwer.demo.calgo-lab.de) that integrates Deep Learning forecasting methods in both cloud and edge settings into an interactive monitoring dashboard for overflow monitoring, resilient to network outages. A video showcase is available online (https://cloud.bht-berlin.de/index.php/s/b9xt4T3SdiLBiFZ).

</details>


### [367] [LLARS: Enabling Domain Expert & Developer Collaboration for LLM Prompting, Generation and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10593)
*Philipp Steigerwald,Mara Stieler,Jennifer Burghardt,Eric Rudolph,Jens Albrecht*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces LLARS, an open-source platform that streamlines the design, testing, and evaluation of LLM-based systems by connecting domain experts and developers in a single workflow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building effective LLM-based applications requires close collaboration between domain experts and developers, but current workflows are fragmented across tools for prompt design, batch generation, and evaluation, making iteration slow, hard to track, and costly.

Method: Propose an integrated platform with three modules: (1) Collaborative Prompt Engineering that supports real-time co-authoring, version control, and instant LLM testing; (2) Batch Generation that runs configurable combinations of prompts, models, and datasets with explicit cost control; and (3) Hybrid Evaluation where humans and LLMs jointly assess outputs using diverse methods, with live agreement metrics and provenance tracking to identify the best model–prompt pair. The system automatically propagates newly created prompts and models to batch generation, and converts completed batches into evaluation scenarios with one click. The authors also conducted interviews with six domain experts and three developers in the online counselling domain.

Result: LLARS successfully operationalizes an end-to-end workflow connecting prompt design, batch output generation, and hybrid evaluation. Interviews in the online counselling use case reported that the platform is intuitive, centralizes previously scattered tasks, and supports efficient interdisciplinary collaboration between experts and developers.

Conclusion: An integrated, open-source platform like LLARS can substantially streamline the development of LLM-based systems, improving collaboration, transparency, and efficiency in prompt engineering, large-scale generation, and evaluation. The initial user study suggests LLARS is practical and beneficial for real-world domains such as online counselling.

Abstract: We demonstrate LLARS (LLM Assisted Research System), an open-source platform that bridges the gap between domain experts and developers for building LLM-based systems. It integrates three tightly connected modules into an end-to-end pipeline: Collaborative Prompt Engineering for real-time co-authoring with version control and instant LLM testing, Batch Generation for configurable output production across user-selected prompts $\times$ models $\times$ data with cost control, and Hybrid Evaluation where human and LLM evaluators jointly assess outputs through diverse assessment methods, with live agreement metrics and provenance analysis to identify the best model-prompt combination for a given use case. New prompts and models are automatically available for batch generation and completed batches can be turned into evaluation scenarios with a single click. Interviews with six domain experts and three developers in online counselling confirmed that LLARS feels intuitive, saves considerable time by keeping everything in one place and makes interdisciplinary collaboration seamless.

</details>


### [368] [Reasoning Is Not Free: Robust Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM-as-a-Judge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10805)
*Wenbo Zhang,Lijinghua Zhang,Liner Xiang,Hengrui Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies when and how to use reasoning-enabled LLMs as automated judges, finding that reasoning helps mainly on complex, structured tasks and proposing a routing framework (RACER) that adaptively chooses between reasoning and non-reasoning judges under distribution shift and cost constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning-capable LLMs are increasingly used as automated judges to evaluate other models’ outputs, but their true benefits, limitations, and costs compared to simpler non-reasoning judges are not well understood. There is a practical need to know when explicit reasoning actually improves evaluation quality, how it behaves under distribution shift, and how to manage the higher computational cost of reasoning in real-world systems with fixed budgets.

Method: The paper runs controlled comparisons between reasoning and non-reasoning LLM judges on various evaluation tasks, distinguishing structured verification tasks (e.g., math, coding) from simpler evaluations. Based on the empirical findings, the authors formulate a routing problem: dynamically choosing whether to use a reasoning or non-reasoning judge for each instance under a fixed budget, in a way that is robust to distribution shift. They cast this as a constrained distributionally robust optimization problem with a KL-divergence-based uncertainty set, derive a primal–dual algorithm to solve it efficiently, and analyze the resulting policy theoretically.

Result: Empirically, explicit reasoning significantly improves judgment accuracy for complex, structured verification tasks but offers limited or even negative benefit for simpler tasks, while being substantially more computationally expensive. The proposed RACER routing method outperforms baselines in terms of accuracy–cost trade-offs, especially when there is distribution shift between training and deployment data. Theoretical analysis shows that the optimal routing policy is unique and the primal–dual algorithm converges linearly.

Conclusion: Reasoning-enabled LLM judges should not be used indiscriminately: they are most valuable on structured, difficult evaluations and can be wasteful or even harmful on simpler ones. By modeling the choice between reasoning and non-reasoning judges as a distributionally robust routing problem, the RACER framework provides a principled, efficient way to allocate expensive reasoning capacity under budget constraints and distribution shift, achieving better overall evaluation quality per unit cost.

Abstract: Reasoning-capable large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted as automated judges, but their benefits and costs in LLM-as-a-Judge settings remain unclear. Through controlled comparisons between reasoning and non-reasoning judges, we show that explicit reasoning substantially improves judgment accuracy on tasks requiring structured verification (e.g., math and coding), while offering limited or even negative gains on simpler evaluations and incurring significantly higher computational cost. These findings motivate that reasoning should be used selectively rather than universally, with awareness of possible distribution shift. We propose a Robust Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing (RACER), which dynamically selects between reasoning and non-reasoning judges under a fixed budget by formulating routing as a constrained distributionally robust optimization problem. RACER explicitly accounts for distribution shift via a KL-divergence uncertainty set, admits an efficient primal--dual algorithm, and enjoys theoretical guarantees including uniqueness of the optimal policy and linear convergence. Extensive experiments show that RACER achieves superior accuracy--cost trade-offs under distribution shift.

</details>


### [369] [Budget-Efficient Automatic Algorithm Design via Code Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10598)
*Maxime Bouscary,Manxi Wu,Saurabh Amin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a more budget-efficient way to use LLMs for automatic algorithm design by searching over small corrections to code within a graph structure instead of repeatedly generating full algorithms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based automatic algorithm design methods are inefficient because they repeatedly regenerate entire algorithms, even when only small changes are needed, and they discard low-performing candidates that might contain useful components. There is a need for a framework that uses limited computational budget more effectively by reusing partial progress and performing finer-grained search.

Method: The authors formalize budget-efficient automatic algorithm design as maximizing fitness under a computational cost constraint. They represent algorithms as nodes in a directed acyclic graph where edges correspond to "corrections"—compact operators that add, replace, or remove code blocks. An LLM is queried to propose such corrections rather than whole algorithms. Each correction extends the DAG, creating new algorithms via composition of multiple corrections. This decomposition enables credit assignment at the correction level, guiding later LLM queries. They also provide theoretical analysis on how to allocate search depth versus breadth under different budget regimes.

Result: On three combinatorial optimization problems, the proposed graph-based correction-level search consistently outperforms traditional full-algorithm search methods when constrained to the same token budget, indicating more efficient use of LLM queries and better algorithm discovery.

Conclusion: Decomposing algorithm search into correction-level operations within a DAG and assigning credit at that level leads to more budget-efficient LLM-based automatic algorithm design than full-algorithm search. Theoretical and empirical results support that appropriately balancing search depth and breadth improves performance, and that providing rich context to the LLM is beneficial mainly when its prior knowledge is limited, but can otherwise be detrimental.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as powerful tools for automatic algorithm design (AAD). However, existing pipelines remain inefficient. They operate at the granularity of full algorithms, redundantly rewriting recurring substructures and discarding low-fitness candidates that may contain valuable algorithmic features. We formalize budget-efficient automatic algorithm design, wherein the search policy maximizes realized fitness subject to limited computational cost. We propose a directed acyclic graph representation of algorithms and build a search framework that fully exploits the LLM's output. Instead of querying the LLM for full algorithms, we use it to obtain corrections: compact operators that add, replace, or remove code blocks. Each correction augments the graph, yielding new algorithms that compose with prior corrections. This graph structure decomposes algorithms into sets of corrections, enabling correction-level credit assignment that informs subsequent queries. We complement this framework with theoretical insights into the ideal balance between search depth and breadth at different budget levels. We validate our method empirically on three combinatorial optimization problems, demonstrating consistent superiority of our graph-based search over full-algorithm search at equal token budget. Finally, our experiments suggest that rich contexts help only when the LLM's prior knowledge is shallow, and can hinder performance otherwise.

</details>


### [370] [The Generalized Turing Test: A Foundation for Comparing Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10851)
*Daniel Mitropolsky,Susan S. Hong,Riccardo Neumarker,Emanuele Rimoldi,Tomaso Poggio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Generalized Turing Test (GTT), a task-agnostic framework that compares agents’ intelligence by how indistinguishable they are from each other in interactive settings, and shows theoretically and empirically that it can induce meaningful rankings among modern models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation of AI systems heavily depends on specific datasets and benchmarks, which can quickly become outdated or be gamed, and do not provide a clean, general, or comparative notion of intelligence across arbitrary agents. The authors want a principled, task-agnostic way to compare agents that can capture broad capabilities and be applicable regardless of the particular tasks or data used, extending the original spirit of the Turing Test to arbitrary agents and interactions.

Method: They define a formal comparator between agents A and B based on indistinguishability: A is at least as capable as B (A ≥ B) if B, acting as an interactive distinguisher, cannot reliably tell whether it is interacting with another copy of itself or with A instructed to imitate B. They study the mathematical properties of this comparator—especially when it is transitive and thus induces an ordering over equivalence classes—and introduce several variants (with query access, bounded interaction, and fixed distinguishers). They then instantiate this framework empirically by running thousands of interaction trials among a set of modern models, measuring how often each model can distinguish others from itself under the GTT protocol, and extracting the resulting pairwise comparison structure.

Result: Theoretical analysis identifies conditions where the indistinguishability comparator is transitive and can be treated as an ordering over classes of agents, and clarifies how different protocol variants behave. Empirically, when applying the GTT to modern models, the pairwise indistinguishability outcomes form a stratified comparison structure. These strata align well with informal and benchmark-based rankings of the models’ capabilities, indicating that the GTT framework can recover sensible comparative intelligence orderings without relying on fixed task benchmarks.

Conclusion: Indistinguishability-based comparison, instantiated as the Generalized Turing Test, offers a unified and task-agnostic way to reason about relative intelligence between arbitrary agents. The framework is mathematically analyzable, can under some conditions induce a meaningful ordering over agents, and in experiments with current models produces rankings consistent with existing evaluations. This suggests it could serve as a foundation for more robust evaluation methodologies and inspire training objectives that are less dependent on static datasets or benchmarks.

Abstract: We introduce the Generalized Turing Test (GTT), a formal framework for comparing the capabilities of arbitrary agents via indistinguishability. For agents A and B, we define the Turing comparator A $\geq$ B to hold if B, acting as a distinguisher, cannot reliably distinguish between interactions with A (instructed to imitate B) and another instance of B. This yields a dataset- and task-agnostic notion of relative intelligence. We study the comparator's structure, including conditions under which it is transitive and therefore induces an ordering over equivalence classes, and we define and analyze variants with querying, bounded interaction, and fixed distinguishers. To complement the theory, we instantiate the framework on a collection of modern models, empirically evaluating pairwise indistinguishability across thousands of trials. The resulting comparisons exhibit a stratified structure consistent with existing rankings, hinting that the proposed framework yields meaningful empirical orderings. Our results position indistinguishability as a unifying lens for reasoning about intelligence, suggesting a foundation for evaluation and, potentially, training objectives that are inherently independent of fixed datasets or benchmarks.

</details>


### [371] [The Open-Box Fallacy: Why AI Deployment Needs a Calibrated Verification Regime](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10601)
*Phongsakon Mark Konrad,Tim Lukas Adam,Ane Cathrine Holst Merrild,Riccardo Terrenzi,Rebecca De Rosa,Toygar Tanyel,Serkan Ayvaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that instead of requiring explainable model internals before deploying AI in sensitive domains, we should focus on calibrated, domain-specific verification and introduce a standard called Verification Coverage to evaluate how well a specific deployment is governed and checked.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice often blocks or delays AI deployment in high-stakes areas until model internals can be mechanistically explained. This both overextends the scope of interpretability research and fails to reflect how real-world institutions manage opaque expertise. The authors are motivated by gaps between interpretability insights and practical control over model behavior, and by the lack of systematic post-deployment monitoring in existing regulatory approvals (e.g., FDA-approved AI/ML devices).

Method: The authors conceptually analyze the mismatch between mechanistic interpretability and deployment governance, use empirical evidence (such as the large gap between understanding internal representations and correcting outputs, and a scoping review of FDA device documents) to motivate their critique, and then propose a new governance and reporting framework. They formulate a six-component, reportable standard called Verification Coverage with a minimum-composition rule, designed to sit alongside capability metrics in documentation such as model cards, leaderboards, and regulatory filings.

Result: They show that (1) model capabilities can vary significantly across closely related tasks, implying that deployment authorization must be scoped to specific uses; (2) mechanistic understanding of internals does not reliably enable control over outputs, as evidenced by a 53-point gap between internal representation understanding and output correction; and (3) existing regulatory practice rarely includes robust post-market surveillance (only 9% of reviewed FDA AI/ML device documents). On this basis, they develop the Verification Coverage standard specifying what kinds of checks and governance structures should be in place for a deployment.

Conclusion: Deployment of AI in sensitive domains should not hinge primarily on explainability of model internals. Instead, authorization should be based on calibrated, domain-specific verification that is independently checkable, monitored after release, accountable, contestable, and revocable. The proposed Verification Coverage metric should be reported alongside capability scores to better align AI governance with longstanding societal practices for managing opaque expertise and to ensure that evaluations are tightly linked to the specific domain and use-case of deployment.

Abstract: AI deployment in sensitive domains such as health care, credit, employment, and criminal justice is often treated as unsafe to authorize until model internals can be explained. This often leads to an excessive reliance on mechanistic interpretability to address a deployment challenge beyond its intended scope. We argue that the gate should instead be calibrated verification: authorization should be domain-scoped, independently checkable, monitored after release, accountable, contestable, and revocable. The reason is twofold. First, model capability is uneven across nearby tasks, so authorization must attach to a specific use rather than to a model in general. Second, societies have long governed opaque expertise through credentials, monitoring, liability, appeal, and revocation rather than mechanism-level explanation. Recent evidence reinforces this distinction between mechanistic understanding and deployment authority: a 53-percentage-point gap between internal representations and output correction shows that understanding may not translate into action, while one scoping review found that only 9.0% of FDA-approved AI/ML device documents contained a prospective post-market surveillance study. We propose Verification Coverage, a six-component reportable standard with a minimum-composition rule, as the metric that should sit beside capability scores in model cards, leaderboards, and regulatory disclosures.

</details>


### [372] [Hierarchical Causal Abduction: A Foundation Framework for Explainable Model Predictive Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10624)
*Ramesh Arvind Naagarajan,Zühal Wagner,Stefan Streif*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method (HCA) to explain why a nonlinear MPC controller chooses specific actions, using physics knowledge, optimization information, and causal discovery, achieving substantially higher explanation accuracy than existing methods across several domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear MPC is powerful for safety-critical systems but its internal decision process is opaque, which undermines operator trust and hinders adoption; existing explainability tools like LIME are not tailored to physics-based control or constrained optimization and may yield unfaithful or low-accuracy explanations.

Method: Introduce Hierarchical Causal Abduction (HCA), which builds explanations by combining three evidence sources: (1) domain knowledge graphs encoding physics-informed relationships, (2) optimization evidence extracted from KKT multipliers of the MPC problem to see which constraints and objectives are active or influential, and (3) temporal causal links learned via the PCMCI algorithm; these are integrated in a ranking-and-validation framework to generate and score candidate causal explanations for each computed control action; cross-domain experiments and ablations evaluate explanation accuracy vs. expert labels and LIME.

Result: On three control problems (greenhouse climate, building HVAC, and chemical process control), HCA improves explanation accuracy by 53% over LIME (0.478 vs. 0.311) using a single parameterization across domains, and with short domain-specific calibration of KKT thresholds (2-3 days) accuracy reaches 0.88; ablation shows removing any evidence source (knowledge graphs, KKT multipliers, or PCMCI) drops accuracy by 32–37%, and the explanation-ranking framework also works for other prediction-based decision systems like learning-based control and trajectory planning.

Conclusion: HCA provides faithful, human-interpretable explanations for nonlinear MPC decisions by fusing physics knowledge, optimization signals, and data-driven causal discovery, substantially outperforming generic explainability methods, and its general methodology can extend to a wider class of prediction-based decision and control systems.

Abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) is widely used to operate safety-critical infrastructure by predicting future trajectories and optimizing control actions. However, nonlinear dynamics, hard safety constraints, and numerical optimization often render individual control moves opaque to human operators, undermining trust and hindering deployment. This paper presents Hierarchical Causal Abduction (HCA), which combines (i) physics-informed reasoning via domain knowledge graphs, (ii) optimization evidence from Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) multipliers, and (iii) temporal causal discovery via the PCMCI algorithm to generate faithful, human-interpretable explanations for control actions computed by nonlinear MPC. Across three diverse control applications (greenhouse climate, building HVAC, chemical process engineering) with expert validation, HCA improves explanation accuracy by 53\% over LIME (0.478 vs. 0.311) using a single set of cross-domain parameters without per-domain tuning; domain-specific KKT-threshold calibration over 2--3 days further increases accuracy to 0.88. Ablation studies confirm that each evidence source is essential, with 32--37\% accuracy degradation when any component is removed, and HCA's ranking-and-validation methodology generalizes beyond MPC to other prediction-based decision systems, including learning-based control and trajectory planning.

</details>


### [373] [Teacher-Aware Evolution of Heuristic Programs from Learned Optimization Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10634)
*Minyu Chen,Song Qin,Ling-I Wu,Jianxin Xue,Guoqiang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a teacher-aware evolutionary framework where learned optimization policies act as behavioral teachers to guide LLM-designed static heuristics for combinatorial optimization, improving over purely performance-driven LLM evolution without needing neural inference at deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches can automatically design heuristics for combinatorial optimization, but they mostly rely on delayed, end-of-episode performance signals, which are sparse and noisy. There is an opportunity to exploit richer, local behavioral feedback from strong learned optimization policies to better guide the search for good heuristics, while still ending up with cheap, static heuristics that do not require expensive neural inference at test time.

Method: They introduce a teacher-aware evolutionary framework where independently trained neural optimization policies serve as behavioral teachers. Candidate heuristic programs generated by an LLM are executed on problem instances, and for the states they visit, the teacher policy is queried. The teacher’s action preferences on those states provide local behavioral feedback used in the evolutionary search, combined with task performance, to select and mutate heuristic candidates. The teacher is not deployed or imitated end-to-end; it only supplies feedback during evolution.

Result: On benchmarks from scheduling, routing, and graph optimization, the proposed teacher-aware evolution yields static executable heuristics that outperform baselines based purely on performance-driven LLM heuristic evolution. The approach preserves the advantage of not requiring neural inference at deployment, since the teacher is only used in the search phase.

Conclusion: Learned optimization policies can be effectively reused as behavioral feedback providers to guide LLM-based automatic heuristic discovery. Incorporating teacher-aware behavioral signals into evolutionary search produces better static heuristics than relying solely on endpoint performance, while maintaining efficiency at deployment because no neural models are needed then.

Abstract: LLM-based automatic heuristic design has shown promise for generating executable heuristics for combinatorial optimization, but existing methods mainly rely on delayed endpoint performance. We propose a \emph{teacher-aware evolutionary framework} that uses independently trained learned optimization policies as behavioral teachers. Instead of deploying or imitating the teacher, our method queries it on states visited by candidate heuristic programs and uses its action preferences as local feedback for evolution. The resulting search discovers static executable heuristics guided by both task performance and teacher-derived behavioral signals. Experiments on scheduling, routing, and graph optimization benchmarks show that our method improves over performance-driven LLM heuristic evolution baselines while requiring no neural inference at deployment. These results suggest that learned optimization policies can be repurposed as behavioral feedback sources for automatic heuristic discovery.

</details>


### [374] [Navigating the Sea of LLM Evaluation: Investigating Bias in Toxicity Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10639)
*Regina Gugg,Selina Niederländer,Andreas Stöckl,Martin Flechl*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how reliable and unbiased current LLM toxicity/safety benchmarks are, showing they behave inconsistently across tasks, data domains, and model choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are rapidly being deployed in real products, and organizations depend on toxicity/safety benchmarks to decide whether models are safe enough. If these benchmarks themselves are biased or unstable, models that appear safe could still behave harmfully in the wild. The authors want to understand and expose these hidden weaknesses in existing evaluation practices.

Method: They systematically vary key components of standard safety evaluation setups—such as the type of task (e.g., text completion vs. summarization), the domain of the input data, and which LLM is used—and then measure how existing toxicity/safety benchmarks respond. By comparing benchmark outputs under these controlled changes, they identify intrinsic biases and robustness issues in the benchmarks and setups.

Result: They find large discrepancies in how benchmarks behave when the evaluation configuration is changed. For example, when the same underlying content is evaluated via a summarization task instead of a completion task, benchmarks are more likely to label it as harmful. Some benchmarks also lose consistency when the domain of the input data is modified, and different LLMs show model-specific instability under the same benchmark conditions.

Conclusion: Current toxicity/safety benchmarks and evaluation setups are not robust: they are sensitive to task formulation, data domain, and model choice, which introduces hidden biases and instability. Therefore, relying on these benchmarks as-is to certify LLMs for safety is risky, and the field needs more reliable, comprehensive, and systematically designed safety evaluation frameworks.

Abstract: The rapid adoption of LLMs in both research and industry highlights the challenges of deploying them safely and reveals a gap in the systematic evaluation of toxicity benchmarks. As organizations increasingly rely on these benchmarks to certify models for customer-facing applications and automated moderation, unrecognized evaluation biases could lead to the deployment of vulnerable or unsafe systems. This work investigates the robustness of established benchmarking setups and examines how to measure currently neglected intrinsic biases, such as those related to model choice, metrics, and task types. Our experiments uncover significant discrepancies in benchmark behaviors when evaluation setups are altered. Specifically, shifting the task from text completion to summarization increases the tendency of benchmarks to flag content as harmful. Additionally, certain benchmarks fail to maintain consistent behavior when the input data domain is changed. Furthermore, we observe model-specific instabilities, demonstrating a clear need for more robust and comprehensive safety evaluation frameworks.

</details>


### [375] [diffGHOST: Diffusion based Generative Hedged Oblivious Synthetic Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10647)
*Florent Guépin,Cheick Tidiani Cisse,Denis Renaud,François Bidet,Arnaud Legendre*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Paper proposes diffGHOST, a conditional diffusion model for synthetic mobility trajectories that aims to preserve privacy while keeping data utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobility trajectories are highly valuable but sensitive personal data. Existing synthetic trajectory generators incorrectly assume that generative models naturally protect privacy and thus fail to provide explicit privacy guarantees without degrading utility. There is a need for methods that explicitly detect and mitigate memorization of sensitive individual trajectories while still generating useful synthetic data.

Method: They design diffGHOST, a conditional diffusion generative model operating in a learned latent space. The latent space is segmented into condition-specific regions (condition segments). The method includes a methodology to identify when the model memorizes critical or sensitive training samples, using these condition segments, and then applies mitigation strategies in the diffusion/latent space to reduce memorization while maintaining generative quality.

Result: diffGHOST can generate synthetic mobility trajectories that better balance privacy and utility compared to state-of-the-art models, with reduced memorization of critical samples while preserving trajectory usefulness for downstream tasks. (Specific metrics are not given in the abstract.)

Conclusion: Conditional diffusion in a segmented latent space, as implemented in diffGHOST, offers a promising approach to privacy-preserving synthesis of mobility trajectories. By explicitly identifying and mitigating memorization of critical samples at the latent level, the method improves privacy protection without sacrificing the utility of synthetic data.

Abstract: Trajectories are nowadays valuable information for a wide range of applications. However they are also inherently sensitive, as they contain highly personal information about individuals. Facing this challenge, synthesizing mobility trajectories has emerged as a promising solution to leverage mobility information while preserving privacy. State-of-the-art models, often rely on the false assumptions of generative models implicit privacy and fails to provide privacy guarantees while preserving trajectories utility. Here, we introduce diffGHOST, a conditional diffusion model based on latent space segmentation, designed to answer this challenge. Thus, this paper propose a methodology that identify and mitigate memorization of critical samples using condition segments of a learn latent space.

</details>


### [376] [Evolving-RL: End-to-End Optimization of Experience-Driven Self-Evolving Capability within Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10663)
*Zhiyuan Fan,Wenwei Jin,Feng Zhang,Bin Li,Yihong Dong,Yao Hu,Jiawei Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Evolving-RL, a framework that jointly trains an experience extractor and a task solver so that large language model agents can better distill and reuse past experience, substantially improving performance on out-of-distribution tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing experience-driven self-evolving LLM agents depend heavily on ad‑hoc system design choices about how to store and retrieve experience, while largely ignoring how to optimize the underlying model’s ability to abstract, generalize, and use that experience. Prior work either focuses only on representation/management of experience or optimizes only the utilization stage with reinforcement learning, without treating self-evolution (extraction + reuse) as a single, jointly learnable process. There is a need for an algorithmic framework that directly trains LLMs to more effectively extract reusable patterns from interaction histories and apply them to novel tasks.

Method: The authors introduce Evolving-RL, an RL-based framework that explicitly models and trains two key components: an experience extractor and a solver (the agent that acts in the environment). The core of the learning process is experience extraction and evaluation: experiences are extracted from past trajectories, then evaluated, and these evaluations yield two distinct supervisory signals. One signal is used to optimize the extractor, teaching it which experiences are useful; the other optimizes the solver, teaching it how to effectively incorporate extracted experiences. This leads to coordinated co-evolution of extraction and utilization, rather than optimizing them in isolation. The framework also internalizes reusable experience patterns into model parameters, making it an experience-augmented RL algorithm.

Result: On the ALFWorld and Mind2Web benchmarks, Evolving-RL significantly improves LLM agents’ ability to extract and reuse experience, leading to large performance gains on out-of-distribution tasks: up to 98.7% relative improvement over a GRPO baseline on ALFWorld unseen tasks and 35.8% on Mind2Web. Ablations indicate that these gains emerge only when both experience extraction and utilization are co-evolved; optimizing just one side is insufficient. Moreover, the method yields strong performance on both seen and unseen tasks even when no new experience is collected at test time, indicating that much of the benefit is encoded directly into the model parameters.

Conclusion: Evolving-RL provides an effective, unified algorithmic approach to self-evolving LLM agents by jointly training experience extraction and utilization. This coordinated co-evolution improves the abstraction and generalization capabilities needed to distill reusable experience and apply it to new tasks. The framework not only boosts performance on out-of-distribution tasks but also serves as an experience-augmented RL method that embeds learned experience patterns into the model itself, delivering gains even without test-time experience accumulation.

Abstract: Experience-driven self-evolving agents aim to overcome the static nature of large language models by distilling reusable experience from past interactions, thus enabling adaptation to novel tasks at deployment time. This process places substantial demands on the foundation model's capacities for abstraction, generalization, and in-context learning. However, most existing studies focus primarily on system-level design choices, such as how experience is represented and managed, neglecting the inherent capabilities of the underlying model. While some recent works have started to optimize the experience utilization stage via reinforcement learning, they still fail to treat self-evolution as a unified process to be jointly optimized. To this end, we propose Evolving-RL, an efficient algorithmic framework that jointly improves the experience extraction and utilization capabilities required for self-evolution. Specifically, we center the learning process on experience extraction and evaluation, using the two supervisory signals derived from evaluation to optimize the extractor and solver separately and thus enable their coordinated co-evolution. Experiments on ALFWorld and Mind2Web show that Evolving-RL effectively enhances LLMs' ability to extract and reuse experience, leading to strong performance gains on out-of-distribution tasks (up to 98.7% relative improvement over the GRPO baseline on ALFWorld unseen tasks and 35.8% on Mind2Web), and these gains are fully unlocked only through the coordinated co-evolution of experience extraction and utilization. Furthermore, Evolving-RL inherently functions as an experience-augmented RL algorithm. By internalizing reusable experience patterns directly into model parameters, it achieves remarkable performance gains over standard baselines on both seen and unseen tasks, even in the absence of test-time experience accumulation.

</details>


### [377] [GESR: A Genetic Programming-Based Symbolic Regression Method with Gene Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10685)
*Yanjie Li,Liping Zhang,Min Wu,Weijun Li,Lina Yu,Jingyi Liu,Yusong Deng,Mingzhu Wan,Xin Ning*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes GESR, a symbolic regression method that replaces random genetic operations in Genetic Programming with BERT-guided, “gene-editing” style mutation and crossover, significantly improving efficiency and accuracy on symbolic regression benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symbolic regression seeks to discover interpretable mathematical expressions from data, often tackled using Genetic Programming (GP). Standard GP relies on random mutation and crossover, which wastes computation on many unproductive variants. The authors are motivated to make this evolutionary search more directed and efficient by using a model that can “foresee” promising mutations and crossover points, akin to an intelligent designer guiding evolution.

Method: The authors design GESR, a GP-style symbolic regression framework augmented with two BERT models. One BERT model, using masked language modeling, guides mutation by predicting better replacements for masked symbols in expression trees, effectively performing targeted edits instead of random changes. The second BERT model predicts effective crossover points between parent expressions to guide recombination. These BERT-based “hands of God” are trained to understand expression structure and then used during evolution to bias genetic operations toward more promising offspring.

Result: Experiments on multiple symbolic regression benchmarks show that GESR achieves higher computational efficiency compared with traditional GP algorithms, likely via faster convergence and fewer wasted evaluations, while also attaining strong or superior overall performance in terms of solution quality across tasks.

Conclusion: By integrating BERT-based guidance into the mutation and crossover steps of Genetic Programming, GESR transforms blind random search into informed gene editing. This leads to more efficient exploration of the expression space and competitive or improved performance on symbolic regression tasks, suggesting that learned models can effectively steer evolutionary symbolic regression processes.

Abstract: Mathematical formulas serve as a language through which humans communicate with nature. Discovering mathematical laws from scientific data to describe natural phenomena has been a long-standing pursuit of humanity for centuries. In the field of artificial intelligence, this challenge is known as the symbolic regression problem. Among existing symbolic regression approaches, Genetic Programming (GP) based on evolutionary algorithms remains one of the most classical and widely adopted methods. GP simulates the evolutionary process across generations through genetic mutation and crossover. However, mutations and crossovers in GP are entirely random. While this randomness effectively mimics natural evolution, it inevitably produces both beneficial and detrimental variations. If there existed a metaphorical `God` capable of foreseeing which genetic mutations or crossovers would yield superior outcomes and performing targeted gene editing accordingly, the efficiency of evolution could be substantially improved. Motivated by this idea, we propose in this paper a symbolic regression approach based on gene editing, termed GESR. In GESR, we trained two "hands of God" (two BERT models). Among them, the first leverages the BERT's masked language modeling capability to guide the mutation of genes (expression symbols). The other BERT model guides the crossover of individual genes by predicting the crossover point. Experimental results demonstrate that GESR significantly improves computational efficiency compared with traditional GP algorithms and achieves strong overall performance across multiple symbolic regression tasks.

</details>


### [378] [The Agent Use of Agent Beings: Agent Cybernetics Is the Missing Science of Foundation Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10754)
*Xinrun Wang,Chang Yang,He Zhao,Zhuoyi Lin,Shuyue Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a theoretical framework, Agent Cybernetics, to ground LLM-based foundation agents in principles from classical cybernetics, aiming to move from ad-hoc engineering to principled design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based long-horizon agents are built in an ad-hoc, engineering-driven way. While useful implementation primitives exist (tool loops, memory, reflection, etc.), they are combined via trial and error, leaving fundamental questions unanswered about task adherence, handling environmental complexity, and safe self-improvement. The authors are motivated to provide a scientific, first-principles foundation for designing such agents.

Method: The authors revisit classical cybernetics—the science of control and communication in complex systems—and map six canonical laws of cybernetics onto six design principles for foundation agents. They then synthesize these into three concrete engineering desiderata: reliability, lifelong running, and self-improvement. They apply this framework analytically to three domains (code generation, computer use, automated research) to expose typical failure modes and derive engineering recommendations.

Result: The main result is a conceptual framework, Agent Cybernetics, that systematically links cybernetic laws to agent design principles and engineering desiderata. Through case analyses in code generation, computer use, and automated research, the framework yields concrete insights into where current long-horizon agents fail and how to improve them along the axes of reliability, lifelong operation, and safe self-improvement.

Conclusion: Agent Cybernetics is proposed as a foundational theory for LLM-based foundation agents, offering a principled way to reason about their control, communication, robustness, and self-improvement. The authors argue that importing and adapting cybernetic laws can guide more reliable, long-lived, and safely self-improving agents, and they envision this framework as the basis for a new research direction that turns today’s largely ad-hoc engineering practice into a more rigorous science.

Abstract: LLM-based foundation agents that perceive, reason, and act across thousands of reasoning steps are rapidly becoming the dominant paradigm for deploying artificial intelligence in open-ended, long-horizon complex tasks. Despite this significance, the field remains overwhelmingly engineering-driven. Engineering practice has converged on useful primitives (tool loops, memory banks, harnesses, reflection steps), yet these are assembled by empirical trial and error rather than from first principles. Fundamental questions remain open: under what conditions does a long-running agent remain on-task? How should an agent respond when its environment exceeds its representational capacity? What architectural properties are necessary for safe self-improvement? We argue that cybernetics, the mid-twentieth-century science of control and communication in complex systems, provides the missing theoretical scaffold for foundation agents. By mapping six canonical laws of classical cybernetics onto six agent design principles, and synthesizing those principles into three engineering desiderata (reliability, lifelong running, and self-Improvement), we arrive at a framework termed Agent Cybernetics. Three application domains, code generation, computer use and automated research, exemplify the analytical framework of agent cybernetics by identifying failure modes and concrete engineering recommendations. We hope that agent cybernetics opens a new research venue and establishes the scientific foundation that foundation agents need for principled, reliable real-world deployment.

</details>


### [379] [MATRA: Modeling the Attack Surface of Agentic AI Systems -- OpenClaw Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10763)
*Tim Van hamme,Thomas Vissers,Javier Carnerero-Cano,Mario Fritz,Emil C. Lupu,Lieven Desmet,Dinil Mon Divakaran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MATRA, a practical threat modeling framework to systematically assess and mitigate risks in agentic LLM deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous agents with access to tools, data, and services, practitioners lack structured, repeatable methods to translate general LLM threat classes into concrete, deployment-specific risks. Existing security and risk assessment methodologies are not directly tailored to the unique attack surfaces and behaviors of agentic AI systems, leaving a gap in practical guidance for securing such deployments.

Method: The paper proposes MATRA, a threat modeling framework that adapts established risk assessment methodologies to agentic LLM systems. MATRA starts from an asset-based impact assessment to identify what needs protection and what harms matter most. It then uses attack trees mapped onto the system architecture to analyze how known LLM-specific threats could lead to those impacts, allowing estimation of likelihood and risk. The framework is demonstrated on a concrete personal AI agent deployment using OpenClaw, showing how to model the system, enumerate attack paths, and quantify risk under different architectural controls.

Result: Using MATRA on a personal AI agent deployment with OpenClaw, the authors show that specific security controls—such as network sandboxing and least-privilege access for tools—measurably lower risk. These controls reduce the potential blast radius of successful prompt injection or similar attacks as reflected in the attack-tree-based likelihood and impact estimates. The case study demonstrates that MATRA can turn high-level LLM threat classes into concrete, quantifiable risk assessments for a given architecture.

Conclusion: MATRA provides a pragmatic, systematic framework for threat modeling agentic LLM systems, bridging the gap between abstract LLM threat taxonomies and deployment-specific security analysis. By combining asset-based impact assessment with attack trees grounded in system architecture, practitioners can evaluate and compare security controls, understand how threats manifest in their particular setup, and prioritize mitigations such as sandboxing and least-privilege designs to reduce overall risk.

Abstract: LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents with access to tools, databases, and external services, yet practitioners (across different sectors) lack systematic methods to assess how known threat classes translate into concrete risks within a specific agentic deployment. We present MATRA, a pragmatic threat modeling framework for agentic AI systems that adapts established risk assessment methodology to systematically assess how known LLM threats translate into deployment-specific risks. MATRA begins with an asset-based impact assessment and utilizes attack trees to determine the likelihood of these impacts occurring within the system architecture. We demonstrate MATRA on a personal AI agent deployment using OpenClaw, quantifying how architectural controls such as network sandboxing and least-privilege access reduce risk by limiting the blast radius of successful injections.

</details>


### [380] [TrajPrism: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Language-Grounded Urban Trajectory Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10782)
*Lihuan Li,Wilson Wongso,Baiyu Chen,Hao Xue,Ruiyi Yang,Yifan Duan,Xiachong Lin,Yang Song,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrajPrism is a large multi-task benchmark connecting natural language and real-world urban trajectories, enabling generation, retrieval, and captioning tasks with a unified evaluation protocol and baseline models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban mobility data comes both as geometric trajectories and as natural-language descriptions (intent, constraints, preferences), but prior work typically focuses on only one modality: geometry-centric trajectory modeling or language-centric route planning/tool-use benchmarks. There is a lack of resources and evaluations that jointly test fine-grained, verifiable alignment between natural language and actual real-world trajectories over maps.

Method: The authors build TrajPrism, a multi-task benchmark which (1) defines three language-trajectory alignment tasks: instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, language-driven semantic trajectory retrieval, and trajectory captioning; (2) proposes an evaluation protocol measuring trajectory fidelity, retrieval quality, and language groundedness; (3) constructs the dataset by pairing real urban trajectories from Porto, San Francisco, and Beijing with judge-filtered language annotations organized under a four-dimensional travel-intent taxonomy. The dataset totals 300K trajectories, with three instruction variants, three retrieval queries, and one caption per trajectory, leading to 2.1M task instances. They also design three proof-of-concept models—TrajAnchor, TrajFuse, and TrajRap—to instantiate each task.

Result: TrajPrism provides a large-scale, multi-city dataset and unified benchmark for language-trajectory alignment, with 300K trajectories and 2.1M task instances. The baseline models (TrajAnchor for generation, TrajFuse for retrieval, TrajRap for captioning) demonstrate that existing geometry-only trajectory baselines perform substantially worse on the new protocol, particularly when language is part of the input or output, indicating a significant performance gap and the necessity of language-aware models.

Conclusion: TrajPrism establishes a unified benchmark and evaluation protocol for aligning natural language with real-world urban trajectories, covering generation, retrieval, and captioning tasks. The initial models show that incorporating language is crucial, as geometry-only methods leave large gaps. The authors release the dataset, code, and a reproducible, city-portable annotation pipeline, aiming to catalyze research on multimodal urban mobility modeling that generalizes across cities given compatible trajectory and map data.

Abstract: Urban mobility is naturally expressed both as trajectories in space and as natural-language descriptions of travel intent, constraints, and preferences. However, prior work rarely evaluates these two modalities together on the same real-world trajectories: trajectory modeling often stays geometry-centric, while language-centric mobility benchmarks frequently target route planning and tool use rather than fine-grained, verifiable alignment between text and the underlying route. We introduce TrajPrism, a multi-task benchmark for language-trajectory alignment that unifies (i) instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, (ii) language-driven semantic trajectory retrieval, and (iii) trajectory captioning, together with an evaluation protocol that measures trajectory fidelity, retrieval quality, and language groundedness. We construct TrajPrism by pairing real urban trajectories with judge-filtered language annotations generated under a four-dimensional travel-intent taxonomy. The benchmark contains 300K selected trajectories across Porto, San Francisco, and Beijing, yielding 2.1M task instances from three instruction variants, three retrieval queries, and one caption per trajectory. We further develop proof-of-concept models for each task: TrajAnchor for instruction-conditioned trajectory generation, TrajFuse for semantic trajectory retrieval, and TrajRap for trajectory captioning. These models instantiate the proposed tasks and show that geometry-only trajectory baselines leave a large gap on our protocol, especially where language is part of the input-output interface. We release TrajPrism with code and a reproducible annotation pipeline that is designed to be portable across cities, given compatible trajectory inputs and map resources.

</details>


### [381] [ComplexMCP: Evaluation of LLM Agents in Dynamic, Interdependent, and Large-Scale Tool Sandbox](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10787)
*Yuanyang Li,Xue Yang,Longyue Wang,Weihua Luo,Hongyang Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ComplexMCP, a benchmark to test LLM agents on realistic, interdependent software tool use, revealing large gaps between current agents and human performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agents work reasonably well with simple, isolated API calls but fail on realistic, commercial software automation where tools are interdependent, stateful, and subject to noisy, failing environments. Current benchmarks do not adequately capture this complexity or the challenges of robust multi-tool workflows, so there is no rigorous way to measure or improve agents for real-world automation.

Method: The authors build ComplexMCP on top of the Model Context Protocol (MCP), constructing 7 stateful sandbox environments (e.g., office suites and financial systems) that expose over 300 tested tools. They use a seed-driven architecture to generate dynamic but reproducible environment states and to inject unpredictable API failures. They then evaluate different LLMs, both in full-context and RAG settings, on this benchmark and perform trajectory-level error analysis to identify core failure modes.

Result: Across models and paradigms, no LLM agent surpasses about 60% task success, substantially below human performance of roughly 90%. Detailed analysis of agent trajectories shows recurring problems: difficulty retrieving the right tools as the tool set grows (tool retrieval saturation), agents acting without necessary checks due to misplaced confidence (over-confidence), and agents giving up prematurely while rationalizing failure instead of attempting recovery (strategic defeatism).

Conclusion: Current LLM agents are not yet reliable for complex, interdependent software workflows, particularly under noisy, stateful conditions. ComplexMCP offers a deterministic yet diverse benchmark that surfaces key failure modes and can serve as a crucial testbed for developing more resilient, recovery-capable autonomous systems.

Abstract: Current LLM agents are proficient at calling isolated APIs but struggle with the "last mile" of commercial software automation. In real-world scenarios, tools are not independent; they are atomic, interdependent, and prone to environmental noise. We introduce $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$, a benchmark designed to evaluate agents in these rigorous conditions. Built on the Model Context Protocol (MCP), $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$ provides over 300 meticulously tested tools derived from 7 stateful sandboxes, ranging from office suites to financial systems. Unlike existing datasets, our benchmark utilizes a seed-driven architecture to simulate dynamic environment states and unpredictable API failures, ensuring a deterministic yet diverse evaluation.
