  We evaluate various LLMs across full-context and RAG paradigms, revealing a stark performance gap: even top-tier models fail to exceed a 60% success rate, far trailing human performance 90%. Granular trajectory analysis identifies three fundamental bottlenecks: (1) $\textbf{tool retrieval saturation}$ as action spaces scale; (2) $\textbf{over-confidence}$, where agents skip essential environment verifications; and (3) $\textbf{strategic defeatism}$, a tendency to rationalize failure rather than pursuing recovery. These findings underscore the insufficiency of current agents for interdependent workflows, positioning $\textbf{ComplexMCP}$ as a critical testbed for the next generation of resilient autonomous systems.

</details>


### [382] [PathISE: Learning Informative Path Supervision for Knowledge Graph Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10791)
*Shengxiang Gao,Chao Lei,Jey Han Lau,Jianzhong Qi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PathISE is a KGQA framework that learns path-level supervision from only answer labels, then uses it to train an LLM path generator that supplies compact KG evidence for answer reasoning, reaching SOTA/competitive performance without costly manually/LLM-curated supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most KGQA systems using retrieval-augmented generation need high-quality intermediate supervision, such as gold reasoning paths or subgraphs, to train their retrievers. Obtaining these paths is labor-intensive, expensive, and often requires additional LLM refinement, limiting scalability and practicality. There is a need for a method that can learn effective path-level supervision directly from cheaper answer-level labels while still supporting strong reasoning over KGs and being reusable across models.

Method: PathISE introduces a two-stage framework. First, it trains a lightweight transformer-based estimator that, given candidate relation paths for a question, scores their informativeness with respect to the answer, essentially constructing pseudo path-level supervision from answer-only labels. Second, this pseudo supervision is distilled into an LLM-based path generator which, conditioned on a question, generates likely reasoning paths. These generated paths are then grounded back to the KG to form compact evidence subgraphs, which are fed into an inductive answer reasoning module (LLM-based) for final KGQA. The learned path informativeness signals can be reused to enhance other KGQA models.

Result: On three standard KGQA benchmarks, PathISE attains competitive or state-of-the-art performance compared with existing KGQA approaches. It does so without relying on expensive, manually annotated or LLM-refined intermediate supervision and shows that its learned supervision signals can be successfully transferred to and improve other KGQA models.

Conclusion: Answer-level labels are sufficient to derive effective pseudo path-level supervision for KGQA. By estimating path informativeness with a lightweight transformer and distilling it into an LLM path generator, PathISE can provide compact KG evidence for strong inductive reasoning, reaching SOTA or competitive KGQA performance while reducing supervision costs and offering reusable supervision that benefits other models.

Abstract: Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) aims to answer user questions by reasoning over Knowledge Graphs (KGs). Recent KGQA methods mainly follow the retrieval-augmented generation paradigm to ground Large Language Models~(LLMs) with structured knowledge from KGs. However, training effective models to retrieve question-relevant evidence from KGs typically requires high-quality intermediate supervision signals, such as question-relevant paths or subgraphs, which are time- and resource-intensive to obtain. We propose PathISE, a novel framework for learning high-quality intermediate supervision from answer-level labels. PathISE introduces a lightweight transformer-based estimator that estimates the informativeness of relation paths to construct pseudo path-level supervision. This supervision is then distilled into an LLM path generator, whose generated paths are grounded in the KG to provide compact evidence for inductive answer reasoning. ExtensiveISE experiments on three KGQA benchmarks show that PathISE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art KGQA performance, and provides reusable supervision signals that can enhance existing KGQA models, without relying on costly LLM-refined supervision signals. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PathISE-2F87.

</details>


### [383] [Interpretable Machine Learning for Football Performance Analysis: Evidence of Limited Transferability from Elite Leagues to University Competition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10796)
*Yu-Fang Tsai,Yu-Jen Chen,Kok-Hua Tan,Sheng-Chieh Huang,You-Ying Ji,Yu-Lun Chen,Chun-Yi Wang,Chien-Ming Hsu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper examines whether machine‑learning–based performance determinants and their explanations learned from elite football transfer reliably to university-level football, finding that interpretability degrades under domain shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most football ML research focuses on prediction accuracy and assumes that learned determinants (and their explanations) generalize across competition levels. However, it is unclear whether interpretability methods remain reliable when models trained on elite football are applied to non-elite (e.g., university) football, where game structure and behavior differ.

Method: Train Random Forest and Multilayer Perceptron models on large-scale event data from the top five European football leagues using a shared feature space. Apply the trained models to university football data from National Tsing Hua University. Analyze and compare performance determinants using SHAP and Counterfactual Impact Score across five experimental settings, assessing stability, ranking consistency, and structural agreement between elite and university domains.

Result: In elite competitions, the importance ranking of performance determinants is stable and consistent across leagues, models, and explanation methods. In NTHU university football, key indicators are substantially reordered, explanation stability is lower, structural agreement with elite determinants is weaker, and explanations are more sensitive to the chosen interpretability method.

Conclusion: Interpretability robustness is domain-dependent: explanations that appear stable in elite football do not reliably transfer to university-level football. Instability of explanations under domain shift should not be viewed solely as a failure of interpretability methods; it can act as a diagnostic signal indicating structural ambiguity or genuine differences in the target domain’s performance determinants.

Abstract: Machine learning has become increasingly prevalent in football performance analysis, yet most studies prioritize predictive accuracy while implicitly assuming that learned performance determinants and their interpretations are transferable across competition levels. Whether interpretability remains reliable under domain shift-from elite to university football remains largely unexplored. This study investigates whether performance determinants learned from elite competitions are structurally transferable to university-level football and whether their interpretations remain robust under domain shift. Models were trained on large-scale event data from the top five European leagues and applied to university football data from National Tsing Hua University (NTHU) using an identical feature space. Random Forest and Multilayer Perceptron models were interpreted using SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Counterfactual Impact Score (CIS). Across five experiments, elite football exhibited a stable and consistent hierarchy of performance determinants across leagues, models, and explanation methods. In contrast, NTHU university football showed substantial reordering of key indicators, reduced explanation stability, weaker structural agreement with elite domains, and increased sensitivity to explanation method. These findings suggest that interpretability robustness is domain-dependent. Rather than reflecting methodological limitations alone, instability in explanations under domain shift may serve as a diagnostic signal of structural ambiguity in the target domain.

</details>


### [384] [NanoResearch: Co-Evolving Skills, Memory, and Policy for Personalized Research Automation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10813)
*Jinhang Xu,Qiyuan Zhu,Yujun Wu,Zirui Wang,Dongxu Zhang,Jianxin Tang,Marcia Tian,Yiling Duan,Siyuan Li,Jingxuan Wei,Sirui Han,Yike Guo,Odin Zhang,Conghui He,Cheng Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NanoResearch is a personalized, LLM-powered multi-agent framework that automates the research pipeline while adapting to each individual researcher via skills, memory, and preference learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent research systems can automate many tasks but treat all users uniformly, ignoring differences in resources, methods, and goals. This lack of personalization leads to suboptimal support and limits real-world usability. The authors aim to make research automation genuinely useful for diverse researchers by enabling systems to remember, adapt, and reuse procedural knowledge across projects and sessions.

Method: The authors design NanoResearch, a multi-agent framework with three co-evolving components: (1) a skill bank that abstracts recurring operations into reusable procedural rules applicable across projects; (2) a memory module that stores user- and project-specific experiences to inform future planning and decisions; and (3) a label-free policy learning mechanism that transforms free-form user feedback into parameter updates of the central planner agent, thereby internalizing user preferences without explicit labels. These components are integrated into a tri-level co-evolution loop where skills, memory, and preference policy continually refine one another during repeated research cycles.

Result: Experiments show that NanoResearch outperforms state-of-the-art AI research systems on measures of research quality and efficiency. Over multiple cycles, the system not only produces better research outputs but also reduces the cost of achieving them, indicating successful accumulation of reusable skills, effective use of historical memory, and improved alignment with user preferences.

Conclusion: Personalization is essential for practical research automation, and can be achieved by jointly evolving procedural skills, user-grounded memory, and preference-aware planning. NanoResearch’s tri-level co-evolution framework enables an LLM-based multi-agent system to adapt over time to individual researchers, leading to higher-quality, lower-cost research outputs than existing systems that lack such adaptive capabilities.

Abstract: LLM-powered multi-agent systems can now automate the full research pipeline from ideation to paper writing, but a fundamental question remains: automation for whom? Researchers operate under different resource configurations, hold different methodological preferences, and target different output formats. A system that produces uniform outputs regardless of these differences will systematically under-serve every individual user, making personalization a precondition for research automation to be genuinely usable. However, achieving it requires three capabilities that current systems lack: accumulating reusable procedural knowledge across projects, retaining user-specific experience across sessions, and internalizing implicit preferences that resist explicit formalization. We propose NanoResearch, a multi-agent framework that addresses these gaps through tri-level co-evolution. A skill bank distills recurring operations into compact procedural rules reusable across projects. A memory module maintains user- and project-specific experience that grounds planning decisions in each user's research history. A label-free policy learning converts free-form feedback into persistent parameter updates of the planner, reshaping subsequent coordination. These three layers co-evolve: reliable skills produce richer memory, richer memory informs better planning, and preference internalization continuously realigns the loop to each user. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NanoResearch delivers substantial gains over state-of-the-art AI research systems, and progressively refines itself to produce better research at lower cost over successive cycles.

</details>


### [385] [Probing Cross-modal Information Hubs in Audio-Visual LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10815)
*Jihoo Jung,Chaeyoung Jung,Ji-Hoon Kim,Joon Son Chung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how audio and visual information interact inside audio-visual large language models, finding that specific “cross-modal sink tokens” store most integrated cross-modal information and using this insight to reduce hallucinations without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While text-only and vision-language models have been widely probed, the internal mechanics of emerging audio-visual LLMs are poorly understood, especially how audio and visual information flow between modalities. The authors want to identify where and how one modality’s information is encoded in the token representations of the other, to both deepen understanding and improve model reliability.

Method: They study several recent AVLLMs and perform representation-level analyses to trace cross-modal information flow, focusing on how audio-derived information appears in visual tokens and vice versa. They examine the roles of special tokens (sink tokens) and identify which ones actually carry integrated audio-visual information, distinguishing them from generic sink tokens. Based on these insights, they design a training-free procedure that steers model reliance toward these cross-modal sink tokens at inference time to mitigate hallucinations.

Result: The analysis reveals two key patterns across different AVLLMs: (1) integrated audio-visual information tends to be concentrated in sink tokens rather than being evenly distributed, and (2) only a specific subset of these sink tokens—termed cross-modal sink tokens—actually specialize in encoding cross-modal information. Leveraging these findings, the authors show that reweighting model behavior to rely more on cross-modal sink tokens can reduce hallucinations without additional training.

Conclusion: AVLLMs encode most cross-modal (audio↔visual) information in a small, specialized subset of sink tokens. Recognizing and exploiting these cross-modal sink tokens provides both a mechanistic insight into AVLLMs’ internal representations and a practical, training-free way to mitigate hallucinations by encouraging the model to base its outputs on genuinely integrated cross-modal evidence.

Abstract: Audio-visual large language models (AVLLMs) have recently emerged as a powerful architecture capable of jointly reasoning over audio, visual, and textual modalities. In AVLLMs, the bidirectional interaction between audio and video modalities introduces intricate processing dynamics, necessitating a deeper understanding of their internal mechanisms. However, unlike extensively studied text-only or large vision language models, the internal workings of AVLLMs remain largely unexplored. In this paper, we focus on cross-modal information flow between audio and visual modalities in AVLLMs, investigating where information derived from one modality is encoded within the token representations of the other modality. Through an analysis of multiple recent AVLLMs, we uncover two common findings. First, AVLLMs primarily encode integrated audio-visual information in sink tokens. Second, sink tokens do not uniformly hold cross-modal information. Instead, a distinct subset of sink tokens, which we term cross-modal sink tokens, specializes in storing such information. Based on these findings, we further propose a simple training-free hallucination mitigation method by encouraging reliance on integrated cross-modal information within cross-modal sink tokens. Our code is available at https://github.com/kaistmm/crossmodal-hub.

</details>


### [386] [CLEF: EEG Foundation Model for Learning Clinical Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10817)
*Peng Cao,Ali Mirzazadeh,Jong Woo Lee,Aleksandar Videnovic,Dina Katabi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CLEF, a long-context, clinically grounded EEG foundation model that integrates full-session EEG spectrograms with clinical reports and EHR data, achieving strong performance across many clinical prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical EEG reading requires interpreting long sessions and combining EEG patterns with rich clinical context, but most existing EEG foundation models focus on short signal windows and ignore associated clinical information, limiting their clinical utility.

Method: Proposes CLEF, which tokenizes entire EEG sessions as 3D multitaper spectrograms suitable for Transformer-based long-context modeling and aligns learned EEG representations with neurologist text reports and structured EHR data using contrastive learning; evaluates on a large, diverse benchmark of 234 clinical tasks covering diseases, medications, and EEG findings.

Result: On more than 260k EEG sessions from over 108k patients, CLEF outperforms prior EEG foundation models on 229/234 tasks, increasing mean AUROC from 0.65 to 0.74; reconstruction-only pretraining already beats prior models, and additional alignment with reports and EHR further improves performance; representations also transfer to unseen concepts and external cohorts.

Conclusion: Session-scale, clinically grounded representation learning that jointly leverages full-length EEG signals and clinical context is an effective paradigm for EEG foundation models and yields broadly transferable, high-performing clinical EEG representations.

Abstract: Clinical EEG interpretation requires reasoning over full EEG sessions and integrating signal patterns with clinical context. Existing EEG foundation models are largely designed for short-window decoding and do not incorporate clinical context. We introduce CLEF, a clinically grounded long-context EEG foundation model. CLEF represents EEG sessions as 3D multitaper spectrogram tokens, enabling tractable Transformer modeling at session scale, and aligns embeddings with neurologist reports and structured EHR data through contrastive objectives. We evaluate CLEF on a new 234-task benchmark spanning disease phenotypes, medication exposures, and EEG findings, with more than 260k EEG sessions from over 108k patients. CLEF outperforms prior EEG foundation models on 229 of 234 tasks, improving mean AUROC from 0.65 to 0.74. Reconstruction-only pretraining surpasses prior EEG foundation models, while report and EHR alignment yields further gains. Held-out concept and external-cohort experiments suggest that these representations transfer beyond observed alignment targets. These results support session-scale, clinically grounded representation learning as a promising foundation-model paradigm for clinical EEG.

</details>


### [387] [MaD Physics: Evaluating information seeking under constraints in physical environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10820)
*Moksh Jain,Mehdi Bennani,Johannes Bausch,Yuri Chervonyi,Bogdan Georgiev,Simon Osindero,Nenad Tomašev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MaD Physics is a benchmark to test how well AI agents can make and plan measurements under resource constraints to discover physical laws.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks either test static reasoning over known facts or unconstrained experimental design, and thus fail to capture the realistic, resource-limited nature of scientific measurement and discovery. There is a need for a benchmark that evaluates whether agents can strategically trade off measurement quality and quantity to infer underlying physical laws.

Method: The authors design MaD Physics, a benchmark composed of three simulated environments, each governed by a (sometimes altered) physical law. In each trial, an agent is given a limited budget that constrains both how many measurements it can make and their quality. The agent must choose which measurements to perform, then—after the budget is exhausted—infer the governing law and make future-state predictions. The benchmark is used to test multiple agent capabilities, including model inference, constrained planning, multimodal reasoning, and in-context learning. The authors evaluate several Gemini models on these tasks.

Result: Across the MaD Physics environments, the tested Gemini models (2.5 Flash Lite, 2.5 Flash, 2.5 Pro, and 3 Flash) exhibit limitations in structured exploration, measurement selection, and data collection strategies under constraints, indicating that current models struggle to perform optimal scientific measurement and discovery in resource-limited settings.

Conclusion: MaD Physics effectively exposes key weaknesses of current AI agents in data-driven model inference and constrained planning for scientific discovery. It provides a more realistic and challenging evaluation setting than prior benchmarks, and the authors suggest it as a tool to guide future improvements in agents’ scientific reasoning, exploration strategies, and multimodal and in-context learning capabilities.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is fundamentally a resource-constrained process that requires navigating complex trade-offs between the quality and quantity of measurements due to physical and cost constraints. Measurements drive the scientific process by revealing novel phenomena to improve our understanding. Existing benchmarks for evaluating agents for scientific discovery focus on either static knowledge-based reasoning or unconstrained experimental design tasks, and do not capture the ability to make measurements and plan under constraints. To bridge this gap, we propose Measuring and Discovering Physics (MaD Physics), a benchmark to evaluate the ability of agents to make informative measurements and conclusions subject to constraints on the quality and quantity of measurements. The benchmark consists of three environments, each based on a distinct physical law. To mitigate contamination from existing knowledge, MaD Physics includes altered physical laws. In each trial, the agent makes measurements of the system until it exhausts an allotted budget and then the agent has to infer the underlying physical law to make predictions about the state of the system in the future. MaD Physics evaluates two fundamental capabilities of scientific agents: inferring models from data and planning under constraints. We also demonstrate how MaD Physics can be used to evaluate other capabilities such as multimodality and in-context learning. We benchmark agents on MaD Physics using four Gemini models (2.5 Flash Lite, 2.5 Flash, 2.5 Pro, and 3 Flash), identifying shortcomings in their structured exploration and data collection capabilities and highlighting directions to improve their scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [388] [The First Drop of Ink: Nonlinear Impact of Misleading Information in Long-Context Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10828)
*Muhan Gao,Zih-Ching Chen,Kuan-Hao Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how a small number of misleading but relevant documents in long contexts can disproportionately harm large language model performance, identifying a sharp initial performance drop as distractors are introduced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are used in retrieval-augmented and agentic systems with very long contexts, they often receive mixtures of helpful and misleading but relevant documents. While prior work shows such "hard distractors" hurt performance, it is unknown how performance scales with the fraction of distractors, which is crucial for designing retrieval and filtering strategies.

Method: The authors systematically vary the proportion of hard distractor documents within fixed-length input contexts and measure task performance across this spectrum. They analyze the behavior using attention-based theoretical models and empirical attention pattern measurements. They also conduct controlled experiments that compare the impact of removing distractors versus simply shortening the context to understand whether gains come from content filtering or context-length reduction.

Result: They find a nonlinear relationship between distractor proportion and performance: performance drops steeply when even a small fraction of hard distractors is introduced, then declines only marginally as more distractors are added. Attention analyses show that hard distractors attract a disproportionately large share of attention even at low proportions, and that additional distractors have diminishing marginal effects. Filtering experiments reveal that most performance gains arise from reducing context length, and that substantial recovery occurs only when the hard-distractor proportion is driven close to zero.

Conclusion: The work identifies "The First Drop of Ink" effect: the first few hard distractors in a long context cause most of the performance degradation, with later distractors adding relatively little extra harm. This is explained through attention mechanics and implies that high-precision upstream retrieval, which minimizes the presence of hard distractors rather than merely limiting context length, is critical for maintaining LLM performance in long-context, retrieval-augmented and agentic systems.

Abstract: As large language models are increasingly deployed in retrieval-augmented generation and agentic systems that accumulate extensive context, understanding how distracting information affects long-context performance becomes critical. Prior work shows that semantically relevant yet misleading documents degrade performance, but the quantitative relationship between the proportion of distractors and performance remains unstudied. In this work, we systematically vary the hard-distractor proportion in fixed-length contexts, revealing a striking nonlinear pattern: as the proportion of hard distractors increases, performance drops sharply within the first small fraction, while the remainder of the range yields only marginal additional decline. We term this ''The First Drop of Ink'' effect, analogous to how a single drop of ink contaminates water. Our theoretical and empirical analyses grounded in attention mechanics show that hard distractors capture disproportionate attention even at small proportions, with diminishing marginal impact as their proportion grows. Controlled experiments further show that filtering gains mainly come from context-length reduction rather than distractor removal; substantial recovery requires reducing the hard-distractor proportion to near zero, highlighting the importance of upstream retrieval precision.

</details>


### [389] [From Controlled to the Wild: Evaluation of Pentesting Agents for the Real-World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10834)
*Pedro Conde,Henrique Branquinho,Valerio Mazzone,Bruno Mendes,André Baptista,Nuno Moniz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new, practical benchmark protocol for evaluating AI pentesting agents based on discovering real vulnerabilities rather than just completing simplified tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for AI pentesting agents focus on narrow, pre-defined tasks (e.g., CTF, exploit reproduction) that do not reflect the open-ended, complex, and strategic nature of real-world penetration testing. There is a need for an evaluation framework that better predicts how these agents will perform on realistic, multi-surface targets.

Method: The authors design an evaluation protocol that measures validated vulnerability discovery across complex targets with multiple attack surfaces and vulnerability types. It uses a combination of structured, expert-annotated ground truth and LLM-based semantic matching to map agents’ findings to known vulnerabilities. A bipartite resolution mechanism scores findings under ambiguity, with continuous ground-truth maintenance, repeated and cumulative evaluation for stochastic agents, efficiency metrics, and a reduced benchmark suite for sustainable experimentation. They also release code and annotated ground truth (Ethibench) for reproducibility.

Result: The protocol enables more realistic and operationally relevant comparisons between AI pentesting agents, capturing their ability to discover diverse vulnerabilities efficiently in complex environments. It demonstrates that evaluation centered on validated vulnerability discovery provides richer insight than task-completion metrics used in prior benchmarks. The authors provide an open-source implementation and dataset to support community use.

Conclusion: By shifting the evaluation focus from narrow task completion to validated vulnerability discovery and by incorporating mechanisms to handle ambiguity, stochasticity, and efficiency, the proposed protocol advances the state of the art in benchmarking AI pentesting agents. The released Ethibench resources make this approach reproducible and practical for ongoing research and development.

Abstract: AI pentesting agents are increasingly credible as offensive security systems, but current benchmarks still provide limited guidance on which will perform best in real-world targets. Existing evaluation protocols assess and optimize for predefined goals such as capture-the-flag, remote code execution, exploit reproduction, or trajectory similarity, in simplified or narrow settings. These tools are valuable for measuring bounded capabilities, yet they do not adequately capture the complexity, open-ended exploration, and strategic decision-making required in realistic pentesting. In this paper, we present a practical evaluation protocol that shifts assessment from task completion to validated vulnerability discovery, allowing evaluation in sufficiently complex targets spanning multiple attack surfaces and vulnerability classes. The protocol combines structured ground-truth with LLM-based semantic matching to identify vulnerabilities, bipartite resolution to score findings under realistic ambiguity, continuous ground-truth maintenance, repeated and cumulative evaluation of stochastic agents, efficiency metrics, and reduced-suite selection for sustainable experimentation. This protocol extends the state of the art by enabling a more realistic, operationally informative comparison of AI pentesting agents. To enable reproducibility, we also release expert-annotated ground truth and code for the proposed evaluation protocol: https://github.com/jd0965199-oss/ethibench.

</details>


### [390] [BenchCAD: A Comprehensive, Industry-Standard Benchmark for Programmatic CAD](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10865)
*Haozhe Zhang,Kaichen Liu,Miaomiao Chen,Lei Li,Shaojie Yang,Cheng Peng,Hanjie Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BenchCAD is a benchmark of execution-verified parametric CAD programs for evaluating and improving multimodal LLMs on industrial CAD reasoning and code generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial CAD automation needs models that can go beyond recognizing shapes to generating correct, executable, parametric CAD programs that reflect real design and manufacturing workflows. Existing evaluations of multimodal LLMs do not sufficiently test whether models jointly understand 3D structure, engineering parameters, and appropriate CAD operations in realistic industrial settings, leaving a gap between research demos and industrial needs.

Method: The authors construct BenchCAD, a unified benchmark containing 17,900 execution-verified CadQuery programs covering 106 realistic industrial part families such as gears, springs, and drills. They design multiple evaluation tasks: visual question answering, code question answering, image-to-code generation, and instruction-guided code editing. These tasks probe distinct abilities: visual perception of 3D parts, abstraction into parametric design variables, and synthesis or modification of executable CAD code. They then systematically evaluate more than ten frontier multimodal large language models on this benchmark.

Result: BenchCAD reveals that current multimodal models can often reproduce the coarse outer geometry of CAD parts but frequently fail to generate accurate parametric CAD programs. Typical errors include omitting fine internal or 3D structural details, misunderstanding key engineering parameters, and substituting complex CAD operations (e.g., sweeps, lofts, twist-extrudes) with simpler but incorrect sketch-and-extrude approaches. While fine-tuning and reinforcement learning can improve performance on part families seen during training, the models still struggle to generalize well to unseen part categories.

Conclusion: BenchCAD provides a challenging, execution-grounded benchmark for assessing industrial CAD reasoning in multimodal LLMs. It exposes current limitations in parametric understanding, 3D structural reasoning, and operation-level CAD modeling, especially for out-of-distribution parts. The benchmark is positioned as a tool for tracking progress and guiding methods that move multimodal CAD automation closer to industrial readiness.

Abstract: Industrial Computer-Aided Design (CAD) code generation requires models to produce executable parametric programs from visual or textual inputs. Beyond recognizing the outer shape of a part, this task involves understanding its 3D structure, inferring engineering parameters, and choosing CAD operations that reflect how the part would be designed and manufactured. Despite the promise of Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for this task, they are rarely evaluated on whether these capabilities jointly hold in realistic industrial CAD settings. We present BenchCAD, a unified benchmark for industrial CAD reasoning. BenchCAD contains 17,900 execution-verified CadQuery programs across 106 industrial part families, including bevel gears, compression springs, twist drills, and other reusable engineering designs. It evaluates models through visual question answering, code question answering, image-to-code generation, and instruction-guided code editing, enabling fine-grained analysis across perception, parametric abstraction, and executable program synthesis. Across 10+ frontier models, BenchCAD shows that current systems often recover coarse outer geometry but fail to produce faithful parametric CAD programs. Common failures include missing fine 3D structure, misinterpreting industrial design parameters, and replacing essential operations such as sweeps, lofts, and twist-extrudes with simpler sketch-and-extrude patterns. Fine-tuning and reinforcement learning improve in-distribution performance, but generalization to unseen part families remains limited. These results position BenchCAD as a benchmark for measuring and improving the industrial readiness of multimodal CAD automation.

</details>


### [391] [Remember the Decision, Not the Description: A Rate-Distortion Framework for Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10870)
*Mingxi Zou,Zhihan Guo,Langzhang Liang,Zhuo Wang,Qifan Wang,Qingsong Wen,Irwin King,Lizhen Qu,Zenglin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a decision-centric framework and algorithm (DeMem) for memory in long-horizon language agents, optimizing what to forget based on impact on decision quality rather than descriptive fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory systems for language agents focus on descriptive properties (relevance, salience, summary quality) under limited runtime memory, but these do not directly measure how memory affects the agent’s ability to make good decisions. There is a need for a principled way to decide what information to retain or forget under a fixed memory budget, grounded in decision performance.

Method: They formulate memory management as a decision-centric rate-distortion problem where compression is evaluated by the loss in achievable decision quality. This yields theoretical constructs such as an exact forgetting boundary and a memory–distortion frontier. Building on this, they introduce DeMem, an online memory-learning algorithm that maintains and refines a partition of histories, splitting states only when data show that merging them would lead to conflicting decisions. They analyze DeMem theoretically, proving near-minimax regret guarantees.

Result: In synthetic diagnostic environments and long-horizon conversational benchmarks, DeMem improves decision performance compared to baselines under the same runtime memory budget, consistently achieving better agent behavior while using the same amount of memory.

Conclusion: Decision-centric memory—organizing and compressing experience around distinctions that matter for decisions rather than descriptive faithfulness—leads to better use of limited runtime memory. The DeMem algorithm operationalizes this principle, achieving theoretically grounded performance guarantees and empirical gains on long-horizon tasks.

Abstract: Long-horizon language agents must operate under limited runtime memory, yet existing memory mechanisms often organize experience around descriptive criteria such as relevance, salience, or summary quality. For an agent, however, memory is valuable not because it faithfully describes the past, but because it preserves the distinctions between histories that must remain separated under a fixed budget to support good decisions. We cast this as a decision-centric rate-distortion problem, measuring memory quality by the loss in achievable decision quality induced by compression. This yields an exact forgetting boundary for what can be safely forgotten, and a memory-distortion frontier characterizing the optimal tradeoff between memory budget and decision quality. Motivated by this decision-centric view of memory, we propose DeMem, an online memory learner that refines its partition only when data certify that a shared state would induce decision conflict, and prove near-minimax regret guarantees. On both controlled synthetic diagnostics and long-horizon conversational benchmarks, DeMem yields consistent gains under the same runtime budget, supporting the principle that memory should preserve the distinctions that matter for decisions, not descriptions.

</details>


### [392] [Shepherd: A Runtime Substrate Empowering Meta-Agents with a Formalized Execution Trace](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.10913)
*Simon Yu,Derek Chong,Ananjan Nandi,Dilara Soylu,Jiuding Sun,Christopher D Manning,Weiyan Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Shepherd is a functional programming model and system for meta-agents that logs all agent-environment interactions as typed events, supports fast forking and replay of any past state, and enables efficient meta-operations like intervention, counterfactual search, and RL training improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Meta-agents—systems that supervise, intervene in, or optimize other agents—need a principled and efficient way to control, inspect, and manipulate target agents over time. Existing infrastructures (e.g., Docker-based forking or ad-hoc logging) are too slow or coarse-grained to support fine-grained replay, branching, and counterfactual experimentation at scale. The authors aim to provide a formal model and practical system that treat meta-operations as first-class, composable functions with reliable, replayable execution traces.

Method: They define Shepherd as a functional programming model where meta-agent operations on target agents are formalized as functions, with core operations mechanized in Lean for rigor. Every interaction between agent and environment is recorded as a typed event in a Git-like execution trace, so any prior state can be forked and replayed deterministically. System-level optimizations allow forking the agent process and filesystem significantly faster than Docker while leveraging prompt-cache reuse during replay. The authors then instantiate this infrastructure in three application settings: runtime intervention via a live supervising agent, counterfactual meta-optimization via branching exploration strategies, and Tree-RL training where rollouts are selectively forked at key decision turns.

Result: Empirically, Shepherd’s forking mechanism is five times faster than Docker for forking an agent process and its filesystem, and replays achieve over 95% prompt-cache reuse. In applications, a Shepherd-based live supervisor improves pair-programming pass rates on CooperBench from 28.8% to 54.7%. For counterfactual meta-optimization, branching exploration using Shepherd outperforms competing baselines by up to 11 percentage points on four benchmarks and can reduce wall-clock time by up to 58%. In Tree-RL training, selectively forking rollouts at specific turns boosts performance on TerminalBench-2 from 34.2% to 39.4%.

Conclusion: Shepherd provides a formal, efficient, and composable infrastructure for programming meta-agents, with a rigorously defined functional model and a Git-like execution trace enabling fast forking and replay. The demonstrated gains in intervention effectiveness, counterfactual optimization quality and speed, and RL training performance show that such an infrastructure is practically valuable. The authors conclude that Shepherd can serve as a foundation for future meta-agent research and release it as open-source to facilitate adoption and further work.

Abstract: We introduce Shepherd, a functional programming model that formalizes meta-agent operations on target agents as functions, with core operations mechanized in Lean. Shepherd records every agent-environment interaction as a typed event in a Git-like execution trace, enabling any past state to be forked and replayed. The system forks the agent process and its filesystem $5\times$ faster than Docker, achieving $>95\%$ prompt-cache reuse on replay. We demonstrate the model through three applications. First, in runtime intervention, a live supervisor increases pair coding pass rates from 28.8% to 54.7% on CooperBench. Second, in counterfactual meta-optimization, branching exploration outperforms baselines across four benchmarks by up to 11 points while reducing wall-clock time by up to 58%. Third, in Tree-RL training, forking rollouts at selected turns improves TerminalBench-2 performance from 34.2% to 39.4%. These results establish Shepherd as an efficient infrastructure for programming meta-agents. We open-source the system to support future research.

</details>
